### certus 'fixed, certain'
certus *fixed,certain' [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: certare 'to contend for superiority' (Pl.+), certamen, -inis 'competition, contest' (Pl.+), certatim 'with rivalry' (Pl.+), certatid 'contention' (Enn.+), concertare 'to contend' (Ter.+), decertare 'to fight an issue out' (Asellio+), incertus 'not fixed, uncertain' (Pl.+), incertare 'to make uncertain' (Pl.+).

Pit. *krito- 'distinguished'.

PIE *krito- 'sifted'.

IE cognates: Gr. κριτός 'separate'. The adj. certus continues the earlier ppp. to cemere 'to distinguish'. EM dismiss the view that certare would be denominative to certus, and argue that it is an iterative to cernd. However, the iteratives are built on ppp. in *-fos, so the explanation is essentially the same. The meaning of certare can be based on the original ppp-meaning 'distinguished, decided' of *kritos, before it acquired the meaning 'certain'. > "

Bibl.: WH I: 205, EM llo^lEW 938-947, Leumann 1977: 548, Schrijver 1991: 407f., Meiser 1998: 80, LIV *krehi(i)-- → cernd, cribjym r



### cervix, -icis
cervix, -icis 'neck' [f. k) (Pl.+)

Derivatives: incurvicervicus 'having the neck arched' (Pac.). WH suggest original *kers-ueikrs 'head band' to vincire, but this is semantically very doubtful. The suffix recalls that of coxendix 'hip', but the analysis of cerv- does not yield clear results (cf. Nussbaum 1986: 5).

Bibl.: WH I: 207, EM 116, IEW 576,



### cervus
cervus 'stag, deer' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: cerva 'hind, doe' (Pl.+), cervinus 'of a deer' (Varro+).

Pit. *ker(a)wo- 'homed animal, stag'.

PIE *ker(h₂)-uo- 'having horns*.

IE cognates: W. carw, Bret, karo 'stag, deer' < *kr(h₂)uo-; CLuw. zoruoniQa)- [adj/J Of a horn' < *]cr-uen-, Hit. ^karauar / karaun- 'horn, antlers'; Av, sruua- 'hom, nail', Gr. κεραός 'homed*; OPr. sirwis 'roebuck* < *kr(H)u-, curwis 'ox', Lith. karvi 'cow', OCS krava 'cow' < *Jkorh₂-uh₂-. Originally 'horned (animal)'; this derivative from 'horn' seems to be Italo-Celtic. The different ablaut grades (e/o/zero) found in the root of the -w-derivative in the IE languages suggest that many forms were influenced by the noun *ker-h₂- 'horn, bone*.

Bibl.: WH I: 208, EM 117, IEW 576, Nussbaum 1986, Schrijver 1995: 73f. → cerebrum, cernuus, cornu

<!-- pdf-page: 126; printed-page: 112; running-header: 112                                     ceterus -->



### ceterus
ceterus 'the rest, the other' [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

Pit. *etero- 'other'.

It. cognates: U. etraraa [acc.sg· + ad\ , etru [abl.sg.m.], etre [loc.sg.m/n.], etraf [acc.pl.fj, etres [abl.pl.jp/nj 'second, other' < *etero-.

PIE *h₁e-tero- 'the other (of two)'.

IE cognates: Av. atara- 'this one (of two)', Alb. Jeter, tjeter * other', OCS etertjeterb 'someone'. A compound of the particle ce 'here' (prefixed, as in cede) and

Pit. *etero- 'other'. The latter goes back to a PIE adj. of comparison in *-tero- derived from the pronoun *A/e- 'this, here'.

Bibl.: WH I: 208f., EM 117, IEW 281-286, Untermann 2000: 242f. → -ce, iterum



### ceu
ceu 'as, like' [ptcle.] (Enn.+)

Pit. *keiwe.

PIE *ke 'here* + *(hi)i-ue 'as'.

IE cognates: Skt iva 'like, as'. WH assume *fe/ 'here' + *ue 'or', but Watkins 1973b compares Skt. iva, which seems morphologically more convincing. Hence, ceu can be explained from the particle *fa? (Lat -ce) and

PIE *(h₁)i-ue 'as'. With Watkins, we can posit a development *keiwe > *keiw > *kew > ceu. The development *-eiVF- > *-iwV- did not take place because of the early apocope of *-e; compare seu next to sive.

Bibl.: WH I: 209, EM 117, IEW 73-75, Watkins 1973b: 202-206, Leumann 1977: 64, Meiser 1998: 86. → -ce3 -ve



### caveō
c£vefl 'to shake the hips' [v. II] (Mart.+)

PIE *kehiu-eie/o- 'to throw, shake'.

IE cognates: OCS kyvati, Cz. kyvati, Sin. kivati 'to nod' < *&/-. Schrijver suggests that ceved reflects a causative verb with e-grade in the root, of which there are more examples in Latin. But the meaning is iterative rather than causative. If the word originally had an o-grade (as LIV assumes), it is difficult to see why this was replaced by an e-grade.

Bibl.: WH I: 209f., EM 117, IEW 595, Schrijver 1991: 288f, 449, LIV ?*keh,u-.



### cibus
cibus 'food' [m. o] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: cibarius 'of food' (Pl.+), cibatus> -its 'food, fodder' (Pl.+). A root structure *k-bʰ is not allowed in PIE, whereas *b is an exceedingly rare phoneme. Hence, a PIE etymology would have to start from a root *lci-, with a suffix *-bʰ-. No good match was found. It may very well be a loanword, for instance, from Gr. κίβος> κίβισις 'box, chest', as proposed by Thurneysen 1907 (cf. WH).

Bibl.: WH I: 210f., EM 118, Untermann 2000:379.



### clclda
clclda 'cicada, cricket' [f. a] (Lucr.+) Probably an onomatopoeic formation, with reduplication. Compare cicoma 'stork', a bird also characterized by its clappering sound. Maybe, but not necessarily, a loanword.

Bibl.: WH I: 211, EM 119. -^ cicoma

<!-- pdf-page: 127; printed-page: 113; running-header: cieo                                        113 -->



### cicatrix» -icis
cicatrix» -icis 'scar-tissue, cicatrice' [f. k] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: cicatricosus 'scarred' (Pl.+). Etymology unknown.

Bibl.: WH I: 211, EM 119, IEW 565, Leumann 1977: 377.



### cicer, -eris
cicer, -eris 'chick-pea' [n. r] (Pl.+)

Pit. *hiker~.

IE cognates: Gr. κίκερροι 'pale' (Hsch.); Arm. sisefn 'chick-pea', Alb. ihjer * lentil9. Alb. ihjer might reflect *kiker- (cf. Demiraj 1997), but the initial syllable *&/- is of course not warranted by the Albanian word. The preform of Latin and Albanian might be derived from *kerh₃- 'to feed', as Jokl assumes - I do not share the semantic objections voiced by Demiraj. On the other hand, Arm. sisern could reflect *ke/oikerrn-7 which together with Latin would rather point to a loanword from an unknown source. Gr. κριός 'ram; kind of chick-pea' is unrelated: it is named after its curved shell.

Bibl.: WH I: 212, EM 119, IEW 598, Sihler 1995: 293.



### ciconia
ciconia 'stork' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: conea 'stork' (PL True. : 'Praenestinis conea est ciconia9). Cannot be connected with canire 'to sing', unless via *kph₂n-. But I doubt that anyone would refer to the bill-clattering of a stork as 'singing' - unless in a very ironic way. The reduplication is probably part of the onomatopoeic formation, as in cicada.

Bibl.: WH I: 212, EM 119, IEW 525f, Leumann 1977: 382. → cicada



### cicur, -is
cicur, -is 'tame, mild' [adj. r] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: cicurare 'to tame' (Pac.+). The alleged cognate Skt. sakura- 'tame, quiet' (only in Harsa carita) is attested too late to be credible. The etymology of cicur remains unknown.

Bibl.: WH I: 213, EM 119,



### cieo
cieo 'to move, stir up' [v. II; pf. civT, ppp. cittan] (Pl.+; variant cid, -ere)

Derivatives: accire 'to summon' (Pl.+)> conciere 'to stir up' (Pl.+), exciere 'to cause to move away, summon' (Pl.+), perciere 'to stir up' (Pl.+), citus 'quick, rapid' (Andr,+), cito [adv.] 'quickly, fast' (Naev.+), incitae [f.pl.] 'checkmate' (Pl.+), incites 'set in rapid motion' (Lucr.+); citare 'to set in motion, summon' (Pl.+), concitare 'to set in rapid motion, stir up' (Acc.+), excitdre 'to rouse' (Pl.+), suscitare 'id.' (Pl.+); sollicites 'restless, uneasy, anxious' (Pl.+), sollicitare 'to disturb' (Pl.+)> sollicitiido, -inis 'anxiety' (Pl.+), sollicitatid 'vexation' (Ter.+).

Pit. pr. *ki-eje-? aor. *kei-l ppp. *kito- 'stirred'.

PIE aor, *(Jc)ei(h₂)- 'to start to move', ppp. *(k)i(h₂)-to-.

IE cognates: Gr. aon εκιε * started to move', κίατο 'they moved' (Hsch.), pr. κϊνυμαι 'to be moved' < *(k}ih₂-. The verb -ciere has become obsolete in CLat., and was replaced by the frequentative citare. Solli-citus derives from sollus 'entire' and citus 'stirred'. LIV interprets cieo as

<!-- pdf-page: 128; printed-page: 114; running-header: 114                                    cilium -->

a secondary causative present with zero grade of the root; as Garcia-Ramon 1993a argues, it may have been grafted on ci-tus. The long vowel -J- of accire surfaces in the pf. (which might have *kei-w-, and concur with the Greek aorist). For citus, we must posit a preform without laryngeal (cf. Schrijver), or assume that the ppp. was grafted relatively recently on the pr. *ki-€~.

Bibl.: Sommer 1914: 509, 561, WH I: 213f, EM U9£, IEW 5381, Leumann 1977: 544, Schrijver 1991: 237f., Untermann 2000: 396, Meiser 2003: 228, LIV *(#φ2~. → concinno, cunctus



### cilium
cilium 'the eyelid* [n. o] (Plin.+)

Derivatives: supercilium 'eyebrow1 (Pl.+).

Pit. *kel-jo- [n.] 'eyelid'.

PIE *kel-io- 'hiding'.

IE cognates: see w.v. celo> occulo. Since cilium is more recent than supercilium, the former can be interpreted as a back-formation to the latter (WH, EM). If indeed derived from the root *kel- 'to hide', we must still assume that a noun *kilium 'eyelid' existed, since the eyelid can 'hide' the eye, whereas the eyebrow does not have such a iunction. Thus, supercilium may originally have meant 'what is above the cilium\

Bibl.: WH I: 214f, EM 120, IEW 553f., Leumann 1977: 294, LIV 1. *kel- 'to hide1. → celo, clam



### clmex, -icis
clmex, -icis 'bed-bug' [m. k] (Andr.+) For the suffix, cf culex, pulex. The etymological connection with IE words for 'black, dark-coloured* such as Skt. syava- 'dark, black' < *kiehruo- (thus e.g. IEW) is not compelling. The etymology must be regarded as unknown.

Bibl.: WH I: 216, EM 121, IEW 540f.



### cingo, -ere
cingo, -ere 'to surround, gird' [v. Ill; pf. cinxi, ppp. cinctum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: cingulum 'belt' (Varro+), cingillum 'woman's girdle' (Varro-H), cinctus^ -us 'the means of girding up clothes' (Lucil.+), cincticulus 'belt or girdle' (Pl.); accirtgere 'to gird, surround' (Ter.+X discingere 'to remove the belt' (Pl.+), praecwgere 'to encircle, gird* (Pl.+), succingulum 'garment round the loins' (Pl.+), succinctus 'gathered up, girded' (Pl.+).

Pit. *keng~?

It. cognates: *kink-ίο- in U. sihitu ansihitu, sihitu ansihitu [acc.pl.m.]9 sihitir ansihitir, sihitir ansihitir, sitir ansihitir [datpl.m.] 'girded ungirded'? These words refer to certain high officials, but meaning and etymology are uncertain. Uncertain etymology. Of the possible cognates adduced in IEW, Lith. kinkyti 'to bridle horses' fits semantical^ but requires a root *kin-kr, which cannot be PIE because of its structure, and which conflicts with Meiser's explanation of the Italic words (from *kenk-). Skt. koncate 'he ties', kancuka- 'armour' do not help much, cf. EWAia III: 42-45: the words are attested at a late date, and present formal irregularities,

Bibl.: WH I: 216f, EM 121, IEW 565, Sihler 1995: 534, Meiser 1998: 83.

<!-- pdf-page: 129; printed-page: 115; running-header: CIS                                         115 -->



### cinis, -eris
cinis, -eris 'residue of fire, ashes, ashes of a dead person' [m. r] (Pl.+; sometimes f.)

Derivatives: cinerarius [m.] 'hair-dresser' (Lucil.+).

Pit. *kenis- 'ashes'.

PIE *£/kon(H)-i-, *£/ken(H)-i- 'residue of fire, ashes, dust\

IE cognates: Gr. κόνις, gen. -ιος, -εως (-εος) [f] 'ashes, dust', κονίσαλος 'cloud of dust', κονϊω 'to cover with dust', κόνιος 'dusty', ToB kentse 'dust' < *koniso-. Lat. *kenis > cinis by assimilation, cf. similis < *semili- < *semali-. Whereas Latin seems synchronically to reflect a normal .s-stem (except maybe for cinxsculus\ Greek contains an original *-£y-stem, which was secondarily interpreted as /-stem. Latin *ken-i- may reflect either *ken(H)-i- or zero-grade *knH-i→ ToB kentse seems to reflect *kon-is-o-. If PIE, this would have to be a very early s-extension of an z-stem *kon-i-* Thus, there may have been a PIE ΐ-stem noun *kon(H)i-1 *ken(H)-i- 'dust', of which Latin cinis continues the e-grade. IEW connects cinis to

PIE *kneh₂- 'to plane, rub' (Gr. -κναίω), which is not compelling.

Bibl.: WH I: 217f., EM 121, IEW 559f, Leumann 1977: 101, Sihler 1995: 308.



### cippus
cippus 'boundary stone' [m. o] (Lucil.+; Mars., CIL 5: Cei/p.apur()finem)

Pit. *keipoA

PIE *(s)keip-o- 'pole, stick'?

IE cognates: Skt sepa-, sepha- 'tail, penis'. If the attestation ceip does indeed represent ceipus, oc if it is directly cognate with sapid 'stick', later cippus is an;instance of the littero:Tulc (see s.v. cello). Together with the ^-variants, discussed under scipio,

Pit. *keipy- can then go back to a noun *£e/oip-o- 'pole, stick'-

Bibl.: WH I: 219, EM 122, IEW 543, Leumann 1977: 183. → scTpio



### circus
circus 'circle; circus' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: circum [prep.] 'round about, near' (Pl.+), circa [prep.] 'round, about' (Lex Repetund.), circiter [adv,; prep.] 'nearly; near' (Pl.+), circumcirca [adv.] 'round about' (Pl.+), .idcircd [adv.] 'therefore' (Pl.+), quocirca 'on account of which' (VarrcH-); circulus 'circle, ring' (Acc.+). Circum is the old acc.sg. of circus; circa is a more recent formation (end 1st c. BC). Circus is probably a loanword from Gr. κρίκος 'ring', also κίρκος (Hell.), κιρκόω 'to hoop round, secure with rings' (Aeschylus), of uncertain origin. EM suggest that circus shows 'partial reduplication' of the element *Ar- in curvus 'curved', hence *ki-krro-, but this is speculative.

Bibl.: WH I: 220f, EM 122, IEW 935-938, Untermann 2000:419.



### cis
cis 'before, within; on this side of [prep.] (+ ace.) (Pl.+)

Derivatives: citer 'nearer' (Cato), exterior 'nearer, earlier' (Cic.+), ciiimus 'nearest' (Cic.+), extra [prep. + ace] 'on this side of (Lucr.+), citrd [adv.] 'to this side; on both sides' (Cato+).

Pit. *foi 'here, by', *ki-tero-.

It. cognates: U. ςίιηιι, simo [adv.] 'back' < *ki-mo- 'on this side*, U. $ive 'outside, in front* < locsg. of *iauo-. Theoretically, a preform *keim/uo- is also possible.

<!-- pdf-page: 130; printed-page: 116; running-header: 116                                      citrus -->

PIE *ki 'this here'.

IE cognates: Hit. ka- / ku- / ki-, Pal. ka- 'this', kiiat 'here', CLuw. za- /zi- 'this' <

PIE *ko-, *la-, OPr. schis, Lith. sis, OCS sb 'this'; Go. himma 'to this", hina 'this' [ace], Go. hidre 'hithe{'. The older system is cis - citer (citro) - citimus\ Lat citerior is a more recent remake of citer. Final -s in cis (as in uls) may be analogical to abs, usque, ex. That would imply that *ki remained alive as a separate word until quite recently; it shifted from being a pronoun to a preposition,

PIE *ki is a variant of *Jfce 'here, this', as represented in Latin by -ce. For the IE background, see Kortlandt 1983b.

Bibl.: WH I: 222, EM 123, IEW 609f., Leumann 1977: 317f., Untermann 2000: 397ff. → -ce, ceu, cedo



### citrus
citrus 'citron-tree (citrus medico cedra); African tree (thuia articulata)9 [f. o] (Varro+)

Derivatives: citrum 'the wood of the citron-tree' (Cato+), citrosus 'smelling of citron-wood' (Naev.).

IE cognates: Gr. κέδρος 'juniper-berry, cedar-cone'; κίτριον (Hell.) 'citron-tree'. Probably, Gr. κίτριον was borrowed from Latin citrus, which itself may have been borrowed from Gr. κέδρος and underwent the Latin change of *dr > tr (later, the word was again borrowed, now surfacing as Latin cedrus). But the initial vowels of κέδρος and citrus differ, and there may have been an Etruscan intermediate between Greek and Latin. Alternatively, both citrus and κέδρος might have been borrowed from a third (Mediterranean?) language.

Bibl.: WH I: 223, EM 124, Leumann 1977: 198.



### civis
civis 'citizen' [m., f. i] (Lex XII+ (ceivis), Pl.+)

Derivatives: civicus 'of one's town; civil' (Pl.+), cmlis 'of the citizens, civil' (Lucr.+), cTvitas 'an organized community, state' (Pl.+).

Pit. *kehvi- 'society'.

It. cognates: O. ceus [nom.sg.] 'citizen'.

PIE *kei-uo- 'friendly, intimate, dear'.

IE cognates: Skt. siva- 'favourable', Latv. sieva 'wife', Go. heiwa-frauja 'master of the household', OE hlwan [pi.] 'members of the household', OHG hiwo 'husband', OIc. hyski 'household, family'. O. ceus was probably borrowed from Latin *cevis before this became civis; hence, between 250 and 150 BC. The Lat. ί-stem inflection is mostly explained from analogy to the word hostis, but it could also be older. Vine 2006b assumes a PIE /-stem abstract noun 'society' derived from the adj. *kei-uo- 'socially close' continued in other IE languages. This would also explain why the derivatives civicus, cfvilis mean 'pertaining to society/the civic order' rather than 'pertaining to a citizen5. Lat. civis resulted from the singulative use of the abstract, as in optio [fi] 'choice' > [m.] 'assistant'. The IE root behind this formation is often assumed to be *kei- 'to lie', but this is semantically difficult. Vine 2006b: 148 embroiders on an earlier suggestion by Bader: he proposes a derivative in *-wo- from the pronominal stem *£/- 'this (here)' (> Skt. siva-), with full grade *keiuo- 'close'.

Bibl.: WH I: 224, EM 124, IEW 540, Untermann 2000: 395, LIV *kei-.

<!-- pdf-page: 131; printed-page: 117; running-header: clarus                                     117 -->



### clSdes, -Is
clSdes, -Is 'destruction, disaster, defeat' [f. e/i] (Pl.+)

Pit. *klade~, obi. *klad-.

PIE *klh₂-dʰehr 'beating, destruction, disaster'.

IE cognates: Gr. κλαω 'to break', Lith. kalti 'to beat (with a hammer)', Ru. kolot\ SCr. klati 'to slaughter'. Schrijver 1991 has argued that no cognate forms of the root *klh₂- 'to beat' can be found outside Italic with a certain dental enlargement. Hence, he reconstructs a PIE compound of the type Skt. sraddha- 'trust, belief < *kredrc?ehr> In view of the circumflex accentuation of Lithuanian nouns in -d!l, one might also envisage *-ctehr for the Latin nouns in -des (cf. Kortlandt 2005: 169).

Bibl.: WH I: 225, EM 124, IEW 545-547, Schrijver 1991: 173-175, LIV *kelh r . → -cello (2), calamitas



### clam
clam 'secretly' [adv.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: clandesfinus 'done in secret' (Pl.+), clanculwn 'secretly' (Pl.+).

Pit. *klam. PEE *kl-eh₂- 'concealment'. For the suffix, cf. palam, coram. Clanculum is a dim. in -culum directly built on clam (cf. plusculum). Clandesfinus presupposes an earlier adv. *clam-de (cf. quande\ to which the suffix *-stmo- was added (see s.v. intestmus). The form calim in Paul, ex F. 'antiqui dicebant por clam' is probably corrupt (thus! WH I: 138). It seems to be mostly agreed that clam derives,from *klam, but the PIE origin is less clear. Schrijver assumes a root variant *kelhr> the final laryngeal ofjwhich would be necessary for καλύπτω. Yet this stem has variants with different labials in Greek (καλυπ/β/φ-), and no forms without this suffix, or with e-grade. The Greek forms are not taken into account by LIV, who reconstruct only an anit root *kel-. Under these circumstances, a reconstruction as acc.sg. *klh₂-m is hard to justify. The zero grade in cU cannot be secondary within Latin, and probably reflects

PIE *£/r. The best option is to assume a stem *kl-eh₂- 'concealment', and regard clam as its original acc.sg.

Bibl.: WH I: 226f, EM 111, IEW 553f, Schrijver 1991: 175, 209, LIV *£ek → cella, celare, color, occulo

## V

\ clamo, -are 'to shout' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: clamor 'shout, cry5 (Pl.+, clamos Enn. Lucr.); clamitare 'to shout repeatedly' (Pl.+), clamitatid * shouting' (PI·); exclamare 'to cry out' (Pl.+), inclamare 'to call out to' (Pl.+X reclamare *to call out in response' (Lucr,+).

Pit. *kiam-o/a- 'shout(ing)'.

PIE *klhrm(o)- .

IE cognates: see cold. Lat. clamare looks like a derivative of a noun, maybe *klama- or *kIamo- 'shout, shouting', which was later replaced by clamor.

Bibl.: WH I: 227, EM 124, IEW 548ff., Schrijver 1991: 175, LIV *kleh r . -* calendae; cold; clarus clarus 'loud, sonorous; bright, shining, clear' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: clarere 'to shine brightly' (Enn.+), clarescere 'to become loud or clear'

<!-- pdf-page: 132; printed-page: 118; running-header: 118                                      classis -->



### (Lucr.+); claritudo
(Lucr.+); claritudo 'fame; brightness' (Cato+); clarore 'to make clear' (Enn.+), declarare 'to make known' (Lucr.+); praeclarus Outstandingly bright' (Lucil.+).

Pit. *klaro- 'loud' > 'clear'. *

PIE *klh r ro- 'loud, sonorous'.

IE cognates: see calare. The semantic shift from 'loud' to 'clear, bright' is also found in OHG hel 'loud', NHG hell 'clearly resounding; shining'.

Bibl.: WH I: 228, EM 125, IEW 548f, Schrijver 1991: 175, LIV *kleh r . → calendae\ cold; clamo



### classis
classis '(social) class; levy; fleet' [f. /] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: clossicus 'of the highest class; of the fleet' (Cato+).

Pit. *klassi- 'call-out, appeal'.

PIE *klhrd(h)-ti- 'a call' EM regard 'roll-call, appeal' as the oldest meaning. The word has been connected with Gr, κέλαδος 'noise', but this is semantically unattractive. If we accept the native Roman etymology with calare, we can reconstruct a derivative in a double dental, yielding *classis > classis. Obviously, without external support for this morphological build-up, it remains a paper reconstruction. Also, one would expect a simplification of the geminate ss to 5 after -5-. The meaning would have been 'the calling, call-out', which developed through 'class of soldiers being called out' to the later Latin meanings.

Bibl.: WH I: 228, EM 125, IEW 548ff, Schrijver 1991: 185, LIV *kelh r . -^ calo



### claudo, -ere
claudo, -ere 'to close' [v. Ill; pf. clausl, ppp. clausum] (Cato+)

Derivatives: claustrum 'bolt, bar; prison' (Pl.+); concludere 'to shut up, confine' (Pl.+), discWdere 'to separate' (Varro+), excludere 'to keep out' (Pl.+), exclusio 'debarring' (Ter.+), includere 'to insert, shut up' (Varro+), intercludere 'to block, cut off (Pl.+), occludere 'to block access, to stop* (Pl.+), recludere 'to open' (Pl.+).

Pit. *klaud-e/o-.

PIE *kleh₂u-d- 'to shut\

IE cognates: see clavis. Note that the simplex does not yet occur in Plautus, but some compounds do. Lat claustrum < *klaud*~tro~. See clavis for the reconstruction of the root Latin claudere might reflect *klaw-V-dry in which case it can be a derivation of atf-stemto clavis or clavus, or from their earlier basis *klaw-. But claudere may also reflect *klaudr < *kleh₂u-d-, in which case it would be rather close to OHG sliozan 'to shut' < *skleud-. Obviously, the Gm, verb must have a secondary e-grade. I prefer the second, verbal solution for claudere.

Bibl.: WH I: 229f, EM 126, IEW 604, Leumann 1977: 97, Schrijver 1991: 175, LIV ?*kleuH-. → clavis, clavus



### claudus
claudus 'lame, limping' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+ also clodus)

Derivatives: claudere 'to limp' (Caecil.+), claudicare 'to be lame' (Pl.+). The etymology is uncertain. Many scholars have tried to connect claudus with clavis

<!-- pdf-page: 133; printed-page: 119; running-header: clemens                                     119 -->


'bar' and clova 'club, stafP, as 'he who goes with a staff or 'he who has a hook, is hampered'· In that case, claudus may reflect *klawidos < *klaw- + -φο-. Yet adj. in -uhds are usually derived from verbs (which is unlikely here), or from nouns, in which case they indicate that someone or something is (like) the basic word. But a 'lame' person is not 'stick-like', at most, he 'walks with a stick'.

Bibl.: WH I: 231, EM 126, IEW 604,Nussbaum 1999a.



### clavis
clavis 'door-key, bar' [f. /] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: conclave [n.] 'a room' (Pl.+),

Pit. *klawi- 'bar, bolt\

PIE *kleh₂u- 'to close'.

IE cognates: Myc. ka-ra-wi-po-ro /κλαρνφόρος/, Gr. ep. Ion. κληίς, -ΐδος 'bar, bolt', Dor. κλσϊς, Att. κλείς, κλειδός < PGr. *klauT- based on a noun *klau(o)-. Denominative pr. κλείω, Old Attic κλήω, Hdt κληίω 'to shut' < ♦κλάρ-ie-·. SCr. klj'uc 'hook, seedling, key', gen.sg. kljuca [m.], Ru. kljuc' 'key', SCr. kljuka 'hook, door-knob * [fJ, Ru. kljuka 'walking-stick' < *kleh₂u-ks Lith. klwti, 3s, kliiiva 'to brush against, be caught in, obstruct' < klh^u-; OHG sliozan 'to shut*, sluzzil 'key', sloz 'lock' < *skleu-d-. Maybe here also OIc. hljota, OE hleoton 'to ballot for, toss', Go. hlauts 'fate, inheritance' < *kleud-, *kloiwk The oldest reconstructive form in Greek is *klau-\ if we assume that this was the basic form in Italic too, we cap explain why Latin derived both an /-stem (clavis) and an o-stem (clavus) from it. Thev*5 of Latin and Greek,* together with the acute accent of the Slavic forms, points to *kleh₂u-. In Germanic, if the forms are cognate, we find a root enlargement *-tf-, as well as s-mobile. The e-grade in sliozan must be secondary if the root was *klh₂d-, and the same goes for Slavic (*kleuh₂-kr « *kluh₂- < *klh₂u-). The Gm. ^-enlargement would match Latin claudere.

Bibl.: WH I: 229f, EM 125, IEW 604, Schrijver 1991: 175, LIV ?*kleuH-. → claudo, clavus



### clavus
clavus 'nail, peg' [m. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: clova 'wooden club, staff (Pl.+), clavator 'one who fights with a club' (Pl.+), clavula'\graft, cutting' (Varro), clavulus 'small naiF (Cato+).

Pit. *klawo- * closing, barring'.

It. cognates: possibly U. klavlaf [acc.pl.], klavles [abl.pl.] 'sacrificial instrument' < *klayVla-.

PIE *kleh₂u- 'to close'.

IE cognates: see clavis. Clavus derives from the same nominal basis as clavis. Since we also find Lat. clava 'wooden club*, the Pit. form *klawo- may have been an adj. 'which closes off.

Bibl.: WH I: 229f, EM 125, IEW 604, Schrijver 1991: 175, Untermann 2000: 399f., LIV ?*kleuH-. → claudd, clavis



### clemSns, -tis
clemSns, -tis 'mild, lenient' [adj. nt] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: dementia 'clemency' (Ter.+), inclementer 'rudely' (Pl.+). WH adopt an earlier suggestion by Breal and Osthoff, positing *klejomenos 'leaning' > *klemenos > *klemens, with analogical shift to ^-inflection. Yet the contraction in initial syllable of *ejo > e is doubtful, and the disappearance of o-stem inflection

<!-- pdf-page: 134; printed-page: 120; running-header: 120                                         clepo -->


completely unexpected. In view of vehemens 'violent', with its clear reference to veho 4 to move\ EM's suggestion that clemens is its antonym, and that both are compounds with mens 'mind' as their second membej, is attractive. In order to connect clemens with clino, one might reconstruct *k!oi-wo-ment→ compare the most productive nominal derivative of this root in Latin: clivus, prdclfvis 'slope, hill*. Latin *oi under stress between / and *w developed via *e to ϊ (as in clivus), but in front of *-wo-, it remained at a stage *e, cf. deus < *deos < *dewos < *deiuos 'god'. Thus also oleum, balneum. But the examples of shortening of *e > e all occur in front of word-final -us, -um. The answer will thus depend on the relative chronology. If the reduction of the medial vowel precedes the loss of *-w-, we expect an outcome *klewoment > *klewiment > *klfwiment- > *climent-. An ad hoc solution to our problem might be an analogical remodelling of *clTment- into clement- on the example of vement-* If the reduction of medial vowels occurred after w-loss, the result might have been *klewomeni- > *kieoment-. The outcome of the latter is uncertain, but contraction to e in the first syllable seems possible (cf dego < *de-ago).

Bibl.: WH I: 23If, WH 126, IEW 600ff → clivus



### clepō, -ere
clepO, -ere 'to steal' [v. Ill; pf. clepsf, ppp. cleptum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: Lat. cleps is attested once in a gloss, translated as 'thief. Whether this is an old form is impossible to say.

Pit. *k!ep-e/o- 'to steal', *kolpo- 'thief.

It. cognates: O. kulupu [gen.pL] < *kolpo~ 'thief (Rix 2002b: 424ff.)-

PIE *klep-e/o-, *kolp-o- 'thief.

IE cognates: Mir. cluain 'deceit', Gr. κλέπτω 'to steal', κλέπος [η,], κλέπτης [m.] 'thief, κλοπή 'theft, secret act', έπί-κλοπος 'deceitful', κλοπός, κλώψ 'thief, OPr. aukJipts 'hidden', Go. hlifan 'steal', ToB sb. kalypi- 'steal'. -

Bibl.: WH I: 232, EM 127, IEW 604, LIV *klep-.



### cliens, -us
cliens, -us 'client, vassal* [m., f nt] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: clienta [f.] 'female dependant' (Pl.+), clientela 'clientship' (Ter.+).

Pit. *klient- 'who supports' or 'who depends'.

PIE *kli-ent- 'who leans',

IE cognates: Skt. aor. asret 'leaned', YAv. ni-srita 'is handed over', Skt. srayate 'to lean', PAh.fle 'to sleep' (< *kloi-eie-7\ Lith. slieti, 3s. slieja 'to lean, rest (against)', slieti, 3s. slieja cid.', Latv. sliet, Is. slienu 'to support, erect, (-ties) lean' < *klek

PIE *kli-ent- is in origin an act. ptc. of the root aorist *klei-/*kli- 'to lean',

Bibl.: WH I: 233, EM 127, IEW 600ft, Meiser 1998: 183, LIV *klek → -clino, clitelh, clivus



### dingo, -ere
dingo, -ere 'to gird / close' [v. III?] (hapax legomenon: Paul, ex F. 'clingere, cingere, a Graeco κυκλοΰν dici manifestum est')

Pit. *kleng-e/o-?

IE cognates: Lith. klenkti 'to walk with difficulty', Latv. klencet 'to hobble', OCS kl?cf$ta [nom.du.] 'kneeling', SCr. klecati 'to kneel' < *klenkr; OHG lenk*, lenc*

<!-- pdf-page: 135; printed-page: 121; running-header: chtellae                                     121 -->


'left' < *kleng-o-, OE hlanc [adj.] 'slim' < *klong-o-, OIc. hlekkr [m, /] 'chain, ring', OE hlence [f. n] 'armour' < *klong-i-, OHG lanka*, lanca, hlanko* 'kidney, loin, hip', OEflanc [m.] 'flank'. The precarious transmission of this word in Latin renders its linguistic reality uncertain. If taken seriously, dingo might go back to a root *kleng- 'bent', as found in Germanic nouns and adjectives, BSL has a verb with a different final stop, but similar semantics. Possibly, U, *kreng/ka-tro- 'girdle' (?) is cognate.

Bibl.: WH I:233f„ EM 127, IEW 603, → U, krenkatrum



### -cēnō, -āre
-cBnfl, -are 'bend' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: dectinare 'to deflect, divert' (Pl.+), incUnare 'to cause to lean, bend downwards' (Pl.+), rectinare 'to cause to lie back' (Varro+); triclinium 'dining-room' (Naev.+); clinamen 'a turning aside' (Lucr.),

Pit. *kI(e)in-e/o-.

PIE *kl-n(e)-i- 'to lean to, bend' (often replaced by *kli-n-).

IE cognates: YAv, -sirinaoiti 'leans', Gr, κλίνω, Lesb. κλίννω 'to cause to lean' < PGr, *klin-j-e/o-, OHG hlinen, OE hlinian 'to lean', Lat, triclinium is a caique on Gr. τρίκλϊνον 'dining-room with three couches' (thus WH). Lat, -ctinare can be derived from the PIE nasal present *kli-n- 'to lean'; cp, -nare next to a simplex in *-nere corresponds to a pattern found in other verbs too, e.g. spernere - aspernarl The lbng vowel in -elm- may have been introduced from the root aorist *A/ez- / *Wi- (cf; cliens). J

clipeus 'round shield' [m., n. o] (Pl.+; also clupeus)

Derivatives: cli/upeatus 'armed with a shield' (Pl.+), cli/upeare 'to provide with a shield' (Pac). The vacillation between ι and u is observed especially after /-, and in front of labials; cf. HbeL The etymology of clipeus, however, remains unknown.

### clttellae
clttellae 'pack-saddle' [f.pl a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: cITtellarius 'used for carrying a pack-saddle' (Pl.+),

Pit. *kleitra- 'support'.

It. cognates: U. kletram [acc.sg,], kletra [ablsg.] 'portable litter, seat' for the icons of the divinities (M Weiss, fthc.b),

PIE *klei-tro/h₂- 'instrument for leaning, supporting',

IE cognates: Mir. clithar 'shelter', Go, hleipra 'tent' < *-tro-; OHG (h)leitara, OE hlced{d)er 'ladder' < *-dʰro-. Lat, clTtella < *klei-tre-la, the Latin diminutive of

Pit. *kleitra-9 which is also continued in Umbrian, Peruzzi 1992 assumes that clTtella had the form of two L- shaped supports which were jointly placed over the back of the pack animal.

Bibl.: WH I: 236, EM 128, IEW 600ff., Leumann 1977: 313, Untermann 2000: 400f., LIV *klei-. → cliens, -clTno, clTvus

<!-- pdf-page: 136; printed-page: 122; running-header: 122                                       clivus -->



### cUvus
cUvus 'hill, slope, declivity' [m. o] (Pl.+; clrva [n.pl.] Cato)

Derivatives: clivius 'bad, ominous* (Paul, ex F.y Plin.); acclfvis 'sloping upwards' (Lucil.+); procltve [n.] 'downward slope, (Naev.+), proctivis 'sloping down' (Pl.+), proclrvus 'sloping down' (Naev.+). Pit Hle/oiwo-.

PIE *kloi-uo- 'slope, hill' or *ldei-uo- 'sloping'.

IE cognates: Lith. sletvas 'bow-legged', Go. hlaiw, Old Norse hlaiwa, OHG OS hleo 'grave mound'. Lat clivius was used in the religious practice of auspicies (auguria clrvia Paul, ex F.) and referred to birds (avis cllvia Plinius) that entered the augur's templum from the left side. It thus shifted from 'sloping' through 'awkward, wrong' to 'ominous'.

Bibl.: WH I: 236, EM 127f„ IEW 600ff., LIV *klei-. → diem, -clino, clitello



### cloaca
cloaca 'sewer, underground drainage' [f. a] (Pl.+; clouaca Varro, inscr,)

Derivatives: Cloacina 'cult-title of Venus' ('the Purifier?) (Pl.+), cluere 'to purify (lx Plin.), chare 'purify' (Ix Serv.).

Pit. *klowa~.

PIE *kleuH-o- 'clean'.

IE cognates: W. clir 'clean' < *k!uH-ro-, Gr. κλύζω 'to wash' < *]clu-d-, Lith sluoti 'to sweep', sluota 'broom' < *kleh₃u-tei or *(ke-)kloH-tei, Go. hlutrs 'clean' < *kluH-d-ro-. Since an original sequence *kIowV- would have yielded *clau- (at least, in pretonic position), Vine 2006a: 217f posits an adj. *kleuH-o- 'clear, clean' from which a factitive pr. *kleuH-ehrie/o- > *klewaje/o- > *klowa- could have been derived. This verb might be preserved in the Servius gloss chare, although its reliability is often doubted. From *c!owa-9 the noun cloaca can then be explained. WH and Rix argue that dud may have been invented by Plinius to explain Cluacina\ but it might also derive from *cluwere < *klowere < *k!euH-e/o-. For the root, Derksen (fthc.) reconstructs *klh₃-u-, whereas Rasmussen posits *ldehru-. If one accepts such a root structure, the ablaut *kle/ou(H)- of Latin must represent a secondary full grade based on a zero grade *kluH- < *klHu-(X The short vowel of Greek κλύζω remains unexplained under any account.

Bibl.: WH I: 237, 239£, EM 128, IEW 607, Leumann 1977: 99, Rasmussen 1989: 71, Schrijver 1991: 394,447f„ Rix 1999: 519, LIV *kleuH-.



### clueō
clueO 'to be known' [v. II] (Pl.+; cluere only Sen.)

Derivatives: inclitus, inclutus 'famous, renowned' (Naev.+).

Pit. *klu-e- 'to be known', *k!u-to- 'known'.

It. cognates: SPic. kduiu [ls.pr.] Ί am called' < *lclu-e-6.

PIE *lcl(e)u- [aor.] 'to hear', *klu-to- 'known'.

IE cognates: Skt. aor. asrot 'heard', OAv. sraota 'must hear', Gr. aor. κλΰτε 'hear!', them, έκλυον, Latv. sluv, sluvet 'to become known', OCS sluti, Is. SIOVQ 'to count as, be called'; nasal pr. Olr. ro*cluinethar, SkL sppoti 'hears', YAv. surunaoiti, ToB kalnem, To A kalninc 'to resound'. Ppp. Olr. cloth [n.J 'fame', Skt. sruta-, Av. sruta-, Gr. κλυτός 'known, famed', Arm. low 'known', OHG Hlot- in names. Both inclitus and inclutus are found in inscriptions and in the mss., but inclitus seems

<!-- pdf-page: 137; printed-page: 123; running-header: cohum                                       123 -->

more frequent in the older authors, cf. TLL. Since, next to clued, inclitus is also the lectio difficilior, it seems likely that the latter represents the regular Latin development; inclutus may be due to restoration of the -w-. Among the other IE languages, only Latvian also has a stative *e-present, but this is probably an independent innovation. The use of in- in inclutus probably indicates intensification of the sound, as in increpare and insonare.

Bibl.: WH I: 237ff., EM 129, IEW 605ff., Leumann 1977: 80, Untermann 2000: 378, LIV *ldeu-,



### clunis
clunis 'buttocks, haunch (of animals)' [m.f. i] (Pl.+)

Pit. *klouni-.

PIE *£lou-ni- 'hip, buttock',

IE cognates: W, clun 'haunch', Skt. sronl· 'buttock, hip, loin', Av, sraonl· 'id.', Lith. slaunis 'haunch, hip', OIc, hlaun 'buttocks, loin\ Maybe Gr, κλόνις 'haunch'; it cannot continue *kloun-, but is often assumed to have undergone contamination with κλόνος * excitement'. This is an imaginative hypothesis, but rather gratuitous. The morphological formation of *£lou-ni- is unknown, there is no good PIE root v etymology. :

Bibl.: WH I: 239, EM 129, IEW 607f. > cohors, -tis 'farmyard, contingent, cohort' [f. i] (Pl.+)4

Derivatives: cohortan 'to exhort, encourage' (Cato+),

Pit. *kom + *xortl·.

PIE *gʰr-ti- 'enclosure' or 'bundle',

IE cognates: Skt. harati 'takes, brings'. Cohors looks like a /f-derivative to a verbal root, possibly to

PIE *gʰer- 'to take, grab', maybe even a compound *kom-gʰer-.

Bibl.: WH I: 242f, EM 131, IEW 442f, Leumann 1977: 345, LIV ?2,*gʰer-. → hortus


cohum Varror^'the hollow in the middle of a yoke', Paul, ex F.: 'leather strap, by which the polevof a plough-beam is connected with the yoke" [n. o] (Varro+)

Derivatives: cohum 'vault of the sky' (Enn,); incohare 'to start making' (Enn.+), incohatus 'unfinished, only begun' (Pl.+).

Pit. *Ιωχο- 'hole' or 'tie, juncture*.

It. cognates: O. kahad [3s.pr.cj.], καίιας [2s,pr.cj.?] maybe 'to take' < *kagʰ-? U. cehefi [inf.ps.], kukehes [com + 2 or 3s.fut,] 'to take/get' < *kax-je~ (M, Weiss, p,c),

IE cognates: GauL caii 'fence', OBret. caiou [pL] 'stockade', W. cae 'fence, collar, Co, he 'fence'; OHG hag 'hedge, fence', OE haga 'fence, garden'. The word cohum, coum "is not attested outside glosses, and its primary meaning is obscure" (Schrijver 1991: 441), Varro seems to be influenced by folk etymology with cams, whereas Paulus invokes cohibere. It has been derived from *couom 'hollow' by Thurneysen, but he later dropped this explanation. In fact, it is extremely unlikely for chronological reasons. On the other hand, incoho is a frequent verb, and its meaning can easily have derived from 'to yoke a plough to a team of oxen' vel sim.,

<!-- pdf-page: 138; printed-page: 124; running-header: 124                                     coinquo -->

in other words, 'to start work'. Thus, there might be a core of truth in the ancient connection of cohum with a yoke. In order to connect cohwn with the Sabellic root *kax- 'to take\ only *kh₂gʰ- would be poesible, but such a root violates the PIE root structure constraints. Since we further only find Germanic and Celtic nominal forms in "tag*-, we are probably dealing with a loanword into Germanic and Italo-Celtic.

Bibl.: WH I: 243f., EM 131, 314, IEW 518, Rix 1976, Schrijver 1991: 441, Untermann 2000: 362, 380f, LIV *(£agʰ-. → coulae



### coinquo, -ere
coinquo, -ere 'to prune' [v. Ill] (Acta Arvalia, Paul, ex F.) The Acta Arvalia speak about Wed co(i)nquendi 'of pruning in the grove'; Paul ex F. defines coinquere as coercere and deputare. WH propose an etymology *co-in-sec-o to secare 'to cut', via *co-insco > *coined. This would have secondarily got -uo, as in instingud next to instigd; yet see s.v. stinguo, where it is assumed that -wo was original.

Bibl.: WH I:244, EM 131.



### colet
colet 'testicles' [m.pl. o] (Lab.+)

Derivatives: coleatus "provided with testicles' (Pompon.). WH derive cdleus from colum 'sieve' as original 'sieve-sack', but an adjective *coleus derived from colum would rather heave meant 'made of a sieve', which makes less sense. It seems preferable to connect culleus 'bag', but the details are unclear; an IE origin seems distant anyway.

Bibl.: WH I:244, EM 131. -* culleus



### collis
collis 'hill' [m. /] (Pac.+)

Derivatives: collmus 'belonging to hills' (Varro+).

Pit. *koIni-.

PIE *kolH-n- 'hill'.

IE cognates: Gr. κολώνη 'hill', also κολωνός (Hymn to Ceres+) < *kolff-on-o/hr, Lith. kalnas 'hill', Latv. kalns 'mountain' < *kolHno-9 Lith. kedva [f.] 'small hiir, Latv. kalva 'hill, island in a river'; Go. hallus 'rock' < *koIH-nu-, OE hyll 'hill' < •A/ff-ni-. The root is

PIE *klH- 'to lift' as in -cello J. The cognate Greek, BSl. and Germanic words for 'hill' or 'mountain' are built on an w-stem *koIH-on-, which itself is not preserved, but Greek does preserve the d-grade of the nom.sg. suffix. The other languages have the zero grade *-n- plus an extra suffix -*-, -u- or -CK In Latin, *kolHni- would be expected to yield *kotanis whence *colnis. Therefore, collis may be an instance of the disappearance of *H when bordering a resonant which in its turn borders on an o-grade (the so-called 'de Saussure effect'). Of course, *H may regularly have been lost in front of the suffix variants *-6w and *-on. Nussbaum 1997 considers the alternative etymology *kol(H)u-i- equally possible; this is true as far as the phonetics are concerned, but the pervasive w-stems in the other languages (only Baltic has *kolH-u-) render the traditional etymology more likely.

Bibl.: WH I: 245, EM 132, IEW 544, Schrijver 1991: 326, Nussbaum 1997: 195, LIV *kelH- 'to tower'. → -cello U columen

<!-- pdf-page: 139; printed-page: 125; running-header: colo                                        125 -->



### collus
collus 'neck (of men and animals)' [m. o] (Naev.+; collum Cic.+)

Derivatives: collare [n.] 'neck-band, collar' (Pl.+).

PIE *k(w)ol(H)s/no- 'neck'.

IE cognates: Go. hah 'neck', OHG hals, OIc. hab. The word for 'neck' may derive from 'wheel', as is shown by Lith. kaklas 'neck', Latv. kakts 'neck, throat' <

PIE *kʷo-kʷl-o- 'wheel1 to the PIE root *kʷel(H> 'to turn'» Since Latin -//- may reflect *-Zs-, collus could go back to a thematized derivative *kʷe/ol-s-o- of the s-stem for 'wheel' attested in Slavic: OCS kolo [n.], gen.sg. kolese, Ru. koleso 'wheel' <

PIE *kʷol(H)-es-. Go. hals is also often derived from

PIE *kʷel(H)- 'to move, turn', but it is uncertain whether *kʷo- was regularly unrounded to *ha- in Germanic; it seems safer to reconstruct only *kolso~. The Gm. word could still be related to Lat collusr, but in that case the derivation of collus from *kʷel(H> must be given up. Mir. coll 'head, chief could simply be an abridged form of Lat collus (LEIA C-158).

Bibl.: WH I: 245, EM 132, IEW 639f, Meiser 1998: 116.



### colo, -ere
colo, -ere 'to live in, inhabit; to take care of, honour' [v. Ill; pf. colui^ ppp. cultum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: coldrrus 'cultivator, farmer; colonist' (Cato+), coldnia 'settlement, colony' (Pl.+), colonicus Of a colony' (Varrp+); incultus 'not cultivated' (Pl.+); cultor 'inhabitant, cultivator' (Pl.+), cuhus, -its 'habitation; cultivation' (Pl.+), cultura 'cultivation, care' (Cato+); actqlere 'to live near' (Naev.+), accola [m.] 'neighbour' (Pl.+), incolere 'to dwell' (Pl.+), incola [m.] 'inhabitant' (Pl.+), excolere 'to cultivate, develop' (Varro+), recolere 'to resume, practise again' (Pl.+); ogricola [m.] 'farmer' (Pl.+), caelicola [m.] 'inhabitant of heaven' (Enn.+), latebricola [m.] 'who skulks in concealment' (Pl.), silvicola 'inhabiting woodlands' (Naev.+); inquilimis 'inmate, lodger' (Cic.+). Maybe Exquiliae 'one of the hills of Rome' (Varro+),

Pit. *kʷele/o- [pr.], *Κ*οΙδ-ηο-'>

It. cognates: U. afpeltu [3s,ipv.II]?

PIE *kʷelhre/o- 'to go round'.

IE cognates: Skt. carati, Av. cara- 'to move, walk', Gr. πέλομαι 'to move (intr.)\ Cret. τέλομαι Ί will be', άμφιπέλομαι 'to surround', Alb. sjell 'turn,;bring' < *kʷel-e/o-; Gr- τέλος [n.j 'goal', Lith. kelys 'knee' < *kʷel-; OCS koleno 'knee, descent', OCS kolo, gen.sg. kolese 'wheel' < *kʷolres- [n.]. The change of *e to ο was conditioned by initial *kʷ- and a back vowel in the next syllable, and/or by the intermediate velarized /. The vowel -o- was retained in open syllable, and shifted to u in front of -/C-. If inquilimis (which is not attested before Cicero) is to be trusted, it probably shows the developement *-#V > *-£"'/- (no change to *-λΗο- because of the palatal /; reduction to / in medial syllable). Since there is no vowel reduction in incola, agricola, these nouns must have restored -o- from the simplex colere. The suffix οι col-onus is explained by Leumann 1977: 323 as an antonymical formation versus patromis, grafted on the stem of agri-cola. Alternatively, one might regard coldnus as a deinstrumental noun in *-no- from an ins.sg. *kʷolhrohj 'with cultivation', i.e. from an earlier action noun *kʷolhr°- 4 going round'. The agent noun *k*olhro- is represented in Latin anculus.

Bibl.: WH I: 245ff., EM 132f., IEW 639f., Leumann 1977: 86f.s 323, Untermann 2000: 52f., LIV *k w elh r 'turn'. → anculus

### color
color 'colour' [m. r] (Pl.+; nom.sg. colds Pl.+)

Derivatives: colorare 'to give colour' (Cato+).

Pit. *kelos- [m.] 'outside, aspect'. t

PIE *lcel-os, acc.sg. -os-m 'cover, covering'.

IE cognates: Skt. soras- 'skin on milk', OHG hulis(a) 'hull, shell', MHG huhi 'cover'. Latin *kel5s is an animate collective, built to an s-stem

PIE *keLos- 'cover'. This might be directly preserved in Skt saras-, although this is semantically not compelling (cf Stuber). Germ. *xulisjo- has also been derived from the PIE s-stem. In Latin, the word shifted its semantics from 'covering' to 'colour' (cf. Skt. varna-).

Bibl.: WH I: 247, EM 133, IEW 553f, Nussbaum 1999a: 387, Stuber 2002: 116, LIV 1 .*kel-, → celare, cello, occulere, clam



### colubra
colubra 'snake, serpent' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: colubnnus 'snake-like' (Pl.+), coluber, -bn 'snake' (Verg.+).

Pit. *kolos-ro-l WH and IEW propose a preform *(s)kelo-c?lro- 'winding itself, whereas EM consider colubra a possible loanword from Gr. χέλυδρος 'amphibious serpent' (Nicander, 77f,411). The etymology with the suffix *-dʰro- is difficult, since this usually makes instrument nouns. A borrowing from Gr. χέλυδρος is very unlikely, since the word is attested too recently, and its meaning 'serpent' is probably secondary; note that χέλυς means '(kind of) tortoise'. In view of the meaning, one might connect colubra with colus 'distaff < *kʷelhru-· A distaff is used to wind a thread or fibre around it. Hence, a preform *kolos-ro- would mean 'distaff-like' or 'of a distaff (cf. tenebrae\ and since a snake also winds around its own axis, it might have been called 'distaff-Jike animal'. Morphologically, this solution is not straightforward, since colus probably continues a w-stem. We would have to assume that an s-stem *kʷelhros- existed beside it, which is unwarranted - but not impossible, cf, Slavic kolo 'wheel' ( « *kʷolhro-y

Bibl.: WH I: 248, EM 133, IEW 928. → colus



### colum
colum 'strainer, sieve' [n. o] (Cato+)

PIE *kʷot-slo-? Since the oldest form of a sieve was a wicker basket, it has been assumed that colum is identical to caulae 'railing, lattice barrier'. In that case, δ would be a non-urban monophthongization of au, but it seems strange that au is not once attested in colum. Since Lat qualum 'basket' denotes more or less the same object as colum, it seems preferable to look for a joint solution for both. See s.v. qualus/m.

Bibl.: WH I: 248f., EM 134, IEW 518, Schrijver 1991:462. → qualus/m



### columba
columba 'pigeon, dove' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: columbus 'male pigeon' (Pl.+), columbinus 'of pigeons' (Cato+), columbar(e) [n.] 'compartment for pigeons' (Pl.+), columbarium 'id.' (Varro-l·), columbarius 'pigeon-keeper' (Varro).

IE cognates: OPr. golimban 'blue', Lith. gelumbi 'cloth', OCS golgbb 'pigeon,

<!-- pdf-page: 141; printed-page: 127; running-header: colustra                                    127 -->

dove', Ru. golub' 'id.'; Ru. goluboj 'pale blue'. Further removed in meaning is Gr. κόλυμβος'small diver'. The BSl. forms presuppose a form *gefol-ofembifo- 'blue', whence 'dove'. Whereas a suffix *-bʰo- is often found in PIE colours and animals, the preceding syllable with a nasal consonant does not make a very IE impression, Ifcolumba is cognate, we would have to posit an irregular consonant alternation in anlaut Latin palumbes 'wild dove' may have replaced an earlier *pale$ (vel sim.) 'gray/blue dove' on the example of columba, cf Lockwood 1990.

Bibl.: WH I: 249, EM 134, IEW 547f. →palumbes



### columen, -inis
columen, -inis 'top, summit' [n. n] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: oilmen, -inis 'summit (of a building), peak' (Varro+); columna 'column, pillar' (Pl.+), columella 'small upright post' (Cato+), columnatus 'supported by pillars' (Pl.+).

Pit. *kelamen~.

PIE *keIH-men- 'top, summit'.

IE cognates: OS holm 'hill\ NHG Holm, OE holm 'island', OIc. holmr, holmi 'small island' < *yulma- < *klH-mo-. Lat. culmen arose in the foursyllabic oblique case-forms: nom. *kelamen, gen. *kelamenos > *kolamen> *kolamenos > *kohmen, *kolmenos > columen, *culmini$. On the basis of the oblique forms, a new nom.sg. culmen was then created. But it seems difficult to derive columna from columen by any normal process. WH reconstruct **kel-o-meno- 'who towers up' to ex-cella Yet this verb goes back to *fcel-n-9 and there is no other evidence for a present FTE*kelH-(e/o-) in Italic with the meaning 'to rise'. There is a present *kʷelH-e/o- 'to turn', of course, but the semantic shift from 'going around' to 'pillar' is difficult (although not impossible: the pillars of a portico or a temple together usually 'surround' the sanctuary).

Bibl.: WH I: 249f., EM 134, IEW 544, Schrijver 1991: 326f, LIV *kelKk → -cello 1, co11is (. colus, -I/-fi$ 'distafF [m./f. o/u] (F1.+)

It. cognates: *k™elu~.

PIE *kʷelh₁-u- 'turning*.

IE cognates: see s.v. cold. Has been compared with Gr. πόλος 'axis, pivot', but WH are hesitant, because a distaff itself does not spin around.

Bibl.: WH I: 250, EM I34£, IEW 639f, Schrijver 1991: 469, LIV *k w elh r 'turn'. → cold, colubra



### colustra
colustra 'beestings, first milk of a milking' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: colostrum 'beestings' (Mart.+). The older form colustra (PL, LuciL, Lab,) has developed into colostra in more recent texts, probably by progressive vowel assimilation of the type alacer, vegetus (cf. Sommer 1914: 66f.)- The etymology is uncertain. It is tempting to connect colustra with color 'colour' < *'cover', and to postulate a semantic link between 'colour' or

<!-- pdf-page: 142; printed-page: 128; running-header: 128                                      com- -->


'cover* and 'beestings'. Yet this is not semantically straightforward (see WH), nor is the suffix -tero- normally used to derive comparatives from any adjective.

Bibl.: WH I: 247, EM 133. → color



### com-, con-, co-; cum
com-, con-, co-; cum 'ready, completely; with' [pref.; prep.] (VOLat.^: Garigliano Bowl kom). Variants: com- before p/b/m-, con- before t/d/n/s/j/f/v-, con- [korj-] before C/qu/g-, cor- before r-, col- before /-, co- before vowels and Λ-.

Pit. *kom.

It. cognates: U, kum-, ku-, com-, co-, O. kum-, com-, Vol. co- 'to, together, with'; prepos. O. com, con, U. com + abl. 'with', postpos. U. -kum, -ku, -com, -co 'at'.

PIE *kom 'with' (+ ins.).

IE cognates: Olr. con- 'with', pref. Gaul, com-, Olr. com- (proclit con-), W. cyf-, cyn~, Co. kev-\ Hit. -khan '?' (locatival sentence particle); Gm.

Rosen 1992 distinguishes the perfective-valency prefix co- from the comitative-sociative postposition and prefix cum, cu, co-. He shows that in the oldest texts, co- does not contain the notion of 'together', but only perfectivizes the verb: concedere vs. cedere7 complere vs. plere, colloqui vs. loqw, combibere vs. bibere, etc. Only from the end of the Republican era do we find co(n)- in the function of an adverb 'together': concadere (older concidere\ compugnare, etc. Also, the word-final nasal is absent in front of vowels, where we most expect it. Yet we cannot reconstruct a different preform for this perfectivizing co(n)-, such as *kʷo, which would match Celtic forms. Attractive as this may seem, it does not explain why Sabellic does not continue a labiovelar stop, while it does show a final nasal. Also, Latin q- should have been retained in initial position. Thus, it seems better to stick to the traditional etymology *komy and to assume that com- and cum 'with' are the same words. The 'perfective' meaning of co(m)- present in older Latin is unproblematic (compare Slavic siy- and Lithuanian su-); nor is it problematic that a preverb meaning 'towards' when movement or action of two or more people is referred to, comes to mean 'together* at a later stage. The disappearance of -m before vowels (and *w-?) and h- points to word-final sandhi behaviour, *kom a- > *ko a- > co-a-. In words in which the morpheme boundary after corn- has become obscure, m remains: comes, -itis, comedo.

Bibl.: WH I: 25If., EM 156, IEW 612f., Leumann 1977: 137, 226, 559, Schrijver 1991: 82, Rosen 1992, Untermann 2000: 409ff. -* contra, curia



### combretum
combretum 'some kind of aromatic plant with thin leaves' [n. o] (PIin.+) Probably, a plant collective in -etum. In Latin, the ο of combretum is probably secondary, or reflects *-we-. IEW connects combretum with Molr. cuinneog, Gael. contran 'Angelica silvestris', Lith. pi. svendrai 'reed, reed-mace, Typha latifolia', MoDan. quander 'Angelica silvestris', OIc. hvqnn 'Angelica silvestris'. These alleged cognates have been studied in detail by Heiermeier 1980, who concludes that the evidence is uncertain or simply does not exist. Therefore, this connection can be abandoned. No alternative etymology is available.

Bibl.: WH I: 253, EM 135, IEW 631, Heiermeier 1980.

<!-- pdf-page: 143; printed-page: 129; running-header: concinnus                                    129 -->



### comes, -itis
comes, -itis 'companion' [m. /] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: comitium (Lex XIR), pi. comitia 'people's assembly' (Pl.+), comitan 4 to accompany, attend' (Lucr.+), comiiialis Of the comitia' (Pl.+), comitiatus, -us 'an assembly of the people' (Lex XII+), incomitiare 'to revile, abuse' (Pl.+).

Pit. *kom-i-t- 'going together' or (pi.) 'who come together'.

PIE *-hli-t- 'going, goer'. A compound of com- plus the root for 'to go', enlarged with a PIE suffix *-/-. The nom.sg. -es must be due to analogical replacement of *-is by -es on the model of /-stem nouns.

Bibl.: WH I: 253, EM 135, IEW 293ff., Leumann 1977: 94, 275, Lindner 2002: 225, LIV *hiei-. → ed, pedes



### comis
comis 'kind' [adj. i] (Naev.+; Duenos inscription cosmis)

Derivatives: cdmitas 'friendliness' (Pl.+)·

Pit. *kom-smi-7

PIE *smi- 'smile'?

IE cognates: Skt. smayate 'smiles', Gr φιλο-μμειδής 'who likes to laugh*, Latv. smiet 'to laugh', OCS smijatis^ ToB smiyarn 'smiles'. The etymology *ko-smi- (cf. WH) is based on an interpretation ofi comis as 'smiling (to/along)'. Eichner 1993a: 212, 230 proposes to derive VOLat. cosmis from a verb *ko-smei- 'to contract the face-to a smile, smile'; but he^leaves open the possibility of a prefixed possessive adj. 'who has a smile, who is accompanied by a smile'. In my view, only the latter option c^n explain why comis is^an adjective. Still, this would require a root noun *smi- 'smile' for which there is no supporting evidence in IE, and which, in the absence of a cognate Latin verb, cannot be a recent formation. Therefore, I regard the proposed etymology as uncertain.

Bibl.: WH I: 254, EM 135, IEW 967f, Meiser 1998: 4, 112, LIV *smei-,



### concinnus
concinnus 'set in order, neat' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: concinnare 'to make ready, make into' (Naev.+), exconcinnare 'to embellish' (Pl.+), reconcinnore 'to refurbish' (Pl.+), cinnus/m 'grimace' (Sen.).

Pit. *kom-kid-np- 'set in motion'.

PIE *kid-no- 'which started to move'. WH derive concinnarefromcinnus 'mixed drink' (Nonius), but EM are probably right when they point to the rare and late attestation of cinnus, see also Haug 2004: 24f. Leumann suggests that concinnus might be a back-formation to concinnatus. Latin -nn- may reflect *-tn-y *-dn- or *-nn-. Hence, a connection with caedo 'to hit', which is mentioned but rejected by WH, seems quite possible: *kom-kid-no- 'hit, arranged'. Since the root is reconstructed as *kh₂id-, this would imply a development *kom-kh₂id-no- > *kom-kinno- > concinnus. Haug also rejects this etymology, since it would require an intermediate step 'to cut', for which there is no evidence. But in fact, I do not think that this step is necessary. Haug 2004 proposes a different etymology: a root *keid- 'to set in motion', which would be a variant of *keih₂- 'to start to move'. He then derives concinnusfrom*kom~kid-no- and -cinnare from an adj. * kid-no-, which would survive in cinnus 'grimace'. This etymology is attractive on the formal

<!-- pdf-page: 144; printed-page: 130; running-header: 130                                     condiS -->

side; it would imply that the Germanic forms reflecting

PIE *keid- 'to call' (Go. haitari) and Latin *kidno- would derive from a variant of *k/keih₂- 'to set in motion'.

Bibl.: WH I: 218f., EM 136, Leumann 19f7: 268, Haug 2004, LIV *(k}eih₂-. → caedo, cieo



### condiō, -īre
condio, -Ire 'to season, flavour (food)' [v. IV] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: condimentian (Pl.+) 'seasoning, condiment7, conditio 'method of preserving (food)' (Varro+), condifivus 'suitable for preserving' (Cato+). Probably a derivation from condere 'to put; store up (e.g. food)' (see s.v. -do, -dere) by analogy with another verb in -ire (but which one is uncertain).

Bibl.: WH I: 258f., EM 137. — -do



### coniveo
coniveo 'to be tightly closed, close (of the eye)' [v. II; pf. convci, more recent variant οδητνί] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nictare 'to blink, signal with the eyes' (Pl.+), adnictare 'to wink (to)' (Naev.), nictus, -its *a wink' (Caecil.+).

Pit. *kneixw-e- 'to blink', or 'to draw together (the eyes)', *knikto-.

It. cognates: uncertain U. kunikaz, conegos [ppp«, nom.sg.m.]*?' ('kneeling'?) < *kom-niga-tos·

IE cognates: Go, hneiwan, OHG nigan 'to bow, be inclined', caus. Go, hnaiwjan 'to humiliate', OHG neigen. Contvere shows a phonetic development *kom-kn- > *kor)xn- > *\ωχη- > cdn-, cf. Leumann 1977: 218. Nictare is afrequentativebuilt on the ppp. *nikto-. The root has the structure *T-Dh, which was rare or absent in PIE; therefore, and since the word occurs only in Germanic and Italic, we may be dealing with a loanword from an unknown, 'European' language. The vowel -F- in medial syllable points to *-«-, not *-ϊσ/-, hence the verb may have been a stative 'to be blinking, drawing the eyes together'.

Bibl.: Sommer 1914: 265, WH I: 260, II: 166, EM 137f., 440, IEW 608, Leumann 1977: 188,218, Meiser 1986: 88, Sihler 1995: 163, de Vaan 1999: 12, 21, Untermann 2000:417, LIV *Kneigyh-. → nxtor



### cūnor, -ārī
cunor, -Sri 'to make an effort, attempt' [v. I; ppp. conatum) (Pl.+)

Derivatives: conatus, -us Effort, attempt' (Ter.+), cdnamen 'effort' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *konaje/o- 'to attempt'.

PIE *kon- 'ascent'?

IE cognates: MW di-gawn, di-chawn 'works; is able', Av. asBnaoiti * ascends', sanat 'ascended', Gr. έγ-κονέω 'to be quick and active', διάκονος 'servant', Ann, med. snow 'was raised', SCr. kaniti 'to intend'. LIV reconstructs

PIE *kdnhrie-9 adopting a specific type of presents posited by Klingenschmitt 1978, In my view, the existence of this type is unwarranted. Isebaert reconstructs *kon-eh£e→ whereas Tremblay, who connects Iranian *san-, reconstructs the root as *ken- *to be raised, moved'. Tremblay assumes that this type of verbs derives from lengthened-grade thematic adjectives which in their turn are based on nouns with o-grade. Hence, *lcon-o- » *kon-o- [adj,] » *kon-eh₂-ie- [v.]. This analysis is attractive, but it is uncertain at which stage the denominal adj. and hence

<!-- pdf-page: 145; printed-page: 131; running-header: contamino                                    131 -->

the verbs were derived: it may have been a post-IE process. Also, the original PIE formation may rather have been a root noun *lcon- 'ascent' > 'effort, attempt', with regular development of a long vowel in the monosyllabic nom.sg.

Bibl.: WH I: 262, EM 138, IEW 564, Tucker 1990: 226f£, Sihler 1995: 528, Tremblay 1998, Isebaert 1992: 205, LIV *kenh r , *Jcen-,



### conquinlscO, -ere
conquinlscO, -ere 'to crouch down1 [v. Ill; pf. conquexi\ (Pl., Pompon.)

Derivatives: ocquiriiscere 'to bend over before someone5 (Pompon.). The pf. -quex- contains an original long vowel, since short *kʷeks- would yield *-cox-. From a stem *kʷeg-, we can explain the present via *-lcitfeg-no- > *-k"igno- > *-qmno-y remade into -quimsco. That is, if -qui- has long T, which is not certain - the dictionaries give it as short For the adv. cossimy see s,v. coxa. The IE etymology is unknown.

Bibl.: WH I: 262, EM 138, Leumann 1977: 536, LIV *kueit-, *kuenk-.



### consulo, -ere
consulo, -ere 'to consult' [v. Ill; pf consult*^ ppp. consultum\ SCBac. co[nJsoleretury consoluerunt] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: consul, -is 'consul, magistrate' (Naev.+, Elog.Scip. consoty consilium 'debate, advice' (Pl.+), consiliarius [adj.] 'advising5 (Pl.+), cdnsilidsus 'instructive' (Cato apud Gell.+); cdnsultum-/decision, decree' (Pl.+), incdnsultus 'thoughtless, rash' (Pl.+), incdnsultus* -us 'lack of consultation' (Pl.); consultare 'to deliberate, consult' (Pl.+), consultatio 'deliberation consultation' (PI;+).

Pit. *kom-se/ol-e/o- 'to consult', *kom-sel- [m.J 'advisor'.

PIE *s(e)thre/o- 'to take'.

IE cognates: Olr. ad-roilli, do-sli 'to earn', MW dyrllid 'id.\ MCo, deleth 'to be appropriate', MBret dellit 'to earn' (< *tu-arifro-) < PCl. *-stiie/o- ( « *s(e)lhre/o-\ Gr εΐλον 'took* < *selh r , Go, satjan 'to sacrifice', Olc. selja 'to sell, hand over' < *solh₁-eie-. The oldest inscriptional evidence predates the change *-ol- > -ul-. The noun consilium < *kom-sel-io- was derived from consul· Since consulere does not look like a derivative of consul (we would rather expect consulare\ it appears that the verb was original and meant 'to get together, deliberate'. This might have been a full-grade present, or a zero-grade present or aorist. The noun consul can be a root noun of the productive type which was made to verbs (cf. -fexs). The alternative etymology *kom-se/od- cto sit together' is based on the dialectal change of intervocalic *rf to / in Latin. This proposal is semantically attractive but formally less so: it would require a verb *korn-se/od-e/o-y but as a present to this root, Latin only has sedeo. If the noun *kom-se/od- was original, the change of ά to / would be unlikely in view of the retained d in obses, -idis and others.

Bibl.: WH I: 264f., EM 138f., IEW 899, Leumann 1977: 293,393, Sihler 1995: 151, 304, Meiser 1998: 82, Schumacher 2004: 588-590, LIV *selh r .



### contamino, -are
contamino, -are 'to pollute, spoil' [v. I] (Ter.+)

Derivatives: incontaminatus 'uncorrupted* (Varro+).

Pit. *tag($)men 'contact'.

<!-- pdf-page: 146; printed-page: 132; running-header: 132                                     contio -->

PIE *th₂g-men-. The original meaning was 'to come into contact with*. In view of the other verbs in -mind and -minor, contamino is most likely based on a noun *tamen 'contact'. The fact that contaminatus serves as the antonym to integer has led to the etymological connection with *tag- already in Roman times. We may thus reconstruct *-tagmen or *tagsmen~; the latter form would be parallel to examen < *ex-agsmen, but note that it is impossible to prove the presence of *s in this formation; cf. ago. In theory, long a could stem from contagio, tactus, but this seems unlikely since no -g- is synchronically present in the verb. Verbs in co(m/n)- can be formed either from a prefixed noun (often with sociative meaning), or from an uncompounded noun, cow- giving the verb its telic meaning (cf. Haug 2004: 44). The meaning of contaminare points to the second alternative: *tagmen 'contact' > *con-tagmen-a- 'to make contact with'.

Bibl.: WH I: 266f.f EM 139f., IEW 1054f, Leumann 1977: 208, 371, LIV *teh₂(g)-. → tango



### contio
contio 'meeting, assembly' [f. ri\ (Pl.+; coventionid [abl.sg.] SCBac.) Pit» *ko(m)-wention-.

PIE *gʷm-ti- 'the coming'.

IE cognates: Skt. gati- [f.], Av. gati- 'motion', Gr. βάσις 'step', Go. ga-qumps 'meeting', OHG chumft 'coming, motion', OIc. samkund 'meeting'. The PIE ft-stem was remade into an οκ-stem in Latin, and prefixed with sociative *kom~. Lat. contio and curia (see s.v.) show the loss of the final nasal of *kom in front of *w-, which is probably the older development in contrast with the ClLat. sequence con-v-. Subsequently, contraction of *kowentr (with unsyncopated *e in a closed syllable) yielded -δ-.

Bibl.: WH I: 267, EM 140, IEW 464, Leumann 1977: 133, Schrijver 1991: 277, Sihler 1995: 57, LIV *gyem-. → venio



### contra
contra 'in front of, against' [prep. + ace, advj (Naev.-H)

Derivatives: contraries 'opposite' (Varro, Lucr.+).

Pit. *kom-tero- 'the other of two who meet' > 'opposite'.

It. cognates: O, contrud prep, + loc. 'against', abl.sg,m./n. *kom-terod.

PIE *kom- 'with'. Latin has contra- in the cps. controversia and controversies, see s.v. vertd. The existence of O. contrud suggests that the Latin forms are not merely recent innovations on the model of other adverbs in -tro, -tra, but continue a Pit. adj. *kom-tero-. Whereas contra continues the abl.sg.f. in *-ady contrd- represents the abl.sg.m.n. in *-ddy as does O. contrud. There is no evidence for a PIE formation in *-tero- to this preposition, hence we must interpret it as an Italic innovation.

Bibl.: WH I: 25If., EM 140, IEW 612f, Untermann 2000:417. → com, verto



### contumax,-acis
contumax,-acis 'proud, stubborn' [adj. k] (Cic.+)

Derivatives: percontumax 'very stubborn' (Ter.).

Although percontumax is attested significantly earlier than contumax, this does not guarantee that the latter was back-formed from the former, Conturnax may be derived from a verb ^con-turned 'to be proud' (also presupposed by contumelia, see below) to turned 'to be swollen',

### contumetia
contumetia 'insulting language or behaviour' [f. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: contumelidsus 'insulting, outrageous' (Ter,+),

Pit. *tum-e-. Probably a substantivization of an adj, *contumelis 'insulting', which suggests a derivational basis *contume- 'to insult1. This would formally be easiest to derive from the present tum-e- 'to be swollen', via 'boasting' > 'insulting'. Possibly, the prefix com- was added in analogy with contemnd 'to scorn, despise', which is semantically very close,

Bibl.: WH I: 267f, EM 140, Leumann 1977: 350, → conturnax, turned



### convexus
convexus 'curving outwards, arching' [adj, o/a] (Cic.+)

Derivatives: devexus 'sloping downward, inclined' (CIL 1.583+), subvexus 'sloping upwards from below' (Liv.).

Pit. *wexso-7 > I h :

PIE *ueg - 'to carry'. > Lat -vexus may contain *-veK- or *-vaK-. The connection, made by WH, with Skt. vancati 'staggers, sways', vakra- 'curved* is implausible, since these words are now derived from

PIE *uenk-, a root with a nasal which could not have disappeared in Latin, A connection with the word-family of vacare, vacuus seems difficult semantically, Nussbaum 2007b connects Oln fan 'a slope, a hollow' < *uagno-, OW guoin, W, gwaun [f.] 'lowland, meadow' < *uagna-. Judging by the meaning, -vexus would best be connected with vehd 'to convey', cf. English way from 'to move', Latin convexus might be 'moving along' (with outward movement), devexus 'moving down', I do not understand WH's blunt rejection of this etymology, which was proposed by Thumeysen 1907. He interprets convexus as 'moved together', but 'moving along'.seems preferable to me. The form -vexus could represent *-vefa-fo-, a ppp. to a verb *vektere (attested is vectare, but this is irrelevant), or the ppp, *vectus might have been changed to vexus by analogy with flexus, nexus. A third possibility is inherited *uegʰ-so-y derived from a PIE ^-present,

Bibl.: WH I: 268f„ EM 141, IEW 1134f, Leumann 1977: 617, LIV *uegʰ-, → veho, vexd



### convicium
convicium 'angry noise; mockery' [n. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: convlcian 'to scold' (Varro+), Etymology disputed. The older suggestion that it represents *ue1t- to vox 'voice' is untenable for morphological reasons, Leumann suggests a denominal origin to a noun * convict 'house-mates', but this is far-fetched. Semantically, I find a connection with vincid 'to tie' much more attractive: mockery and scolding can be described as

<!-- pdf-page: 148; printed-page: 134; running-header: 134                                     coquo -->


'encircling' someone. In that case, *kom-uoik-io-?

### coquō, -ere
coqu5, -ere 'to boil, bake' [v. Ill; pf. coxf, ppp. coctum] (PL-H)

Derivatives: coquus, cocus 'cook' (Naev.+); coquinare 'to cook' (Pl.); coquinus [adj.] 'of cooks' (Pl.), conquinaris 'belonging to a kitchen' (Varro); coculum 'cooking vessel' (Pl.+); coctilis 'baked1 (Varro+); praecox, -ocis/-oquis 'early ripening, premature' (Enn.+); concoquere 'to cook down, digest, ripen1 (Pl.+), decoquere 'to diminish by boiling, melt away1 (Cato+), excoquere 'to remove by cooking, melt down1 (Cato+), percoquere 'to cook or ripe thoroughly* (Pl.+); ρορϊηα 'eating-house, bistro1 (Pl.+); aulicoquisf-cocius 'cooked in a pot1 (inscr. Pratica di Mare, 3d c. BC, dat.abl.pL auliquoquibus, Paul, ex F. aulicocia extd)\ cultna 'kitchen1 (Pl.+).

Pit. *kʷekʷe/o- 'to cook1, *kʷokʷo- [m.] 'a cook\

PIE *pekʷ-e/o- 'to cook1.

IE cognates: MW pobi, MBret pibu poba, Co. pobas 'to bake, cook1 < PCI. *kʷokʷo- < *kʷekʷo-, MW poeth, MBret. poaz 'hot1 < *k"ok-to-; Skt. pacanti 'they cook1, YAv. pacaiti 'bakes1, Skt. pacyate 'ripens1; Gr. πέσσω, Att. πέττω < *pekʷ-ie-, Myc. a-to-po-qo, Gr. άρτο-κόπος 'bread-baker', Alb. pjek, Lift. kepti, OCSpesti, \s.pek<p 'to bake1, ToABpak- 'to cook, ripen1. The oldest forms are probably coquere (< *pekʷ-e/o-), the noun coquus (< *pokʷos), and maybe the ppp. coctus (< *pe/okʷto~). The mutation of *e > ο seems to be regular in front of ο in the next syllable, thus e.g. in coquo, from where it would have spread to 2ss. *quequis etc. But -o- may also stem from the noun coquus. The cp. praecox contains a root-noun *-pokʷ-s, probably with o-grade, since there would not have been many case-forms with a back vowel in the final syllable in order to cause vowel mutation. Alternatively, one might assume that *-kʷekʷ- yielded *-kʷokʷ- anyway. The length of the i in the verb coquinare is not unambiguously ascertained. Most likely, it is a short vowel, cf Leumann 1977: 551 and Steinbauer 1989: 165ff„ Steinbauer therefore posits a noun *coqud, -inis 'cook1 (not *-5, -onis\ from which the verb was derived as *kʷokʷe/on-a- 'to be a cook'. The noun popina must reflect *kʷokʷTna, and hence represent the Sabellic development of the same preform as Latin coquina 'the art of cookery1 (ApuL). Probably unrelated is Lat cocio 'dealer1 (Lab.+). The form cultna cannot be derived in a regular way from coquo. EM suggest that it was formed from *kʷokʷina under the influence of cuius 'arse1, "les latrines etant le plus souvent attenantes a la cuisine.11 This explanation is not credible,

Bibl.: WH I: 270£, 303, II: 338, EM 141, 155, 521, IEW 798, Leumann 1977: 551, Steinbauer 1989: 165ff., Schrijver 1991: 467, Sihier 1995: 41, 145, Meiser 1998: 82, Lindner 2002: 222, Schumacher 2004: 429f., LIV l.*peky-.



### eor, cordis
eor, cordis 'heart1 [n, d] (Andr.+; long scansion Icorl < Icorrl in PL is uncertain)

Derivatives: corculum 'little heart, sweetheart' (Pl.+), cordatus 'sensible, judicious' (Pl.+); cordolium 'heartfelt grief (Pl.+); concors, -dis 'agreeing, harmonious7 (Naev.+), concordia 'harmony, agreement' (Pl.+), concorditas 'id/ (Pac), concordare 'to be on good terms, agree' (Ter.+); discors, -dis 'disagreeing1 (Lucr.+), discordia 'discord1 (Pl.+), discordabilis 'disagreeing1 (Pl.), discorditas

<!-- pdf-page: 149; printed-page: 135; running-header: corbis                                    135 -->


'disagreement* (Pac.), discordare 'to disagree1 (Pl.+); excors 'senseless' (Pl.+), misericors 'tender-hearted' (Pl.+), misericordia 'compassion' (Pl.+), socors * inert, inactive' (Pl.+), socordia 'lack of energy' (Pl.+), vecors 'mad, demented' (Andr.+), vecordia 'frenzy' (Ter.+); praecordia, -drum Mower part of the chest, body-parts in that area' (Pl.+); recordan 'to call to mind' (Pl.+).

Pit. *kord, *kordo/es. PEE nom.sg. *kerd, gen.sg- *krd-o/es 'heart'.

IE cognates: Olr. cride 'heart', W. craidd (< PCI *kred-jo-); Hit. oms>ker I kard(i)- [n.] 'heart, center', Pal. /cart-, CLuw. zart-, HLuw. zart(i)- < *ker, gen. *krdios, Skt. hfd- 'heart', nom.sg. hardU Gr, κήρ, καρδία (Att.), κραδίη (Η.), Arm. sirt, Lith, sirdL·, Go. hairto, OHG herza, OIc. hjarta 'heart', ToA hi 'will\ ToB pi karyan 'hearts'. The Latin paradigm of cor was built on the oblique case forms in *krdr of the PIE noun. The dim. corculum represents *kord-keb-, which means that the athematic stem was used as a derivational basis. Leumann 1977: 295 assumes that cordolium goes back to *kordi-doliom> but Kummel 2004b: 348 objects that compounds in cordi- are generally more recent, and that cordolium shows that we may just as well posit *kordrdolium. For so-cors < *swe-kordr or *sed-kordr, see s.v. se-.

Bibl.: WH I: 271f., EM 142, IEW 579f> Leumann 1977: 217, 221, Schrijver 1991: 484. → credo > \ cSram 'face to face' [adv,] (Pl.+);^lso prep, 'in the presence of (Cic.+) A compound' of co- 'with₁ + as; oris 'mouth, face'. WH explain coram from an adj. *co-os-o- 'in front of the face', which adopted the ending of its antonym clam 'secretly1 and its synonym palam 'overt'. Hence, we might posit *ko-os-om » *ko-os-am > coram.

Bibl.: WH I: 272, EM 142, IEW 612f., Leumann 1977: 119. → os



### corbis
corbis 'basket5 [f, m. i] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: corbita 'ship-load; cargo ship' (Pl.+), corbula '(small) basket* (Pl.+).

Pit. *k(o)rfi- 'basket'.

PIE *k(o)rbh-i- Vbasket, wickerwork'?

IE cognates: Lith. karbas 'basket', Latv. karba 'bag made from alder or birch baric', Ru. korob 'box, basket', Sin. kraba 'box' <PSL *#or£b, *korba. The Balto-Slavic words might go back to a similar preform as corbis; in that case, the circumflex accentuation of Lith. and Slavic would point to aspirated *bʰ. The root structure *k-bʰ was irregular in PIE, and would point either to a loanword, or to an original root *skrbʰ-. But many scholars argue that the BSl. words have been borrowed from Germanic (cf. NHG Korb), which itself has borrowed the word from Latin. Thus, the whole question is undecided. Words for * basket' are frequently loanwords. Very questionable is the comparison with Gr. κάρφος [n.] 'dry stalk, dry twigs' and its Greek cognates.

Bibl.: WH I: 272, EM 142, IEW 948f.

<!-- pdf-page: 150; printed-page: 136; running-header: 136                                    cordus -->



### cordus
cordus 'produced late in the season (crops, hay, lambs)' [adj. o/a] (CatcH-) If inherited, cordus must go back to *k(o)rd-o-. WH compare OE scort, OHG scurz 'short' < *skr-d- if from the root *($)ker- 'to fcut\ The semantic motivation would be 'which stayed behind in growth, withered'. Yet there is no indication in the texts that cordus has a negative connotation, it merely concerns products with late ripening, or (with hay) from a second harvest. Etymology unknown.

Bibl.: WH I:273,EM142£



### corium
corium 'skin, animal hide' [n. o] (Pl>; rarely -us 2x PL, Varro)

Pit. *korjo-.

PIE *k(o)r-io- 'torn off.

IE cognates: Skt. carman-, Av. carBman- 'hide, skin', OCS kora, Cz. kura, SCr. kora 'bark, crust' <

PIE *(s)kor-li2-. Probably a derivative of

PIE *kor-o- 'which is torn off, peeled ofF > 'bark, skin'.

### cornīx, -īcis
cornlx, -Ids 'crow' [f. k] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: Cornlscae [fpL] 'local deities' (Paul ex F,).

Pit. *kornik-y *kornak- 'crow'.

It. cognates: U. curnaco [acc.sg.], curnase [abl.sg.] 'crow' < *kornako-.

PIE *kor-n- 'crow, raven'.

IE cognates: Gr. κορώνη 'crow; shearwater', κόραξ, -ακος 'raven', OPr. sarke, Lith. sarka 'magpie', CS svraka, Ru. soroka < PS1, *s(v)orka 'magpie' < *korH-k-, The suffix -ik- occurs in £ agent nouns, and in f. individuals, as here in 'crow'. The alternation between Lat -Ik- and U. -ok- points to a recent date of these formations (although U. may have suffix substitution for *-ifr-). Maybe the vowel -a- is preserved in Ital. cornacchia < cornacula. All IE languages show words in *Ar- denoting 'crow', 'raven', 'magpie5 or similar birds, which clearly are onomatopoeic. The suffixes usually are productive, as here in the case of comix.

Bibl.: WH I: 275, EM 143, IEW 567, Leumann 1977: 377. → corvus, crdcid



### cornil, -us
cornil, -us 'horn' [n. u] (Pl.+; cornus Varro, cornum Ter.+)

Derivatives: corniitus 'horned' (Acc.+); cornicen 'trumpeter' (Varro+)> cornifrons 'with homs on the forehead' (Pac), corniger 'having horns' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *kornu-, *korno- 'horn'.

PIE *krn-o- » *lcm-u- 'horn'.

IE cognates: Galat. κάρνον 'trumpet', Gaul, καρνυξ 'trumpet', Gallo-Lat carnuatus 'horned', W. earn 'hoof < *ATW7U-, W. asgwrn^ Bret. Qskorn 'bone' < *ast-kor-n- 'bone-horn'; Skt. sffiga- 'horn' < *krn-^w)-; Lith. stirna 'roe', Latv. pi. sirnas, RuCS sn>na *id/ < krH₂-nh₂S Go. haurn 'horn', OHG Olc. horn < PGm- */urna-; OHG (h)rindy OE hrider [n.] 'homed animal' < *ar-en-fo-, OE hryder> Dutch rund 'cow' < *kr-n-to. The quantity of the vowel in cornu, and in other n. «-stems such as genii, veni, is uncertain. See Klingenschmitt 1992 for a detailed account of the (impossibilities to

<!-- pdf-page: 151; printed-page: 137; running-header: corpus                                      137 -->

use the metre as indication for the vowel length in coma. If the original form was corrru, word-final -u instead of *-e might be due to restoration of -u on the basis of the acc.sg. If u is original, comu may reflect the nom.acc.dual ending *-u-hi\ especially for cornii *'pair of horns' > 'horn' and genu *'pair of knees' > 'knee', this would be attractive. Others (e.g. Klingenschmitt 1992: 125) posit an original mpl. in *-u-h₂. Klingenschmitt mentions as an alternative explanation metrical lengthening in sequences short-short-short (e.g. in *genu, if followed by a following short syllable). Obviously, this would not work for cornii. Many languages show an «-extension of the root *kr- 'horn', but only Celtic and Gm. seem to share *Ar-H-o-, whereas no other language has a α-stem. It thus appears most likely that Latin has switched from o-stem to w-stem at an earlier stage, maybe on the example of pecuy or of a derivative *kr-n-(o)u-o-, which may be regarded as the source of W. Cernyw, Co. Kernow, Bret, Kemev < *kor-ou-Q)i, cf. Schrijver 1995: 54, There may also have been influence from the noun *ker-u-o- 'horned animal' > Latin cervus, see s.v. The root *Ar- is usually regarded as the derivational basis for *krh₂- 'head, skuir, from which Latin cerebrum is derived. This presupposes that *kr- indicates the material ('bone, horn'), with *ker-hr indicating a collective 'head' or 'made of bone' (or 'top'?), and *kr-n- an individual 'horn'.

Bibl.: WH I: 276, EM 143, IEW 576, Leumann 1977: 441f, Nussbaum 1986, Beekes 1989: 55ff., Schrijver 1991: 83, Klingenschmitt 1992: 12Iff,, Schrijver 1995: 54, Sihler 1995: 67, 323, Meiser 1998: 146, → cerebrum, cernuus, cervix, cervus; comix t



### cornus
cornus 'cornel cherry-tree' [f. o] (Varro+; variants cornus, -us [f.], cornum Varro+)

Derivatives: corneus Of cornel-trees' (Cato+), cornetum 'plantation of cornel-cherries' (Varro).

Pit. *korno- 'cornel cherry, cherry'.

IE cognates: Gr. κράνον 'cornel cherry tree', κράνεια 'id.', Lith Kimis 'divine protector of the cherry'; Gr, κέρασος 'bird-cherry'. The Lithuanian form Kimis might also derive from ^fimo-, but the comparison is rather isolated. The Latin fc-stem may be secondary after quercus, -us *oak\ It is likely that the Greek-Latin correspondence between comus and κράνον represents a loanword from a third language, Gr. κέρασος may be related, and would then point more strongly to a loanword, probably from an eastern language, from where the cherry was introduced into Europe,

Bibl.: WH I: 276f., EM 143f., IEW 572f



### corpus, -oris
corpus, -oris 'body, substance' [a r] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: corpuscuium 'small body, small object' (Pl.+); corporeus 'corporeal, material' (Varro, Lucr,+), corporare 'to kilF (Enn.); corpulentus 'corpulent' (Pl.+); bicorpor, -is 'double-bodied' (Naev.+).

Pit. *korp-os-,

PIE *krp-os-, *kip- 'body'.

IE cognates: Mir. cri 'body' < *Ar/>-?; Skt krp-ά {RV; ins.sg.f ] 'appearance', OAv. kdfnpam [acc.sg.], YAv. kahrp- [f] 'figure, appearance,

<!-- pdf-page: 152; printed-page: 138; running-header: 138                                     corngia -->


body'; OE hrif*be\ly, womb', OHG href'lap', OFr. r # re/'belly, abdomen' < *xref-o/es-. Italo-Celtic and Germanic point to a PIE (palato)velar, and to an ablaut *-/-- vs. *-re-. Since palatovelars were depalatalized in* Ilr. in front of
