Driessen (p.c.) suggests a different etymology: -crepitus might be from a root *kr(e)p- 'strong5 found also in Celtic, Slavic and Germanic.

Bibl.: WH I: 332, EM 167-

<!-- pdf-page: 179; printed-page: 165; running-header: delicus                                      165 -->

dSfrutum 'grape juice boiled down into a syrup* [n. o] (Pl.+; once defritum Nonius 551.7 15)

Derivatives: defrutare 'to boil down into a syrup' (Cato+).

Pit. *fruto~.

PIE *bʰru-to- 'boiled*.

IE cognates: W. brwds f. brod, Bret, brout 'warm, fervent5 < *bʰruto-, Olr. bruth [n.] < *-/w-, bntithe 'boiled, broth' < *-//o-; OHG prod, OE brod, OIc brod [n.] 'broth* < *bʰruto-. Lat./is the reflex of *όΛ- in anlaut, hence the compound of de and *fruto- is not very old. In defritum we find regular vowel reduction in medial syllable. Lat -frutum reflects the ppp. of the same root 'to boil' from which ferveo derives. In contrast to earlier treatments, Schrijver 1991: 252-256 separates IE reflexes of a root *bVe)hiU-/*bʰruhr 'to whirl, seethe' from a root *bʰru- 'to boil', even if these may be cognate at a deeper level (p. 256). After all, the meanings are quite similar.

Bibl.: WH I: 333f., EM 167, IEW 144f., Schrijver 1991: 254f., LIV * b W . ^ fermentum, ferveo

deleo cto remove, efface* [v, II; pf. delevi, ppp. deletum] (Cato+)

Derivatives: deletilis [adj.] 'that erases' (Varro), deletid 'destruction' (Lucil).

Pit. *oI-eje/o-.

PIE *h₃eIhreie/o- 'to destroy'.

IE cognates: Hit. hallarma/i- 'to trample down, to flatten (fields and plants)', Gri ρλλυμι, -μαι 'to wreck; destroy, lose', intr. cto go to waste, be ruined, be lost'. * ; WH and IEW assume that delere is a back-formation to pf. de-levi to *delinere cto wipe off, because of the long vowel e in the pf. The model would have been the antonym implere - implevu In the oldest attestations, the main meaning is 'to wipe out' rather than 'to destroy'. Yet in view of the persistance of lino, leviy the alleged disappearance of *delino would be conspicuous (a new delino exists since Varro). Schrijver hesitatingly suggests that deled may stem from *-h₃lehrti or *-h₃lehrie-ti. Meiser 2003 simply assumes that deled derives from a causative *de-oleo; according to him, the pf. and ppp, have been influenced byfleo and com-pleo. If Hit hallanna- is indeed cognate, it might continue the original PIE meaning, which shifted to 'destroy' elsewhere (Kloekhorst2008: 27If).

Bibl.: WH I: 335f, EM 167f., IEW 662ff, Schrijver 1991: 403, Meiser 1998: 190, 2003: 139, Seldeslachts 2001: 84f., LTV *h₃elh r . → aboleo, lino, ulciscor



### delicus
delicus 'weaned' [adj. o/a] (Varro, Rust. 2.4.16: cum porci depulsi sunt a mamma, a guibusdam deli<c>i appellantur neque iam lactantes dicuntur [mss.: delitU delicti^ Cato, Agr. 2.7: boves vetulos, armenta delicula, oves deliculas, lanamy pellem, ... vendat.)

Derivatives: delicuus 'lacking, missing' (Pl.+), deliculus 'having a small defect* (Cato). Several explanations have been put forward, but none is obviously correct. WH and IEW opt for a connection with lac 'milk*, hence *de-lak-o- 'who has been taken away from the milk\ A connection with lacio 'to entice' is semantically less

166 -dem


straightforward, whereas a connection with delinquo 'to be lacking, fair (Pl.+) does not explain the form deliculus, according to WH.

### -dem [ptele.]: ibidem
-dem [ptele.]: ibidem 'in the same place' (Andr.+), idem 'the same' (Andn+), identidem 'repeatedly' (Pl.+), itidem 'in the same way' (Pl.+), quidem 'certainly, surely' (Pl.+), pndem 'previously' (Pl.+), tandem 'really; at last' (Pl.+), tantusdem 'just as much' (Pl.+), totidem 'just as many' (Pl.+)

Pit. *-WJ.

It. cognates: see s.v. idem for the Sabellic cognates with *-o/w.

PIE *im 'this'. Originally a suffix -em indicating emphasis or focus, as in aut-em, quid-em. This was metanalysed from the n, idem 'the same' (analysed as id-dem), or, according to Sihier, from the abl.sg. eddem, eadem < *eod-em, *ead*-emy which came to stand beside simple eo% ea < *eod9 *ead. But I see no great problems in assuming a metanalysis of *idem as Hd-demL Latin suggests a particle *im, which was the PIE acc.sg. of anaphoric *h₁e 'he, that one' (Beekes 1995: 203), and is found as im, em in Old Latin (see s.v. is, ea^ id). Sabellic has a suffix *-om attached to the (reduplicated) pronoun (O. isidum, esidiim etc.), and one would naturally prefer a common Italic ancestor; but this appears to be impossible. If PauL ex F. emem is reliable, and O. idik, idik, idic> LL efek reflect *id-id-ke9 it is possible that Latin changed *imim [acc.sg.m.], *idid [n.] to *imim, *idim, with *-//κ being reanalysed as a fixed suffix.

Bibl.: WH I: 326, 671, II: 406, EM 168, IEW 181-183, Sihier 1995: 392. → aut; -dam-de-do-dum, -de; idem; /to; -nem, quisy quid; tarn



### dens, -Us
dens, -Us 'tooth* [t /] (Lex XII+; abl.sg. dente, gen.pl. dentium, Varro dentum)

Derivatives: dentatus 'with teeth' (Pl.+); edentate 'to knock the teeth out of (Pl.+), edentulus 'toothless' (Pl.); dentifrangibulus 'that breaks teeth' (Pl.), dentilegus 'one who collects teeth' (Pl.); bidens, -ntis 'with two teeth or points' (Acc.+), bidens^ -ntis [mVf.] 'sacrificial animal, esp. sheep' (Lab.+).

Pit. *dent-. It- cognates: O. dunte[ (Capua 37) is regarded by some scholars as the word for 'tooth', but the actual meaning is uncertain.

PIE *h₃d-nt- 'tooth'.

IE cognates: Olr. det 'tooth', W. dant < *h₃dnt-; Skt. dant- (nom.sg. dan9 acc.sg. dantam, gen.sg. datas) , Av. daitika- [m.] 'wild, undomesticated animal', YAv. vimlto.dantan- [adj.] 'with shapeless teeth', MoP dandan 'tooth'; Gr. Ionic οδών, later οδούς 'tooth', νωδος 'toothless' < *n-h₃d-o-; Aeol.Gr. έδοντες 'teeth' (only in a 12Λ-α AD gloss which states that "the Aeolians say edontas for odontas., and edunas for odunas") , Arm. atamn 'tooth'; OPr. dantis, Lith. dantls 'tooth', gen.pL dantq, Ru. desna 'gum\ Go. tunpus, OHG zand. From the same root: Lith. uodas, Latv. uods 'gnat' < *odas < *odos < *h₃e/odos, Gn όδυνή 'pain'. The initial laryngeal of the PIE preform can only be decided on the basis of Armenian and Greek. In Greek, Aeolian ed- is found versus od- in Ionic-Attic. Sihier aptly summarizes the possibilities: either *ed- is archaic, and odont- was assimilated from *edont-\ or od- is archaic, and *odont- was changed analogically to edont- in Aeolic

<!-- pdf-page: 181; printed-page: 167; running-header: deus                                       167 -->


under the influence of εδω 'to eat\ In Armenian, at- can reflect *h₂d- or *h₃d-, which confirms that Gr. odont- is original

Bibl.: WH I: 340, EM 169, IEW 289, Beekes 1969: 55, Leumann 1977: 343, Schrijver 1991: 23, 416, Sihler 1995: 85, 89, Beekes 1995: 179, Meiser 1998: 99, Untermann 2000: 189f.



### densus
densus 'dense, thick, closely packed' [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: densare 'to thicken, condense' (Enn.+), densere 'id/ (Lucr.+); condensus 'dense, tightly packed' (Lucr.+)-

Pit. *d(e)nso-?

PIE *d(e)ns-o- 'thick'.

IE cognates: Gr. δασύς 'hairy, thick with leaves; aspirated'. Kloekhorst 2008: 853ff. reconstructs *dens-u- for Hit. dassu- 'strong' and derives it from the root *dens- 'to be skilled'. This is semantically somewhat removed from the Latin and Greek adj., so that the connection is uncertain. The factitive densed is regarded as a nonce-form instead οϊ densare by Sihler 1995: 531. If connected with the Greek adj., Latin has a different stem formation, *d(e)ns-o- or *dns-uo- (since from *d(e)ns-u- one would expect *densuis). The connection with δασύς can only be upheld if

PIE *s was indeed retained after *p in Greek, which is disputed

Bibl.: WH I: 341f., EM 169f, IEW 202f ■ • > deus 'god, deity' [m. o] (VOLat+: nom.sg. deiuos (Duenos inscr.), deiva (Pisaurum, 3d c); CLat deusy noni.pl. di,'dei, dit, gen.pl. dedrum/deum, databl.pl. dis, dels, dils, dibus)

Derivatives: dea 'goddess' (Pl.+); divus [m./adj.] 'a god; godlike' (CIL, Andr.+), dins [adj.] 'divine' (Enn.+); divinus 'id/ (inscr., Pl.+) (variants: deinus, dinus CIL, Pl.), divinitus [adv.] 'by divine inspiration* (Pl.+).

Pit. *deiwo~.

It. cognates: Ven. deivos [acc.pl.] 'god'; O. deivai [datsg.f.], deivas [gen.sg.f.?], Vol deue [datsg.m. or f.] 'goddess' or 'godlike'; O. deiuatud [3s.ipv.II], deiuaid [3s,pr.sb.], deiuast [3s.fut], deiuatu<n>s [ppp., nom.pl.m,] 'to swear', denom, verb to <*cfe/vw-. Probably O. deivinais [databLpl.L] 'of the Deiva' < *deiwo- + -ΪΠΟ-.

PIE *dei-u-o- 'god, divinity (god of the clear sky)'.

IE cognates: Olr. dia 'god', OW duiutit 'divinity'; Skt. deva-, Av. daeuua- [m.] 'god*, OPr deywis, Lith. dievas, Latv. dievs 'god'; OIc. Tyr 'name of a wargod', pi. tivar 'gods', OE 7Tw, OHG Ζϊο 'wargod\

Pit. *deiw- monophthongized to *dew-, at which stage a split occurred: *w was lost before back vowels, but not before front vowels. Hence, e was retained in front of u < *vra, yielding *dewos > *deus > deus, *dewom > deum, and similarly dat.sg. ded; but the long vowel was raised to ϊ where *w had remained: gen.sg., nom.pl. *dewi > *drwi, dat.abl.pl. *drwis. Eventually, the two different reflexes were semantically differentiated: deus, del 'god', but divus, drvJ 'godly, heavenly'. The latter stem changed from noun to adj., probably in appositional position (e.g. et Manibus dfvfs inferias mittunt Lucr. 3.5.2). A subsequent sound-law (thus Nussbaum 1999b) brough

<!-- pdf-page: 182; printed-page: 168; running-header: 168                                     dexter -->

about the loss of *w between two identical vowels (often, this law is restricted to certain positions of the stress). This yielded e.g. nom.pl. *drwi > dif, dat.abl.pl. d&, which formed the basis for the creation of a new adj. dius, with basically the same meaning as divus. Note that, under this analysis, the adj. dius 'divine' is not the same word as dins 'daylit' (see s.v. diu),

PIE *deiuo- is a derivation from

PIE *<A"-w- 'God of the sky, divine sky', with introduction of e-grade in the root, and o-suffixation.

Bibl.: WH I: 345f., EM 170f., IEW 185f., Lejeune 1974: 332, Meiser 1998: 86, Untermann 2000: 161-163. → dies, diu, Iupiter



### dexter, -(e) ra, -(e)rum
dexter, -(e) ra, -(e)rum 'right (opposite of left)' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+; comp. dexterior, sup. dextimus;)

Derivatives: dext(e)ra [f.] 'right hand' (PI), dext(e)m 'on the right-hand side' (Pl.+), dextrorsum, dextrduorsum (Pl.+) [adv.] 'toward theright-handside\

Pit. *deksiero-; *deks(i)wo-.

It. cognates: U. destrame [acc.sg.f. + -en] , testru [abl.sg.m/n.], testruku, destruco [abl.sg.m. + -com] , testre e, desire [loc.sg.m. + -en] , O. destrst [nom.sg.f. + ist] 'right5; U. desua, dersua, tesvam [acc.sg.f-], dersua [abLsg.f.] 'right' [adj.] (o/a) < *deksuo- or *deksiuo-.

PIE *de]*s-tero- 'right, right-hand side', *delcs-(i)uo- 'what is proper, right'.

IE cognates: Olr. dess 'right, south' (< *deks-uo-\ MW deheu, W. deau, MCo. dehow, Bret, dehou 'right, south' < LPBr. *dexou < *deksouia 'right (side)' « *deks(i)uo- 'right'; Skt. daksina-, Gr. δεξιτερός, δεξιός '(to the) right (side)', Myc. PN de-ki-si-wo fdeksiwos/, Gr. δεξιρος, δεξιότης 'dexterity, cleverness' (Pamphyl.), Alb. djathte 'right', Lith. desinas, OCS desnt, Go. taihswa, OUGzeso 'id.\ On the basis of syncopated forms such as dextrum (in which *-ter- > -fr-), Beekes 1994: 88 assumes that the preform had no */, hence was *dehteros in Latin; this is in agreement with the Sabellic forms, cf. Meiser 1986: 41, 169. The stem *deks(i)wo- found in Umbrian can be connected with PCI. *deks-wo- and with Germanic *texsvw(n)-. For PIE, Beekes assumes doublets *deks and *defa-i 'right(-hand side)', from which tero-derivatives could be derived. Stuber (2002: 77) suggests that *deks might contain the s-stem noun *dekos 'what is fitting, proper' (see s.v. decet). In Stuber 2006, she proposes to regard *deks-iyo- as analogical to the word for 'left5 < *skeh₂iuo- and *leh₂iuo- (see s.v. scaevus, laevus).

PIE *deksitero- would be a more recent derivative in *-tero- on the basis of *deksiuo-.

Bibl.: WH I: 331, EM 171, IEW 189-191, Beekes 1994, Schrijver 1995: 330f„ Untermann 2000: 169-171.



### Diana
Diana 'goddess' [f. a] (Naev.+; insc. also Deana, Deuiana. In older scansion Diana, with long 1) Probably derived from dius 'godly'. Explained from *diwja-na by Solmsen, as 'the one who belongs to the moon goddess *Divia (the Shiner)'. Leumann 1977 explains the name as a derivation from sub diu 'in the open air'.

Bibl.: WH I: 347, EM 172, IEW 183ff., Leumann 1977: 106, 325. → deus, dies, diu

### dīcō, -ere
dicO, -ere 'to talk, speak; declare' [v* III; pf. dixT, ppp, dictum; gdve. also dicundus (Pl.+)] (Naev.+; inscr. deicere SCBac, deixsistis CIL 586)

Derivatives: (1) indicens 'without (my) speaking' (Ter.+), indictus 'not said' (Cato+); addicere 'to assign, award' (Lex XII+), addictus 'enslaved person', condicere 'to engage oneself in, fix by contract' (Pl.+), conductor 'one who fixes' (Pl.), edicere 'to proclaim' (Naev.+), edictio 'decree' (Pl.), edictare 'to declare' (Pl.), indicere 'to proclaim' (Pl.+), indictfvus 'publicly proclaimed' (Varro+), interdicere 'to forbid' (Pl.+), praedicere 'to prescribe; say beforehand' (Naev.+), praecd 'crier, announcer' (Andr.+), praeconium 'declaration, auctioneer's function' (Pl.+), prodicere 'to give notice' (CIL 583, Varro+); benedicere 'to speak well of (Pl.+), maledicere 'to insult' (Pl.+), maledicax 'slanderous' (Pl.); (2) dicare 'to assign, dedicate; indicate' (Pl.+), abdicare 'to deny, renounce' (Pac.+), dedicare 'to declare, dedicate' (Cato+), indicare 'to make known, reveal' (Pl.+), indicatio 'valuation' (Pl.+), praedicare 'to make known' (Pl.+), praedicatio 'special mention, statement' (Pl.+); (3) dicax 'having a ready tongue' (Pl.+), dicaculus 'talkative' (Pl.+); (4) dicio 'dominion, power' (Pl.+), conditio 'contract, term' (Pl.+); (5) dicis causa 'for the sake of appearance' (Varro+); (6) -dicus 'one who says': benedice 'with friendly words' (Pl.), causidicus 'advocate' (Lucr.+), maledicus 'evil-speaking' (Pl.+); (7) -dex, -dicis One who indicates/declares': index (Acc.+) 'revealing, a sign9, indicium 'disclosure, sign' (Pl.+), iudex 'judge' (Lex XII, ¥\.+)„iiidicium 'legal process, trial; decision' (Naev.+), iudicare 'tojudge, try' (Pl.+), iiidicaium 'judgement debt' (Lex XII+), iudicatio 'juridical power' (CIL 1.583+), vindex 'surety, defender' (Lex XII, Pl.+), vindiciae [f pL] 'interim possession' (Lex XIl/Cato+), vindicta 'the claiming of liberty' (Pl.+), vindicare 'to lay claim to' (Lex XII, Pl.+); (8) dictator 'dictator' (Naev.+), dictatrix 'f dictator' (Pl.); dictio 'speaking, utterance' (Ter.+); dictitare 'to repeat' (Pl.+); dictus, -us 'the saying' (Ter.+); dictare 'to indicate, dictate' (Cic.+).

Pit. pr. *deik-e/o- 'to say', aor. *deik-s-; pf *de-dik-[us-J, ppp. *dik~to~; root noun *-dik-s 'saying'. The meanings of Lat. judex and PSab. *med(es)-dik- are so similar that they suggest a common origin or mutual influence.

It. cognates: (1)0, deikum, deicum [inf pr.], deicans [3p,pr.sb.], U, teitu, deitu [3s.ipv.II] < pr. *deik-e/o-; O. dicust, U. dersicust [3s.fiit.pf.], dersicurent [3p.fiit.pf ] 'to say' < pf. *de-dik-us~; (2) 0. dadikatted^[3s*pf] is probably a caique on dedicare. Very uncertain is U. tikamne [dat.sg.], a theonym, which has been explained as *dik-a-men-(o-)\ (7) O. meddiss, meddis, meddis [nom.sg.], μεδεκον [acc.sg.], medikeis [geasg.], medikei [dat.sg.], medikid [abl.sg.], medd[i]ks, μεδδειξ [nom.pl.], Marr. medix, Mars, medis, meddiss [nom.sg.], PaeL medix, Vol. medix [nom.pl] 'judge' (vel sim.) < PSab. *medrdik- or *medes-dik- 'who speaks law', Ο. μεδεκαν [acc.sg.] to f *med-de/ika- 'female judge', O. meddikkiai, medikkiai, μεδικιαι [locsg.] < *medrdik-ja- 'the office of a meddix', 0. medicim [acc.sg.] , meddixud [abl.sg.], μεδδικεν [loc.sg. + -en] < *medrdik-jo- 'place of the tribunal, office of the magistrate', O. medicatud [ppp., abl.sg.m.] 'judged' < *medrdik-a-to-, O. medicatinom [acc.sg.] 'judgement'. This points to a PSab. verb *medrdika-.

PIE pr. *deik-e/o- 'to show', aor. *deik-s-, root noun *-dik-s.

IE cognates: Skt. dis- 'to show': disant- [ptc.act.], disamana- [ptc.med.]; aides- [pr.]; dista- 'shown

<!-- pdf-page: 184; printed-page: 170; running-header: 170                                        dies -->

(direction)'; dis- [f.] 'direction, point of the compass'; OAv. doedoist [3s.inj.act.], aor. dais [2s.inj.act.], ddisa [ls.sb,act.], doisi [2s.ipv.act] 'to show'; Gr. δείκνϋμι 'to show'; δίκη 'manner, custom'; Go. ga-teihan 'to indicate, proclaim', OHG zihan 'to show; incriminate'. The relative chronology shows that dictator and dictatrix (8) are independent of the verb dictare, which was formed later. The verb dicare may well have been backformed from compounds in -dicare. This verb in its turn may derive from the root noun -die-. In (7), nom.sg. -dex is analogical for *-<#*, probably on the model of -spex (to -spicio) and -fex (to -ficio). Lat index < *ious-dik-, vindex from vindicit (Lex XII) < *vim dicit, and index analogical to indicare. Praeco shows syncope from *praidiko > *praedk5 >praeco.

### dies, diaf
dies, diaf 'day, daytime' [m. (£) e] (Lex XII, Andr.+)

Derivatives: diecula 'brief day' (Pl.+); hodie 'today' (Naev,+), merldie 'midday' (Pl.+), cottldie 'daily' (Pl.+), perendie 'on the day after tomorrow' (Pl.+), postndie 'on the following day' (Pl.+), pridie 'the day before' (Pl.+); dialis 'of Jupiter' in flamenDialis (Varro, C i O ) and in novendialis 'Masting nine days' (Cic.+).

Pit. *die-.

It. cognates: O. iuklei [loc.sg.] 'day' < *dio-kelo- « *diou-kelo-; O. zicolom [acc.sg.], ziculud, [abl.sg.], zicelfei] [loc.sg.], zicolom [gen.pL] 'day' < *die-kelo- 'little day'. Disputed: U. ύςέΐ [nom.sg.], titfu [abl.sg.] 'day'(?).

PIE *di-eu- 'God of the clear sky, Clear Sky'.

IE cognates: see s.v. lupiter. Lat. dies is based on the disyllabic Pit. acc.sg. *dijem (Lat. diem) <

PIE *di(i)eu-m '(god of the) sky', with di- analogically from gen.sg *diwos. A new stem *dije- was created on the basis of this acc.sg., and became one of the sources for the fifth declension: the endings were largely adapted to those of the first declension (α-stems). Oscan *dje-kelo- shows the monosyllabic variant of the stem. The semantic shift to 'day' is based on the period in which the sky is clear, i.e. the day. The original meaning together with the form die- has been preserved in Diespiter, cf. lupiter. Oscan iuklei « *djou-kelo~ may have been built on the Italic stem *djou- from loc.sg, *djeu 'in the sky', see s.v. diu.

Bibl.: WH I: 350, EM 174f., IEW 184£, Sihler 1995: 339, Untermann 2000: 352, 754, 868f. → lupiter, peren[die



### digitus
digitus 'finger, toe' [m. o] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: digitabulum 'finger-stall' (Varro), digitulus 'finger, toe' (Pl.+). The meaning makes a connection with died very attractive, which is why many scholars prefer to explain the g of digitus from assimilation to the preceding d (thus Meiser), or dissimilation from the following voiceless t (Sommer 1914: 213, WH, IEW). Yet distant voicing assimilation or dissimilation is otherwise unknown in Latin (Leumann 1977: 232 does not have a single other example), and the semantic connection of a hypothetical form *dicitus with died and died would have remained

<!-- pdf-page: 185; printed-page: 171; running-header: dis-                                       171 -->

clear throughout the prehistory of Latin. Unfortunately, no PIE root of the form *d(e)ig- is with certainty attested. IEW reconstructs *doig- for Go. taikns 'sign', but k might be due to Kluge's Law in Germanic.

Bibl.: WH I: 351, EM 175, IEW 188f, Leumann 1977: 232, Meiser 1998: 126.



### -dinus
-dinus 'day' [adj. o/a\: dies perendinus 'the day after tomorrow' (Pl.+), rnindinoe [f.pl.] 'a maricet-day, occurring every ninth day' (Lex XII, Cic.+), nundinalis 'of market-days' (Pl.+), nundinum [n.] 'the period from one market-day to the next' (Cic.+) noundinum [gen.pl.] (SCBac)

Pit. *perno-dino~ 'of the foremost day' > 'of the day after', *noweno-dino- Of the ninth day\

PIE *di-n- 'day' ( » *-dino- 'of a day').

IE cognates: Olr. dertus 'period of time', tredenw 'period of three days'; Skt madhyamdina- [m.] 'midday, noon', su-dina- [n.] 'dawning beautifully, light of the day'; OPr. deinan [acc.sg.], Lith. diena, Latv. diena, OCS dbnb 'day' [m.], dbne [gen.sg.j, Ru. den' [m.], gen.sg. dnja < BSl. *d(e)in-; Go. sinteins 'always, daily' < *sem-deino-* These are petrified occurrences of the adj, *di-no- 'of a day'. The BSl. forms show that this was probably a PIE Η-stem, whereas the widespread o-stem must have arisen in compounds.

Bibl.: WH II: 188, 287, EM 175, 447, 498, IEW 183-187, Leumann 1977: 134, Schrijver 1991: 275, —^ dies, diu\ heri, peren[die t dTnis 'awful, dreadful' [adj. o/d] (Cic.+) .

Derivatives: dime [f.pl.] 'bad omens' (Cic.+), DTrae 'the Furies' (Verg.+).

PIE *duei-ro- 'fearful' / *dueis-o- 'hated'.

IE cognates: Skt. dvis- 'hate, enmity, enemy', 'enemy7, -dvis- 'hating' (RV+), OAv. daibis-uuant- 'enemy'; Skt. dves- 'to hate7, dve$as- 'hate, enmity, hater, enemy', a-dvesa- 'not hostile'; Gr. δείδω 'to fear1 < pf. *δέ-δροι-α; AtL δέδοικα < *δέ-δροι-κα, aor. δεΐσαι (II.) < *δρεϊ-σαι. A religious term. Mostly reconstructed as *dwei-ro-, in which case initial d- instead of b- < *dw- is unexpected. Dims is therefore explained as a dialectal form (EM, IEW), which seems to be confirmed by Serv. auct. Aen. 3, 235 Sabimet Umbn, quae nos mala, dira appellant^ cf. Rix 2005: 569. The recent date of appearance, and the absence of any derivatives within Latin, might also be interpreted as a support for this explanation. We may accept it, but with the necessary precautions, since it remains an explanation ex obscuro. Since intervocalic *s yields r in Latin and in Umbrian, one might also posit original *dweis-o- 'hateful / to be hated'.

Bibl.: WH I: 353f., EM 176, IEW 227f., LIV *duejk



### dis-
dis- 'away, apart7 [pref ] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: di- (in front of b.d^J.m.n.v.r; dismota SCBac), dir- (in front of vowels), dif- (in front of/).

Pit. *dis- 'in two, apart'; *dus- 'bad' (in difficilis).

It. cognates: possibly U. disleralinsust [3s.fut.pf.] '?will have gone wrong' < *dis-leis-l

PIE *dus 'into two > bad', *dui- 'two, into two'.

IE cognates: Gr. διά 'in two, apart,

<!-- pdf-page: 186; printed-page: 172; running-header: 172                                    discipulus -->

through' [adv.], 'through' [prep.] < *δισ-α; also δα- in δαφοινός, esp. from animals, 4 very red', δάσκιος 'very shady', developed from δια-, or Aeol. ζα-. Borrowed into Gothic as the prefix dis-' apart'. Lat. dis- seems to be cognate with Gr. διά < *δισ-α. Whereas διά can function both as a preverb and as a preposition, in Latin, dis- is only a preverb. In the older texts, it is nearly always prefixed to verbs, with the exception of the adj. difficilis. Exactly in this compound, dis- does not mean 'away, apart5, but rather 'non-, opposite1. Therefore, it may well be that difficilis contains

PIE *dus- 'apart; bad' (thus Wackernagel and Leumann 1977: 400), which is otherwise unattested in Latin. Yet Forssman 1992: 309 maintains that difficilis was built from dis +facilis on the example oisimilis : dissimilis. Even if difficilis does not directly continue *dus-y the restriction of dis- to (verbal) compounds would suggest that all of Latin dis- is a remake of *dus- by analogy with *dui- 'into two, apart'. Another possibility is a dissimilation *dwis- > *dis- in front of verbs starting in *w-, especially in the compounds dividere 'to divide' and divertere 'to divert'. Yet a separation of dis- from δια- is unattractive; and in Greek, δυσ- has remained alive as a prefix - but meaning 'bad*. Hence, Proto-Greek may have had all three forms: *dus- 4 bad\ *dui- 'two' and *dis-(a~) 'into two, apart'. Whereas *dus- developed from 'into two' to metaphorical 'bad*, the novel form *dis- retained the literal meaning 'into two, apart'.

Bibl.: WH I: 354L, EM 176, IEW 232, Untermann 2000: 180f. → bis



### discipulus
discipulus 'pupil' [m. o] (Pl.)

Derivatives: disciplina 'teaching, discipline' (Pl>), disciplinosus 'well-trained' (Cato).

Pit. *kapelo- 'who takes'. WH derive discipulus from *dis-capio> 'to assume mentally, interpret' (cf. discepiare 'to negotiate, decide' Cic.+), which is semantically not compelling. EM are very hesitant about it. On the other hand, -pulus is difficult to explain on the basis of disco.

Bibl.: WH I: 355, EM 176. → capid



### disco, -ere
disco, -ere 'to learn5 [v. Ill; pf. didici\ (Pl.+)

Pit. *dikske/o- [pr.], *de-dok- [pf.] ( » *di-dok- after the present, Leumann 1977: 586).

PIE *di-dk-slt-e/o-, pf * de-dolt- 'to take, accept'. The present is reconstructed by LIV (after Leumann 1977: 586) as a desiderative pr. *di-dk-se- > pre-Lat. *dik-se→ *dik-ske- > disce-, but this seems unwarranted. The meaning is not 'desire to observe' vel sim., but 'to take in (repeatedly)'. Thus, I reconstruct an iterative suffix *-ske/o- plus reduplication.

Bibl.: WH I: 331, EM 176, IEW 189-191, Sihler 1995: 115, 507, Meiser 1998: 210, LIV *dek-. → decet, doced



### diu, diu
diu, diu 'by day; for a long time' [adv.] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: (1) nudiusteriius 'three days ago' (Pl.+), Dius Fidius 'god of oaths' (Pl.+); (2) dius [adv.] in noctu neque dius 'by night nor day' (Pl.); (3) diu 'by day' (usually together with noctu 'by night') (Pl.+), diu [adv.] 'for a long time' (Andr.+),

<!-- pdf-page: 187; printed-page: 173; running-header: dives                                         173 -->

comp. diutius, interdiu [adv.] 'by day' (Cato+), interdius [adv.] 'by day' (Pl.+), quamdiu 'how long, as long as' (Pl.+); (4) diurnus 'of the day; daily' (Cic.+), diutinus 'lasting for a long time' (Pl.+); biduum 'period of two days' (Cato+), triduum 'period of three days' (Pl.+), quadnduum 'period of four days' (Pl.+); (5) dhts [adj.] 'daylif (Enn.+). Pit. nom. *di(j)ous, gen. *diwos, loc. *di(j)ou I *djowi 'day'; ad. *-diwo- Of the day'.

PIE *di-eu- 'day; sky\

IE cognates: see s.v. lupiter; Skt. divya- [adj.] 'heavenly, divine' (RV+), Gr. δϊος [adj.] 'heavenly' (II.), also 'belonging to Zeus' (trag.). (1) The form dius, which is suspected to be attested in rtudiustertius and mediusfidius, might be the old nom.sg. *diieus > *diieus (syllabic *di- analogically from the gen.sg.), with monophthongization of *eu > u. (2) The adverb dius can directly represent the PIE gen,sg. *diuos 'of the day'; maybe noctii also replaces an older gen.sg. *nox used as a temporal adverb. (3) The disyllabic form di% with iambic shortening diu, can represent the loc.sg. *diieu > *diiu > *diu. It also occurs as dm, with long ι imported from divus. Nussbaum analyzes interdiu as a sequence of two originally independent adverbs, 'in the meantime by day\ The comp. diutius might have its t from diutinus (thus Leumann 1977: 322; this would be unique); WH I: 358 assume that the suffix was taken from setius, citius. (4) The adj. diurnus has probably adopted -urnus from nocturnus 'by night'. The adj. diutinus has the suffix -tinus which is also found in other temporal adj., e.g. crastinus. Maybe it is

PIE *~t-ino- (cf. vermis, Gr. ειαρινός 'in the spring'). The adj. in -duiim are from *-diuom, n. of the adj. *-diu-o-. WH I: 104 explain ζ from analogy v/ith'postridie9 but the model is not perfect, since *biduus is an adj. One might consider analogy with bimus 'lasting two years' < *bi-him-o-. (5) The adj. dius (often substantivized to dium 'the open sky1) may be compared to Sab. *djouwjo- 'of Jupiter'; it may thus reflect *diu-io- (cf. Gr. δϊος; and Gaius < OLat. kavios), unless it is identical with dius 'god-like' after all.

Bibl.: WH I: 104, 357-360, II: 740, EM 174-177, IEW 183-187, Leumann 1977: 357, Sihler 1995: 339. → deusy dies, lupiter*, -dinus

0. diuvil(u) , iuvilu, iuvil(u) [nom.sg.], diuvilam, iuvilam [acc.sg.], iuvilas, iuvilas [nom.pl.] 'kind;of stele, image' < *djowjela-l Since (d)iuvila- is the name for stone or clay steles found in a Capua necropolis, it is tempting to derive it from the name of Jupiter. Since a preform *djow-e/ila- would undergo syncope in open second syllable, the suffix -ila- could be explained from *-i/e!a-, from *-elja- or from *-je!a-. The latter option would enable us to derive (d)iuvila~ from the adj. (d)iuvio-, U. iuvio- 'belonging to Jupiter'; hence *djowje-lo- 4 which belongs to the (feast?) of Jupiter'.

Bibl.: IEW 183-187,Untermann2000: 188. ^lupiter



### dives, -itis
dives, -itis 'wealthy, rich' [adj. /] (Pl.+; contracted dis Ter., ditis, comp. ditiorNaev.-J-)

Derivatives: divitiae [f.pi.] (Pl. Ter. also ditiae) 'abundance, riches' (Pl.+), cKvitare 'to enrich' (Acc.+), ditescere 'to grow rich' (Lucr.+); Dis, Ditis [m.] 'ruler of the underworld' (Cic.+). Lat. dives is an adj. which was probably derived from divus as *deiu-(o/e)t- 'who is

<!-- pdf-page: 188; printed-page: 174; running-header: 174                                       divido -->

like / protected by the gods\ The older paradigm was nom. dives, gen. ditis, which contraction *-nvj- > L This led to the creation of two new, full paradigms, one in divit-, one in dit- (with nom.sg. dis). The occurrence of the deity Dis together with pater may be due to association with Di(e)spiter,

### divide, -ere
divide, -ere 'to separate, divide' [v. Ill; pf. dwlsr, ppp. divlsum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: drvidia 'vexation' (Naev.+), dividus 'separated' (Acc.+), drviduus 'divided into two or more parts' (Pl.+).

Pit. *dis-wi-p-e/o-.

PIE *(d)ui-dhhr 'to separate, distinguish'.

IE cognates: Skt. avidhat [3s.aor.act.] 'allotted', OAv. vida- 'to devote oneself to'; ToAB watk- 'to separate, distinguish* <

The original PIE verb *dui-dʰhr (which became thematic in Latin) meant 'to divide in two, separate'. It lost initial *c/- through dissimilation in front of the next dental stop, and was reinforced by dis- in Latin, which itself is another reflex of *duis 'two'.

Bibl.: WH I: 359, EM 177f., IEW 1127f., Lubotsky 1994, LIV *d h eh r . → viduus



### dō, dare
d6, dare 'to give' [v. I] (VOLat.+: 3s.pf dedet (Elog.Scip. 230 BC), dedit (211 BC), Tibur dede, Tusculum deded, dedet, Minturnae dede, Cales ded, Praeneste dedit, 3p. Praeneste dedrunU Paestum dedere\ data [nom.n. of ppp.] in Faliscan inscr.). In OLat.: pr. do7 das, dat, damus, datis, dant\ danunt 'dant' (CIL 1.1531.7, Naev., Pl. Paul, ex F.); pf dedi, ppp. datum, fut dabo, ipv. da, date.

Derivatives: datare 'to be in the habit of giving' (Pl.+), datatim 'from hand to hand' (Naev.+); datid 'the act of giving' (Varro+), datum "present; debit' (Pl.+), dator 'a giver' (Pl.+), datus, -us 'the act of giving' (Pl.); dos, -tis [f] 'dowry, endowment' (Pl.+), dotalis 'forming part of a dowry' (Pl.+), dotatus 'provided with a dowry' (Pl.+); circumdare 'to place round' (Pl.+), dedere 'to surrender' (Naev.+), didere 'to distribute' (Pl.+), edere 'to eject, emit' (Pl.+), interdare 'to place between' (Pl.+), prodere 'to project, betray' (Pl.+), proditor 'traitor' (Pl.+), reddere 'to give back' (Naev.+), tradere 'to hand over, deliver' (Naev.+), traditid 'delivery' (Varro+), vendere Ho sell' (Naev.+), venditare 'to offer for sale' (Pl.+); Forum inscr. dotauefre?] *he gave/they gave (as a privilege)'.

Pit. redpn *di-dre-ti < *didati [Sab. pres, Latin cp. in -dere], aor. *do-lda~, pf *deda-, ppp. *datos; *dot- [f.] 'endowment'.

It. cognates: Ven. doto [3s.pret] 'gave' < *d(e)h₃-to; Vest, didet [3s.pr.], Pael. dida, U. tera, dirsa, dersa [3s.pr.sb.], dirsans, dirsas [3p.pr.sb.], titu, tetu, ditu, teftu, tertu, dirstu [3s.ipv.II], tefte [3s.pr.ps.], O. didest [3s.fut], deded, δεδετ, Mars, ded, Presam. tetet, U. dede [3s.pf.], O. dedens, δεδενς [3p.pf ], U. terust, dirsust [3s.fut.pf.], Ο. δατας, Pael. datas, Vest, data [ppp., gen.sg.f.]; with prev. dad- O. dadid [3s.pf.sbJ , da[da]d? [3s.pr.sb.]?; with preverb am- 'around' U. aterafust, andersafust, andirsafust [3s.fut.pf.], all 'to give'. Ven. [?d]idor [3s.pr.med.] of'to give', if dr is correctly restored.

PIE pr. *di-d(e)h₃- 'to give', pf. *de-dʰ₃-, pf.sb. *de-dʰ₃-ihr; root aor. *deh₃-m,

<!-- pdf-page: 189; printed-page: 173 -->

-do 175

*dʰ₃-me (whence Latin dare); ppp. *dʰ₃-to-; noun *de/oh₃-t-.

IE cognates: Olr. dorata* < *tu-ro-ad-da- 'be able to give*, iadaid 'to close' < *epi-da- < PCI. *da- 'give'; Hit. da- / d- 'to take, wed, decide', CLuw. la- 'to take', laid- 'id.' < *dohrldʰr, Skt. pr. dadati, aor, adat, OAv. pr. dada-, YAv. daSaiti, Gr. δίδωμι 'to give', aor. έδωκα, έδομεν, δώς 'gift', Arm. tarn Ί give\ etow Ί gave', OAlb. dhae 'gave', Lith. duoti 'give', OCS c/αΛ" 'give'. The form danunt must be secondary. Sommer has proposed an equation situs : sinunt = datus : X, X = danunt, this is accepted by Livingston 2004: 15, who argues that the other 3p. forms in -nunt(ur) were modeled on danunt. But Sihler 1995: 544f. rightly objects that this equation renders the restriction to the 3p. difficult to understand. In view of the pervading short vocalism in the present of dare, the two forms das and da! are probably analogical (thus Schrijver). Due to the merger of *(di)dare and ^icfare in compounds, the appurtenance of all compounds in -dere to either do or -do (see the following lemma) is not always certain. Judging by their meanings, circumdare and interdare may represent *-ctere, with hypercorrect -are. Vendere may reflect *venum dare 'to put up for sale'. The reduplicated present generalized the zero grade of the root in Italic, hence *dida- in all forms. In Sabellic, this was preserved, whereas in Latin, a new present was created on the basis of the pi. of the root aorist Possibly, the loss of the reduplication syllable in compounds led to the analogical loss of *<#- in the present stem. The root aorist was preserved in Venetic.

Bibl.: WH I: 360-363, EM 178480, IEW 223-226, Giacomelli 1963: 2421, Lejeune 1974: 332f., Leumann 1977: 527, 560, Schrijver 1991 ? 147, 402, Sihler 1995: 520, 544ff., Meiser 1998: 185, 188/216, Untermann 2000: ί73-179, 613-615, Untermann 2002:494, LIV *deh}- 'give'. → cedo, donum, duim; sacerdds



### -do, -dere
-do, -dere 'to put' [v. Ill; pf. -didf, ppp. -ditum; pr.sb. sometimes -duim, -duis,-duit, -duint in Pl.]: abdere 'to conceal' (Pl.+), addere 'to add' (Lex XII, Pl.+), condere 'to put, insert, establish' (Pl.+), indere 'to introduce, apply* (Pl.+), perdere 'to destroy; lose' (Naev.+), subdere 'to place under, subject' (Andr.+)

Derivatives: additio 'addition' (Varro); condire 'to season, flavour' (Pl.+), condimentum 'seasoning, spice' (Pl.+), conditio 'method of preserving food' (Varro+), conditaneus 'suitable for preserving' (Varro), conditfvus 'suitable for preserving' (Cato+);perditus, -us 'ruination' (Pl.); subditivus 'spurious' (Pl.+).

Pit. pr. *-po, *-pas, etc.; pf. *-pepaiy ppp. *-pato-.

It. cognates: O. pruffed [3s.pf.], pniftuset [3p.pf.ps.n.], priiftas su[nt [3p.pf.ps-f.] 'to erect' < *profefet, *profato- < pf ^pro-c^e-cthj-e-, ppp. *pro-c?hrto-; O. manafum [ls.pf.?], aamanaffed, aamanafed, amanafed [a- + 3s.pf.], emanafed [e- + 3s.pf.] 'to commission, order' < pf. *man-fe-f- < *man- 'hand1 + *c?e-cfhr.

PIE pr. *dVd h h r 'to put\ aor. *dʰ(e)hr.

IE cognates: PCI. *-df- 'to put' in Olr. creitid 'to believe' (see s.v. credo) ; Hit. dai-1 ti- 'to lay, put, place', Lye. ta- 'to put' < *dʰhroi-, Hit. titta-Ititti- 'to install, assign' < *dʰi-dʰhroi-, Skt. dha-, Av. da- 'to put, make', Gr. τχύημι, aor, εΰηκα, pi. &θεμεν, Lith. dSti 'to lay, put', OCS deti 'do, say', Toch. ta- 'place, set'. Verbal compounds in -do can represent either

PIE *deh₃- 'to give' or *d h eh r 'to put'.

<!-- pdf-page: 190; printed-page: 176; running-header: 176                                      doceo -->

Since the simplex of 'to put' is not retained in Latin, I here give the verbs of which WH and EM suggest that they may be ascribed to

PIE *d h eh r . The Latin reflex -d- is regular only after certain consonants, but not in absolute anlaut, where/· results from *dʰ-. Thus, -dere may reflect an athematic aorist *-dʰh₁-sii possibly thematized to *dʰhi-e/o-. It is also possible that the forms go back to the PIE reduplicated present, e.g. Ip. *-dʰi-dʰhrmes >

Pit. *-pipames > *-pparnes.

doceo 'to tell, inform; teach' [v. II; docui, doctum] (Andr.+)

### doleō
dolefl 'to be in pain, feel painfuF [v. II; pf. dolui, ppf. doliturum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: dolor, -oris 'pain' (Pl.+); dolitare 'to be painful' (Cato+).

Pit. *dole- 'to pain, cause pain5.

PIE *dolhreie- 'to split'.

IE cognates: MW e-thyl 'chooses' < * (do-)eks-dofi- < *-dol-e- < *-dol-eie- 'to split out'. Derived from the root *delh r 'to chop' under the assumption than 'pain' was expressed by the feeling of 'being torn apart'. A causative *do!hreie- 'to make somebody (feel) split' could have become 'to cause pain5. The experiencer must originally have been expressed in the dative.
