### dOlium
dOlium 'large earthenware vessel5 [η. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: doliaris [adj.] 'of or like & dolium' (Pl.).

IE cognates: maybe Ir. delb 'form' [f,], OCS dbly 'barrel' [f. u] , dbhve [gen.sg.] < *dl(H)-u-. The connection with dolare 'to chop5 is doubted by EM and Schrijver because the root of dolare usually refers to woodwork, not earthenware. To me, this does not seem a decisive objection, but the long δ cannot be regularly explained. Since it is a pottery term, it may be a loanword.

Bibl.: WH I: 364, EM 181, IEW 194-196, Schrijver 1991: 123.



### dolō, -āre
dolO, -are 'to hew or chop into shape' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: dolabra 'a pick or similar tool' (Liv.+).

<!-- pdf-page: 191; printed-page: 177; running-header: dominus                                        177 -->

Pit. *dolaje~.

PIE *dolhrie/o- cto cut repeatedly' or *d(e)lhpie/o- 'to cut'.

IE cognates: Lith. dilti, Is. delii 'to wear off, disappear' (Zem.), Latv. dilu Is, d?lu 'to decrease' <pr. *delh,-e-. Lat. dold- may reflect *dela- or *dola-. LIV posits a pr. *delhrie/o-y whereas Rix 1999 considers a zero grade of the root possible. Rix also regards the instrument noun dolabra (for *dolabra < ^delhr^reh-^) as evidence for a primary verb, since a denominative verb would have us expect a noun **dolamentum. Schrijver 1991: 215 reconstructs an <?-grade, which he derives from the basic noun (if dolare is a denominal verb), but which may as well stem from an iterative verb in PIE. The PIE root *delgʰ- 'to cut, hew* (Olr. as-dloing 'splits', 01c. telgja 'to cut up') has a very similar meaning, but the root forms cannot be reconciled.

Bibl.: WH I: 364-365, EM 181, IEW I94ff., Schrijver 1991: 215f, 400, Rix 1999: 527-28, LIV *delh r . → doled



### dolus
dolus 'unlawful intention, malice' [m. o] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: doWsus 'sly, deceitful* (Pl.+); subdolus 'id/ (Pl.+); sedulo 'sincerely; diligently' (Pl.+\ sedulitas 'painstaking attention' (Varro+).

Pit. *dolo- 'trick'.

It. cognates: 0. dolom, dolum [acc.sg.], dolud [abl.sg.] 'intention, ruse\ Probably a loanword from Latin.

PIE *do!hro- [m.] 'pain'?

IE cognates: Gr. δόλος 'bait;trick', OIc. to/ [n.] 'account, number'. ~f\ For sir 'apart, away' < *sedr;in sedulo < *se dolod [abl.sg.] 'without malice', cf. Meiser 1998: 157f IEW argues that dolus was borrowed from Greek; others have compared Gm. *ta/a- 'reason, account'. In view of doled 'to feel pain' and dolor 'pain', an inherited word *do!hro- 'pain/which pains' > 'deceit, malice' cannot be dismissed.

Bibl.: WH I: 366, II 509, EM 182, IEW 193, Untermann 2000: 189. → doled



### dominus
dominus 'master of a household, ruler' [m. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: domino 'female head of the household, mistress' (Pl.+), dominium 'rule, dominion' (Laev\+), dominicus 'of a master' (Afran.+), dominari 'to rule, be in control' (Acc.+). \

Pit. *dom-o/u-no- 'of the house'. Pinault 2000: 90-91, building on a suggestion by Schindler, reconstructs ^dom-h^en-^ thematized as *dom-h₃n-o- 'who profits of the house'. Since Skt. damunas- 'lord of the house' probably goes back to *dom-i/-/?j«-o-, Pinault suspects that both words go back to the same PIE word; Skt introduced the w-stem into the noun. But since Skt. and Lat. have independent remodellings anyway, and since dominus can go back to *domHno- or *domVno-9 there is no way to exclude that dominus continues *domu-no- or *domo-no-. Semantically, dominus can simply be 'he of the house' (like Go. kindins 'he of the gens\ thiudans 'he of the people'), and does not have to mean 'he who possesses/profits from a house' (like Bellono, Portimus, colonus).

Bibl.: IEW 198f, Meid 1956-57, 1957, 1958, Peters 1980: 172, Pinault 2000, LIV L*demhr· -^ domus

<!-- pdf-page: 192; printed-page: 178; running-header: 178                                        domo -->



### domō, -āre
doraO, -are 'to subdue' [v. I; pf. domuiy ppp. domitum] (Pt+)

Derivatives: domitare 'to subdue by taming' (Verg,+), domitor 'a trainer (of animals); conqueror' (Cic.+).

Pit. *domaje-.

PIE *domh₂-eie/o- 'to tame\

IE cognates: Olr. daimidP, -daim 'to allow, give in', MW adef 'to confess' (< *ad-\ MCo. godhaff, MBret. gouzaff 'to suffer' < PCI. *dam-je/o-, Olr. damnaidP, -damna* 'to bind, subdue' < PCL *dam-na-\ Hit. tamass-/tame/iss- 'to (op)press' <

PIE *dmehrs- I *dmh₂-s-, Skt. d W 'to control', caus. damayati (RV+), damitar^ [m.] 'tamer, subduer\ Gr. δάμνημι *to tame, subdue, conquer', Go. ga-tamjan "to tame'. Lat. domitare was either built directly to domare as an iterative, or to the ppp. domitus as a denominal verb. Theoretically, Lat. domare might reflect *dema-, but the absence of a nasal present or ^-suffix, and the suffix -ά-, suggest an original causative. The perfect domm < *doma-xvai was built secondarily on the basis of the present, which was reanalyzed as *doma-je-.

Bibl.: WH I: 367f., EM 182, IEW I99f., Isebaert 1988, Schrijver 1991: 400, Sihler 1995: 505, Meiser 1998: 186,2003: I37f, LIV *demh₂-. → redimio



### domus, -ϊ/ -iis
domus, -ϊ/ -iis 'house, home; household' [f. o/u] (Andr.+); sg.nom. -us, voc. -us, ace. -urn, gen. -/"(usually in Pl.) » -u$7 dat. -ui, also -0, abl. -ό, also -u, loc. -T; pl.nom. -us, ace. -iis or -os, gen. -wwm, -drum, dat.abl. -ibus. The abl.sg. domu once in PL {fugere ... e* /KH: domu), otherwise domo as adv. (diom<5 abeas); lx acc.pl. domds in Pl.

Derivatives: domesticus 'of the household' (Varro+); domicilium 'habitation, dwelling-place' (Pl.+).

Pit. *domo- [f ] 'house\

PIE *dom, *dem- 'house'; *dom-o-?

IE cognates: Skt. dam- [n.], Av, dam-, Skt. dama- [m.] 'house', Gr. δόμος 'house, layer of bricks', Lith. nomas 'house' < *dom-o-; Gr. δμώς, -ωός [m.] 'slave, servant' < *dmdu-, OCS donrt [m. u/o] 'house', Ru. dom, loc.sg. na domu 'at home' < *dom-u-, Ru. domovyj, domovoj [adj.] * house-'. In PIE, there was a root noun nom.sg. *dom, gen. *dem-s. Probably, a w-stem derivative also existed (yielding Slav. *domu- 'house' and Gr. δμώς 'slave' < *dm-ou-). There are o-stems in Lithuanian and Hr., but they appear to be independent innovations of. those branches. The f. gender of domus probably is due to the original root noun. Old Latin mainly has o-stem forms; the change into a w-stem which some case forms show may be explained from an attempt to adapt the declension type of domus to its f. gender, which is unusual for Latin o-stems (apart from tree-names) (M. Weiss, p.c). Pace Gerschner 2002: 172, the loc.sg. domt does not warrant an original loc. *dem-ei of a root noun, but can represent a genuine o-stem form *domoL

Pit. *-om-, *-oNC- mostly became -urn-, -uNC- in Latin, but this change is sometimes impeded by a preceding dental: domare, tongeo, longus. Hence, domus may be the regular outcome of

Pit. *domos. The adj. domesticus cannot contain an 5-stem *domes- (for which there is no evidence), but was rather formed to its antonym *rowestikos (> rusticus), cf.

<!-- pdf-page: 193; printed-page: 179; running-header: dormio                                     179 -->

Leumann 1977: 339. For domicilium, WH suggest an abstract *domo-koUio- 'house-dwelling' from *domo- + *kʷel- + -ium. Phonologically more regular would be *-kʷoUio-1 since *-kʷel-io- would yield Lat. *-quilium. EM suggest that Lat. domicilium might be based on Lat. *domicola 'house-dweller'. But such a noun is unattested, and if Lat. domicilium is a relatively late creation, one would rather expect *domicolium. A third etymology, dismissed by WH as less likely than *domokolio- (see above), entails a reconstruction *domu/o-kel-io- 'domestic shelter, cover of the home', from domus + the root of celare 'to cover, hide', cella. This reconstruction seems both semantically and morphologically plausible and is phonologically impeccable: *domo-kel(H)-io- regularly yields Lat. domicilium. Instead of initial dom-, there are several forms in dem-: demi Mi. 738, demum Am. 654, Au. 326, Ep. 452, each time only in one ms. branch (Gerschner 2002). It seems to me that these are too few to warrant a linguistically real stem demu- at the time of Plautus. The three instances of demum occur in sentences where one can imagine that a writing error de- for do- might not have been corrected because demum would also fit the context (though not the metre, which requires a short penultimate): Am 654 Edepol me uxori exoptatum credo adventurum domum /, Ep 452 Immo si audias / meas pugnas, fugias manibus dimissis domum /, Ba 326 ut illud reportes aurum ab Theotimo domum A In Mi 739 this argument does not hold, but demi is found in only one of the 4 main mss.: ut... /meae domi accipiam benigne, lepide et lepidis victibus.

Bibl.: Sommer 1914: 65,-,404f., WH I: 367-370, EM 182f„ IEW 198f., Meier-Briigger 1977, Meiser 1998: 83, 150, Pinault 2000: 89, Gerschner 2002: I72f., LIV *demh₂- 'to build'. → dominus *



### dunum
dunum 'gift, present* [η. ο] (Pl.+; VOLat. donom [ace.])

Derivatives: donare sto present, give' (Pl.+), donabilis 'worthy to be the recipient of (Pl.), donations formally presented' (Cato+).

Pit. *dono- [n.] 'gift*.

It. cognates: Ven. donom [nom.acc] 'gift'; donasto, tonasto [3s.pret.] 'gave* < *dona-s-to, donasan [3p.pret.] < *dona-s-nt\ O. dunum, dunum, dunum, U. dunum, dunu, dum, Mars, dunom, donom, Vol. duno, Pael. donom [acc.sg.], O. diineis [gen.sg.] n. 'gift'; Ο. δουνακλομ [nom.acc] 'gift' or 'act of giving' < *donatlom; O. dunat [3s.pr.], duunated [3s.pf ] to *dona- 'to bestow'.

PIE *doh₃-no- or *deh₃-no- 'gift'.

IE cognates: Olr. dan 'poem, gift' < *ddno-, Olr. dan, W. dawn 'gift, talent' < *donu-\ Skt dana- 'gift, present* (< *dehreno-, Lubotsky 1988: 84), Lith. duonis 'gift*, OCS dam 'tax, tribute'. The verb donare is derived from dormm. In view of the Oscan and Venetic verb forms, this derivation may go back to Pit.; but it cannot be excluded that these are separate innovations of the different languages (thus Untermann 2000: 195).

Bibl.: WH I: 371f, EM 179, IEW 225, Lejeune 1974: 333, Untermann 2000: 190, I94f., LIV *deh₃-. → do, dare; duim



### dormiō, -īre
dormic, -Ire 'to sleep' [v. IV] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: dormitare 'to feel sleepy' (Pl.+), dormitator 'one who sleeps all day* (Pl.); obdormlre 'to fall asleep' (Pl.+).

<!-- pdf-page: 194; printed-page: 180; running-header: 180                                    dorsum -->

Pit. *dormje/o-*

PIE *drm-ie- 'to sleep'. DE cognates: CS dremati, Ru. dremat\ SCr. drijemati 'to doze, slumber' < *drem-9 Gr. δαρΰάνω 'tj> fall asleep\ The PIE roots *drem- and *dreH- both have two meanings: 'run' and 'sleep'. The meaning 'sleep' is only found in Latin and Slavic for *drem^ in Indie for *dreH, and in Greek for *dr-. The accentuation of the Slavic verb points to *drem-\ this may be regarded as an inner-Slavic formation with lengthened vowel (Klingenschmitt 1989: 81,Derksen2008: 117).

Bibl.: WH I: 372, EM 184, IEW 226, LIV 2. *drem.



### dorsum
dorsum 'the back, a ridge' [η. ο] (Pl.+; dorsus [m.] Pl.lx; A variant doss- occurs for both words, and is due to a recent assimilation.)

Derivatives: dorsuarius 'that carries a load on its back' (Varro). A compound of de- 'away from' and versus 'turned towards', as the Romans themselves thought: Paul, ex F. equals dorsum with deorsum 'down, below'. This is phonologically impeccable: *de-worsum 'turned away from' > *deorsum > deorsum (cf. deus). As in seorsum 'apart' < se-worsum, a recent change has reduced -eo- to -o- in front of rs, hence sorsum 'apart' and dorsum *'turned away from' > 'back'. Apparently, lexical differentiation made the spelling deorsum canonical for the adv. 'down' (but inscriptional evidence for dorsum does occur), whereas the noun 'back' is always spelled dorsumldossum. I do not understand WH's statement that "herabgewandt den Bedeutungskem des vulgaren Wortes nicht trifft": the 'back' is precisely what is 'turned away' from the viewer.

Bibl.: WH I: 372, EM 184, Leumann 1977: 211, Sihler 1995: 83, 178,221. -* verto



### dubius
dubius 'hesitant, in doubt' [adj. o/a\ (Andr.+)

Derivatives: dubat 'dubitat' (PauL ex K); dubitdre 'to be in doubt' (Pl.+), dubitatim * hesitatingly' (Sis.+), dubitatio 'doubt, hesitation' (Varro+); addubanum 'dubium' (Paul. exF.).

Pit. *du(iyfwo-.

PIE *dui-bʰh₂u-o- 'double'. These forms presuppose an adj. *dubos 'in doubt', from which a denom. verb *dubare and a new adj. dubius were derived. The origin may have been *du-bʰHu-o- 'of two forms, double'. Leumann 1977 suggests an original ins.pl. *du-bʰi 'on two sides', remade into an adjective; yet in view of Gr. διφυής 'with two shapes', a connection with *bʰh₂u- 'to be(come)' seems more likely. The formans may be the same as in probus and superbus. The form du- must represent an inner-Italic abstraction from dud, *dui- (as in ducenti, duplex); the older form of 'two' in possessive compounds was *dui-*

Bibl.: WH I: 375f., EM 185, IEW 146ft, Leumann 1977: 278, Untermann 2000: 179» → probus, superbus

<!-- pdf-page: 195; printed-page: 181; running-header: dudum                                       181 -->



### dūcō, -ere
dflcO, -ere 'to lead, conduct' [v. Ill; pf. duxiy ppp. ducturn] (Lex XII+; Elog.Scip, 3s. abdoucif)

Derivatives: dux, -cis 'leader, guide' (Pl.+); ductare 'to conduct, lead' (Pl.+), ductus, -us 'military leadership; motion' (Pl.+), ductabilitas 'gullibility' (Ace), ductim 'in draughts' (Pl.+), ductitare 'to regularly lead off (Pl.); abducere 'to lead away5 (Elog.Scip.+), adducere 'to lead, bring* (Pl.+), conducere 'to bring together5 (Pl.+), conductlcius 'hired5 (Pl.+), conductor "hirer5 (Pl.+), diducere 'to lead away' (Andr.+), diducere 'to divide' (Cato+), educere 'to lead out* (Pl.+), educare 'to bring up, rear* (Pl.+), eductio 'the leading out (of troops)' (Cato), inducere 'to lead, bring' (Pl.+; indouc- CIL 586), intrdducere 'to introduce* (Pl.+), obducere 'to lead towards' (Pl.+), obductare 'to introduce before' (PK)5 perducere 'to conduct, bring' (Pl.+), perductare 'to conduct' (Pl.), perductor 'who conducts' (Pl.+), producere 'to bring forth' (Pl.+), productare 'to prolong5 (Ter.), reducere 'to lead back5 (Pl.+), redux, -ucis 'leading back home, returning5 (Naev.+), seducere 'to draw aside' (Pl.+), tradticere 'to bring across, convert' (Pl.+), tradux, -ucis [m./f] 'side-branch of a vine' (Varro+).

Pit. *douk-e-, *{-)duk-aje- [v.], *(-)duk- [m.].

PIE *deuk-e/o- [pr.] 'to draw5, *-duk- [in compounds].

IE cognates: MW Is. dygaf, 3s. dwe 'to lead, bring', OBret 3s. due "carries', MCo. 3s. dek, deg "takes' < PCL *duke/o-; Hit. tufoi-, tukkanzi- "cultivation of plants and animals' [c] < *duk-(ent)-i- (Melchert 1999), Oss. duc-fdqc- 'to milk5, Gr. δα-δύσσομαι "to be distracted', ένδυκέως 'kindly, greedily', Alb", n-duk 'to pull', Go. tiuhan 'to draw5. All forms in duct- (except ductor) are derived from the ppp. ductus. The zero grade and the agentive meaning of dux suggest that it was taken from compounds (cf. OHG heri-zogo). The type e-ducare probably also goes back to such nominal forms.

Bibl.: WH I: 377f, EM 185f., IEW 2201, Leumann 1977: 549, Schrijver 1991: 164, Schumacher 2004: 286-288, LIV *deuk-.



### dudum
dudum 'some time ago' [adv.] (Pl.+)

Pit. *dw? [adv.].

PIE *duh₂- (age. *dueh₂m, gen. *duh₂0s?) 'far, long'.

IE cognates: Hit. tuuan 'to this side5, tuuaz 'from afar' <

PIE *dueh₂-m, Skt. daviyas- 'further, more distant', sup. davistha-, OP duvaistam [adv.] 'very long, very far', Gr. δήν 'long, far5 (< *δράν), Dor. δάν, δοάν < *duehrm; Arm. tev 'duration' < *deuh₂-; Ru. dave (dial.) 'recently, yesterday' [adv.] < PS1. *dave <

PIE *douh₂-, Ru. davno 'long ago', SCr. davan 'ancient'. Consists of the root du 'long (time)' (see diirus) plus enclitic -dum. Latin du- might represent the zero grade of the root noun which is also reflected in Gr. δήν and in the Hit. adverbs. This root is homonymous with the verbal root *deuh₂- 'to fit together'. It seems to show schwebe-ablaut: *dueh₂m and *dueh₂-ro- (cf. durus) next to *deuh₂- and *douh₂-. Indo-Iranian (Old Persian) suggests that *dueh₂- is the older variant.

Bibl.: WH I: 378f., EM 186, IEW 219f., Schrijver 1991: 232. — durus; -dam-de- do - dum

<!-- pdf-page: 196; printed-page: 182; running-header: 182                                      duim -->



### duim
duim 'that I give' [pr.sb.]: Lex XII duuit, PL duim, duis* duit, duint *dem, des, det, dent'; duas 'des'; duis 'dederis' (Paul, ex F.),produit 'porro dederit' (Fest)

Derivatives: PL concreduo 'concredide^o' is a secondary form, based on the (secondary) pf. concredw; PL wterduo from interdare is an uncertain form; if real, it will be based on the sb. interduim. The occurrences of duim etc. in the verbs with -doy -dere *to put' are analogical. Pit pr. *dowje/o-, aor.opt. *du-h, ppp. *du-to-*

It. cognates: Fal. douiad [3s.sb.] < *dowja- (Giacomelli 1963: 242f, Untermann 2000: 174); U, purtuvitu, purtuviuu, purtuvetu, purtuetu, purdouitu [3s.ipv.II], purtuvies [2s.fut], purtiius, purtitius, purtin^us, purdinsiust, purdinsus, purdinsust [3s.fut.pf], purtitu futu [3s.ipv.II.ps.], purditom fust, purdito fust, purtitu fust [3s.iut.pfps.], purtitaf, purdita [ppp., acc.pl.f,] 'to proffer, offer'; pf. *di-nki- vs. *di- < *dii- < *duH-9 ppp. *dito-. Maybe U. dia [3s.sb.ps.?] 'one can'? < *diat/r< *duh₃-i-eh₂-t/r (Untermann 2000: 174),

PIE aor. *d(e)uh₃- to *dʰ₃-u- 'to give'.

IE cognates: CLuw. tuua-9 HLuw. tu(va)~ , Lye. tuwe- 'to put, place\ Skt duvas- [n.] 'gift, oblation, favour' < *duH-as-, Lith. daviau, dave *I, he gave\ dovana 'gift', Latv. davana 'gift', OCS -davati, Ru. davat' •togive'<BSI. *do?ua?-. Latin duim is explained by Meiser 1998 as an original optaor. of a stem *deuH-. This stem may be based on a zero grade *dHu-C- > *duH-C- of *dʰ₃-u-, that is, the root 'to give' plus a ^/-extension. The present stem *dowje- of Sabellic and Faliscan may reflect a secondary present *deh₃-u-ie- > *douie-, cf. LIV.

Bibl.: WH I: 362f, EM 178, IEW 223-226~Meiser 1998: 184, Sihler 1995: 544f, Meiser 1998: 184, 2003: 182f., Untermann 2000: 173f., 613-615, LIV ?*deh₃u-. -* dautia; do, dare; donum



### dulcis
dulcis 'sweet' [adj. i] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: dulciculus [adj.] * sweet little' (Pl.+), dulcitas 'sweetness' (Acc,+), dulcedo, -inis * sweetness' (Lucr.+); dulcifer 'containing sweetness' (PL, Enn.).

Pit. *dulkʷi-*sweet'.

IE cognates: Gr. γλυκύς [adj.] * sweet' > Myc. de-re-u-ko perhaps ldleukosly Gr. γλεύκος [η,] 'sweet wine' (Arist; recent); αγλευκής 'sour' (Epich.)- Since Latin /-stem adj. are often formed from w-stems, it is often assumed that dulcis represents *dulku-i-\ yet the disappearance of the second *w is unexpected: *dulquis would be unproblematic. Greek γλυκύς is explained as an assimilation from *d\kus, while Lat could show *djk- > *dolk- > dulcis-, but the explanation for Greek is ad hoc. Even if it were correct, we would still not have an etymology for Graeco-Latin *dl(u)ku~. It is likely that we are dealing with a common borrowing from an unknown source. Since 'milk' is termed a 'sweet' substance in languages, there may be a link with lac, lactis, Gr. γάλα < *glg-t.

Bibl.: WH I: 379f., EM 186f., IEW 222.



### dumus
dumus 'shrub' [m. o] (Andr.+; sg. only in Ον., usually pi. dumi Cic.+; dusmo [adj.] Andr. apud Paul, ex, J\)

Derivatives: dumetum 'thicket' (Cic.+).

<!-- pdf-page: 197; printed-page: 183; running-header: duo                                      183 -->

Pit. *dusmo-.

IE cognates: Olr. dos 'shrub' (< *du$-ol). The appurtenance of Gm. forms in *to-, *tus- 'to spread, tear* is uncertain: Norw. tos 'ravel, fuzz', tossa 'to strew', ME totusen 'to ruffle', MLG tosen 'to tear', OHG zirzuson 'to ruffle', MHG zusach 'brushwood', zuse 'brushwood, frizzle'. A Latin-OIr. correspondence *dus-, without further connections.

Bibl.: WH I: 381, EM 187, IEW 175ff, Leumann 1977:205, Schrijver 1991: 246.



### duo, duae, duo
duo, duae, duo 'two' [adj. num.] (VOLat., Naev.+). Forms: nom.m. duo (Pl.+), acc.m, duds, nom.accn. duo (also dua in inscr.), gen.m.n. duom (Garigliano Bowl), duum (Naev.+), dudrum (Pl.+), dat.abl.m.n. duobus, nom.f. duae, acc.f. duas, gen.f duarum, databl.f. duabus. The form duo is also used case-indifferent, e.g. duo verbis (Nov.), ace. duo vilicos (Pl.), inter nos duo).

Derivatives: duodecim 'twelve' (PL*), ducentx 'two hundred' (Pl.+), duumviri 'board of two men' (CIL, Cic.+). Pit nom.m. Vwo.

It. cognates: U. dur [nom.m.], tut [acc.f.?], tuva [nom.acc.n.], tuves, duir [dat.m., η J, tuvere [loc.-abl.f. + -en] 'two'. U. desenduf /acc.pl.f.] 'twelve' < *dekm + *duons\ U. dupursus [dat.pl.] 'biped' < *du-pod~.

PIE *du-o-hi (nom.du.m.) 'two', *du-o-ihj (nom.du.n.f.).

IE cognates: Olr. duu, dou, do; OW dou, MW deu < PC]. *duuo\ Hit. tan 'for the second time' < *duoiom9 HLuw. twa/i- 'two', twisu 'twice*, Lye. kbi- 6(an)other,; < *dui-\ Skt. dvau I duvau I dva [nom.acc.d.m,], dve I duve [nom.acc.dXn.], YAv. duua [nom.djn.], duuae-ca [n.], duiie [accdil]; Gr. δύο, δύω, Arm. erku 'two', erkotasan 'twelve', Alb. dy, Lith. m. dii, OCS dbva 'two' <

PIE *duo-h,t Lith. dvi, OCS dbve <

PIE *duo-ih,; Go. twau OE m. tu, OIc. m. tveir 'two'; ToA m. wu, f. we. Original *duo has become duo by iambic shortening. A hapax dud is sometimes alleged for Plautus {Mil 1384), but the example is not probative (WH I: 382). The Latin forms other than the nom-m., and all the other Italic forms of the cardinal, must have been modeled on the nominal and pronominal plural endings. The retention of -u- shows that the syllabification was *duo rather than *<Λνο. The noun duumviri goes back to a syntagm duum virum 'of two men'. U. dupursus has replaced

PIE *dui-pod- by *du-podr or *duo-podr. Latin has replaced * φ / - by *du- in a number of transparent possessive compounds containing 'two': dubius, duplex, duplus, dupondium9 ducenfi. Greek δύο beside the dual δύω must be old, and the existence of a form with final short vowel is confirmed by Arm. erko-tasan 'twelve' and Skt. dva-ka- 'joined as a pair'. The vacillation between

PIE *duo and *duuo seems also to have been of PIE age, at least, it appears in Rigvedic, in Greek (δώδεκα 'twelve') and in Latin (bis but duo). Different solutions are conceivable. Lindeman 1965 has posited a phonetic origin, and this was accepted by Schindler 1977»

Bibl.: WH I: 381f, EM 187f., IEW 228f., Leumann 1977: 4851, 488, Cowgill 1985, Coleman 1992: 390-392, Kortlandt 1993: 253, Schrijver 1995: 331, Sihler 1995: 407f., Meiser 1998: 170, Untermann 2000: 192-194. → bi-3 bis, dis, dubius; U. duti

<!-- pdf-page: 198; printed-page: 184; running-header: 184                                      dOrus -->



### durus
durus 'hard, solid, endurable' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: duriter 'harshly' (Enn.+), duritia 'hardness' (Pl.+), dttritudn 'insensibility' (Cato), diiracinus 'having ^hard berry or fruit* (Cato+), duramen 'hard growth' (Lucr.+), durare 'to harden; hold out, endure' (Pl.+); obdurare 'to be persistent' (Pl.+), obdurescere 'to become hard' (Pl.+), perdwrare 'to hold out' (Pl.+).

Pit. *duro-.

PIE *du(e)hrro- 'far, long'.

IE cognates: Skt. dura- 'far, distant', Av. diirat 'from afar', duire 'far', OP duraiy 'far and wide', duvaistam 'very long, very far'; Gr. δηρός 'long, too long', Dor. δαρός, mostly δηρόν, δάρόν [adv.] 'long' < *dueh₂ro-, Arm. erkar 'long (in time)' < *dueh₂ro-. The meaning of durare must have evolved from 'be hard' to 'endure, last'. The difference in root ablaut between

PIE *duh₂-ro- (Ilr., Latin) and *dueh₂-ro- (Greek, Arm.) is explained by Vine 2002 from the original substantival value of the latter. He adduces textual evidence from Homer to show that δηρός was actually a noun δηρόν 'extent, duration1, and this seems convincing.

Bibl.: WH I: 384-386, EM 188f, IEW 219f., Schrijver 1991: 232, Vine 2002: 340-342. → dudum



### U. duti [adv.]
U. duti [adv.] 'for the second time\

Pit. *du-tjo-m.

IE cognates: Skt. dvitfya- 'second', OAv. daibitiia-, YAv. bitiia- 'second', abbitlm [adv.] 'two times' < Pllr. *dwi-t(i)ja-\ ToA ννόΥ, ΤοΒ wate 'second' < *dui-to~.

Pit. *du-tjo- probably replaces

PIE *dui-tio-, cf. U. dupursus 'biped' for *dui-pod~. The suffix was probably adopted from *tri-tio- 'third', shown by U. tertio- and Latin tertius. A similar formation arose independently in Ilr.

Bibl.: WH I: 376, EM 188, IEW 228ff., Untermann 2000: 193f. → Lat. dud

## Ε



### ebrius
ebrius 'drunk' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: ebriacus 'intoxicated' (Lab.), ebriolus 'tipsy' (Pl.), ebriolatus 'id.' (Lab.).

Pit. Vjf "*-·

PIE *h₁egʷʰ-ro- or *h,eh!gwh-ro- (or *h,(o)gwh-r-o-) 'drinking'.

IE cognates: Hit. eku^/aku- /?egw-, ?gw-/ 'to drink, drink to, toast', Pal. ahu- 'drink', CLuw. w-, 2s.pr.act. iittis 'drink(?)\ HLuw. inf.gen. BIBERE-u-na-sa 'drink'; Gr. νήφω 'to be sober', Dor. ναφε (να- is of secondary origin, cf. Weiss 1994: 97) < *negʷʰo < ^ne-h^h^-e/o- 'not-drink'; ToAB yok- 'to drink', yokiye ~ yoko [n. f.] 'thirst; desire' < PTo. *yok- < *yekʷ- <

PIE */i,e-/r,gH*-. Ebrius can be derived from

PIE *hIeg'vh- 'to drink'. The phonetic development of

<!-- pdf-page: 199; printed-page: 185; running-header: edo                                          185 -->

*-gʷ r- to Latin -br- is also attested in febris. Weiss (1994) posits *hjeg -r-io- « *hjegʷʰ-r-o-J derived from a PIE r/n-stem which would also be continued in Greek νήφων, -όνος (whence the verb νήφειν). Long *e would be due to Narten ablaut in the verb. But there is no long vowel in Hittite (which continues a normal root present), and, in general terms, the long vowel may just as well reflect reduplication: *hiehig"vh-, cf. Schrijver 1991: 139. Since we do not in principle expect reduplication in a ro-derivative, Latin ebrivs must have its e- from a disappeared verb form.

Bibl.: WH I: 387f., EM 190, Schrijver 1991: 54, 139, LIV *h,egwh-. → sobrws



### ebulus
ebulus 'dwarf elder, danewort (a plant)' [f. o] (Cato+; also ebulum)

Pit. *eplo-.

PIE *hiedʰ-l(o> 'kind of tree\

IE cognates: OPr. addle, Lith. egle 'spruce, fir'; Ru. el\ QCzJed! 'spruce, fir' [f. i\ , 0?o. jedl [i.]Jedla < BSl. *edli- < PIE hiedh-li- 'pine'; maybe with root ablaut Gaul, odocos 'elder' (borrowed into Gm. > OHG attuh, attah 'dwarf-elder, danewort').

Bibl.: WH I: 388f., EM 190, IEW 289f.



### ecce
ecce 'look!' [interj.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: eccum, eccds, ecca, eccam, eccas 'here he/she/they (is/are)' (Pl., Ter.); ecqui 'any?' (Pl.+), ecquid 'is it true that? whether' (Pl.+), ecquis 'is there anyone who?' (Pl.+). '> ^

Pit. *ek + *ke.

It. cognates: Sabellic *ek(e)~ in Pael. ecuc, O. ek(iik) , U. eso [nom.sgX] 'this' < *afo, *eka [nom.acc], *ekso, -a [obi.]. A combination of

PIE *h₁e- 'he' (see s.v. is, eoy id) extended by means of *£, plus *-fe (see s.v. -ce). Originally, the Italic combination *ek(e) was uninflected; it became an inflected pronoun in Sabellic. In Latin, it was reinforced with *-ke9 and originally remained uninflected. The gendered forms eccum etc. are explained from *ecce + *hom etc. by WH, but it cannot be excluded that these are simply inflected forms of what was originally an uninflected demonstrative; cf. the same process in iste and ipse. The older etymology of ecce as *edrke is unlikely, since Latin and PIE had no n. pronoun *ed± only *#/.

Bibl.: WH I: 390, EM 190f„ IEW 281ff., Schrijver 1991: 33, Untermann 2000: 217f. → -ce\ is, ea, id,



### edo, esse
edo, esse 'to eat' [v. irr.: edo, es, est, edimus, estis, edunt; estur; inf. esse, pf. edi, ppp. esum] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: edax 'voracious' (Pl.+), edacitas 'voracity' (Pl.+), edo 'glutton' (Varro), edulia, -ium [n.pl.] 'eatables' (Afran.+)7 ellum 'spoon' (gloss.); esca 'food; bait' (Andr.+), escarius [adj.] 'of food' (Pl.+), inescare 'to entice with bait' (Ter.+); esitare 'to feed on' (Pl.+); esus, -us 'the eating' (Novius+); esurire 'to be hungry' (Pl.+), esurio 'a hungry man' (Pl.), esurigo 'hunger' (Varro), esurialis 'of famine' (Pl.+); inedio 'starvation, fasting' (Pl.+); adesse 'to eat into' (Lucr.+), ombesse 'to consume' (Pl.+), ambestrix 'gluttoness' (Pl.), comesse 'to eat up, spend' (Pl.+), comedo 'a glutton' (Lucil.+), exesse 'to eat up' (Pl.+), exesor 'which eats away'

<!-- pdf-page: 200; printed-page: 186; running-header: 186                                      egeo -->

(Lucr.), obesus 'fat, thick' (Laev. apud Gell.+\ peresse cto eat up' (Pl.+), subesse 'to eat away below' (Pl.+); vescus 'thin, attenuated' (Lucil.+).

Pit. *ed- 'to eat\

It. cognates: O. edum [inf.] 'to eat'; maybe also U. ezariaf [acc.pl.] 'certain oblation' < *ed-es-asio-*>

PIE *h₁ed-mi, *hid-enti [pr.] 'to eat', pf. *h,e-hid-.

IE cognates: Olr. ithid> ith 'eats', eihait 'they eat' < *ed-, sb. estir< *ed-; MWjs 'eats' (denominal); Hit. ed~zt / ad-, Pal. ad-, CLuw. ad-/ad- 'to eat', HLuw. ad-, Skt. admi, atti, adanti 'id.', Gr. έδω c to eat, devour', inf έδμεναι (Η.), fut. έδομαι, Arm. owtem, OLith. edmi 'to eat', Lith. isti 'feeds' (of animals), eska 'food', OCS jasti 'to eat', Is. jamb, Ss.jastb < hied-tei, jadb 'poison' < *h₁ed-o-i Go. itan9 OHG ezzan, OIc. eta < *ed-e/o-, ToB matsts- "to starve", ToA natsw- < PTo. *natsw- <

PIE *n-h₁d-tu-ie/o-, ToByesti 'food, meal'. Long e in the present paradigm and in esus is due to Lachmann's law in the sequences *edtr (> ess-) and *eds-. Lat. ellwn < *edlo-9 esca < *edr$k-a. The derivational basis for edulis is unknown. According to Risch 1954, esurire was formed as an antonymous formation to *satur-Tre 'to be satisfied', giving rise to the class of desideratives in -urlre. Like Keller 1982: 88f, I doubt whether the adj. vescus is related to vescor, since the meaning can only be connected via a complicated chain of shifts. One may alternatively suggest a compound of ve- (taken from vesanus^ vegrandis) 'unlike, undercover-' and *esko- 'nourishing' or *eska- 'food'. For PIE, the assumption of a long vowel in the root (as per Meiser 1998 and LIV) is unnecessary: the long vowel in Latin is due to Lachmann's Law, the long vowel in Lithuanian to Winter's Law. In Hittite, the normal root present ablaut is found. For Old Irish, Schumacher's assumption (2004: 378) of an original ablaut *firf- : *edr is not compelling, as the extant forms can also be explained on the basis of original *ed- in sg. and pi. (Kortlandt 2007: 137).

Bibl.: WH I: 392f., EM 191f., IEW 287ff., Leumann 1977: 350, 528f., 557, Schrijver 1991: 35, 135, Sihler 1995: 543, Meiser 1998: 99, 223, Untermann 2000: 197, 245, LIV *hied-. -^prandium



### egeo
egeo 'to need, want' [v. II] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: egertus 'lacking, in need of (Pl.+), egestas 'extreme poverty' (Pl.+); indigere 'to need, lack' (Pl.+), indigent [acc.sg. of *indigis] 'bereft' (Pac. apud Cic), indigus 'needy' (Lucr.).

Pit. *egos, -es- 'need, necessity'; *ege- 'to lack'.

It. cognates: O. egmo [nom.sg.], egrnfas] [gen.sg.], egmad [abl.sg.], egmazum [gen.pl.] 'case, question' < *eg-md-,

PIE *h₁eg-es- 'lack, need'.

IE cognates: OIc. ekla 'lack', OHG eko-rodo 'only'; ToAByak- 'to neglect, be careless about', ToB sb.yaknantar 'they must neglect'. The noun eges-tas and the adj. egenus < *egesno- betray an old s-stem *egos, -es-. The compounds *indigis and indigus < * end-ego- contain *endo c in\ Untermann suggests that O. *egma- 'case, question4 may be derived from 'what one needs', hence from the root of 'to want, need' attested in egeo. The pair egere : *egos- matches part of the 'Latin Caland system' as described by Nussbaum 1999a. Either of these forms may be old. The root etymology and the cognates are not tremendously convincing, Tocharian 'to neglect' seems to match best. Hit. ak-V akk- 'to die, be

<!-- pdf-page: 201; printed-page: 187; running-header: Ο. eitiuvam                                   187 -->


killed' is reconstructed as *Ho(k?-ei, trffi-enti to a root *h}/3e(&- by Kloekhorst
killed' is reconstructed as *Ho(k?-ei, trffi-enti to a root *h}/3e(&- by Kloekhorst (2008: 167f.), and must thus be separated. Latin also continues a (probable) root *hieg- in aio cto say'; it is possible to derive the meaning 'case, question' (O. *eg-ma-)from'to say', but a connection of cto say' and 'need' (Latin *egos-) is much more difficult.

Bibl.: WH I: 394, EM 192, IEW 290, Schrijver 1991: 35, Untermann 2000: 198, LIV ?*hie(g)H-, → aid



### egO CF
egO CF [pron. pers. Is. nom.] (VOLat+; Axdea, vase inscr. (CIL 474) ego; ego Pl.+)

Pit. *ego.

It. cognates: Ven. ego, Fal. eqo, eko, eco T , SPic. eku Τ (?).

PIE *h₁eg, *h₁eg-(o)H, *hleg-H-om T .

IE cognates: Hit uk; Skt. aham, OAv. az§m, as-cft, YAv. az9m, OP adam < hiegHom; Gr. έγω, Lesb. ep. Dor. also έγών, Lac. Tarent έγώνη < *hseg- + -e/oH; Arm. es; OPr. es, as, Lith. as, OLith. es, Latv. es, OCS αζτ>, Ru./α, ORu, (j)azb < BSl. *e?z(-um); Go. ik, OHG ih, OS /c, Runic ek, ik, OIc. ek7 end. -ika < PGm. *ekan; ToA wo? [m.], huk [f.J, ToB Has T . The stem ^ e g - without enlargements is found once in OAv. as, in Baltic, and maybe in Hittite. Elsewhere, either a suffix yielding long -δ was added (Latin, Greek), or a suffix yielding aspiration of the velar in Sanskrit and an ending *-om in Ilr., Sl. and Gm. Probably, several variants of this pronoun existed side by side in PIE.

Bibl.: WH I: 395, EM 192f.,«EW 291, Giacomelli 1963: 244, Lejeune 1974: 333f., Schrijver 1991: 36, van der Staaij 1995: 140, Sihler 1995: 368f, Beekes 1995: 207, Meiser 1998: 156, Untermann 2000:340, Kloekhorst 2008:111-115. -* me



### IL eikvasatis [abl.pl.], eikvasese [abl.pl. + postpos. -en]
IL eikvasatis [abl.pl.], eikvasese [abl.pl. + postpos. -en] 'gathering, meeting'. May reflect an adj\ /eikwlssati-/, while eikvases- may represent a noun /eikwlssi-/. The forms probably go back to a compound with a second element *wa$si- and *wasso- (> *wassato-). Meiser 1986 interprets eikvasatis as 'containing a formula', and etymologizes eik- as *agiko- 'speech, formula' and vasi-< *wassi- < *h₂u(e)d-ti- 1 speaking' (to Skt. vadati). Yet this does not explain the -a-, and is furthermore tautological. Weiss 2007a: 367-369 suggests that the second elements *-wassi- and *-wassati- derive from a //-abstract *wassl· < *ueh₂dʰ-ti- and a /o-verbal adjective *wasso- < *ueh₂dʰ-to- to the root of vado 'to go', cf. Lat. con-ven-tio, con-ven-tus, con-gressus for the semantics. The first element eik- could reflect, according to Weiss, *V[_iH!ck]Ke/iKV-J but he finds no good candidates for etymologizing this part. The sequence -KV- could reflect */co- 'together', as in contio and maybe curia, U. ko-, kom-. This would leave initial *Vf_backjKe/i- to be explained: it could be etymologized as *hi/2eg-io- 'speech', as in U. aiu [nom.pl.] (Meiser 1986: 205). The cp. would then reflect

Pit. *ajjo- + *ko-wassi-.

Bibl.: Meiser 1986:250fi, Untermann 2000:204-206. → aio, vado



### O. eitiuvam, eituam
O. eitiuvam, eituam [acc.sg,], eituas [gen.sg.], ei(tuo) [nom.sg.], eitiuvad [abl.sg.]; Marr. eituam 'money' [acc.sg.] < *eitua- [f.]. Etymology uncertain. Possibly to *h,ei- cto go': cthat which goes' > 'livestock' > 'money' (cf. pecunia). Semantically, a connection with Lat. utor < *oit- would be

<!-- pdf-page: 202; printed-page: 188; running-header: 188                                     emo -->

attractive, but if this root indeed goes back to

PIE *h₃eit-, there is no way in which Sab. *eit- could be justified.

Bibl.: Untermann 2000: 211-213.

emo, -ere cto take (Paul, ex F.), buy (Pl.+)? [v. Ill; pf. emi, ppp, emptum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: (1) em particle chere you are', emox 'fond of buying' (Cato+), empticius 'obtained by purchase' (Varro+), emptor cbuyer' (Pl.+), empturvre cto hanker after buying things' (Varro), emptus, -Us 'the purchasing' (Varro); maybe ellum 'there he is!' (Pl., Ten) and ellam 'there she is!' (Pl.) is from *em-illu/am; (2) Compounds based on the meaning 'to take': adimere 'to remove, take away' (Naev,+), comere (pf cdmpsf) 'to adorn, arrange' (Pl.+), comptus, -us 'adornment' (Lucr.+), demere, pt dempsi 'to remove' (Pl.+), demptio 'removal' (Varro), vindemia 'vintage' (Pl.+), vindemitor 'grape-picker' (Naev.), vindemiator 'grape-picker' (Varro+), virgidemia 'a harvest of rods' (Pl.+), dirimere 'to pull apart, separate' (Lucr.+), eximere 'to take out, omit' (Pl.+), eximius 'excepted; exceptional' (Pl.+), exemptio 'removal' (Varro+), exemplum cexample' (Pl.+), exemplar, -oris 'typical instance' (Lucr+), interimere 'to kill, destroy' (Pl.+), perimere 'to destroy' (Cato+), praemium 'payment, reward, booty' (Pl.+), praemiosus 'profitable' (Cato+), praemiator 'who collects rewards' (Naev,), promere, pf prompsi 'to bring out, forward' (Pl.+), promptus, -us 'the action of maming available' (Lucr.+), promus 'steward' (Pl.+), suppromus 'assistant steward' (Pl.), promptdre 'to be in charge, be steward' (Pl.), promptarius 'that serves for storing' (Pl.+), expwmere cto bring out, reveal', sumere, pf suremiy surempsit (Andr. apud Paul, ex F.) and sumpsT 'to take up, seize' (Andr.-f-), sumptio cthe taking' (Cato+), sumptuosus 'expensive, lavish* (Pl.+), sumptus7 -us 'spending, expense' (Pl.+); absumere 'to use up' (Pl.+), obsumedo 'act of squandering' (Pl.), assumere 'to take, add' (Varro+), consumere cto consume, destroy1 (Cato+), resumere 'to pick up again, put on again' (Enn.+); (3) Compounds based on the meaning 'to buy': coemere 'to buy' (Ten+), compiionalis 'suitable for purchase in batches' (Pl.+); redimere 'to buy back; make good' (Pl.+), redemptitare 'to redeem1 (Cato), redemptor 'contractor* (Cato-f).

Pit. pn *eme/o-, pf. *em- cto take'.

It. cognates: U, emantur, emantu [3p.pnsb.ps.], emps (est) [3s.pf.ps.], O. em mens [3p.pf act] cto buy, take'; O. peremust [3s.fut.pf ] 'to receive', pertumum [inf ], pertemest [3s.fut,], pertemust [3s.fut.pf.] 'to prevent', U. sumtu [3sJpv.II] 'to grab' < *em-e/o-.

PIE pr. *hiem-e/o- cto take', pf. *hie-*h₁m-.

IE cognates: Olr. arfoim cto receive, allow' < *ari-wo-em-9 3s.sb.reL ar-afoimo < *ari-wo-em-y pret. arfoet < PCI. *ew-o-; OPr. Tmt, Lith. imti, 3s.pr. imay 3s.pret ime, Lstvjemt 'to take', OCS jpu Is- img, Rxx.jat' (dial.) 'to take, begin', ORxxJati < BSl. *im- <

PIE *h,m-. The pel. em < ipv,sg. *eme 'take!'. The pf in -(p)si has secondarily been created in those roots which did not synchronically seem to belong to emere anymore, such as demere. The pf suremit 'sumpsit' and surempsit 'sustulerit' are of uncertain origin: they might continue *suz-em- < *subs-em~, but they may also have introduced *sus- more recently on the model of other verbs, such as suscipio. The long vowel in emptus may be phonetic (as in front of net in iunctus), but there is no other example;

<!-- pdf-page: 203; printed-page: 189; running-header: endo                                      189 -->

in theory, e may stem from the perfect. Lat. praemium < *prai-em-io-, vindemia < *wino-de~em-ia 'wine-reaping'. The forms promus* praemium and vindemia point to an agent noun *emo- 'who takes'. The noun exemplum < *ex-em-lo- 'what is taken out, highlighted',

Bibl.: WH I:400f, 426, II: 354, 630, EM 195, 205, 531,666f, IEW 301f, Leumann 1977: 209, Sihler 1995: 75,205, Untermann 2000: 219-221, LIV *h,em-, → pomus



### en
en 'ever?; behold!* [interj.] (Pl.+; in OLat. en only occurs in en umquam 'ever')

PIE *hien?

IE cognates: Gr. ην, ήήν [interj.] 'hey!'. Interjection which calls the attention. At least a part of the Latin attestations, esp. from Verg. on, will represent the identical Greek word ήν. According to Leumann, en derives from *est~ne 'is it not?\ For the OLat usage with umquam, this seems quite conceivable; the blunt rejection by WH is unmotivated. But it seems doubtful whether all syntactic uses in Lat. can be explained either from *estne orfromthe Greek use of ήν; hence, it remains possible that Latin inherited a particle *en.

Bibl.: WH I:403f, EM 196, IEW 314, Leumann 1977: 93, 209.



### endo, indu(-), indi-
endo, indu(-), indi- 'in, on, to' [pref., postpos.y prep,] (Duenos inscr. ted, endo 'to you', Lex XII endo 'within, onto, thereupon', OLat. endo, indu [prep.] from Ennius to Cicero, as an archaism) > *

Derivatives: indoles, -is [fj 'nature' (Pl.+); indotuetur 'watches' (Enn.); induere 'to put on' (Pl.+); indugredior /ingredior' (Lucr.); indupedire 'to hinder' (Lucr.); induperator 'imperator' (Enn., Lucr., Juv.), induperare 'imperare' (Enn.); industrius 'diligent' (Pl.+); induvolare 'involare' (Enn.); indigena [f] 'native' (Verg,+); indigere 'to need, require' (Pl.+), *indigis 'bereft of (Pac), indigus 'needy' (Lucr.); indiges 'native (deity)' (Verg>); indipisco/or 'to overtake, acquire' (Pl.+).

Pit. *<^rfo'in, into'.

PIE *h₁(e)n-do 'inside'.

IE cognates: OIn and, 'in it', Hit. anda 'into, within', andan 'within, inside',. CLuw. anta 'into', HLuw. anta 'within', Lye. hie 'inside' < *hindo(m); Gn ένδον 'inside, at home'. Word-final short Vo after a heavy first syllable can only be explained as a retention of

PIE *-o, since iambic shortening does not apply. It must be assumed that the shift of *~o > *-e (whence indi-gena; cf. 2s.ipv.med. -re < *so) was relatively recent. Sommer 1914 argues that ~o was also supported by Greek reminiscences (like ένδον). Leumann (1977: 562) assumes that endo altogether was borrowed from Greek, first into indigena as a caique on Gr. ενδογενής, then becoming separated. This seems unlikely in view of the recent date of indigena, and because of the generally archaic look of the words in which indi/u- occurs. The vowel raising of endo to indo can be explained as part of the general raising of *e in front of nasal + consonant, or from pretonic vowel reduction, as must in any case be assumed for in 'in'.

Bibl.: WH I: 694, EM 312, IEW 181ff., Sommer 1914: 145, Leumann 1977: 561, Schrijver 1991: 58f, Sihler 1995: 40. → -de, egeo, in, indiges, apiscor, -oles,paro

<!-- pdf-page: 204; printed-page: 190; running-header: 190                                     enim -->



### enim
enim 'well!; for' [ptcle.] (Naev.+)

Pit. *eno- 'that one', *enim [acc.sg.].

It. cognates: O. inim, inim, inim, ini, εινειμ, inim, Pael. inim /i(:)ni(:)m/, U. ene, eiye, enem 'and' /enem/ < *enim; in U, only connecting two sentences. The first element also in O. e-tanto. With a different suffix vowel: Pael. inom 'and' < *enom (or spelling error for inim) . U. enu, ennom, enno, enom, eno '(and) then' < *endom < *eno-dom; U. emik 'then' < *en-do(d)-k(e); U. enumek 'then' < ennom-ek, inumek, inumk, inuk '(and) then' < inom + -ek, or spelling variant for enumek,

PIE *h₁e-no-.

IE cognates: Gr. εκείνος 'that' < *e-ke-eno-. IEW derives enim from the stem of Lith. anas 'that', OCS om> 'he\ Yet I reconstruct this pronoun as *h₂en-. Since Gr. εκείνος seems to contain a stem *en(i/o)-7 Beekes (1995: 202) suggests that *h₂en- was "transformed in *eno-" in Greek. Initial *e-, I presume, would have been taken from the pronoun *hte, *hii 'this' (see Lat is, ea, id). This is possible. Alternatively,

PIE *hje might have acquired a suffix *-HO-, directly yielding

Pit. *e«o. This is reminiscent of Skt. ana [adv.] 'thus', OAv. ana, YAv. ana [ins.sg.] 'with this' (Fischer-Ritter 1991: 10). Since Sabellic also shows evidence for a stem *eno-, this may have been the Pit. stem. The isolated Pit form *enim may point to the earlier existence of pronominal variants with an additional *-z, as found e.g. in Hittite asi / uni / ini 'that (one)' < *h₁os~H, *h₁omH-i, *h₁i+m+i (Kloekhorst 2008: 220f). If interpreted as an accusative of direction, *eni-m can be explained from a semantic shift 'to that' > 'in addition' > 'and'.

Bibl.: WH I: 404f., EM 96, IEW 319ίΤ., Meiser 1986: HOf, Klingenschmitt 1987: 175, Schrijver 1991: 36, Untermann 2000: 225f, 343f. → nam, nunc



### ensis
ensis 'sword' [m. i] (Cato+; almost only in poetry)

Derivatives: ensiculus 'toy sword' (Pl.).

Pit. *ensi- 'sword\

PIE *(h₁e)ns-i- 'sword, large knife'? All the alleged IE cognates are uncertain: the connection with Skt. asi- [m.] 'sword, slaughtering knife' is doubted by EWAia I: 145; in Palaic hasira- 'dagger', there is no trace of a nasal. The appurtenance of Gr. δορ 'sword', allegedly an Aeolic reflex of *«s-r, is also disputed. Morphologically, *ns-(o)r in Greek vs. *ns-i in Latin would be difficult to account for.

Bibl.: WH I: 406, EM 197, IEW 771, Schrijver 1991: 63.



### enubro
enubro 'restraining, prohibitive (in augury)' [adj., dat.sg. o/a] (PauL ex F. 76)

Derivatives: in glossaries OLat. ember [nom.sg.m.J, enibra [nom.sg.f.], enibrum [acc.sg.m.]; inebrae [nom.pLf.j (PauL ex F. 109) 'aves, quae in auguriis aliquid fieri prohibent', inebra [nom.pl.n.] 'omnia, quae tardant vel morantur agentem'.

Pit. *η-χαβ-ο- 'inhibiting'. The meaning suggests a derivation from (the same preform as) inhibed 'to hold back, prevent'. We find once <enubro> and several times <enibr→ (gloss.) and <inebr→ (PauL ex F.). These point to a stem *enn/ibro-, nom.sg.m. *enu/iber. The forms in unraised en- are the oldest, while the vowel quality -u- is determined by the following

<!-- pdf-page: 205; printed-page: 191; running-header: eo                                         191 -->

labial If cognate with *en-habere, the source form was *p-xqfros > *en-hsbros > nom.sg.m. OLat. en-uben In the oblique cases, *a in a closed syllable regularly gave e, hence inebr-. The resulting alternation *enu/iber : *enebr- was levelled in different directions.

Bibl.: WH I: 406, EM 197. → habeo



### eō, īre
eo, Ire 'to go' [v. irr.] (Lex XII, Pl.+). Forms: pr. eo, is, it, imus, Jtis, eunt, ptc. tens, euntis; 3p.pr.ind. obinunt, prodinunt, redinunt (Enn.); pf. IV: if/ w" (both Pl.+), ppp, itum.

Derivatives: tius9 -us 'the going' (Lucr.+); abvre 'to go away' (Naev.+), abitio 'a departing' (Pl.+), abitus, -Us 'departure' (Pl.+), adire 'to approach' (Pl.+), aditio 'the act of approaching1 (Pl.+), aditare 'to approach often' (Enn.), aditus, -lis 'approach, access' (Pl.+), amblre 'to solicit (for); go round' (Pl.+), ombitid 'soliciting, candidature1 (Pl.+), ambitus, -us 'circuit, cycle1 (Varro+), ant(e)Tre 'to surpass; precede' (Pl.+), antidire 'to surpass1 (Pl.), circumire cto go round' (Pl.+), circu(m)itid 'circumlocution, rotation' (Ter.+), circu(m)itus, -us 'cycle1 (Varro+), coire cto come together, have sexual intercourse' (Pl.+), coetus, -us 'encounter, assembly1 (Pl.+), coitid 'meeting, partnership' (Ter.+), exire 'to come out' (Naev.+), exitio 'the going out1 (Pl.), exitus, -us 'departure, conclusion' (Lucil.+), exitium 'destruction, death' (Pl.+), exitiabilis 'causing death' (Pl.+), exitialis 'causing death' (Lucr.), iriire 'to go into; begin' (Pl.+), initare 'to enter habitually' (Pac.), initus, -us 'entry' (Lucr.+), initium 'start, original form1 (Ter.+), initiate 'to admit,;introduce' (Ter.+), intenre 'to die, be lost1 (Pl.+), introire 'to enter' (Pl.+), introitus, -us 'entry' (Lucr.+), oblre 'to meet, visit' (Pl.+), obitus 'a dead person' (Laev.+)> obitus, -us 'approach; death' (Ter.+), perire 'to vanish, perish' (Andr.+), disperlre 'to perish₁ (Pl.+), praeire 'to go in front, dictate' (Pl.+), praetor 'leader, magistrate' (Naev.+), praetorius 'of/by a praetor' (Varro+), praetwra 'the office of praetor' (PL +), praeterire 'to go past, pass' (Pl.+), praeteritus [adj.] 'past' (Lucr.+), prodire 'to come forward, advance1 (Pl.+), redire 'to come back, return' (Andr.+), reditid 'return' (Pl.+), reditus, -us 'return5 (Lucil.+), seditip 'violent discord, rebellion' (Pl.+), seditiosus 'factious1 (Varro+), sublre 'to go -underneath, undergo' (Lex XII+), subitus 'sudden1 (Pl.+), subito 'suddenly1 (Pl.+), subitarius 'requiring prompt action1 (Pl.+), transire 'to cross over, transfer, pass' (Naev.+), transitid 'the passing1 (Varro+); simitu [adv.] 'at the same time, together' (Pl.+).

Pit. *ei- / *i- 'to go', *ito- 'gone1, *eitu- / *itu- cthe going'.

It. cognates: PaeL eite [2p.ipv.], U. ς^ [2s.ipv.], etu, etu, eetu [3s.ipv.II], etuta, etutu, etuto [3p.ipvJI], est, eest [3s,fiit.], iust [3s.fut.pf], ier [3s,ps.?] 'to go1. With preverbs: U. amprehtu, apretu [3s.ipv.II], ambretuto [3p.ipv.II], amprefu<u>s [3s.futpf], ambrefurent [3p.fut.pf] 'to lead the way' (< *ambi-prai-); U. enetu, enetu [3s.ipv.II], SPic. enet [3s.pr.?] 'to start' (*ew-); U. daetom est, peretom est [3s.pf.ps.] 'is neglected'. From *el· [pr.], *eise- [fut.], *i-us-e- [fut.pf], *>- [futpf. of'to be'].

PIE pr. *hjei-/*hji- cto go'.

IE cognates: ?Celtib. (ne-)ito 'must not go', Olr. ethae*, eth 'someone went1, doeth 'someone came' < *i-to-\ Hit. i-\ CLuw. HLuw. /- 'to go' < *hiei-/*hii-, Hit iianna- /iianni- 'to march', Hit. ie/a-na(n} 'to go, come, walk1

<!-- pdf-page: 206; printed-page: 192; running-header: 192                                    epulum -->


< *hii-ie/o-, paii-2*
< *hii-ie/o-, paii-2* 'to go, pass\ HLuw. pa- *to go' < *hjpoi + *hi(e)i-, we-" /uwa- 'to come' < *h₂ou + *h₁(e)i-; Skt. pr.3s. eti, 3p. yanti 'to go\ pf iyaya, YAv. aeiti 'id/; Gr ειμί; OPr. 3s eit, OLith. e/im, OGS iti, Is. ί φ 'to go'; Go. iddja Vent 5 ; ToB j><3m cgoes\ ToKyinc 'they go\ In the pr, Latin and Sabellic seem to have generalized the full grade *£/-; with Dunkel 1998: 97, this can be explained from the use of the sb. *ei-e/o-. In the pf, the form ϊνϊ is recent. Whether the pf ii continues an old pf is disputed, but in view of the limited likelihood that *hji- had a pf. in PIE, it seems more likely that it is a recent formation. The Ennian pL forms in -munt are also recent, probably on the model of danunt 'they give\ Lat. praetor < *prai-itor\ sirmtu < *sem-eitu-. For an explanation of the pr.ptc. iens, euntis, see Beekes 1985. The nouns comes and pedes, -itis probably contain a noun *-is, -itis < *hii-t-, see. s.v. comes. The U. deity puemunes [gen.sg.], puemune [datsg.], Mars, poimunien [datsg.], is one of the two chief honorands of the New Year's festival, as interpreted by Weiss 2007b. He posits *po-ei-mdn 'the goer' or 'who has a going' {*po-ei-mn\ which came to mean '(of the) year', as often happens with words meaning 'to go'.

Bibl.: WH I: 406ff, EM 26, 197ff., IEW 293ff., Leumann 1977: 521, Beekes 1985: 67f, Sihler 1995: 542, Meiser 1998: 222f, Untermann 2000: 207-210, Schumacher 2004: 375f, LIV *hiejk → iter, queo\ comes



### epulum
epulum 'public feast' [n. o] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: epulae [f.pl.] 'sumptuous meal, banquet' (Pl.+), epuldnus 'banqueter' (Paul. exF.), epulari'to dine sumptuously' (Acc.+), coepulonus 'table-companion' (Pl.).

Pit. *ep(V)lo- 'ritual'?

PIE *h₁ep-lo-.

IE cognates: OHG uoba 'festival' (deverbal to uobenl) . There is no hard evidence that epulum referred to a religious or sacrificial banquet: all attestations can mean just 'banquet, large meal for celebrating'. IF derived from a verb, the only good candidate within Latin is apio < *htp- 'to seize': a 'banquet' as an 'opportunity to grab food'. In fact, Nussbaum 1997: 188 has proposed the etymology *hjep-lo-; he sees the same stem reflected in sollemnis. Since the cognate forms (if they are cognate) in Gm. contain a different root vowel, and since it is uncertain that epulum has anything to do with 'taking' or 'seizing', the connection with *htep- 'to take' remains a root etymology* WH and EM connect epulum with ops 'work\ but this is semantically uncompelling, and it has now formally become impossible in view of the reconstruction *h₃ep- for ops.

Bibl.: WH I: 410, EM 199, Schrijver 1991: 36. → apiscor, sollus



### equus
equus 'horse' [m. o] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: equa 'mare7 (Pl.+), equola 'young mare' (Pl.+), eculeus 'foal, pony' (Varro+); equile 'stable for horses' (Cato+), eqmnus 'of horses' (Acc.+); equiso 'horse groom' (Varro+); equaria 'herd of horses' (Varro), Equirria/Ecurria, -drum [n.pl.] 'annual horse races' (Varro+); eques, -itis [m.] 'horse rider; knight' (Pl.+); equitare 'to ride a horse' (Lucil.+), equitotus9 -lis 'cavalry' (Cato+).

Pit. *ekʷo- chorse\

It. cognates: Ven. ekvon [acc.sg.] 'horse', ekvopetaris, ekvopetars,

<!-- pdf-page: 207; printed-page: 193; running-header: erclsco                                     193 -->

equpetars^ ecupetaris, ekupetaris, eppetaris, epetaris [norn.sg.rn.] 'of a horse-rider' < *ekʷo-pet-ari-y presupposing *ekʷo-pei-o- 'hoise-rider' or 'charioteer'.

PIE *hiek-u- 'horse'.

IE cognates: GauL epo-, Olr. ech 'horse'; Hit. *e/c/cw- [c], CLuw. assu- or azzu- [c], HLuw. asu-y Lye. esb- 'horse' < PAnat *Hek-u-; Skt. cava- chorse', Av. aspa-; Myc. i-qo, Gr. ίππος 'horse', ιππότης 'horse rider', Arm. es 'horse'; OPr. aswincm 'horse milk', Lith. asvienis 'stallion'; OE eoh9 OIc. jor, ToB yakwe, ToA yuk 'horse'. The original Latin paradigm must have been *ekos, acc.sg. *ekom, gen.sg. *ekvJ; the stem equ- was generalized from the oblique cases. The noun eques < *ekʷ(o)-it- may have been formed on the model of pedes 'pedestrian' < *ped-it- 'going on foot'. Kloekhorst 2008: 237ff. argues that the Anatolian forms point to an original PIE w-stem *hiek-u- 'horse', which was thematized to *h₁elcuo- in the other branches of IE.

Bibl.: WH I: 412, EM 199f., IEW 301, Lejeune 1974: 334, Pulgram 1976, Leumann 1977: 86, 372, Schrijver 1991: 36.



### er, -is
er, -is 'hedgehog' [m. r] (Pl.+; <irim> Capt. \%4;<erem> Nemes. Cyn. 57)

Derivatives: erTcius 'hedgehog; spiked barrier' (Varro+).

Pit. **er-.

PIE *gʰer- 'hedgehog'.

IE cognates: Gr. χήρ 'hedgehog' (only attested in Hesych), χοίρος 'porcupine' < *gʰorio-,Alb, derr 'pig, swine'. r The form encius suggests that PL· irim is a mistake of the transmission for *erim» In that case, the original stem may have been /er(-i)-/, which can be connected with Greek χήρ if Latin earlier had *her < *^§Γ(-). This may reflect a PIE root noun nom.sg, *gʰer-s to the root *gʰer- 'to be excited, be bristly' found in honor and others.

Bibl.: WH I: 413f., EM 200, IEW 445, LIV *gʰers-. → hircus, hirsutus, hordeum, honor\ horreo



### ercīscō, -ere 'to divide (an inheritance)'
ercīscō, -ere 'to divide (an inheritance)' [v. Ill] (Cic.+; usually only in gdve. erciscundus. Sometimes spelled here-, probably under the influence of heres)

Derivatives: erctum (ciere) 'inheritance, inherited part' (Cic.+), inercta 'indivisa' (Paul, ex K), disertiones 'divisiones patrimoniorum inter consortes* (Paul, ex F. 72) (if *dis-erctiones).

Pit. *erk-i-.

PIE *htrk-i- 'to divide'?

IE cognates: Hit. ark-1 / ark- 'to cut off, divide' < PIE ♦Wtf- / *h,ii£)-. The Latin formation suggests an original present *ercire. According to Eichner 1982: 23, this may be connected with Hit. arki, which would point to a root in *h r . He suggests original *orc- for Latin, which was analogically changed to ere-, LIV, on the other hand, reconstructs a preform *hirk~ie- which would phonetically yield arc- (thus Schrijver 1991: 72f.); in view of existing arceo, it seems unlikely that *arcire would have been changed to *erc-. Hence, the preform for Latin *ercire may have been an /-present ^h^rk-i-. Yet all of this remains rather uncertain. Petit 2004 argues that Lith. arsyti 'to tear up' < *hiOrk- also belongs here,

Bibl.: WH I: 640f., EM 200, Leumann 1977: 536, LIV ?*h₁erk-.

<!-- pdf-page: 208; printed-page: 194; running-header: 194                                       ergo -->



### ergO
ergO 'on account of; therefore' [prep. (+ gen.); ptcL] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: erga [prep. + ace] 'next to, towards* (Pl.+), corgo 'forwards' (Paul. exFm\

Pit. *re/ogo- 'direction'. ·

PIE *h₃re/og-o-.

IE cognates: see s.v. rego. In the collocation *e re/ago 'from the direction of, the middle vowel was syncopated, whence ergo. Lat corgo < *com re/ogo 'with the direction*. Since no other IE language shows a thematic noun *h₃rog-o-, this may be an inner-Italic formation. In that case, the root vowel may also have been *e.

Bibl.: WH I: 273,415, EM 143, 201, IEW 854ff., Leumann 1977: 239, LIV *h₃reg-. -^rego



### βιτδ, -ire
βιτδ, -ire 'to roam; waver* [v. I] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: errantia 'misguided condition* (Ace), errabundus 'wandering* (Lucr.+), erraticus 'wandering, wild* (Cato+), erratio 'the roaming* (Pl.+); error 'doubt; mistake' (Pl.+); aberrare 'to wander away, stray* (Pl.+), deerrare 'to wander off (Pl.+)·

Pit. *ers-qje-.

PIE *hjers-o/h₂- 'erring, error'.

IE cognates: Arm. efarn 'to boil; be restless1 < *ersa-; Go. axnexs 'mistaken', airzjan, OHG irren 'to be mistaken; lead into mistake* < *erz-(i)io-. Both Latin and Armenian seem to continue a denominative verb *ers-qje- 'to be restless* to a noun *erso- or *ers-hr- Although these words are not discussed in LIV, their phonological shape matches

PIE *hjers- 'to flow* (Skt. ar$ati). If the noun meant 'flowing to and fro, uncontrolled flowing', it is conceivable that a derived meaning 'erring, error* arose already in PIE. Nussbaum (2007b) connects the PIE root *hjer- 'to arrive, reach*, and compare the .s/c-present *h₁r-ske/o- (Skt. fcchatU Gr. έρχομαι).

Bibl.: WH I: 416f., EM 201, IEW 336£, Klingenschmitt 1982: 96, Schrijver 1991: 36, LIV *h,ers-.



### Sruca
Sruca 'caterpillar; rocket (kind of herb)' [f. a] (Moretum+; a variant is uruca) The most likely analysis is er-uca. If the basis is indeed the same is er 'hedgehog', the caterpillar was denominated for its rugged back, as it often is. The suffix was probably adopted from a semantically close word, cf. especially verruca 'wart; hillock*.

Bibl.: WH I:417, EM 201, Leumann 1977: 340. → er



### erus
erus 'master, lord* [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: era 'woman, mistress' (Pl.+), erilis 'of a master* (Pl.+).

Pit. *eyo- 'master*.

PIE *hiesh₂-o- 'master*.

IE cognates: Gaul. Esus, Aesus 'important god of the Gauls*; Hit. isha- 'master, lord' [c.]. The most obvious connection is with the Gaulish deity *Esu/o-; Lucian indicated that

<!-- pdf-page: 209; printed-page: 195; running-header: ex                                          195 -->

the e- of the Celtic word equals Latin e, but that does not mean much historically. IEW connects ems with Gn έύς 'good, noble* < *h,su-. If Ilr. *asu- 'life' also belongs to this root, Latin eras might also continue *hies-u- 'the good one' > 'master'. Yet the w-stem would have switched to an o-stem in the prehistory of Latin, which is unusually early. Also, the semantics are not compelling, Hit. isha- would match perfect semantically, as well as phonetically (if the reconstruction of Hit. is correct), and this option is preferred by Schrijver. Still, the word remains isolated within IE.

Bibl.: WH I:419, EM 201f„ IEW 342, Schrijver 1991: 36.



### ervura
ervura 'a kind of cultivated vetch' [n. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ervilia 'cultivated vetch' (Varro+).

Pit. *erVgʷ-o-7

IE cognates: Mir. orbaind 'grains'; Gr. δροβος, Gr, έρέβινΰος 'chick-pea'; OHG araweiz, OS erwit 'peas' < PGm. *arwait-. The Latin, Greek and Gm. words clearly belong together, but a common phonological preform cannot be reconstructed. Since the morphological make-up of these words is different (Gr. -ινθος, Gm. *-a/Y; presence vs. absence of a medial vowel) and the word refers to an autochthonous crop, it may represent a loanword from a non-IE substratum language. >

Bibl.: WH I:419, EM 202, IEW 335, Schrijver 1991: 3f6. / * et 'and what is more, too; and' [cj., adv.] (Lex XII, Andr,+)

Derivatives: etiam 'still, also, even' (Andr.+), etenim 'and indeed' (Pl.+), etsT 'even if (Pl.+).

Pit. *eft\

It. cognates: I I et, et, PaeL et 'and' < *eft\ In U., et connects syntagms, not sentences,

PIE *hieti 'still, also".

IE cognates: Gaul, eti 'yet, still, equally', etic 'and7 < ^eti-k^e; Skt. ati, Av, oiti 'beyond, over, to', Gr. έτι 'still, also, further', NPhr. ετι- 'and'; Go. ip \but', id-weit [n.] 'reproach', OIc. ioja-grcenn 'green again', OHG it(i)-wiz [n.], OE ed-wTt [n.] 'disgrace' < PGm. *ip(i), OHG it-mali 'festive, solemn', MDu. ad/t-mael [n.] 'period of the day', OFr. et-mel [n.] 'half or whole a day', OE ed-mcele [n.] 'feast' < PGm. *id(i). Originally used to add something to a previous utterance, or to indicate the sequence of different actions. Later on, it replaces -que. Possibly,

PIE *hjeti is an old loc.sg. to a root noun from *h}et- 'go beyond' (reflected in Skt. at- 'to travel, wander').

Bibl.: WH I:421, EM 202, IEW 344, Schrijver 1991: 37, Untermann 200: 240.



### ex, δ, ec-
ex, δ, ec- 'out of, away* [prep., prev.] (Lex XII, Andr.+). In composition, we find ex- in front of vowels, o , qu-, /- and s-\ in front of p-, ex- is normal except for epascere and epotare. Lat. has e- < *egz- before voiced b-, d-, g-, /-, m-, n-, r-, i- u-\ Lat. ec- only occurs in compounds front of/-, and even there, it is often assimilated to ef-)

Derivatives: exter(us) 'external; foreign' (Lucr.+), extra [adv., prep.] 'outside, apart from* (Pl.+), extrarius 'external' (Pl.+), extrinsecus [adv.] 'from without; on the

<!-- pdf-page: 210; printed-page: 196; running-header: 196                                     expretus -->

outside' (Cato+); exterior 'outer, outward' (Varro+), externus 'on the outside, extraneous5 (Lucr.+); extimus 'outermost, farthest' (Pl.+)> extremus 'situated at the end, last' (Cato+). %

Pit. *e#, *eks 'out'.

It. cognates: O. eh, U, ehe, eh, e, SPic. eh- [prep. + abl.] 'out'. As a preverb: O. eh-, ee-, U. e-, eh-, eAe-, e-, eh- /e-/ < *e/-. O. ehtrad [prep. + ace] 'outside', U. apehtre 'from outside' < ap- + *ektere(d).

PIE *h₁egʰ, *h,eghs Out'.

IE cognates: Olr. ess-, W. eh- 'out', Olr. echtar, MW eithyr 'outside'; Gr εξ 'from, out of, έσχατος * outmost, last' (< ^ek^s-katos); OPr. is, assa, assae, Lith if, Latv. iz 'from, out of, OCS iz 'out' < BSL */i (*£s) < *htegʰ(s) [*/- of BSL is unexplained]. The original sup. extimus < *ekstamo- was eventually replaced by extremus < *eksterezemo- < *ekster-isamo-. The cp. extrfnsecus was builtfrom*exterim '(to) the outside' and -seats. Sabellic continues the s-less variant of the PIE preposition *hiegʰ which is also found in Gr. έσχατος and in Balto-Slavic (cf Weiss 2002: 356).

Bibl.: WH I: 423f., 433£, EM 203f, IEW 292f., Leumann 1977: 558£, Schrijver 1991: 37, Meiser 1998:117,152, Untermann 2000: 114,199,202, Derksen 2008: 217.



### expretus
expretus 'extinguished (vel sim.)' [adj. o/a] (PL lxBac.446> Paul. exF.)

Pit. *eks-preto- or *eks-spreto-. In the literature, expretus only occurs in Plautus, in the line it magister quasi lucerna / undo expretus linteo. Equating linteus with the wick of a candle, Klingenschmitt (1989: 95) translates the line as follows: 'The teacher withdraws like an oil lamp, extinguished, when the wick is still drenched [sciL with oil]'. Hence, expretus would mean 'extinguished'. K. assumes a word play with linteum, which can also mean 'hair', which, in the given context, may be drenched with blood from a wound inflicted on the teacher, K. assumes that a candle was usually 'extinguished* by means of blowing it out, and hence he suggests that expretus belongs to a root *preh r 'blow', which LIV and others reconstruct for Gr, πίμπρημι. This is conceivable. Yet a derivation from spernere, spretus *to disdain, scom' cannot be dismissed as easily as K. does. He doubts that spernere, which originally meant 'to kick, tread', would have undergone a semantic shift from 'kicked out' to 'blown out'. Yet in view of the sudden extinction of a flame when blown out, I think that such metaphorical usage cannot be excluded.

Bibl.: WH I: 431, EM 206, Klingenschmitt 1989 (= 2005: 269-283), LIV *preh r ? *spherH-? → spernere



### ex(s)ul, -is
ex(s)ul, -is 'banished person' [m. f] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ex(s)ulare 'to be banished' (Pl.+), ex(s)ilium 'exile' (Pl.+) [only <exuI→ and <exil→ in inscr.].

Pit. *ek(-)s(-)Vl- (or derived from a verb in *ek(-)s(-)Vl-). Several etymologies are possible. It might be a derivative of a verb *ex-sulere 'to take out' to the root *selh r 'to take', cf. consul and cdnsulere; hence exsul 'the one who is taken out'. It might belong to amb-ulare < *-al- 'to walk', hence 'who walks out'. It might even belong to *h}elh₂-, the root of Gr. έλαύνω 'to drive5: ex-ul 'who is driven

<!-- pdf-page: 211; printed-page: 197; running-header: faber                                     197 -->

out' (cf. proelium).

Bibl.: WH I: 432, EM 207, Benedetti 1988: 164-169. → ambulo

## F



### faba
faba 'bean' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fabulus 'bean' (Pl.+), fabaginus 'of beans' (Cato)9fabalia 'bean-stalks' (Cato*-), fabalis 'of beans' (Varro+), fabarius 'of/for beans* (Cato+\ fabatus 'made of beans' (Varro+).

Pit. *fqfa- 'bean'.

It. cognates: Fal. haba 'bean' [Velius Longus, 2nd c. AD],

IE cognates: OPr. babo 'bean', Ru. bob; OIc. baun, OHG bona> OE bean 'bean' < PGm. *bauno < *bab-no? Farther removed are Gr. φακός 'lentil' and Alb. bathe 'horse-bean' < *bP}ak-. No PIE word can be reconstructed. Since the Italic, Slavic and Germanic words are similar in form and meaning, they are probably independent loanwords from a European substratum word of the form *bab- (or similar) 'bean'.

Bibl.: WH I: 436, EM 208, IEV{ 106, Schrijver 1991: 488, Kuiper 1995:79f.

faber, ~bn 'craftsman, artisan* [m, ο] (Pl.+) -

Derivatives: fabre 'skilfully' (Pl.+), infabre 'unskilfully' (Pac.+), fabnlis 'of a workman' (Varro+); fabrica 'workshop; craft; plan' (Pl.+)> fabricare/i 'to fashion, build; devise' (Pl.+), perfabricare 'to fashion completely' (Pl.), fabricator 'maker' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *fafro-.

It. cognates: ΡζέΙ. faber [nom.sg.?] is probably a loan from Latin.

PIE *dʰHbʰ-ro- 'craftsman'?

IE cognates: Arm. darbin 'smith' (< *dabr-mo-); Lith. daba 'nature, habit, character*, dabaf 'now\ dabniis 'smart, well-dressed, elegant', OCS podoba 'ornamentation, decency', Ru. doba (dial.) 'time, measure', OCS dobrb 'good, beautifur, Ru. dobryj 'good'; Go. ga-daban 'to happen', pf. gadob 'it fits', gadofist 'it is fitting', OE gedefe 'fitting'. The noun fabrica suggests the earlier existence of a feminine noun to which an adj. *fabriko- referred; maybe ars 'art, craft'. According to Leumann 1977: 337, fabrica was formed after a Greek example, e.g. ναυτική (τέχνη). The closest cognate is Armenian darbin; together with faber, it could reflect

PIE *dʰHbʰ-. The BSl. words meaning 'nature' and 'good' (with short vowel in front of b < *bʰ) and the Gm. words *rfoo-, *dob- could go back to

PIE *dʰobʰ-. Their connection vAih faber is possible, but semantically not compelling, Beekes (1996: 230) argues that, if all these words are cognate, they can only be explained from a non-IE root *<ΛΪ£Λ-,

Bibl.: WH I: 436f, EM 208, IEW 233f., Schrijver 1991: 102, Untermann 2000: 254, LIV *dʰeHbʰ- 'to fit'.

### faciō, -ere
faciO, -ere 'to make' [v. ΠΙ; pLJeci; ppp. factum, sb. faxo, faxim, faxis, pr.ipv.jfac] (VOLat+: Duenos inscr. feced Ifekedi 'fecit', Praeneste (315BC)_/fec/cf, Corchiano ('th c. BC)feced, Lacus Fuciniu&fecront, Yaestumfecere 'fecerunt'; Lex ΧΠ>)

Derivatives: facilis 'easy' (Pl.+\facul 'facile' (Pac., Ace, LuciL), facilitas 'ease' (Ter.+), facultas 'easiness; ability, skill, chance* (Ter>), difficilis 'difficult5 (Pl.+; difficul Varro), difficulties 'difficulty' (Pl.+); fades 'appearance, face' (Naev.+), superficies 'top, surface' (Varro+); facinus, -oris [n.] 'deed, act; crime' (Pl.+); factor 'maker, perpetrator' (Pl.+), factum 'deed' (Naev.+), prqfecto [adv.] 'undoubtedly' (Pl.+), foetus* -iis 'a pressing of olives' (Cato, ¥\in.), factio 'the making; group, party' (Pl.+), factiosus 'busy; factious' (Pl.+); prqficlscor 'to start on a journey, start' (Yl.+);facessoy -ere 'to carry out, perform; depart' (Pl.+);factitare 'to do frequently' (Pl.+); caiefactare 'to heat5 (Pl.+); -fex* -ficis 'maker': artifex 'practitioner of an art, craftsman' (Pl.+), aurifex 'goldsmith' (Pl.+), carnifex 'executioner, murderer' (Naev.+), opifex 'craftsman' (Pl.+), pontifex 'priest' (Cato+); derived from the cp. in -fex are: -ficus 'doing, making', -ficium "the office of»/; afficere 'to have an effect on, influence' (Pl.+), afficficius 'attached' (Varro+), affectare 'to try to accomplish, aspire' (Ter.+), conficere "to do, perform, complete' (Andr.+), confector 'destroyer (Lucil.+), maker (Cic.+)\ deficere 'to be lacking, fail; defect' (Cato+), efficere 'to make, cause' (Pl.+), inficere 'to dye, colour; infect' (Pl.+), infector 'a dyer' (Pl.+), interficere 'to kill, destroy' (Naev.+), afficere 'to stand in the way' (Varraf), perficere 'to bring to an end, achieve' (Pl.+), perficus 'that completes' (Lucr.),perfector 'who brings to completion' {Tzx.+\praeficere 'to put in charge' (Pl.+), praefica "woman who leads the funeral mourning' (Naev.+), praefectio 'appointing as leader' (Varro), praefectura 'command; rank or duty of a praefectus' (Pl.+), praefectus ' p e r s o n in charge; praetor' (Pl.+), prqficere 'to make progress' (Lucil.+),profectio 'departure' (Pac.+), reficere 'to restore, repair' (Lucr.+), sujficere 'to supply, provide; be sufficient' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *fak-i- [pr.], *fek- I *fak- [aor.].

It. cognates: Ven. vhagsto, hvagsto, vhasto [3s.pret] 'made' < *fak-s-to\ FaL faced (Berenguer Sanchez - Lujan Martinez 2004), facet (Wallace 2005) [3s.pf.]; O. fakiiad [3s.pr.sb<], faciud [3s.ipv.II], fe<f>acid [3s.pf.sb.], fefacust [3s*fut.II], facus estud [3s.ipvJLps.in.], Mars, fecront [3p.pf], Mam fec(ed?) [3s.p£], VoLfagia, U. fa?ia, feia [3s.pr.sb.], U. fetu, feitu, fetu, feitu [3s.ipv.II], PalU. face [3s.p£], U. fakust [3s.fut.p£], fakurent, facurent [3p.fut.pf.], fa^iu, fa?u [inf.], fetu [3s.pf.ps.n.?], feta [ppp. acc.sg.L] 'to do, act, sacrifice, make'; Ο. ανα:ίακετ [3s.p£] 'has erected'. O. fakinss [acc.pl.] 'deeds' < *fak-jon~. U. fafefe<I>e [acc.sg.f] 'fit to be sacrificed' < *faki-fli- < *-<///-. Maybe U. anfehtaf [acc.pl.f.]. O. praefucus [nom.sg.] 'praefect' (caique on the Latin term). For Fal. fifiked andffif/iqod, see SM.fingo.

PIE *d h eh r 'to put' [aor.]; *d h h r k- 'to make', *d h h r k-i- [pr.].

IE cognates: Gr, aor. εύηκα Ί put, placed', Phryg. αδ-δακετ "afficif, middle αδ-δακετορ. The Italic evidence mainly continues a root *fctk> pf also *fek- 'to make'. Probably, the root-final k has arisen in the PIE root aonst of *d h eh r , and can be equated with Gr. -κ- in the aor./pf. of these verbs, and with Phrygian αδδακετ(ορ). In Italic, the

<!-- pdf-page: 213; printed-page: 199; running-header: fallo                                     199 -->

stem *fak-y in the pf. also *fek-, was generalized in the whole paradigm. For difficilis see s.v. dis-,

Bibl.: WH I:439-444, EM 209-212, IEW 235ft, Lejeune 1974: 334, Schrijver 1991: 92, Untermann 1993, Untermann 2000: 255-261, Marinetti 2004: 426, LIV *dʰhik-. → -do, -dere; Jecundus; feriae; fetialis



### faex, -cis
faex, -cis 'wine-lees, dregs' [f k] (Cato+)

Derivatives: faecarius 'used for lees' (Cato), faecatus 'made from lees' (Cato), faeceus 'resembling dregs, foul' (Pl.), faecula 'dried lees of wine' (Lucr.+); defae/fcare "to remove the dregs' (Pl.+). No etymology.

Bibl.: WH I:444, EM 213.



### fāgus
fSgus 'beech' [£ o] (Varro+)

Derivatives: jSgweuy 'beech-' (Cato+),jagHtalis 'of the beech-grove' (Varro+).

Pit. *jSgo-,

PIE *bʰeh₂g-o- 'oak* / 'beech'.

IE cognates: GauL *bagos 'beech' (in French place names), Gr. φηγός, Don φάγός [f.] 'kind of oak, acorn', OHG buoh 'written document, book', OHG buohstap 'letter' < *'piece of beech for writing', OIc. bok, OE boc, bece (< *b6kjon-\ OHG buohha 'beech', Go. boka 'letter'. Unrelated: Alb. bung 'kind of oak' and Slav. *huzb 'elder'. The 2x£y fagiitalis presupposes a derivative *fagutumtbeech-grove'. The restriction to western IE languages and the reference to different trees have suggested to some scholars that this word was not PIE, but a later loanword. In the Balkans, from which the beech started to spread after 6000 BC, the Gr. word means 'oak', not 'beech'. Yet 'oak' and 'beech' are both 'fruit-bearing trees', so that a semantic shift from 'oak' to 'beech' appears quite conceivable. The word itself may then have been PIE after all.

Bibl.: WH I: 445, EM 213, IEW 107f., Leumann 1977: 276, Schrijver 1991: 142, Demiraj 1997; 112f, EIEC 58f. ^fraxinus



### falls, «ere
falls, «ere 'to deceive' [v. Ill; pf.fefelti\ ppp. falsum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fallacio 'deceptive behaviour, trick' (?L+)9fattax 'deceitful' (Lucil.+); falsus 'erroneous, untrue' (Lex XI1+), faharius 'a forger' (Cato*); falsidicus 'lying' (Pl.), falsificus 'acting deceitfully' (Pl.), fakiiurius 'swearing falsely' (Pl.), fabiloquus 'speaking deceitfully', falsimonia 'deception' (Pl.); refellere 'to refute' <Ter+).

Pit. *fal-s-e/o- [pr,]> *fa!ns- [pf.].

PIE *sgʷʰh₂(e)l-n- 'to stumble'·

IE cognates: Skt. (Br.+) skhalate [3s.pr.med.] 'to stumble, fair, MP skarwidan 'to stumble, stagger'; Gr. σφάλλω 'to bring down1, σφάλλομαι 'to fall', aor. έσφάλην Ί fell down', Arm. sxalem 'to stumble, fail'. The pn may represent either *fal-d~, *fal-n- or *fal-s~. The pf. fe-felli is a recent formation *fe-fall- on the basis of the pr., as shown by word-internal -f- and by -ellT (instead of -w/i< *- Vl-ai, cf pello - peputt). The original pf. may have been *fal-s-ai; thus Meiser 1998 and 2003. The ppp. can have been formed analogically after e.g.

<!-- pdf-page: 214; printed-page: 200; running-header: 200                                       falx -->

sallo -salsus. The Ilr., Arm. and Gr. forms are very close in meaning and form, and can be connected if Gr. continues a labiovelar. The correspondence is irregular inasmuch as it seems to require a PIE voiceless aspirate stop, but this might be due to an original heavier cluster of consonants in (pre-)PIE, cf. Lubotsky 2006 on

PIE *TsperH- 'to kick with the heeP possibly from *pds-per(H)~. Iffalld belongs here, it has developed metaphorically from 'stumble' to 'deceive' (the tr semantics are explained by the nasal present, as in Greek).

Bibl.: WH I: 447ff, EM 213f, IEW 489f, Leumann 1977: 169, Meiser 1998: 211, 2003: 176f, LIV*(s)g%el-.



### falx, -cis
falx, -cis 'hook, scythe, sickle' [f k\ gen.pl. -ium] (Cato+) Derivatives^/cw/a 'pruning-knife, bill-hook' (Cato+).

IE cognates: Olr. delg [m.] 'thorn, pin', W. dala 'sting' < PCI. *delgo- cpin, needle'; Lith. dilge 'nettle', Lith. dilgus 'stinging, smarting', OIc. dalkr 'pin, spine, dagger, knife1 [m.], OE delg 'clasp', dale 'bracelet'. The suggested connection with Sicilian Ζάγκλη, Δανκλε" 'sickle' (thus still IEW) is too uncertain. EM suspect a loanword origin, which in case of a technical word like this would not be surprising. Semantically, the comparison with Olr. delg, OIc. dalkr seems acceptable, but Latin -al- cannot be derived from a root *c?lg~, nor can word-final k So at most, falx is a borrowing from a non-Latin IE language of Italy.

Bibl.: WH I:449f, EM 214, IEW 247.



### fames, -is
fames, -is 'hunger' [f e » i (gen.sg.famt Cato, LuciL.famis more recent; abhsg. fame, laterfame)] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: famelicus 'starved' (Pl.+). The noun is shifting from the fifth to the third declension type. Lat famelicus presupposes an earlier adj. *famelis 'hungry'. WH and, hesitatingly, IEW, reconstruct *dʰH-m-, connecting Latin fatfgo> Olr. ded- 'to vanish', and OIc. dasi 'slow', MiE dasen 'to deafen, daze'. Yet this comparison is too vague, both semantically and formally. The formation offam-e- might point to a root *fam~.

Bibl.: WH I: 451, EM 215, IEW 239, Leumann 1977: 444ff, Schrijver 1991: 381, Sihler 1995: 342. ^faiigd

fSmexAix, -icis 'swelling, abcess' [m.] (Col.; acc.sg.famicem) Etymology unknown,
