### flos, -ris blossom, flower'
flos, -ris blossom, flower' [m. s] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: Flora 'the goddess of flowers' (Varro+), floralia, -ium [n.pl] 'flower-gardens' (Varro+), florere £to blossom, bloom' (Cato+), florens, -ntis prosperous' (Pl.+), florescere 'to begin to flower' (Varro+), floridus 'flowery' {Vano+);florifer 'producing flowers' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *flos{-) 'flower', *flos-a- 'goddess of flowers'.

It. cognates: Vest, flusare, [abl.sg.rn.] month name, from *flos-ari- 'of Flora'; O. fluusai [dat.sg.] the goddess 'Flora'; O. fOuusasiais [dat.abl.pL] 'Floralia', feast of Flora < *flds-asio-.

PIE *bʰleh₃-os 'blossoming' > 'flower'.

IE cognates: Olr. blath, W. blawd 'flower, bloom', OCo. blodon, LCo. bledzhan 'flower' < *blatu- <

PIE *bʰleh₃-tu-, MBret. blezuy Bret, bleunv 'flowers' < *blatmV-; OHG bluowen, OE blowan 'to bloom' < *blojan-, Go. bloma, NHG Blume < *blo-m- 'flower'. The derivation of Flora from flos is reminiscent of aurora to earlier *ausos> Oscan fluusa- shows that the cult of this goddess was known more widely among the Italic

<!-- pdf-page: 242; printed-page: 228; running-header: 228                                       fluo -->

peoples. The ablaut grade of the root in the PIE s-stem cannot be determined with certainty.

Bibl.: WH I: 518f., EM 241, IEW 160, Sohrijver 1991: 131, 1995: 179, Sihler 1995: 310, Untermann 2000: 290-293, Stuber 2002: 76, LIV *bʰleh₃-. ^flavus, flows



### fluō, -ere
flud, -ere 'to flow, run (of waters)' [v. Ill; pf.fluxT, ppp.fluctum (younger flwatm)\ inscr. (CIL 584) conflouont] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fluentum *a stream' (Lucr.+), flu(u)idus 'flowing, liquid* (Lucr.+); flu(u)itare/fliitare 'to flow, run' (Lucr.+); fluctus, -us 'wave, billow' (Pl.+Xfliictidare 'to surge, be in agitation' (Pl.+), fluctuosus 'agitated' (?L+);fluctifragus 'that breaks the waves' (Lucr.); flumen 'river, stream' (Enn>); fluvius 'river* (Naev.+;/7zra72 [f.] Ace. Sis.); flustra, -drum 'calm (of the sea), swell' (Naev.+); qffluere 'to flow towards; be rich' (Laev.+), afluere 'to flow away; abound in' (Pl.+), confluere 'to flow together, assemble' (Pl.+), confluvium 'place where streams meet' (Varro), confluges 'meeting place of rivers1 (Andr.; conflages in Paul, ex F. may be a mistake for *confluges\ defluere 'to flow down, away' (Cato+), diffluere 'to flow away in all directions, dissolve' (Ter.+), effluere 'to flow out, escape* (Cato+), mfluere lto flow in' (Varro+), perfluere lto stream' (Ter.+), perfluctuare 'to flood over' (Lucr.+), profluere "to flow forth, overflow' (Naev.+), profluvius 'fluctuating' (Caecil.), prafluvium 'a discharge' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *flow-e/o-.

PIE *bʰleuH-(e/o-) 'to flow (over)\

IE cognates: Gr. Att. φλέω 'to abound*, φλύω 'to seethe, boil over'; Lith. bliauti, 3s. bliauna 'to bleat, sob, weep', Latv. bjaut 'to bleat, bellow', OCS bltvati, Is. b\ujQ 'to vomit', Ru. blevat' (vulg.) <

PIE *bʰleuH-, Leumann regards the spelling/7w- as irregular for *flou-y having been introduced from compounds where *-flou- was in non-initial syllable. Leumann 1977: 279, 436 explains fluentum as a singularized form of a n.pl. fluenta to fluens, -ntis. The verb form conflouont points to a pr.stem *fluu- < *flou-y but confluges and the pf. fluxi seem to require a stem *flug-. Hence, Meiser reconstructs *bʰleugʷ-. Yet, as EM have already pointed out, PIE labiovelars lost their labiality after *-u-. Weiss 1994 shows that bothfluxi and the noun confluges can easily be explained as secondary creations by means of proportional analogy, cf. contomen : contages for confluges, and struo : struxi fovfluxh The same goes ioxfluctus.

Bibl.: WH I: 519£, EM 241f, IEW 158f, Leumann 1977: 135, Sihler 1995: 214, 583, Weiss 1994: 139f, Meiser 1998: 194, 208, LIV L*bʰleuH-,



### focus 'hearth, fireplace'
focus 'hearth, fireplace' [m. ο] (Pl.+) Den\2iuvts:foculus 'small stove' (Cato+). A connection with Lst.fax 'torch* and Lith. zvake 'candle* is formally impossible, since Pre-Lat *fwakV- would not yield foe-. Any etymology positing

PIE *Dh-k(w) would violate the PIE root structure constraints. Hamp 1992 proposes to explain foais as a back-formation to foculus, which he explains from *fweklo- < *ctgʷʰ-e-tlo~ 'hearth'. Yet this is chronologically difficult: initial *g"* >

Pit. * / V is expected to yield *fe-9 Mid could only yield *fo- if the simplification of *#w- > fl were dated after *-we- >

<!-- pdf-page: 243; printed-page: 229; running-header: foeteo                                      229 -->

*-wo-, which happened in the fifth c. BC. But that is rather late for the change *χ"- >f.

Bibl.: WH I: 521, EM 243, IEW 495, Hamp 1992.



### fodio, -ere
fodio, -ere 'to pierce; to dig' [v. IH; pf fodi, τρρρ. fossum;fodiri Cato lx] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fodicare 'to stab, prick' (Pl.+); fossa 'ditch, trench' (Cato+), fossula 'small trench' (C2&o+),fossTcius 'obtained by digging' {Vdno+),fossilis 'obtained by digging* (Varro+); circumfodere 'to dig round' (Cato+), confodere 'to dig up; pierce* (Pl.+), defodere 'to bury, insert in the ground' (Pl.+), effodere/ecfodiri 'to dig up, gouge out' (Pl>), Jnfodere 'to place in the earth' (Cato+), interfodere *to pierce* (LuCT.),perfodere 'to make a hole through' (Pl.+), perfossor 'burglar' (Pl.+).

Pit. *fop-ir.

PIE *bʰodʰ-i- 'to poke, dig'.

IE cognates: HiLpadda- /padd- 'to dig (the ground)7 < *bbodʰh₂-; OPr. boadis 'stab* [m.], embaddusisi 'they stick', Lith. besti, 3s. beda "to stick, drive (into), dig', Latv. best 'dig, bury', Lith. badyti, Latv. badit 'to butt, prick*; OCS bosti, Is. bodp, Is.aon bast Ho stab', SCr. bosti, bodem < BSl. *bed-, *bod^; ToApatar 'they ploughed* < *bʰodʰ-. The long -<5- in the pf. is probably analogical to pr.fodr, for instance on the model of venio : vera. Since all languages show verb formations with c?-grade (Hit, Latin, BSL, Toch.), the original formation may have been iterative or intransitive: *bʰodʰ-(i-). The Latin z-stem conjugation might^be a rest of. the PIE suffix, which surfaces as *-ie/o- in this category in other branches of,IE.

Bibl.: WH I: 521, EM 243, IEW 113f., Leumann 19y7: 568, Schrijver 1991: 411, Meiser 1998: 212, LIV *bʰedʰh₂-.



### foedus
foedus 'foul, unclean; fearful, repugnant' [adj\ o/a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: foedare 'to wound, dishonour, make unclean' (Pl.+).

Pit. *fo(j)ipo- 'afraid5.

PIE (*bʰe-)bhoiH-/bhiH- [pf] or *bʰoH-i- [pr.] «to be afraid'.

IE cognates: Skt. bibhaya 'is afraid', ptc. bibhivams-, YAv. bifihma 'afraid'; OPr. biatwei, Lith. bijoti(s) , Latv. Mies Ho fear, be scared', OCS bojati s? 'id.' < BSl. *b(o)i(?)-a?-; OHG bibet (< PGm *bi-bai~) [3s.pr.act.] 'to tremble' (from an old pf). If the original meaning was 'awful, frightful, terrible* (thus surmised by WH, and reiterated by Nussbaum), the adj. can be derived from

PIE *b iH- 'to be afraid'. Since there is no productive adjectival suffix *-d{h)o-, and since foed- may represent either *foid- or *fojid-^ Nussbaum interprets foedus as an κ/ttf-adj. He does not dwell on the o-ablaut, but since idus-adj. are often derived from stative verbs (pn in -e-\ foedus may be based on the PIE pf *(bʰe-)bhoiH- (preserved in Skt and Slavic) 'to be afraid', or from an c?-grade /-present *bʰoH-i- (cL Hamp 1985b).

Bibl.: WH I: 522f., EM 244, IEW 16If, Nussbaum 1999a: 390f, LIV *bʰeih₂→



### foeteo
foeteo 'to stink' [v. II] (Pl.+; variants faet-,jet-)

Derivatives: foetidus 'stinking' (Pl.+).

Pit. *jwoit-1 *fwoje/ot- 'smoking, smelly'.

PIE *dʰuoh₂-i- 'to smoke'.

IE cognates: Olr. de [f.], gen. diad 'smoke', Mir. deihach

<!-- pdf-page: 244; printed-page: 230; running-header: 230                                       folium -->

'id/ < *duiiotr < *dʰuh₂-i-ot- (see s.v. suffid for the phonetics); Hit tuhhuuai- / tuhhui- [c] 'smoke' < PIE nom. *cfuehru-di-s, ace. *ctuh₂-u-oi-m, gen. *auh₂-u-i-os. % EM suggest that faeted may be the original form, but also consider a relationship with foedus 'repulsive*. If we apply the rule that *oi yielded oe after a labial (Poenus) except in front of i in the next syllable (punicus), then foetidus cannot be old, or proves *faetidus. But if foete- is more original, we can posit earlier *f(w)oit- or *ffw)ojVl·, which enables a connection with Olr. de 'smoke'. Tocharian and Hittite show that the full grade of *duh₂- was of the type *dueh₂-, so that we could assume a basis *dʰuoh₂-i-t- (> *fwoit-) or *dʰuoh₂-i-e/ot- (> *fwoje/ot-) for the verb foetere. The /-stem derivative which forms the basis of Italo-Celtic *dʰuoh₂-i-t-, *d\ih₂-i-ot- may be attested in Hit. tuhhuuai- /tuhhui- 'smoke'.

Bibl.: WH I: 499f, EM 244, IEW 261-67, Leumann 1977: 65, Schrijver 1995: 292, Kloekhorst 2008: 895. —yfimum, suffid, fumus,fu!igo



### folium
folium 'leaf [η. ο] (Pl.+)

Pit. *folio-.

PIE *dʰolH-io- 'leaf.

IE cognates: W. dail 'foliage* < *dʰlH-ih₂-; Gr. aor. έΰαλον, pr. ύάλλω 'to flourish, grow', ΰάλος [n.] 'sprout', *ΰαλύς, -ύ [n.] (only in gen.pl. ύαλέων) , ΰαλερός 'flourishing', ύαλέΰω 'to flourish', ΰύλλα 'leaves' (Hsch.); Arm. dalar 'green, fresh', Alb. dot [Is.], del [23s.] 'to come out\ Folium has often been compared with Gr. (ρύλλον 'leaf < *bʰol-io- (with raising of *o in Gr. between labial and resonant). Beekes 1991 considers the possibility that OS blad, OHG blat 'leaf is a ^-derivative from the stem *6A/-. Yet there is no verbal stem from which the noun could have been derived. Michiel Driessen (px.) proposes to connect folium with the root *dʰlH- 'to sprout, blossom'. The colour of the laryngeal might be determined as *hj on the strength of θαλερός, but this might be a recent formation to the stem *ϋαλύ-. Possibly, the o-grade and the suffix of folium are also preserved in Gr. ϋυλλα 'foliage*, although this is a hapax from Hsch.

Bibl.: WH I: 523f, EM 244, IEW 122, 234, Klingenschmitt 1982: 172, Sihler 1995: 43, LIV *d h alh r . ^fiilvus



### follis
follis 'bag, sack; ball, testicles* [m. i] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: follitus 'enclosed in a sack' (Pl.),folIiculus 'bag, skin, husk' (Lucil.+). The meaning suggests a connection with PCI. *boU as in Oh\ ball [m.] 'member, body part', W. balleg 'sack, purse'; W. dyrn-fol 'glove', arfolli 'to become pregnant* (LEIA B-12). No further etymology.

Bibl.: WH I: 524, EM 244, IEW 120ff, Schrijver 1991: 177.



### fons, fontis
fons, fontis 'spring, well' [m. i] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: Fontanalia^ -ium [n.pl.] 'the festival of Fons' (Varro).

Pit. *fonti-.

It. cognates: possibly U. funtlere, fondlire [abl.pl. + -en] '?', a certain locality; maybe *fontelo-,

PIE *dʰonh₂-ti- 'flowing'.

IE cognates: Skt. dhari 'to run, to flow', pr. dhanva

<!-- pdf-page: 245; printed-page: 231; running-header: forceps                                       231 -->


[2s.ipv.act], OP danu[.„] [3s-pn] 'flows' < IE *c?enhru-; maybe Av. danu- 'river', Oss. don 'water, river' < Pin *dana-, ToB tsnamo* 'flowing', ToA sb. tsnantar, ptc.pf. tsno 'to flow' < *tsan- <

PIE *(?en-. Semantically, the connection with words for 'to run, flow' are unproblematic. Formally, WH suggest a contamination of *fontos and *fentis7 whereas EM argue that the o-grade betrays an old root noun. Since we now reconstruct the root as *dʰenh₂- with a final laryngeal, neither hypothesis is very attractive: the zero grade *dʰnh₂-ti- would yield Lat. ^fnatis^ whereas an old root noun would yield *dʰonh₂ > *fona. One might instead posit a ft-stem *dʰonh₂-ti- with loss of the laryngeal due to the de Saussure effect For the formation type, cf marts.

Bibl.: WH I: 525, EM 244C, IEW 249, Untermann 2000: 301, LIV *dʰenh₂- 'to run'.



### for, faiT
for, faiT 'to speak, say' [v. II; occurs mainly in 3s. and 3p.; ppa.^ns, gdve.fandus, ΡΡΡ-fi*turri\(Pl.+)

Derivatives: jatum 'prophecy, destiny1 (Pl.+); Jama 'news, rumour, public opinion, reputation, fame' (Naev.+), mjamia 'disgrace, bad reputation' (Pl.+), mjamis 'of ill repute' (V\.+),Jamigerabilis 'famous' (Varro), famigeratio 'gossip* (Pl.), Jamigerator 'a gossip' (P\m)9famosus 'infamour, notorious' (¥\.+);Jabula 'talk, rumour; story, tale' (Naev.+),y5irc//im/s 'the god of speech' (Varro), Jabulari 'to talk' (Pl.+), cdnjabularl 'to converse' (Pl.+); Jacundus 'eloquent' (PK+), ficunditds 'eloquence' (Pl.+), Jacundia 'the ability to speak eloquently' (Ter.+); qffaMis 'easy to talk to' (Ter.+), qffari 'to speak to' (Acc.+), effan 'to utter, say' (Enn.+), wjans, -ntis 'unable to speak; infant' (Pl.+), Infantia 'muteness; childhood' (Lucr.+), mfandus 'unspeakable, monstruous' (Ter,+), nefans, -ntis 'wicked' (Lucil.), praejarl 'to recite; say beforehand' (Cato+), profari 'to give warning, speak out' (Andr.+).

Pit. *Ja- 'to speak', *fato- 'said', *fimo/a- 'tale', *fa-ph- 'story'.

It. cognates: U. fato [ppp., nom.accsg.n.] 'said' < *Jato-; O. faamat [3s.prJ, faammant [3p.pr.], famatted, faamated [3s.pfJ; with preverb a- or ad-: οίααματεδ, αίαματεδ [3s.pf.], unclear ατίαματτεν[ς [3p.p£] 'to order', denom. to a noun *Jama-\ O. fatuveis [gen.sg.], faTopafvocsg.] maybe 'Fatuus* < *fatowo~.

PIE pr. *bʰeh₂V*bʰh₂- 'to speak', *bʰeh₂-mo/h₂- 'speech'.

IE cognates: Gr. φημί, Dor. φάμί, AeoL φαμι Ί say'; Gr. φήμη, Dor. AeoL φάμα 'speech', φάτις, φάσις [fj 'speech, rumour', άφατος 'unknown; ineffable', 7ταλαί-φατος 'long said', φωνή 'voice, sound'; Arm. bay 'says'; RuCS bajati 'to tell fables', SG\ bajati 'to practise sorcery, exorcize'; ?OE bd(ia)n 'to brag'· The origin of the suffix in Ja-cundus is uncertain; see s.v. jecundus for a possible explanation. In this verb, Latin (or already Italic?) has generalized the full grade of the root in all derivatives except fateor and fatuus, which show the original ppp. *_/3fo-.

Bibl.: WH I: 437f, 444, 450, 525f., EM 245, IEW 105f., Schrijver 1991: 142, 405, Untermann 2000: 253f, 267£, LIV 2.*bʰeh₂- -+Jas, fateor Jatuus



### forceps, -ipis
forceps, -ipis 'tongs, pincers' [f.p(ij] (Cato+; gen.pl. -ium Lucil.) Since the meaning is the same as withfor/ex, it is often suggested that one arose from the other by way of metathesis. The first member is regarded as a reduced form of

<!-- pdf-page: 246; printed-page: 232; running-header: 232                                       forda -->

formus 'warm' on account of Paul. exF. 91 vformucapes forcipes dictae, quod:forma capiant, id est ferventia. Since the first member of cp. in -ceps are normally nouns, this requires a substantivized adj, * forma 'warm things' as the first member: *forma-kap- > *formkap- > *forkap~. The formation gives an artificial impression, and even liformucapes ever existed, it may well have been a folk etymology. There are several alternatives. Forceps might reflect *foro-kap-, in which the second vowel would syncopate in the foursyllabic case forms (cf auceps, manceps> etc.). The element *foro- might reflect *bʰor-o- 'burden' to the root of ferd. Another option would be that for- is cognate with ferrum 'iron', a word with an uncertain etymology, but possibly a loanword. Note thsXforceps often occurs as the instrument of a smith.

Bibl.: WH I: 526, EM 246, Leumann 1977: 393, Sihler 1995: 68. → few, ferrum, forfeXy formus



### forda
forda 'carrying, pregnant (of cows)' [adj. a] (Varro+; also horda)

Derivatives: fordicldia> -drum [n.pl.] 'festival at which pregnant cows were sacrificed' (Vam>+).

Pit. *for(i)da-.

PIE *bʰori-d- 'giving birth'?

IE cognates: Lith. ber(g)zdzias 'barren (of a cow)', ber(g)zdi 'barren cow', RuCS brezda, brez(d)a 'pregnant', Ru. berezaja (dial,) 'mare in foaF < *bʰerdio- 'carrying, pregnant'. The initial syllable of the BSL words is circumflex (suggesting *-dʰ- in the suffix rather than *-d-), but Latin forda cannot reflect *-rdʰ- (which would yield *forba\ Thus, Latin and BSl. continue two independent formation on the basis of

PIE *bʰr-, Nussbaum 1999a proposes that Latin forda was built on an /-stem *bʰori- 'birther, who gives birth', which was enlarged by *-d- (as often in Greek, and as in Latin pecu-d- of the same semantic sphere) and finally hypercharacterized by adding fern. *-a Pre-Latin *forida > forda.

Bibl.: ; WH I: 527, EM 228, IEW 128ff., Nussbaum 1999a; 381,406, LIV *bʰer-. -+ferd



### forfex, -ids
forfex, -ids 'tongs, pincers; shears, scissors' [f. (m.) k] (Celsus+; also forpex)

Pit. *forpo- 'shearing', *forpaje/o- 'to shear'. It. cognates; U. furfati, furfant [3p.pr.], efurfatu [3sJpv.II + e- 'out'] certain action with 'sheep' as direct object, 'to shear'?

PIE *bʰrdʰ-o- [adj.].

IE cognates: Gr. πέρϋω 'to capture, take in', πτολίπορύος 'capturing cities', πορύέω 'to pillage'. Either a noun with a word-internal cluster -rf-, which is irregular according to the standard Latin sound laws, or a compound in -fex 'making'. However, since cp. in -fex normally do not show syncope (aurifex, opifex, etc.), the latter is less probable. Also, the recent date offorfex makes a foreign origin more likely. An old connection is with U.^wr/α-, which is often translated as 'to shear' (e.g. Meiser 1986: 101). One might connect Gr. πέρΰω 'to capture, take in', πτολίπορύος 'capturing cities' (Janda 2000: 230-240), in which case the U. verb could be denominal to an adj. *bʰrd -o- 'capturing, harvesting, shearing'.

Bibl.: WH 1; 526, EM 247, IEW 138, Untermann 2000:302f, LIV ?*bʰerdʰ-. ^forceps

<!-- pdf-page: 247; printed-page: 233; running-header: forma                                      233 -->



### foria
foria 'diarrhoea' [f. a] (Varro)

Derivatives: foriolus csuffering from diarrhoea' (Lab.); conforire 'to defile with ordure' (Pompon.).

Pit. *foria-l WH tentatively propose a connection with a PIE root *dʰer- "to shit', which is otherwise only attested in enlarged form in other branches of IE: Lith. defkti cto make dirty' < *dʰer-k-, OIc. drita (dreit), OHG trlzan 'cacare' (maybe a Gm. rhyming formation to *sklta- 'to shit'), RIL dial, dristath 'to suffer from diarrhoea', Bulg. driskam, dristb. WH admit themselves that this renders it uncertain that Latin would continue an unenlarged root form. Since 'to defecate' is often refened to by means of euphemisms, a derivation from the root *bʰer- 'to carry', e.g. iterative *bʰer-bʰor-je- *to bring (away) repeatedly' seems at least equally likely; or foria might be a derivative ixom forare 'to pierce', as 'piercing' (cf. German Durchfall).

Bibl.: WH I: 527f, EM 247, IEW 256. -^ferdjord



### foris
foris 'door' [f. i] (Pl.+;rarelysg., usually pi. fores, '-ium ('folding doors').)

Derivatives: fords 'to the outside' (Naev.+), foris 'on the outside' (Pl.+), foricula 'window-shutter' (Varro+).

Pit. *fwor-(i-) > *for-i-\ *for*a~.

PIE *dʰu6r-, *d\ir- 'door'.

IE cognates: Olr. dorus [mru] , Dor^ Duir [toponyms], W. dor, Bret, dor < PCI. *duora, fduro- 'door'; Hit. andurza [adv.] 'inside, indoors' < *h}n-<fur-, Skt. dvar- [f. (du.pl.)] 'door, gate', YAv. duuar-, OP duvar(a)- 'gate', Gr. ϋυρά 'door', Myc. o-pi-tu~ra-jo lopithuraidi/ 'door-keeper', Ann. dowr-k' [pi.] 'door', Alb. dere < *d(u)or-om/ay Lith. pi. durys, OCS dvtrt < BSl. *rfWr-, *dur-\ Go. dour [n.], OHG turi, OIc. dyrr < ^ur-(i-), ToB twere 'door' < ^uoro-. Lat. foras and foris reflect the acc.pl. and loc.pl. ( » ins.pl.) of an a-stem*fora-: hence, these developed from cto the door' and 'at the door', respectively. The evidence points to a PIE root noun *d\i6r, *dʰuor-, *dʰur- 'door', maybe used as a plural or dual tantum; Greek, Albanian (possibly) and Latin (and Celtic) show an additional derivative *d\i(o)r-h₂- '(set of) door(s)\ The Latin /-stem was probably derived from *dʰuor- (cf. Schrijver 1991:472) and may originally have been used as a singular, but it usually occurs as a plural from the earliest literary texts onwards. Sihler assumes that foris is a back-formation to the pi. foresy which cannot be ruled out In view of the zero grade which is reflected e.g. in Germanic and Slavic, however, the original pi or du. seems to have had *d\ir-, which would yield Latin *fur-es (the mi. fore < *fuse has lowering in front of r < *z < *s). Also, the sg. foris is attested in the oldest literature (Plautus),

Bibl.: WH I:529f, EM 246, IEW 278f., Schrijver 1991:47If, Sihler 1995:148. →forum



### forma
forma 'form, contour, appearance; beauty* [f a] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: formare 'to mould, fashion' (Lucr.+), formamentum 'arrangement' (Lucr.), formaster^ -tri 'kind of pastry' (Titin.), formatura 'the shaping' (Lucr.), formosus 'beautiful' (Jzr.+)>formula 'pretty appearance; register, formula, document' (Pl.+); deformis 'misshapen, disfigured' (Lucil.+); deformare 'to design, sketch;

<!-- pdf-page: 248; printed-page: 234; running-header: 234                                    formica -->

spoil' (Pl.+), Informare 'to fashion, sketch' (Varro+), EM stress the length of ο in jorma, but there seems to have been a recent lengthening of short *o in front of -rC- (Leumann 1977: 114). Leumann derives formaster (with the pejorative suffix -aster) fcomformus 'hot', but it seems more likely that forma was its basis. Since formica 'ant' and forrmdo ' ghost, scarecrow' point to a dissimilation *m - m> * / - m, forma might go back to *morma» Maybe forma and Gr. μορφή 'form, shape, contour' were borrowed from a third party; or the Greek word was borrowed into another language, such as Etruscan, and passed thence into Latin as *morma.

Bibl.: WH I: 530, EM 247, Leumann 1977: 21,114, 319, Sihler 1995: 76.



### formica
formica 'ant* [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: formwmus 'ant-like, crawling' (Pl.)-

Pit. *morrmka-.

PIE *moru-o/f~(?), IE cognates; Olr. moirb, W. myr, Bret, merien 'ant' < *morui-; Skt. vamra- 'ant', valmika- [m.] 'ant-hilP; Av. maoiri-, Oss. mcelzyg/mulzug, Pasto mezay, Sogd. ym'wrc < PIr, *marui(ka)-, Sogd. zm W £ , Khwar. zmwrk^ MP, MoP wor < *maruaka-; Gr. μύρμηξ, -ηκος 'ant', μύρμος, βύρμαξ βόρμαξ, δρμικας (with β- and zero < *ρ-); RuCS mravbjb, ORu. morovej, Po. mrowka, SCr. /wrdv 'ant' < *moru-iH-; OIc, mawrr < *mowr-o-, Far. meyra < *mauron-, Dan. wyre, OE /wyre, ME wire, MDu. miere, Crimean Go. //«era < *meur(j)on- [f.] 'ant'; ToB warme*, nom.pl. warmi 'ant' < *upno-. The word for 'ant' is difficult to reconstruct because of the deviating forms; probably, taboo distortions took place in many languages. This process can still be observed in modem dialects, for instance of Dutch and German. Latin/- might go back to *m- via a dissimilation of *iw- — iw > *f-m. Meillet (1918) assumes an intermediate stage *[b] (voiced labial fricative), which he regards as an argument for the view that all

PIE *bʰ changed to *θ before yielding Latin/-. In the IE branches, we find the stem structures *moru-o/i~ (Celtic, Iranian, Slavic, Gm.), *mormo/i- (Greek) and *uormo/i- (Skt., Toch., Greek?). Since the latter is quite similar to

PIE *u(o)rmo/l· "worm, insect' (see s.v. vermis), it may have been influenced by it; consequently, for 'ant' the form *moruo/i- is more likely to be old.

Bibl.: WH I: 531, EM 247f., IEW 749, Leumann 1977: 191.



### formido, -inis
formido, -inis 'fear, alarm, awe; bogy' [f. «] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: formidare 'to fear, dread' (Naev.+), formido/ulosus 'alarming; frightened' (Naev.+).

Pit. *morm-TdofenS!

PIE *mor-mo- 'fear'?

IE cognates: Gr. μορμώ, -ους 'bogey, monster', μορμύσσομαι 'to frighten', μόρμορος, μύρμος 'fear'; maybe to Gr. μορμύρω 'to roar and boil'? Leumann (1977: 341) regards formidtdosus as a rhyming formation to peric-ulosus 'dangerous'. The noun formido can be interpreted as the antonym of cupldo 'desire' and lubido 'lust', which may explain the suffix. Since there are no verb forms *form-(f-f indicating 'to fear', Latin form- might go back to *morm-, in which case a

<!-- pdf-page: 249; printed-page: 235; running-header: foro                                       235 -->

connection with the Greek words is possible. This would imply a noun *mormo- 'fear' as is reconstructed by IEW. There is no PIE etymology for this connection, if it is correct.

Bibl.: WH I: 532, EM 248, IEW 749, Leumann 1977: 191,367.



### formus
formus 'warm' [adj. o/a] (Paul. exF.)

Derivatives: formidus 'warm' (Cato apud Paul, ex F.\

Pit. *χ"οηηο-.

PIE *gʷʰo/er-mo- 'warm'.

IE cognates: Skt gharma- 'heat, glow', Av. garsma- 'warm; heat', OP garma-pada- 'the fourth month (June-July)' < *gʷʰor-m<>; Gr. ΰερμός, Arm. jerm 'warm' < *gʷʰer-m<>; maybe Alb. zjarm 'heat' < *gʷʰermo- (cf. de Vaan 2004b: 82); OPr. gorme 'heat\ Latv. gafme 'warmth' *gʷʰor-mo-. The adj. formidus can be explained as modelled on its antonym frigidus* Theoretically, formus may reflect *gʷʰormos or *gʷʰpnos; but since no other language shows a zero grade, *gʷʰormo- seems more likely. The PIE vacillation in the root vowel between *gʷʰermo- and *gʷʰormo- may be due to the different verbal formations of the root, of which we find bot *gʷʰer-e/o- 'to heat' and *gʷʰor-i- 'to be hot', cf LIV.

Bibl.: WH I: 532f, EM 248, IEW 493ff, Leumann 1977: 329, Schrijver 1991: 420, Meiser 1998: 103, Nussbaum 1999a: 405, LIV *gyher-. -tforcepsjornus

fornus 'oven' [m. o] (Varro; usually fumus Pl.+) j Oenvativesifornax [f.j 'furnace' (Cato+Xfornacalia, -drum [n.pL] 'Baking Festival' (Varro+);praefurnium 'furnace-entrance' (Cato+).

Pit. *x"orno-.

PIE *gʷʰr-no- 'heat' or 'oven'.

IE cognates: Olr. gorn 'fire' < *gʷʰor-no-; Skt ghpia- 'heat, glow' [n.], ghfni- 'hot time, heat' [f.], Alb. zjarre 'fire' < *gʷʰer-no- [n.] or *gʷʰer-os- [nj (de Vaan 2004b: 82), RuCS gn>no 'cauldron, pot, oven' [n.], Ru, gorn 'blacksmith's hearth' [n.], gen.sg. gorna; gorno 'blacksmith's hearth, clay-oven' < *g*v>. The original form must have been forn-, which became furn- in part of the Roman speech. The raising of -or- in front of a consonant seems to have been an ongoing process; it might have been dialectal, since it is regular in Sabellic. Since Skt. and Slav, show zero-grade *gʷʰr-no-, this is also the most likely reconstruction for fornus. Of course, in view of formus with a PIE ograde, it is conceivable that Pit or Latin introduced the o-grade into the noun, which would give *gʷʰor-no- > fornus.

Bibl.: WH I: 533L, EM 248, IEW 493ff., Leumann 1977: 48, Sihler 1995: 43, Meiser 1998: 64, LIV *g**er-. → formus



### foro, -are
foro, -are 'to bone through, pierce' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: foramen 'aperture, hole' (Cato+); mforare 'to bore into' (Pl.+), perforare 'to make a hole, pierce' (Varro+)·

Pit. *foraje/o-.

PIE *bʰorH-ie/o- 'to pierce, strike'? Or a noun *bʰorH-h₂~ 'hole'?

IE cognates: Lith.

<!-- pdf-page: 250; printed-page: 236; running-header: 236                                       fors -->

bartU 3s. bara 'to scold, accuse, forbid', Latv. barti 'to scold, blame*, OCS brati (s$)y Is. borjQ 'to fight', Ru. borof 'to overpower' < *bʰorH-, OIc. berja 'to beat, hit', OHG berjan 'to hit, pound, knead* < PGra. *barjan-. Regarded as a denominal verb by WH and IEW. This is conceivable, but the ograde presents in other branches of IE suggest a different solution: an iterative verb *bborH-(i-) 'to pierce many times, bore\ If the final laryngeal was vocalized in Latin, we can assume a phonetic development *bʰorHie- > *foraje- >fora-.

Bibl.: WH I: 481f, EM 248, IEW 133ff., Schrijver 1991: 216, LIV *bʰerH-. →ferio



### fors, -tis
fors, -tis 'chance, luck' [f. i] (Pl.+; only nom. fors and abL forte 'by chance5)

Derivatives: forsitan 'perhaps' (Ύ^τΛ); fortasse (Pl.+), fortassis 'id' (Pl.+); fortuitus 'by chance, random' (?l.+),fortuna 'fortune, favourable outcome' (Nasv.+),fortunatus 'fortunate, lucky' (Naev,+), fortQnatim 'prosperously* (Erin.), fortunare 'to make fortunate' (Pl.+), infortunatus 'unfortunate' (Pl.+), infortunium 'misfortune' (Pl.+)<

Pit. *forti- [f] 'luck\ *fortu- 'luck'.

It. cognates: PaeL forte [gen.sg.?] *forteis, maybe borrowed from Latin.

PIE ♦bV-ti- 'bearing, case'?

IE cognates: see s.v.fero* The adv. forsitan derives from *fors sit an 'perchance it be that'. The origin of fortasse seems to be *forte an sit/s, but the phonetics (especially the short final vowel) are unclear. The noun fortuna and the adj. fortu-itus presuppose a i/-stem *fortu- 'chance, luck', whence an adj. *fortuno- 'lucky'. The precise origin of -Ttus in fortu-itus and in its semantic neighbour gratu-itus 'free of charge' is unclear. The semantic shift from 'load' or 'the carrying' to 'chance, luck' is not obvious, and EM go so far as to reject the connection between fors and few* Yet the co-occurrence of //- and /w-stems seems old, and there are many ways in which a meaning 'chance, luck' can originate.

Bibl.: WH I: 534f., EM 249, IEW 128ff., Leumann 1977: 323, Untermann 2000: 304, LIV *bʰer-. →fero

for tis 'strong, robust' [adj. /] (Lex XII+; variant horctus,forctus,forctis 'good' Festus 348, Paul. exF. 84, 102)

Derivatives: fortitudo 'strength, courage' (Ter.+).

Pit. *forkti/o-.

It. cognates: O.fortis [comp., nom.acc.sg.n., adv.] 'stronger'; maybe borrowed from Latin.

PIE *bʰorg-to- 'stuffed'? *dʰ(o)rgh-to- 'strong, robust7?

IE cognates: Skt caus: m barhayati 'to lay low7, pr. "tymha-, aor. barh- 'to make strong', pari-bfdha- [ppp.] 'firm, strong', dvibarhas- [adj.] 'with double strength', brhant- 'great, large', YAv. us .. . barszaiia- 'to make strong / big', barazah- [n.] 'height', barasna [ins.sg.)] 'elevated place, height', YAv. bvrBzant- 'rising high, high, loud' < bh(e)rgh-, Olr. bri 'hill', Brigit (woman's name), Arm. barjr 'high', ToA parkar 'long' // Skt, dfrnha- 'to make firm', pf άάάχΗάηά- [ptc.med.] 'holding fast', ptc. drdha- (< *dfzdʰ&) 'fixed, firm, solid', OAv. diddrBzo [2s,desid.inj.act] 'to desire to fasten', ddrdz- 'fetter', YAv. dardzaiia- 'to fasten', cbrvzra- 'strong, firm'; Lith. dirzti 'to become hard'.

<!-- pdf-page: 251; printed-page: 237; running-header: foveo                                      237 -->

The form and meaning are not specific enough to decide on the etymology. In view of the adj, meaning, one would expect the suffix to have been *-to- rather than *-ti-. The root may have a structure *bʰ/dʰ/gʷʰ + (o)r + K-. Within Latin, farcio 'to stuff seems the best semantic match which would also satisfy the phonetics:

PIE *bʰorg-to- would yield *forcto-. A connection with

PIE *bʰergʰ- 'high, elevated' (WH) does not explain the meaning offortis. Alternatively, one might connection fortis with the Ilr. and Baltic forms for 'to make firm, become hard*; but only if these continue

PIE *</-, not if they continue

PIE *tf- (as LIV suspect). Semantically, this would be satisfactory.

Bibl.: WH I: 535f., EM 249f., IEW I40f., Untermann 2000: 304f, LIV *bʰergʰ- / *dʰergʰ-. -+firtmts



### forum
forum 'market place, public space; place where the fruit was laid for pressing (Cato+)' [n. o; fonts LuciL, Pompon., CIL] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: fonts 'deck (on a ship); passage (in a beehive); rows of benches (in a stadium)' (Enn.+), forensis 'of the forum, public'(Varro+).

Pit. *Jworo- '(room) near the door*.

It. cognates: U. furu, furo [acc.sg.]'forum*.

PIE *dʰuor-o- '(room near the) door'·

IE cognates: Skt. dvaram [n.] Moor, gate, passage*, Lith. dvaras [m.] 'estate; court', OCS dvorb 'court*, PTo. *twere 'door1. WH interpret forum as 'fenced area' to the root offorare, but Pokorny 1959 rejects this. Forum is generally regarded as a derivative of PIE 'door', and connected with other IE forms from *<fuor-o-. The required semantic development is 'area at the doors' > 'entrance room, vestibule* > 'public room' > 'public space*; this is not so problematic as to overrule the formal correspondences with Lith. dvaras.

Bibl.: WH I: 537f, EM 250, IEW 278f., Meiser 1986: 116, Schrijver 1991: 471f, Sihler 1995: 180, Untermann 2000: 305. →foris



### fovea
fovea 'pit' [£ a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: favlsae/favissae [f.pL] 'vaults, subterranean chambers' (GeL+). The connection with Gr. χειά, Η. χειή 'serpent's den' must be dismissed. It is uncertain that fovea anafavisae belong together, as their etymology is unknown.

Bibl.: WH I: 538, EM 250, IEW 451, Schrijver 1991: 448.



### foveō
foveO 'to make or keep warm; to relieve* [v. II; ρΐ.βνϊ, ppp. Jotum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: Joculum 'device for warming' (Pl.), Joculare sto revive, cherish' (Varro+);Jomentum 'soothing application, remedy' (HoT.+)9Jomes, -itis [m.] 'chips of wood' (Verg.+), jS/wj, -us 'heating* (Plin.)·

Pit. *Jofeje/o-.

PIE *dʰogʷʰ-eie- 'to burn'.

IE cognates: Mir. daig, gen.sg. dega 'fire, pain'; Skt. dahati, caus. dahayati, YAv. dazaiti 'burns', Gr. τέφρα 'ashes', Alb. djeg 'to burn', n-dez 'to ignite'; Lith. degti 'to burn*, OCS zesti, Is. zegg, Toch. tsak- 'to bum up, consume by fire', tsak-1 'to illuminate5. Schrijver 1991: 278 concludes that Jo- probably reflects unsyncopated *fowi- < *fowe-. Thus, Joculum < *fowe-cuhtm, fomentum < *fowe-mentum. Apart from

<!-- pdf-page: 252; printed-page: 238; running-header: 238                                     fraces -->

ficulum, all derivatives appear quite late. The pf. βνί has developed by contraction from *fowe-wai. Foveo escaped the delabialization of *ow > *au because *gM* had not changed into *w at that time (Schrijver 19SU, Vine 2006a: 212).

Bibl.: WH I: 466f, EM 250, IEW 240f., Schrijver 1991: 277ff., 448, Meiser 1998: 206, LIV *dʰegyh. → favittajebris



### fraces, -urn
fraces, -urn 'fragments of olive pulp left after pressing' [f.pl. k] (Cato+; sg. frax (gloss.), pi. also flaces)

Derivatives: fracidus 'soft, pulpy' (Cato), fracescere 'to become soft' (Cato+), cdnfracescere 'to putrify, rot' (Varro).

Pit. *frak-.

IE cognates: OPr. dragios 'yeast', Lith. drages, Latv. drodzi, OCS drozdbje 'dregs' < BSL *dro(z)gi(?)a?; Olc. dregg 'yeast, dregs'. Alb. dra 'dregs, sediment' (maybe to drege 'scab, sore', Demiraj 1997) may have *drag-, but *drab- also seems possible* With *-b-: Mir. drab 'dredgs', Ru. drob 'yeast', OHG pi. trebir 'husks (of grapes)' < ^rab*2-. PGm. *drosna- in OE drosne, OHG truosana, MoDu. droesem 'dregs'. The spelling fracc- may have been influenced by flacced 'to decline in strength', floccus 'drooping, floopy', which are semantically quite close. Flaces may have -/- from flocces. The Gm., BSl. and Latin words for 'dregs' clearly belong together, but their vowels do not match, and point to a non-IE *a. The velars do not match either, but Latin voiceless *k may have arisen in the nom.sg, *praks and thence spread through the paradigm. It appears that we are dealing with a loanword from an unknown, non-IE language, or from a lost IE language in which the root *dʰregʰ- "to drag' or *dʰreh₂gʰ- 'to soil' yielded a form *dʰragʰ- 'dredges of wine, oil, fat'.

Bibl.: WH I: 538f, EM 251, IEW 251, Schrijver 1991: 486.



### fragro, -are
fragro, -are 'to smell strongly' [v. I] (Cat.+)

Pit. *fragro-.

PIE *b\hig-ro- Smelling'.

IE cognates: Mir. bren 'putrid, foul', MW braen, Β brein 'putrid, corrupt' < PCL *bragno- < *bbrh,g-no-; Olr. braig(a)id*> -braig 'to fart, break wind' < *brag-ie→ Olr. broimm, W. bram, Co. Bret, bramm 'fart' < *brag-smrt; MHG brcehen < *brekjan 'to smell' < *bʰrehig-; OHG braccho, MLG, MDu. bracke 'hound' < *bʰrhjg-n-. Schrijver adopts the etymology preferred by WH and IEW, viz. of an adj. *fragros from which the verb fragrare was derived. The postulated *fragros can reflect

PIE *bʰrHg-ro-; if one connects the Gm. words cited here, the laryngeal is *hi- Leumann has proposed a connection with Skt. ava-ghraii 'smells', abhi-jighrani- 'smelling', but Schrijver shows that this is formally very difficult» In 1995, Schrijver connects the Celtic words cited. Schumacher is hesitant to build on MHG brcehen, and reconstructs the root as *bʰrHg-.

Bibl.: WH I: 540, EM 251, IEW 163, Leumann 1977: 166, Schrijver 1991: 185f, 1995: 170f., Schumacher 2004: 232.

<!-- pdf-page: 253; printed-page: 239; running-header: frater                                     239 -->



### frSga, -Orum
frSga, -Orum 'strawberry' [n.pl. o] (Vergilius+) ?U.frago-.

IE cognates: Alb. (Eastern Geg) drathe, (Tosk) dredhe 'strawberry'. The meaning oifraga closely matches that of Alb. dredhe^ and the two may reflect ♦d^Hg-o-. It seems unlikely that the word indicated 'strawberry' in PIE, however, and it may well be a loanword. An alternative connection of fraga with Gr. ραξ, ραγός 'grape', ρώξ 'grape' is possible if one starts from *srag~; both the phonological form and the diverging meanings 'strawberry' and 'grape' would then point to a third (non-IE?) language from which the word was borrowed.

Bibl.: WH I: 540, EM 251, Schrijver 1991: 177, Demiraj 1997: 144.



### frango, -ere
frango, -ere 'to break" [v. Ill; pf. fregf, ppp. fractum] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: fragescere 'to become subdued' (Acc.)5 fragilis 'fragile, crackling' (Lucr.+), fragmen 'a piece broken off (Sis.+X fragmentwn 'fragment' (Lucil.+), fragor 'the breaking, crash, roar' (Lucr.+), fragosus 'brittle, rugged' (Lucr.+); confringere 'to destroy, ruin' (Andr.+), confragdsus 'uneven, difficult' (Pl.+), defringere 'to break off (Cato+), diffringere 'to break up' (Pl.+), effringere 'to break open' (Pl.+), infringere 'to break, crush, deprive of (Pl.+), interfringere 'to break (here and there' (Cato), qffringere 'to break up by cross-ploughing' (Varro+), perfringere 'to break, fracture^ (Lucr.H-), praefringere 'to break at the end' (Pl.+), refringere 'to break back, force open' (Pl.+), suffringere 'to break the lower part of (Pl.+); compounds in -fragium '.the breaking', -fragus 'who breaks'.

Pit. *frang- [pr.], *fragto- [ppp.].

PIE *bʰr-n-g- [pr.] 'to break', *bʰrg-to- [ppp.].

IE cognates: Go. brikan, OHG brehhan 'to break' < *bʰreg/g-. The long vowel of fractus is due to Lachmann's Law. Schrijver 1991 argues that Latin and Irish have continued a PIE root present *bʰreg-/*bʰrg- in different ways, whereas LIV opts for an old athematic aorist. The latter seems more likely in view of the meaning. Since the Irish forms which Schrijver connected in 1991 are now connected with fragrare, it may be that Gm. continues the old root aorist, and Latin the nasal present^

Bibl.: WH I: 539, 541, EM 251, IEW 165, Schrijver 1991: 137, 478, 483f, LIV *bʰre(g)-, → suffragium



### frater, -trfc
frater, -trfc 'brother; member of a fraternity, friend' [m. r] (Naev>)

Derivatives: fratercuius ' little brother' (Pl.+), fraterculare 'to swell up (of a boy's breasts at puberty)' (?l)\fraternus 'of a brother' (Pac.+).

Pit. *frater-.

It. cognates: Ven. [fljhraterei [dat.sg.]5 U. frater, frater [nom.pl.], O* fratrum, U. fratrum, fratru, fratrom [gen.pL], fratrus [dat.pl.], fratruspe(r) [abl.pl. + -per] 'brother'; U. fratreks, fratrexs [nom.sg.m.], fratreci [datsg.m.] ,fratreca [abl.sg.f.] 'belonging to the brotherhood' *fratMko~\ U. fratrecate [loc.sg.?] 'the office of afratrex* < *fratrikato-.

PIE *bʰreh₂-tr- 'brother'.

IE cognates: Olr. bratkair, W. brawd; Skt. bhratar-, Av. OP bratar- 'brother', Gr. φρατηρ [m.] 'member of a brotherhood', άφρήτωρ 'not

<!-- pdf-page: 254; printed-page: 240; running-header: 240                                      fraus -->


bound by social ties', Phryg. βρατερε 'brothers', Arm* elbayr 'brother', OPr. brati, brote, Lith, brolis, Latv. bralis, OCS bratrb^ broth, Go. bropar, OHG bruoder, OIc. brodir^ ToBprocer, ToApracar 'brother' 4 For PIE 'brother', Pinault (2007: 276f.) assumes an etymology *bʰr-eh₂- 'group of males borne by the same mother' > *bʰreh₂-tr 'belonging to the *bʰreh₂\ In his view, kinship terms in

PIE *-t(e)r- contain the "contrastive" suffix *-t(e)r which is also found in adverbs, e.g. Latin subter,

Bibl.: WH I: 541£, EM 252, IEW 163£, Lejeune 1974: 335, Schrijver 1991: 178, Untermann 2000: 293-295.



### fraus, -dis
fraus, -dis 'harm, danger; deceit' [f. d] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: fraudare 'to cheat, swindle' (Pl.+) (pf. fraudavi and frausus sum\ fraudatio 'cheating' (Pl.+), defrau/udare 'to cheat' (Pl.+); fraudulentus 'dishonest' (Pl.+),fraudulentia'dishonesty' (Pl.); frustra 'in vain' (later frustra) (Pl.+), friistrarJ 4 to delude; frustrate' (Pl.+), frustratio 'deception, trick' (Pl.+), friistratus, -us 'deception' {?L\frustramen 'id/ (Lucr.), defrustrari'to foil completely' (Pl.).

Pit. *frawV-.

It. cognates: U.frosetom est [3s.pf.ps.] Ms not valid (?)' < *frauss-ito- < intensive formation on the basis of *fraudrto~? (Meiser 1986: 242).

PIE *dʰrou-V-d(h)-?

IE cognates: Skt dhruti- 'deception, error', -dhrut- 'deceiving', YAv. drauuaiiat 'will deceive', Parth. dr'w- 'to seduce' < *dʰr(o)u-. Lat. frustra is pi. of *frustrum < *fraud-tro-. Szemerenyi 1989: 33ff. and Schrijver 1991: 444, independently of one another, derive fraus from

PIE *dʰreugʰ- *to deceive', but not in the same way. Szemerenyi posits an abstract *dʰreugʰ-os, which would have yielded a paradigm *fros, *froris9 whence with diss. *frodis, and with hypercorrect au for urban δ finally fraus. These assumptions (*eu > *d, the dissimilation and the hypercorrection) are ad hoc and render the solution unlikely. Schrijver postulates that fraus derives from a PIE root *<fru- as reflected in Skt. dhruti- 'deception', varuna-dhriit- 'deceiving Varuna\ Parth. dr'w 'to deceive'. He then posits *ctrou-V-dʰ)- whence *frowVd- and with unrounding of *ow > *frawVd- >fraud-.For frustra, Schrijver reconstructs *dtrou-C- or *<?reu-(V)C-. This solution is relatively elegant on the phonetic side, but the status of the reconstructed suffix remains unclear. According to the rule established by Vine 2006a, the first syllable should have been pretonic: */row-.

Bibl.: WH I: 543, EM 252, 257, IEW 277, Szemerenyi 1989: 33ff., Schrijver 1991: 444, Untermann 2000: 301, LIV 2 . * d W .



### fraxinus
fraxinus 'ash-tree' [f. o] (Enn.+)

Pit. *frakse}\no-.

PIE *bʰrHg-s-e/ino-.

IE cognates: Skt. bhurja- 'kind of birch', Oss. bcerz I bcerza 'birch', OPr. berse, Lith. berzas, Latv. b$rzs, CS breza, Ru. bereza, OIc. bjgrk, OHG birihha 4 birch*. The quantity of the a is unknown, but Schrijver convincingly argues that short a is more likely. All existing etymologies start from the assumption that fraxinus is cognate with the word for 'birch' in other IE languages. Schrijver proposes the

<!-- pdf-page: 255; printed-page: 241; running-header: frenum                                      241 -->


following scenario: a PIE root noun *bʰerHg-/*bʰrHg- 'birch' acquired the meaning 4 ash-tree \ and became formally influenced by the PIE paradigm of *Heh₃-s- 'ash' (Lat ornus). The new s-stem *bʰerHg-(6)s, gen.sg. *bʰrHg-s-os 'ash' was extended by means of the suffix *-e«o- or *-mo- (cf alnus, farnus, ornus, quernus). The resulting *bʰrHg-s-e/ino- yielded *frak-s-e/ino- by means of Schrijver's vocalization rule of *CRBDC > *CraCC If PIE 'birch' is indeed derived from the root *bʰreh₁g- 'to shine', it probably refers to the white barch of the birch. But the full grade in the word for 'birch* (in BSl. and Gm.) would have schwebe-ablaut with regard to the verbal forms Skt. bhraja-, YAv. braza- 'shines'.

Bibl.: WH I: 544, EM 252, IEW 139f, Schrijver 1991: 186ff., LIV "bVel^g-. → fortius, ornus



### fremo, -ere
fremo, -ere 'to utter a deep dull continuous sound* [v. Ill;fremiti,fremitum](Enn.+)

Derivatives: fremitus, -us 'roar, rumble' (Pl.+), fremibundus 'roaring, growling* (Acc.+); perfremere 'to fill a place with roaring* (Ace).

Pit. *freme/o-.

PIE *bʰrem-e/o- 'to hum, rumble'.

IE cognates: W. brefu 'to bleat, roar', Parth. brm-, MP bram- 'to cry' < Ilr. *brama-, Po. brzmiec 'to hum, sound', OHG pram [pret], MHG brimmen 'to roar'. The reconstruction of the PIE root is not completely certain: especially from the point of view of Latin morphology, atfe/ root is also conceivable (Meiser 2003).

Bibl.: WH I: 544f, EM 252f, IEW 142f, Meiser 2003: 125, LIV *bʰrem-.



### frendō, -ere
frendO, -ere 'to grind one's teeth' [v. Ill; ppp.fresum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nefrens, -ndis 'not able to chew yet, infant' (Andn+).

Pit. *frend-.

PIE *gʷʰrend(h)-e/o-.

IE cognates: Lith. gr$sti9 Is. grendziu 'to plane, scour' < *-nd-t OE grindan 'to grind', Go. ^grinds 'ground, crushed', OHG grini, MoDu. grind 'gravel', OIc. grandi 'sandbank', OIc. grunnr, Go. grundu- 'ground, base' < *-w/-. The morphology of the adj. ne-frend- 'not grinding its teeth' is odd: one would expect *in-frend- (Bader 1962: 37). Livingston 2004: 67f argues that ne-frend- is a recent reformation of *in-frend-, to avoid semantic ambiguity with *frendere *in 'biting (on)'. She assumes that nefrens was modeled on deprans 'not eating' (Naev.+). For PIE, it is impossible to unite the Latin, Baltic and Gm. words under one certain preform: Gm. must have *-dʰ-, whereas Baltic need *-d- (Winter's Law). Are these different dental suffixes to an earlier root *gʷʰren-?

Bibl.: WH I: 545, EM 253, IEW 459, Schrijver 1991: 186, Sihler 1995: 163f, LIV *gftf*rend-,



### frtnum
frtnum 'horse's bridle or harness' [n. o\ nom.acc.pl. usually frem.frenos] (Acc.+)

Derivatives: infrenare 'to curb, restrain' (Acc.+), effrenatus 'unrestrained' (Acc.+), refrenare 'to hold back, restrain7 (Varro+).

Pit. *freno-.

PIE *dʰr-ehrno- 'holding'.

<!-- pdf-page: 256; printed-page: 242; running-header: 242                                     frequens -->

The nom.pl. frem (usual in prose instead of frena) possibly continues a PIE n. dual ending *-oihj (Sommer 1914: 335). EM propose an etymology *freds-no-m (to frendo) 'what the horses chew on\ vjz. 'what they have in their mouth'. This is possible, but not very straightforward. Also, this presupposes thatfrendere has a nasal infix to a root *fredty which is uncertain (see s.v. frendo). WH in their turn connect frenum with fretus 'relying on\ which EM reject without telling why. I think that it is semantically and formally more straightforward. It would presuppose a stem *fre- 'to support, rely\ for which see s.v. fretus.

Bibl.: WH I: 546, EM 253, IEW 252ff, LIV *dʰer-. -+fretus



### frequens Occurring at close intervals9
frequens Occurring at close intervals9 [adj. nt] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: frequentare 'to populate, occupy, visit' (Pl.+), Tnfrequens 'not crowded, absent' (Pl.+); fraxare 'to go the watchman's rounds' (Paul, ex F\ flaxare 'id/ (gloss.). Nussbaum 2007b connects frequens with fraxare. Semantically, this is quite attractive. Formally, the forms could go back to a root *bʰrkʷ-: frequens < *bʰrekʷ-, whereas the zero grade *bʰrks- would yield *farL·- (> *far$a~) by Schrijver's rule (1991: 495f.). The prefonn *farks- could then have been leveled to *fraks- by analogy with *frekʷ-. Note that a *bʰrekʷ- would have an unusual PIE root structure *Dh-T. Alternatively, frequens has been connected with farcio (WH, Eichner 1982: 19), which is semantically less straightforward. See s.v. farcio for further considerations,

Bibl.: WH I: 456, EM 253, IEW 1 IOf., LIV *bʰreku-. -^farcio



### fretum csea strait'
fretum csea strait' [n. o] (Naev.+: alsofretus,-its I -i)

Derivatives: yre/a/e 'frying-pan' (Apicius)? Schrijver defends the etymology *bVeto- 'which seethes' to the root of fermentum, ferveo, but I see little support for it. The meanings 'raging, swelling' (Lucr.) are clearly derived from 'strait, channel'. Formally, the suffix *-eto- usually takes the zero-grade of the root only in compounds (cf. Vine 1998a), or when derived from existing nouns (νιφετός). No good other etymology exists. Note that

PIE *Dh-T is not a canonical root structure, so that we cannot reconstruct a root *bʰrt-.

Bibl.: WH I:546f., EM 253, IEW 132f, Schrijver 1991: 255.



### fretus
fretus 'relying on' [adj. ο/α] (Naev.+)

Pit. *freto-.

It. cognates: V.frite [abL/loc.sg. or adv.] 'relying on' or 'with the aid of from *fret(o)-.

PIE *dʰr-ehrto-.

IE cognates: see s.v.firmus. Since the PIE root did not end in a laryngeal, the only way to explain fretus as a ptc. is to a (stative) verb 'to rely' in *-e-, like EM suggest This would require *-ehrto- in the vein of Nussbaum 1999a: 409, who discusses the type acetum 'vinegar' to acere 'to be sour'. The basic verb might be *dʰer- 'to hold', unattested as a verb but continued infirmus mdfere> or *bʰer- %to bear' as inferre. Semantically, since *aer- is an aoristic root, it seems more likely that this would have served to derive a stative verb: 'to be holding'.

<!-- pdf-page: 257; printed-page: 243; running-header: frio                                       243 -->

Bibl.: WH I: 505, EM 253f, IEW 253, Untermann 2000: 298, LIV *dʰer-. -^ fere, firmus.frenum



### ftigo, -ere
ftigo, -ere 'to roast' [v. Π1];^τ&/[not in ClM.],fiictum (Pl.+)

It. cognates: maybe LL frehteff [acc.pL]'?5, frehtu '?' (object).

IE cognates: Gn φρϋγω 'to roast5. Since the Greek verb is very close in form and meaning, but cannot be matched by means of sound laws, the verb might be a loanword from a third party, or from Greek into Latin in an indirect way. Giacomelli 1994: 36 assumes a Greek loanword, of whichph-» Lat./- and u » Lat. / show that it does not belong to the oldest layer of Greek loanwords, MMP bryz 'to roast' < Pin *bra(i)j~ has secondary j-vowels, cf. Cheung 2007. The absence of a pfi in CLat (fiixl only attested in grammarians) could also point to a loanword (M. Weiss, p.c).

Bibl.: WH I: 548, EM 254, IEW 137, Schrijver 1991:256, Untermann 2000:296.



### frigus, -oris
frigus, -oris 'cold, frost5 [n. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fiigere 'to be cold, lack vigour* (Andr.+; pf fiixi)^fiigefactare 'to make cold' (Pl.), frigescere cto become cool5 (Cato+), frigidus 'cold' (Pl.+)> fiigidariwn 'cold room' (Lucil.+), fiigedd 'cold' (Varro);perfiigefacere 'to make very cold5 (Pl.), perfiigescere 'to become very cold5 (Varro+), refiigerare 'to cool down (tr.)' (Cato+X refiigescere 'to cool down (intr.)' (Cato+).

Pit. *srigos- [n.], *snged-n-.

PIE *sriHg-o/es- 'frost, bitter cold'.

IE cognates: Gf. ρίγος 'cold, frost5, £ίγιον 'colder', pf έρρϊγα 'to be cold', pr. ρϊγέω (Pl.+)> ριγεδανός 'ghastly, terrible5. In view of the zero grade in the root, which is unusual for ^-sterns, Schrijver considers the possibility that Gr. ρίγος and Lztmfrigus are independent formations. In his view, fiigus might be derived fromfrigere, a stative verb with regular zero grade. However, the productive process in Latin usually yields s-stems in -or, -oris from verbs in -ere; hencefiigusseems older than this process. It seems more likely that the root had zero grade throughout-in PIE, or in the derivative from which Gr. and Lat took their reflexes. According to Nussbaum 2004a, Latmjrigedo and Gr. ριγεδανός jointly point to an apparent «-stem *snged-(6)n-,fromearlier *srige-(e)d-,

Bibl.: WH I: 547,'EM 254, IEW 1004, Schrijver 1991: 231, Stuber 2002: 152, LIV ?*sreiH(g)-.



### frio, -are
frio, -are 'to pulverize, crumble' [v. 1] (Varro+)

Derivatives: infriare cto crumble ingredients in or on' (Cato+); fricare 'to rub, chafe5 {Pl.+; pf fricul· » fricavi, ppp. fric(a)tum), circumfricare 'to rub round about' (Cato), confricare 'to rub, massage' (Pl.+), dejricate [adv.] 'sharply, keenly5 (Naev.), perfricare 'to rub all over5 (Cato+), refricare 'to rub again' (Cato+); fiivolus [adj.] 'worthless, insignificant' (Phaedrus+), JrTvolaria 'a play about trifles5 (PL apud Varronem), fiivusculum 'slight quarrel5 (Ulp.)> refrivus 'shredded' (Fest, Plin.).

Pit. *frt-a^ */r/-*-, *fii,wo-.

PIE *bʰriH-o-, *bʰriH-uo- 'cut\

IE cognates: Olr. brieid*, -bria 'to hurt, damage' <

<!-- pdf-page: 258; printed-page: 244; running-header: 244                                       frons -->

*brei-ase/o- [pr.sb.], Skt. pr. bhrinanti, YAv, pairlbrma- 'to cut (around)', broitira- 'blade'; OCS britU Cz. bfiti (arch.) 'to shave'. The verbfriare can be denominal to qn adj. *frio- < *bʰriH~o-, while re-fnvus and fnvolus continue an adj. *bʰriH-uo-, The v. fricare presupposes an adj. *fri-ko-y which was probably built secondarily to *fiio~.

Bibl.: WH I: 549, EM 255, IEW 166L, Schumacher 2004: 235, LIV ♦b'VeiH-.



### frōns, -ondis
frOns, -ondis 'foliage, leaves' [f. nd] (Enn.+; \%x.fiims,frund- a few times in Enn.)

Derivatives: frondere 'to have leaves* (Cato+), frondescere 'to become leafy' (Enn.+), frondosus 'leafy' (Enn.+)\frondifer 'leaf-bearing' (Naev.+). WH hesitatingly connect Germanic words in *bʰrm- for 'brim, bud', whereas EM regard the etymology as unsure. Solmsen (ZVS 35, 474ff.) compared Ru. dern 'lawn, grass', Gr. ύρόνα 'herbs, flowers', which might go back to a stem *dʰr(°)rK Yet in order to connect Lat frond- to this, a suffix *-rf- or *-di- must be added, which makes the comparison less likely. Of course, the Greek and Latin forms might go back to an identical Mediterranean substrate word of floral character. Alternatively, one might think of a stem *bʰr-n- 'load, yield, fruit' to the root *bʰer-, the nom.sg. of which would have been *bʰ(e)ron. To this, a suffix *-dʰ- could have been added (as in glans 'acom'), yielding *bʰr-on-dʰ-.

Bibl.: WH I: 550f., EM 255, IEW 142.



### fruns, -ontis
fruns, -ontis 'forehead, brow; front' [f. nt(i)] (Naev.+; Pl.+ also m.; gen.pVfrontium) No plausible etymology. WH and IEW connect several Celtic and Germanic words in *bʰrVnT- meaning 'side' or 'steep', but their meaning is not specific enough. Seniantically, it is tempting to compare frdns with PIE ^ b V u H - 4 eyebrow', but a putative formation *h₃bʰrouH-nt~ > *frowant- would have a suffix *-nt- that remains unaccounted for, and might phonetically rather be expected to yield *frUnt-. Xifrendd 'to grind one's teeth' is indeed an extension of a root *gʷʰren-, front- might reflect *gʷʰron-t- 'the side where the mouth is, front', vel sim. But this is very speculative, of course.

Bibl.: WH I: 551, EM 255, IEW 167.



### fruor, frill
fruor, frill 'to enjoy (the produce of)' [v. Ill; ppp. fructum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives:fructus9 -us 'enjoyment, revenue; fruit, produce' (Lex XII+),fruciuarius 'fruit-bearing, usufructuary' (Varro+), fructudsus 'fruitful, profitable' (Varro+); fiOx, -iigis [f.] 'crop, yield (sg.), fruit(s) (pi,)' (Lex XII+; esp. pLJ^wges),J/rwgf[*dat.sg. of frux] 'having merit, good, valuable'; frugaliter 'economically' (Pl.+), frugalior 'having more merit, more honest' (Pl.+); frumentum 'fruit of plants, corn, grain' (Pl.+), frumeniarius 'of com' (Cato+), frumeniarius [m.] 'a dealer in corn' (Pl.+); frumscere, frumius sum 'to enjoy' (Pl.+); frUgifer 'fruitful, productive' (Enn.), frugiferens 'fruitful' (Lucn), frugiparus 'giving birth to fruits' (Lucr,).

Pit. pr. *friig-je/o-y root noun *friig-.

It. cognates: U. frif, fri [acc.pl.] 'fruits' < *bʰrug~; O. fruktatiuf [nom.sg.] 'usufruct' < *frugtati6n- built to *frug-i-td- < *bʰrug-ito-, ppp. of *bʰrtig~io- 'to use'.

<!-- pdf-page: 259; printed-page: 245; running-header: fucus                                     245 -->

PIE pr. *bʰruHg-ie/o- 'to use',

IE cognates: Go, brukfan, pret bruhta, OE brUcan, OHG bruhhan, OS briikan 'to use', Go, bruks, OHG bruhhU OE bryce 'useful', Fruor reflects a development *frug-ie- > *fruje- > *fruwe- > *frue-. The stem *frug- appears in the root nounyhix (from Pit. date, as U,yh"#) shows), and mfrumentum < *friig-men- and friiriiscere < *frug-n-. The latter can reflect an earlier nasal present stem *fiHg-n-e/o→ which may be compared with Go, us-bruknan 'to break off, even though -wow-inchoatives are productive in Gothic, The restriction to Gm, and It., and the pervading zero grade, may cast doubts on a PIE origin; yet there is no decisive argument against it,

Bibl.: WH I: 551ff,, EM 256f,, IEW 173, Schrijver 1991: 232f,, Untermann 2000: 297-299, LIV ?*bʰreuH(g)-,



### frustum
frustum 'crumb, fragment' [n. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: frustulentus 'full of crumbs' (Pl.), frustatim 'in little pieces' (Pompon^), frustillatim 'id,' (Pl.+).

Pit. *frusto-.

PIE *bʰrus-to- 'broken',

IE cognates: Olr. bruid 'to break, smash', Old French bruiser 'to break' < PCI. *brus-ie^ Olr. bronnaid 'to injure' < *brus-na-ti, MW breu, W, brau 'brittle, fragile', MCo, brew, Bret, brev 'broken' < *bruso-, OE briesan, brysan 'to break, bruise' < *brqusija-; maybe Alb, bresher 'hail' < *bʰreus-n-. Latin frustum could represent an isolated Latin reflex* of a root *bʰrus- 'to break' found in Celtic and Germanic (and maybe Albanian), It has also been connected with Lith. druska 'salt', Latv, druska 'crumb, scrap, bit', Go, drauhsos 'crumbs, lumps', W. dryll 'piece, lump' < *dʰrus-. This is phonetically equally possible, but the presence of verb forms from *bʰrus- in Celtic and Germanic renders a derivation of frustum from *bʰrus- more likely (because of *-to~),

Bibl.: WH I: 553, EM 257, IEW 171, Schrijver 1995: 341, LIV *bʰreus-.



### frutex, -icis
frutex, -icis 'shrub, bush; shoot, 'blockhead'' [f, k] (Pl.+)

Pit. *frut-. '« Since the root of OE breowan 'to brew' is now reconstructed as *bʰreuH- 'to sprout', frutex can only be connected through a shortening from *fruto-3 but Schrijver 1991 acknowledges no shortening of long pretonic vowels in front of stops. Also, the semantic link between 'to boil' saidfrutexis not obvious. Alternatively,^/^ may be connected with Olr. broth 'awn, hair' (possibly from *bʰruto-\ but PIE origin is uncertain; it might be a borrowed word for a plant,

Bibl.: WH I: 554, EM 257, IEW 169, Schrijver 1991: 254.



### fucus
fucus 'drone, gadfly, hornet' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

PIE *bʰoi-ko-? *bʰou-ko-?

IE cognates: Olr. bech 'bee' [m.] < *bʰeko-; OPr. bitte, Lith. bite, Latv. bite < *bʰi-t-, OCS bbcela, tbcela 'bee' < *bʰi-kel-eh₂; OIc. by [nJ, MoDu, bij9 OHG bini [n.], OHG bia [f.], OE beo 'bee' [f] < *bVon-3 *bʰi-ni-, OE beaw 'gadfly', MoFr. bait 'hornet'?

<!-- pdf-page: 260; printed-page: 246; running-header: 246                                         fiigio -->

If from *foiko-,fiicus can be cognate with the Celtic, Gm. and BSl. words for 'bee*. But the ablaut grade *bʰoi- does not occur elsewhere. Since Celtic has *bʰe- in Olr. bech, it might be the case that we are pealing with an onomatopoeic woTd*bʰe/i-; Latin *bʰoi- can be a separate ablaut variant. There is another possibility, which was favoured by WH: a connection with Gm. *bau-a- (?) 'hornet, gadfly'. In fact, fiicus does not refer to the drone only, because it is said to be black by Varro, and Plautus says that it apibus peredit cibum. This would point to one of the bee's enemies, such as a wasp or a hornet. Instead of WH's reconstruction *bʰoukʷo-, which is unlikely (cf. Weiss 1995), *bʰou-ko- would do.

Bibl.: WH I: 555, EM 258, IEW 163.



### fugio, -ere
fugio, -ere 'to run away, flee* [v. Ill; fiigi,fugitum\ (Lex XII, Andr.+)

Derivatives: fuga 'the running away, fleeing, escape* (Pl.+),fugax 'fugitive, elusive' (Pl.+), fugare 'to cause to flee* (Pl.+),fugel(I)a 'flight' (Cato+\fi4gitare 'to run away from, avoid* (?\.+\fugitor 'who runs away' (Pl.), fugifivus 'runaway, fugitive' (Pl.+), fugitivarius 'who recaptures runaway slaves* (Varro+); aufugere 'to run away' (Pl.+), cdnfugere "to flee for protection' (Pl.+), defugere 'to make one's escape' (Pl.+), diffiigere 'to scatter* (Lucr.+), effugere 'to flee, escape' (Naev.+), effugium 'means of escape' (Lucr.+),perfugere 'to take refuge' (Sis,+),perfugium 'refuge, shelter' (Pl.+), prdfugere 'to run away* (Pl.+), refugere 'to turn back and flee, recoil' (Enn.+), suffugere 'to escape from under* (Lucr.+), transfugere 'to go over to the other side, desert' (Pl.+); lucrifuga 'a spendthrift' (Pl.).

Pit. pr. *fug-i-, pf. *foug-, subst *fiig-(a-).

PIE pr. *bʰug-i- 'to flee', pf. *(bʰe-)bhoug- (or root aorist *bʰeug-).

IE cognates: Gr. φεύγω, aor. φυγείν, pf. πέφευγα lto flee', φύγα-δε 'on the flight*, φύζα [f.] 'flight, panic', φυγή 'flight, exile'; Av. buj(i)- [f.] 'penance, atonement', bUjat 'frees*, bunjainti 'they free', bujaiiamna-, MP boz- 'to free, release'.

Bibl.: WH I: 556f., EM 258, IEW 152, Schrijver2003: 66, LIV l.*bʰeug-.



### fuī
fuT 'to be(come)' [v. pf.] ind, JuT, fuistl, Juit, fuimus, fuistis, fuerunt; sb. fua, fuat, ptc.fut.^/wrws, inf.fut.Jfcre (Lex XII, Andr.+)

Derivatives: future 'saepius fuisse' (Cato apud. Paul· ex F.)\ for the preverbial cp., see s.v. sum.

It. cognates: U.fittu, futu [2s.ipv.II], M.fututo [2p.ipv.II], Presam. ίυήτοδ [3p.pf.]5 O. fufans [3p.ipf.]5 fusid [3s,ipf.sb.], fufens [3p.pf.],find[3s.pf.sb.], O.fust, adfust, U. fust, fust, fits, atefafust, andersqfust, andirsafust, amprefu<u>s [3s.fut.pf.] , furent, fefure, ambrefurent [3p.fut.pt] 'to be'.

Original *fu-i, with shortening of long u in front of the next vowel. The form jfc- was generalized quite early, yielding fu-turus and fore < *fu-se. The forms fuds, fuat

represent an old aor.sb, in -<5-.

### fulcio, -ire
fulcio, -ire 'to support' [v. IV; ptfulst, ppp.fitltum](Pl.+)

Derivatives: fulcrum 'head- or back-support of a couch' (Varro+), fulmenta 'prop, support7 (Pl.+), fulmentum 'support' (Varro+); confulcire 'to press together' (Lucr.), praefulcire 'to place as a support, prop up' (Pl.+), suffulcire 'to propfrombelow' (Pl.+).

Pit. *dVbʰ-(o)l-k-. Lat. fulcrum < *fiilk-(t)lo-9 fulmentum < *fulk-rnen-to-, pf fubi < *fulk-s-aL The etymology is unclear. It is often assumed that Latin fitlk- is cognate with Gr. φάλκης [m.j 'rib (of a ship)', φάλαγξ, -γγος 'beam' and OIc. bjalia [m.], OHG balko 'beam' (<

PIE *2/Wg-?). These are connected with the verbal root *bʰelgʰ- 'to swell\ from which words for 'beam' seem to be derived in Baltic: Lith. balzieno Mongbeam', Latv. balziens, belziens 'prop'. Yet the semantic connection of'beam' or 'prop' to 'swelF is unclear to me, and the velar suffixes of Gr. and Baltic do not match; at most, we could posit a root *bʰel- with different velar suffixes.

Bibl.: WH I: 559, EM 258f, IEW 122f, LIV ?*bʰelk-. > '\ fulgG, -ere 'to shine brightly' [v: III; ptfulsT\ (Pac.+) ;

Derivatives: fulgere (Lucil,+), refitlgere 'to shine brightly' (Lucr.+), fulgens 'flashing, gleaming' (Enn.+), fulgetrum 'lightning' (Varro+), fulgidus 'shining' (Lucr.), fulgor 'brightness; flash' (Lucr.^); fulgur, -uris [n.j 'flash of lightning' (nom.sg. fulgus Paul, ex F*; also -eris Lucr.), fiilgurator 'one who interprets omens from lightning' (Cato+), fitlgurire 'to send lightning' (Naev.+; Naev. fitlgorivit); fulmen, -inis 'lightning, thunderbolt' (Naev.+\fulmineus 'of/like lightning' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *folg-e/o- 'to shine', *folg^os- [n.j 'brightness'.

PIE *bʰlg-e/o- 'to shine'.

IE cognates: Skt. bhargas- 'radiance, splendour' (appurtenance uncertain; schwebe-ablaut?); Gr. φλέγω 'to bum', Gr. φλόξ, -γός 'flame', φλογμςς 'flame'; OHG blecchen 'to make visible' < PGm. *blakjan\ ToAB polk- 'toshms\loBpalketar 'burns' < *bʰlg-. From VOLat. *folg~from*fjg>-. For the primarity offulgere over fulgere^ compare the similar situation with fervere and fervere. Yet the attestations allow no certain order, and the ptc. fulgens (the oldest attested form) is ambiguous. Fulgidus and fulgor are recent derivatives of fulgere following the productive pattern. The zero grade in fiilgur < *flgos^ -es- suggests that this noun was derived from the verb fulgere at an earlier stage; for fulmen < *fulgmen, the deverbal origin is certain, since the sound laws would have us expect **fiagmen as the outcome of

PIE *bʰlg-men-, \ϊfulgere is indeed more recent than fulgere, the cause for the zero grade in Latin (and in Tocharian) remains somewhat unclear. LIV assumes a PIE athematic present, but only Tocharian possibly presents evidence for such a formation,

Bibl.: WH I: 511, EM 259, IEW 124f, Schrijver 1991: 477, 483f, Stuber 2002: 75, UV^lJgK^flagro

<!-- pdf-page: 262; printed-page: 248; running-header: 248                                      fulica -->



### fiilica
fiilica 'a water-bird (the coot?)' [I a] (Afran.+)

Derivatives:^/**, -ids 'heron' (Cic). The suffix -ik- is found in other bird-namtfs too. Fulica is often compared with OHG belihha, -o, NHG Belche 'coot' < PGm. *bel-ik- < *bʰel-ig- having a white spot'; but Latin fill- can only reflect *-o/- (unless it is a form from a different dialect, which is an ad hoc assumption), and the suffixes do not match completely. The basic word is assumed to be *bʰe/olH- 'white' (Lith, balas, OCS belt 'white', Gr. φαλός 'white' Hsch.), but there is no guarantee that the Latin words refer to a bird with white characteristics. I conclude that the origin offiilica and fulix remains unknown»

Bibl.: WH I: 559f., EM 259, IEW 118ff.



### fiffigo, -inis
fiffigo, -inis 'soof [f. n] (Pl.+)

Pit. */w//- 'smoke*.

PIE *dʰuh₂-li- 'smoke, dust*.

IE cognates: Mir. duil 'desire'; Skt dhuli-, dhuli [I] Must', dhulika 'fog', Lith. dulis, Latv. dulis 'smoke used in bee-culture'. Latin derivation to an original /-stem *d\iH-Ii- 'dust', which may be derived from an adj. *dʰuli2-io- 'smoking, dusty'.

Bibl.: WH I: 560, EM 259, IEW 261-67, Leumann 1977: 368, Schrijver 1991: 233, 342, LIV *dʰueh₂-. -^fimus, suffio



### fulle, -nis
fulle, -nis 'fuller, launderer; kind of beetle' [rn. n] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fulldnia 'the fuller's trade' (Pl.), fullonica 'a fuller's shop' (Lab.+), fulldnius [adj.] 'otffor fulling' (Pl.+), Since *o does not become u in front of lUfullo must represent PLat. *fii-. There are many combinations which would yield -//-, but no root is available which contains *-u- and would provide a probable basis for fullo. Etymology unknown.

Bibl.: WH I: 560, EM 260.



### fulvus
fulvus 'brown, sandy, gold-coloured' [adj. o/a] (Enn.+)

Pit. *fefolawoA

PIE *dʰe/olH-uo- 'yellow'? The theoretical preforms for fulvus were listed by Driessen 2005: V/d^g^/g^? + *~e/o- + *-IHVU-I*-IHU- + -os. Unless, of course, *-wo- (which is also found in other colour adjectives) was a recent addition. The often cited connection with Lith. geltas 'yellow' <

PIE *gʰelh₃- is not possible, see s.v. helvus. Driessen connects fulvus with EMoDu. deluw, MoDu del 'yellow(ish)\ which could, among other forms, go back to *t/dʰelH-uo-. Latin fulvus could, in theory, reflect the same preform *dʰelH-uo-. Yet in view of the isolated position of the Dutch word within Gm., and the presence of rhyming colour words in -uw in Dutch, it seems a hazardous assumption. More promising is the connection with Armenian dalowkn 'jaundice', deiin 'yellow', delb 'yellow, blond', which could point to a root *dʰlH-, *dʰelH- for 'yellow'.

Bibl.: WH I: 561, EM 260, IEW 160, Schrijver 1991: 469, Nussbaum 1999a: 386f, Driessen 2005.

<!-- pdf-page: 263; printed-page: 249; running-header: fijndo                                       249 -->



### fūmus
fllnius 'smoke, fumes' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fumare 'to emit smoke or steam' (Pl.+), fiimosus 'smoky' (Cato+), fimidus 'smoking, smoky' (Lucr.+); fumigare 'to treat with smoke' (Var.+); fiimificare 'to make smoke; (Pl>),fiimificus'making smoke' (Pl.+).

Pit. *jumo-.

PIE *d\ih 2-mo- 'smoke, fume1.

IE cognates: Skt dhuma- 'smoke, fume', Gr. ΰυμός 'spirit', OPr. dumis 'smoke', Lith. pi. dumai 'smoke', OCS dymt 'smoke'; OHG town 'steam'. In a preform *dʰuHmo- we would expect Dybo's shortening (of pretonic shortening of long vowels in front of resonants in Proto-Italo-Celto-Germanic) to have taken place. The long vowel οι fiimus is explained by Schrijver 1991 from (possible) analogy with the (*)« infixUgoand suffio. The reconstruction of root-final *-h₂ is based on Hit. antuwahhas 'human* and tuhhae- 'to cough'.

Bibl.: WH I: 56if., EM 260, IEW 261, Schrijver 1991: 233, 342, 357, LIV *dʰueh₂- 4 to smoke' (intr.). → fimum,foeted,filigo,fur5, suffio



### funda
funda 'leather strap, sling' [f! a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fundulum 'the blind gut, caecum' iy^xm\jundula 'blind alley' (Varro). Theoretically^/idfl may go back to *bʰondʰ-h₂- 'tie, strap', a derivative of the verbal root *bʰendʰ- 'to bind' attested>in Gm. and Ilr. Alternatively, it might be a loanword from an unknown language; the Gr. noun σφενδόνη 'sling' might come from the same l source. ; i

### fundo, -ere
fundo, -ere 'to pour, let go, emit' [v. HI; pf.jftdi, ppp.fiisum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fiisus, -us 'a pouring' (Varro), fiisns 'spindle' (Lucr.+), funditare 'to pour out continuously; shoot at with slings' (Pl.+); qffimdere 'to pour onto' (Varro+), circumfundere 'to pour round, distribute' (Cato+), confundere 'to pour together, mix, confuse' (Pl.+), confiisicius (Pl.) 'confused', confiisim 'confusedly' (Varro), defimdere 'to pour out' (Cato+), diffundere 'to spread widely, diffuse' (Cato+), diffunditare 'to dissipate' (Pl.), diffusilis 'diffusive' (Lucr.), effundere 'to pour out, shed, utter' (Pl.+), infundere 'to pourin' (Pl.+), infundibulum 'funnel, hopper' (Cato+), offundere 'to pour, extend' (Pl.+), prqfundere 'to pour forth' (Naev.+); fiitis, -is 'water-vessel' (Varro), exfuii "ejfusT (PauL ex R).

Pit. *xund-{e/o-\

It. cognates: maybe Fal. huti[c]ilom [acc.n.?} '?'; uncertain U. hondu [3s.ipv.II] 'throw down!'(?).

PIE pr. *gʰu-n-d- 'to pour', pf. *(gʰe-)*gʰ(o)u-; noun *gʰe/ou-ti-?

IE cognates: Hit. kutt-, kutt- [c] 'wall' < *gʰeut-, *g\it-; Skt. juhoti 'pours; sacrifices' < *£i-ff(e)u-w a-huti- [f] 'offering', Av. a-ziiiti- [f.] 'clarified butter, sacrificial fat' < *-gʰuti-, Gr. χεω < pr. *g eu-e-, aonintr. έχυτο 'to pour', χυτός 'spilled', πάγχυ [adv.] 'completely'; Go. giutan, ToB. kit- 'to pour'. See Kiimmel 2004b: 357 on confisicius. Initial/ <

PIE *gʰ- is only attested in fundo, and might be conditioned by the vowel u (labial assimilation). Germanic also shows the root extension *-d~. In Latin, the ^/-present was remade into a nasal present. The

<!-- pdf-page: 264; printed-page: 250; running-header: 250                                     fundus -->

forms fiitis and *ex-futus could continue the d-less form of the root, with fiitis < *gʰeu-ti-. Note, however, that the semantics are not perfect (fiitis is not an abstract) and that the noun appears only in Varro.t

### fundus
fundus 'bottom' [m. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: funditus [adv.] 'completely; from the bottom' (ΡΙΛ), fundare 'to lay the foundations' (Pl.+), fundamentum 'foundation, basis' (Pl.+); profundus 'very deep, boundless' (Pl.+).

Pit. *fundo-.

IE cognates: Mir. bond 'foot sole', Skt. budhna- 'bottom* foot, root', A v. buna-, Gn πυύμήν 'bottom, depth, root', OIc. botn^ OHG bodam, OS bothme [dat.sg.], OE botem, OE bodan, OFr. bodem, MDu. bodem 'bottom' < PGm. *budme/on [nom.], *buttaz [gen.] < *bʰudʰ-me/6n, *bʰudʰ~(m)n-os (thus Kroonen 2006: 21-23). The Latin form is due to metathesis of

PIE *bʰudʰno- to Italo-Celtic *bʰuncto-.

fungor, -ϊ 'to perform; go through, enjoy; die; function' [v. Ill; functus sum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: fungfnus 'like a mushroom' (Pl.).

funis 'rope, cable' [m. (f.) i] (Cato+; gen.pl. funium, abl.sg. fune, acc.sg. funem, ablsg. fun! lx Cato) Derivatives; funiculus 'thin rope, cord' (Cato+); funambulus 'tightrope walker' (Ter.+); semifunium * half-length of rope' (Cato).

of the root *£"*ίΗ- or «g^Hi- which can be reconstructed for filum,
