### inSnis
inSnis 'empty, hollow' [adj. i] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: inaniae [f pi.] 'nothingness' (Pl.)5 inanlre 'to make empty' (Lucr.+); inanilogista [m.] 'babbler' (Pl.). The chronology of attestations suggests that 'empty, devoid of is older than 'hollow*. No certain etymology.

Bibl.: WH I:688f.,EM314.



### inciens
inciens 'big with young (of a female)' [adj. nt\ (Varro+) On the strength of the comparison with Gr. κυέω 'to be pregnant', inciens is mostly analysed as a derivative of the^PIE root *kuH- 'to swell'. The formal aspects of this etymology are disputed. According to Thumeysen's' rule (cf Meiser 1998: 86 and Schrijver 1991: 322-324), *« became ί before */, yielding a phonetic change *£uH-ie- > *kiije- > *kije-. Yet according to Schrijver 2003 and 2006: 50, the sequence *kuH-ie- would undergo a development to *kʷTje- in Proto-Italo-Celtic, whence we expect Lat. *qiti-. This problem is absent from the other examples for Schrijver's rule {plus, suffire, fieri) since *w might have disappeared after the labial obstruent. An alternative etymology is the following. The attestations show that inciens meant a woman 'at the verge of parting' (Paul ex F. inciens propinqua partui) as opposed to gravida and praegnans, which were more general terms for 'pregnant'. Of course, this might be a recent semantic specialization. Nevertheless, if inciens derives from a verb *inciere of the same semantic structure as incipere £to take in hand, start, begin an action', it could mean 'starting to give rise to, giving birth'. In that case, it would be a simple and recent derivative of tied.

Bibl.: WH I: 690, EM 314, IEW 592if, Meiser 1998: 86, Schrijver 1991: 322ff, 2003: 77f, LIV *kueh r . → cavus,tied,cumulus



### indiges, -etis
indiges, -etis 'epithet of certain gods' [m., adj. /] (Verg.+)

Derivatives: indigitare 'to invoke (deities) by certain formulas' (Varro+), indigitamenta [n.pl.] 'certain formulas used in invoking deities' (Paul, ex F.).

Pit. *end[o]-ag-et- 'working within'. The noun indiget- can be a derivative in *-o/et- of a compound *endo-ag-o- * working within (the community)', cf. inter-pret- 'go-between', prae-stes, -stit- 'witness*. The

<!-- pdf-page: 316; printed-page: 302; running-header: 302                                     indulgeo -->


verb indigitare will then be a recent derivative from the noun, meaning 'to turn to the indigites\

Bibl.: WH I: 693, EM 315. → ago, endo



### indulgeō
indulges 'to be indulgent' [v. II; pf. indulsi, ppp. indultum] (Ter.+)

Derivatives: indulgitds 'leniency' (Caelius, Sisenna).

Pit. *-άο\χ-ε-.

PIE *dlgʰ-ehr 'to be(come) fixed'?

IE cognates: Gaul, delgu [ls.pr.] (?) 'to hold' < PCI. *delge/o-, MW daly, data 'to hold', OBret. delgim 'to hold' < *da!ge/o- (<

PIE *dlg-ske/o~); Skt. dfmha- [pr.], dfhya [2s.ipv.act.] 'to fix, make firm', OAv. didBrzzo [2s.desid.inj.act] 'to desire to fasten', d&raz- 'fetter', YAv. darzzaiia- 'to fasten, tie', darzzra- 'firm, strong'; Go. tulgjan 'to fasten\ Probably a compound verb, with as a first member *en- 'in', *w- 'not' or *end(o) 'in', and as the second member *dVlg- or *Wg- or (after *end-) *(d)IVg-. Indulgere has been compared with longus, under the assumption that 'to be indulgent with someone' can mean 'to show perseverance, wait a long time'. But the nasalless variant of this adj. (PIE *dlHgʰ-) is not attested in Italic. A connection with langueo has been proposed, and is semantically better, but the root was *slh₂g-. LIV connects the root *delgʰ- 'to be hard, get fixed'. If the verb was a causative *dolgʰ-eie, one could posit a semantic shift from 'let so. become hard' to 'let so. get his way, be indulgent toward so/ Yet initial in- remains hard to explain. If analysed as a stative verb in *-eAr, indulged might be derived from a negated form of an adj. *n-dl$-ro- 'not hard\ Thus *n-dlgʰ-ehr 'not to be hard toward' - 'to be lenient toward, indulge'.

Bibl.: WH I: 694f, EM 315, IEW 196f., Schumacher 2004: 271f., LIV *delgʰ-.



### indutiae
indutiae 'armistice, truce' [f pi. a] (Pl.+) ' The noun suggests an adjectival base *duto-. Michael Weiss suggests to me a possible connection with

PIE *duh₂- 'to be able, arrange', hence *n-duh₂-tio- 'inability'. This would be interesting in view of the possible derivation of helium from bonus (see s.v.), which also concerns the context of war and battle.

Bibl.: WH I: 696, EM 316.



### Inferus
Inferus 'lower' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+; Cato nom.sg.m. infer)

Derivatives: infen, -drum [m.pl.] 'the inhabitants of the underworld' (Pl.+), inferior 'lower' (Pl.+); infimus 'lowest' (Pl.+), infu/imatis [adj.] 'of the lowest rank' (Pl.); infernus 'of the underworld; further down' (Pac.+); infra [adv.; prep.] 'below' (Pl.+)-

Pit. *enpero- 'lower'.

It. cognates: Fal. ifra 'beneath' [adv.].

PIE *ndʰero- 'lower', *ndʰmHo- 'lowest'.

IE cognates: GauL anderon [gen.pL] 'gods of the underworld'(?); Skt, adhara-, YAv. aSara- 'lower', ms <'dl> er 'low', YAv. adairi 'below'; Go. undar, OHG untar, untari 'under', Nom.sg. inferus must have restored -us on the basis of the other case forms. According to Giacomelli, the Fal. form confirms that the/in Latin can be a dialectal form. Others (WH, Leumann, Meiser) assume that *c? underwent treatment as if in anlaut because *en- was metanalysed as the preposition 'in' and *-dtero- as a separate

<!-- pdf-page: 317; printed-page: 303; running-header: inguen                                     303 -->

stem. This view can be supported by suffio (where the primary status of the simplex is clear) and maybe -fariam, where/< *-dʰ- also occurs word-internally.

Bibl.: WH I: 698, EM 317, IEW 771, Giacomelli 1963: 248f, Leumann 1977: 169, 423, Sihler 1995: 69, Meiser 1998: 105. → imus



### Infestus
Infestus 'hostile, aggressive' [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: manu/ifestus 'caught in the act, plainly guilty; obvious' (Lex XII+), manifestarius 'caught in the act' (Pl.+).

Pit. *en-fristo- 'rushing in'? According to Leumann 1977: 390, manu/ifestus derives from *manu festus with iambic shortening of the first element. If manifestus may be interpreted as 'caught by hand', the meanings seem to point to 'grabbing' or 'attacking' for -festus. The connection with

PIE *dʰers- 'to be bold' is not very compelling» Latin -fest- may stem from *fast-, -st- may be from -Rst-, -RTst- or -7i/-. Closest in form would be fastigium 'top, summit', fastus 'pride' < *bʰrst-, but this yields no sense. The etymology of infestus as 'implacable' < *n-gʷʰect-to~y still supported by Leumann 1977: 168, is semantically farther off, and phonetically possible only if we assume restoration of *-fo- after dental clusters became ss (unlikely) or if we assume a suffix *-5/o-. If festtnare^ confestim contain a noun *festi- 'hurry' < *fristi-y this would match infestus ('rushing in'), but not so well manifestus/Thus, maybe the two must be separated· 'Λ

Bibl.: WH I: 698f., EM 317,385, IEW 259. -^festmo ;



### ingens, -entis
ingens, -entis 'huge, vast, numerous' [adj. nt] (Pl.+)

Pit. *mgBnt-*

PIE *mg-(e)h₂-(e)nt-,

IE cognates: Skt mahantam [acc.sg.f.], mahati [f.] 'great, big', YAv. mazant- 'id.' < Ilr. *maj(a)H-ant- <

PIE *meg-h₂-nt-. WH's explanation as in-gens 'wovon es kein Entstehen gibt' is incomprehensible to me: it cannot be compared with Skt abhva-, since the latter is endocentric, whereas ingens would have to be exocentric. Muller's proposal (1926) of a derivative in *-nt- to

PIE *mg- is more promising. Since a theoretical preform *mg-nt- 'great' would yield Lat *magent~ (cf. magnus < *mg-no-\ we may derive ingens from

PIE *mg-hrent- (> *ingant- > ingent-) or *mg-ahr(e)nt- (> *ingant- > *ingant > *ingent-). Thus, the Latin form would have the same two suffixes as Ilr.

Bibl.: WH I: 700, EM 317, IEW 373ff., Schrijver 1991: 484. → magnus



### inguen, -inis
inguen, -inis 'swelling on the groin, bubo; groin' [n. n] (Lucil.+) Pit V*WK

PIE *ngʷ-en, -n-os 'the nude one'.

IE cognates: Gr. άδήν, -ένος [f., mj 'gland'. Schrijver 1991 and Beekes (fthc.) separate the Greek word from Latin and North-Germanic (OIc. ekkvenn 'thick, clodded', Mole, ekkr [m.] 'glans, gland, tumour' < PGm, *enkua- < IE *engʷo-), because Greek cannot have had an initial laryngeal, whereas in meaning, Latin and Gm. are closer to each other. Yet both Greek 'gland' and Latin 'groin' can be explained semantically on the basis of 'naked',

<!-- pdf-page: 318; printed-page: 304; running-header: 304                                      inquino -->

the meaning of

PIE *negʷ- (see nudus). Hence, we may rather discard the Germanic forms (they primary meaning seems to be 'swelling, ulcer'), and reconstruct a PIE «-stem derived from 'nude\

Bibl.: WH I: 701, EM 317£, IEW 319, Schrijver 1991: 59, Sihler 1995: 162. → midus



### inquinO, -are
inquinO, -are 'to make dirty' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: coinquinare 'to pollute' (Acc.+); cunire 'to defecate' (Paul, ex F.); ancunulentae 'women having their period' (Paul ex F.\

Pit. *kʷina-je/o-, *k"oin-je/o-l The word cannot be connected with caenum; see s.v.

### Insece / inquam
Insece / inquam 'to say' [v. in\] (Andr.+); pr.ipv. insece, inseque (Andr., Επα), pf. insexit (Enn.) 'to tell'; pr.ind. inquam (Pl.+) Ί say; of course', inquis, -it, -imus, -itis, -iunt 'says' > 'said', ipv. inque, inquito 'say!'; other moods and tenses follow conjugation IIlb (Pl.+).

Derivatives: insectid 'story* (GeL).

Pit. pr. *en-sekʷ-e/o- (?), aor. *en-skʷ-e/o~.

PIE aor. *skʷ-e/o- 'to follow'.

IE cognates: Olr. insce 'discourse' < *en(iyskʷ-ia, Olr. seichidf, seich* 'to say', OW MW hebu 'to say', MCo. gorthybi 'to answer', OBret. hep 'says* < PCL *sekʷ-e/o-; Gr. pr. έν(ν)έπω < *en-sekʷ-; aor ένιοπεΐν, ipv. έσπετε < *έν-σπ-ετε; ένίσσω < *si-sekʷ-/skʷ-; Lith. (diaL) sekti, 3s. seka 'to tell'; OIc. segja, OS seggian 'to say' < IE *sokʷeie-. The shift from 'follow* to 'tell' can be explained via 'repeat, relate'. The forms insece and inseque occur in texts modelled on Greek epic, and might be caiques on έννέπω, using Latin *in-sequd. But they can also be regarded as inherited from the PIE present; the delabialized velar would have been generalized (*inseque » insece). Latin inquam < *en-skʷ-a- (original ls.sb. Ί will say') and inquit < *en-sk™-e-t9 probably from the PIE (thematized) aorist. To inquit, a complete verbal paradigm was then built. The disappearance of *s in *en-skʷ- is difficult to account for, but must be accepted. The only possible parallel is tranquillus, but its etymology is uncertain. Much more problematic is the assumption of a reduplication present *en-si-skʷ-e- (Hackstein 1997: 37-42), since this would in addition require syncope in a closed syllable.

Bibl.: WH I: 702f, EM 318, IEW 897f, Leumann 1977: 212, 531, Sihler 1995: 5461, Meiser 1998:117,214, Meiser 2003: 147, Schumacher 2004:565f, LIV 2 *sek-. — sequor



### TnsolSsco, -ere
TnsolSsco, -ere 'to become overbearing' [v. Ill] (Sall.+)

Derivatives: insolens, -ntis 'unaccustomed, unfamiliar' (Ter.+), 'immoderate, haughty' (Cic.+), Insolentia 'unfamiliarity' (Turp.+), 'extravagance, arrogance' (Cic.+). The older etymology says that these words are derived from soled 'to be accustomed1, via a semantic shift from 'to be unaccustomed1 to 'be out of the ordinary, exaggerated'. This is actually supported by the chronology of the meanings of Insolens and Insolentia. The pr, Insolesco would then be a more recent derivative

<!-- pdf-page: 319; printed-page: 305; running-header: mstigare                                      305 -->

from Tnsolens (Sall.+). Another etymology, first proposed by Prokrovskii in 1898, separates insolesco from soled, and connects it with IE verbs for 'to swelP such as German schwellen, whence with a frequently observed semantic shift 'to brag\ Meichert 2005 has taken up this proposal, connecting Insolesco with Hit. sulle-fsulla- 'to become arrogant', sullatar 'swollenness; wantonness' <

PIE *suIH-ehr 'to be(come) swollen'. Similarly LIV, To my mind, the inner-Latin chronology of the meanings points to the derivation from soled,

Bibl.: WH I: 704, EM 318£, LIV ?*suelH-. → soled



### Instar * counterpart, the equivalent'
Instar * counterpart, the equivalent' [n. only as nom. or ace] (Varro+) It is tempting to see in instar a derivative of *in-stare [inf.] 'to stand in' > 'balance' (WH), but the use of an inf. as a n. noun is not ancient in Latin. Also, the apocope of -e is unusual (even if it might be regular originally). Finally, the semantic motivation is weak: instar + gen. simply means 'the equivalent oF whereas instare means 'to assail, take a stand'. The origin would have to lie in an earlier period, when in + stare meant 'to stand in\ One might think of an original cp. *(hje)n-stehros 'the standing in' > *in-stas 'the cost' vel sim. Compare iubar for the phonetics of -or.

Bibl.: WH I: 705, EM 319. → sto



### instaurō, -āre
instauro, -Sre 'to repeat, restore' [v. I] (Cic.+)

Derivatives: restaurdre cto restore, rebuild' (Tac.+).

Pit. *stauro- 'big, strong'. Λ ■

PIE *sth₂u-ro- ( » *steh₂u-ro-) 'big, strong5.

IE cognates: Skt. sthura-, ΥΑν. °stura- [part of names] 'big, strong', Oss. styr/(i)stur4 id.'; OSw. stur, MLG stur cbig, strong, coarse'. If the word is inherited, the composition of in + staurare must be recent, otherwise we would expect *insturare. According to EM, restaurdre replaced mstaurare because the meaning 're-' is unusual for in-. A nominal form *stauro- could reflect

PIE *steh₂u-ro-, which reminds us of Skt. sthiira- 'big, strong' < *stuh₂-ro- < *sth₂-u-n>, to the root *steh₂- 'to stand'. Thus, pre-Latin *stauro- would have meant 'strong, big\ from which 'to restore' is easier to understand than from words for 'pole, staff adduced by WH (Gr. σταυρός, OIc. staurr\ The full grade can be due to influence from the verb.

Bibl.: WH I: 705f., EM 319, IEW 1004ft → sto



### InstTgflre
InstTgflre 'to incite, provoke' [v. I] (Ter.+)

Pit. *steig-(e/o-).

PIE *steig- 'to prick, sting'.

IE cognates: Skt. ati stigh- 'to overcome', ά-stig- 'to harm, penetrate, assail', stega- [m.] 'which stings / cane', YAv, stija [ins.sg.] 'with the tip (of the tusk)', Skt. tejate [pr.med.], tetikte [3s.med.int] 'to sharpen', fiksna- [adj.] 'sharp, keen-eyed', tejas- [n.] 'sharp edge (of knife), sharpness', YAv. bi-taeya- 'having two sharp edges', brdWrdJaeza- 'sharp due to the blade', tiyra- 'cutting, pointed, sharp' (in cp. tizi-), Gr. σχίζω 'to sting, tattoo', στίγμα [n.] 'stab, brand'; NHG stechen 'to sting', Distel 'thistle' (< PGm. *pihstila-).

<!-- pdf-page: 320; printed-page: 306; running-header: 306                                    insula -->

Probably a denominal verb to *steig-(o~) *sharp point', or a Latin compound verb in -are derivedfroman earlier present *st!ge/o- < *steig-(e/o-).

Bibl.: WH I: 706£, EM 649, IEW 1016f., Cheung 2007: 361f., Lubotsky (fthc.), LIV *(s)teig-, → stingud



### Insula island'
Insula island' [f. a] (Naev.+)

IE cognates: Olr. wis, W. ynys 'island' < *ine/issT-9 Gn νήσος [f.] 'island', Dor. νασος, Rhod νασσος. The etymology as *en-sal-o- 'what is in the salt(y)' > 'in the sea5 > 'island' is theoretically possible as far as the phonetics go, but being 'in the sea' is not a very precise description of what an island is; furthermore, the Indo-Europeans seem to have indicated with 'island5 mainly 'river islands'. One might connect Lat solum 'soil, ground', but a formation *en-sol-hr with a preverb would be hard to explain. Since no other etymology is obvious, it may well be a loanword from an unknown language. The same language may be the source for the Celtic and Greek words, which also contain η and s.

Bibl.: WH I: 707, EM 319, IEW 878f.



### inter
inter 'among, between' [prep.] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: interior 'inner, internal' (Ter.+), intra [prep., adv.] 'within, inside' (Pl.+), intm [adv.] «inside' (Pl.+); intrare 'to enter' (Pl.+); interatim «interim' (Pl.+), interea(d) 'in the meantime5 (Andr.+), interim 'meanwhile5 (Pl.+); intimus 'inmost, closest5 (Pl.+).

Pit. *nter 'between', *ntero- «interior5, *ntamo-»

It. cognates: Ven. a(n)tra 'within'; O. anter prep. + ace. 'between', + abl. 'within'; as preverb (λ anter-, ander-, U. anter-; *nter 'between'.

PIE *hi(e)nter [adv.] 'between', *hi(e)n-tero- 'situated within', *hi(e)n-tmHo- 'innermost'.

IE cognates: Olr. eter, OW ithr, Co. ynter, yntre, Bret, etre 'between5 < PCI. *enter9 Skt antar, OAv. antardy YAv. antar3 'between, within' < *enter9 Skt. anfara-, YAv. antara- 'interior' < *entero~; Skt. antama-, YAv. cmtema-'most intimate'; Gr. έντερα [piJ 'intestines', Arm. 9nderk\ RuCSjatro 'liver, (pi.) entrails', SCr. jetra 'liver' < PS1. *j?tro < *hien-tr-om; OIc. idrar [pi.] 'intestines' < PGm. *enperdz, Go. undaurni-mat 'midday meal', OIc. undorn 'before midday5, OHG untorn 'midday5 < *npurna- < *ntrno~9 OE OS undern 'before midday5 < *nterno-9 Go. undar, OHG untar 'between' < PGm. *unber < *nter; OPr. instran 'fat' < *n-s~tro-9 Lith. jscios [f.pl.] 'womb, entrails, interior', Latv. ieksas 'entrails5 < *n-s-tio-. Lat. inter continues the PIE locatival adverb derived from *hien 'in5. The adj. in *-tero~ is continued in the original abl.sg. forms intra and intrd, and in inter-ion The sup. intimus is also inherited from PIE. The n. of ^en-tero- was apparently lexicalised to 'entrails' in PIE already.

### interpres, -tis intermediary, agent'
interpres, -tis intermediary, agent' [m. /] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: interpretari 'to explain, interpret' (Pl.+), interpretatio 'explanation' (Varro+).

Pit. *-pore-t- 'who crosses',

PIE *por-o- 'crossing'?

IE cognates: Gr. πόρος [m.] passage, ford, road; means', άπορος 'with no way out, impassable'; see s.v. porto. WH propose that interpres is a backformation to interpretari, which would mean 'to determine the mutual value' to pretium. Yet in that case, one would expect *interpretiaru A root *preU has been proposed for Go.frapjan and Lith, prasti 'to understand', but 'understanding' is a secondary meaning of the Latin forms. Forms such as super-stes to stare bring Nussbaum 2004b to the following analysis: the second member contains a /-stem derivative to the IE root *per- 'to come over, cross\ Hence: *enter-poro- 'going between' » *enter-pore-U 'who goes between', then syncope nom.sg. *enterporess > *enterpress > interpres. A thematized compound *enter-pr-o- may also be envisaged.

Bibl.: WH I: 710f, EM 320, LIV l.*per-. -oporto\pretium



### intestīnus
intestfnus 'internal, civic, domestic' [adj. o/a\ (Varro+)

Derivatives: intestvnum 'intestines, guts' (Pl.+), 'alimentary canal' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *nter-sto- 'internal'. The meaning 'intestines' in the earlier attested intestfnum, and the meaning 'domestic' of the later adj., suggest that we are deajing with an adj. originally meaning 'internal'. WH seem to assume a derivative in *-ft"o- to

PIE *entos 'inside', which is possible, but not the best solution. Leumann 1977 reconstructs *inter-stmo- with regular development of *-rst- to -sf-, referring to Forssman 1965. This would mean that interstes has restored r, which is unproblematic. The suffix can be reconstructed as *-stlno-, which Forssman regards as the noun *^sth₂-i- plus the suffix *-HO-. Maybe more likely is *-sth₂-o- (based on compounds in *-steh₂- as in Ilr.) with subsequent replacement of the suffix *-o- by *-ϊηο-.

Pit. *wita~je/o- 'to pursue',

PIE *uih r to- 'pursued'.

IE cognates: see invitus. We may posit a derived verb *witqje- 'to pursue', prefixed with in- 'in'. Yet the frequentatives are normally built on the ppp., but votus < *uoueto- shows o-vocalism.

### iocus
iocus 'joke, jest' [m. ο] (Pl.+; also ioca, -drum [n.pl.])

Derivatives: iocan 'to jest, joke' (Pl.+), iocosus 'fond of jokes, funny' (Varro+), iocularis [adj.] 'laughable' (Ter.+), iocularius 'id.' (Ten), ioculus 'joke' (Pl.).

Pit. *joko-.

It. cognates: U. iuka, iuku [acc.pl.] 'words1 or 'prayers' < *iok-o-, PaeL iocatin [3p.pf. ] maybe 'to order, command', denom. to *ioko-.

PIE *iok-o- 'word, utterance1,

IE cognates: MW ieith, W. iaith, Bret, yezh 'language', Mir., Molr. icht 'people' < PCL *iextV-; OHG jehan, OS gehon 'to express, utter', OHG jiht 'confession1. Lith. juokas 'laugh, laughter, (pi.) joke(s)', Latv.ywofc 'joke' are probably borrowings from German.

### ipse, ipsa, ipsum
ipse, ipsa, ipsum 'himself, herself, itself [pron. adj.] (Lex XII, Andr.+; nom.sg.m. ipsus Andr. to Cato)

Derivatives: ipsissimus 'the very same' (Pl.+).

Pit. *so-pe-so » *e(s)-pe-so.

It. cognates: O. essuf, esuf, U. esuf [pron.] '(he) himself or 'there7, maybe < *eps(o) + -on-s.

PIE *soso 'that'. The oldest forms are compounds of inflected is, es, id + -pse, probably < *-pe-so, with the PIE pronoun *so: eapse, eumpse, eampse, eapse. Some forms have double inflection in Plautus and other OLat. authors: eumpsum, eapso; but Jimenez Zamudio 1989: 120 argues that these forms are erroneous spellings for eumpse, eopse. There are also a few isolated forms with *so- in both members: nom.sg.f sapsa (Enn.Pac), accsg.m. sumpse (Pl.). Meiser regards -p- as an anaptyctic consonant between two m's in the acc.sg. {*sumsum, *samsam), but it may have been the particle *-pe: *so-pe-so, etc. In that way, the Sabellic forms can be connected more easily. Thus, we may posit the following chronology:

PIE *soso 'this' (cf Lat. -so) was replaced in Pit. by *so-pe-so (> Lat. sapsa) and *e(s)-pe-so (> *ispse, eapse, eumpse etc.). Both members of this reduplicated pronoun were originally inflected. In the nom.sg.m., final *-so gave Lat. ~sey medial *-e- was syncopated (as it was in Sab.) and initial i- was not recognized as a pronoun anymore. Final -se seems to have been generalised in the pronouns attested in Plautus, but it is doubtful whether this happened in the whole language. In post-Plautine Latin, initial i- spread to the other forms of the pronoun, and the inflection was restricted to the ending of the word: ipse, ipsa, ipsum.

Bibl.: WH I: 716f., EM 322f, IEW 281-286, Leumann 1977: 471, Jimenez Zamudio 1989, Sihler 1995: 394f., Meiser 1998: 163f, Untermann 2000: 235f. -* is, *pe, -so



### īra
īra 'anger, rage' [f. a] (Naev.+; PL <eira>)

Derivatives: iracundus 'irascible' (Pl.+), Tracundia 'hot temper, passion' (Pl.+);

<!-- pdf-page: 323; printed-page: 309; running-header: IS                                        309 -->

īrascī 'to be(come) angry' (Pl.+), iratus 'angry1 (Pfi+).

Pit. *eis/ra-.

PIE *hieis-h₂- 'anger'.

IE cognates: Av. aesma- 'wrath5, Gr. οιμα 'spring, rush' < *hiois-mo-; Skt. is- 'refreshment strength', ToB aise 'power5. The spelling eira is found in a word-play with era 'mistress', which might point to an (archaic) pronunciation [e:ra] of the word 'anger'. In that case, the word must contain

Pit. *ei. The suffix -cundus is rare, but cannot be original in iracundus; it must have been adopted from other adj. in -cundus. The adj. iratus was formed directly from Tra; the verb irascl, on the other hand, cannot be derived from the noun, and must be a back-formation to iratus. It is uncertain that ira contains intervocalic *-s-9 and also the semantic connection between the surmised PIE root *hiis- 'to urge5 and words for 'anger5 is hardly compelling. Thus, I accept this etymology in the absence of a better one.

Bibl.: WH I: 717f, EM 323, IEW 299-301, Leumann 1977: 63fi, 333, Schrijver 1991: 37, Meiser 1998: 58, LIV *hieish₂-.

PIE *h₃riH-to- 'whirled, stirred\

A frequentative would be possible only if we assume haplology from *ntitare.

### is, ea, id
is, ea, id 'this, that' [pron. adj.] (Lex XII, Andr.+); sg. nom.m. is (lx eis)9 acc.m. im9 em (Lex ΧΠ) » eum, nomacan. id, gen.m.n.f. eius (= eiius), dat.m.n. *eiiei > ei > ei9 abl.m.n. eod > eo, nom.f. eay acc.fi earn, dat.fi eae » ei, abLf. ead > ea; pi. nom.m. eeis (CIL), e/, els (Pl.) > «, &, acc.m. eos, nom,acc.n. ea> gen.m.n. eum (Paul. ex F,, CIL) »edrum9 dat.abl.m.n. eieis (CIL) > /&, » ibus (Pl.+), nom.fi eae, acc.f. eas, gen.f earum, dat.abl.fi eis (Pl.) > i&, » eabus (Cato)

Derivatives: ea 'along that path' (Cato+), eo 'thither' (Cato+), 'therefore' (Pl.+), ideo 'for the reason (that)1 (Pl.+). Pit nom.sg.m. *iy, acc.sg.m. *im9 nom.acc.sg.n. *id; f. *ejo/a- in nom.sg.fi, acc.sg.f, *esm- in dat.loc.sg.m.n.

It. cognates: O. izic, U. erek, ere, erec> ere [nom.sg.m.], CX iuk, iiuk, ioc [nom.sg.fi], ionc, U. eu [acc.sg.m.], O. Iak, U. earn [acc.sg.f.], O. idik, idik, idic, U. efek [nom.acc.sg.n.], O. eiseis, elseis, eizeis, U. ererek, erer9 irer [gen.sg.m.n.], erar [gen.sg.fi], esmik, SPic. esmik [dat.sg.m.n.], O. eisud, eis[ud] , eizuc, eizucen, U. eruku, erucom (+ -com) [abl.sg.m.n.], O. eisak, U. erak [abl.sg.fi], O. elsei, esei, eizeic [loc.sg.m.n.], SPic. esmen, esmin [idem + -en] , (X ejisai [loc.sg.fi], ivsc [nom.pl.m.], eisiuss [acc.pl.m.?], U. eaf, eaf9 Marr. iafc [acc.pl.fi], O. ioc, U. eu, eo [nom.acc.pl.n.], O. eisunc, Marr. esuc„ U. em, erom, ero [gen.pl.m.n.], O. eizazunc [gen.pl.fi], eizois [dat.abl.pl.m.n.], eizasc [dat.abl.pl.fi]

<!-- pdf-page: 324; printed-page: 310; running-header: 310                                          iste -->


'this* < stems */-, *ςιο-, *eiso-, *esmo- with or withour particle *-te: *i- in nom.sg.m., nom.acc.sg.n; *ejo- in nom.sg,f., acc.sg.pLf.; *esm- in dat.loc.sg.m.n.; *eiso- in remaining case forms. Nonp.acc.sg.n. *id-id-k(e), the second syllable of which spread to the nom.sg.m. and sometimes gen.sg.

PIE *(h₁)i- [nom.acc.sg. m.f.n.], *h₁e-sm-/-si-/-i- (elsewhere) 'he, she, it*.

IE cognates: Olr. e (he) 'he' < *e(i)s or *em, ed(hed) 'it', si 'she', gen.sg.m.n. ai, ae 'his (one)\ MW eid-aw 'his' < *esio; Gaul, eiabi [ins.pl.f], eianom [gen.pl.f] < *e(s)ia-\ Hit. asi / uni / ini 'that (one)' < *hios + -i, •hjom + i (hio- « ^ e - according to Kloekhorst 2008), *hii-, datloc.sg. edi < *h₁e-; Skt iyam [nom.sg.f ], idam [nom.acc.sg.n.], imam [acc.sg.m.] 'this here, he', OAv. it [nom.acc.sg.n.], YAv. Jm [nom.sg.f], imsm [acc.sg.m.], OP iyam [nom.sg.m.f.] < *h₁i-; Skt. ayam [nom.sg.m.], asmai [dat.sg.m./n.], asmat [abl.sg.m./n.], asyo [gen.sg.m./n.] 'this here, he', Av. aem [nom*sg.m.], OAv. aiism [nom.sg.m.], ahmai [dat.sg.rn.], ahmat [abl.sg.m.]; ahiia, axiiaca [gen.sg.m./n.], YAv. ahe [gen.sg.m7n.] <

PIE *h₁e-; Gr. (Cypr.) xv 'eum, earn', Gr. μίν, νίν; Lith.jis *he\ji 'she', OCS ϊ 'that, he, who',ya 'she',ye 'it' <

PIE *(h₁)i-(o)-; Go. is 'he', acc.sg. ina, n. ita9 acc.sg.f. ija, OHG er, ir [nom.sg.m.]. In Latin, the stem */- survives in is, id, and archaic /m, em, whereas *ei- from the plural and from gen.sg. has become the basis for the other case forms. Beekes (1995: 203) explains nom.sg.m. is < *es, with unstressed development of the voweL This is possible, but not compelling. Gen.sg. *esjo[s] has been used as a new stem for the whole pronoun. The gen. eiius itself is difficult to explain. In Sabellic, we find remains of

PIE *e-sm- in the oblique case forms, the partial spread of *eis- and also of * ejo-.

### iste, ista, istud
iste, ista, istud 'that of yours' [pron., pron. adj.] (Naev.+); gen.sg. istius, dat.sg. isti, but Pl., Cato also gen.sg.m. isti^ datsg.f. istae

Derivatives: istic, -aec, -uc [pron. adj.] 'that of yours' (Pl.+); isiic 'over there' (Pl.+), istinc 'from over there' (Pl.+), isto (Pl.+), istoc (Pl.+), istuc (Pl.+) 'to where you are', istorsum 'in your direction' (Ten).

Pit. *es-to-.

It. cognates: U. estu [acc.sg.m.], Presam. estam [acc.sg.f.], U. este, este [acc.sg.n.], SPic. estas [nom.pl.f.?], U. estu, esto, estac [acc.pl.n.] 'this' < *i/es-to-; n. *es-ti-d.

IE cognates: see s.v. is and so-. Latin iste and Sab. *esto- may go back to the same preform *es-to- if Latin has replaced *es- by is-. The first element might be the same *es- found in the oblique case forms of *e-//-, dat. *esmoi, etc. The second element can be identied with the

PIE pronoun *so-/*to- (see Lat. so-). The asigmatic nom.sg.m. *so was apparently replaced (maybe already in Italo-Celtic) by *fo. It has been proposed that, alternatively, this may have been the uninfected particle *-te, but since all Italic

<!-- pdf-page: 325; printed-page: 311; running-header: iterum                                      311 -->

languages show inflection (unlike with *-ke), this seems less likely.

Bibl.: WH I: 72Ifi, EM 324, IEW 281-286, Leumann 1977: 470, van der Staaij 1995: 137, Sihler 1995:394, Meiser 1998: 163, Untennann 2000: 236f. → is, so-



### ita
ita 'in the same way as, thus' [adv.] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: itaque 'in consequence, so' (Pl.+), item 'in the same way' (Pl.+), itidem 'in the same way' (Ph+).

Pit. *i-to-.

It. cognates: U. itek [adv.] 'thus, as said' < *itei + -k(e) . Itidem < *ita-dim. Theoretically, ita may represent *ito with iambic shortening, but item and itidem cannot have *ita. When we compare Skt iti 'in this manner', Lith. it [adv.] 'just like, in a manner of speaking' < *hiiti (?), Skt ittha, itthad 'here, there', Av. ipa 'thus', Lat ita could reflect

PIE *hiith₂. Alternatively, we may compare -ta with the second element of is-te, and regard it as the original nom.acc.pl.n. *teh₂ 'those', which acquired short *-a as the other neuters, and was used as ace. of extension: 'in this respect' > 'in this way\ In origin, then, ita could go back to a compound pronoun from

PIE *hji 'it' and *to- 'that'.

Bibl.: WH I: 722fi, EM 324, IEW 281-286, Leumann 1977: 92, Schrijver 1991: 80, Untermann 2000: 349f → is, iste



### iter, itineris journey, route, road'
iter, itineris journey, route, road' [n. r/n] (Naev.+; variants: nom.acc.sg. itiner Pl.+, gen,sg. iteris Naev.+, abl.sg. iter§ Acc.+)

Pit. *eitor, *iten(o)sl j PEE *hiei-tr, gen.sg. *hji-ten-s, loc.sg. *h₁i-ten 'way, journey'.

IE cognates: Hit itar [n.] 'way' (< *hlei-tr, ^i-ten-s? cf Rieken 1999: 374-377); YAvrpairiuna- 'due lifetime' (< Pllr. *pari-itna- <

PIE *hjit-n-o-)'9 ToB ytarye 'road, way', ToA ytar 'road, way' < *hii-tor. The nom.sg. in -er seems to point to *-er, which would be unique. Lat *}aser! 'blood' (if this is the right reconstruction) would be the only other r/n-stem in -er, but its IE cognates point to a proterodynamic neuter. For iter, Tocharian seems to continue a collective in *-qr, but Hittite -t- /d/ may be best explained by assuming an original PD paradigm. For Li&tin, one could assume that the nom.acc.sg. iter replaces earlier *itur < *itor « *h₁ei-tr, Klingenschmitt and Meiser assume a paradigm with nom.sg. *h$i-tir, gen,sg. *hji-tn-es, which would be unique for a n. noun. The loc.sg. *hIi-ten which Meiser 1998 assumes to have served as the basis for itin-eris, is consistent with either kind of paradigm. The syllable *-en- can be the source for the analogical -e- in the nom.acc. iter. Willi 2004: 326 assumes that the oblique ending -neris was adopted as such from the neuter abstracts in -nus, -neris such as facinus 'crime'. Obviously, there is no perfect model for such a replacement, but it seems the best explanation for itineris so far.

Bibl.: WH I: 408, EM 197, IEW 294f, Klingenschmitt 1992: 18, Meiser 1998: 142, UV *hirih. → eo



### iterum
iterum 'again, for the second time' [adv.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: iterare 'to repeat' (Pl.+).

### iuba
iuba 'mane; plume' [f. a] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: iubatus 'having a mane' (Naev*+).

Pit. *jupa-.

PIE *(H)iudh-h₂- 'that moves, moveables'. WH assume that iuba was derived from the root of iubere as 'moving to and fro', Leumann 1977 'shaking5. This is rejected by EM, but since a better etymology is absent, we may accept it for the time being: *iub-a 'that moves (to and fro)' > 'mane, plume'.

Bibl.: WH I: 724, EM 325, IEW 51 If., Leumann 1977: 279, LIV *Hieudʰ-. → iubeo



### iubar, -aris
iubar, -aris 'the first light of day; brightness1 [n. r] (Enn.+; lx iubar Enn.)

Pit. *dju-faos, dju-Jas-,

PIE *diu-bʰeh₂-es- 'having/bringing the light of daytime'.

IE cognates: Olr. ban 'white'; Skt. bhas- [n.] 'light, radiance, gleam, glow' < *bʰeh₂-s-, bhasas- [n.] 'splendour', maybe HLuw. pihas- 'splendor, might' < *bʰeh₂-o-\ Skt. bhati, YAv. fra-uuaiti 'to shine'; Gr. φάε 'lighted up, appeared', φαίνω 'to shine, reveal'. The -a- in gen.sg. iubaris must have been adopted from the nom.acc.sg.; conversely, the -r~ in the nom.acc.sg. must have originated in the oblique case forms. Dunkel 1997 summarizes the earlier proposals to see in iubar a compound of *diu- with the root *bʰeh₂-, and gives a convincing etymological analysis. Since it is a n. noun, the original cp. iubar must originally have been an epithet to a n. head noun, but which one is uncertain.

Bibl.: WH I: 724, EM 325, IEW 104, 184-87, Leumann 1977: 84, Dunkel 1997, LIV 1 .*bʰeh₂-. → deus, Iupiter



### iubeo
iubeo 'to order' [v. II; pf. iussi, ppp. iussum; SCBac. ioub-, ious-] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: iussus, -Us 'bidding, command' (Pl.+), iniussu [adv.] 'without orders' (Cato+).

Pit. *joup-eje/o-.

PIE *Hioudʰ-eie/o- 'to cause to move'.

IE cognates: Skt. pr. yudhya- 'to fight', yodha- 'to rebel', caus. yodhayati, ppp. yuddha- 'conquered', yudh- [fj 'fight', YAv. yuioiia- 'to fight', aspaiiaoSa- [m.j 'horse warrior'; Gr. ύσμΐνη (< Gr. *husmo-) [f.] 'battle, fight1; LiOLfitdus 'belligerent'; Lith.jud&i 'to move (intr.)'; ToA yutk- 'to care for' < *ieuct-ske-. The oldest pr. form ioub- matches the reconstructed PIE causative type. It has been replaced by iub- on the model of the short vowel in the pf. and ppp. The pf. ious- has been replaced by ius- on the model of the ppp.; but OLat ious- itself must be secondary for *iup-s-, the expected ^-perfect to a secondary verb stem. The Latin meaning has developed from 'to cause to move' > 'order\

Bibl.: WH I: 724f., EM 325, IEW 51 If., Meiser 1998: 209, LIV *Hieudʰ-,

<!-- pdf-page: 327; printed-page: 313; running-header: iuncus                                     313 -->



### iabilfi, -Sre
iabilfi, -Sre 'to let out whoops' [v. I] (Varro+)

Derivatives: iiigere 'to utter its natural cry': milvi dicuntur cum vocem emittunl (Paul, ex F.).

Pit. */w.

PIE */w.

IE cognates: Gn ίύ 'interjection of amazement', ΐΰγή, ιυγμός 'crying', ιυζω (fut ιύξω) 'to cry aloud'; MHGju,juch 'exclamation of joy' whence MHGjuwen, juwezen 'ju rufen, jube\n\juchezen, MoDu.juichen < ^'ϋ(χ)αη, OIc. yla^ MoE yowl 4 to howl' < *juljan. Probably, a derivative in -bilare (as in sTbilare 'to whistle') from an exclamation of joy *w. There seems to be enough evidence to reconstruct a PIE exclamation */w, even though this is obviously an onomatopoeia.

Bibl.: WH I: 725f., EM 326, IEW 514, Schrijver 1991: 75. → sTbilare



### iugis
iugis 'constant, continuous' [adj. i] (Pl.+)

Pit. *j(o)u-gi~.

PIE *h₂iu-gʷih₃- 'having eternal life, living forever'.

IE cognates: A v. yauuaeji- 4 living forever'; Gr. υγιής 'sound, healthy'; Go. ajukdups 'eternity' < *aiuki-dupi→ OE ece9 cece 'eternal' < *aiuki-. Usually used of waters ('everf lowing'). Traditionally, iugis is regarded as a derivative of iugum 'yoke', in the sense ?connected to each other':. This etymology was rejected by Weiss 1994, since the meaning points rather to 'continually' from the start, and long -u-r is' difficult to explain from iugum. Weiss proposes an etymology as 'having eternal life*, a combination found in other IE languages as a compound *h₂iu-gʷih₃-. Weiss assumes that *gʷ was delabialized in PIE after *w (which is conceivable), and that the final *-f which should have resulted from *-iH- was reintegrated as a short i-stem, since Latin had no long Γ-stems. The long u instead of u he explains from the introduction of the full grade *(H)ieu- on the basis of the putative comp. and sup. containing such a full grade. Alternatively, one could derive the full grade from the paradigm of the noun *h₂i-u-, as found in Av. aiiu, gen. yaos < *Hoi-u-, gen.sg* *Hi-eu-s. ^

Bibl.: WH I: 727, EM 327, IEW 508-510, Weiss 1995. → iSs, iuvenis



### iuncus
iuncus 'reed, rush' [m. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: iunceus OfTlike rushes' (Pl.+), iuncetum 'bed of rushes' (Varro); eiuncidus 'soft like a rush' (Varro+); mnipe/irus9 -F[f.] 'juniper-berry; juniper-tree' (Cato+),

Pit. *joiniko-,

IE cognates: Mir. ain 'reeds, rushes' (< Olr. *oin < */o/«/-); OIc. einir, Swed. en 'juniper' iffrom*jainia-. According to WH, iuncus derives from *ioini- by means of the common ko-suffix. It developed from *ioiniko- > *ioinko- > *iunko- > iuncus. The stem *ioi-ni- gives the impression of being non-IE. If iuniperus was derived from the same stem, we must explain why syncope took place in iuncus but not in iuniperus. One would expect the reverse, if anything: retention in *ioiniko-, syncope in *ioiniipVro-.

Bibl.: WH I: 729f., EM 328, IEW 513.

<!-- pdf-page: 328; printed-page: 314; running-header: 314                                       iungo -->



### iungō, -ere
iungfi, -ere 'to put in the yoke Join' [v. 111] (Pl.+; ίϋηχϊ, iiinctum)

Derivatives: (1) adiungere 'to join, combine, attach' (Pl.+), coniungere 'to connect' (Pl.+), coniunctio 'joining, union' (Vauro+X coniugulus [adj.] 'name of a species of myrtle* (Cato+), deiungere 'to unyoke' (Varro+), di(s)iungere 'to separate, unyoke' (Pl.+), seiungere 'to separate, exclude' (Lucr.+), subiungere 'to harness, attach' (Varro+); iunctus, -us Rejoining' (Varro); (2) coniu(n)x, -gis 'husband, wife' (Pl.+), coniugium 'marriage' (Ter.+), coniugalis 'marital' (Varro+); seiugis [adj.] 'separate' (Paul, ex F.); iniuges 'boves qui sub iugo non fuerint' (Paul, ex F.)\ (3) iugus [adj.] Combined together' (Cato+), btgae [f pi.] 'pair of horses' (Enn.+), biiugus 'yoked in pairs* (Lucr.+), quadriiugus 'drawn by four horses' (Enn.+), quadriga 'a chariot with its team of four horses' (Pl.+), quadrigarius 'charioteer' (Varro+); (4) iugera^ -um [n.pl.] 'a measure of land' (Cato+; sg. iugerum Varro+); (5) iugum 'yoke' (Pl.+), lora subiugia 'yoke-straps' (Cato+); iugare 'to fasten' (Laev.+), iugatid 'the training of vines along crossbeams' (Varro+), abiugare 'to separate' (Pac.), adiugare 'to attach' (Pac.+), deiugare 'to disconnect' (Pac); (6) iugulum (also -us) 'throat' (Pl.+), iugulae [f.plj 'name for part of the constellation of Orion' (Pl.+), iugulare 'to kill by cutting the throat' (Pl.+); (7) iuges, -etis [adj.] 'relating to yoked animals' (said of auspicium) (Cic, Paul, ex F,); (8) iumentum 'beast of burden* (Pl.+; Forum cippus iouxmenta\ adiumentum 'assistance' (Pl.+); (9) iug(u)mentum 'lintel' (Cato+).

Pit. *jung-e/o-9 *-jug-, *jug-o-, *joug-es-, *joug-s-mn-to-.

PIE *(H)iunegti, *(H)iungenti 'to yoke'; *(H)iugo- [n.] 'yoke', *(H)ieug-os- [nj 'yoked animals, team'.

IE cognates: (1) Skt. pr. yunajmi [Is.], yuhkte [3s.med] 'to yoke', YAv.yunjinti [3p.pn], Gr. ζεύγνυμι, Lith. jungti, 3s. jungia 'to tie, join, yoke', Latv. jugt. (2) Hit iuk-y (GI )iuka- [n.] 'yoke, pair* < *ieug-, *iugo-, Hit iuga- 'yearling', taiuga- 'two-year-old' < *iugos, gen.sg. of iuk-. Since Hit. iuk- was only thematized to iuka- within the Hittite period, the other IE words that reflect *iugom might be due to a post-IE thematization (Rieken 1999: 61 f.). Skt. yuj- [m.] 'yoke-fellow, ally, associate', Gr. δζυξ 'not yoked' (4) Gr. ζεύγος [n.] 'team', άζυγής 'unbound', MHG jiuch [n.] 'a morgen of land', OHG jiihhart 'a measure of land', Go. jukuzi [f ] 'yoke, servitude', OE gycer 'yoke' (< *jukizi-) (5) Olr. cuing [f.] 'yoke' (< *kom-jung-i); OW iou 'yoke', MW iau, yeu9 Co. ieu, Bret, yeu, ieo < PCI. *jugo-\ Skt yuga- [n.] 'yoke, team, race, tribe', pra-uga- (< *pra-yuga-) [n.] 'the fore part of the shafts of a chariot', YAv. yuiid.sBml (< *yugo.s-) [nom.du.] 'yoke and (wooden) pin', MP fuy, MoP firy, Gr. ζυγόν, Arm. lowc, Lith. jimgas, Latv. jugs, OCS igo, Ru. igo 'yoke' < PSI. *jtgo; Go. juk 'pair', OHGyoA, OIc. ok 'yoke'; (8) Gr. ζευγμα 'what is used for joining, bridge of boats, canal-lock'. (1): PIE nasal present */w→7-g-, thematicized in Latin; (2) prepositional compounds with the root *iug-\ (3) ostem derivative *iug-o- 'yoked', with numerals *dui-iug-o- etc.; (4) 5-steni

PIE *ieug-e/os- 'yoked animals, team'; in Latin, it shifted to indicate the amount of land which a team of oxen could plough in a day; (5) PIE n. o-stem 'yoke', with derived verbs; (6) probably 'small yoke', 'connecting part' > 'throat'; (7) the r-stem is uncertain; the attested form iuges might also belong to the adj. iugis (cf 2); (8) Probably, *ieug^s-mn-to- is the earlier remake of

PIE *ieug^mn 'yoking'; (9) is the more recent, productive deverbal derivative in -mentum-

### Iuppiter, Iovis
Iflpiter, lovis 'Jupiter' [m.] (VOLat., Naev.+); nom. Iupiter, later Iuppiter, also louis (Enn., Acc.+); acc.sg. Diouem [Praeneste], louem; gen. Diouos; Iovos, Diovo [Praeneste, Norba], Diouis, louis; dat. Diovei [Falerii, Mesagne], Iovei [Praeneste, Spoleto], Ioui, abl. Ioue; nom.pl. Ioues (Cic.)

Derivatives: Diesptr [nom. Praeneste], Diespiter, -tris (PL, Var.+X Dispiter (Paul, ex F. ) 'father Jupiter'; Vediovis, Veiovis 'an ancient deity, considered to be an underworld counterpart of Jupiter', Vedius 'name of a Roman gens' (Sen.+). Pit nom. *djous, ace. *d(i)jem, gen. *diwos, dat. *djowei » Pit· (1) nom. *dijes, ace. *dijem7 (2) nom. *djous, ace. *djowem, gen. *djowes, dat. *djowei. Sab. adj. *djow-jo- 'of Jupiter'.

It. cognates: (1)0. διωρηις, Ιύveis, zoves [gen,], Mars, ioue, O. δισυρει, ζωρηι, χουρηι, diuvei, iuvei, iuvei, U. iuve, iuue [dat.], U. di, dei [voc,] 'Zeus' < gen. *djoweis, dat. *djowei7 voc. *dije (and *djou in iupater) . O. dipatir [nom.], Presam. διποτερες, Marr. ioues patres, u. iuvip(atres?) [gen.], U. iuvepatre, iuve patre [dat.], iupater [voc.] 'Iuppiter'. Possibly Presam. i/opuoi llowijoil (in Tortora AI), but the ending does not fit. (2) U. iuvie, iuvl, tout, iouie, Vol iouio, O. δ]ιοριοι [dat.sg.m,], LL ioui [acc.sg.m.j, U. iuviu, iouiu [abl.sg.m.], iouie, iiouie [voc.sg.m.], Pael. iouiois, Mars, iouies [dat.pl.rn.], Marr. iouia [nom.sg.f.?], O. διωριας, Marr. iouios [gen.sg.f.], U. iuvie, iouie [dat.sg.f ], O. iuviia [acc.sg.f.], U. iouia [voc.sg.f.], O. diuvia[s [nom.pl.f.], iuviais [acc.pl.f.] 'belonging to luppiter' < *djou-io-, from the obi. stem of luppiter, Iovis\ O. diiviiai [datsg.f.] 'of luppiter' (? for *diuviiai?).

PIE nom. *di£us, voc. *dieu^ ace. *diem (< *dieum\ gen. *diuos ( »

Pit. *dieuos\ dat. *diueL

IE cognates: Olr. die {did) , OW did [m.] 'day'; Hit. id) «to,-. siuna-7 Pal. tiuna-, Lyd. ciw- 'god' < *diau-, Skt dyav- [m.f.] 'heaven, god of the sky, Father Sky, day' (dyauh [nom.sg.], dyauh [voc.sg.], dyam I divam [acc.sg.], diva [instr.sg.], dive [datsg.], divas I dyoh [gen.abl.sg.], dyavi I divi [loc.sg.]), YAv. diiaos 'of the hell', Myc. dat. di-we /diwei/, Gr nom. Ζευς, voc. Ζευ, gen. Δι(ρ)ός, dat. (loc.) Δι(ρ)ί, dat. also ^Δψεί (e. g. Διρεί-φιλος) , ace. Ζην, since H. also Δί-α, Ζήν-α; Arm. tiw 'day(-time)'·; OCS dbzdb 'rain' <

PIE *dus-diu- 'bad weather, rainstorm'. In combination with

PIE *ph₂ter: Gr. Ζευς πατήρ, Skt. dyauh pita. The Latin forms Diespiter, Dispiter, U. di, dei together with the word dies 'day' point to the generalization of a stem *dije-9 whereas Iupiter, lovis reflect

Pit. *djow-. These can be derived from a single PIE paradigm for '(god of the) sky, day-light', which phonetically split in two in Pit. and yielded two new stems with semantic specialization. Syllabic *dij- in the nom.acc.sg. can stem from the oblique cases (gen.sg. *diwos9 etc.), in which syllabic *<Λ- occurred. The acc.sg. *dijem led to the creation of a new nom.sg. *dijes and a separate paradigm meaning 'day' (see Lat. dies); some traces of 'sky(-god)' remain in Lat. Diespiter, LL di, del The acc.sg. of 'Zeus' was restored as *diewm > *diowem in Pit., on which the attested paradigm in Latin (louis etc.) and Sab. (*dioueis etc.) was built. The word *dieu- also occurred as a fixed combination with

PIE *ph₂ter- 'father', meaning 'Zeus'. Both members were

<!-- pdf-page: 330; printed-page: 316; running-header: 316                                        ius -->

originally declined, and the voc.sg. *dieu pater yielded Lat Iiipiter, U. iupater. In Sab., this combination is still declined for case forms. Other case forms of *dieu- have been petrified in Latin diu and -djus-, see s,v.

Bibl.: WH I: 732, EM 329, IEW 183-187, Leumann 1977: 357, Sihler 1995: 339, Meiser 1998: 143f., Nussbaum 1999b, Untermann 2000: 163, 179, 182-187, Rix 2004. → dens, dies, diu, iiiglans, pater



### ius, -ris
ius, -ris 'broth, sauce' [n. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: iiisculum 'broth, soup' (Cato).

Pit. *jowas- or *jus-.

PIE *i(e/o)uH-s- * broth, soup'.

IE cognates: Skt- yus- 'soup, broth', Khot yiis 'id/, Gr. ζϋμη * leaven, beer-yeast' < *iuHs-mehr (more difficult to connect: Gr. ζωμός [m.] 'sauce, soup'); GPr.juse [f.], Liihjuse 'broth, soup'; CSjuxa 'broth', Ra uxa 'fish-soup', Cz. jicha 'liquid, sauce, (arch.) soup' < PS1. *juxa <

PIE *ie/ouH-s-. Whereas Skt. and Lith. point to *iuH-s-, Slavic requires *ious- < *ieuH-s-. Latin could have either the full grade or the zero grade. Hence, we seem to be dealing with an original s-stem. The appurtenance of Gr. ζΰμη is not completely certain because of the deviant meaning. The root is often analysed as */ew- 'to hold tight, fasten', as attested in Skt. yauti, Lith. jauti, but the semantics are remote, and the root does not contain a final laryngeal (unless, of course, 'soup' would be analysed as */w-//-).

Bibl.: WH I: 734, EM 330, IEW 507, Schrijver 1991: 233.



### ius, -ris Maw'
ius, -ris Maw' [n. r] (Lex XII+; in VOLat. inscr. ious)

Derivatives: iiistus 'lawful, just' (iouestodi 'iusto' Forum inscr.+, iouiste Paul. exF.\ iiistitia 'justice' (Ter.+); iwrare 'to take an oath, swear' (Pl.+) (3s. iouvesat 'swears', Duenos inscr.), iurator 'certain official' (Pl.+); abiwrare 'to deny knowledge of (Pl.+), adiurare 'to swear' (Pl.+), deie/urare 'to swear' (Pl.+), eiu/erare 'to reject under oath', peiie/urare 'to swear falsely' (Pl.+), peiiuriosus 'addicted to perjury' (Pl.), peiurium 'perjury' (Pl.+), peiurus 'perjured' (Pl.+); iniiiratus 'unsworn' (Pl.+), iniuria 'unlawful conduct, injustice' (Lex XII+), iniurus 'lawless, unjust' (Naev.+), iniurius 'unjust' (Naev.+); iur(i)gare 'to quarrel' (Pl.+), obiur(i)gare 'to reprove' (Pl.+), iurgium 'quarrel, dispute' (Pl.+); iudex 'judge' (Lex XII, Pl.+), indicium 'legal process, trial; decision' (Pl.+), iiidicare 'to judge, try' (Pl.+), iOdicatum 'judgement debt' (LexXII+), iudicatio 'juridical power' (CIL 1.583+).

Pit. *jowos9 *jowes- 'oath, law', *jowesto- 'just', *jowes-a-je- 'to swear', *jowesago- 'dispute', *jowes-dik- 'judge'.

PIE *h₂oi-u, gen.sg. *h₂i-eu-s 'vital force, eternity' » 5-stem *h₂ieu-os, -es-.

IE cognates: Olr. uisse 'just, right, fitting' (< *iu-s-t-io-); Skt. yos(-) 'of life', Av.yaoz-da- [adj.] 'possesing power (of life)'; OAv. yaos 'life, health'; Skt ayus- Mife, life span', Av. aiiii- [n.] 'life, lifetime, time' (gen.sg. OAv. yaos, datsg. OAv. yauuoi, yauue, YAv. yauue) , yauuae-su- 'thriving forever', OAv. yauuae-μ- 'living forever1 <

PIE *h₂oiu- (gen.sg. *fi2eius I *h₂ieus\ in compounds *-h₂iu-); Gr» ου, Arm. oc\ Alb. as 'not' < *h₂oiu(-kʷe). The noun ius probably reflects an s-stem *ieuos > *iouos > ius, with iustus as a

<!-- pdf-page: 331; printed-page: 317; running-header: iuvenis                                    317 -->

/o-derivative. The verb iurare reflects a denominative verb

Pit. *jowes-a-je-. The verb (ob)iurgare, with spellings (ob)iurigare in PL, and iurgium, seem to be based on a noun *iiirago- < *ious-ago- < *ieuos-hjgrO- 'bringing the oath* vel sim. According to Leumann 1977: 546, the alternation between -ierare and -iurare in die-, e-, pe/per-ie/urare is due to contamination of per-iurare with peierare 'to worsen' to peius. The noun iudex has analogical -ex instead of -ix\ it probably reflects *ieuos-diJcr, or it has been formed from iiis plus *dik- after the contraction of *iowos > iiis. The Latin word goes back to a PIE .s-stem, which apparently was derived from an ablauting w-stem *h₂oi-u, gen.sg. *h₂i-eu-s, as reflected in Av. aiiu-.

Bibl.: WH I: 733, EM 329, IEW 512, Leumann 1977: 96, 391, 546, Schrijver 1991: 273f., Sihler 1995: 213, 306, Dunkel 2000a: 94. → aevum, iiigis, iuvenis



### iuvencus
iuvencus 'young bull' [m. o] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: iuvenca 'young cow, heifer' (Varro+).

Pit. *juwnko-.

It. cognates: U. iuengar [nom.pl.], iveka, iuenga [acc.pl.] 'young cow'.

PIE *h₂iu-htn~ko- 'young one, young animal',

IE cognates: Olr. oacy W. ieuanc 'youth' < PCI. *iuuanL·-, Skt.yuvasa- 'young', Go. jugga-laups 'youth₁, OHGjung, Olc. ungr 'young1. A derivative in *-ko- of the PIE word reflected in iuvenis. The sequence -enc- instead of -inc- is unexpected. Iuvencus can be explained as a Sabellism, or as influenced by iuvenis and iuventus. \.

### iuvenis syoung man'
iuvenis syoung man' [m. i] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: iuventus 'youth' (Pl.+), iuventa 'youth' (Lab.+), iuventas 'youth' (Lucr.+); iunior 'younger' (Pl.+); iunix, -wis 'young cow, heifer' (Persius Flaccus, hapax), Iiinius [adj.] 'the month of June' (Enn.+), Ιϋηό 'the goddess Juno' (Pl.+).

Pit. *juwen- 'young', * juntos 'younger'.

It. cognates: maybe U. iouies [dat.pl.], iouie [acc.pl.] ' ? \ party of able-bodied men: ^ieu-ie-Ί

PIE *h₂iu-hien- 'who possesses vital force5 > 'young'; comp. *h₂iu-h₁n-ios.

IE cognates: SkL yiivan- 'young; young man', YAv. yuuandm [acc.sg.], j w ^ m [gen.pl·] 'youth', yoista- 'youngest'; yauua [m.] 'youth'; Lith. jaunas 'young', OCS jum> <

PIE *h₂iou-Hn-o-; Go. jundr [f.] 'youth' < *h₂iu-hin-ti-. The original η-stem *iuwen- was made into an i-stem. The e instead of/ in the second syllable of iuvenis is probably due to iuventus, where -e- was phonetically retained. The comparative iunior occurs beside iuueniory suggesting that it represents a contraction of the latter. The abstract iuventus is clearly more original than iuventa and iuventas. Lat. iunTx is derived from *h₂iu-Hn-ih₂- by Rix 1981: although ιϋηϊχ is a comparatively recent hapax, it is difficult to see how it could have arisen secondarily. Rix assumes that the Etr. counterpart uni of Lat. Ιϋηό reflects a borrowing from Latin *iuni before the suffix *-k- was added. The goddess fund will then represent a different extension of the stem *iun- 'young'. The month name Iiinius might be a derivative off. *ium-9 as Rix p. 279 proposes. Fortson 2002 connects the family name

<!-- pdf-page: 332; printed-page: 318; running-header: 318                                       iuvo -->

Junius as from */ow-.

Bibl.: WH I: 735f, EM 331, IEW 510f, Leumann 1977: 83, Rix 1981: 274-279, Schrijver 1991: 152, 322, Meiser 199£: 155, Untennann 2000: 353. → aevum, iugis, ius, iuvencus



### iuvō, -āre
iuvō, -āre 'to help, assist' [v. I; pf. iūvī, ppp. iūtum] (Carmen Arvale+, Pl.+; CIL sb. iouent, also pr. iuvere Ace.)

Derivatives: iucundus 'agreeable' (Pl.+); adiuvare 'to help' (Pl.+), deiuvare 'to refuse help to' (Pl.); adiutare 'to help' (Pl.+), adiutabilis 'helpful' (Pl.+), odiutor 'helper' (Pl.+), adiutrix 'female helper' (Pl.+).

Pit. */yow-.

PIE *h₁i-h,euH-(e/o-) [pr.] 'to help\

IE cognates: Olr. con-οί, -oat 'to protect'; Skt. pr. avati, pf ova [3s.act], ta-ptc. uta- 'to help, protect', OAv. auuami [ls.pr.] 'to help, care', uz-uiuiidi [inf.] 'to protect'. Pf. ιύνϊ < *iuwa-wi, iutus < *iuwatos. This suggests an earlier stem *iuwa-, as is preserved in some forms of the verb iuvere <

Pit. *i(j)ewa-. Lat. iuvere has replaced *iovere by analogy with iuvare. Lat. iuvare may be a secondary iterative to (the predecessor of) iuvere, or it has been backformed from adriuvore (cf pellere - appellare); in the first case, iuvare has its -w- (for *iovare) by analogy with adriuvare. The pf.sb. forms iuverit, iuverint could be replacements of *iuveri(n)t on the model of fuerit (thus Leumann), or they reflect a Pit. reduplicated s-present *Hi-HieuH-es- (as O. didest 'will give'; cf. also monerint < *-e.s-).

Bibl.: WH I: 736f, EM 331, IEW 77-78, Leumann 1977: 596, Garcia Ramon 1996, Meiser 1998: 184, 188,206, Rix 1999: 520, LIV *h,euH-.



### iuxta
iuxta 'near by, equally; next to' [adv.; prep.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: iuxtim [adv.] 'in close proximity' (Andr.+).

Pit. *jougVsto-.

PIE *(H)ieug-s- 'yoke'? The preservation of the cluster -kst- points to syncope from *iugVstad>i abl.sg.f. of *iugVsto-. One might posit an adj. *ieug-s-to- "yoked5 derived from *ieug-e/os- [n.] 'team of animals, yoke' which is preserved in Lat. iiigera. This would have to be a rather old derivative, from before the generalization of the full-grade suffix *-es-/-os-. This hypothesis seems more likely than to posit *iug-isto- 'most connected with, closest' with the PIE superlative suffix *-ist(H)o-, since this suffix is not otherwise found in Latin, and also, *iwg- is not an adjective.

Bibl.: WH I: 737, EM 328, IEW 508-510, Cowgill 1970: 125, LIV *ieug-. → iungo

<!-- pdf-page: 333; printed-page: 319; running-header: labo                                          319 -->

## L



### labium
labium 'lip' [n. o] (Pl.+; mostly pi. labia)

Derivatives: labia I labea 'lip' (Pl.+), labedsus 'thick-lipped' (Lucr.); labrum 'lip, brim' (Naev.+), labellum 'lip' (Pl.+), collabellare 'to make by putting lips together' (Lab.).

Pit. *labjo-7 *labro-~

IE cognates: OE OFr. lippa 'lip' < PGm. *lepjan-, OLFr. lepor, OFr. lepur 'lip', OHG lefs < P G m *lep-e/os-. Less certain: Gr. *lob- in λοβός [ m j 'lobe, lap, slip', πρόλοβος 'crop of birds, Adam's apple', προλόβιον 'the front part of the lobe of the ear'. L a t a in labrum might be due to the development *(C)RDC > *(C)RaDC proposed by Schrijver 1991 (cf. magnus < *mg-no-Y Yet nominal cognates of *lb- 'lip' are only found in Germanic, and *fc is a rare PIE phoneme. It is furthermore uncertain that 'lip' can be derived from the verb forms for 'to waver' (It.+Gm. *lab-, LIV *lembH- 'to hang loosely'), as IEW assumes. Hence, *lab- 'lip' may be a borrowing from an s unknown adstrate. \χ -

Bibl.: W H I: 738, EM 333f.f IEW 655-657, Schrijver 1991: 479, Sihler 1995: 146. → labdy lambo *



### labō, -āre
labO, - a r e 'to stand unsteadily, waver' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: labascere 'to become uncertain; dissolve' (Pl.+), collabascere 'to waver at the same time' (Pl.), labefacere 'to make unsteady, weaken' (Ter.+), labefaciare 'to undermine' (Pl.+), collabefien 'to collapse' (Lucr.+); labt, pf. lapsus 'to glide, slip' (Pl.+); labes, -is 'fall (of earth), landslip; disaster' (Pl.+), labdsus 'slippery' (Lucil.), labundus 'gliding' ( A c e ) , lapsus, -us 'the gliding, falling' (Andr.+); collibi 'to slip, collapse' (Pl.+), delabi'to slip down, drop' (Varro+), dilabT 'to flow away, perish' (Naev.+), perlabi 'to glide along, skim' (Lucil.+), prolabi 'to slide forwards' (Acc.+).

PIE *lh 2 b-eh 2 -, *Ieh 2 b-e/o-, *leh 2 b-eh r 'weak'.

IE cognates: OCS slabh, Ru. slabyj, SCr. slab 'weak' <

PIE *slob-(n)o- (according to Dericsen 1996: 83, Latv, slabs, slab$ns 'weak', and Lith. slabnas (Zem.) 'weak' were borrowed from Slavic); OHG slaf, MoDu. slap. Schrijver regards labare as the regular reflex of a zero-grade of a root *(s)lh 2 b-. For the assumed cognates of labo, a different solution is proposed by LIV: *sleh l b-. With some additional assumptions, this would explain all the cognates, but it cannot explain the Latin ablaut lab- : lab-. Even if lab- < *lHb- were the oldest form within Latin, it is difficult to imagine lab-e/o- as a secondary full grade to this root. There is one other way out: the meanings 'to waver' and 'to slip' are not necessarily connected. Thus, one might separate labare and connect it to the Baltic and Germanic

<!-- pdf-page: 334; printed-page: 320; running-header: 320                                       labor -->


words for 'weak'; lab!then remains isolated.

Bibl.: WH I: 739, EM 333f, IEW 655-657, Schxijver 1991: 163£, 179, 378f., LIV ?*sleh,b-.



### labor
labor 'work, labour' [m. r] (Naev.+; nom.sg. labos Pl.+)

Derivatives: laborare 'to toil, labour, be worried' (Pl.+), laboriosus 'toilsome' (Pl.+). Often connected, albeit hesitantly, with labd 'to waver', under the assumption of a semantic shift from 'nearly collapsing under a load' > 'burden' > 'labour'. This seems unconvincing to me.

Bibl.: WH I: 739f., EM 334, IEW 655-657.



### lac, -tis
lac, -tis 'milk' [n, t] (Pl.+; nom.acc.sg. also lacte Pl.+, lact Varro, Pun.)

Derivatives: lactes, -itan 'the small intestines; chitterlings' (Pl.+), lacteus 'of milk' (Andr.+), lactarius 'suckling' (Varro+), lactam 'unweaned, sucking; full of milk' (Andr.+), lactens 'unweaned, sucking; full of milk1 (Cato+), lactuca 'lettuce' (Varro+).

Pit. *(g)Iagt-.

PIE *glg-t- £milk\

IE cognates: Gr. γάλα, γάλακτος/γλάγος [n.] 'milk' < *glg-(t-), γαλαι5ηνός 'sucking milk', Aim. kaxc* (dial.) < *glg-t-s, kat'n 'milk' < acc.sg. *glg-t-m. The semantics of Hit. kala(n)kr 'to soothe, satisfy* (3s.ipv.act. kalankaddu, ptc. kalankant-) are too far removed to warrant a connection. The a of *lakt- can be explained by Schrijver's rule of a development *CKDC > *CRaDC. The loss of initial *g- is explained by distance dissimilation by Meiser, whereas e.g. Sihler reconstructs the word with initial **//-, in which case Greek and Armenian would have undergone assimilation to the following velar. Leumann regards lac as a loanword from Greek *glakt~. To my mind, the last explanation is very unlikely (Greek has a different nom.sg. form), and original *<i/- is not supported by any evidence. Thus, Lat lad- goes back to *glgt- > *glagt-, and initial *g- has been lost in Latin through dissimilation.

Bibl.: WH I: 741, EM 335, IEW 400f., Leumann 1977: 187, Schrijver 1991: 479f, Sihler 1995: 96, Meiser 1998: 114. → delicus



### lacer
lacer 'mutilated' [adj. o/a] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: lacerare 'to tear, torment, ruin' (Pl.+), dilacerare 'to tear to pieces' (Pl.+); lacinia 'the edge of a garment' (Pl.+); lancinare cto tear in pieces' (Cat.+).

Pit. *lak(V)-ro- 'torn, ragged', *Iank- 'to tear'.

PIE pr. *lh₂-n-k- 'to tear', adj. *lh₂k-(V-)ro-.

IE cognates: Gr. [aor.] άπέληκα Ί have torn off among the Cyprians (Hsch.; for *άπέλακα); λακίς, -ίδος 'rent, rending; tatters of clothes', λακίζω 'to tear', λάκη 'rags' among the Cretans (Hsch.), λάκημα 'rent, piece, fragment' (pap.); Po. lach, Ru. lachon 'rag'? (Dplacerare is denominal to lacer, even if lacer is attested much later The pr. lancinare looks like a contamination of a pr. *lank- with a nasal stem *lakre/on- (which sometimes gave rise to verbs in -inare), from which also lacinia must be derived. While Meiser 1998 reconstructs *lh₂k-ero-, Melchert 2007a: 257 suggests

<!-- pdf-page: 335; printed-page: 321; running-header: lacio                                       321 -->


that λακίς was made from an earlier noun *Ih₂ki- 'tearing', and that lacer represents a derived adj. *laki-ro- 'torn',

Bibl.: WH I: 742f., EM 335, IEW 674, Schrijver 1991: 164f., Meiser 1998: 107.



### lacertus
lacertus 'upper arm' [m. o] (Lucil.+; also n. lacertum)

Derivatives: lacertosus 'muscular' (Varro+), lacerta / lacertus 'lizard; Spanish mackerel' (Cic.+). IEW proposes a connection with Gr. λάξ, λάγδην [adv.] 'with the foot', λάκτις, -ιος 'pestle' on the one hand, and OIc. leggr 'lower leg, bone', arm-, hand-leggr 'arm', fit-, Icer-Ieggr 'calf of the leg' (< *lagi-\ Langob. lagi 'thigh' on the other hand. None of these connections is semantically convincing. The connection with λικερτίζειν 'to jump, dance' (Hsch.) is adopted by WH-

Bibl.: WH I: 743f, EM 336, IEW 673.



### lacio, -ere
lacio, -ere 'to entice' [v. III(?)] (Paul. exF. lacit)

Derivatives: (1) allicere 6to entice, attract' (Pl.+; pf. -/exi, ppp. -tectum), delicere 'to lure' (Thin.), delicatus 'luxurious, self-indulgent' (Pl.+), deliciae [f.plj 'pleasure, luxuries' (Pl.), elicere 'to coax, draw forth' (Pl.+; pf. elicui), inlicire (Naev.), illicere 'to entice' (Pl.+; pf. illexi), illicium 'lure' (Varro+), pellicere 'to win over, seduce' (Lex ΧΠ+), prolicere 'to lure,forward' (Pl.+); lactare ;to entice' (Pl.+), delectare 'to charm, entice' (Pl.+), delectamentum 'instrument of'pleasure' (Ter.+), delectatio 'source of delight' (Ten+), electare 'to worm out (information)' (Pl.+), oblectare 'to delight' (Pl.+),protectare 'to induce to do' (Pl.+), sublectare 'to coax' (Pl.); lacessere 'to challenge, provoke' (Pl.+; pf -τνϊ, ppp. -itum); lax 'deceit' (Paul, ex F. ), iltex 'who attracts' (Pl.+); elecebra 'a means of wheedling something out of a person' (Pl.+), illecebra 'enticement' (Pl.+), illecebrosus 'enticing' (Pl.), peltecebra 'decoy' (Pl.);pellacia 'seductiveness' (Lucr.+); (2) colliciae [f.plj 'gutter' (Vitr.+; -qu- Col.), colliciaris 'designed for making gulleys' (Cato), delicia 'corner beam supporting a roof (Vitr.+); elix, -cis [m.] 'furrow in a com field for draining off water' (Ov.+); sublica 'woodeji stake or pile' (Naev.+), sublicius 'supported on wooden piles' (esp. of bridges) (Varro+).

Pit. *lak-i-, aor. *lak-s-y ppp. *lak-to- 'to draw, pull'.

It. cognates: maybe Ven. lagfsto] [3s,pret] 4?offered'; O. kellaked as 3s.pf. *ke-te-lak-ed in the analysis of Untermann 2002:492f WH connect *Iak- to the root *MTV- of laqueus 'loop, rope'. Whereas *kʷt > ct and *kʷs > χ are unproblematic, the supposed development *kʷi > ci (in lacit, deliciae) is uncertain; one would rather expect -qui-, as in reliquium. Similarly, one would expect *laquessere and -lequebra. Therefore, we must assume a Pit. stem *lak-. It is possible to connect this with lacer 'torn, tearing' if lacio originally meant 'to draw, attract'. Note that the simplex verb is only attested by Paulus ex F.y and hence likely to be a nonce form. The others are all compounds, in which -lak- may simply have meant 'to draw'. The technical words collie-, delic- and elix are usually derived from liqueo 'to liquify'. None of them is attested before CLat, except colliciaris. EM ascribe the spellings with -c- instead of -qu- to analogy with adjectives of the type elicius. It

<!-- pdf-page: 336; printed-page: 322; running-header: 322                                    lacruma -->

seems uncertain to me that they derive from liqu- at all: they may also be derived from lacio. Formally this is easier because of -c-, and semantically it is more straightforward: gutters, corner-bean^ and furrows are not made for 'making something liquid*, but for 'draining', thus 'drawing away\ rain and other liquids.

Bibl.: WH I: 336, 744f., EM 168, 346f., IEW 673f., Lejeune 1974: 335, Leumann 1977: 148,592, Schrijver 1991: 411, Meiser 2003: 115. → delicus, laqueus, liqueo



### lacruma
lacruma 'tear* [f. a] (Andr.+) Variant forms: dacrima (Andr., Paul, ex F.\ lacrima (Andr., Naev.). According to EM, the PL mss. often have -uma; TLL does not give the distribution of -ima and -uma.

Pit. *d(r)(k)akruna-7

PIE *drk-h₂(e)kru- 'eye-bitter5.

IE cognates: Olr. der, W. deigr 'tear', Hit ishahru- [n.] 'tear(s), weeping' < *s + ^h&kru-l, Skt asm-, YAv. asrii [pi.], Gr. δάκρυ, Arm. artasuk' [pl.]5 Lith. asara, OHG zahar, ToB akruna [obl.pl.] 'tear\ Many investigators regard the word as a loanword from Greek δάκρϋμα / δάκρυμα 'tear'. EM give the following arguments for this view: if the form were genetically related to the Greek form, i.e.,

Pit. *dakru-mn, one would expect Latin *dacrumen. The suffix -ma is not productive in Latin, and there are no examples of a suffixation -u-mo Latin. Lat. -ma only occurs in flamma < *flag-ma,where it is found directly after the root, as expected from a PIE point of view (Gr. φλογμός). But Hamp 1972 points out that Greek δάκρϋμα / δάκρυμα itself is relatively recent (unattested before the fifth century), and it never becomes the normal word.for 'tear' in Greek (which is δάκρυον). If the Latin word was inherited, the origin is still problematic:

PIE *drk-h₂(e)kru- + -mo-? To explain -ma, Hamp 1972: 296 suggests an original n.pl. *dlakruna, in which *w assimilated to u to give *dlakruma. To me, this assimilation and initial *dl- seem unlikely. A good alternative would be to suppose a distant dissimilation of *rfrw to *d-m9 thus *dakruna > *dakruma; compare the reverse in *temabrae > tenebrae. Obviously, this solution is speculative. For the IE words in initial *rfr, the solution proposed by Kortlandt 1985b is attractive: the more archaic form *fj2ekru- has been replaced by the compound, or perhaps syntagm, *iM-A2Aru- 'eye-bitter'. The plural to this word must have been *(drk-)h₂ekru-n-h₂> which is reflected in ToA obl.pl. akrunt, ToB obl.pl. akruna and Lat. dacruma.

Bibl.: WH I: 746, EM 336, IEW 179, Hamp 1972: 292, Kortlandt 1985b, Schrijver 1991: 98, Sihler 1995: 150, Meiser 1998: 100, LIV *derk-.



### lacus, -ūs
lacus, -0s 'lake' [m. u] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: lacuna 'hollow, pit, pond', lacunar, -aris [n.] 'a panel in a panelled ceiling' (Cic.+).

Pit. *laku-.

PIE *lok-u- 'lake'.

PIE *lok-u-; Bret lagen 'small lake'; Gr. λάκκος 'pond' < *lk-u-o-, OCS loky, gen.sg. lokbve 'puddle, pool, reservoir' [f. u] , SCr. lokva < *lok-uH-\ OE lagu < *loku-. laedo, -ere 'to injure, damage' [v. Ill; laesu laesum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: elldere 'to crush, force out' (Pl.+), allTdere 'to strike against, crush' (Acc,+), illidere 'to injure by crushing, beat' (Varro+), IEW connects some Greek and Baltic words. The connection with Gr, λίστρον 'spade, shovel', however, is very uncertain. ### laetus
laetus 'flourishing, rich; happy' [adj. o/a] (Naev,+)

Derivatives: laetare 'to gladden' (Andr.+), laetarl 'to be glad' (Pl.+), laetitia 'joy' (Pl.+), laetitudd 'joy' (Acc.+), laeiiscere 'to delight' (Sis.+); laetificare 'to gladden, fertilize' (Pl.+), laetificus 'joyful' (Enn.+), Under the assumption that 'fat, rich' is the older meaning, WH and IEW connect lar(i)dus 'bacon' and largus 'generous', which would contain *lai-es~; laetus would then be *lai-t<h. This is a very artificial reconstruction,

Bibl.: WH I: 750, EM 337f, IEW 652. 1



### laevus
laevus 'left' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: laeva 'the left hand; the left' (Pl.+).

Pit. *laiwo-,

PIE *Ieh₂i-uo- or *lh₂ei-uo- 'left'.

IE cognates: Gr. λαι(ρ)ός 'left*, OCS levt, Ru. levyj, SCr. Rjevi Md.', ToB laiwo 'lassitude' (< *-uehr\ The original meaning may have been 'curved', although this can hardly be considered proven by the quotation from Servius: laevi (sc, boves) quorum cornua ad terrain spectanL Steinbauer (apud Stiiber 2006: 68) proposes to derive *leh₂iuo- (if this was the original form) from the root *leh₂- 'to hide' that we find in Lat. lated. The left hand would be called 'the hidden hand', similarly to the 'shaded' hand that can be reconstructed for scaevus. If the original meaning indeed was 'curved', we may look for a root *lh₂(-i)- that fits this meaning, but none is immediately convincing, LIV has a root *leih₂- 'to stop, stop doing', and also *leh₂- 'to hide'. The PIE preform of laevus is reconstructed as *lh₂eiuo- by Schrijver 1991 on account of the accentuation of the Slavic forms (thus also Derksen 2008: 275); but for Latin, Greek and Toch., *leh₂i-uo- would also work,

Bibl.: WH I: 750f, EM 338, IEW 652, Schrijver 1991: 203. → lateo, scaevus



### lallō, -āre
lallO, -are 'to sing a lullaby' [v. I] (Persius Flaccus+) PIt*tofa(velsim.).

IE cognates: Gr. λαλέω 'to talk, chat, prattle', λάλος 'chattering', λαλία 'talk'; Lith.

<!-- pdf-page: 338; printed-page: 324; running-header: 324                                       lama -->

laluoti 'to babble', NHG lallen. Onomatopoeic, reduplicated imitation of a baby's sound.
