letum 'death' [n. ο] (Pl.+) Pit Veto-,

PIE *leh r to- [n.].

IE cognates: OCS letb cit is possible, allowed' [f ], Ru. let' (arch.) 'it is possible, allowed' < *leh r ti-; OCS leto 'summer, year', Ru. leto 'summer, (pi.)

<!-- pdf-page: 350; printed-page: 336; running-header: 336                                       levir -->


age, years' <

PIE *lehrtom; OIc. lad, OE Iced 'land', Go. unleds 'poor' (Schaffher 2001:241). May be derived from a ppp. *leh r to- 'let, let g o \ substantivized as *leh r to- 'letting go' > 'death'. It would then be identical in origin to Slavic */ito- 'summer, year' (from 'going5).

Bibl.: WH I: 787, EM 352, IEW 666, Neri 2007: 32, LIV *leh r . → lassus, lenis



### tevir/laevir * husband's brother'
tevir/laevir * husband's brother' [m. ο (r)] (Paul. exF.+) Only attested in glosses, with vacillating spelling lae-1 le-. The latter would allow for an interpretation as levir, with monophthongization of ae.

Pit. *daiwer.

PIE *deh₂wier- 'husband's brother'.

IE cognates: Skt. devar-, Pashto levar, Gr. δάήρ, Arm. taygr, Lith. dieveris I dieveris9 Latv. dieveris, Ru. dever\ SCr. djever < BSl. *da?iuer-; OHG zeihhur, OE tacor 'husband's brother' < PGm. *taikura-(?y Lat. levir is often regarded as a loanword from a non-urban Latin dialect, because of initial /- and e for *aL But /- for dr is also found in lingua and lacrima, so there is no compelling reason to regard this as non-urban. Final -v/r instead of -ver is probably due to influence of Lat. v/r 'man'. In theory, the PIE noun *deh₂i-uer- 'husband's brother' could be a derivative of *deh₂i- 'to distribute', but there is no obvious semantic connection.

Bibl.: WH I: 787f, EM 352, IEW 179, Leumann 1977: 68, 155, Schrijver 1991: 269.



### levis
levis 'light in weight, slight, weak' [adj. i\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: levitas 'lightness' (Lucr.+), levare 'to relieve, lessen, lift up' (Pl.+); sublevare 'to alleviate, assist' (Varro+); levifidus 'untrustworthy' (Pl.), levipes 'light-footed' (Var.+), levisomnus 'sleeping lightly* (Lucr.),

Pit. *leyu-» Vqcm-.

PIE *h,legwh-u- > *hilegʰ-u- 'light'.

IE cognates: Olr. laigiu 'lighter, poorer', MW llei 'less', Skt. raghu- 'fleet, fast', YAv. rmaa- [adj.f] 'agile, fast, quick', sup. rBrtjisto-^ Gr. έλαχύς 'small, little', Alb. lehte, Lith. lengvas 'light', OCS Ibgbkb, Ru, legkij, Go. leihts, OHG Kht, OIc. lettr, ToB lankjse 'light5 <

PIE *hilengʷʰ-to-; Gr. ελαφρός 'light, dexterous, fast, little', OHG lungar 'fast, cheerful' < *h₁lngʷʰ-ro-. There appear to be two PIE forms of the root, *h₁l(e)ngwh- and *hilegʰ-; Latin continues the latter one. PCI. has *fog-w- (cf. Schrijver 1995: 302-305), which is difficult to explain from either of these PIE forms, but seems closest to Latin levis. The shift from *gʷʰ to *gʰ in (Italo-)Celtic can be due to delabialization in front of *-w-. The nasalless root would then seem oldest, and the *-w- found in most languages must be secondary; EIEC explained it from influence of the PIE verb *hilengʷb- 'to move fast, easily' (Skt. ramhate 'hastens', Olr. *ling 'to leap'). Like most u-stem adjectives, levis was remade into an /-stem in Latin.

Bibl.: WH I: 788, EM 353, IEW 660f, Sihler 1995: 163, Meiser 1998: 120.



### levis
levis 'smooth' [adj. i] (Cato+)

Derivatives: levare 'to polish* (Varro+), levamentum 'a means of obtaining a smooth

<!-- pdf-page: 351; printed-page: 337; running-header: liber                                       337 -->


surface' (Varro), levigare 'to make smooth' (Varro+), levitas
surface' (Varro), levigare 'to make smooth' (Varro+), levitas 'smoothness' (Varro+), levor 'id.' (Lucr.-l··)*

Pit. *leju- » *leiw-i-.

PIE ^lehii-u- 'smooth'.

IE cognates: Gr. λείος [adj.] 'level, smooth' < *Iehii-u-o-, λίς, gen. λιτός [adj\] 'smooth \ [m.] 'smooth linen' < *UH-l·. Following Schrijver, we may assume that levis is an old w-stem adj. From a preform

PIE *lehii-u-, we can explain the result *leju- » *leiw-i- > levis by normal syllabification rules. Most of the other connections within Latin which were proposed (especially with lino and with limits) must then be discarded; but ob-livlscor 'to forget' may still be cognate.

Bibl.: WH I: 789, WM 353, IEW 662-664, Schrijver 1991: 283f. → obliviscor



### lex, legis'law'[f g]
lex, legis'law'[f g] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: legare 'to send as an envoy, bequeath' (Lex XII+), legatus 'envoy, deputy' (Pl.+); legitimus 'legal, legitimate' (Varro+); cottega [m.] 'colleague, fellow' (Pl.+), collegium 'guild, board' (Pl.+); ablegare 'to send away on a mission' (Pl.+), allegare 'to send as a representative, employ' (Pl.+), allegatus, -us 'instigation' (Pl.+), delegare Ho appoint, assign a task to', relegare 'to banish' (Cic.+); legerupa [m.] 'law-breaker' (Pl.), legerupio 'law-breaking' (Pl.).

Pit. */eg-_

It. cognates: Marr. lixs [nom.sg.], O. ligud< [abl.sg.], ligis [abl.pl.] 'law' < *legr; maybe 0. legu '?' if flaws' (Weiss 1993: 22f); maybe O. Uganakdikei [dat.sg.] name of a goddess; O. ligatus [nom.pl.], ligatuis [dat.pl.] 'envoy', probably a loanword from Latin,

PIE *leg- 'collection'? The verb legare and its compounds all have a meaning which involves a 'task, assignment', and can therefore be interpreted as derivatives of lex 'law'. The Pit. root noun */eg- 'law' can be interpreted as a 'collection' of rules. Whether the root noun existed already in PIE is uncertain for lack of precise cognates.

Bibl.: WH I: 779f., 789f.5 EM 350, 353f, IEW 658, Schrijver 1991: 120, Untermann 2000: 427-435,-UV *leg-, → lego if



### liber, -bn
liber, -bn 'bark of a tree; book' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: libellus 'document, pamphlet' (Pl.+), libellio 'bookseller' (Varro+); maybe Paul, ex F. 73 delibratum, hoc est decorticatum.

Pit. */w/ro-.

PIE *lubʰ-ro- 'leaf, rind'.

IE cognates: Olr. luib, Mir. luibh f 'herb', lub-gort 'garden', Alb. labe 'rind, cork' (< *loub-)y OPr. lubbo 'bast, plank, shelf, Lith. luba, Latv. luba 'plank, board', Ru. lob 'forehead, brow', gen.sg. Iba, Cz. leb 'skull' < BSl. *lub~, Lith. luobas 'bast', Latv. luobas 'peel', Ru. lub 'bast' < BSl. *loub-o-; Go, lubja-leis 'who knows poisons', Olc. lyf[t] 'medicinal herbs', OE lybb [n,] 'poison, magic' < *lubʰ-; Go. laufs [m.], lauf[n.] 'leaf, foliage', OE leaf, OHG hub [n.] <

PIE *loubʰ-. Since several IE languages show forms in *lubʰ- meaning "bast, leaf, liber has been reconstructed as *lubro-, and compared with the vacillation in Latin between lubet

<!-- pdf-page: 352; printed-page: 338; running-header: 338                                         liber -->

and libet, clupeus and clipeus. For liber, however, no form in -w- is attested. Still, for want of a better alternative, we may surmise that liber is cognate with *lubʰ- and goes back to a PIE word or a European word Vleaf, rind\

Bibl.: WH I: 790, EM 354, IEW 690f., Schrijver 1991: 23, LIV ?*leup-.



### liber
liber 'free' [adj. o/a] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: Uberare 'to free, release' (Pl.+), liberalis 'of free men, generous' (Naev.+), Uberalitas 'generosity' (Ter.+), liberator 'one who sets free' (Pl.+); Ubertas freedom1 (Naev.+), libertus [m.] ^eedman' (Pl.+), liberta 'freedwoman' (Pl.+), flbertma 'freedwoman' (Pl.+), liberfinus 'freedman' (Pl.+); liberi, -(or)um [m.pl.] 'children' (Pl.+); Liber, -en 'Italian god of vegetation* (Andn+).

Pit. *ieup-ero-.

It. cognates: Ven. louderobos [datpl.m.] 'for the children1, louderai [dat.sg.f] 'for a daughter* (if not a PN); FaL loferta, loifirta [nom.sg^f] 'free woman5 (substantivized adj.), loifirtato [gen.sg.] 'freedom'; PaeL loufir [nom.sg.] 'free man'; O. luvfreis [gen.sg.] epithet of luppiter; maybe U. vufiune, uofione [datsg.] a deity, often explained as *leuct-i-5no- 'he of the people'.

PIE *hileudʰ-ero-,

IE cognates: Gn έλεύΰερος 'free'; Lith. liaudis 'lower people5, CS Ru. ljud 'people', OCS ljudbje, Ru. ljudi [pi.] 'men, people' < IE *hJeuc?-o-, -i-, OHG liut, pi. liuti9 OE leod 'people'. The spellings loebesum and loebertatem adduced by Varro and Paul, ex F. confirm the diphthong -oi- found in Faliscan, and show a phonetic change in Latino-Faliscan of *Ιοηβ- > */oi/k This can be explained as a dissimilation of u to i in front of the bilabial fricative. Leumann explains libertus as a backformation to llbertas on the model of honestus vs. honestas. The BSL and Gm. noun *hjleuct- 'people' derives from a verb *h}leuct- 'to grow'; the deity Liber shows that *h}leuct- originally meant 'to grow' in Latin too. The derived adj. *hileudt-ero- originally indicated one 'belonging to the people', hence 'free' in Latin liber and Gr. έλεύύερος. The change of the pi liben to 'children* is explained by Benveniste (1969 I: 324) from legal terminology, in which the legitimate 'children5 of a free couple were denominated as "free ones'.

Bibl.: WH I: 791-793, EM 355, IEW 684f., Giacomelli 1963: 236, 249f., Benveniste 1969 I: 321-325, Lejeune 1974: 336, Leumann 1977: 61, 335, Meiser 1998: 87, Untermann 2000: 438f„ 862f., LIV ^leud 11 -.



### libet
libet 'it is pleasing' [v. II (impers.), pf. libuit / lubitum est] (Pl.+; the older variant is lubet)

Derivatives: libere 'to be willing* (Pl.), lu/ibens 'pleased, willing' (Andr.+; lub- mainly in inscr. from outside Rome), lu/ibentia 'pleasure' (Pl.+), Libentma 'title of Venus' (Varro+), lu/ibido [f.] 'desire, lust' (Pl.+), libidinitas 'lustfulness' (Lab.); prolubium 'desire' (Naev,+); allubescit 'is pleasing' (Pl.+); Lat. -libet 'any, whichever' in quilibet 'whoever/whatever you please', etc.

Pit. Huf-e- 'to desire'.

It. cognates: O. loufir 'or' [pr.sb.impers.] < *loubʰ-e/i-K

PIE *lubʰ-ehr 'to please'.

IE cognates: Skt. lobhaya- 'to make crazy', pf. lulobha 'is in disorder', MoP ahiftan 'to be tangled, be hit down, be in love' < Ilr. *l(a)ubh-\ OCS

<!-- pdf-page: 353; printed-page: 339; running-header: libra                                     339 -->



### ljubb
ljubb 'sweet, pleasant', Ru* ljuboj 'any, either' < *leubʰ-o-, CS ljuby [f.], gen.sg. ljubovb Move' < *leubʰ-uH, OCS ljubo, Ru. /ΐ&ο 'or'; OCS ljubitU Ru. tfuW/' 'to love' < *leubʰ-; Go. /m/&, OHG Hob [adj.] 'dear, sweet'. Original *w has been unrounded between velarized / and b. The suffix of the derived noun probably was *-edon (from the v. *lub-e-) but it was influenced by cupido. For the semantics of O. loufir and Lat. -libet 'any', cf, OCS #wfco Or'. The original meaning of O. loufir may have been 'is wanted, one wants'.

Bibl.: WH I: 793, EM 367, IEW 683f., Leumann 1977: 367, Meiser 1998: 80, Untermann 2000: 437, LIV *Ieubʰ-.



### Bbo, -are
Bbo, -are 'to pour a libation' [v. I] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: libamentum 'sacrifiacial offering, taste' (Varro+), llbum 'sacrificial cake' (Enn.+); deltbare 'to remove, take a small piece from1 (Erm.+), delibutus 'thickly smeared, imbued' (Pl.+).

Pit. *leifo-1 *loifo- 'sacrificial cake', */#w- 'greasy'.

PIE *h₂Ie/oibʰH> [n.], *h₂libʰ-u- [adj.],

IE cognates: Gr. αλείφω 'to anoint', άλειφαρ 'unguent'. Steinbauer 1989: 120-122 regards Ubare as an original denominal verb 'to make a sacrificial cake' to libum. It is uncertain, then, whether the noun continues */e/Z>- or */ο/ό-, with the change of *oi > ϊ between velar / and a labial. The ppp. delibutus has short i; it indirectly points to a 'denominal verb *delibuere to an adj. *libu- 'greasy, dripping'. Gr, λείβω 'to pour' and λοιβή 'offering' are not cognate, but have undergone the same semantic shift(s). The root *h₂libʰ- which llbum and Gr. αλείφω share might be an enlarged variant of *h₂li- 'to anoint', which is found as *h₂liH- in Lat. lino.

Bibl.: WH I: 336,794-796, EM 356, Steinbauer 1989: 120-122, Schrijver 1991:243, Sihier 1995: 53.→/iwo



### libra
libra 'pound (measure of weight) (Lex XII, Pl.+); pair of scales (Varro+)' [f. a] (Lex ΧΠ+) i

Derivatives: Ubrare 'to make level, balance' (Cato+), librarius [adj.] 'of a pound's weight' (Cato+), libella 'small silver coin; plumb-line' (Pl.+); selibro 'half a libra5 (Cato+), simbella la coin worth half a libella' (Varro); collibrare 'to measure' (Cato), deliberdre 'to consider carefully, consult' (Pl.+); libripens, -ndis One who holds the balance'(Lex XII+).

Pit. VeiPra- 'pound'. Delfberare must be due to influence of liberare 'to free' on *de-librare 'to weigh'. The weight selibro was probably derived from libra on the model of semodius 'half a modius' < *semi-modius. Libella < *librala. Simbella < *sembella < *sembella < *semibellay with haplology < *semilibella. The Greek coin λίτρα 'name of a Sicilian coin' was probably borrowed from an Italic language at the stage containing *-#■-. In theory, an etymon with the PIE suffix *-dʰro- is possible, but the root is unidentified. Of course, a coin may well be a loanword.

Bibl.: WH I: 336,795f, EM 168, 356, Sihler 1995: 139, Untermann 2000: 828.

<!-- pdf-page: 354; printed-page: 340; running-header: 340                                         licet -->



### licet, licēre
licet, lic5re 'it is permitted' [v. II; licitum est] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: licere 'to fetch (a price)' (Pl.+), Hceri, licitus sum cto make a bid' (Pl.+), licitari'to make a bid, compete' (Pl.+), iicentia 'freedom, licence' (Pl.+), licentiatus, -us 'permission' (Lab.); pollicen 'to promise; offer' (Pl.+), pollicitari 'to promise (assiduously)' (Pl.+), pollicitatio 'promise' (Pl.+); ilicet 'you may go; that's the end!' (PL-*-), scilicet 'obviously, to be sure' (Pl.+), videlicet 'evidently, of course' (Pl.+)-

Pit. *lik-e- 'to be available, have the value of.

It. cognates: O. likitud, licitud [3s.ipv.II] cit is permitted' (borrowed from or calqued on Latin); maybe Ο. λεικειτ [3s.pr.?] < *lik-e-tL The verb licitari is the frequentative to licet. The form ilicet < Tre licet contains the infinitive plus licet. The original meaning of *like- seems to have been 'to be available, be offered for sale, have the value of. The deponent is interpreted as 'to make oneself available', whence 'offer, promise, make a bid' by Nussbaum 1994: 171. There are no certain cognates of the root *lik- outside Italic.

Bibl.: WH I: 679, 797, EM 356f., IEW 669, Nussbaum 1994, Untermann 2000: 434, LIV ?*levk-. →polluceo



### lScium
lScium 'thread, cord' [n. o] (Lex XII, Lucil.+)

Derivatives: bilix, -icis 'having a double thread' (Verg.), trilix, -icis 'having a triple, thread' (Verg.+). Eichner 1995: 68f. interprets the form louiquiod on the Forum cippus as a mistaken rendering of *loiquiod = *loikʷiod> abl.sg. of *loikʷio- 'thread'. No IE etymology.

Bibl.: WH I:798, EM 357, IEW 307-309. → obliquus



### lien, -enis
lien, -enis 'spleen' [m. n] (Pl.+; nom.sg, lien (Prise); nom.sg. lien, -enis (Pl.) with iambic shortening)

Derivatives: lienosus 'affected by a disorder of the spleen' (Pl.+).

Pit. *1ίχέη-.

PIE *splgʰ -en, -en- 'spleen*.

IE cognates: Ofr\ selg, MBreL fetch, Skt plihan^ Av. spzrzzan-, Khot spuijei 'spleen' < Ilr. *spljhan-, Gr. σπλήν 'spleen', σπλάγχνα 'intestines', Arm. p'aycain spleen', Lith. bluznis, Latv. liesa 'id.' < *lielsa, OCS slezena. Long -e- is claimed by grammarians (Servius, Priscian) and said to be attested in the writer Serenus ('th century AD), but the Plautine occurrences of lien{-) can be read either as two shorts or as a long syllable with synizesis (M Weiss, p.c). In Italic,

PIE *splgʰ- was apparently replaced by *(s)lig\ maybe for reasons of taboo; but the model for introducing -/- is unknown.

Bibl.: Soorner 1914: 366, WH I: 799, EM 357f, IEW 987.



### lignum
lignum 'wood' [n. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ligneus 'wooden' (Pl.+), ligneolus 'id.' (Lucil.+), lignan 'to collect firewood' (Pl.+).

Pit. *legno~. PIE-*leg (h W.

<!-- pdf-page: 355; printed-page: 341; running-header: limbus                                       341 -->

If derived from */eg- 'to collect', lignum must originally have indicated 'wood collected for firemaking', from the root *feg- 'to collect'. The full grade might have been adopted from the present. The phonetic change of *-egn- [βηή\ > -ign- is regular. Ritter 2004 prefers to derive lignum from *legʰ- 'to lie', as 'stray wood'. Both etymologies seem equally possible.

Bibl.: WH I: 799, EM 358, IEW 658, Sihler 1995:208, Ritter 2004, LIV *leg-, → lego



### ligo, -are
ligo, -are 'to fasten, bind' [v. I] (Cat>)

Derivatives: alligare 'to tie, fetter' (Pl.+), colligare 'to tie up' (Naev.-l·), deligare 4 id.' (Pl.+), obligare 'to tie up, assign, oblige' (Pl.+), praeligare 'to tie round, fasten' (Pl.+), religare 'to make fast, tie out of the way' (Andr.+), subligare 'to fasten, gird up' (Cato+), subligaculum 'sort of loin-cloth' (Varrcrf); lictor 'attendant to a magistrate' (Pi+); religid 'taboo, impediment, sanction; religious practice' (Pl.+), religiosus 'scrupulous, devote, religious' (Pl.+).

Pit. *-//g-o-.

PIE *lig- 'to tie'?

IE cognates: Alb. lidh 'to tie'. The appurtenance of lictor and of religid to this verb is uncertain. The fact that the simplex ligare does not occur before Catullus, all the earlier verbs being compounds, suggests a basis *lig-e/o- or *leg-e/o-. Semantically, the root *Iig- 'to tie' is a more likely source. Rather than being a denominal verb, *-lig-a- seems to be of the factitive type (capere - occupdre). Apart from Alb. lidh, which can be explained in various ways, there are no certain cognates.

Bibl.: WH I: 800, EM 357f; 569, IEW 668, LIV *leig-.



### lilium
lilium 'lily* [n. o] (Varro+) Probably a borrowing from an (eastern) Mediterranean language, compare in the first place Gr. λείριον 'lily, narciss'. A similar designation is Coptic hreriy hleli 'lily'.

Bibl.: WH I: 801, EM 358.



### Bma
Bma '(carpenter's) file' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: Uynare 'to rub smooth, polish' (Andr>+), The etymology,accepted by WH and IEW connects lima with levis 'smooth' and OHG slim 'mud, slime', slxmen 'to polish, rub smooth', from a root *slei-. Alternatively, it could belong to lino < *h₂li-n-, hence ^le/oi-mbr (Seldeslachts 2001: 91). But a 'file' itself is not 'smooth', at most it 'makes smooth', which does not fit the morphology of lima < *-ma-.

Bibl.: WH I: 801f., EM 358f, IEW 662-664.



### limbus
limbus 'ornamental border, fringe' [m, o] (Var.+; Varro also lembus)

Derivatives: limbularius 'concerned with making fringes' (Pl.).

PIE *lemb-o-,

IE cognates: Skt. rambate (RV), lambate 'to hang down limply'; OE (ge)limpan 'to happen, succeed', Eng. limp [adj.] 'limp', MHG lampeny slampen 'to hang down limply'; ToB lyama, ToA lyam 'sat' [v. pret.].

<!-- pdf-page: 356; printed-page: 342; running-header: 342                                      Iimen -->

In view of the phoneme *£, the very specific meaning of limbus and its absence from the oldest literature, the etymology remains uncertain.

Bibl.: WH I: 802f.f EM 359, IEW 655-657, Schrijver 1991: 179, LP/ *lembH-.



### Iimen, -inis
Iimen, -inis 'beam in a doorframe, threshold, ΗηίεΓ [η. η] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: eliminare 'to go outdours, let out' (Enn.+); limes, -itis [m.] 'boundary strip of land, boundary; road' (Varro+), limitare 'to define, line off (Varro+), limitaris 'of a boundary' (Varro+).

Pit. *lim-en- [n.] 'crossbar', *Um-etr 'boundary'.

It. cognates: O. Himitu[ra [acc.sg. or gen.pL] 'boundary path, limit', probably borrowed from Latin. Derived from (the base of) the adj. limns 'transverse, oblique', possibly *lim-en- 'crossbar\ Limes can be from *lim(o)-it- 'going transverse' (thus WH), but more likely seems a derivational analysis as *limo- 'oblique5 » *lun-et- 'the oblique one, boundary'.

Bibl.: WH I: 803£, EM 359, IEW 307-309. -* limus 2



### limpidus
limpidus 'clear, transparent' [adj\ o/a] (Cat+)

Derivatives: limpor [m.] 'a clear liquid' (LuciL). Since limpor is attested earlier than limpidus, the latter may well have been derived from the former by a productive pattern» According to Solta 1967, who follows an earlier idea by Emout, limpidus may be a dialectal (Sabellic) form in Latin, derived from a verb *limp-e- 'to be liquid' < *li-n-l?-e- (which Solta regards as identical with the root of linquo 'to leave', rather than from *ulinkʷ-). The nasal present of 'to be moist' can be recognized in pol-lingd (see s.v.), and would then indirectly also be attested for Sabellic, viz. in limpor. This account seems possible; but it would presuppose that Sabellic introduced the nasal infix into the stative ^-present which Latin retains unchanged in liqueo, since limp-or is easier to derive from a pr. in -e- than from a nasal present such as pol-lingd,

Bibl.: WH I: 804, EM 359, Untermann 2000: 182, Solta 1967,



### līmus 1
Dmus 1 'mud, slime' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: Umax, -acis 'slug, snail' [f. (m.)] (Pl.+).

Pit. *(s)le/oimo-.

PIE *loimo- 'loam' or *sle/ohii-mo- 'slime, slimy\

IE cognates: OS lemo, OE lorn, OHG leim 'loam' < PGm. *laima/on-; OPr. slayx, Lith. sliekas, Latv. slieka 'earth-worm' < *sloiH-k-; Latv. sliertas, OCS sliny [nom.pl.], Ru. dial, slina, SCr. stina 'saliva' < *slehji-n-h₂; Ru. slimak (dial) 'snail, slug' < ^slehj-m-; OIc. OE MHG slim 'slime'. Gr. λείμαξ 'snail' (Hsch.) is probably (borrowed from) Latin. Lat. limus could belong to PGm. *laima/5n- 'loam' <

PIE *loimo- (to

PIE *h₂H- 'to smear') or to BSL *sloiH-/*sleHi-y PGm. *slima- 'slime, saliva1.

Bibl.: WH I: 802-805, EM 359, IEW 662-664, Schrijver 1991: 243, Sihler 1995:214.



### līmus 2
limus 2 'transverse, oblique' [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: limulus 'oblique' (Pl.); sublimen [adv.] 'in a lofty position' (Naev.+),

<!-- pdf-page: 357; printed-page: 343; running-header: lingua                                      343 -->

subttmis 'high, elevated5 (Naev.+; also subUmus Enn.+), sublimare 'to raise, send up5 (Enn.+).

Pit. ^(s)limo-l Latin continues *lwio- 'transverse' and a compound *sub-lfm-i- 'transverse from below upward* with the original meaning 'upward' of sub. The adv. subUmen originally occurred mainly in subUmen ouferre 'to carry away aloft' vel sim., and probably represents the accsg. *subtlmem of the adj. None of the cognates adduced for Umus in IEW is convincing by its semantics, nor does the vowel always fit: Gr. λειμών 'meadow5, λιμήν 'harbour', Thess. 'market', λίμνη 'lake'; OIc. limr (u) [f.], OE lim [n.] 'member, twig', OIc. limi [m.] 'bundle of twigs, broom'.

Bibl.: WH I: 805, II: 6l8f., EM 359,661, IEW 307-309. —ftmen,lituus



### lingo, -ere
lingo, -ere 'to lick' [v. Ill; pf. Ιϊηχϊ, ppp. Unctum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: delingere 'to lick up, off (Pl.+), sublinguld 'under-dish-licker' (Pl.); ligurrire 'to lick, sponge5 (Pl.+).

Pit. *ling(-e/o)-.

PIE *li-n-gʰ- [pr.J 'to lick'.

IE cognates: Olr. ligid*, -tig*, MW llyu, MBret. leat fito lick' < PCI. *lige/o-; Skt. redhifledhi [3s,pr], ppa. ririhvdms-, Gr. λείχω, Arm. lizanem, Lith. liezti, OCS lizati, Is. liz<? 'to lick' < BSI. *leiz-, OE liccian, OHG leckon < *ligʰ-n~. >

Bibl.: WH I: 806, EM 360, IEW 668, Schumacher 2004:450, LIV *leigʰ-.



### lingua
lingua 'tongue' [f. a] (Lex ΧΠ+; dingua Mar. Victorinus VI 26.2)

Derivatives: li(n)gula 'short sword, tongue-shaped object' (Naev.+), lingulaca (Pl.+) 'talkative person'; bilinguis 'of or with two tongues' (Pl.+), elinguis 'speechless7 (Pac.+), elinguare 'to tear the tongue out of (Pl.).

Pit. *d&x(u)wa-.

It. cognates: O. fangvam [acc.sg.], fancuo [nom.pl.] 'tongue' < *f3tixua- < *</*-.

PIE *dngʰ-uha 'tongue'.

IE cognates: Olr. tengae, MW tafawt 'tongue' < *tng\ Skt. jihva- 'id.', Av; hizuua-, Arm. lezow, OPr. insuwis, Lith. liezuvh, OCS j?zykb, Go. tuggOi OHG zunga, OIc. tungo < *dngʰ-, ToA kaniu, ToB kantwo 'tongue' « *tankʷo. The /- of lingua has been adopted from lingo 'to lick', not from Sabellic, which has/-. The form dingua is poorly attested, but would match the evidence of the other IE branches. Sabellic/· presupposes *dʰ-, which must be due to association with another word in *dʰ- (but which?), cf. van der Staaij 1995: 23. The absence of the development *gʷ > b in Oscan suggests that 'tongue' had the structure *-gʰuH-V- in at least some forms of the paradigm, cf. Weiss 2007a: 368, Kortlandt 1997b: 160 assumes original proterodynamic inflection in PIE; but this does not exclude the rise of, say, a gen.sg. *dngʰ-uh₂-os at a later, pre-Italic stage. We find tabooistic or simply associative (with 'to lick') changes of the phonological form of 'tongue' in many Indo-European languages.

Bibl.: WH I: 806f, EM 360, IEW 223, Sihler 1995: 39, 151, Untermann 2000: 264.

<!-- pdf-page: 358; printed-page: 344; running-header: 344                                         lino -->



### lino, -ere
lino, -ere 'to smear, seal, rub' [v. Ill; pf levi, ppp. liium] (Cato+)

Derivatives: circumlinere 'to smear round, decorate' (Cato+), elinere 'to smear all over' (Lucil.+), oblinere 'to smear oven close5 (Pl.+).

Pit. */mo- 'to smear'.

PIE *h₂li-n-H- [pr.], *h₂li-to- [ppp.].

IE cognates: Olr. lenaid, Λβη 'to stay, glue, follow', W. llynu Ho besmear' < PCI. *lina-; Hit. halina- 'clay(?)' < *h₂liH-no-; Gr, άλίνειν 'to smear' (Hsch.). Schrijver (hesitantly) reconstructs a lengthened grade in the pf., whereas Meiser 1998: 88 and LIV explain -e- in lew, from contraction of *feta-. Neither of these explanations carries conviction. Seldeslachts 2001: 80 accepts a specific phonetic development *leiw- > lev- (rather than > /fv-), which would explain the pf. levlori the basis of a root aorist (cf. Meiser 1998: 86). In that case, liium can regularly go back to a root *h₂li- without final laryngeal. For the pr. lino, however, the PCI. cognate *lina- suggests a laryngeal-final stem.

Bibl.: WH I: 807f, EM 360f, IEW 662-664, Leumann 1977: 534, Schrijver 1991: 19f, 283, Sihler 1995: 534, Seldeslachts 2001: 74-91, Schumacher 2004: 453f, LIV *h₂leiH-.



### linquō, -ere
linquQ, -ere 'to leave' [v. Ill; pf, liquid ppp. -licium] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: delinquere 'to be lacking, err' (Pl.+X deliquio 'failure; eclipse' (Pl.+), delictum 'misdeed, offence' (Pl.+), delicuus 'lacking' (Pl.+X derelinquere 'to abandon' (Varro+), relinquere 'to leave' (Pl.+), reliquiae [f.pl] 'remnants, remains' (Pl.+), reliquus 'left, remaining' (Pl.+)«

Pit. *linkʷ-(e/o-)> *(le-)loik\ *lik}Vio- 'to leave'; *-likʷ-e/ow-o- 'remaining'.

PIE pr. *li-n-kʷ-, pf. *(le-)loikw-, p p p . *//Γ/ο-.

IE cognates: Olr. teicid*, *leici* 'to let (go), leave' < PCI. *linkʷe/o-\ Skt. rinakii, pf. rireca, YAv. pr. irinaxti 'to leave'; Gr. λιμπάνω, λείπω [pr.] 'to let, leave'; Arm. Ik'anem 'id/, OPr. po-lxnka 'stays', Latv. likt, Is. lieku 'to leave, put', Go. leifoan 'to lend'. The verb forms are inherited from PIE. The older form of reliquus was *relikuwos, as in delicuus. Similarly, reliquiae < *relikuwia. These, then, reflect *-likʷ-e/ow-o-.

Bibl.: WH I: 808£, EM 361, IEW 669f, Leumann 1977: 136, Meiser 1998: 98, LIV *leik^



### finuro
finuro 'flax plant, Unseed; linen' [η. ό] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: Unea 'string, cord; line' (Pl.+), lineare 'to make straight' (Pl.+); linteum 'piece of linen cloth, towel, sail' (Pl.+), linteolum 'piece of linen' (Pl.+), linted * weaver of linen' (Pl.).

Pit. *lino- [n.].

IE cognates: Gr. λίνον 'linen', OPr, lynno 'flax', Lith. Unas 'flax (plant)', [pi.] linoi 'flax (fibres)', Latv. lint 'flax' [pi.], CStem>9Ru. ten, gen.sg. Vna 'id.' < BSl. * linos. The noun ITnea can be understood as a substantivization of the adj. Imeus 'made of flax or linen', which itself is not attested before Verg. The alternative adj. *linteus is somewhat surprising, and suggests an unattested noun *linto- 'linen5 as its basis. The variation between *Ιϊηο- and *linto- in Latin, to which *Λ/?ο- from Greek and BSL

<!-- pdf-page: 359; printed-page: 345; running-header: Us                                        345 -->

can be compared, makes it impossible to reconstruct a common PIE protoform. Probably, it was a loanword meaning 'flax'.

Bibl.: WH I: 810, EM 361, IEW 691, Schrijver 991: 243.



### lippus
lippus 'having watery or inflamed eyes' [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: lippire 'to have watery or inflamed eyes' (Pl.+), lippitudo 'imflammation or watering of the eyes' (Pl.+).

Pit. *l(e)ipo-7

PIE *l(e)ip-o- 'sticky, fat'?

IE cognates: Gr λίπος [n.] 'fat5, λιπαρός 'fat, greased'. One of the many adjectives of physical defect with geminated stop. Might belong to ΡΪΕ *leip- 'to be sticky, be fat', but the semantics are not compelling.

Bibl.: WH I: 811, EM 362, IEW 670f., LIV l.*leip-,



### liqueo
liqueo 'to be clear, evident' [v. II; pf. licuf/liqui\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: liquor ca liquid; fluidity' (Pl.+), liquidus 'liquid; evident' (Naev.+), liquidiusculus 'milder' (Pl.), liquescere 'to become liquid, melt' (Naev.+); liqui 'to become liquid, dissolve' (Pl.+); colliquescere 'to melt, liquefy' (Varro+), eliquescere Ho become liquid' (Varro+), liquefacere 'to melt' (Lucr.+), colliquefacere 'id.' (Varro+), deliquare 'to make clear, strain' (Pl.+), eliquare 'to purify by straining' (Varro+), liquare cto make liquid or clear' (Hor.+). \

Pit. *(w)lei1F-e/o- 'to make/become liquid', *(w)Iik -e-_lto be liquid'. w

PIE *uleikʷ- 'to make moist*?

IE cognates: Olr. fliuch 'moist', W. gwlith [m.] 'dew'; ToA [inf.] lyikisi 'to wash', ToB [pn] laikanatra 'washes', liksan- 'washes', [pf.] laikate 'washed'. The verbs in -are are compounded factitives to the stative e-verb. The set liqueo - liquor - liquidus is of a productive type; beside Uqui< *w/ez/:w-, lique- is probably the old stative. The old nasal present changed to -nwgo, see s.v. pollingd.

Bibl.: WH I: 244f., 812, II: 618, EM 131f., 362, 661, Leumann 1977: 117, LIV *uleiky-, → //χα, pollingd



### lira
lira 'furrow5 [f. a] (Columella+)

Derivatives: delirus 'insane' (Lucr.+), delirare 'to be mad; deviate from the balks (in ploughing' (Pl.+), deliritas 'insanity' (Lab.), deliramentum 'nonsense' (Pl.+).

Pit. *loisa~.

It. cognates: possibly O. luisarifs [abl.pl.f.] month-name (cin which the furrows are drawn'?).

PIE *lois-h₂- 'furrow, track'.

IE cognates: OPr. lyso 'field bed', OCS lexa 'field bed, furrow'; OHG leisa 'track'. Lat. de-lirus probably developed from *de tirad{ire) '(to go) off the track'.

Bibl.: WH I: 338, 812Γ, EM 362f., IEW 671, LIV *leis-.



### lis, -tis
lis, -tis 'lawsuit; dispute' [f.] (Lex XII+; arch. Cic. et aliter stlis, inscn stlivdi = stlitibus iudicandis)

Derivatives: Utigare 'to go to law, dispute' (Pl.+), litigium 'quarrel, lawsuit' (Pl.+).

Pit. *sti-ti- 'accusation, dispute'.

<!-- pdf-page: 360; printed-page: 346; running-header: 346                                          lito -->

PIE *sliH-ti-?

IE cognates: Olr. ft/d*, 7/* 'to accuse', MW lliw 'proof, accusation' < PCI. *liie/o- < *sliH-e/o-, Olr. lithech 'accused person' < *Uti-ko-. Joseph 1986 connects sills with Olr. litffech and the verb Hid 'to accuse'. He reports (p. 122) a suggestion made by Jochem Schindler during a presentation in 1980, to the effect that Lat. slls, as found in one inscription from the 2nd c. BC, might be older than stlis. Initial stl- could be the contemporary phonetic rendering of the rare sequence sl·, which only survived in archaisms, but had otherwise changed to initial /-. If this is correct, we could reconstruct an Italo-Celtic root *sliH- 'to dispute, accuse*. Oln lithech would contain the same nominalft'-stemas Latin. Gusmani 2002 connects Lat Us with PGm. *sleitan 'to split', OHG hari-sUz 'splitting the army, desertion', which would seem to derive from a form in a final *-d-_ He is hesitant about connecting the Celtic forms. Yet we also find PGm, *sleipa- or *slipa- 'bad' in Go. sleips or sleideis 'pernicious, fierce, dangerous', gasleipjan 'to damage', so that, if at all related, we may posit PGm. *slffi- with different dental enlargements.

Bibl.: WH I: 813, EM 363, Leumann 1977: 189, Schumacher 2004: 452.



### litō, -āre
litO, -are 'to obtain or give favourable omens' [v. I] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: litatio 'obtaining favourable omens' (Pl.+). Denominative to a noun *lita, which was probably borrowed from Gr. λιτή 'request5 < *λιτά, cf. λίσσομαι, λίτομαι 'to pray, beg'.

Bibl.: WH I: 814, EM 363.



### Iittera
Iittera 'letter of the alphabet; a writing; erudition' [f. a] (Naev.+; older litera, disputed by EM)

Derivatives: litterarius 'of writing* (Pl.+), litieratus 'marked with letters; cultured' (Pl.+). WH reconstructs *Utes-a> which would derive from an earlier adj. *lei-to- to the root of lino 'to smear': the semantics would be 'smeared sign' > 'letter'. Yet morphologically, this scenario is completely unconvincing. The ppp. of lino is litus with a short vowel, and it is unlikely that from a ppp. *l(e)i-to- an s-stem *leit-e/os- would be derived without other cognate forms.

Bibl.: WH I: 814, EM 363.



### litus, -oris
litus, -oris 'sea-shore7 coast5 [n. r] (Pl.+)

Pit. Vei/Itos [n.].

PIE *leit-os- 'going'?

IE cognates: Av* iriuiia- 'to die', ppa. inriuus- 'dead', Go. ga-leipan 'to go', ToB lita, ToA lit [pret], ToB laitam, ToA letas [sb.] 'to go away\ A connection with VeiH- 'to pour', as is hesitantly accepted by WH, does not make sense semantically. Somewhat better would be a connection with *lih₂- 'to stop, disappear' (Skt. -tfyate 'dissolves', Gr. λίναμαι Ί avoid', OE linnan 'to stop'), but the morphology would remain difficult. The root */etf- 'to go (away)* seems to be a better candidate: *leit-os- 'the going away' > 'end, side'.

Bibl.: WH I: 815, EM 364, IEW 664f, LIV l.*leit-.

<!-- pdf-page: 361; printed-page: 347; running-header: locusta                                      347 -->



### lituus
lituus 'curved trumpet, curved staff [m. o] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: liticen 'trumpeter' (Cato+). Etymology unknown. If the meaning 'curved' is original, one could connectd Go. lifus, OIc. lidr 'member, part, lid' (thus IEW) and posit *litu- 'curve(d)'.

Bibl.: WH I: 815f., EM 364, IEW 307-309. → limus 2y obltquus



### fividus
fividus 'of a dull or greyish-blue colour, envious' [adj. o/a\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: Uvescere 'to become dull blue' (Lucr+), iTvor 'bluish discoloration, envy' (Pl.+).

Pit. *(s)le/oiwo- or *(s)liwo-,

PIE *(s)l(e/o)iH-uo- 'plum-coloured, blueish'.

IE cognates: Ofc\ //, W. lliw 'colour' < *(s)liH-u-\ Ra sliva 'plum', SCr. slfiva 'id/ < *sliH-uehr, OHG sleha, OE slah7 MoDu. (dial) sleeuw 'sloe' < PGm. *slaix(u)a/o-. Since the other IE languages point to a derivative in *-uo-, the Latin word family must be based on iTvere (only attested after Cic.) or on ITvidus, which replaced *tiuos. The original ablaut grade of Latin /fv- cannot be determined.

Bibl.: WH I: 816, EM 364, IEW 965, Schrijver 1991:231, — luridus

lixa 'water, lye' [f. a] (Nonius) ^

Derivatives: lixrvus '(probably) liquid, flowing' (Cato+); elixus 'boiled' (Pl.+), prolixus 'having extensive growth, extended, long' (Ter>), prdlixitudo 'great length' (Pac); maybe lixula 'kind of cheese pancake' (Varro), r

Pit. *wl(e)ik"so- 'fluid, flowing'.

PIE *ul(e)ikw-s-. The noun lixa is probably a f. of the adj. */ixitf 'fluid' which is contained in the other derivatives. This *lixus probably represents an adj\ in *-so- to the root of liqued. In prolixus, the original meaning was 'flowing forth, continuing', in elixus it must have been 'boiling out'.

Bibl.: WH I: 81'6f., Π: 370, EM 364, 538, LIV *uleikV → liqueo



### locus
locus 'place' [m/o] (Andr.+; stlocus (Quint., Paul. exR); pi loci/loco)

Derivatives: ilico 'on the spot, there' (Naev.+); locare 'to place, award, hire out' (Pl.+), locarium 'rent for a market stall' (Varro), locitare 'to be in the habit of letting out' (Ten), hculus 'small place, box1 (Pl.+), loculatus 'divided into compartments' (Varro); collocare 'to put, set up' (Andr.+), illocabilis 'that cannot be married1 (Pl.). WH and IEW derive stlocus from the root *stel- 'to place', but this does not make sense morphologically: there is no suffix *-oko~. In view of the possibility that initial stl- goes back to *sl- (see s.v. to), one might also consider a preform *slok-o-. The further etymology remains unclear.

Bibl.: WH I: 679, 817f., EM 309, 364f, IEW 1019£, Untermann 2000: 686f



### locusta
locusta 'locust; lobster' [f. a] (Naev.H-) The quantity of the first syllable is not completely certain: we also find luc- and Axx

<!-- pdf-page: 362; printed-page: 348; running-header: 348                                       lolium -->

The only word similar in form and meaning is lacerta 'lizard; mackerel', but there is no common preform in sight. Lat lacerta may have unrounding of *loc- > lac- (cf lacus\ but in that case the roundedt vowel in locusta must be explained from borrowing. Thus, they could be cognate words in the language from which Latin borrowed these forms.

Bibl.: WH I: 818, EM 365, IEW 673- → lacertus



### lolium
lolium 'kind of grass, darnel' [η. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: lolleaceus 'made of darnel' (Varro). No etymology.

Bibl.: WH I: 819, EM 365, IEW 650f.



### longus
longus 'long' [adj. o/a] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: longe [adv.] 'far, for a long while' (Naev.+); longinquus, longincus 4 long; remote, distant* (Pl.+), longinquitas 'distance, duration' (Ter.+), longiscere 'to become long' (Enn.), longitudo 'length' (Cato+), longule [adv.] 'at a distance' (Pl.+); perlongus 'very long' (Pl.+).

Pit. *(d)longo.

PIE *dlongʰ-o-.

IE cognates:

PIE *dolugʰ- in Hit. *taluki- I talugai- [adj.] 'long'; Hit. zaluhtu-21' £to lengthen' < *dlu^-n(e)u-\

PIE *dlh₂gʰ-o- in Skt dirgha→ OAv. dardga-, OCS dlvgb, SCr. dug, Lith. llgas, Alb. gjate 'long' (+ *-ta-)\ Gr. δόλιχος (< *dolhri-gʰ-o-?), ενδελεχής Masting long, uninterrupted' < *delhigʰ-;

PIE *dlongʰ-o- in Go. laggs, OHG lang, OIc. langr, MP drang 'long'. Gr. λόγχη 'spear' would theoretically go back to *dlongʰ-o- (if dl- > l· in Greek), but it is rejected by Beekes (fthc). The adj. longinquus was probably formed on the basis of longe (thus EM) as an antonym to propinquus. A major question is whether

PIE *d(o)lugh- (Hit.), *doligʰ-, *delgʰ- (Gr.), *dlongʰ- (Lat., Gm.) and *dlhjgʰ- (Ilr., BSl., Alb.) are different variants of what was originally a compound petrified in the meaning 'long' (Kloekhorst 2008: 819f. compares cases such as MoE high and dry, safe and sound). The first member could be *de/ol(hi)-, compare Cz. del (poet.) [£ i] 'length' < PSL *dblb, OCS prodbljg Ί prolong', Ru. dlit'sja, Cz. dliti 'to last', Ru. dlina 'length'; the second member containing *-gʰ-.

Bibl.: WH I: 820, EM 366, IEW 197.



### loquor, loqin
loquor, loqin 'to talk, speak' [v. Ill; ppp. locutum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: loquitari 'to talk constantly' (Pl.+), loquax 'talkative' (Pl.+), loquaculus 'gossiping' (Lucr.), loquela 'speech, utterance* (Pl.+); alloqui 'to speak to, invoke' (Naev.+), colloqul 'to speak to, with' (Pl.+), eloqul 'to utter, tell' (Pl.+), eloquentia 'eloquence* (Ter.+), interloqul'to speak between' (Ter.+), obloqui'to interrupt' (Pl.+), praeloqm'to speak first' (P\.+), prdloqw* to speak forth, announce' (Pl.+), proloquium 'axiom, statement' (Varro+), transloqui"'to go through the whole tale' (Pl.).

Pit. *(t)lol?-e/o-.

PIE *tlokʷ- 'to talk',

IE cognates: Olr. adrtluchedar 'gives thanks\ do-tluchedar

<!-- pdf-page: 363; printed-page: 349; running-header: lucrum                                       349 -->


'demands'; Ru, tolk * meaning'. The ppp* locutus must be analogical after e.g. solui: solutus.

Bibl.: WH I: 821, EM 366, IEW 1088, Schrijver 1991: 476, Sihler 1995: 210, 624.



### lōra
IDra 'drink made from watered grapeskins' [f, a] (Cato+; variant Idrea Gell.) A derivation from lavd as *loweray as proposed by WH, is unlikely for phonetic reasons, since Vowera would normally yield Lat. *lura. For a form Wra to emerge, a sequence */ow- would have to be restored, which is unlikely in view of the Pit. unrounding in lavd. Morphologically, *louH-ro- is not very convincing. Semantically, lora could be many things; there is no obvious connection to * washing'.

Bibl.: WH I: 821, EM 366.



### lōrum
lōrum 'leather strap, thong' [η. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: loreus 'made of strips or thongs' (Pl.+), lorica 'corselet or cuirass' (Pl.+), loricare 'to cover with a cuirass' (VarrcH-); loripes, -dis 'having deformed feet' (Pl.+).

IE cognates: Gr. ευληρα, Dor. αϋληρα [pi.], Hsch, αβληρα 'reins' (< *ά-ρληρο-?)3 Arm. for'cord\ Beekes (fthc.) regards the alternation ε-/α- within Greek as inexplicable; he therefore suspects Pre-Greek origin. If ευληρα reflects *ηυληρα (which would not fit the hexameter), Proto-Greek may have had *aulera. Theoretically, this might reflect a

PIE form *h₂e-h₂ul-er-, but the suffixation is strange. Thus, we may indeed be dealing with a loanword, but the ablaut Gr. e vs. Lat. δ does look Indo-European, Maybe the giving language was a now extinct IE language? For lorica, a separate loanword etymology is often proposed, viz. from Gr ϋώραξ 'cuirass', Ion. ύώρηξ, or both could be from an unknown Mediterranean language. Yet WH reject this solution, arguing that lorica was originally made from leather, and hence belongs to lorum.

Bibl.: WH I: 822, EM 366£, IEW 1140-1144, Schrijver 1991: 122f,



### lūbricus
lii brie us 'slippery' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+)

Pit. *(s)Ioufre/o-L·-.

PIE *sle/oubʰ-rp- cgliding\

IE cognates: Go. sliupan 'to creep, slide' (withfp < *pp < *-bn-)y OE slupan (with secondary u) ; OE slie/e [f.] 'sleeve'.

Bibl.: WH I: 822f., EM 367, IEW 9631, Meiser 1998: 87, LIV *sleubV



### lucrum
lucrum 'material gain, profit' [n. ο] (Pl.+; luenfacere 'to make a profit' Pl.+)

Derivatives: lucrifer 'bringing gain' (Pl.), lucrificabilis 'profitable' (Pl.), lucrifuga [m.] 'spendthrift' (Pl.), lucripeta [adj.m.] 'avaricious'.

Pit. *luklo- > dissim. *lukro-.

PIE *Ih₂u-tlo- 'seizure, gain\

IE cognates: Gr, απολαύω 'to enjoy', λεία, Don λάίδ (< *λαρ-ία) 'booty'; Go. laun [m] 'reward' < *leh₂u-no-. Short */w- would be regular from pretonic *lh₂u- according to the rules of Schrijver 1991: 248f. The PIE basis *l(e)hzu- found in lucrum, as well as in Gr, 'to enjoy', Go. 'reward', could formally be connected with Hit lahu-llahu- 'to pour, cast' <

<!-- pdf-page: 364; printed-page: 350; running-header: 350                                       luctor -->

*l(o)h₂u-, lahhu- 'container' < *leh₂u-; but the semantics are difficult. Alternatively, the non-Anatolian forms in *l(e/o)h₂u- could be compared with Hit. lahh- 'military campaign, journey', CLuw. lahhi(ia)* 'to travel, campaign' < *loh₂-/*leh₂-, Gr. λα(ρ)ός 5men, army', Olr. laech 'warrior' < *leh₂u-. The original meaning of *leh₂- could have been 'to fight', that of *leh₂u- 'to acquire by fighting\

Bibl.: WH I: 826, EM 367f., IEW 655, Schrijver 1991:240f



### luctor, -ārī
luctor, -Sri 'to wrestle, struggle' [v. I] (Pl.+; active luctare in OLat., dep. luctarl in CLat.)

Derivatives: luctator 'wrestler' (Pl.+); deluctarT 'to fight it out with' (Pl.).

Pit. *lukto-.

PIE *lug-to- 'bent\

IE cognates: Olr.fo-loing 'supports', inloing 'connects', MW ellwng- 'to set free' < PCI. *-lunge/o- 'to place', Gr. λύγος [f.] 'withy, twigs', Go. ga-lukan 'to shut', us-lukan 'to open'. Frequentative verb based on a ppp. *luktos 'bent\ If the etymology is correct, it is striking that there is no (visible) reflex of Lachmann's lengthening in luctor nor in luxus. Since Celtic shows a nasal present, the short reflex may have been restored from this present; but this is speculative, since no trace of such a present exists in Latin.

Bibl.: WH I: 826f., EM 368, IEW 685f., Schumacher 2004: 460f., LIV 2.*leu(g)-. -* luxus



### liicus
liicus 'sacred grove, wood' [m. o] (Pl.+, CIL 1.366 loucom)

Derivatives: lucar, -aris [m.] 'sacred grove' (CIL 1.401, 1.1730); collucare 'to thin out (trees)1 (Cato+); Lucina 'cognomen of Iuno, invoked by women when giving birth' (Pl.+).

Pit. *louko- 'sacred grove'.

It. cognates: Ven. louki [gen.sg.] 'sacred grove', O. luvkei [loc.sg.], U. vukum-en, vuku [acc.sg.], vukes [gen.sg.], vuku-kum, uocu-com [abl.sg. + -com], vuke [locsg.] '(sacred) enclosure'.

PIE *Iouk-o- [m.] 'light place'.

IE cognates: Skt loka- [m.] 'free space, world', Lith. laukas 'field, land', Latv. lauks 'field, clearing in the woods', OHG Idh 'clearing7. For the derivation of Lucina from liicus 'sacred grove', cf. Leumann 1960.

Bibl.: WH I: 828, EM 368, IEW 687-690, Lejeune 1974: 336, Untermann 2000: 439f., LIV *leuk-. → luna, liix



### ΐαάδ, -ere
ΐαάδ, -ere 'to play, sport' [v. ΠΙ; pf. lusi, ppp. lusum] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: Indus (arch, inscr. loed-, loidr) 'sport, play, jest' (Naev.+); Itidibrium 'plaything,ridiculousthing' (Pl.+)> liidibundiis 'merry* (Pl.+), ludicer [adj. ο/δ] 'of the stage, of sport' (Pl.+), ludicre [adv.] playfully' (Enn.+); ludificori 'to make an object of sport, play with' (Pl.+), ludificator 'who plays with' (Pl.), liidificatus, -lis 'the teasing' (Pi.), ludificabilis 'suitable to play with' (Pl.); lusio 'play, sport' (Varro+), lusitare 'to amuse oneself (Pl.+), lusor 'player' (Pl.+); alludiare 'to play with' (Pl.+), alBdere 'to play, jest' (Ter.+), collusor 'fellow gambler' (Lucil.+), collusim 'in collusion' (Pl.+), deludere 'to deceive' (Pl.+), deludificare 'to make a complete fool

<!-- pdf-page: 365; printed-page: 351; running-header: lumbncus                                       351 -->


of (PI-X eiudere 'to deceive, dodge' (Ter.+), eludificart 'to fool completely' (Pl.), illudere 'to make game of (Ter.+), obludiare 'to play the fooF (Pi.)-

Pit. *loid-e/o- 'to play', *loido- 'play'.

PIE *Ioid- 'to let go frequently, play\

IE cognates: Mir. laidid 'impels', Gr. λίνδεστ3αΐ 'to contend' (Hsch.), λίζεΐ 'plays' (Hsch.), Alb. lind 'gives birth5, lindet 'is born' < Hi-n-d-\ OLith. leidmi Ί let', Lith. leisti, Is. leidziu 'to let' < pr. *leid~; Lith. laidyti 'to throw', Latv. laist 'let, publish, set in motion' < */οκ/-. The o-grade found in Irish, Baltic and Latin could point to an original causative present, but the suffix of Latin does not fit. We seem instead to be dealing with an o-grade present of frequentative meaning. The noun Indus is probably deverbal, since no equivalents are found elsewhere,

Bibl.: WH I: 829, EM 368f, IEW 666, LIV *Ieid-.



### lugeō
lflgeo 'to mourn, grieve' [v. II; pf. Ιϋχϊ, ρρρ. factum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: lugubris 'mourning, sad' (Pl.+).

Pit. Voug-eje- [v.], *loug-os-ri-l

PIE *loug-eie~ 'to swallow, gasp'.

IE cognates: Olr. loingid fcto eat' < *(s)Iu-n-g-\ Gr. λυγγανώμενον 'sobbing' (Hsch.), λύζω 'to have the hiccup, sob' < *(s)lug-ie«, λυγμός 'the hiccup(s)'. WH suggest that lugubris more probably reflects *lugos-ri- (with an «s-stem *lugos- belonging to the e-present lugere) than *luge-bli- (with regular dissimilation to -bri- after the /-containing stem). It is disputed to which root lugere belongs. It is often connected with

PIE */wg- 'to break5, and indeed a semantic connection between 'breaking' and 'mourning' can often be found in etymology, due to the intensity of the acts of mourning. Lat lugere would fit the morphology of the causative, but semantically a stative (*lug-ehr, in which case the zero grade of the root is striking) or a frequentative {*(le-)loug-ie-) of'to break' are more likely. Kolligan 2005 objects that there is no evidence for the assumed shift in meaning from 'break' to 'mourn' in Latin, and also, that lugeo is normally used as an active, transitive verb. He therefore proposes the; alternative connection with

PIE *(s)leug- 'to swallow', which would have developed via 'to gasp' to 'wail, mourn'. The causative *loug-eie- could be understood as/to bemourn (someone)'. This solution seems more straightforward to me than the connection with 'to break\

Bibl.: WH I: 830f, EM 369, IEW 686, Kolligan 2005, LIV *(s)leug-.



### lumbncus
lumbncus 'earthworm, intestinal worm' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

IE cognates: see s.v. lumbus. The meaning 'earthworm' is more frequent and more ancient in the texts than 'intestinal worm', The only OLat. place where lumbncus is translated as 'intestinal worm' by OLD refers to the form of the worm: Ad tormina et si alvus consistet et si taeniae et htmbrici molesti erunt Tor gripes, for loose bowels, for tapeworms and stomach-worms, if troublesome' (C&o,AgK 126). The word lumbrici will have been used metaphorically to indicate the form of the worm plaguing the patient; compare taenia 'ribbon, string; (here:) intestinal worm'. The suffix recalls umbilicus (also

<!-- pdf-page: 366; printed-page: 352; running-header: 352                                     lumbus -->


situated in the lower belly) and formica 'ant' (also an insect). WH assume that lumbr- reflects *lonctr-, and derive the word from a root *lndʰ-, see s.v. lumbus. But since the PIE voiced aspirates normally yield stops after a nasal in all Italic languages, thus bleeding the specifically Latin change *-dtr- > -br- (cf. Meiser 1986: 75f), *lonc?r- would yield Latin *lundr-. As Latin does not possess a productive suffix -ricus, the origin of lumbncus remains unclear.

Bibl.: WH I: 831, EM 369, IEW 960f



### lumbus
lumbus 'hips, loins' [m. ο] (Pl.+; usually pi.)

Derivatives: lumbifragium * loin-wreckage' (Pl.).

Pit. *londwo- 'loins'.

PIE *londʰ-u-o-.

IE cognates: HLuw. la-tara/i- llandri(ya)-l 'to expand (a country)', Skt. randhra- 'loin (of animals); weak spot on the body; cavity' < *londʰ-ro-; OCS l$dvij$ [f.pL] 'loins, insides, kidneys', Ru. Ijadveja (arch.) 'thigh' < *lenct-u-\ OIc. lend, OHG lentin 'kidneys', OE lendenu 'loins' < *londʰ-io-; Olr, land 'free space', MW llan 'area', Co. Ian, Bret, lann lieath' < *lndʰ-h₂-; OPr. acc.sg. lindan 'valley'; Ru. Ijdda, Cz. lada9 lado 'fallow land' < *lenc?-o-; Go. OIc. OS OE land, OHG font 'land' < *londʰ-o-; Lith. lendu 'to creep, sneak (into)'. Goto 1985 has argued that Skt. randhra- can be connected with lumbus and with Gm. *landa- 'land', under the assumption of a PIE verb *lenct-e- 'to sink, go down' (Lith. lendu 'to creep into') which was adopted as such by LIV. Oettinger 2007 has made the comparison more explicit and has clarified the semantics and the morphology. Adding HLuw. latara/i- 'to expand (a country)' to the dossier, he posits an original meaning 'to enter, penetrate' for

PIE *lendʰ-. This would be continued (in a specific usage) in Lith. lendii, whereas 'land' in BSl., CI. and Gm, can be understood as 'the area which is entered'. The HLuw. verb 'to expand', a denominative to *landra- < *loncfro- 'free space', could be understood as 'to make into one's (own) land'- The Skt. meaning 'weak spot on the body' and 'loin' would have developed in hunter's jargon for the spot where a hunted animal was the most vulnerable, viz. below the ribs. This, then, would explain Gm. and Latin 'loins', Sl. 'thigh'. The wo-stem of Latin could be built on the w-stem *lendʰ-u- still visible in Slavic. The development *-ndw- > -mb- would be parallel to initial *dw- > Lat. b-,

Bibl.: WH I: 832, EM 369, IEW 675, Meiser 1998: 120, LIV *lendʰ-. → lumbncus



### luna
luna 'moon' [f. a] (Pl.+; Praeneste losna)

Derivatives: lunula 'crescent-shaped ornament' (Pl.). Pit» *louksna→

PIE *le/ouk-s-nh₂- 'shiny, bright, gleaming'.

IE cognates: Olr. dia luain 'Monday', Av. raoxsna- [adj./n.] 'light', Gr. λύχνος [m.] 'lamp', OPr. lauxnos [pi.] 'stars', MHG liehsen 'bright'. The base *le/uk-s- is also found in lumen and lustrum (see s.v. lux). It might be derived from the 5-stem *leuk-os-. The word *le/ouk-s-no- was probably used as an epithet for the moon in PIE.

Bibl.: WH I: 833, EM 373, IEW 687-690, Meiser 1998: 62, LIV *Ieuk-. → lucus, lux

### luo, -ere
luo, -ere 'to suffer, make amends for' [v. Ill; pf, lui(lm Varro)] (Acc,+)

Derivatives: luella 'expiation' (Lucr.), lues, -is 'plague, affliction' (Cic,+); reluere 'to redeem' (Caecil.).

Pit. *luwe/o-.

PIE aor. *l(e)uH- 'to cut loose', pf *louH-?

IE cognates: Olr, os-foi* 'to flee' < PCI. *eks-luue/o-; Skt lunati 'cuts off, aluna- 'not shaved'; Gr. λύω 'to loosen, liberate', aor. midd. λύμην, λύ(ν)το; OE le [m.] 'sickle'; ToB lyuwa, ToA lyu 'sent3. If Lith. liauti 'to stop', PSL *leviti 'to diminish, weaken' are cognate, the root was *lhiu-. The thematic pr. of Italo-Celtic and Greek is probably secondary to the root aorist (LIV). The Latin form /w? might preserve a trace of earlier */ow-w>-.

### lupus
lupus 'wolf [m. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: lupa 'whore' (Pl.+), 'she-wolf (Prop.+), lupanar 'brothel' (Pl.+), luparT 'to prostitute oneself (Lucil.+), lupmus 'of a wolf (Pl.+), lupvnum 'the lupin (plant)' (Cato+), lupmarius 'of lupins' (Cato), lupillum/s 'lupin-seed' (Pl.).

Pit. *lukʷo-7 ηιιρο-Ί ; J w

PIE *ulk o- 'wolf,

IE cognates: Skt. vfka-, YAv. vahrka- [m.], MP gurg, Gr. λύκος [m.], Lith. vilkas, Latv. vilks, ORu. vtlkb [m.] < *w/£V, Go. wulfs [m.], ToB walkwe [m.] 'wolf. The earlier derivation of lupus from

PIE *ulkʷo- 'wolf requires two ad hoc assumptions: metathesis to *lukʷo- in Italic (as is often assumed for Gr. λύκος 'wolf), and borrowing by Latin of the Sabellic outcome *lupo-. This is conceivable. Alternatively, one could derive lupus from

PIE *ulp- / *lup- 'marten' (whence, among others, *Av. urupi-, Lat. volpes 'fox'); cf de Vaan 2000: 289. This would amount to a semantic shift from 'marten' to 'fox' to 'wolf, the latter one maybe by tabooistic replacement of an earlier word for 'wolf. The disadvantage of this approach is that the stem *ulp- is already continued in Lat. volpes, so that an additional reflex in lupus would require further special pleading,

Bibl.: WH I: 835-837, EM 370, IEW 1178f, Leumann 1977: 101, Sihler 1995: 161. → volpes



### lurco
lurco 'glutton, gourmand' [m. η] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: lurcare/ϊ 'to eat greedily' (Lucil.+), lurcinabundus 'eating greedily' (Cato apud Quint,). According to Steinbauer 1989: 168, lured was derived from lurcare, while from the nominal stem *lurk-e/on- a new verb *lurke/on-a- 'to be a glutton' was derived. The further etymology is unclear. WH and IEW suggest a preform Vurgiko-, the element

<!-- pdf-page: 368; printed-page: 354; running-header: 354                                      luridus -->

*/wrg- being cognate with MHG slurc 'glutton', slurken 'to swallow'. But since Gm. has different root enlargements (with a labial, we find MoDu. slurpen), and the syncope assumed for Latin is uncertain, tftis is probably a chance similarity.

Bibl.: WH I: 837, EM 371, IEW 965f



### luridus
luridus 'ghastly yellow, colour of bruises* [adj\ o/a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: Iwror 'sickly yellow colour' (Lucr.+); elurescere Ho become pale' (Varro apudNon.); latum 'yellow dye, colour5 (Verg.+X liiteus 'pink, pale-red' (Pl.+).

Pit. *(s)lous-l *(Ξ)ΙΟΪΤΟ-Ί ^(s)loito^ The exact source and etymology are very uncertain. Schrijver 1995: 332 suggests that luridus belongs to MW lieu Might', MW lloer 'moon' < */e/ous-, while Nussbaum 1997: 199f connects it with Lat. iTvidus (see s.v. for IE cogaates). In the latter case, Lat lu- can reflect *(s)loiH-. For the meaning 'pink' (not 'yellow') of luteus, see Edgeworth 1985.

Bibl.: WH I: 837f, 841, EM 37I£ — lividus



### luscus
luscus 'blind in one eye' [adj. o/a] (PI+)

Derivatives: lusciosus 'suffering from night-blindness' (Var.H-), luscitiosus 'suffering from night-blindness' (Pl.+), lusciniola 'nightingale' (Pl.+X luscinia 'id/ (Hor+).

Pit. *luk-sko- 'with partial sight, visually handicapped'.

PIE *luk-(e/o-)'to see\ The derivatives suggest the previous existence of a v, *luscTre 'to be night-blind'. The nightingale might be explained with haplology from *lusci-cania 'singing in the night' or 'blind singer', but this is speculative. Alternatively, it may be based on an «-stem *lusk-e/on- 'blind one\ WH hesitantly explain luscus from *nuk-sko-, which they connect with Lith. niukti 'to make a dull sound*; Latin would have changed */i- > /- under the influence of lux. Yet the semantics of the Baltic forms do not match very well Lat nuscitiosus is a variant of luscitiosus, only attested in Paul ex F. Fruyt (1986: 162) suggests that Lat. luscus could reflect *luk-sko- derived from the root *leuk- 'light'. In this respect, compare the cognate PCL verb *luk-e/o- 'to see', as in MW adolwyn cto beseech', gorllwyn 'to expect, observe5 (Schumacher 2004: 459f); also MW llygaU MCo. lagas [m.], MBret. logat *eye' < *luk-ati- 'seer' (Schrijver 1995: 166). This explanation seems more attractive to me.

Bibl.: WH I: 838, EM 371, IEW 768, Leumann 1977: 556. — lux



### lustrum
lustrum 'ceremony of purification; five-year period' [n, o] (Cato+)

Derivatives: lustrare 'to purify ceremonially, move round, spread light' (Andr.+); circumlustrare 'to pace round* (Lucr.+); armilustnum 'ceremony of purifying the arms' (Varro+X tubilustrium 'festival at which the sacred trumpets were purified' (Varro+X

Pit. *lustro- 'expiation'. According to WH and IEW, lustrum 'purification' is based on an earlier meaning 'illumination' < *l(e/o)uk-s-tro-9 to Lat lux. Yet there is no good evidence for 'enlightening' in the meaning of the lustrum. One might connect it with lovo 'to

<!-- pdf-page: 369; printed-page: 355; running-header: lux                                          355 -->


wash' - but still, no clear passages exist which link the lustrum with washing. Thus, I
wash' - but still, no clear passages exist which link the lustrum with washing. Thus, I find Serbat's conclusion (1975: 312) most likely, viz. that lustrum was derived from *luH~ 'to set free', Lat. /wo. The suffix *-stro- is also found e.g. in monstrum, cf Leumann 1977: 313.

Bibl.: IEW 687-690, Serbat 1975: 310-312. → luo



### lutra
lutra 'otter' [f. a\ (Varro, Plin.+)

Pit. *udra-.

PIE *ud-ro- [adj.] 'of the water' > 'water animal, otter' [m.].

IE cognates: Skt. udra-, YAv. udra- 'otter', Gr. ΰδρος, ΰδρα 'water-snake', OPr. wudro, Lith. udra, Ru. vydra 'otter' < PS1. *vydra < BSl. *u?dra?, OHG ottar, OIc. otr [m.] 'otter'. The change of

PIE *dr to Latin tr is regular. At some stage, initial /- was added, maybe taken from the verb Ιανό 'to wash'. But since an otter is a carnivore, the /- may also have been taken from lupus 'wolf ♦ Otters are known for their playfulness, so that one might also consider influence from ludere 'to play\ viz. at the stage *udra. The t gender in BSl. and Latin (and partly in Greek) is probably due to the generalization of the fern, of the adjective.

Bibl.: WH I: 840, EM 372, IEW 78-81.

lutum 'mud, dirt, clay' [n. o]:(Pl.+)

Derivatives: lutare 'to coven with mud' (Cato+), lutamentum 'coating of mud' (Cato), luteus 'worthless, dirty' (Pl.+), lutitare 'to d,rag in the mud' (Pl.+), lutdsus 'very muddy' (Enn.+); lustrum I lustra [n.pl.] 'muddy place, den of vice' (Pl.+), lustro 'a frequenter of brothels' (Naev.+), lustrarl 'to haunt brothels' (Pl.+).

Pit. */wfo-, *lustro-.

PIE *l(H)u-to- 'dirt', *l(H)u-(s)tro- 'dirty place'.

### lux, -cfe
lux, -cfe 'light', [f k] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: Iticere *to emit light, dawn; to ignite, cause to shine' (Pl.+), lucidus 'bright, shining' (Lucr+), lucescere 'to begin to shine, dawn' (Pl.+); lucubrare 'to work by lamplight' (Varro+), lucubratio 'work done by lamplight' (Cato+); luculentus 'excellent, splendid' (Pl.+), luculentitas 'splendour5 (Caecil.+), luculentaster [m.] 'a kind of confection5 (Titin.); lumen, -inis [n.] 'light, daylight' (Naev.+), luminare [n.] 'lamp' (Cato); lucerna 'oil-lamp' (Pl.+); Lucius praenomen (Sis.+); allucere 'to light; be lit' (Pl.+), dilucere 'to be clear5 (Varrcrt-), dilucescere 'to dawn' (Lucr.+), dilucidus 'clear, lucid' (Ter.+), diluculum 'daybreak' (Pl.+), illucere 'to shine (on)' (Pl.+), illucescere 'to begin to shine' (Pl.+), perlucere 'to transmit light' (Pl.+), perlucidus 'transparent, clear' (Pl.+)5 relucere 'to shine out' (Varro+), lucifer 'light-bringing; the morning star [m.]' (Acc.+), lucifugiis 'avoiding the light of day, skulking' (Lucil.+); illustris 'bright, famous' (Pl.+), illustrare 'to illuminate' (Lucr.+).

<!-- pdf-page: 370; printed-page: 356; running-header: 356                                      luxus -->

Pit. *louk- 'light', *loukeje- 'to make shine', *l(o)uke- 'to be light\

PIE *leuk- 'light', *louk-eie- 'to make light', *luk-ehr 'to be light', *le/ouk-s-mn 'light'.

IE cognates: Hit lukk-na 'to get light, dawn5 < *leuk-/*luk-, lukkatt- [c] 4 dawn, next morning' < *l(e)uk-ot-; Skt. rocate 'to shine', rue- [f.] 'light, splendour, lustre, appearance5, Av. raocah- [n.] 'light', YAv. raocaiia- 'to make shine', raoca- 'to shine', raoxsna- [adjyn.] 'light'; raocana- [adj.] 'shining, light', OP raucah- [n.] 4 day'; Gr. λευκός 4 clear, light', λεύσσω 'to see'; OPr. luckis * torch, log', RuCS lucb 'ray, light, shining', Cz. louc 'torch' < *louki-, OCS luciti s? 'to happen', Go. liuhap 4 light'; Toch. luk- 'to shine'. Lat. lux is a root noun, also attested in vestiges in Skt The transitive meaning of liicere only occurs in Plautus, but may be old. Stative liicere has an unexpected full grade in the root which may have been adopted from causative *louk-eie-, or from the root noun (Nussbaum 1994: 168). Lucubrare is a denominative of a noun *lukubro~ 'lamplight' < *lukV-dro-. Lat. lumen < Housmen < *le/ouk-s-mn. Lucerna seems to have the suffix of lanterna, but its short -u- is unexplained.

Bibl.: WH I: 823-833, 839, II: 333, EM 368, 372-374, IEW 687-690, Nussbaum 1994, Meiser 2003: 141, LIV *leuk-, → focus, luna



### luxus
luxus 'dislocated, sprained' [m. o/a\ (Cato+)

Derivatives: luxare 'to sprain, dislocate', luxari 'to live riotously' (Pl.+), luxuria/luxuries 'indulgence, immoderate growth, unruly behaviour' (Pl.+), luxuriosus 'given to luxury, excessive' (Cato+), luxus9 -us 'extravagant living, opulence' (Ter.+).

Pit. *Iufo<h.

PIE *lug-so- 'bent\

IE cognates: see luctor. If the etymology is correct, the absence of Lachmann's lengthening must be explained; see s.v. luctor. From an original meaning * lug-so- 'bent', we can explain the semantic shift to 'sprained' on the one hand, and to luxus [m.] 'the bending' > 'lack of restraining, exaggeration' on the other. The noun laxuriaf-es is probably based on an adj. *luxuros.

Bibl.: WH I: 841, EM 374, IEW 685f, LIV 2.*Ieu(g)-. -»luctor

### macer, -a, -urn
macer, -a, -urn 'thin, lean' [adj. ο/α} (Pl.+)

MCo, maga, MBreL maezur, maguaff*to raise, feed' < PCI, *make/o- 'to nourish' (< *'to make long'?), OW MCo, MBreL map 'son' < *makuo→ Olr. mace < *makkuo-; Hit, maklant- 'thin, meagre' < *m(e)h₂lc-lo-nt-, Av. masah- 'size', Gr μακρός Mong', μήκος [n,] 'length', OHG magar, Olc, magr 'meagre'.
