### nam
nam 'certainly, for, well' [ptcle.] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: namque 'certainly, for, well' (Pl.+), numnam [interr.ptcl.] '...not?' (Pl.+), quianam cwhy ever?' (Naev.+), utinam 'if only!5 (Pl.+).

Pit. *no/e- cthat\

IE cognates: If to

PIE *h₂n-:'Skt. ana [adv.], anena [ihstr.sg.m.], anctya [instr.sg.f.], anayos [gen.loc.du.] 'through this', OAv. ana [instr.sg.], anais [instr.pL], YAv. ana [instr.sg.], Arm. ayn cille', na c is', noyn cidem\ Lith. anas 'that', OCS om> 'that, he' [nom.sg.m.], ona [f. ], ono [n.] < *h₂en-o-. If to

PIE *ne 'like': see s.v. ne- and -ne. It might be an original acc.sg.f *nam to a thematic stem *wo/e- 'that', from which also the particles nem(-) and num might be derived. The stem *no/e- can be interpreted in different ways: as an Italic inflected continuant of a PIE particle *ne 'like' (see s.v. ne, cf. Beekes 1995: 222), or as reflex of the PIE deictic pronoun/particle *h₂(e)n- cthat' (Beekes 1995: 202).

Bibl.: WH II: 140f., EM 428, IEW 319-321. -♦ -dam, -ne, nem-, num \ nanciscor, -i 'to acquire, get' [v. Ill; ppp. nactum » nanctum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nancio> -ere/nancor, -i 'to acquire' (Lex XII, Gracch., Fest.).

Pit. pr. *nank-(i-), ppp. *nak-to-.

PIE pr. *h₂n-n-k- 'to reach', pf *h₂ne-h₂no(n)ie-, ppp. *h₂n£-to-.

IE cognates: Olr. aric* 'to decide', domic* 'to come', ro-ic* 'to reach', MW renghu rynghu 'to reach', kyfrang, kyfreing 'meets, fights' < PCI. pr. *-an-n-ke/o-7 Olr. ro-anaic* [3s.pf ] 'to reach' < PCI. *-anonk-. Skt. pr. asnoti, aor. anaty naty pro nak [3s.in|] (< IE *hje-h₂nek-t\ Skt. pf. dnamsa (< IE *hiz-h₂no(n)k-e) 'to reach, attain', Skt. ppp. ά-sam-asta- 'not yet reached'; OAv. frqstd [3s.aor.med.], frosiiat /fra asiat/ [3s.aor.opt.act.], nasama [lp.s-aor.sb.] cto reach', asta- 'arrived', YAv. auuaasnaoiti [pr.] 'to reach, hit'; Gr. ποδ-ηνεκής 'reaching down to the feet', διηνεκής, Att. διάνεκής 'continuous'; Arm. hasanem cto arrive'; Lith. nokti, 3s. noksta 'to grow ripe, (dial,) grow weak, wither', dial. fcto pursue, chase', Latv. nakt cto come' (with

<!-- pdf-page: 414; printed-page: 400; running-header: 400                                      nans -->

elimination of the second n) . The pr. nancTscor was built on an i-stern present, which is still feebly attested by grammarians and glossators. The oldesi system seems to be a pr. *ncmkri- and a ppp, *nakto-. The α-vocalism of *nakto- must be secondary. The pr. is doubly characterised by a nasal infix and a suffix -/; since Celtic shows a nasal present, this will be the older variant. The pf of Ilr. and Celtic shows that *-«- must have become a fixed part of the root at an early date, hence there was a root pr. *h₂ne(n)k-. The Italo-Celtic nasal present *h₂n-n-k- may have phonetically yielded

Pit. *nankr (Schrijver has a phonetic rule for this, cf. p. 491ff-)> and if a concurring form pr. *h₂n-ne-lc- > **anek- ever existed, it was ousted with the support of the perfect *anonk^ The tf-vocalism of the pr.

Pit. *nank- was then imported into the ppp. *ankto- » *nakto-.

Bibl.: WH II: 14If, EM 428f, IEW 316-318, Schrijver 1991: 491, Meiser 1998: 193, Kummel 2000: 284-287, Schumacher 2004: 200-204, LIV *h₂ne£-.



### naris
naris 'nose; pL nostrils, nose' [f. i; pL nares, -ium] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nasus/m [m./n.] 'nose' (Naev.+), nasutus 'having a long nose; witty1 (Lucil.+), Naso 'Roman cognomen' (Ov.+).

Pit. *nas- [f ], *nasi-, *naso- 'nose'.

PIE *Hneh₂-s-, *Hnh₂-es-, *Hnh₂-s- 'nose\

IE cognates: Skt. nas- [f.] 'nose' (nasa [nom.du.], nasos [gen.du.], nasi ° [loc.sg.]) , nasa- [f.] 'nose', nasika- [f] 'nose', urunasa- 'with a broad nose\ pavmasa- 'with a nose like a rim\ YAv. nitqha [nom.acc.du,?], naqhanat [ablsg.] 'nose1 (secondary fl-stem) , OP naham [acc.sg.] 'nose'; Lith, nosis, Latv. nass < *(H)neh₂S-7 RuCS non, Ru. nos' < *nhres-; OHG nasa, OE nasu 'nose1, OE nose [f] 'promontory1, OFr. nasi 'moustache'; MoDu, neiis < *nus-i-. Latin has two derivatives of the (pre-)Pit. stem *nas→ an /-stem and an o-stem. It appears that the PIE word was often used in the dual ('the nostrils'), and the dual of a f. root noun in PIE would have ended in nom.acc. *-h{c >

Pit. *-e. When the dual (lost in Latin) was reinterpreted as a plural, *nase may have been remade into *nases (with the regular pi. ending of C-stems) or *nasoi (as an o-stem). Other scenarios may be envisaged. The non-rhotacized -s- is difficult to explain, other than possibly being a remnant from pre-rhotacism times, from a different social layer. The PIE root cannot be identified with that of anhelus and animus (as proposed by Fritz 1996), which I reconstruct as *h₂nh r 'to breathe, blow\

Bibl.: WH II: 143-146, EM 429, IEW 755, Kortlandl 1985a: 119, Schrijver 1991: 143.



### nascor, nSsci
nascor, nSsci 'to be born1 [v. Ill; ppp. (g)natum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: (g)natus 'son', pi. 'children' (Naev.+), (g)nata 'daughter' (Andr.+), natalis Of birth' (Pl.+), natio 'people, race1 (Pl.+), 'birth of a child' (CIL 1.60 nationu gratia 'nationis gratia', Varro+), natlvus 'original' (Varro+); natu [abl.sg.m.] 'of age, by birth' (Pl.+), natura 'conditions of birth, character' (Pl.+), naturalis 'natural' (Varro+); agnascl 'to be born in addition' (< *ad~gn~) (Varro+), agnatio

<!-- pdf-page: 415; printed-page: 401; running-header: narfnor                                    401 -->


'agnation' (Varro+), agnatus 'younger child; blood relation on father's side' (Lex XII+X cognata 'kinswoman* (Pl.+), cognatus 'male relation' (PK+), cognatus 'related by birth' (Ter.+), cognatio 'blood-relationship' (Varro+), dendsci 'to lose vigour' (Varro+), enasci 'to arise' (Varro+), innoscJ "to be born, arise' (Pl.+), innatus 'innate' (Ter.+),prognatus 'originated, sprung forth' (Elog.Scip., Naev.+);praegnas, -tis (also -ns, -ntis) 'pregnant' (Naev.+), praegnatio 'pregnancy' (Varro+).

Pit. *gnask-e/o- 'to be bom', *gndto- 'bom; son', *gnatu- 'birth', *gnati- 'birth', *gnati(j)on- 'family'.

It. cognates: Pael. cnatois [datpl.] 'son'; U. natine [abl.sg.] 'patrician family' < *gnhrti-(H)on-,

PIE *gnhrsk-e/o- 'to be born', *gnh r to- 'born', *gnhj-ti- 'birth'.

IE cognates: Gaul. Cintu-gnatus 'first-born', f. gnatha 'daughter', MW gnowt 'relative'; SkLjata- [m.] 'born man, son, living being', Av. zata- 'born'; Gr. κασίγνητος [m.] 'brother, sister (of the same mother), cousin' < *gnhrtos + *hpt~i 'born with (from the same mother)'; Go. -kunds 'originating from', OIc. kundr 'son', OE fieofon-kund, OIc. os-kunnr 'of divine descent'. The basic formations are the pr, *gnaske/o-, the ppp. *gnatos 'bom', probably the /w-abstracts *gnatu- and *gnati-9 and the extended //-stem *gndtion-. The latter is also attested in Umbrian with different suffix ablaut, suggesting that Pit. still had an ablauting suffix. Lat. praegnas is explained by Schwyzer 1929: 10 from a possessive cp. *prai→gnati- 'who has birtb ahead of her'. The nom.sg. in *-atis > -as later gave rise to an oblique stem praegnant-, whence a new nomig. in -ans.

Bibl.: WH I: 598, II: 354, EM 272, 429f., 531, ΙΕψ 373ffi, Leumann 1977: 146, 398, Schrijver 1991: 178, 330, Sihler 1995: 296, Meiser 1998: 108, Untermann 2000: 402,487f., LIV *genh r . → natinor



### nassa
nassa 'fish-trap made of wickerwork' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Pit. *nasso-.

PIE *nHd-to- 'tied, knotted'.

IE cognates: Olr. nascaid*, MBret. naska 'to bind' < PCI. *nad-sk-, Olr. nassae 'bound' < *nad-to-, Go. nati, OHG nezzi, OE, OIc. net 'neV<*nad-i-.k If the root etymology given here is correct, Skt. nahya- "to tie together' must be explained differently. However, it is disputed which forms belong together. If Lat. nodus is cognate, it must reflect *noHd-o-, but Schrijver explains it from *nod-o-, and connects OIc. nist 'brooch, pin', OHG nestilo 'string1, which are easier to derive from a root *rak Even PGm. *nat-i- 'net' would be easier from

PIE *nod-i- than from *nHd-i-, morphologically. If PGm. *nati- belongs to nassa, we have a Germano-Italo-Celtic root *nad- 'to bind, plait', which may go back to

PIE *nHd-.

### nutlnor, -an
nutlnor, -an 'to be busy' [v. I] (Cato apud Fest.)

Derivatives: natinatio dicebatur negotiatio et natinatores ex eo seditiosa negotia gerentes (ibidem).

Pit. *gnati-.

<!-- pdf-page: 416; printed-page: 402; running-header: 402                                     natis -->

PIE *gnh r ti- 'birth, production'. A possible origin from *nauaiinor was rejected by WH, hesitantly retained by Leumann, and adopted by Flobert 1975? 94. Vine 1999c, inspired by these accounts, gives an alternative which seems preferable. He starts from a /i-stem

PIE *gnh₃-ti- 'knowledge' or *gnh r ti- 'production' (the latter one suggested by Michael Weiss) >

Pit. *gnati-, from which an adj. *gnatwo- 'energetic, busy' was derived (maybe still attested in the gloss natina 'discordia'). From this adj. the verb nattnan would then derive. For the basic root, *&nhr seems semantically more straightforward.

### natis
natis 'buttock' [f. i] (Pl>)

Derivatives: mostly pL nates, -htm.

Pit. *(g/s)nati~.

PIE *nHt-?

IE cognates: Gr. νώτον [a] 'back; wide surface'. The connection between natis and Gr. νώτον, two isolated words, is certainly possible from the semantic side, but remains formally uncertain. Schrijver reconstructs a root noun *n(e)h₃t- from which Latin would have derived an /-stem *nh₃t-i- and Greek an o-stem *ne/oh₃t-o-. Another possibility is a root *nh₂t- (Greek *noh₂t-o-) or *nh₂- (Latin *nh₂-ti- and Greek *noh₂-to-). But the etymology remains very uncertain.

Bibl.: WH II: 146, EM 431, IEW 770, Schrijver 1991: 169.

### navis
navis 'ship' [f /'] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: navicula 'small ship' (Afran.+), navicularius 'ship-owner' (Varro+), navalis 'of a ship, naval' (Pl.+), navigare 'to go by ship, sail' (Pl.+), navigmm 'vessel, boat" (Lucr>); naufragus 'shipwrecked' (Varro+), naufragium 'shipwreck' (Pac.+), naviger 'navigable' (Lucr,+).

Pit. *nau-, *«<5w-.

<!-- pdf-page: 417; printed-page: 403; running-header: ne                                        403 -->

PIE *neh₂-u- 'ship'.

Probably a PIE w-stem noun or adj. *neh₂-u- derived from 'to swim': 'the swimming, floating one'. In the oblique case forms, *neh₂uV- yielded *nawV-, whence the long vowel was imported into the nom.acc.sg. Finally, the stem was remade into an /-stem. Schrijver 1991 reconstructs a lengthened grade *neh₂-u-, but there is no need to,

### ne-
ne- 'not, un~' [pref ] (Foruminscr.+)

Derivatives: non 'not' (Naev>; PI, noenum, Lucil. noenu); nee, neque 'not, and not, not either' (Lex XII, Andr.+), negare 'to deny, refuse' (Naev.+), negitare 'to deny repeatedly1 (Pl.+).

Pit. *ne 'not', *ne-kʷe 'and not'.

It. cognates: O. nep, U. nep 'not' maybe < *ne-kʷe. The Sab, continuants of *ne and *ne are not easy to distinguish (see s.v. ne) .

PIE *ne l not\

IE cognates: Olr no, nach, Μ We. nac 'not' < *ne-kʷe, Skt. ηά *not\ naca 'and not', Lith. ney Latv. ney OCS ne 'not', Go, ni 'not* < *ne, nih '(strongly) not' < *ne-klve. Neque < *ne-kʷe 'and not', non <*pe-oinom 'not one'. In the latter form, apocope of -urn is irregular (maybe due to unstressed use of the word), and -δ- can only be explained from a contraction *ne-oino- > *noino- > non. The form nee has two functions: 1) apocopated form of neque 'and nof, 2) simple 'not' (esp. VOLat). They may be etymologically identical, but the second nee could also reflect *ne-ke (cf. the suffix *-Ae in hie); in that case, negare and neglegere can be directly explained from this nee. If they were formed from neque, they would postdate the apocope otneque > nee,

Bibl.: WH II: 152, 174f., EM 432f, 439, IEW 756-758, Leumann 1977: 67, Untermann 2000:494. → in-, ne, ηϊ



### -ne
-ne 'then? or, whether' [ptcle.] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: Is sometimes apocopated to -«: ain, satin^ viden, audin.

Pit. *-ne.

PIE *-ne.

IE cognates: Skt. ηά 'as, like', Av.yau-na 'namely', Gr. Thess. όνε, τόνε, τόνε, OLith. ne 'as', Lith. ne, negi, negu 'than' (after comp.), nei 'as\ Latv. ne 'than'; OCS, SCr neze 'than' < *ne-g(w)V May ultimately be the same word as

PIE *ne 'not'. The scepticism towards this view uttered in WH and EM is excessive.

Bibl.: WH II: 150, EM 434, IEW 319-231, Beekes 1995: 222.



### ne
ne 'not, that not' [ptcle., cj.] (Duenos inscr., Lex XII+)

Derivatives: neu, neve 'nor, and not' (Pl.+).

Pit. *ne.

It. cognates: O. wi, Marr. niy Hern, ni, O. neip, nip, nep, U. ne, neip 'not' < *ney *ne«kʷe; Marr. nipis [nom.sg.], O. ne.phim [acc.sg.] 'nobody' < */?e *Aww·

<!-- pdf-page: 418; printed-page: 404; running-header: 404                                     nebula -->

PIE *ne.

IE cognates: Olr. no, W. neu, OBreL nou Or* < *ne-ue\ Skt. nova 'or not', Av. nauua. Lat neu, neve < *ne-we. The apocope of final -e led to *neu > neu. Long e can be explained in two ways: either it is the PIE stressed reflex of *ne 'not', or it was remade from

PIE *meh₁ '(that) not' by replacement of the m- by «-. Since

Pit. *ne-we can be compared with Celtic *ne-ue <

PIE *newe, it appears that Latin has introduced the variant *ne into original *ne-we. This, in turn, suggests that *we is a stressed variant of *ne, rather than a continuation of *mehL.

Bibl.: WH II: 150, 165, EM 432f, IEW 756-758, Schrijver 1991: 121f„ van der Staaij 1995: 38, Untermann 2000: 494L, 498,504. -» ne-, ηϊ



### nebula
nebula 'mist, fog' [f. a\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nebulosus 'foggy' (Cato+), nebula 'worthless person, scoundrel' (Ter.+).

Pit. *nefela-.

PIE *nebʰ-e-lo- 'small cloud'.

IE cognates: MW nyfel 'clouds', Gr. νεφέλη 'cloud', OIc. nifi 'darkness, haze', njol 'night\ OHG nebul, OS nebal 'darkness, haze'.

Bibl.: WH II: 151, EM 434, IEW 315, Meiser 1998: 103, LIV l.*nebʰ-. → imher, nimbus



### neeto, -ere cto weave, twine together'
neeto, -ere cto weave, twine together' [v. Ill; pf. nex(u)i, ppp. nexum] (Lex XII, Pl.+)

Derivatives: nexilis 'plaited, intertwined' (Varro+), nexus, -us 'binding, bond' (Lucr.+), nexere (also -are) 'to bind, plait' (Andr.+); adnectere 'to tie on, attach' (Varro+), conectere 'to join, relate' (Pl.+X conexus, -us 'connection' (Lucr,+), innectere 'to fasten* (Varro+), obnectere 'to entwine' (Acc.+). WH follow the etymology first proposed by Osthoff in 1890, viz, that nectere is a remake of earlier *nedere under influence of pectere. The root would be that of nodus 'knot', and may be connected with Skt. nahyanti 'to bind together', sam-naddha- 'bound together' (RV+), upanah- [f] 'shoe, sandal', YAv. naska- 'collection of texts', ηαδα- 'headgear' < Ilr. *Hnadʰ-, This remains a remote possibility since there is no evidence for *-<fh)- in Latin. An alternatively etymology would be to connect necto with the root *h₂nic- 'to reach' (see s.v. nanclscor), for which LIV reconstructs a root aorist *h₂nek-,

Bibl.: WH II: 155L, EM 435, IEW 758f -*flecto,plecto



### nefrones, nefrundinSs
nefrones, nefrundinSs 'kidneys, testicles' [m.pl. n(d)] (Paul. exF.)

Derivatives: nefrundines, nebrundines (Lanuvihl), nefrones (in Praeneste); Paul, ex F. nefrendes is probably due to confusion with nefrens 'toothless'.

Pit. *ne%*ron-.

PIE *negʷʰ-ro- 'kidney'.

IE cognates: Gr. νεφροί 'kidneys', OHG nioro, OIc. nyra, OSwe. niure 'kidney' < PGm. *neuron-. The Latin evidence points to o-vocalism: *nefron(d)-. This can be connected with a

PIE stem *negʷʰ-ro- 'kidney', extended by an n-stem (as in Gm.), to which Latin then added a sufTix *-d(h)-. Judging by febris 'fever' < *dʰegʷʰ-ri-, the cluster *-gʷV >

Pit. * - / V developed into PLat. *→-. The Latin result -fr- (instead of *-br-) is attributed

<!-- pdf-page: 419; printed-page: 405; running-header: nepos                                       405 -->


by Leumann to reanalysis: ne- was regarded as the negation ne- 'not', and *fr- was treated as if in anlaut, where the development to/-- is regular.

Bibl.: WH II: 156, EM 436, IEW 319, Leumann 1977: 166.



### nem-
nem- 'of course, certainly' [ptcle.] (Naev.+): nempe Of course, to be sure' (Pl.+), nemut 'unless' (Naev.+)

IE cognates: see s.v. nam. For the etymology of the stem, see s.v. nam. The ending -em in nem- is probably the same as in -dem, viz. *-im; see s.v. -dim.

Bibl.: WH II: 158, EM 437, IEW 320, Beekes 1995: 202. → nam



### nemus, -oris
nemus, -oris 'wood, forest' [n. r] (Enn.+)

Pit. *nem-os-.

PIE *nem-o/es- [n.] 'what is distributed, sacrifice'.

IE cognates: Olr. nemed Sanctuary, holy place', MBret. neved Esacellum', W. Bret nant 'vallis\ Skt. namas- [n.] 'worship, honour', Av. n3mah- 'homage; interest on a loan', Gr. νέμος 'forest', GaL νεμητον 'holy place', Alb. neme 'curse, imprecation', ORu. (Novg.) namt 'interest*. The meaning 'forest, (holy) clearance' is shared by Greek, Celtic and Italic. It originates from 'sacrificed > 'the place of the sacrifice'. In Ilr., the s-stem means 'worship'. LIV assumes two different roots, *nem-l 'to distribute' and *nem-2 'to bend', but the meanings are distributed complementarity across the IE languages: Ilr. and Toch. have 'to bend\ the European languages 'to distribute' or 'to take'. Since the j-stem is attested in all languages and presupposes the verbal meaning distribute', there can be little doubt that PIE had only one root *nem-.

Bibl.: WH II: 158f, EM 437, IEW 764, Benveniste 1931: 79ff, EWAia II: 16, Sylvain Patri 2001, Stuber 2002: 132f, LIV L*nenK → numerus



### neo
neo 'to spin' [v. II; pf nevi% ppp. netum] (Pl.+)

Pit. *(s)ne(je)-.

PIE *(s)nebr/*(s)nhr [pr«] 'to spin',

IE cognates: Mir. sniid* -sni 'spins, restores', W. nyddu, MCo. nedha, MBret. nezaff'to spin' < PCI *smje/o-; Gr. 3s. vf}, 3p. νώσι, ipf έννη (Aeol.), inf. vfjv 'to spin', beside νήύω; Latv, snqju, snat 'to wind together loosely, braid'; OHG naen 'to sew'. It is impossible to say whether Latin continues the PIE root present or a (Italo-Celtic?) derivative in *-ie/o-.

Bibl.: WH II: I59f, EM 437, IEW 973, Schrijver 1991: 403, Schumacher 2004: 598f, LIV *sneh r . → natrix, nervus



### nepos, -otis
nepos, -otis 'grandson, -daughter; descendant; playboy' [m, (f.) /] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nepotulus 'grandson' (Pl.), neptis, -is [f] 'granddaughter, female descendant' (Afran.+).

Pit. *nepot- [m.], *neptT- [f.].

PIE *h₂nep-6t- 'male descendant other than son, grandson, nephew5, *h₂nep-t-ihi/2

<!-- pdf-page: 420; printed-page: 406; running-header: 406                                    Neptunus -->


'female descendant', *h₂nep-t-iHo- [adj.] 'of the descendant'.

IE cognates: (1) m.: Olr, ηία, gen. niad Cousin', W. nei, nai 'cousin', Skt napat [nom.sg,], napatam [acc.sg.], naptuhi [gen.abl.sg.] 'descendpnt, grandson', OAv. ηαβιι [loc.pl.m.] (< Pllr. *napt-$u\ YAv, napa9 napasB (t-) [nom.sg.], napatsm [acc.sg.], naptar9m [acc.sg.] nafsdro [gen.sg.] (< *naptras), OP παρά 'grandson' < Ilr. *napat-\ Alb. nip 'grandson, nephew'; OLith, nepuotis 'grandson, granddaughter', neptis 'grandson' [m.], OE nefa, OHG nevo 'nephew' (2) L· Olr. necht 'niece'; Skt naptt- daughter, granddaughter', YAv. napti [f.] 'granddaughter' < Ilr, *(H)napt-iH-, Alb. mbese 'niece\ OLith. nepte 'granddaughter', RuCS nestera 'niece', OHG nifi(a) 'niece'; (3) adj.: Skt. naptriya- 'belonging to the grandson', OAv. naptiia- 'belonging to the offspring?1, YAv. nauua.naptiia- [n.] 'the kinsmen of the nine degrees of kinship of a family'; Gr. ανεψιός 'cousin, son of sister', CS ORu, netii 'nephew', SCr. necak 'sister's son', netjak 'sister's son'. The meaning 'cousin' does not appear in Latin before 150 AD, and is therefore secondary (Beekes 1976). The f neptis was not remade into an -jfc-stem or an ia-stem like other f nouns. The nom.sg. is reconstructed as asigmatic *nep-ot by Beekes 1995: 178, The only indication for the initial laryngeal is Gr. ανεψιός.

Bibl.: WH II: 161, EM 437f, IEW 764, Schrijver 1991: 21, 150-153, 363-366, Meiser 1998: 142.



### Neptunus
Neptunus 'Neptune; the sea' [m. o] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: Neptunalia, -drum [n.pl.] 'the festival of Neptune' (Varro+).

Pit. *neptiino-?

PIE *nebʰ-tu- 'moisture'?

IE cognates: YAv. napta- [adj.] 'moist', aifiunapiim [absol.] 'moistening'. Rix 1981: 123 (= 2001: 291) supports the derivation from an abstract *nebʰ-tu- 'moisturing' with a suffix *-h₃n- for indicating 'rulers': hence 'Lord of moisturing' > of irrigation, of wells, of rivers. Yet the existence of this suffix is not established» Alternatively, one might posit a de-instrumental derivative *nebʰtuhrn- 'who is with moisture' in the vein of Nussbaum 1996. The Etruscan form Neuuns would be borrowed from Umbrian because of the dental fricative, which might be rendering a hypothetical U. cognate *Nehtuns.

Bibl.: WH II: 162f., EM 438, IEW 315f., LIV l.*nebV — nimbus



### Ner»
Ner» 'Nero' [m. n\ (Liv.+)

Derivatives: Nerid / Neria / Nerienis 'Roman goddess, wife of Mars' (Pl.+), neridsus 'strong, resistant1 (gloss.); Suet, nero 'lingua Sabina fortis ac strenuus\ Lydus (de mensibus): νερίκη - ανδρία, νέρωνας - ανδρείους... οί Σαβίνοι; Suet, nerio 'Sabinum verbum est eoque significatur virtus et fortitudo'.

Pit. *ner, *ner- *man\ *ner-o- / -δη- 'strong'.

It. cognates: O. niir, SPic. nir [nom.sg.], O. ner(eis) [gen.sg.]> ner. [acc.sg. or gen.pl.?], nerum [gen.pL], U. nerf> SPic. nerf [acc.pl.], U. nerus [datpl.] 'man'.

PIE *h₂ner [nonhsg.], *h₂ner-, *h₂nr- 'man'.

IE cognates: W. ner 'hero', CLuw. annara/i- * virile' > Skt. nar- 'man, hero' (naram [acc.sg.], naras [nom.ph], nfn

<!-- pdf-page: 421; printed-page: 407; running-header: nex                                       407 -->

[acc.pl.]), Αν. nor- (OAv. na [nom.sg.], nar§m [acc.sg.], r&rss [gen.sg.]), Gr. άνήρ, NPhryg. αναρ, Arm. ayr. If derived from

PIE *h₂ner- 'man', the PN Nerd originally meant 'the manly, strong one'. Sab, Inerf retains the long vowel in the nom.sg. A cognomen Nero is reported for the gens Claudia from Varro onwards. Since it is mainly restricted to onomastics, Nerd could be a Sabellic loanword.

Bibl.: WH II: 164, EM 438f, IEW 765, Schrijver 1991: 213 Untermann 2000: 495-497, Kloekhorst 2008: 386f.



### nervus 'sinew, muscle, nerve'
nervus 'sinew, muscle, nerve' [m. o] (Lex ΧΠ+)

Derivatives: nervine [f.pl.] 'strings of a musical instrument' (Varro+), nervia, -drum [n.pl.] 'sinews or tendons' (Varro+), Nervolaria title of a lost play ascribed to Plautus (Varro+).

Pit. *(s)neuro-.

PIE *snehrur [nom.acc], *snhruen- [obi.] [n.] 'sinew'.

IE cognates: Skt. snavan [n.] 'sinew', YAv. snauuars.bazura- [adj.] 'having arms like sinews', Gr. νεΰρον [nj, νευρά [f.] 'string, sinew1; Arm. neard 'sinew'; ToB snaura [pi.] 'sinews'. Hit ishunauuar is a ghost word, see Kloekhorst 2008: 396. Nervus probably stems from *neuros by metathesis. A similar development can be seen in parvus < *pauro-.

Pit. *(s)neuro- is a thematization of

PIE *(s)nehjur.

Bibl.: WH II: 165, EM 439,JEW 977, Schrijver 1991: 269, LIV *sneh,-. -* natrix, neo ; $



### O. nessimas
O. nessimas [nom.pl.f], nessimass [acc.pl.f], nesimum [gen.pl.n_], nesimois [abl.pl.n.], U. nesimei [loc.sgji.] 'next\

Pit. *ness- 'next'

PIE *nesd-is- nearer'?

IE cognates: Olr, nessa [comp.], OW nes9 MCo. nes, MBret. nes 'nearer', Bret, nes 'near' < PCI. *nessa- ( « *nedda-, Jasanoff 1988-90: 185); Gaul. neDDamon [gen.pl.], Olr. nessam 'nearest, next', W. nessaf, OCo. nesheuin, Co. nessa, Bret, nessqff 'nearer' < PCI. *nessamo- (< *nezd-isamo-? Jasanoff 1988-90: 172); Skt. nadiyas- 'closer, very close', nedistha- 'closest, next', Av. asna- [adj.] 'close' (< *nzd-no-\ YAv. nazdiio [adv.] 'closer', nazdista- 'closest, next', OP asna- [adj.] 'close'. Since the Ilr. forms in *n(e)zd- are the closest in form and meaning, it seems preferable to derive nessimo- from the same root. Cowgill 1970: 131, 138 reconstructs

Pit. *ness-isamo→ with haplology > *nessamo-. Yet this does not explain suffixal -i- in Oscan, which should continue a long vowel. Van der Staaij therefore reconstructs *-io-/wo- or *-iH-mo-, but refrainsfroma further explanation.

Bibl.: IEW 758f„ Meiser 1986: 242, van der Staaij 1995: 106, Untermann 2000: 493.



### nex, necis
nex, necis 'violent death, murder' [f. k] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: necare 'to kilP (Pl.+), denicalis 'reserved for the purification of the family of a deceased person' (Ctc.+), eni/ecare (ppp. enectus) 'to kill' (Pl.+X

### internecare
internecare 'to exterminate' (Pl.+), internecid 'extermination' (Lucil.+), pernicies 'destruction, ruin' (Pl.+)-

Pit. *nek-.

It. cognates: maybe U. viinctu [3s,ipvJI] '?' (prayer for hurting the opponent).

PIE *nelt-s [fj 'death'.

IE cognates: Skt. nasyati [3s.act.] 'to perish, disappear', aor. nesat [3s.inj.act], pf. nanasa [3s.act], ppp. nasta-, OAv. anqsat [3s.aor.act], ngsat [3s.aoninj.act] 'to disappear', Av. nasiia- 'to stray from', nasta- 'having missed', Av. nas- 'distress, misfortune', nasu- 'corpse', Gr. νεκρός [m,] 'body, dead person', νέκυς 'corpse'; Toch. nak- 'to ruin'. The meaning οΐηεχ presupposes a semantic development from 'disappear' to 'die' at an earlier stage. I regard nequalia 'detrimenta' (Fest) as a likely derivative of qualis. This removes the Latin evidence for a PIE stem *neic-u-. The ppp. e-nectus could imply that necare arose in compound verbs, esp. e-necare, and substitutes an earlier verb *necere. But necare could also be denominal in origin. For pernicies, see permities.

Bibl.: WH I: 339, II: 153-155, EM 439f., IEW 762, Sihler 1995: 532, Untermann 2000: 497f., LIV *nek-, → noceojpermities



### nī
ηϊ 'not, that not, that, unless' [ptcle.; cj.] (VOLat+: Duenos inscn, Garigliano Bowl, SCBac. net)

Derivatives: nfve, neive 'if not' (Pl.+), Htff'unless, but that, except7 (Naev.-l·)»

Pit. *nei.

It. cognates: O. nei 'except' < *ne + f; O. neip, neip, nei, U. neip, neip 'not' maybe < *ne-T-kʷid 'nothing'.

PIE *nei.

IE cognates: OAv. nae- 'not' in Av. nae-cis 'none', OP nary, nai- 'not', Lith. rtef'nor, than', OCS ni, Ru. ni eand not, nor', Go. nei 'not', OHG ηϊ. A combination of ne 'not' and a deictic particle *-/.

Bibl.: WH II: 166, EM 433, IEW 756-758, Beekes 1995: 222, Untermann 2000:489.



### nidor
nidor 'strong smell, fumes' [m. r] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: mddricupius 'who loves the smell of cooking' (Plrl·).

Pit. *kneifid-os-.

PIE *knei(H)d-os- 'itch'?

IE cognates: Gr. Att κνϊσα, Horn κνϊση 'smell, fume', Gr. κνίδη 'nettle' < *knid-, κνίζω 'scratch, irritate (skin)' < *knid-ie-, Latv. kniest 'to itch', Lith. kniedenti 'to clinch', OIc, hniss (< *knid-toycooking odour', OE hmtan 'to sting', OIc. hnita 'strong odour', OIc. knita 'wound*. Under the assumption that Gr. κνΐσ- continues *krifrks-1 and that a 'strong smell' is derived from a verb 'to itch, sting', nidor could belong here. The vowels are difficult to reconcile: Lat. and Gm. can have *iH9 *eiH or *e/, Baltic can have *ei(H), but Greek has *i// in *kmd- and *knid- in κνίζω,

Bibl.: WH II: 166, EM 441, IEW 562f., Meiser 1998: 112, LIV *kneid-, → lens

### niger
niger 'black, dark' [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

### nimbus
nimbus 'rain-cloud, shower' [m. o] (Pac.+)

Derivatives: nimbatus 'stormy' or 'clouded, shrouded in mists' (of a girl) (Pl.).

Pit. *nefos-?

PIE *nebʰ-os [n.], gen. -es-os 'cloud'.

IE cognates: Hit. nepis [n. > c j , CLuw. tappas- [nj, HLuw. tipas-*[n.] 'sky, heaven', S\<A.)nabhah [n.] 'cloud, sky\ Av. nabah- 'sky\ Gr. νέφος [n.] 'cloud', OCS nebo, Lith. debesis 'sky'; MP namb, MoP namb 'dew, haze', nem 'moist'? i Nimbus might be connected to nebula 'cloud' and imber 'shower of rain' < *n(e)bh-. One might explain the -m- in nimbus by assuming that nimbus continues *nebʰ-os- but was reshaped under the influence of imber, which has a similar meaning to nimbus. This is possible, but gratuitous.

### nimis
nimis 'too much' [adv.] (Andr.+)

Derivatives:\nimiumis [adj.] 'excessive, too much' (Naev.+).

Pit. *«e *meijis (or *ne *mis) 'not too little, quite a lot'.

PIE *m(e)ihris- 'less'.

IE cognates: see s.v. minor, Nimius must be a recent adjectivization of nimis. In the account of Meiser 1998, nimis < * nimis (iambic shortening) continues *ne meis 'not too little', with adverbial *meiH-is of the adj. *meihr 'little, few* (cf, magis, satis). Since e.g. magis 'more' shows the zero grade of the root, one may also reconstruct *miH-is.

snig* 'to snow* < *snigʷe/o- or *snigie/o-, Oh, snechtae, MW nyf[m.] 'snow\ Skt. aor. asnihat [3s.act.], caus. snehayat (RV) [3s.act.] 'to lie down; lay down', YAv. snaezana- [adj.] 'slavering', snaeza- '*o snow' (Ilr. *snaif-ja-)9 Sogd snys^ Gr. νείφει 'it is snowing', νίφα [acc.sg.], Lith. sninga 'it snows', jsnaigos 'snow', Latv. sniegs, OCS snigb, Go. snaiws, OHG sneo 'snow\ Lat. nix < nom. *sniks, gen, nivis < gen. *sni^os. ### mtela
mtela 'kind of rodent' [f a) (Plin.+)

Derivatives: mtedula 'kind of dormouse' (Cic.+). A connection with nitor 'to lean, exert' is unlikely for semantic reasons. Nitor does not mean 'to climb', as WH argue in order to support this etymology. It is formally more attractive to look for a base *mte-, but I see no good candidate. Maybe mtela can be connected with nidor 'smell' and its IE cognates meaning 'to scratch'. This makes non-IE origin possible.

Bibl.: WH II: 170, EM 442.



### niteo
niteo 'to be radiant, shine' [v. II; pf. nitui\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nitor [m.] 'brightness, elegance' (Pl.+), nitidus 'bright, elegant' (Pl.+), nitidare 'to make bright' (Enn.+), nitidiusculus 'a little bit more shiny' (Pl.), nitiditds 'elegance' (Ace), nitescere 'to become bright' (Enn.+); enitere 'to shine forth' (Acc.+). The Latin words are easiest understood as building on a ppp. *nitos 'made bright, shining', but there is no known IE verbal root which fits the form and meaning. If remdeo is connected, the root would be *neit-9 *noit-y *nit-. The appurtenance of Skt. ritlch 'dark-coloured' < *nei(H)-? is very uncertain.

Bibl.: WH II: 171, EM 442, IEW 760. → renideo



### nitor, -ī
mtor, -ϊ 'to lean on, support oneself; strive, strain' [v. Ill; ppp, nixus, msvs\ Paul, ex F. gnitor, gnixum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nisus, -us 'advance, pressure' (Pac.+), nixari 'to support oneself (Lucr+), nixus, -us 'straining, travail' (Lucr.+); admit 'to exert oneself, lean' (Pl.+), coniti 'to strain, strive' (Pl.+), eniti 'to struggle out, give birth' (Pl.+), emxim 'strenuously' (Sis.), obmfi 'to thrust, make a stand' (Enn.+), obriixe 'strenuously' (Pl.+), subriixus 'propped up, assisted' (Pl.+).

Pit. *hxif-et- [pn], *kniXw-s-.

IE cognates: see s.v. cdnived. The original meaning could be Mean on' but also 'exert pressure'. Since the original meaning is unclear, the connection with Skt. yat- 'to be/put in place, line up' (thus LIV) remains gratuitous. Even for clean on', it would not be compelling. Furthermore, note that *w does not occur as a preverb in Latin, Lat riixus (possibly for *nictus, cf Leumann 1977; 615) is difficult to explain secondarily, and points to a velar-final

<!-- pdf-page: 425; printed-page: 411; running-header: noceo                                     411 -->

root. Thus, the connection with conrveo 'to be tightly closed' put forward by WH and IEW still seems preferable; in that case, gn- in Paul, ex F. could be etymologically justified. Nitor is explained as a back-formation to nisus by Leumann 1977: 188, but there is no good model for such replacement. Phonetically, nJtor could reflect *(g)nfwet-e/o- < *kn(e)igwh-e-t-; but the origin of -f- remains unclear.

Bibl.: WH II: 171, EM 442, IEW 608, Sihler 1995: 163, 208, LIV *jet-. → conned



### no, nare
no, nare 'to swim' [v. I; pf. ηάνΐ\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: natare 'to swim, float' (Pl.+), natator 'swimmer' (Varro+); enare 'to swim out, forth' (Pl.+), transnare 'to swim, sail across' (Enn.+).

Pit. *(s)nqje/o- 'to swim', *(s)nato- 'bathed'.

It. cognates: ? U, snata, snatu [acc.pLn.], snates, snate [abl.pl.n.] "cleansed", asnata, asnatu [acc.pl.n.], asnates, asnate [abl.pl.n.] 'uncleansed5?

PIE pr. *(s)neh₂-(ie/o~) 'to swim, float', ppp. *(s)nh₂-to-.

IE cognates: Olr. sndid, •sna* 'to swim' < PCI. *sna(-ie/o)-, Skt pr. snatas [3d.act.], snatf- [ptc.act.f], caus. snapaya-, ta-ptc. snata- 'to bathe, wash', YAv.ns„.snaiia- 'to wash out', fra-snaiia- 'to wash clean', fra-snata- 'washed', usnaiti- [f.] 'washing off, Gr. νήχω, Dor. ναχω 'to swim', Gr. νέω, ipf. έννεον, aor. νευσαι 'to swim' < *sna-; ToB nask- 'to bathe'. Natare was built to a ppp. *natos. Whether its preform was *nh₂to- or *snh₂-to- is hard to say (cf. Schrijver 19ftl: 171). The PIE present ,was probably a root present, to which Italic added *-je/o-. The meaning and therefore the appurtenance of the U. forms is uncertain; they refer to vessels used in connection with sacrifices, maybe with ointments.

Bibl.: WH II: 146f, 172, EM 442f, IEW 971f., Schrijver 1991: 168f, 403, Sihler 1995: 529, Meiser 1998: 112, Untermann 2000: 687f, Schumacher 2004: 596, LIV *(s)neh₂-- → navis



### noceo
noceo 'to hurt, damage' [v. Π; pf nocui, ppp. nocitum; s-sb. noxit (Lex ΧΠ, Lucil.)] (LexXILl·) ,

Derivatives: Unnocens 'not guilty, virtuous' (Naev.+), innocentia 'innocence' (Caecil.+); rioxa 'injurious behaviour, punishment, harm' (Andr.+), noxia 'wrongdoing, damage' (Lex ΧΠ+), noxius 'guilty, harmful' (Pl.+), innoxius 'innocent, harmless5, noxitudo 'wrongdoing' (Ace), obnoxius 'indebted, liable, submissive' (Pl.+), obnoxidsus 'subject, subordinate' (Pl.+).

Pit. *nokeje- 'to cause death₁.

PIE *nok-eie- 'to make disappear, cause death₁.

IE cognates: Skt. nasaya- 'to make disappear, destroy', OP vinadayatiy [3s.pr.act.] 'to damage'. The 5-sb. noxit and the noun noxa betray an earlier sfe/oj-present, but it must have been formed secondarily to noced, since it has the meaning and the root vowel of that verb. Lat. obnoxius is generally explained as deriving from the syntagm ob noxiam esse 'to be involved in misdemeanour'. Noxius could be a backformation to obnoxius (Leumann 1977: 290), and noxia can be the f. to noxius. All of these can be derived from noxa.

Bibl.: WH II: 153-155, EM 440,455, IEW 762, LIV *nek-. → nex

nodus 'knot, node' [m. o] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: nddare 'to furnish with joints, tie in a knot' (Cato+); enoddre 'to prune surplus nodes from, unraveP (Cato+), inenodabilis 'that cannot be disentangled* (Acc.+), internodium 'intemode' (Varro+).

PIE *nodo- / *noHdo- / *neh₃do- *knot\

IE cognates: OIc. not 'net'; see s.v. nassa. The root etymology is uncertain; apart from the possible PIE preforms, an analysis as a loanword is also possible. See s.v. nassa.

### liftmen
liftmen 'name' [η. ή\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ndminare 'to name, designate* (Pl.+), ndminitare 'to calf (Lucr.+), nominalis 'of nouns' (Varro), nominatim 'by name, specifically' (Pl.+), n6minatusy -Us 'noun' (Varro), nominaffvus 'nominative* (Varro+); cognomen 'surname, sobriquet' (Pl.+), cognomentum 'surname, cognomen' (Pl.+), cognominis [adj.] 'having the same name' (Pl.+), cogndminare *to give a (sur)name' (Vanxtf-), ignominia 'disqualification, disgrace' (Lucil.+), praenomen 'personal name' (Varro+), praenominare 'to name with the prenomen* (Varro), pronomen 'pronoun' (Varro+); nuncupare 'to declare, pronounce, appoint' (Lex XII+).

Pit. *nom-n-.

It. cognates: O, nuraneis [gen.sg.], num(nud) [abl.sg.], U, numem, nome [nom.acasg.], nomner [gen.sg.], nomne [dat.sg.], nomne [abl.sg.], nomneper [+ -per] 'name' [n.].

PIE *h₃nehrmn, *h₃nli3-men- [n.] 'name*.

IE cognates: Olr. ainm (pi. anmann) , OW anu 'name*, Hit. laman- /lamn- [n,], HLuw. alaman- [nj, Lye. alaman- 'name', Skt. naman-, Av. naman- [n.], nama, noma [adv.] 'named, by name', Gr. Ονομα 'name*, νώνυμ(ν)ος 'without a name*, Phryg. onomany Arm. anown, Alb. emer (< *emen); ToAflow,ToB nemy OPr. emmens, OCS im$ [n.] < BSL *in?men-, Go. namoy OE namay OIc. nafn. The g in agnomen and cognomen was introduced on the model of a-, cd-gnoscd 'to know, recognize1. The verb nuncupare must be denominal to a noun *nomiceps 'taking a name, declaring' > *nomicupare > *nomcupare > *nomcupare > nuncupare. The reconstruction of the root is disputed. Originally,

PIE *(H)nom- was posited, thus still by Sihler. Yet this does not explain Latin and Germanic unless by an unmotivated o-grade. Thus, the root structure was *HnH-, Many scholars now seem to agree on the identity of the second laryngeal as *h₃, and this had led to an identification with the verbal root attested in Hit. hanna- I hann- 'to judge, sue' (< *h₃ne-h₃noh₃-) and Gr. δνομαι 'to blame, repudiate*, LIV reconstructs initial *h r , which requires that Gr. o- be secondary (assimilation to the second o?). Beekes reconstructs initial *h₃- for όνομα and Arm. anown, which seems compelling: Aim. can have *h₂ or *h₃, but Greek only *h₃. Others have reconstructed initial * h r on the strength of Gr. personal names Ένυμα-κρατίδας and Ένυμαντιάδας attested in Doric dialects, which would contain *enoma- 'name'. But these (partially restored) readings cannot be proven to contain the word for 'name*, and thus do not outweight the evidence of όνομα and Arm. anown,

24, Beekes 1987a, Sihler 1995: 85, 97, Stuber 1997, Untermann 2000: 500f, LIV *h₂neh₃-.



### nonnus
nonnus 'monk, nun' [m. f. o, a] (Ecclesiastical Lat.)

PIE *nVnV.

IE cognates: W, nain 'grandmother', Skt. nana, MoP nana 'mother', Gr. νάννας [m./f.] 'uncle, aunt1, νέννος 'uncle', Alb. nene 'mother', Ru. njanja 'child maid' (dial, also 'older sister'), Bulg. neni 'the elder'. Nursery word meaning 'daddy, nanny*, reduplication of dental nasal plus (mostly) /a/.

Bibl.: WH II: 175, EM 444, IEW 754. → onus, mamma



### nos
nos 'we1 [pron. pers. lp.] (Naev.+; nom.acc. nos, databl. nobis; gen. nostrum, nostn; nostrorum [m.], nostrarum [f.])

Derivatives: noster 'our' (Andr+), nostras, -tis 'native, of our country' (Cato+), nostratim 'in our manner' (Varro).

Pit. *nos 'we, us', *nofei 'us1, *nos-tero- 'our'.

PIE ace. *w>s, gen. *no/es, adj.poss. *ns-tero-*i

IE cognates: Olr. sni 'we' (< *snes? *snoi?), -nn 'us' < *(s)nos*i, nathar 'ours' < *nostrom, ar n~ < *nstrom; Hit. anz- 'us* [ace. an-za-as (OS), an-za-a-as, gen.sg. an-ze-el (OS)], Luw. anza 'we, us', HLuw. anz- 'we, us' <

PIE *ns-V, enclitic Hit. =nnas '(to) us, our', HLuw. =nz 'us' < *-nos; Skt. nas [ace], nas [gen.dat] 'us\ OAv. na [act.] < *nas, na [gen.dat.] < *nas, YAv. no [acc.gen.dat.] < *nas_; Av. ahma 'us' < *ns-me; Gr. nom. ήμεΐς, ace. ήμας, Ion. ήμέας, Dor. nom. αμές; ace. αμέ, Aeol. nom. αμμες, ace. άμμε < PGr. *άσμε <

PIE *ns-me; Alb. na < *nos; OCS nom.pl. my, ny, gen.pl. nast, Go. uns < *ps-, ToA nas. The original nom. *uei(s) was replaced by the stressed ace. form *nos, apparently already in Pit. The gen. nostrum is originally the gen.pl. of noster, after Plautus it was replaced by nostrt The opposition between stressed *nos and enclitic *nos may date backtoPIR

Bibl.: WH II: 175£, EM 444f., IEW 758, Leumann 1977: 463f., Schrijver 1991: 122, Sihler 1995: 381, Beekes 1995: 208f., Meiser 1998: 158.

## I



### noscu, -ere
noscu, -ere 'to get to know' [v. Ill; pf, ηόνϊ, ppp. notum] (VOLat.+; SCBac, gnoscier, Pl., Caecil. also gn-)

Derivatives: ignotus 'ignorant; unknown' (Naev.+); noscitare 'to investigate, recognize' (Pl.+); ηδύδ 'acquaintance, examination' (Pl.+), notitia I notifies 'acquaintance, knowledge' (Ter+; -ies Lucr.+), (g)nobilis 'familiar, famous, noble' (Andr.H-), nobilitas 'renown, nobility' (Pl.+), nobilitare Ho make noted' (Ter.+), ignobilis 'unknown; of low birth' (Andr.+), ignobilitas 'humble origin; obscurity' (Lucil.+); agnoscere 'to recognize, acknowledge' (Pl.+), cognoscere 'to get to know' (Pl.+), cogndbilis 'understandable5 (Cato+), cognitus 'known (from experience)' (Pac.+), incognitas 'unheard, uninvestigated' (Cic.+), Tgnoscere 'to forgive' (Pl.+), interndscere 'to know apart' (Pl.+), pernoscere 'to get to know well' (Pl.+).

Pit. *gnaske-1 *gn6ske-.

PIE pr. *gnh₃-ske-, aor, *gneh₃-/gnh₃- 'to know', pf. *ge-gnoh₃/gnh₃-; ppp.

<!-- pdf-page: 428; printed-page: 414; running-header: 414                                      nota -->


*gnh₃-to-.

IE cognates: Oln ad-gnin* 'to recognize' < *ati-gni-na-, W. atnabot (3s. Qtwaen) , Co. aswonvos 'to know' (3s. aswori) < *ati-wo-gn-\ Hit. kane/iss-*1 'to recognize, acknowledge' < *gnh₃-s-;iSkt. pr.janati, x>fjajmvarns-janu$- [ptcj, aor. ajnayi 'to know, recognize', ppp. ό-jnata- 'unknown', jnata- 'known, recognized'; YAv. paitlzana- 'to acknowledge, comprehend', znatar- [m.] 'knower\ znoista- 'who knows the best'; OP dan- [ipf.], xsnasa- [pr.sb.] 'to learn, know', Khot paysan- 4 to recognize'; Gr. γιγνώσκω 'id.', aon έγνων, ρρρ. -γνωτός 'known', often γνωστός; γνωμών 'knower', γνώσις 'inquiry, knowledge'; Arm. canac'em < *gnHrsk-i- (assimilated from * canac'em), Alb, njoh, 23s.pr. njeh < *gnV-sk-9 Lith, zinoti 'to know', OPr. posinnaU Latv. zinat 'to confess'; OCS znati9 Is. znqjg, Ru. znat' 'to know'; Go. hunnan, OHG hmnan 'to know, be able'; ToA aknats, ToB aknatsa 'foolish, stupid; fool' < PTo. * aknatsa- < *#-gneh₃-to~. Originally, gndscere meant 'to get to know', and novT 'to know'. The adj. gnobilis is explained by Leumann 1977 as a backformation to ignobilis, a negated derivative of *gno-bulum 'landmark, recognition sign'. The verb ignoscere contains the preverb *en- 'in'. By regular sound change, the pr. *gnh₃-ske- and the adj. *gnh₃-to- would have become *gnasco, *gnatus, which would have been homonymous with nosed 'to be born', natus 'born'. This may have been the reason why the full grade was introduced in 'to know': *gneh₃-ske- > gtiosco, *gneh₃-to- > gnotus. The full grade may stem from the aorist, or maybe from the perfect. For the form -gnitus in cognitus, see the discussion in Schrijver 1991: 199ff. This form can hardly be explained on the basis of *-gnh₃-to-. Schrijver concludes that it may reflect *-genotos < *-gnhret-9 for which he adduces two different morphological solutions. The first one, proposed by Beekes, assumes the suffix *-eto- which is often found in Greek; hence, *-gnhreto- 'which is to be known' > *-genoto-. The second one, proposed by Schrijver loccit, starts from a PIE i-stem *-gnehrt-3 found in compounds such as Gr. αγνώς, άριγνώς, άλλογνώς, the accsg. of which would be *-gnhret-m. By means of thematization, this stem would have yielded an adj. *-gnhret-o-.

Bibl.: WH I: 677, II: 176f, EM 445ft, IEW 376ff., Leumann 1977: 349, Schrijver 1991: 147, 184,199fif, Meiser 1998: 78f., Schumacher 2004: 347-349, LIV *gneh₃~. → gnarus, ignore, nomen, nota



### nota
nota 'mark, sign' [ft a] (Lucil.+)

Derivatives: notare 'to mark, indicate' (Cato+). Schrijver 1991 has clearly shown that it is impossible to derive nota from either *gnh₃- 'to know' or from the root of Gr. aor. όνόσ(σ)ασΰαι 'to blame'. Schrijver hesitatingly proposes to derive nota from *snot-a 'mark' as a derivative of the root *snt- 'to notice' which is reflected in Lat. sentio. Semantically, this would work, but if Go. sandjan 'to send' is cognate, the Latin form would have schwebe-ablaut, which makes this solution less attractive.

Bibl.: WH II: 177f., EM 446, IEW 377, Schrijver 1991: 197ff„ LIV *sent-.



### novacula
novacula 'razor' [f. a] (Cic.+)

Pit. ^(h)nowatlo-.

<!-- pdf-page: 429; printed-page: 415; running-header: novem                                       415 -->

PIE *ks-n(e)-u- [pr.] 'to scrape'.

IE cognates: Skt. ova ksyaumi [pr.], ksrmvana- [ptcmed.] 'to whet, sharpen', °ksnut- [adj.] '-sharpened', ksnotra- [n.] 'whetstone'; k$wrfc 'razor, sharp knife'; OAv. xsnaus [3s.aor.act], xsrmruvsa [2s.pr./aor.opt.med.] 'to hear', YAv. hu-xsnuta- 'well-sharpened', OP a-xsnauvaiy/a-xsnumiy, Ί hear', a-xsnudiy 'hear!\ Instrument noun *sneuak!o- < *ksneuaklo- based on a verb stem *(ks)neua-, which is itself derived from a PIE stem *ksneu- 'to scrape (so as to make smooth), sharpen'. The retention of-ov- proves

PIE *-euV- rather than *-owfS which would have yielded *-tfvv- (at least, in pretonic position). Vine (2006: 214-216) posits an original deverbal adj. *ksneu-o- '(scraped) smooth' whence a factitive 'to make smooth' or denominative present *ksneu-eh₂-ie/o- would have been formed in Italic. In view of the meaning, Ilr. and Latin *fa-w-w- is likely to be an original nasal present to *kseu- 'to scrape, make smooth' (see s,v, saucius) rather than a ^/-extension to the stem *ks-en- 'to comb, card*. Both probably go back to a single PIE root *kes- 'to scratch, dig, order' and/or *kes- 'to cut off (Lat. castrare, cared). The latter two roots may in origin be identical.

Bibl.: WH II: 178, EM 446, IEW 585f, Schrijver 1991: 449, Meiser 1998: 112, LIV *ksneu-, *kseu-. → saucius

novem 'nine' [num. indecl.]«(Naev.+) ;

Derivatives: november, -bris\[adj.] 'the ninth month' (Cato+), novenarius 'ninefold' (Varro+), novem [pl.adj.] 'nine each, nine at a time* (Varro+), novie(n)s [adv.] 'nine times' (Varro+); norms 'ninth' (Cato+), Nonae [f.pL] 'the ninth day before the Ides' (Enn.+), nonalis 'of the Nones' (Varro), nonaginta 'ninety' (Varro+), ndnagesimus 'ninetieth' (Varro+), nonussis 'the sum of nine asses' (Varro+); rmndinae [f-pl-] 'market day (held every ninth day)' (Lex XII+), mindinalis 'of market-days' (Pl.+), internundinum (Lucil.+) 'period of nine days'.

Pit. *newy 'nine'.

PIE *hineun 'nine', ^neun-o- 'ninth'.

IE cognates: Olr. noi w-, W. Co. now, Skt ηάνα, Αν. nauua, Gr. εννέα, Arm. inn, Alb. nende, Lith. devyni, OCS dev$tb, Go. OHG niuny Toch. nu 'nine*. Nundino- goes.back to *noweno-dino- 'of the ninth day', with *di-n- 'day' (cf dies). Internundinum must be explained as an univerbation of inter nundinos 'between market-days' (Risch 1985: 333). The existence of a sg. nundinum 'the period from one market day to the next' (SCBac.+; nound- SCBac.) has been refuted by Risch 1985, who shows that all old attestations of trwurn nundinum (in laws, in Cicero) 'period comprising three market-days' must be understood as gen, of pi. nundinae. In norms 'ninth', *-owe- was restored after regular syncope of *noweno- > *nouno- had taken place; the contraction product of this later sequence *-owe- was o, not ϋ. Nonaginta is either based on *nonginta, or was formed secondarily to the ordinal norms.

Bibl.: WH II: 179f., 188, EM 446f, IEW 318f, Schrijver 1991: 17, 275, 278-281, Meiser 1998: 172, van der Staaij 1995: 111, Coleman 1992: 396,412f.

### novus
novus 'new' [adj. o/a\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: denuo [adv.] 'anew, ooce more1 (Pl.+); novellus 'young, tender' (Varro+), novalis 'left unploughed, fallow' (Varro-*-), novitas 'novelty* (Lucr>), novdre 'to make as something new, renew' (Lucr.+), noverca 'step-mother1 (Pl.+), novicius 'newly imported, recently discovered' (Pl.+); renovare 'to restore, renew' (Acc.+); novensides 'title of an obscure set of deities* (Varro+).

Pit. *nowo-, *nowjo-.

It. cognates: U. nuvis [nom.sg.], nuvime [accsg, + -en] 'new* < *nouio-.

PIE *neuo- 'new, young', *neuio- 'new7.

IE cognates: Hit neua- 'new, fresh', neuahh- 'to renew1, CLuw. naua/i- 'new', Skt. nova-, YAv. nauua- 'new, young*, Gr. νέος, Myc. ne-wo, Cypr. νερό-στατος *new, young'; OCS novb, Ru. noyyj, ToA nu, ToB move 'new, young' < *neuo-; GauL Nevio-, Novio-, Olr. nue, W. newydd, OBret. nouuid, neuued, Bret, neuez, Skt. navya-, Lith, naujas, Go. w«/7s 'new' < *neuio-; Gr. νεάω 'to work fallow land', νεανίας cyouth, young man' (< *neua-n-\ νεαρός 'young, fresh', νηρός 'fresh' < *neua-r-; Arm, nor 'new* < *neu-ro-. The adv. denuo goes back to abL *de *nowo 'a-new* > *denuwo. Whereas Latin continues

PIE *neuo-, Sabellic has reflexes of *neuio~. The noun noverca cannot be directly linked with the Armenian or Greek r-derivatives, since *nevr- would yield Latin *niir- (or *nerv-\ and so would *neuVr-. Hence, noverca is probably a recent formation made to novus; the suffix is unclear.

PIE *neuo- may originally be derived from the word *HW 'now'.

Bibl.: WH II: I80£, EM 447f, IEW 769, Schrijver 1991: 449, Meiser 1998: 68, Untermann 2000: 505f., Beekes 2003: 165. → nunc, niiper



### nox
nox 'night' [f. /] (abLsg. nocte^ gen.pl. noctium) (Naev.+)

Derivatives: nox 'by night' (Lex XH+); noctu 'at night' (Naev.+), nociua 'night bird, little owl' (Pl.+), noctulnus 'of the little owl' (Pi.), nocturnus 'of the night' (Naev.+); pernoctare 'to spend the night1 (Pl.+); aequinoctium Equinox' (Cato+X aequinoctialis 'of the equinox' (Varro+); noctuvigilus 'wakeful' (Pl.), nocticolor 'night-coloured* (Laev. apud Gell.), noctiluca 'that shines by night' (Laev.+), noctipuga 'the female genitals' (LuciL), noctivagus 'roaming by night' (Lucr,+).

Pit. *nok*w)-t-.

PIE *nogʷʰ-t-s [nom.sg.], *negʷʰt-s [gen.sg.] 'night\

IE cognates: Olr. innocht 'tonight', Hit. neku-' 'tobecome night' (< *neglvh-ti), nekuz [adv.] Mn the evening' (= gen.sg.); Skt nakt- 'night' (nom.sg. nak^ acc.sg. naktam) , Av. upa.naxturvsu [adj.loc.pl.] 'bordering on the night' for *ηαχίθ!ν-Ί> Waxi ncryd 'night', Gr. νύξ (νύκτ-) 'night' (< *noKvts\ νύκτωρ [adv.] 'at night', νύκτερος 'nightly', νυκτερίς, -ίδος [f] 'bat', νυκτερινός 'nightly'; -νυχ- e.g. έν-νυχ-ος, έν-νύχ-ιος 'nightly, in the night', νύχιος 'nightly', νυχεύω 'to wake through the night'; Lith. naktis 'night' (gen.pL naki-tf), OCS nostb 'night'; Go. nahts 'night'* The form nox 'at night' is regarded as the remainder of an old gen.sg. *nok -t-es (Leumann 1977: 431), but phonetically more likely is a gen.sg. *nokʷ-t-s, as Meiser assumes. Pinault 2006a explains nox 'at night' from an old loc.sg. *nokʷt > *noh which was remade into nox by analogy wih dins 'by day5. He also assumes that noctu

<!-- pdf-page: 431; printed-page: 417; running-header: nudus                                      417 -->

and nocturmis presuppose a locsg. *wo#vi-ew, which was made at an earlier stage on the example of loc.sg. *dieu 'by day*. Noctua is derived from nox 'night' or noctii 'at night\ Nocturnus follows diurnus 'daily', vespernus 'of the evening', and probably reflects *noctu-rino- (or it has acquired -rnus directly at a more recent stage). Verbal forms are only attested in Anat, the word for 'night' must be an old root noun from this root. On the strength of the lenis consonant -k- in Hittite (normally from *gTV or V * ) an d Greek -νυχ-, Kloekhorst 2008: 602 reconstructs the root as "ng**-.

Bibl.: WH II: 181-183, EM 448, IEW 762f., Meiser 1998: 139, Pinault 2006a: 228, LIV *negV



### nūbēs, -is
nQbes, -is 'cloud' [f. (m.) i] ( Andr.+; nubs [nom.sg.] in Andr.)

Derivatives: nubilus 'cloudy, hazy, dark' (Pl.+), nubildrefi 'to become cloudy, overcast' (Cato+), innubilus 'cloudless' (Lucr.), rmbilarium 'shelter from the rain' (Varro+); obnubere 'to veil, cover' (usually the head, but also other objects) (Varro+).

Pit. *{s)noup-(i-\

PIE *(s)ne/oudh-,

IE cognates: W. nudd 'haze'; YAv. snaoSa- 'clouds5, snaoSent- 'weeping' (vel sim.), BaL «otf'(rau>)cloud\ If nubs, -is is the old inflection, this may be a root noun or /-stem. The only direct cognate is W. nudd, which would imply an etymology *(s)ne/oudh-. In Latin, obnubere suggests that the stem nub- meant a 'cover'; note that 'clouds' are often denominated as 'cover5. This renders a connection possible with the word nubere 'to marry" (said of the woman), see there. An alternative analysis is as *sn-eu- + * - d \ to be compared with Skt pra-snauti 'drips, drops',pr&snuta- 'releasing mother's milk5, see s/v. nutrlx.

Bibl.: WH II: 183, EM448£, IEW 978, Schrijver 1991: 375.



### nubo, -ere
nubo, -ere 'to get married' [v. Ill; pf. nupsi, ppp. nuptum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: nupiiae [f.pl.] 'marriage, wedding' (Pl.+), nuptialis 'of a marriage or wedding' (Pl.+), nupta 'bride' (Pl.+), nuptula 'young bride' (Varro); conubium 'intermarriage;, marriage' (Lucr.+), inrmba 'unmarried5 (Varro-f).

Pit. *{s)nu/ouf-e/o- f *(s)nu/oup-e/o-. Long δ in conubium is supposed to prove original *sn-. A connection with Gr. νύμφη 'bride' must be dismissed. Nubere has been compared with RuCS snubiti 'to court, love' (e.g. in LIV), but EM object that this verb is used for the man seeking a bride, not for a woman marrying. Hence, this connection becomes less certain. EM favour an interpretation of nubere as 'to take the veil', especially in view of obnubere 'to cover' (see s.v. niibes). Semanticaiiy this is attractive, although it cannot be proven. Morphologically, however, an etymology involving root-final *-dʰ is difficult, since the ppp. is nuptum rather than *nussum (cf. tubed - iussus). Thus, nuptum would have to be a fairly recent formation on the basis of the present stem.

Bibl.: WH I: 268, II: 183f., EM 449, IEW 977f., Leumann 1977; 190, LIV *sneubV



### nūdus
nfidus 'naked' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nuddre 'to make naked, strip5 (Enn.+), denudare 'to lay bare, strip'

<!-- pdf-page: 432; printed-page: 418; running-header: 418                                      nugae -->

(Pac,+).

Pit. *nowo/epo- 'naked'.

PIE *nogʷs [nom.], *negʷs [gen.] 'naked'j » LatGm. *nogʷodʰo-,

IE cognates: Olr. nocht, W. noeth (< *no^-to-), Hit nekumanza, Skt nagna-, YAv, τηαγηα-, Khot. bunaa- (< Plr.*bagnaka-) 'naked*, Gr. γυμνός (< *gogʷno- < *nogʷno-)J Arm. merk (< *megʷro- < *negʷ-ro-\ OPr. nognan 'leather', Lith. nuogas 'naked', OCS nagb 'naked' (<

PIE *nogʷ-o-); Go. naqaps, OE nacod, MoDu. naaki (< *nogʷot/dʰ-o-), OIc. nakinn, OFr. naken (< *nogʷno-?)> The adj. can be explained via a phonetic development *nogʷopo- > *nowodo- > *noodo- > *npdo- > nudus (Schrijver 1991). The Pit. form is based on a PIE root adj. which acquired different extensions in different IE languages (cf. Beekes 1994).

Bibl.: WH II: 185, EM 449f., IEW 769, Schrijver 1991: 274-281, Beekes 1994: 91-94.



### Dugae
Dugae 'worthless things, nonsense' [f.pl a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: mjgan 'to speak without seriousness, tell stories' (Pl.+), nugdtor 'one who trifles, romancer' (Pl.+), nugax 'incompetent, farcical' (Pl.+), nugatorius [adj.] 'futile, romancing' (Pl.+); nugigerulus 'who peddles trumpery' (Pl.).

Pit. *hmg-. No PIE etymology. Since words for * trifle' are sometimes formed from words for 'nut' or 'seed', a connection with Lat nux is quite likely. In view of the difference in vowel length {it vs. u) and in the velar (g vs. k) between nugae and ma, this might be a foreign loanword in Latin.

Bibl.: WH II: 185, EM 450. → nux



### num Ί suppose, whether'
num Ί suppose, whether' [ptcle.] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: nunc 'now' (Naev.+), nwnciam 'here and now' (Pl.+); nuper [adv.] 'recently' (Pl.+), nuperus 'fresh' (Pl.+).

Pit. *num 'now', *ηύ 'now'.

PIE *nu 'now', *nu-n(-6/oi) .

IE cognates: Hit. nu [ptcle.] 'and, but', Pal. nu, nu, Hit. ki-nun 'now', CLuw. naram 'now', Skt. nunom 'now, indeed', OAv. niirvm, YAv. nuram, nurqm, OP nuram 'now', MP mm 'now', Gr. νυ, νυν, νυν 'now', Lith. nun, ηΰηαϊ 'now, today', OCS rtyne, CS ntne, Ru. nyne 'now, today', none (dial.) 'now, today', Go. nu, OHG nu, late MHG nun 'now'. Like some other languages, Latin preserves traces of the PIE adv. *nu (in mipef) and of extended *nu-n(V) (in nunc < *num-ce). In (*)«wm, final -m might reflect

PIE *-« (for which hardly any good evidence is available in Latin), or a remake of earlier *nun on the analogy of nam and -nem. For nuper, two etymologies have been proposed: an original adj. *new(nparo- 'bringing forth new things', or an adv. *m/ 'now' + -per. Phonetically, it is impossible to decide; but semantically, the latter is definitely more likely, since nuper does not have an active meaning (thus WH). Also, if the adj. was older (nuperus), one would expect an adv. *nuperum. Probably,

PIE *nu turned to *nu in stressed position in many individual languages.

Bibl.: WH II: 186-188, 190, EM 450, 452, IEW 770, Leumann 1977: 401. → -ce, novus, -per

<!-- pdf-page: 433; printed-page: 417 -->

-nuo 419



### numerus
numerus 'number, rank, category* [m. o] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: numerare lto reckon, count* (Pl.+), Numeria 'goddess of delivery and counting' (Varro); adnumerare 'to tell out, reckon5 (Pl.+), denumerare 'to pay in full' (Pl.+), dtnumerare 'to calculate* (Pl.+), enumerare *to count up, enumerate' (Pl.+), pemumerare 'id.' (Pl.+), renumerare 'to pay back, report* (Pl.+); innumerus 'countless' (Lucr.+), innumeralis 'id/ (Lucr.+), innumerabilis 'id.* (Lucr.+).

Pit. *nomeso-.

PIE *nem-e/os- [n.] 'apportioning, sacrifice', *nom-o-.

IE cognates: Hit. lammar / tamn- [n.] 'moment', HLuw. lam(i)ni 'at the time' < *nom-r^ *n(o)m-n-os\ Gr. νέμω 'to dispense, distribute', νομή [f.] 'pasture', νομός [m.] 'pasture, habitation, province', νόμος [m.] 'custom, usage, law, composition'. Probably a thematized form of the PIE s-stem *nem-os seen in nemus. The o-grade in numerus must be due to analogical replacement of the e-grade of the root by the o-grade on the model of *nom-o- 'rank, number', Lat. *nemos\ -esos » *nomos, -esos (cf. pondus, -eris for the same development). Subsequently, the gen.sg. *nomes-os must have been reinterpreted as the new nom.acc.sg. *nomeso-s, yielding a thematic paradigm.

Bibl.: WH II: 186f., EM 450f., IEW 763f., Meiser 1998: 83, LIV I.*nem-. → nemus



### nOntius
nOntius 'messenger, message1 [m. o] (Naev.+; grammarians nountius^ CIL nontius)

Derivatives: nuntiare cto bring word of, deliver a message' (Pl.+), nuntium 'message* (Varro+); enuntiare 'to make known, express' (Pl.+), internuntius 'messenger* (Pl.+), interriuntia 'female messenger* (Pl.+), obrmntiare 'to announce' (Ter.+), praenuntius [adjVm.] 'heralding; herald' (Lucr.+), rermntius 'reporter' (Pl.), renuntiare 'to report, announce, renounce*. Probably *nountio-y but the further etymology is unclear. It has been derived from the root *neuH- 'to cry* (Skt novate), unattested in Latin, but also from novus 'new'. But in the latter case, a reconstruction *no(wo)wentio- does not make sense morphologically.

Bibl.: WH II: I88f„ EM 45If., IEW 767, Schrijver 1991: 275.



### -nuo, -ere
-nuo, -ere 'to nod' [v. Ill; pf. -nut, ppp. -nuturn] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: numen 'motion of the head, nod; divinity' (Acc.+); abnuere 'to refuse, deny, forbid* (Pl.+), abnuere 'id.' (Enn.), adnuere 'to nod, permit* (Pl.+), innuere 'to nod, beckon' (Pl.+); nutare 'to nod with the head, bend forward, sway1 (Pl.+), nutusy -us 'nod* (Lucr.+), abnutare 'to disapprove' (Pl.+), adnutare 'to nod, assent' (Naev.+)3 reniitare lto refuse' (Lucr.).

Pit. *nowe/o-.

PIE *neu-e/o- 'to nod*.

IE cognates: Olr. as-noi* 'to promise' (< *eks-nou-% αώηόϊ* 'to entrust', MW dineu, MBret dinou 'to pour forth, flow' < PCI. *neue/o-, Gr. νεύω [pr.] 'to nod, beckon* (< *neu-s- or < *νέω with introduction of νευ- from the aorist). Less clearly: Skt. -nauti [3s.act.pr.], caus. ati-navayet [3s.optact.] 'to move', Sogd. nw- 'to go slowly', η V- 'to shake', MoP navidan/nav- 'to shake, tremble; move (esp. when rising from a place)', Lith. niausti 'to bend'.

<!-- pdf-page: 434; printed-page: 420; running-header: 420                                       nurus -->

Numen and nutus can have *ne/ou- and -nuo can be from *-neuo. The thematic present is also found outside Italic, while the Lat noun and the ppp. were derived from the pr. ,

Bibl.: WH H: 189, EM 452, IEW 767, Meiser 2003: 235, Schumacher 2004: 491, LIV *neu-, → cernuus



### nurus, -us
nurus, -us 'daughter-in-law' [f u] (Lex Reg., Ter.+)

Pit. *snuso-» *snusu-.

PIE *snuso- [f] 'daughter-in-law'.

IE cognates: Skt snusa-, Sogd. swnsh, Oss. nostce, MoP suna* sun(h)ar 'daughter-in-law', Gr. νυός [f,] * daughter-in-law'; Arm. now, gen.sg, nowoy [f.], SeCS sm>xa, Ru. snoxa^ SCr. snoha < BSl. *snus-ehr, OE snoru [f.], OFr. swore 'daughter-in-law'. The noun has shifted from o-stem to w-stem inflection under the influence of socrus, -Us 'mother-in-law \ Possibly,

PIE *sra/-s-o- is a derivative of the same root *sneu- 'to drip, nurse' from which nutrix is often derived.

Bibl.: WH Π: 190, EM 452, IEW 978, LIV *sneu- (?). → nutrix



### nutrtx
nutrtx 'child's nurse' [f. k] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nutrire 'to suckle, nourish' (Cat.+); nutricula 'child's nurse' (Lucil.+), nutricare 'to suckle, nourish' (Pl.+), nutrTcatus, -us 'nurturing' (Pl.+), nutricatid 'suckling, rearing' (Varro+), niitrlcius 'foster-father, guardian' (Var.+).

Pit. *(s)noutrl

PIE *sneu-tr-ih₂- 'female nurser'.

IE cognates: Skt. pra-snauti [3s.act.] 'to drip, release liquids', pra-snuta- 'releasing mother's milk'. Appurtenance of Gr. νάω 'to flow, stream5 (< *νάρΜ]ω) is difficult and requires special pleading. The verb nutrire was derived from *noutrJ before this acquired the suffix *-£-. Afterwards, from *nutrik- the verb nutricare was derived (thus Steinbauer 1989). It has been proposed that the PIE root *sneu- is cognate to *sneh₂- 'to bathe, swim', pointing to an analysis *sn-u- and *sn-hr-

Bibl.: WH I: 190f, EM 453, IEW 971 f.f Leumann 1977: 376, Steinbauer 1989: 76f, Schrijver 1991: 152f., LIV *sneu-. → nubes, nurus



### niix, -cis
niix, -cis 'nut' [f. k] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: nuceus 'of a nut-tree' (Cato), nuc(u)leus 'kernel, stone (of fruit)' (Pl.+); nucifrangibulum 'nutcracker' (Pl.).

Pit. *knuk-.

IE cognates: Ofr. cnu [f], gen. cnao, cno, ace. cnoi, MW cneu 'nuts', LCo. knyfan 'a nut', MBret. cnou, Bret, hraoh 'nuts' < PCL nom. *knus, obi. *knuu~ (< *£/iw-i/-?); OIc. hnot, OE hnutu 'nut' < *knu-d-. Lat. nuc(u)Ieus presupposes a diminutive *nuculus/-a. As argued s.v. nugae, the irregular alternations within Latin between nugae and nux already suggest a non-IE loanword. To this we may add the different suffixes in Celtic (*-//-?) and Germanic (*-d-) and the sequence *AHW-, which does not look very Indo-European (only with a full grade *kneu-9 but this is not attested). In combination with the restriction to

<!-- pdf-page: 435; printed-page: 421; running-header: obliquus                                      421 -->

Italo-Celtic and Gm,, and the semantic field of trees and fruits, we may safely assume non-IE origin,

Bibl.: WH II: 191f, EM 453, IEW 558f, Leumann 1977: 287, Strunk 1993, Schrijver 1995: 329f —» nugae

ο ob 'towards, in front oft with regard to, because of [prepj (Lex ΧΠ+); variants: ob in isolation and as a preverb in front of vowels, s-, t→ h- and all voiced consonants except g- (variants ops-, opt- especially in inscriptions); oc- in front of c-\ of- in front of/·; og- in front of g-; o- in front of w- (only in omitto), more usually obm- or om-\ op- (in front of/?-).

Derivatives: optu/imus cbest? (Naev.+), optu/imas, -tis [adj.] 'aristocratic* (Enn.+).

Pit. *op(i). Lejeune 1974 assumes Ven. op < *opi, but this is uncertain.

It. cognates: Ven. op 'because of, for', 0, up, op + abL 'at\ FaL opidque cob idque\ Maybe in U, ustentu, ostendu [3s.ipv.II] (meaning uncertain).

PIE *hiopi 'at, by\

IE cognates: Lith. ap(i)- 'around' < *h₁opi; Skt dpi, Av. aipi, OP apiy 'also; by, in', Gr. έπι, επί 'on, at', Arm. ew calsp, and' < *htepi. The variant *obs- only occurs in a few forms, and seems to be relatively recent and short-lived. Older are oscen> ostendo, ostento and derivatives. Original *opw- gave op-, as preserved in oportet and operio. Cowgill 1970: 116f, 147, Leumann 1977: 317f. and Sihler 1995 regard optumus as a sup. to ob, meaning 'uppermost'. Since ob does not mean fiup, above' in Latin but 'towards', one may also conjecture a meaning 'foremost'. WH explain optumus as a sup, *opi-tVmo- 'most powerful', but this is much less likely. Lat ob and Lith. ap(i)- can be taken to reflect *hiop(i) 'at, by' (the Italic forms mi^ht be better explained from a monosyllable *hiop), an ablaut variant of *hjepi as seen in Ih\, Gr. and Arm, Kloekhorst 2008: 194 argues on semantic grounds that Hit appa, CLuw. appa 'back, again' probably reflect *h₂op-, and go together with Gr; δπι-σύεν '(from) behind', Myc, οφι9 όψέ 'afterwards', δψι(-) (AeoL and in cp.).

Bibl.: EM 454, Lejeune 1974: 337, IEW 323ff., Leumann 1977: 157f., Hamp 1981a, Schrijver 1991: 50f, Sihler 1995: 203fi, 368, 440, Meiser 1998: 153, Untermann 2000:799f.,812f.,816fi



### obliquus
obliquus 'slanting, transverse' [adj. o/a] (Cato+) The etymology is unknown. Closest in form and meaning are limus 'transverse' and subtimis 'transverse from below upward5, and the latter would be morphologically similar to obliquus. Yet a root */T- with different suffixes *-mo- and *-kʷo- does not immediately make sense, and has no clear connections outside Italic.

Bibl.: WH II: 194f, EM 455, IEW 307-309. → limus 2

<!-- pdf-page: 436; printed-page: 422; running-header: 422                                    obKvTscor -->



### oblīvīscor, -ī 'to forget'
oblMscor, -14to foiget' [v. Ill; pf. obtitus sum] (Andr.+; pn also obliscT Pl., Ace.)

Derivatives: obltvium 'forgetfulness' (Luci\+), oblrvius Most in oblivion' (Varro), oblrvidsus 'forgetful' (Pl.+), obtivio 'forgetting, oblivion' (Varro+). Schrijver follows Sommer 1914: 602 in assuming an original verb *ob-lrvere 'to be smoothe' < *ob-leiwe- < t-lehji-u-ehr, a stative verb derived from the adj. *lehii-u-i- 'smooth' which yielded Lat levis. Yet a semantic shift from 'to be smooth' to 'to forget' is not very convincing. Keller 1992:254 connects ob-litus with Gr. λίναμαι 'to dodge', Gm. *linnan 'to stop', Skt -ityate 'dissolved' <

PIE *lih₂- 'to stop / disappear'; but the weaknesses of her theory have been addressed by Seldeslachts 2001: 89. The latter returns to the adj. levis, and proposes to regard oblfvlscor 'to start being erased' as an inchoative deponent to a denominative verb *oblivire 'to make smooth, erase' < *ob-levi-ie/o-. The ptc. oblltus would stem from *ob-Iitus, the ptc. of ob-linere 'to smear, stop', taking its -J-fromthe pf. ob-Ιϊνϊ of that same verb. The second part of the hypothesis is weak: why would the ptc. of oblinere have been applied to obthnscorl

Bibl.: WH II: 195, EM 455, IEW 66If, Schrijver 1991: 284, Meiser 1998: 92. -* levis



### obscaenus
obscaenus 'unpropitious, ill-omened; indecent' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+; the second vowel vacillates between ae and e)

Pit. *skai-no- 'left, unpropitious'.

PIE *skeh₂-i-no- 'shaded; left'.

IE cognates: see s.v. scaevus. Since e can be a phonetic development for ae in second syllable (*a/ > *e > ί) whereas ae cannot be explained if the original vowel was e, obscaenus must be the original form. EM propose to compare scaevus 'left, bad', and analyse *ob-skai-no- 'coming from the left side'. This seems worthy of consideration: semantically it would fit perfectly, and, formally, a Pit. formation *skaino- would be quite close to Balto-Slavic *skehzi-n(-i)- 'shadow'.

Bibl.: WH I: 131, EM 456, IEW 628, LIV *skeH(i)-. → scaevus



### obscurus
obscurus 'dark, gloomy' [adj. o/a] (Ena+)

Derivatives: obscurare 'to obscure (heavenly bodies or sources of light), darken' (Pl.+), obsdiridicus 'speaking obscurely' (Ace), obscuritas 'darkness, obscurity' (Varro+).

Pit. *skoi-ro-.

PIE *skoh₂-i-ro- 'dark, shady'?

IE cognates: Gr. σκίρον [η J (description of a white parasol or canopy); Go. skeirs, OIc. skirr, NHG schier 'clear, bright' < *slaro-. The original meaning seems to be 'darkened, covered'. It is disputed whether we must analyse the word as obscurus or obs-curus, since oc- is productive in Latin in front of c-, but oscen shows *obs-. If obscurus, Schrijver supports the derivation of *skziro- from a preform *skuHro- 'roof, cover'; but this is semantically questionable, since the compound would then mean '(what is) toward/in front of the cover', which is not what obscurus means, or a pleonastic 'against-cover', which is a rare type of formation. If obs-ciirusy Schrijver (p. 462) cites Hamp's etymology (1982-1983a: 99)

<!-- pdf-page: 437; printed-page: 423; running-header: occulo                                     423 -->


*obs-kʷois-o- Obstructing one's sight', the phonetic weakness of which Schrijver points out himself. Among the possible phonetic preforms of non-initial -urus are

PIE *-uHro-, *-e/o(H)uro- and (maybe) *-o(H)iro-, Also, a word in *sfc- seems to me more probable than a word in *£-, since sk- could then have been restored after a phonetic change to *osc-. If the etymology of scaevus < *skeh₂iuo- and obscaenus < *op-skeh₂ino- 'shaded, left' is accepted, one might posit an ablaut variant *skoiro- < *skoh₂i-ro- 'dark, shaded'. Semantically this would be fine; morphologically, there is no exact cognate in the other IE languages.

Bibl.: WH II: 196, EM 456, IEW 951-953, Schrijver 1991: 246f, LIV *(s)keuhr.



### obturo, -are
obturo, -are 'to block, stop up' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: returare 'to unplug' (Varro). The stem -turd- seems to presuppose a Pit nominal form */wro-, *tVu(V)ro-y or *toiro-, WH and IEW connect -turd with

PIE *tuhr 'to swell', which is semantically possible {*turo- 'swelling' > 'plug'), but since obturare can also be interpreted as 'to seal, protect', it can also be connected with tiifus 'safe' < *tuH~. But obviously, any plug-like object with the required phonological form could be the input for -turare9 so that all remains uncertain.

Bibl.: WH II: 197, EM 456, IEW 1080-1085. ~> turgeo > occa 'harrow' [f. a] (LLat texts and glosses)

Derivatives: occare 'to harrow, break up the ground' (Pl.+), occator 'harrower' (Pl.+), occillare 'to break in pieces' (Pl.).

Pit. *oketa-l

PIE *h₂ok-et- [f] 'harrow1.

IE cognates: W. Bret, oged 'harrow1, Gr. όξίνα 'an agricultural implement with iron teeth, drawn by oxen' (Hsch.); OPr. aketes, Lith. akicios, dial, ekicios [nom.pl.f. a], Latv. ece(k)sas 'harrow', Ru. osit' 'granary, rack for drying grain' [f ], Fl.jesiec (dial.) 'grain sieve' < BSl. *es-et-i-, OHG egida, OE egede < PGm, *a/epo-. Since the verti occare and other derivatives are attested much earlier and better than the noun, TLL 'suggests that the noun was back-formed to the verb. But since the verb is of the first class, it would still seem to be formed on the basis of a noun. The Latin cluster cc may derive from secondary *tk> which led Hirt (1916-17: 230) to posit a development

PIE *oketa > pre-Latin *okita > *otika > syncope *otka > occa. In view of the closely matching Celtic, Germanic and BSL forms which have the same meaning, this idea is appealing; but the assumed metathesis is not supported by other evidence. Alternatively, occa may be a borrowing from a hypothetical (Italic) dialect in which *kt developed into kk. In that case, we can posit *oketa~ > *okta- > occa.

Bibl.: WH II: 197£, EM 456f, IEW 18-22, Hirt 1916-17: 230, Schrijver 1991: 52. -* aceo, acu-y ocris



### occulo, -ere
occulo, -ere 'to hide, conceal' [v. Ill; occulta, occultum] (Pl.+; SCBac. fojquoltod)

Derivatives: occultare 'to conceal' (Pl.+).

Pit. *kele/o-.

<!-- pdf-page: 438; printed-page: 424; running-header: 424                                      ocior -->

PIE *£el-e/o- 'to hide'.

### ōcior
8cior 'faster, swifter' [adj.comp. r] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: sup. dcissimus (Ter.+) / dxime (Paul, ex F.\

Pit. *ok~.

PIE *h,ohik-u- 'quick', *h₁oh,lc-ios- 'quicker'. BE cognates: OW diouc, MW diawg, Co. dioc, OBret. diochi, Bret, diek 'lazy' < PCI. *di-akV- 'lazy', lit 'un-swift'; Skt asu- 'quick', comp. asryas-, sup. asisfha-, Av. asu- 'quick, fast*, comp. asiiah-, sup. asista-, Gr. ώκύς 'fast, swift', ώκιστος 'fastest'; Ru. jastreb, ORuJastfjab^Jastrebt^ Fo.jastrzqb 'hawk5, SCv.jastrijeb 'kite' < PS1. *jastr?bʰ.
