### ocris
ocris 'rugged mountain' [m. i] ( Andr.+)

Derivatives: mediocris 'of medium size, moderate' (Pl.+), mediocriter 'moderately; on a large scale' (Pl.+).

Pit. *okri- [m.] 'hilltop7.

It. cognates: U. ukar, ocar [nom.sg.], ocrem, ocre [accsg.], oarer [gen.sg.], ukre, ocre [dat.sg.], ocre, ocrem [loc.sg.], ukripe, ukriper, ocriper, ocreper [abl.sg.]; Marr. ocres [gen.sg.]; SPic. okrei [loc.sg.?] 'castle, upper town'.

PIE *h₂olc-r-i- 'shaφ edge'.

IE cognates: Mir. ochair 'edge', W, ochr, Skt. catur-asri- [adj.] 'four-angled' (RV); asri- [f.] 'sharp edge, angle' (Br.+); Gr. δκρις [m.] 'point, sharp edge', ακρις [f] 'summit'. Ocris is found as an independent word only in a few instances; it was apparently ousted by collis. It has been preserved in the compound mediocris, orig, 'situated at the middle, half-way of the top'. Probably an /-derivative of the adj.

PIE *h₂ek-ro- 'sharp'.

Bibl.: WH II: 199, EM 457, IEW 18-22, Leumann 1977: 403, Schrijver 1991: 51, Untermann 2000:79Iff. → deer 'sharp\ acu-



### octo
octo 'eight' [num. indecl.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: octavus 'eighth' (Cato+); octoginta 'eighty' (Cic.+), octdgem [adj.pl.] 'eighty each5 (Lucil.+), October [adj.] 'the eighth month' (Cato+), octonl [adj.pl.] 'eight each' (Pl.+), octdnarius 'containing eight, eight long' (Varro+), octingenti 'eight hundred' (Pl.+), octingenarius 'consisting of 800 each' (Varro).

Pit. *okio 'eight', *oktawo- 'eighth',

PIE *h₃(e)kteh₃'eight\ *h₃kt(e)h₃-uo- 'eighth'.

IE cognates: Olr. ocht n-, W. wyth Bret, eiz ' 8 \ Olr. ochtmad '8th'; Skt asta, astau [nom.acc] '8', astama- '8th', ositi- '80', YAv. asta '8', astama- '8th', aStaiti- '80', Gr. οκτώ '8', ογδορος, όγδοος '8th', όγδοήκοντα 4 80\ OPhryg. otuvoi vetei 'in the eighth year7, Alb. tete '8'; Lith. astuom

<!-- pdf-page: 439; printed-page: 425; running-header: odor                                       425 -->


' 8 \ Go, ahtau; ToB okt, ToA okat 4 8\ The ordinal seems to show a change of *-ouos > ^-auos, which is reinterpreted by Schrijver 1991: 300 as a delabialization of

PIE *-eh₃-uo- (with labialized laryngeal) to *-eh₂-uo- due to the following *-w-.

Bibl.: WH II: 199f, EM 457£, IEW 775, Schrijver 1991: 49, 300, Coleman 1992: 396, 412, Beekes 1995: 213-216, Sihler 1995: 414, 432, Meiser 1998: 56, 86, 175, Beekes2003: 184.



### oculus
oculus 'eye' [m. o] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: ocellus 'little eye' (Pl.+), ocetlatum 'a stone with small spots' (Varro+), oculatus 'having sight' (Pl.+), oculitus [adv.] 'as dearly as one's eyes', oculissimus 'dearest apple of my eye' (Pl.), oculeus 'made of eyes' (Pl.+); unoculus 'that has one eye' (Pl.+); exoculare 'to deprive of eyes' (Pl.+).

Pit. *ok(e)lo-.

PIE *h₃e/okʷ-(e)lo- 4 eye\

IE cognates: Skt. aksnas [gen.sg.], aks-J [nom.acc.du.] 'eye', an-aks- 'without eyes, blind5 < *h₃e/okʷ-s-, YAv. asi [nom.acc.du.n.] 'eye5, Gr. &ψ [f.], gen. ώπός 'eye, face', δσσε [nom.acc.du.n,] 'eyes5 < *h₃kʷ-i-, OPr. ackis 'eyes' [nom.p.], ackins [acc.pl.], Lith. alas, Latv. acs 'eye', OCS oko [nom.sg.], oci [nom.acc.du.n.] 'eye' < *h₃ekʷ-. Since most IE languages have a root noun or an i-stem 'eye', the Latin formation probably does not go back to>PIE. Oculus could be derived from a PIE root noun (*h₃e/okʷ-)> from a PIE thematic noun (probably *h₃okʷ-o-) or from an earlier verb 'to see5.

Bibl.: WH II: 200-202, EM 458, IEW 775-777, Schrijver 1991: 49, Sihler 1995: 120, LIV *h₃eky-.



### ōdī
6dl 'to hate' [v. pf. only, ppp. osum) (Naev.+)

Derivatives: odibilis 'odious' (Ace), odium 'hatred, boredom' (Pl.+), odiosus 'offensive, boring5 (Pl.+), odiossicus 'disagreeable' (Pl.).

Pit. *5d- 'to hate', *odio- 'hatred'.

PIE pf. *h₃e-h₃(o)d- 'to hate', *h₃e/od-io- 'hatred'.

IE cognates: Gr. aon όδύσ(σ)αοτ3αι 'to be angry, grumble', pr. ούδύεται 'contends' (Hsch.) (pr. *όδύομαι from a noun *όδ-υ- 'angriness'?), Arm. ateam 'to hate' < *h₃(o)d-, OIc. etja 'hetzen, treiben', OE atol, eatol [n.] 'evil\ The pf. was used to convey the resultative/stative meaning. Therefore, LIV posits as the basic meaning of the root 'to develop hatred against5, which is possible but not necessary.

Bibl.: WH II: 202f, EM 458f., IEW 773, Schrijver 1991: 49f., Beekes 2003: 157, 184,UV2.*h₃ed-.



### odor
odor 'smell, odour5 [m. r] (Pl.+; nom.sg. odds Pl.)

Derivatives: odorari 'to smell, scent' (Pl.+), odorus 'having a smell5 (Varro+), odorisequus 'following the scent* (Andr. [uncertain]); olere [pf. olui\ 'to smell' (Pt.+), olere [pf. olui\ 'to smeir (PL, Afran., Pompon.), olor 'smell' (Varro: littera

<!-- pdf-page: 440; printed-page: 424 -->

426 -oles


commutate dicitur odor olor)7 ol(e)facere 'to smell, hear about' (Pl.+), odefacere 'olefacere* (Paul ex F.), ol(e)factare 'to smell at, sniff (Pl.+), ol(e)factus9 -us 'th smelling' (Pl.+); inolens 'odourless' (Lucr.), obolere 'to smell, stink' (Pl.+), perolere 'to emit a penetrating smell' (Lucil.+), praeolere *to send out an odour ahead' (Pl.+)> subolere 'to smell faintly' (Pl.+),

Pit. *ode/o- 'to smell', *odos 'odour',

PIE pr. *h₃ed-/*h₃d- 'to smell'.

IE cognates: Gr, δζω (Ion, Att) 'to smell', plqpf όδώδει, Gr, όδμή, οσμή 'odour, scent', Arm. hot 'smeir < *h₃ed-, Lith, uosti, 3s, uodzia, Latv. uost 'to smell, sniff, OCzJadati 'to investigate, explore'. Some forms show the change of intervocalic *<i > /, the exact conditions of which are unclear. In this word, it seems that d was preserved in front of d, but changed to / in front of e. In view oifalgere /fiilgere9fervere /fervere, it seems likely that olere is older than olere (cf. Leumann).

Bibl.: WH II: 203, 206, EM 459f, IEW 772f., Leumann 1977: 544, Schrijver 1991: 50, Meiser 1998: 100, LIV l.*h₃ed-.



### -ol6s, -is
-ol6s, -is 'feeding, nursling' [f /]: indoles, -is f. (Pl.+) "nature, character'; proles (Lucr.+) 'offspring', suboles (Pl.+) 'young growth of a tree; offspring; generation'

Derivatives: proletarius 'belonging to the lowest class of citizens' (Pl.+, Lex XII in Gell.). This noun only occurs as the second member of compounds. It reflects *a!-i- or *aUe- 'upbringing; offspring, descendant' to alere 'to feed'. The vowel was weakened in non-initial syllable to o, but not further to w. Leumann 1977: 553 assumes that these nouns were built to a stative verb *alere 'to grow up' (as reflected in ad-olesco). An old /-stem ^h&hi- or *h₂pl-i- is difficult because one would expect a phonetic outcome *-Ues9 -is. Thus, *ol-i- is probably an inner-Latin formation. The early attestation of proletarius, as well as its meaning which differs from proles, show that proles must have existed a long time before its first attestation in the first century BC

Bibl.: WH I:694, II: 369,619, EM 23, IEW 261, Schrijver 1991: 42, LIV 262, → aid



### olle / ollus
olle / ollus 'that; he, she, it' [pron., pron. adj. o/a] (Andr.+. Forms: dat.sg, ollT, datpL o//&, nom.plm, o//f, nom.acc.pln. olla)

Derivatives: olim [advj 'formerly, once; one day, in future' (Pl.+).

Pit. *olno- > *ollo-.

It. cognates: O. ulleis [gen.sg.rn.]> ulum [acc.sg.m.], ulam [acc,sg.f.], ulas [gen.sg.f], olu [gen.pl.m.] 'that one' < *o/-; U. ulu, ulo 'thither' < *o/d, *o/<3 or *olom.

PIE *h₂ol-no- 'that, yonder'.

IE cognates: Gaulish olio-, OIn ol 'beyond'; OCS lani7 Cz. loni iast year' < PSL *o/m. The oldest Latin form was ollus [o/a] which acquired pronominal endings as in iste: ollus » olle, ollum » ollud. The stem oil- only survives in old formulae and in hexameter poetry, but was already replaced at the beginning of the literary period by Me on the example of is9ea9id and iste. Olim seems to be formed off the pronominal stem *o//o- with the suffix -im (productive in VOLat.) for adverbs of time and place. For the Pit. preform, both *olno- or *olso- are conceivable; but the Celtic and Slavic

<!-- pdf-page: 441; printed-page: 427; running-header: omen                                       427 -->

evidence points to *olno-. For olim, Schrijver 1991 (passim) reconstructs *h₂ol- for the root; although this seems highly irregular from a morphological point of view, note that U. also requires *<5.

Bibl.: WH II: 206f., EM 461, IEW 24-26, Schrijver 1991: 68, Sihler 1995: 393, Kortlandt 1996: 143, Meiser 1998: 163, Untermann 2000: 793-795. → alius, ille, vis



### olor
olor 'swan' [m. r] (Verg.+)

Pit. *elor.

IE cognates: Olr. eluy MIr. elae [f.] < PCI. *eija?, W. alarch, pi- eleirch 'swan' < *alarko- « *elar-sko- < *elrr-sko-; Ru. lebed' [m.], Cz, labut\ SCr. labud 'swan' < *h₂elbʰ-ond-i- (?); OIc. elptr, glpt [f.]s OHG albiz, elbiz, OE celbitu, ielfetu [f.] 'swan' < *albit-. The preform was probably *el5r (with regular e > ο before velarised /), but *olor cannot be completely ruled out, since the evidence for the development of *olV- is scarce: the loanword Ulixes has w-, the verb volvd < *uoluo has o, but in a special phonetic surrounding. The nom.sg. may have regularly been *elor < *e//·, after which an -or-stem was created. Together with Olr. elu and W. alarch, olor goes back to an Italo-Celtic word for 'swan'. The closest connection in form and meaning is with Ru. lebed\ OHG albiz 'swan', which seem to go back to a root *h₂elbʰ- (as in albus 'white'), but with difficulties, since the Slav, accentuation does not match. Maybe *-bʰ- in Slav, and Gm. is a suffix (as more often in animals and colours), and the root was *h₂el-, or, if non-IE, * VI-, If it were *h₂el-:p it would be impossible to connect Italo-Celtic *e/- 'swan'. If a common word *F7- for 'swan' is accepted, it must be a foreign loanword into the IE languages. The appurtenance of Gr. έλέα [f.J kind of singing-bird, perhaps reed-warbler" is very uncertain.

Bibl.: WH II: 207, EM 461, IEW 302-304, Schrijver 1991: 37, 1995: 76, EIEC 538.



### omen, -inis
omen, -inis 'omen, augury' [η. η] (Pl.+; older osmen according to Varro)

Derivatives: dmindri 'to know or tell from omens, predict' (Pl.+), ominator 'one who makes prognostications' (Pl.).

Pit. *oklvs-mn- 'sighting, omen'? PIE*h₃ek w Js-'tosee'[pr.]. It is uncertain whether Varro's osmen ever really existed, or was invented by folk etymology. Benveniste 1962: I Of. connects Hit. ha-1 /h- cto believe, trust' < *h₂/3eH. Oettinger 1979: 361 reconstructs *h₂e/oh₃-s-mn 'trust'. Yet semantically I am not convinced by this etymology. Others have reconstructed *h₃ekʷ-s-mn *a sighting', the meaning of which seems closer to 'omen'. Note Gr. όμμα, -ατός 'eye' < *όπμα < *h₃ekʷ-mn; Latin would have added an *s, as it often does in front of the suffix *-mw. The only drawback is that the suffix -men nearly exclusively occurs after Latin verb stems or verb stems of an earlier period. For *h₃ekʷ- 'to see', we have no evidence in Latin that a verb existed; for PIE, LIV reconstructs a reduplicated perfect and an ^-present (whence Av. aifiii-axsaiia- 'to watch', Gr. fut. δψομαι 'to see'). Thus, omen could be formed from a PIE ^-present. Meier-Brugger 1992 derives omen from *hJ/2og-smen 'speech, what was predicted' to aid < *h,/2eg-. This is semantically

<!-- pdf-page: 442; printed-page: 428; running-header: 428                                    omentum -->

attractive, but no other evidence for an o-grade of this verb exists in Latin, and one can hardly assume a .s/w^«-derivative to have been built on the PIE perfect *He-Hog-.

Bibl.: WH II: 208, EM 461, Meier-Brugge 1992, U V ?*h₂eh₃-. → oculus



### Omentum "fatty membrane of caul, covering the intestines'
Omentum "fatty membrane of caul, covering the intestines' [n. o] (Cat.+)

It. cognates: U. umen [acc.sg.], umne [abl.sg.] 'ointment' < *omben < *ongʷen-, I see no semantic support for WH's hypothesis that omentum is derived from the verb -ud 'to put on/off. Also, *owe-mento- would phonetically yield *umentumy whence o- could only arise by restoration of *cw-; but there was no word to restore this sequence from. EM suspect that it is a loanword, in which case the source might have been (a cognate or derivative of) U. umen Iomen! 'ointment' < *omben, the Sab. cognate of Lat. unguen.

Bibl.: WH II: 208, EM 461, IEW 346, Untermann 2000: 796f → unguen



### omnis
omnis 'the whole of, all, every' [adj. i] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: ommno [adv.] 'in every respect, entirely' (Pl.+), omnipotens 'almighty' (Pl.+).

Pit. *ορ-ηΐ-Ί

PIE *h₁/3e/op-ni- 'working'. Ommno is a petrified case-form of *omn-mo- 'wholly'. The absence of the change *omn- > *umn- (a likely but not completely certain change) can be explained if mn arose from a different sequence, such as *oPn-* WH propose *op-ni- with the stem op- 'wealth', the stem of which is regarded as verbal

PIE *h₃ep- 'to make' by LIV. In fact, a verbal adj. in *-w/- can be reconstructed for other Latin words too (cf lenis, segnis). The verbal root might be ^ o p - lto take' (opto) or *h₃ep- 'to work' > 'possess'. The former seems slightly more likely for semantic reasons.

Bibl.: WH II: 209f, EM 461, IEW 780, Leumann 1977: 347, Sihler 1995: 43, LIV l.*h₃ep-. → ops, opus



### onus, -eris
onus, -eris 'burden, load' [n. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: onerare 'to load with goods' (Pl.+), onerarius 'for carrying loads' (Naev.+); exonerdre 'to discharge, unload' (Pl.+); ortustus 'loaded, laden' (Naev.+).

Pit. *ones- [IL].

PIE *h₃en-os, -es- [n.] 'load'.

IE cognates: Hit. aniie/a-™ 'to work; to carry out, to produce, to treat', Pal. oniie/a- 'to work', CLuw. anni- 'to carry out' < *h₃n-ie/o-; Skt. anas- 'heavy cart', anadvah- [m.] 'ox, draught animal' < *anas-vah- 'pulling a cart'. The adj. onustus is a fo-derivative of the s-stem: *onos-to-. If the Hittite verb belongs here (semanticaily this is not compelling), the root must rather be *hjenH-, according to Melchert 1994: 85. This is adopted by Janda 1999 and Stuber 2002: 88, who reconstruct *h₁enh₃-. The reconstruction with final -h₃ is based on a comparison with Gr. Ένοσίχΰων, which I find unconvincing. Kloekhorst (2008: 179ff) argues that Melchert's scenario is problematic within Anatolian, and instead proposes to regularly derive an(n)iia- from

PIE *h₃n-je/o-.

Bibl.: WH II: 210, EM 462, IEW 321f ,lubotsky 1990: 132, Schrijver 1991: 50.

<!-- pdf-page: 443; printed-page: 429; running-header: opimus                                     429 -->



### opācus
op&cus 'sheltered, shady' [adj. o/a] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: opacare 'to make shady; darken' (Pac.+). Opticus has since long been compared with Skt apaka- 'turned away from' and OCS opaky 'the other way round, behind one's back', Ru. opak(o) (dial.) 'back', but these must be reconstructed as

PIE *h₂epo-h₃kʷ- 'turned away from', which would yield Latin *apdcus. Klingenschmitt 2004: 249 proposes a metathesis from *apokos to *opakos, but this seems unlikely; see the discussion of undecim s.v. decern. It seems more promising to compare the root ♦(h^uelvr 'to abandon, leave empty' found in vartus, vastus, vacuus, maybe also in vagina 'sheath*: *op-waL·- 'situated towards the empty side' > *opako-. Obviously, this is just a theorical possibility,

Bibl.: WH 210, EM 703, IEW 53-55, Leumann 1977: 340, Schrijver 1991: 146, 308, Klingenschmitt 2004: 249 = 2005: 540. → vacuus, vanus



### operiō, -īre
operio, -Ire 'to shut, close, cover' [v. IV; pf operm, ppp. opertum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: opertare 'to cover habitually' (Enn.+), operculum 'lid, cap' (Cato+), operimentum "covering, cover' (Cato+); copenre/cooperire 'to cover completely' (Pl.+), adaperlre 'to open wide, uncover' (Varro+).

Pit. *op-wer-i(e)~ 'to cover up'.

PIE *h₂uer-i(e)- [pr.] 'to cover'.

IE cognates: see s.v. aperio.

Bibl.: WH II: 211, EM 38, IEW 1160-1162, Sihler 1995: 503, Meiser 1998: 116, 195, LIV *Huer-, → aperire, ob \ 3 flpilio 'herdsman; kind of bird' [m. η] (Pl.+; also upilio Verg., Serv.+)

Derivatives: opilius, opilia (Praeneste).

Pit. *owi→pol-o- 'sheep-driver'.

PIE *h₃eui- 'sheep' + *polh₂-o- 'driver*.

IE cognates: see ovis vesp.pello* The distribution of w- (Verg. et al.) and δ- (PL, CIL) could point to a Sabellic origin of the variant opilio; this would have the regular Sab. outcome of *ow- < *owi~. In Latin, the expected outcome would be upilio. The element upilio can contain a derivative in *-io- to a PIE noiin *>polhro- 'who impels' to the root ofpelld 'to impel5. The compound is probably not of PIE date. Vine 2006a: 234 interprets opilio as a possible 'rustic' Roman reflex of *aupolid « *awi-*polo- < *oui→polh₂o- 'sheep-driver' (or ^ouUphj-lo- 'sheep-protector'), with *ow- > *au~ due to Thumeysen-Havefs unrounding of *ow in pretonic syllable.

Bibl.: WH II: 211, EM 462, IEW 80If;, Leumann 1977: 134, Schrijver 1991: 277, LIV *pelh₂-. → ovis,peIlo



### opimus
opimus 'which is taken as trophy, glorious; rich, sumptuous* [adj. o/a] (Lex Reg., Pl.+)

Derivatives: opimitas 'prosperity' (Pl.).

Pit. *opi[ins.sg.]? The older etymological connection withpinguis, Skt ptvan- 'fat* must be dismissed. Form and meaning suggest that opimus was derived from Lat. ops 'power, resources' which almost certainly continues a Pit. /-stem *o/?-i-. We must assume suffixation of

<!-- pdf-page: 444; printed-page: 430; running-header: 430                                     opinor -->

adjectival *-mo- together with vowel lengthening of *i to *f; this recalls the process leading to adj. in *-/V:no/-, -/V;to/-y which Nussbaum explains as derivatives from ins.sg. forms in *-hi. Since Lat has no productive adjectival suffix other than -(t)(i)mus in the superlatives, it might be just this process by which the language created *opPwith power, with riches' » *ορϊ-τηο- 'abundant\

Bibl.: WH II: 211f., EM 462, IEW 793f, Leumann 1977: 319. — ops



### opinor, -art
opinor, -art 'to think, believe, suppose' [v. I] (Naev.+; also oplnare in PL to Caecil.)

Derivatives: opinatus, -us 'supposition' (Lucr.+), adopman 'to conjecture' (Lucr.), opinio 'opinion, imagination' (Pl.+). Steinbauer 1989 proposes a derivative *op-eina- * to try to achieve' to a noun *eino- 'purpose', but this noun is as yet illusive. Schrijver stresses that the semantic link between 'to believe' and 'to choose' is weak, and so is therefore the traditional etymological connection with the root of optare. Vine 1999a argues that it is difficult to reconstruct a stem *opion-/*opion-fromwhich ορϊη-ά- could have been derived, as others had proposed. He argues that the Praenestine spelling opeinor in a third-century inscription proves that the word contains

Pit. *-eJ- and not even *-f-, which reduces the chances of finding a PIE etymology.

Bibl.: WH II: 212f., EM 462, IEW 781, Steinbauer 1989: I88f, Schrijver 1991: 52, Vine 1999a, LIV ?2.*h₃ep-,



### oportet
oportet 'it is proper,right*[v. II; pf. oportuit] (Naev.+)

Pit. *op-wort-(e-).

PIE *ue-u(o)rt- [pf.] Ho turn\ The original meaning is thought to be 'it befits, it comes to one', and the form is derived from *op 'towards' plus a form of the root *uert- 'to tum\ WH assume an original causative ^uort-eie-, yet a transitive form does notfitthe meaning. Therefore, *op-uort-e- (> *oport-e-, with a sound change dated to Pit. by Meiser 1986: 185) may contain the perf. stem *ue-u(o)rt-, which is assumed to be attested in CIL oduoriit 'advertit'. Thus, 'it has turned toward someone' > 'it befits, it is proper'. The e-conjugation would be a secondary addition in connection with the stative meaning of*oport-.

Bibl.: WH II: 214, EM 463, IEW 323-325, Leumann 1977: 553, Schrijver 1991: 472, LIV *uert-, → verto



### oppidum
oppidum '(fortified) town; barriers' [n. o] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: oppido 'utterly, entirely' (Pl.+).

Pit. *op^pe/od-o- 'obstructing'.

It. cognates: U. perum, pefu, perso [acc.sg.], perume,persorne [acc.sg. + -en] 'ground' or 'place' < *pedom.

PIE *ped-o- [n.] 'stepped' > 'place, step'.

IE cognates: Hit. peda- [n.] 'place', HLuw. LOCUS-to- [n.] 'place', Skt pada- [n.] 'footstep, piece of ground', OAv. pada-, ΥΑ\.ραδα- [n.] 'footstep', OP patUpadam kar- 'to retreat', Gr. πέδον 'ground, earth', Arm. het 'footprint', OPr. pedan 'ploughshare', Lith. peda, (dial.) pidas 'footprint', Latv.pfi/o 'foot-sole, footstep' < *>pedrO-; OIc. fet 'step'.

<!-- pdf-page: 445; printed-page: 431; running-header: opto                                       431 -->



### U. *pedom
U. *pedom 'ground* can be interpreted as *pedo- [n.], nominalization of. *pedH>- 5 stepped, trodden', or *pedro- [n.], with accent retraction of the adj. *pedro-; thus Nussbaum, who assumes that the derivational basis was the verb *ped- 'to step' rather than the noun *ped^ 'foot'. Latin oppido might be explained as *'to the ground' > 'utterly' (Nussbaum). For the noun oppidumy the meanings 'barriers' (in a circus) and 'fort' are both attested in Naev, (the former one in a quotation from Varro). In connection with 'foot*, a meaning 'barriers' is easier to understand than 'fortification', so chances are that WH are right in assuming original *op-pedo- 'what is in front of the feet, obstructing the way'.

Bibl.: WH II: 214f, EM 463, IEW 790-792, Untermann 2000: 524f, LIV *ped-. → ob,pes



### ops, opis
ops, opis 'power, ability, resources' [f. i] (Andr.+; abl.sg. opidCIL 1.364, opi Varro)

Derivatives: Opis/Ops, gen. Opis 'a Roman goddess5 (Pl.+), cop(i)s, copis 'rich, wealthy' (Pl.+), cdpia 'abundance' (Pl.+); inops 'poor, powerless' (Pl.+), inopiosus 'destitute' (Pl.+); opulentus 'wealthy, rich' (Pl.+), opulentitas 'richness' (Pl.+), opulenter 'richly* (Pl.+); qfficium 'service, duty* (Pl.+)> officidsus 'dutiful, attentive5 (Afran.+); opitulare/t 'to help, bring relief (Andr.+), opitulus 'cult-title of Jupiter5 (Paul, ex F.)> opiparus 'sumptuous, rich' (Pl.+X opifex 'craftsman, inventor' (Pl.+), opificium 'the performance of constructive work5 (Varro+), opificlna 'performance5 (Pl.)- * *

Pit. *cp/-. . * , 5

PIE *h₃e/op-(i-) 'ability, force .

IE cognates: Hit. happina- 'rich', happinant- 'rich (person)' < *h₃ep-en-o-; Skt. apnas- 'possession, property' [n.], YAv. afnatfhant- 'rich in property5 < *h₃ep-nos-, Gr. δμπνη [fJ 'food, corn' < *h₃ep-n-h₂-? Lat qfficium < *opi-fak-io-. The existence of different derivatives in the IE branches, and also of an s-stem (see s.v. opus), may point to a PIE root noun *op-. Livingston (2004: 73-82) explains opulentus and the other adj. in -ulentus as dissimilated from *-owent-o→ on the model of the semantic proximity of adj. in -uus to those in -ulus.

Bibl.: WH I: 270, II: 204f., 215f., EM 459f, 463£, IEW 780, Schrijver 1991: 50, LIV 1 .*h₃ep-, --*\omniSi opimusy opus



### opto, -are
opto, -are 'to desire, pray for' [v. I] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: optid 'choice, option; junior officer* (Pl.+), optionatus* -us 'the rank of optio* (Cato+), optabilis 'desirable' (Andr.+); adopiare 'to associate, adopt5 (Pl.+), odoptaticius 'adopted' (Pl.+), exoptare 'to long for* (Pl.+), exoptabilis 'desirable' (?L+)9praeoptare 'to prefer' (Pl.+).

Pit. *opeje- 'to choose, grab'.

It. cognates: U. upetu [3s.ipvJI], upetuta [3p.ipv.II], opeter [ppp*, geasg.n.] 'to select'; O. ufteis [gen.sg.], uhftis [nom.pl.] '?' < *op-ti- 'help/wish*?

PIE ^op-eie- 'to choose, grab'.

IE cognates: Hit. epp-™ / app- 'to take, grab' (< PIE root pr. *hiep-/*hlp-), Hit. pai- I pi- 'to give5, CLuw., HLuw. piia-, Lye. pije- 'to give5, Skt. apay YAv. άρα 'has reached' (< IE pf. *hie4ii(o)p-); maybe Alb. (j)ep ogives', Is. (j)ap.

<!-- pdf-page: 446; printed-page: 432; running-header: 432                                     opulus -->

Vine 1999a: 520f. explains U. upetu from a PIE iter. *op-eie~; to the resulting pr. *ope-9 Latin could have made a ppp. *op/o-, from which optare was derived as a frequentative. The connection with opmor 'to suspect* is difiicult for semantic reasons; EM regard it with scepsis. A connection with op- as in apio, apfscor cto take' < *hip- would be possible if the latter indeed started from the meaning 'to reach, get'. U. upetu might then point to a caus. or iter. *op-eie- > *ope- 'make a choice'. A root *hip- is more attractive than *h₃p-, since there is no connotation of 'work* in opto and upetu. LIV and Meiser 2003 assume that a verb form opet fopetl is contained in the Duenos inscr. sequence noisiopetoitesiai, which is merely a guess. The original form of the hapax praedotiont in Festus, glosses with 'praeoptanf, is too uncertain to be taken into consideration; Vine 1999a: 522f. suggests that the original form may have been *praeponont 'they prefer'.

Bibl.: WH II: 217, EM 464, IEW 781, Schrijver 1991: 52, Untermann 2000: 210, 786, Meiser 2003: 65, LIV ?2.*h₃ep-. → aplscor, opmor

opulus 'kind of maple5 [f. o] (Varro+) Since Pliny assigns this tree name to the Mediolanenses 'Milanese', it might be a Celtic word.

### opus, -eris
opus, -eris 'work' [n. r] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: opera [f.] 'effort, activity' (Naev.+), opella 'little effort5 (Lucr.+), operarius 'labourer' (Pl>), operaria 'woman who hires out her services' (Pl.), operatus 'busy, occupied' (Lucil.+), operari 'to be at work' (Plin.+).

Pit. *opes- [n.] 'work'.

It. cognates: SPic. opesauom [inf.], U. osatu [3s,ipvJI], Pael. upsaseter [3s/p.ipfsb/ps.], O. upsannum, upjsannu [gdve,acc,sg.m.n.], upsannam, upsan(am) [gdve.acc.sg.f], οπσανω [gdve.acc.pl.n.]; Ο. upsed, upsed, SPic. opsut, ojpsuq [3s.pf.], O. uupsens, upsens, ουπσενς, Vest, osens [3p.pf], U. opsetfa est) , oseto (est) [3s.pf.ps.f-], O. upsatuh sent [3p.pf.ps.n.?] 'to erect': pr., ppp. *op-es-d-, denom. to *opas- 'work', pf. O.U. *5ps-, SPic. *ops-.

PIE *h₃ep-os, -es- [n.] 'work'.

IE cognates: Ski apas- [a] 'work, action', apas- [adj.] 'active, skilful, working*, Av. huuapah- 'doing good work, masterly*, OAv. hauuapar/ha- [n.] 'creativity*, MoP xub 'good*; OE efnen 'to perform' (< PGm. *abnjan\ OHG uoben 'to start to work, practice, worship'. Postgate 1899 has shown that operatus must be a derived adj. 'full of opera, absorbed in5 to opera, whereas operari "was back-formed to operatus more recently. This means that PSab. *opesa- 'to erect5 was an independent creation of Sabellic.

Bibl.: WH II: 2171, EM 465f, IEW 780, Schrijver 1991: 50, Untermann 2000: 801-803, LIV l.*h₃ep-. -* omnis, ops



### orbis
orbis 'disc, flat round object, circle' [m. i] (Cato+; abl.sg. orbi)

Derivatives: orbiculus 'small disc' (Cato+), orbiculatus name of a pear (Varro); orbita 'track made by a wheel; path of a heavenly body' (Var.-l-), orbitus 'circular5 (Varro); orbile 'rim' (Varro).

<!-- pdf-page: 447; printed-page: 433; running-header: ordior                                      433 -->

Pit. *orfi- 'wheel', *orfi/eta- 'wheel-like, disc'.

It. cognates: U. urfeta [accsg.] '?' (an object which the priest holds in his hands).

PIE *h₃orbʰ-i- 'turning thing'.

IE cognates: Hit. harp-HQ(ri) / harp-21 'to change allegiance, join' < *hserbʰ-to, {G{S)harpa/i- [a] 'pile, heap'; Gr. άρφύτνον 'disk (in Lydian) (Weiss 2006: 261); ToAB yerpe 'disc / surface / image' (vel sim. = Skt. bimba) < *h₃erbV. The etymology of orbis is much disputed, see Untermann 2000. Klingenschmitt 1980: 214ff. reconstructs *h₂(o)r-dhhi-i-., which he interprets as 'where the spokes (of a wheel) are attached' = 'felly' to Skt ara- 'spoke of a wheeP. While formally possible, this explanation lacks formal matches in other IE languages, and is therefore less attractive. Driessen 2001 posits *hjorbʰ-(i-) for Latin, with shortening by Osthoff s law. But it may also be *hjorbʰ-i-y if the Tocharian long vowel is secondary. Driessen rejects the connection with urbs. This solution is developed by Weiss 2006. He assumes that orbis derives from

PIE *h₃erbʰ- 'to turn' (*h₂erbʰ- cannot be completely excluded), a meaning he arrives at by comparing the Hit. verb harp- 'to change allegiance, join'. Latin orbita and U. urfeta can go back to the same Pit. preform *orfi/eta-, with vowel restoration in the second syllable in Umbrian. Weiss 2006: 252f., 261 analyzes this as a fern, of an original f-stem *orfi-t- 'wheel-like (one)', which in its turn was derived from an /-stem noun *orbʰ-i- 'turning thing, wheel' from which orbis can be. directly derived.

Bibl.: WH II: 219, EM 466,tqntermann 2000: 805, Klingenschmitt 1980: 214ff., Driessen 2001: 53f., 57f, Weiss 2006. -♦ urbs -



### orbus
orbus 'deprived of, childless, orphaned' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: orbitas 'childlessness' (Pl.+), orbitudo 'bereavement' (Pac.+), orbare 'to deprive of, rob' (Pac.+); orbificare 'to bereave of children' (Acc.+).

Pit. *orfo- 'heir, orphan'.

PIE *h₃orbʰ-o- 'turning, what/who is turned over' > 'inheritance / heir'.

IE cognates: Olr. orb [m.] 'heir; inheritance' < *orbo-y orb{b)e, orpe [n.] 'inheritance' < *orbiio-, Hit. harp-"a(ri) , harp-1 'to change allegiance, join' < *h₃erbʰ-to [med.], harpa/i- 'pile, mound', Gr. ορφανός 'orphaned', όρφοβόται 'orphan's guardian' (Hsch.), ώρφωσεν 'made into an orphan' (Hsch.), Arm. orb, -oy 'orphan', OCS rabt, ORu. robi>, Cz. rob 'servant, slave' [m.] < *orbo-y OIc. arfr 'heir' < *arbaz, Olc. αφ, OSwe. arve 'heir' < *arban-y Go. arbi, ORG arbi, OS erbi [n.] 'inheritance5 < *arbija-, Go. arbija, OHG arpeo, OE irfa 'heir' < *arbijan- 'having the inheritance'. According to Weiss, the root *h₃rbʰ- 'to turn' here had the specific meaning 'to change membership from one social class to another'. Armenian and Greek can have *h₃rbʰ-, *h₃orbʰ- or *Horbʰ-, the other languages *h₃orbʰ- or *Horbʰ-.

Bibl.: WH II: 219f., EM 466, IEW 781 f., Schrijver 1991: 52, Weiss 2006.



### Ordior, -in
Ordior, -in 'to lay the warp (of a web); begin to speak or write; begin' [v. Ill; pf. orsus sum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: exordior 'to lay the warp; begin" (Pl.+), exordium 'the warp set up on a loom before the web is started; the beginning' (Enn.+)5 redordior 'to unweave'

<!-- pdf-page: 448; printed-page: 434; running-header: 434                                        ordo -->

(Plin.), pnmordium 'beginnings, source' (Pac.4-) (ordia prima Lucr.), orsa, -drum 'undertakings; words' (Verg.+); drsus, -us 'web; beginning' (Cic.+).

Pit. *ardA- 'row, arrangement1. f

PIE *h₂or-d-? Long initial o- must be secondary (see s.v. ordo). The original meaning seems 'to be put in a certain order" (medium tantum), especially as a weaving term, of the threads which are laid in a pattern. The verb can be interpreted as a denominative to a stem *ordi-, or as a derived present *ord-ie-. Lat. exordium must be derived from exordirl, cf. Leumann 1977: 294, unless there was an original stem *ord-, from which exordium and ordo were independently derived. The forms drsa and drsus are recent derivatives from the ppp. For further etymology, see ordo,

Bibl.: WH I: 221, EM 467, IEW 55-61, Leumann 1977: 294. → ordo



### ōrdō 'row, line, rank; series, pattern, routine'
6rd8 Vow, line, rank; series, pattern, routine' [m. n] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: ordxnare 'to place in rows, arrange' (Cic.+), ordinarius 'regular, normal1 (Cato apud Fest., Liv.+); ornare 'to prepare, equip, adorn' (Pl.+), drnatus 'well-equipped' (Ter.+), drnatus, -us 'equipment, adornment' (Pl.+), dmamentum 'equipment, ornament' (Andr.+); addrnare 'to prepare (tr./intr.); adorn* (Pl.+)> exornare 'to equip, prepare; adorn' (Pl.+), subdrnare 'to supply, equip1 (Cic.+).

Pit. *ord^n- 'row, order'.

PIE *h₂or-d-? The lenght of the initial 6- is probably secondary, as in other words with *VrC-\ cf Leumann 1977: 114. The original denom. to ordo was ornare < *drd-n~are < *orde/on-a- (cf. type nominare); this has retained the specialized meaning of 'to adorn'. The more recently attested drdinare was made as a denominative to ordo when the link between the latter and ornare had become opaque; it accordingly has the more general meaning 'to put into order'. Semantically, a connection with

PIE *h₂er- as in Lat. artus 'joint, limb' is attractive; thus also IEW. This would imply a reconstruction ^or-d-, with a ^-extension which is not otherwise attested for this root The ograde might then stem from the original perfect. Pokomy considers a caus. *ord^eio-, but this should yield *ordere.

Bibl.: WH II: 222-223, EM 467-469, IEW 55-61, Leumann 1977: 114, Sihler 1995: 76 → ordior



### orior, -Irt
orior, -Irt 'to appear above the horizon, rise' [v. IV; pr. oritur, ppp. ortum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ongd, -inis [f.] 'beginning, source' (Cato+), aborigines, -urn [pi.] 'race of pre-Roman people' (Cato-f); aborln 'to pass away, be aborted' (Varro+), abortid 'miscarriage' (Pl.+), abortus, -us 'failure to fertilize, miscarriage' (Lucr.+), abortare 'to cast its young' (Varro), abonscere 'to fade away' (Lucr.+), adonri 'to attack, assail' (Naev.+), coonn 'to spring forth, be born' (Pl.+), exorm 'to appear, arise' (Pl.+), exortvs, ~us 'rising, emergence' (Pac.+), oborm cto rise up, occur' (Pl.+X subonri 'to come into being' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *or~i- 'to rise', *orto- 'risen \

It. cognates: U, ortom est, orto est [3s.pf.], urtu fefure [3s.fut.pf.?], urtas [ptc.pf, nom.pl.f], urtes [ptc.pf., abl.pLf] 'to arise, occur'

<!-- pdf-page: 449; printed-page: 435; running-header: oro                                         435 -->

< *orto-, U. urnasier [abl.pl.], urnasiaru [gen.pl.f.] name of an early spring month < < *orn-dsio~ to a stem *f-no- * shoot' (M. Weiss, p.c.).

PIE *h₃r-i- 'to rise'.

IE cognates: Hit. arai- / ari- 'to (a)rise, to lift; raise', CLuw. ari(ia)- 'to raise', Lye. erije- 'to raise, levy' <

PIE *h₃r-oi- / *hsr-i-y Hit. hardu- [n.], CLuw, hardu- * brood, descendance' < *h₃er-tu-; Skt. iyarti [3s.pr.act-], irte [3s.pr.med.] (< Pllr. *Hi-Har-y *Hi-Hr-\ pioti [3s.pr.actJ, aor. (ud) arta [3s.med.], (sani) arata [3p.med.], pf. (vy) ara [3s.act.] t o set in motion, move', OAv. pr. zra-, aor. (uz)dr?$uua [2s.ipv.med.], YAv. Brznao °*to set in motion'; Gr. όρνυμι 'to urge, incite' (< *h₃r-n(e)u-), ώρτο [3s.aor.medJ 'to rise, rush on\ Arm. y-afnem cto rise'. The length of the stem vowel is fluctuating: onns adoritur but oritur. Meiser interprets this in such a way that adoritur has a regular Sievers variant *-ye- > *-ije- after two syllables, whereas oritur has retained *-ye-. Yet even *-Je- would be expected to yield -f-, as Schrijver 2003 objects. Hence, the original stem may have been *or-i- / *or-ei-, he argues. This is confirmed by the Hittite paradigm, which, according to Kloekhorst 2006a: 115 and 2008: 200, reflects an /-pr. *h₃r-oi-/*h₃r-k It cannot be decided whether orior and ortus continue

PIE *h₃r- or *h₃er-, but the former is more likely for morphological reasons.

Bibl.: WH II: 222f., EM 468, IEW 326-332, Schrijver 1991: 69, Meiser 1998: 63, 195, Untermann 2000: 806-808, LIV *h₃er-.

ornus 'kind of ash-tree' [f. o] (Verg.+) -;

Pit. *osVnor. , ' ?

PIE *Hh₃-o/es-.

IE cognates: Olr. (h)uinnius 'ash' < *onnistu-y MW coll. onn, sing. ormeny OCo. onnen, Bret coll. onny ounn 'ash-tree' < *osna/-os; OPr. woasis (< *oy/-j), Lith. uosis [m.], Latv, uosis [m.], Ru. jasen', Cz.jasan, SCr. jasen 'ash-tree' < BSl. *o?s-en- (> Slav.), *o?s-i-o- (> Bait.) <

PIE *Heh₃-s-; Arm. hac'i 'ash-tree', Alb. ah Lbeech' [m.], OIc. askr, OHG asc cash-tree' < *Hh₃-o/es-ko-, Gr. όξυα 'beech 7 « *osk-. The vowel difference between BSI. and the other languages is understandable if BSI. has the full grade of a root *Hh₃-, followed by *-s-, whereas the other languages have the zero grade followed by *-e/os-. This would imply that the original noun was an s-stem, Latin has suffixed *-Vno- (as in other tree names, cf. fraxinus) whereas Celtic added *-no~.

Bibl.: WH II: 223, WH 469, IEW 782, Schrijver 1991: 77.



### ōrō, -āre
6r6, -are 'to pray to, beseech' [v. I] (Lex XII, Andr.+)

Derivatives: oraculum 'divine utterance, oracle' (Pl.+), oratio 'speech, utterance* (Naev.+), orator 'envoy, public speaker' (Naev>), oratrvc 'female suppliant' (Pl.+), oratus, -Us 'request' (Pl.+); adordre cto plead with, address, pray to' (Lex XII+), exorare 'to persuade' (Pl.+), exordbula, -drum 'means of winning over' (Pl.+), exorator 'succesful suppliant' (Ter.), perorare 'to plead, argue' (Lex XIH-), indratus 'not pleaded' (Enn.+), inexorabilis 'relentless' (Ter.+).

Pit. *as- 'mouth'.

It. cognates: maybe O. urust [3s.fUt.pf.] 'to prosecute (orally)* < *or-us-t.

<!-- pdf-page: 450; printed-page: 436; running-header: 436                                       os -->

The chronology of the attestations shows that 'to plead, speak openly' is the original meaning of orare; see also Gavoille 2001 on the difference between orare and dicere in OLat. A denominative verb to ds is deemed unlikely by WH and EM, but I fail to see why: English has a verb 'to mouth\ aid -are is the productive suffix also after root nouns; thus also Untermann 2000: 809. The alternative etymology, which is supported by LIV, seems very unlikely to me: a connection with Skt. a-aryanti 'they acknowledge' and Ru. oral' 'to shout', since nothing suggests a meaning cto shout' for the Latin verb, nor does it seem onomatopoeic. If orare is from *osa-7 it would have to be separated from O. urust Rix (1993: 331-335) tries to derive both from a root *h₂er- 'to speak solemnly', (λ from a reduplicated perfect and Latin from a root noun *h₂or-; this solution is adopted by LIV. It is possible within Italic, but the outer-Italic evidence for a root *h₂er- "to speak solemnly' is very weak.

Bibl.: WH II: 224, EM 469, IEW 781, Untermann 2000: 809, LIV ?3.*h₂er-. → os



### ōs, ōris
5s, Oris 'mouth' [n. r] ( Andr.+)

Derivatives: oreae [f.pl.] 'mouthpiece, bit' (Naev.+), osculum 'kiss' (Pl.+), osculdri k to kiss' (Pl.+), osculentia 'kissing' (Pl.), deosculari 'to kiss warmly' (Pl.+), Ostia 'port at the mouth of the Tiber' (Enn.+), ostium 'door, entrance; aperture, mouth' (Pl.+), dstiorius 'janitor' (Varro+), oscitare/l cto gape, yawn' (Pl.+).

Pit. *os-.

PIE *h₃eh r os, -es- [n.] 'mouth \

IE cognates: Olr. ά 'mouth', Hit. ais / iss- [n.J 'mouth' (< *h₃eh r os, gen. *h₃hrs-os?), CLuw. aas- [n.] 'mouth', Skt. as- [n.] 'mouth \ as-ά [instr.sg.] 'with the mouth, before one's eyes, present, visible\ Av. ah-. Ostium presupposes an adj. *os-to- 'having a mouth/like a mouth' (cf. ius-tus, etc.), from which a n. noun in *-/o- was derived

Bibl.: WH I: 272, II: 224-228, EM 142, 469-471, IEW 784f., Schrijver 1991: 55, Stuber 2002: 194-197. → coram



### os, ossis
os, ossis 'bone' [n. ss] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: ossum 'bone' (Acc.+), ossua, -uurn [n.pl.j 'bones' (Pac.+), osseus 'of bone' (Pl.+), ossiculatirn 'bone by bone' (LuciL); ossifraga 'kind of vulture' (Lucr.+), exos 'boneless' (Lucr.+), exossare 'to remove the bones from' (Pl.+).

Pit. nom.acc. *ost, gen. *ostos*

It. cognates: Ven. ostinobos [dat.pl.] 'bones' < *ostino-« *ost-n-»

PIE *h₃o/est- [n.] 'bone'.

IE cognates: MW ascurn, ascwrn, OCo. ascorn 'bone' < *ast-k-y MW assert [L], OCo. asen, MIr. asna 'rib' < *ast¥n- (appurtenance uncertain, see below); Hit hastai I hasti- [n.] 'bone(s), strength', CLuw. has- 'bone' < *h₃esth₁-oi3 -i-; Skt. asthi- [n.] 'bone' (ins.pl. asthabhis, gen.sg. asthnas, gen.pL asnam < *asihnam\ asthanvant- 'having bones', OAv. astsntat- [f.] cmaterialness\ YAv. ast- 'bone, body with bones' (gen,sg. asto, astascay nom.pl. asti, gen.pl. astqm, instr.pl. azdabts-ca) , A v. astuuant- 'having a body with bones', Sogd. 'stk- 'bone' < Hr. *ast-H/n-; Gr. όστέον [n.] 'bone', Arm. oskr < *ostr-zi-; OAlb, ashte [a] 'bone* < *ost-. According to the theories of Steinbauer and Schrijver, the nom.sg. *osta < *HostH

<!-- pdf-page: 451; printed-page: 437; running-header: ovis                                     437 -->

was replaced by *ost to avoid homonymy with the nom.acc,pl. *osta, and the new form *ost was furnished with an extra *-$.

Pit. *osts, *ostos would then have yielded *os5, *ostos, which was remodelled to *oss, *ossos. Yet the introduction of an ending *-s into a nom.acasg. neuter is hardly credible: a n, paradigm *ost, gen, *ostos would have been fine, and the ptc. ferens is not a good comparandum, since it stands within a paradigm with m, and £ Thus, I wonder whether oss directly reflects the nom.sg, *ost of the PIE root noun; the sequence -ss- would then have been introduced into the other case-forms. The stem ossum may be a secondary ostem *osso-y or it hides a w-stem *ossu-, which in any case we need for Pac, ossuwn. The w-stem can be due to analogy with artua 'limbs' or maybe cornua 'horns'. Armenian o- can be from *h₂o- or *h₃(o)-. Only in the former case can the Celtic forms be cognate, viz. from *h₂e- (Schrijver 1995: 53), This is surprising, since o- seems to be prevailing in the other languages (although we cannot tell for Ilr,). An ablaut *h₂est-/*h₂ost- would point to a root noun, and the suffixes *-h₂- (also *-i-?) and *-«- would originally be different extensions. The suffix *-w- seems to be attested in Ilr., Celtic and Venetic, If the hypothetical PIE root noun was an aerostatic n. noun one may reconstruct nom,acc. ♦h^st, gen, *h₂est-s >

Pit. *osty *ass, from which with leveling maybe Lat. oss may be explained. This is very speculative, of course.

Bibl.: WH II: 225f, EM 470, Lejeune 1974: 337, IEW 783, Leumann 1977: 452, Steinbauer 1989: 236f., Schrijver 1991: 50, 80f,, 110f., 1995: 53, Kiingenschmitt 1992: 124, J- otium 'spare time, relaxation' [n. d] (Pl.+) ·

Derivatives: otiosus 'at leisure, inactive' (Pl.+); negotium 'work, business, difficulty' (PI,+), negotiolum 'little business, slight difficulty' (Pl.+), negotiosus 'occupied, busy' (Pl.+), For otium^ an etymology involving *au- is unattractive, since there is no evidence for *au-. Benveniste 1951 argues that negotium must have been a nominal cp, *nec-otiwn from the start, with the meaning 'non-loisir% that is, 'obstacle, empechement'. It would have been a Latin caique on Gr. ά-σχολία 'absence of spare time' > 'occupation'. This leaves unanswered the question of why the Romans not translate the Gr, word as *in-otium. The word remains without etymology.

Bibl.: WH II: 157,228f, EM 436,471-

### awistr, OHG ewist
awistr, OHG ewist 'sheepfold' < *oww///s ToB awi [nom.plf.] 'ewe\ Ovis can reflect

Pit. *owis under the assumption put forward by Vine 2006a that stressed *ow- did not undergo unrounding to *aw-. The appurtenance of aububulcus is extremely uncertain according to Schrijver (1991: 439), but Vine (2006: 233) adopts the view that the original gloss may have run aubulcus 'pastor ouium', in which case au- could reflect pretonic *owi- 'sheep' under Vine's formulation of Thumeysen-Havet's Law; see also Lindner 2002: 218L Some scholars assume initial *h₂o-, ignoring or explaining away Armenian ho-. See Pinault 1997: 19Iff for arguments against *h₃e- and in favour of *h₂e- (Toch. *<5-). The only alternative to *h₃e- would be a static paradigm *h₂oui- I ^h^ui-y while assuming that Arm. and Anat have the ovocalism of the former variant, and the h- of the latter; this seems less plausible.

Bibl.: WH II: 229, EM 47l£, IEW 784, Schrijver 1991: 50, 439, 449-454, Sihler 1995: 178, Meiser 1998: 56, Untermann 2000: 818, Kloekhorst 2006b: 92f. → opilio



### ovo, -are
ovo, -are 'to celebrate a minor triumph, rejoice' [v, I] (Pl.+)

Pit. *ow5-. The Gr. verb εύάζω 'to cry for joy5 is probably denominal to cries such as εΰα (Η.), εύαί (-αϊ) (Ar.), εύάν (Ε.)- Yet the Latin word cannot be a borrowing, since it requires the Pit. change of *eu > *ou.

Bibl.: WH Π: 229f.~EM 472, IEW 347, Schrijver 1991: 37,449.



### ovum
ovum 'egg' [n. o] (Enn.+)

Pit. *6wQ)om»

PIE *h₂ouiom 'egg'.

IE cognates: OW i/i, MW wy [m.], OCo. uy, Co. oyy Bret uy> vi 'egg' < PCI. *auio-; Av. aim (acc.sg.), Khot. ahaa-y MP xayag, Khwar. y'k < PIr. *auia(-ka)-\ Gn φόν (Ion.-Att), ώόν (Hell.), ώΐον (Sappho); Arm./ow, gen./ovwy < *ioio- « *<5ro-; VoJajo\jaje (obs.), SCr Joje 'egg' < PS1. *aje9 OCS ajce, Ru. jajco, Cz. vejce 'egg' < PSL *ajbce; Alb. ve, voe; Crimean Go. aday OIc. egg, OHG ei9 OE &g 'egg' < ¥Gm.*ajjaz-. Schindler 1969 reconstructs *o-h₂uiom, with a preposition *a Yet apart from Ilr. *a toward', such a preposition is unknown, and its existence in PIE is doubtful Moreover, the meaning 'which is near the bird' is not very convincing for an 'egg': it is actually 'in' the bird, or, when it is breeding, 'under' it. I prefer the explanation of ovum as a vrddhi-derivative of 'bird', hence 'which belongs to a bird'. Morphologically, this would imply lengthening of an o-grade of 'bird\ for which - admittedly - there is no evidence: *h₂eu-i- 'bird' > *h₂ou-i-o- 'egg\ On the other hand, it is not unlikely that an earlier ablaut *e/o/zero in 'bird5 would have disappeared, leaving only e/zero.

Bibl.: WH II: 230, EM 472, IEW 783f„ Schindler 1969, Schrijver 1991: 30, 126, 300. → avis

<!-- pdf-page: 453; printed-page: 439; running-header: palam                                     439 -->

## Ρ



### paedor
paedor 'dirt, filth' [m. r] (Acc.+) Etymology unknown.

Bibl.: WH II: 233, EM 474.



### paelex, -icis
paelex, -icis 'mistress' [f. k] (Pl.+; also pelex, pellex) Usually compared with Olr. oirech 'a type of concubine*, Gr. παλλακή 'concubine', πάλλαξ 'young woman', Av. pairika- 'witch \ But Irish has -r-, Av. has *parika-, and Gr. has a and fl, none of which match the Latin form. Within Latin, it seems more straightforward to derive paelexfrom*paed-Vk-s,topaedor 'dirt\ But even this is only a guess which cannot be substantiated Levin 1983 regards paelex as a borrowing from a Mediterranean language, maybe Semitic, in view of Hebrew pigs lpi(y)leyesl 'concubine'.

Bibl.: WH II: 233f., EM 474, Leumann 1977: 69.



### paene
paene 'almost, practically' [adv.} (Pl.+)

Derivatives: paenitere (pf, rut)'to cause dissatisfaction, cause to regret" (Pl.+), paenitudd 'regret' (Pac.),pae/enuria 'shortage, wanf (Ter.+)- The basic meaning of the stem *paen- seems to be 'missing, lacking'. IEW connects paene with Skt. piyati 'scorns', which would fit if we posit *ph₂-i-; but the root is reconstructed as *ph r i- in LIV, which does not explain Latin -ae-. Also, the semantics do not match well. Neri (2007: 78f.) takes up a suggestion by Vine and proposes *p(e)-ai-ni- 'not entirely' < ^firom whom has been taken away' or *'who takes away', from a preverb *pe 'away' and a verb *hjai 'to give, take\ A PIE phoneme sequene *hiai- is in my view not possible, however, and the existence of a

PIE preverb *peys uncertain (see s.\.palor).

Bibl.: WH II: 234, EM 474, IEW 792f.



### palam
palam 'openly, publicly' [adv.; prep. + abl.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives:prdpalam 'openly, evident' (Pl.+).

Pit. *palam>

PIE *plh₂-em-i? The use of palam as a prep, postdates that of clam, Schrijver gives three possible etymologies: (1) an analogical ending -am was taken from clam; (2) a locative *plh}-em-i 'in the flat (hand)' to palma; this might have yielded *palemy however; or a loc. *plh₂-em > *p(a)lam; (3) an adverbialised acc.sg. of aft^-stemof the same root: *plhrehj-m > *palam > palam. Since palma and its cognates provide evidence for a

PIE w-stem, hypothesis (2) is slightly more likely.

Bibl.: WH II: 237, EM 475, IEW 805-807, Schrijver 1991:209f. → clam, palma, planus

<!-- pdf-page: 454; printed-page: 440; running-header: 440                                     palatum -->



### palatum
palatum 'roof of the mouth; dome, vault* [η. ό] (Varro+)

Derivatives: Palatium 'the Palatine' (Naevrl·-), Palatums 'Palatine' (Varro+), Palatua 'tutelary goddess of the Palatine' (Varro), Palatualis 'of Palatua' (Varro+).

Pit. *palato~.

PIE *plh₂-o/u- 'flat, wide'? Since the 'palate' can be referred to as a 'flattened' or Vaulted' part, and since hills are also often referred to as 'flat' or 'vaulted' (if their form so suggests), a derivation of Palatiumfrompalatum is quite conceivable. Palatum could be an adj. in *-5ή> to a stem *pal-(V~) 'flat, broad', e.g. *plh₂-o- or *plh₂-u-, cf. Hit. palhi- 'broad' < *plhri-.

Bibl.: WH II: 237, EM 4751 -^planus



### palea
palea 'chaff, husk' [f. a] (Cato+)

Derivatives: uncertain are palea 'the wattles of a cock' (Varro-»-), palear, -aris [n.] 'dewlap' (Varro+).

Pit. +pcdeja- [f.].

PIE *p(e)lhr 'chaff.

IE cognates: Skt. palava- 'chaff, husk' < *pelH-ou(-), OPr. pelwo, Lith. pelus, Latv. ρςlus [pi.] 'chaff, OCS plevy [nom.pl.f ], Ru, polova, SCr. pljeva 'chaff <

PIE *pelH-u-(eh₂); Gr. πάλλω 'to swing, sway' < *plh r n-, aor. πάλτο (αν-, κατ-έπάλτο), redupl. άμ-πεπαλών, πόλος [m.] '(shaken) lot'. Palea could be cognate with the stem *pelH-u- 'chaff found in Skt and BSl. Since 'chaff are the parts of the corn which are separated by threshing them off, these stems might result nouns derived from

PIE *pelh r 'to swing'.

Bibl.: WH II: 238, EM 476, IEW 802, Schrijver 1991: 210, 256f., Nussbaum 1997: 197.



### palla
palla 'mantle5 [f. a] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: pallula 'little mantle' (Pl.+), pallium 'mantle, garment' (Naev.+), palliatus 'wearing a pallium' (Pl.+), pallidum 'small pallium' (Pl>), palliolatim 'in/with a pallium' (Pl.+). No etymology. A PIE preform could for instance be *pHl-n/d/s/u-, or a secondary full grade a of a root *pelC- as in pellis* But it may well be a loanword.

Bibl.: WH II: 238f, EM 476, IEW 803f



### palleo
palleo 'to be pale' [v. II; plpallui\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: pallescere 'to grow pale, fade' (Acc.+), pallidas 'pale, dim' (Pl.+), pallor 'paleness' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *palwo- 'pale, grey'.

IE cognates: Lith. palvas 'light yellow, straw-coloured', RuCS plavb 'white', Ru. polovyj 'pale yellow, sandy', SCr. plav 'pale blue, with light hair' < BSL *polyo~, Olc./p/r, OHG falo 'faded' < PGm. *falwa-. Nussbaum 1997 assumes a suffix *-uo- on account of the BSl. and Gm. cognates; he explicitly separates *pallo- denoting a pale colour from the adj. *polio- (Latpullus)

<!-- pdf-page: 455; printed-page: 441; running-header: palpo                                     441 -->

denoting a dark one, BSL and Gm. suggest a preform *poluo-, but this would not explain Latin -a-. One might posit *ph₂el-, but that would be ad hoc. Alternatively, we are dealing with a loanword *paluo- 'pale, grey' into the European languages.

Bibl.: WH II: 239f., EM 476, IEW 804f, Nussbaum 1997: 191. →pul!us



### palma
palma 'front part of the hand; palm-tree' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: palmuL· 'palm; an oar' (Varro+), palmm 'width of a palm' (Cato+), palmaris [adj.] 'the width of a palm' (Varro+), palmarhim 'master-stroke' (Ten), palmipedalis 'measuring a palm and a foot1 (Varro+).

Pit. *palama-.

PIE *plh₂-em-h₂- 'flat, wide\

IE cognates: Olr. lam, W. Haw < PCL *<plama-, Gr. παλάμη 'palm of the hand', OHG folma 'hand', OE folm 'flat hand\ The analysis of the root as *plh₂- and the vocalization in Latin and Greek, which does not fit the regular outcome of *plHC- in these languages, suggest an m-stem with full grade of the suffix in Lat and Gr, Others have assumed that palma was borrowed from Greek, but this does not explain Lat. palmus, nor can Gr. -αλα- be explained from *CIHC-. Admittedly, the fact that four branches show a h₂-stem but seem to have different ablaut of the primary suffix (*-m- in Celtic and Germ., *-em- in Lat. and Gn), and especially the separation of Celtic and Italic, are embarrassing.

Bibl.: WH II: 240f, EM 476f, IEW 805-807, Schrijver 1991: 210, Sihler 1995: 96, 111, Nussbaum 1997: 186. → palam



### palor, -art
palor, -art 'to wander, stray' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: dispalarl'to stray off (Sis.+), dispalescere 'to be spread about' (Pl.).

Pit. *palaje/o-* The stem pala- 'to wander' is explained from *pand-slo- by WH, but Weiss 1993: 53-56 rightly objects that it is somewhat complicated to arrive from an instrument noun 'spreading, spreader1 at a verb 'to wander'; and, furthermore, that one would expect to find other traces of the alleged noun *palo-. Weiss proposes the alternative etymology *pe-h₂lh₂- 'to wander off; this would semantically be perfect, and links up with the existence of amb-ulare. However, the existence of a PIE preverb *pe is uncertain: the only independent witness for this form would be Hit pe 'away, thither', which rather reflects *(hi)poi, cf. Kloekhorst 2008: 660.

Bibl.: WH II: 241, EM 477, LIV *h₂elh₂-.



### palpo, -are 'to stroke, soothe'
palpo, -are 'to stroke, soothe' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: palpus 'front or palm of the hand' (Pl.+), palpitare 'to beat, pulsate' (Cic.+), palpatio 'caress' (Pl.), palpator 'cajoler' (Pl.), palpebra 'eyelid; pL eyelashes' (Cd&Q\\.+)\ suppalpari'\o wheedle' (Pl.). No etymology. PIE origin is not easy for a sequence palp-. To explain the second vowel of palpebra and palpitare, Serbat 1975: 107 posits a verb *palpere 'to move repeatedly'. If correct, we could reconstruct a noun *palpo- 'front of the hand' and a verb *palp-e/o- 'to move' at an earlier stage.

Bibl.: WH II: 241 f., EM 477, IEW 798-801

<!-- pdf-page: 456; printed-page: 442; running-header: 442                                    palumbes -->



### palumbes, -is
palumbes, -is 'wood-pigeon' [m., f. i] (Pl.+;palianbus Cato+) Gr. πέλεια, πελειάς 'wild pigeon' to πελι- 'grey' and OPr. poalis 'pigeon' < *po//- 'grey' render it conceivable that palumbes was named after its colour (see palled), with the same suffix as in colwnba. Of course, we cannot be certain.

Bibl.: WH II: 242, EM 478, IEW 804f. → columba



### palus, paliidis
palus, paliidis 'fen, swamp' [f. d] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: paliister [adj.] 'marshy, fenny' (Sis.+).

Pit. *paludr.

IE cognates: Skt. palvala- [n.] 'pond, pool'; OVr.pelky 'marsh', Lith.peZfre 'marsh, Zem. puddle', Latv.pelce 'puddle' < *pelHk-iH-h₂. If the Baltic words for 'swamp' are cognate, Latin palus could be connected with pall- 'grey, pale'. However, Schrijver rightly objects that the semantic connection between 'grey' and 'swamp' is uncompelling.

Bibl.: WH II: 243, EM 478, IEW 798-801, Schrijver 1991: 210f.



### pandu, -ere
pandu, -ere 'to spread out, extend' [v. Ill; ppp. possum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: pandiculan 'to contort one's face in a grimace' (Pl.+), passus 'extended, free; dry, wrinkled' (Naev.+), passim [adv.] 'here and there, indiscriminately' (Pl.+), possum 'raisin-wine' (Pl.+), passus, -us 'step, pace' (Pl.+), pandus 'arched, bowed' (Enn.+), Panda 'a Roman goddess' (Varro+); dispandere 'to open out' [PL dispennite, ppp. dispessum] (Pl.+), expandere 'to spread out* (Caecil.+), praepondere 'to spread in front, reveal' (Laev.+), repandus 'flattened back' (Lucil.+), repandirdstrus 'having aflattenedsnout' (Pac).

Pit. *pand-n-, *pat<-.

It. cognates: O. patanai [dat.sg.] '?', name of a deity; O. patensins [3p.ipf.sb.] 'to open' < *pt-n-s~; U. padellor [gen.sg.] name of a deity, 'Patella'.

PIE *pt-n(e)-h₂- [pr.] Ho spread'.

IE cognates: Gr. πΐτνημι "to spread out, open', aor. έπέτασ(σ)α. I assume that the PIE nasal pr. yielded (pre-)Pit. *pndrn-, with vocalization to *pandn- according to the observations in Schrijver 1991: 486-504. The adj. pandus seems to be a novel creation on the basis QI pandere. A dim. *pandiculum 'little bending > grimace' is behind pandicularL Outside the present, *pf- acquired the secondary full grade *pat^ which is attested in the stative pateo (see s.v.) and the ppp. passus < *patto-, and which was reintroduced in PL dispennite < *dis-pafrn-, and 0. patensins (Kortlandt 1999: 248).

Bibl.: WH II: 241, 244f., EM 477f, IEW 788, 824£, Schrijver 1991: 332, 498-504, Meiser 1993: 263, 1998: 122, Untermann 2000: 516-518, LIV l.*peth₂-. ^pateo



### pangō, -ere
pangō, -ere 'to insert firmly, fix' [v. Ill; pf. pepigi, ppp. pactum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: (1) compingere 'to shut up,fix,build' (Pl.+), depangere 'to drive down' (Varro+), impingere 'to fix on, strike against' (Pl.+), suppingere 'to attach below' (Pl.+), antepagmentum 'the facing of a door- or window-frame' (Cato+); (2) compages, -is [f.] 'framework, joint' (Pac.+), propages [f.] 'which continues' (Pac.+),

<!-- pdf-page: 457; printed-page: 443; running-header: pannus                                     443 -->



### pwpagmen
pwpagmen 'prolongation' (Ena)3 propagare 'to reproduce, prolong' (Cato+), propagatio 'reproduction' (Cato+), propago, -inis Offspring, space for planting' (Cato+), repagula, -drum [n.pL] 'door-bars' (Pl.+); (3) pagus 'country district or community' (Pl.+), paganicus Of the village people' (Varro), paganalia [pi.] 'the village festival of the pagC (Varro); (4) pagina 'column or page of writing' (Cic.+); (5) pah 'long-handed spade' (Pl.+), palus 'wooden post, stake, peg' (Pl.+), tripalis 'having three stakes' (Varro), paxillus 'wooden pin' (Varro+).

Pit. *pang-, *pagto- 'to attach'; *pag-o- 'district', *pag-e- 'joint', *pag-e/on~ 'space, column', *pag-slo- 'spade, pole'.

It. cognates: O. prupukid [ablsg.] '?' < *pro-pak-iom 'previous agreement'?

PIE *ph₂-n-g- [pr.] 'to attach', *ph₂g-to- [ppp.]> *peh₂g-os [n.].

IE cognates: Skt. pojra- 'solid, firm', pqjas- [a] 'surface, face', YAv. pazatfhant- [adj.] (meaning unknown), Khot. paysa- 'surface', Gr. ττήγνυμι 'to attach, to join', Gr. εύ-πηγής Veil-built'. The nominal forms -pages, pagus, pagina, -pagin- are derived from a full grade *pag-, whereas the verb forms reflect *pag-. The nouns palus and pala are derived from an inst. noun *pag-slo-*

Bibl.: WH II: 235f, 245f., 371, EM 474f., 479, IEW 787f., Schrijver 1991: 97, Untermann 2000: 587, Stiiber 2002: 133L, LIV *peh₂g-. -^pax.pignus > pirns '(loaf of) bread' [m. i] (Pli)

Derivatives: panarium 'bread-basket, bread-store' £Varro+), pastillum 'kind of sacrificial cake' (Fest), pastillus 'pastille' (Hor.+)> panici [pL] 'bakers' (Pl.), panicum 'Italian millet' (Cato+); panificium 'the making of bread' (Varro+).

Pit. *past-ni- 'loaf, cake', *pastlelo- or *pastnelo- 'small cake'. The connection with pastilluSy -m suggests that partis derives from *past-ni-. The latter is connected to the root *peh₂-s- 'to graze' (Lat. ppp.pasium) by scholars from WH to Schrijver, although only hesitantly by the latter. In fact, I do not see how the change from 'graze' to 'bread' can be made with only the suffix *-w/-. The meaning 'cake, pastille' hi pastillus/m suggests that the meaning 'loaf may be older than 'bread', but this is of course uncertain. I have no alternative etymology to offer. EIEC 383 connects panicum with Iranian *pana- 'millet' as in Shughni pinj> Yazghulami xar-ban, but this does not explain the long vowel in Latin. Within Latin, panicum could be connected with pdnis 'bread', but 'millet' is not normally processed as bread. The appurtenance of panicum to panus seems less certain to me than to WH and EM.

Bibl.: WH II: 246f., EM 479, IEW 787, Leumann 1977: 209, Schrijver 1991: 144. -* panus



### pannus
pannus 'piece of cloth, rag' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

IE cognates: Gr. πηνος [a], ττήνη [f.] 'woven cloth' (Hsch.) < *pan-, Go. fana [m.] 'cloth, towel', OUGfano 'cloth', NHG Fahne, OE fana 'banner' < *fan-6n. If the Gr. and Gm. words listed are related, they probably represent loanwords from an unknown source. The vacillation between a and a, and Latin -a- and -nn-9 cannot

<!-- pdf-page: 458; printed-page: 444; running-header: 444                                          panus -->

be explained from a common PIE preform.

Bibl.: WH II: 247f., EM 479, IEW 788. t



### panus
panus 'spool with thread; abcess; main stalk of a panicle' [m, o] (Lucil+; Afran.+; Plia)

Derivatives: pam/ucula 'the feathery head of certain reeds and grasses, tuft; kind of wart' (Plin.+). In the meaning 'spool with thread', partus may well be a loanword from Doric Greek *πανος, cf Art. πηνη, πηνος 'yarn'. In the meaning 'abcess', partus is compared with Ru. puk 'bunch, bunder, OCS pQciti s§ 'be inflated', Ru. \puciV 'become swollen' < *ponHk~; a root *pnHk- is reconstructed from which Schiijver phonetically derives partus. This is conceivable, but it is just as likely that the word for 'spool' was metaphorically used for 'abcess', and certainly for 'tuft' of reed (panicula); cf the range of meanings of German ΚΙοβ.

Bibl.: WH II: 248, EM 480, IEW 789, Schrijver 1991: 491.



### papiliō
papiliO 'moth, butterfly' [m, n] (Ov.+)

Pit. *pV(l)pVl·.

IE cognates: OPr.penpalo 'quail', OPr.pepelis, \pV\pippalins 'bird', Lith.piepala* Latv. paipala, Ru. perepel, Cz. prepel, kfepel 'quail', OIc. fifrildi, OEfifealde,OHG fifaltra, MHGfifalier 'butterfly7 < VGm.*fifalor6n*. Pa-pilid can reflect reduplication of a root *pl- 'to fly, flutter', which has also served to build the word for 'quail' in BSL and 'butterfly' in Gm, It seems unlikely that this root */>/- is a very early variant of PIE roots such as *pleu- 'to swim, wander', *plehr 'to swim, float', *pelhr 'to swing*.

Bibl.: WH II: 249f., EM 480, IEW 798-801.



### pSr, paris
pSr, paris 'equal, matching' [adj. /] (Pl.+; sup. parisuma Elog.Scip.)

Derivatives: parilis 'equal, similar' (Lucil.+); compar 'fellow, similar, equal' (Pl.+), comparare 'to align, match, evaluate' (Pl.+), dispar 'unequal, different' (Acc.+), disparare 'to be different' (Pl.+), disparilis 'different' (Varro+), disparilitas 'difference' (Varro+), impar 'unequal' (Cato+); aequiperare 'to compare, become equal' (Pl.+), aequiperabilis 'comparable' (Pl.+).

Pit. *parVsl *pas-i-l

It. cognates: U. pars (est) [nom.sg.] '(it is) prescribed' < *pares or *paros. The origin of a in par is disputed: WH regard it as an ancient lengthened grade, whereas Leumann and e.g. Untermann explain it from *parVs > *pars > *parr. In view of U. pars, the latter seems more attractive, but it is strange that a similar lengthening cannot be observed e.g. in ter 'thrice' < *ters. Sab. *parVs may stem from

PIE *prH-V-, but no good etymology is available. WH connect *perh₂- 'to sell', but 'selling' does not mean 'matching'. Alternatively, Latin may be separated from U, pars and go back to ^pas-, *pas- <

PIE *pHs-; see pared for a possible candidate.

Bibl.: WH II: 250f., EM 481, Leumann 1977: 220, Untermann 2000: 514. → pared, parricjda

<!-- pdf-page: 459; printed-page: 445; running-header: pario                                      445 -->



### parcō, -ere
parcO, -ere 'to act sparingly, refrain from' [v. Ill; p£ peperci (parcuit, parsi)] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: parens 'economical, stingy* (Pl.+), parciter 'sparingly' (Pompon.+), perparce 'very stingily' (Ten), parsimdnia 'thrift' (Pl.+); compercere 'to refrain from; save up' (Pl.+), impercere cto spare' (Pl.), reparcere 'to be sparing with' (Pl.+); compescere 'to confine, restrain* (Pl.+), dispescere 'to divide' (Cato+).

Pit. *pe-arkre/o- [pr.].

PIE *h₂er(lc)"(e/oO *to hold'. LIV's connection of pared with Skt prk- 'to fill, mix with' is unconvincing semantically. Keller 1992: 164 (fh. 21) and Weiss 1993: 49-53 propose *pe-h₂erk- 'to hold ofF > *park- + -e/o > pared to the root of arced; Weiss suggests that the opaque *parke/o- was replaced by *po-ark- > porced in the meaning 'to hold off. Parsimdnia was built on the pf parsi9 which was more common in OLat than in OLat. The compounds in -pescd probably represent *-park-ske/o-,

Bibl.: WH I: 256f., II: 252, EM 482, IEW 820, Keller 1992: 162-165, Untermann 2000: 415f, 530f, Meiser 2003: 184, Neri 2007: 77f, LIV *Ιι2βΓ(£-. → arced



### pāreō
pSreO 'to submit, obey' [v. II; pf pdrui, ppp. paritum] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: paret 'appears, seems' (Cic.+); apparere 'to be visible, appear' (Naev.+), apparitor 'attendant\(Cato+), comparere 'to be seen, appear' (Pl.+)·

Pit. *pas-e-, > c

PIE *peh₂-s- [pr.] to watch, see*?-

IE cognates: Arm. hayim 'to see', Alb. aor. pashe 'Isaw\ Within Latin, it is possible to connect par: parere would then be a stative verb *pare~ or *pase- 'to be visible*, and maybe *par-i- 'visible, obvious' > 'seeming' > par 'equal' (cf German gleich). If the meaning of the root *pHr/s- was 'to see', the obvious connection is with

PIE *pHs- as in Albanian aor. pashe Ί saw', of which we would then be able to prove *h₂. For par, paris? an ablaut *peh₂s-/*ph₂s- would have to be assumed. The meaning of this preform would be so close to *peh₂-s- cto protect, observe', an ^-present to *peh₂- 'to protect', that one may consider their original identity. \

Bibl.: WH Π: 2521, EM 482, IEW 789, LIV ?*peHs-. → par,posed



### paries, -etis
paries, -etis 'wall' [m. /] (Lex XII+)

IE cognates: OIc. sparri [m.] 'pillar, beam', OHG sparro 'roof-beam, pole' < *$porH-en-l The alleged connection with Gm. words in spar^ 'beam' < *spor- is a mere paper reconstruction.

### pario, -ere
pario, -ere 'to give birth to, bear' [v. Ill; pf peperi, ppp. partuni] (VOLat+: Garigliano neipari [2s.ipv.act /pari/ or /parlf] cdo not appropriate', Lex XII, Andr.+)

Derivatives: (1) parens, -ntis [m.]t] 'parent, father or mother5 (Pl.+)> parentare 'to perform the rites at the tombs of the dead' (Varro+), parenticida [m.J 'parent-killer'

<!-- pdf-page: 460; printed-page: 446; running-header: 446                                      paro -->

{?\.\parentatio 'celebration of the family dead' (Cato+); partus, -us 'the giving birth, birth, progeny' (Pl.+), partio 'the giving birth' (Pl.+), partitudo 'the giving birth' (Pl.+), partura 'the giving birth' (Varro), parturire Ho be in labour, give birth' (Pl.+); (2) perJculum 'trial, danger' (Pl.+), penculari 'to try out, risk' (Cato), penclitarj 'to try out, risk; be in danger' (Pl.+), periculosus 'dangerous' (Cato+); peritus 'experienced, expert' (Pl.+), impentus 'unexperienced' (Pl.+); comperin 'to find out, discover' (Ter.+), experm (pf„ expertus sum) 'to put to the test, attempt' (Naev.+), experientia 'trial' (Varro+X experimentum 'test, trial' (Varro+), opperm 'to wait, await' (Pl.+X reperire cto recover, discover' (Pl.+) [pf> reppen, ppp, repertum\

Pit. *per(e)i-, *par-(e)i-, aor.ptc. *par-(e)nt- 'to bear;find,experience'; *per-ei-tlo- 'experience\

It. cognates: FaL pe;para[i] [ls.pf] ςΙ have procured'; U. amparitu [3s.ipv.IL], amparihmu [3s.ipv.ILps,] 'to erect?' <an- 'on' + *pare/i/-ie-,

PIE pr, *perh₃-i- 'to provide', *prh₃-ii-, aor. *p(e)rh₃-, pf. *pe-porh₃-e.

IE cognates: sees.v.^ra (1) Schrijver 1991 explains par- from antevocalic *prH-y which is more attractive than the older assumption of an umlaut *pera- > *para-. The old root aor. might be conserved in parens. The pr. parturire is one of the two oldest presents in -urire, mid may have been formed on the model of esunre (Risch 1954). (2) The semantic connection between parid 'to bear, produce' and -perjre/ϊ 'to find, experience' is explained by Schrijver 2003: 79 in the sense that 'to discover' can be interpreted as Ί bring about that sth. is produced or produces itself to me'; he therefore leaves open the possibility that these compounds contain the thematic suffix *-je/o-. One might also start from 'to bear', from which the deponent verbs meaning 'to experience' are easily understood (in which case thematization is not necessary). The identity of the pf. and ppp. also speaks in favour of the identification of these two verbs as one. The noun peritlum < *peri-tlo- can hardly have been built to the compound verbs, but must represent an older formation. Probably *perei-tlo- 'experience' > 'trial, danger', although, in theory, 1 in perxclum can also be due to a Sievers-like development from *perio-tlo-. But since Fcan be linked to the -i- in parid, the word family of parid and -perire/i provides support for the reconstruction of an *i/e/-present Of course, for the compounds in -penre/Iwe cannot be sure whether they contain *par- or *per-.

Bibl.: WH II: 255, 288f, EM 483f.f 498f, IEW 816-18, Giacomelli 1963: 252, Schrijver 1991: 211, Panagl-Lindner 1995: 173f., Sihler 1995: 538, Rix 1999: 525, Untermann 2000: 88, Schrijver 2003: 74-79, Meiser 2003: 185, LIV *perh₃-. -+paro, pars j pauper, pratum



### paro, -are "to furnish, provide'
paro, -are "to furnish, provide' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: (1) paratio 'obtaining' (Afran.+), paritare 'to prepare, arrange' (Pl.); apparare 'to prepare, provide' (Pl.+), comparare 'to prepare, buy, obtain' (Pl.+), disparare 'to divide' (Pl.+), imparatus 'not ready, unprepared', imperare 'to demand the production or payment of, order, command' (Naev.+), imperdtor 'who gives orders, ruler' (Pl.+), imperium 'supreme power, authority, dominion' (Pl.+), imperiosus 'commanding' (Pl.+), praeparare 'to furnish beforehand, prepare' (Varro+), separare 'to divide, separate' (Cato+); (2) properus 'quick' (Cato+),

<!-- pdf-page: 461; printed-page: 447; running-header: parnclda                                    447 -->

properiter 'quickly' (Pac.+), properare 'to incite; to hurry' (Pl.+), properatim 'hastily' (Caecil.+); puerpera 'woman in labour or who has delivered' (Pl.+), puerperium 'childbirth' (Pl.+), opiparus 'sumptuous, rich' (Pl.+).

Pit. *parqjefo-*

It. cognates: possibly Pael. empratois [dat.abl.pl.] '?' if to Lat imperare; O. embratur [nom.sg .] borrowed from Latin.

PIE *prh₃-o- providing',

IE cognates: Olr. ernaid*y -ern* 'to grant' « PCI. *<parna- (-e- from the sb.), Olr. rath [n.], MW rat 'mercy' < *q>rato-', Skt. ργηαϋ [3s.act.], aor. pwrdhi [2s.ipv.act.], pf. ^pupuryas [2s*optact.] 'to give, grant', sada-prna- [adj,] 'granting continually' < *prH-; Gr. aor. έπορον 'provided, gave', pf.med. πέπρωται 'it has been fated'. Mostly explained as a denominal verb to a noun *paro- < *prHro/hr (cf- puer-pera), but Rix has proposed that it continues *iprHie- > *paraie-. This is accepted by LIV. This will not work with our phonetic rules: *prHie- is expected to yield ^parie-, cf. Schrijver 1991: 293 (but *prio, *pnre according to Schrijver 2003: 78). The verb imperare is different in its semantics and because of its -e-; this it has in common with properare. Panagl-Lindner 1995: 173 interpret properare as an old compound *pro-parare 'to bring to the fore' > 'incite', an ά-intensive to parere. They explain properus as a back-formation to this verb. Similarly, imperare would stem from *in-parere 'to bring in > command'. Opiparus must be a more recent formation to paro. » \

Bibl.: WH II: 256, EM 484,\IEW 816-18, Schrijver 1991: 401, Rix 1999: 525, Untermann 2000: 222, Schumacher 2004: 508f., LIV *jperh₃-. -+pario, pauper



### parra
parra 'kind of bird (of ill omen)' [f. a] (Pl.+) Pit ^parVsa-.

It. cognates: U. parfam, parfa [acc.sg.], pctrfa [abl.sg.] 'certain bird' < *paresa.

PIE *sprH-e/os-?

IE cognates: Co. frau, Bret^/rao 'crow' < *spraua- < *sprehru-l\ Gr. σποργίλος 'sparrow', OPr. spurglis 'sparrow' (< *sprg-\ MHG sperke, Go. sparwa, OE spearwa, OHG sparo < *spor(H)u-en-7 OIc. spprr < *spor(H)uo-.

Pit. *parVsa- may reflect earlier *pHr-Vs- or ^prHVi-s-, This might be an j-less variant of the forms *sprH- for (mainly) 'sparrow' in other European languages; but since none of the other forms matches exactly, the etymology remains uncertain. Like other bird-names, these could be loanwords from a non-IE language.

Bibl.: WH II: 257, EM 484f., IEW 991, Meiser 1986: 174, Schrijver 1991: 21 If., Meiser 1998: 116, Untermann 2000: 513.



### parriclda
parriclda 'murderer of a near relation' [m. a] (Pl.+; also paricida\ nom.sg. paricidas Lex reg. apud Fest)

Derivatives: parricidium 'murder of a near relation' (Pl.+). The original word may have been ^pari-kaida^ with the same development to parr- as in lupitery etc. The connection with Gr. πηός, Dor. πάός 'kinsman by marriage' < *paso- is not convincing, since the etymology of the Gr. word is unknown, and since a word *paso- 'relative' is otherwise unknown in Latin. Still, the absence of syncope to *parkaida suggests that parriclda is a relatively recent compound. For a recent

<!-- pdf-page: 462; printed-page: 448; running-header: 448                                       pars -->

formation, par 'equal' is the most obvious candidate: *pari-kaid-a 'who kills an equaP.

Bibl.: WH II: 253, EM 483, IEW 789, Leumann 1977: 281, Schrijver 1991: 153. → caedo,par *



### pars, -tis
pars, -tis 'part, piece' [£ /] (Lex XII, Andr.+)

Derivatives: partim [adv.] 'partly' (Andr.+), parttre/i 'to share, divide up' (Andr.+), particula 'small part' (Varro+), particulatim 'bit by bit', particulo 'a coheir' (Pompon.+); expers 'having no share, free' (Pl.+); particeps 'participant1 (Andr,+), participare *to share* (Pl.+), participium 'participle' (Varro+), participalis 'participial' (Varro+); dispertire 'to separate, divide up' (Pl.+), impertfre 'to give a share, present with' (Pl.-*-), bipertftus 'bipartite' (Varro+), tripertitus 'tripartite' (V3rro+);portio*degree, portion' (Pl.+).

Pit. *parti- 'part'. Schrijver 1991 explains pars as a recent (e.g. Pit.) but pre-Latin formation based on the present stem par- 'to bring forth', just like the ppp. partus and the noun partus [m.] (see s.v. pario). The original meaning of *par-ύ- would have been 'lot, portion, fate', whence 'part, piece'. The latter shift separated the noun from the verb pario, which is why (in S.'s view) *parti- was not replaced by *partid like many other *tf-stems in Latin. All agree that portid must have a secondary origin. In its oldest attestations, it is only found in the abl.sg. proportione 'proportionally'. It would have arisen either from *pw ratione 'per part' > *prortione > with dissimilation portione, or from *pro par(ti)tione. Since partitio is not attested before Cicero, whereas ratio is fully present from Plautus onwards, the former etymology seems more likely.

Bibl.: WH II: 257-259, EM 485, 524, IEW 817, Leumann 1977: 366, Schrijver 1991: 195-197. -*pario



### parvus
parvus 'small' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: parvulus 'very small, tiny' (Pl.+)> parum [n./adv.] 'too little, not enough5 (P\.+),parumper 'for a short while" (Pl.+).

Pit. *pauro-.

PIE *peh₂u-ro- (or *ph₂eu-ro-).

IE cognates: Gr. παΰρος 'little', Go. fawai [nom.pl.] 'few', Olc.far 'little', ΟΕβα < PGm. */wa- < *ph₂(e)u-o-. With regular metathesis of *-wr- from *pauros, see nervus. The n. parum retains the phonetic reflex from *parom < *paruom, while -v- has been restored in parvus. With different suffixes, the same stem paw- is found in paucus and pauper.

'to graze, forage' (Varro+), pabulatio 'pasture; collecting fodder' (Varro+)*

Pit. pr. *pdske/o-, pr. *pas-, *paplo- [n.J.

<!-- pdf-page: 463; printed-page: 449; running-header: pater                                      449 -->

PIE pr. *peh₂-s- 'to protect, herd', pr. *p(e)h₂-sk-e/o-; *peh₂-dʰlo- [n.].

IE cognates: Hit. pahs-a(ti) , pahs' 'to protect, observe', pahsnu-zi 'to protect' < *peh₂s-o, *poh₂s-, *ph₂s-neu-; Skt pati [3s.act], s-aor. pasati [3s.sb.act.] 'to protect, keep', Av. pa- 'to protect', patar- [m,] 'protector', hu-pata- [adj.] 'well-protected', Gr. πώμα [n.] Mid, cover' < *poh₂-mn, OCSpasti, Is. pasg 'to pasture', Ru. pasti 'to pasture, herd', SCr. pasti 'to pasture, look after' < *peh₂s-; Gr. ποιμήν [m.] 'shepherd', Lith. piemuo [m.] '(shep)herd, shepherd's boy'; Finnish paimen 'shepherd' (loanword from Baltic) < *poh₂i-men-; Ίο A pas- 'to look after, watch'. Latin pastum, pastor are probably based on the PIE s-pr. which is also found in Hit. and Slavic, The sfc-present of Latin and Toch. appears to be an enlarged variant of the earlier ^-present. The noun pabulum continues the unenlarged variant of the root.

Bibl.: WH II: 260, EM 486, IEW 787, Schrijver 1991: 144, Meiser 2003: 124, LIV *peh₂(i)-. —»pared



### passer
passer 'small bird1 [m. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: passerculus 'little sparrow' (Pl.+),passermus 'of sparrows' (Pompon.).

Pit. *pattro-.

PIE *p(e)t-tro- 'who flies, bird'.

IE cognates: see s.v.perma. Schrijver proposes *pttro- 'bird' > *pattro- > nom.sg. *passros, gen.sg. *passri > *passrs, *pazri > *passer, *pari This paradigm would have been skewed, yielding two nouns: passer 'sparrow' and parus 'tit\ It must be remarked that the expected meaning of a form *pt-tro- would rather be Mnstruiiient for flying, wing' vel sim. Also, accipiter may contain *pet-ro- 'wing', although this is uncertain. Hence, the etymology remains uncertain.

Bibl.: WH II: 261, EM 486, Schrijver 1991: 212,495, LIV 1/2. *peth₂-. → accipiter, penna
