### pons, -tis
pons, -tis 'bridge, plank' [m. /] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: depontdre 'to throw from a bridge' (Varro); pontu/ifex, -ficis 'one of the college of priests of Rome' {?\.+\pontificalis 'of apontifex' (Varro+), pontificius 'of apontifex' (Cato+).

Pit. *pont-.

PIE *pont-hrs, *pnt-ehrm, *pnt-hros 'road'.

IE cognates: Skt. pantha- [m.] 'road, path, course' {pcmthas [nom.sg.], pantham [acc.sg.], pathos [gen.abLsg.],/?tf/A/ [loc.sg.]),

<!-- pdf-page: 494; printed-page: 480; running-header: 480                                     poples -->

Ο Αν. ραβδ [abl.sg.], ραίΰϊ [loc.sg.], ραβδ [acc.pl.], padabis [instr.pl.], YAv. panta [nom.sg.], pantqm [acc.sg.], pada [instr.sgj (secondary pantanam [acc.sg.], pantarto [nom.pl.]), OP padim [acc.sg J, Ossi fiertdceg 'path, road'; Gr. πάτος [m.] 'path', πόντος [m.] 'sea', Arm. hown 'ford', QPr.pintis, QCSpptb [m.] 'road'. Developed from PIE 'road' to Latin 'bridge'. The PIE paradigm would have yielded a pre-Latin inflection *pont(a)s, *pontem9 *pontos » nom.sg. *pontes (> -w), which, according to Schrijver 1991: 372, "could easily have been attracted to the inflection of the //-derivatives/* Pontifex has been much discussed, especially the first member. There seems to exist consensus among a majority of scholars that it must indeed be interpreted as 'bridge-maker', in the sense of 'who negotiates between gods and men'.

### populus
populus 'human community, people' [m. o] (Naev.+; Carmen Saliare poploe [nom.pl.], poplois [dat.abl.pl.] (apudFest), CIL· ]poplf9populo,poplo,poplus)

Derivatives: populan 'to ravage, plunder' (Naev.+), populabundus 'engaged in ravaging a territory' (Sis.+), populdris 'of the people, public' (Pl.+), papillaris [m.]t] 'fellow citizen, partner' (Naev.+), popularitos 'fellow-citizenship' (Pl.+), populatim 'universally' (Caecil.+); depopulari 'to sack, plunder' (Enn.+), depopulator 'who sacks' (Caecil.+); Poplifugia, -drum 'annual festival in Rome' (Varro+); Publius PN (gen.sg. popliosio Lapis Satricanus, Cic.+).

Pit. *poplo- 'army'.

It. cognates: U. puplum, puplu,poplom,poplo [acc.sg.],popler [gen.sg.], pople [dat.sg.], pople [loc.sg.], poplu [abLsg.], pupluper, popluper [abl.sg. + -per] 'able-bodied citizens'; U. pupfikes, pupri^es, pupf^es [gen.sg.], puprike, pupfi^e [datsg.j epithet of the deity puemune, probably = lat poplicus. The meaning 'to devastate' for the deponent probably developed through the usage 'to have an army pass through'. The derived adj. publicus has a long -u- that cannot belong to the same stem as *poplo-; see s.v. pubis. The etymology of

Pit. *poplo- 'army' (thus Watmough 1997: 69-81) is unknown. One might suggest appurtenance to *plh r 'to be full', but a reduplicated form *po-plhro- would be strange, and its meaning not evident. Watmough 1997: 91-102 posits an Etruscan noun *>puple 4 ?' on the basis of the toponym pupluna, but this gives little grip on the matter.

Bibl.: WH II: 339, EM 521f., Untermann 2000: 609-611. → pubis



### populus
populus 'poplar-tree' [f. o] (Cato+)

Derivatives: populeus Of a poplar-tree' (Enn.+), pdpulneus 'derived from poplar' (Cato+Xpopulrtus 'id.' (Pl.).

<!-- pdf-page: 495; printed-page: 481; running-header: porrum                                     481 -->

No etymology. The Greek forms Gr. πτελέα, Myc. pte-re-wa 'elm-tree' have *p and */ in common, but if they are cognate at all, no regular correspondence can be discovered.

Bibl.: WH II: 340, EM 522, Leumann 1977: 287.



### por-
por- 'forth, forward' [pref.]

Derivatives: porro [adv.] 'straight on, forward, further' (Pl.+; porod Praeneste, CIL 560).

Pit. *por-.

It. cognates: Fal. por- in porded, U./?wrv in pur-doui-.

PIE *pr- 'forth'.

IE cognates: Gr. παρ 'further, beside' (IonAtt. » παρά) , Go. four, OS for,jur 'for, before'; Gr. πόρρω, πόρσω (Pindar) 'forth' (but Η. πρόσ(σ)ω) , Latin por^ regularly continues

PIE *pr-, while porrd could reflect *p(a)rso(d). The Praenestine form is not an ablative. Nussbaum (1994: 173) tentatively suggests a preform *pr-s (with adverbal *-s) » *prs-o (cf controfversus), intrd, retro, ultra) > *porso > porro.

Bibl.: WH II: 343, EM 523, IEW 810-816, Giacomelli 1963: 252, Leumann 1977: 426,561, Untermann 2000: 612, LIV L*per-. →per



### porca
porca 'ridge of soil between furrows' [f. a] (Cato+)

Pit. *pork-o- ' furrow'. > * 5

PIE *prk-h₂- 'furrow .

IE cognates: W. rhych 'furrow', maybe Bret rec'h 'sorrow', OBret. rec 'sulco', ro-ricsefnjti 'sulcavissenf, Gaul, rica 'furrow' > Fr. raie 'stripe' < PCX *<prika-\ ORG fitruh, OE fiirh 'furrow', OIc,/or [f.] 'canal, ditch5 < PGm. *βίΐγό-\ maybe Lith. persiti 'to ache'.

Bibl.: WH II: 340, EM 522, IEW 821, Untermann 2000: 615, LIV *perk-. -+porcits



### porcus
porcus 'male pig' [m. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: porca 'sow' (Cato+), porculus 'little pig' (Pl.+), porcellus 'piglet' (Varro+), poranus 'of a pig5 (Naev.+), porcinarius 'pork-butcher' (Pl.), porculatio 'pig-rearing' (Varro+),/?ori:e/ra 'a sow which had young once5 (Pompon, apud GelL).

Pit. *pork-o-:^

It. cognates: U. purka,porca [acc.pl.] 'sow'.

PIE *pork-o-/digger, pig'.

IE cognates: Av. parzsa-, Khot. pasa (< *parsa), Kurd. purs, OPr. prastian, Lith. parsas, CS pros? [n. nt] , gen.sg. prasqte, Ru. porosja, porosenok [m.], porosjata [nom.pL], SCn prase, gen.sg. praseta 'piglet' < PS1. *pors-nt-, OHG far(a)h [n.], OE fearh [m./n.] 'pig' < PGm. *farya-. Original meaning 'digger', from the same root Ho dig up, cut' as the word for 'furrow'. A semantic parallel for this derivation is provided by W. twrch, Olr. tore 'boar' < *htrk-o- to the root *tuerk- 'to cut', cL McCone 1992a, Schrijver 1995: 65.

Bibl.: WH II: 341, EM 523, IEW 841, Hoffmann 1976: 491, LIV *perk-, -* porca, scrqfa



### porrum
porrum 'leek' [n. o] (Cato+)

Derivatives: porrina 'bed of leeks' (Cato+).

Pit. *porso-.

<!-- pdf-page: 496; printed-page: 482; running-header: 482                                      porta -->

PIE *prso-,

IE cognates: Gr. πράσον [n.] 'leek'; also Skt. parsa- 'sheaf, bundle', YAv. parsa- [m.] 'ear (of com)', Gr. Περσεφόνεια ΡΝ? The Latin and Greek words have mostly been regarded as an agricultural borrowing from a Mediterranean language. Botanists think that this vegetable has originated in the Mediterranean, and spread across Europe from the Roman period. Porrum and Gr. πράσον 'leek' can go back to *prso-· Weiss (apud Wachter 2006) proposes to connect porram with

PIE *perso- 'ear of grain' or 'sheath', as attested in Ilr and possibly in the theonym Περσεφόνεια, for which Wachter 2006 assumes a first member *Περσο- 'ear of grain/sheath5. Whereas this may seem formally attractive, it is unclear what the meaning of the root *prs- would have been, and how 'leek' could be derived from either 'ear of grain' or 'sheath'.

Bibl.: WH II: 343, EM 523, IEW 846, Wachter 2006: 143.



### porta
porta 'gate' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: portitor 'customs-officer, ferryman' (Pl.+), porticus, -iis [f.] 'colonnade, portico' (Pl.+), portisculus 'who beats the time for rowers, hammer used for this purpose' (Pl.+); portus, -vs charbour, refuge; door' (Lex XII+), portorium 'duty, toll' (Pl.+), Portunus 'deity of harbours' (Cic.+), Portunalis Of Portunus' (Varro+X Portunium 'temple of Portunus' (Varro+), importunus 'unfavourable, troublesome' (Andr.+), importunitas 'unreasonableness5 (Pl.+), opportunus 'favourable, convenient' (Pl.+), opportunity 'opportuneness, chance* (Pl.+); angiportum/s, -us 'narrow passage, lane' (Pl.+),

Pit. *porta- 'gate, door', *portu- 'harbour7.

It. cognates: O. purtam [acc.sg.] 'gate, door'; possibly \S.pertome [accsg. + -en] '?' a certain location < *per-tu-l

PIE *pMo- 'crossed', *pr-tu- 'crossing, ford, bridge'.

IE cognates: Gaul, ritu-, OW rity W. rhyd, Co. rit 'ford' < PCI. *<pritu-; Av. perstu- [m.] 'crossing, bridge', MP puhl 'bridge'; OHGJurt, OEford<?Gm. *Jurou-, OIc.fiqrdr 'bay, fjord' < *fero/}>u-. Porta is probably the f. to a PIE ppp. *pr-to- 'passed, crossed'; from this stem, the verb portare was probably also derived. Portus continues a PIE w-stem 'passage, crossing'. Portunus must be derived from *portu- with the suffix *-//«- or as a deinstrumental derivative *pftuhrno-. Op-portunus has the productive lengthening of the stem vowel for derivatives from «-stems.

Bibl.: WH II: 343-345, EM 523f, IEW 816f, Untermann 2000: 546, 615f, LIV 1.*per-, → per, por→ portd



### porto, -are
porto, -are 'to transport, carry' [v. I] (Andn+)

Derivatives: apportare 'to carry, bring' (Pl.+), osportare 'to carry off, remove' (Pl.+), comportare 'to carry, transport' (PJ.+)> deportare 'to bring, convey' (Pl.+), deportatid 'taking home' (Cato+), exportare 'to carry out, export' (Pl.+)> importare 'to bring in, cause' (Pl.+), reportare 'to bring bad' (Pl.+). Pit oporto/a-.

It. cognates: I I purtatu, portatu [2/3s.ipvJI], portaia [3s.pr.sb.], portust [3s.fiit.pf.] 'to bring, carry' < *porta-ie-.

PIE *prto- 'passed, crossed' or *port-o- 'passage, crossing'.

IE cognates: Skt. pr. piparti [3s.act], aor. pars- 'to bring across', Av. (frajfra [ls.sb.aor.act] 'to cross',

<!-- pdf-page: 497; printed-page: 483; running-header: post                                       483 -->


YAv.paraiia- 'to bring, lead5; Gr. πείρω Ho pierce, penetrate'. Probably denominal to *porto/a- 'passage, crossing'. The theory of WH and Leumann, viz. that portare goes back to a frequentative *poritare built to a PIE o-grade *por-eie/o-, requires an unwarranted shift from *por-eie- (which would normally yield a second-conjugation verb Lat. **pore-) to *por-i-.

Bibl.: WH II: 345, EM 524f, IEW 816f., Leumann 1977: 548, Untermann 2000: 616£,LIVl.*per-.



### posco, -ere
posco, -ere 'to ask for, demand' [v. Ill; pf. poposci] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: exposcere 'to ask for, demand' (Pl.+), reposcere 'to demand back' (Pl.+); postulare 'to ask for, demand, claim' (Lex XII+), postulatio * demand, protest1 (Pl.+), postilid 'sacrifice demanded by the gods' (Varro+), expos tulare 'to remonstrate, complain about' (Pl.+).

Pit. pr. *pork-ske/o- ( » Sab. *perk-ske-), aor. *prek- » *perk- ( » Pit. pf. *pe-pork- » Lat. *pe-pork-ske-).

It. cognates: U. pepurkurent [3p.fiit.pf.], O. comparascuster [3s.fut.pf.ps.] 'to demand/decide'; O. ku]mparakineis [gen.sg. n] 'city council' vel sim. < *kom-prk-ion-. U. persnimu, persnihmu, pesnimu, prepesnimu, persnimu, persnihimu, pesnimu [3s.ipv.II], persnimumo, persnihimumo, pesnimumo [3p.ipv.II], persnis fust, pesnis fus(t) [3s.fuLpf.] 'to pray' < *perk-sk-(i)n-ie/i-, denom. to a noun *perk-sk-(i)on-\ U,; persklum, persclo [acc.sg.], persklum-ar [acc.sg. + -ad] ;perscler, pescler [gen.sg.], persklu, persclu, pesclu r [abl.sg.] 'prayer' < *perk-sk-elo-.

PIE *prk-sk-e/o- [pr.J, *pr(e)i;- [aor.] 'to ask'.

IE cognates: Olr. arcaid*, aire 'to ask' < PCI. *<parske/o- < *pr*ke-, OW MW erchU MCo. erghi 'to order', MBret. archaff^o ask, beg' < PBn *ark-i- « PCI. *<parske/o-, Skt. pr. pfchdmi, Av. psresa- 'to ask', OPpati-prsa- 'to read',p?sa- 'to interrogate, punish\ Arm. (e-)harc4 [aor.] 'asked', Lith.prasytU OCSprositi; Go.fraihnan; ToAprak-9 ToBprek- 'to ask'. Lat. posco < *pork-sk- continues a PIE ^-present. Schrijver 1991: 497 doubts whether posco contains the regular outcome of *pfksk-, or whether we should expect *parksk-. The latter might be found in O. comparascuster, in which case Latin posco must contain a secondarily restored */* > or. WH and Meiser 2003 explain postulare from *posk-to- << *pork-to-, ppp. to posco. Yet the alleged ppp. itself is unattested. Leumann 1977: 208 and Sihler derive postulare from a noun *posculum or *postulum 'query' < *pork-sk-tlo-. In view of the co-occurrence of postul-are and postil-io, it would be attractive to assume a preform *post-elo- 'asking', from which both forms can be explained.

Bibl.: WH II: 346f., EM 525f, IEW 821f., Sihler 1995: 535, Untermann 2000:415f., 530f„ 539-541, Meiser 2003: 187-189, Schumacher 2004: 511-514, LIV *prek-. → prex, procus



### post
post 'in the rear, behind, after' [adv.; prep. + ace] (Lex XII+; var. paste Pl. Enn. Ter.)

Derivatives: pone 'from behind, in the rear of (Pl.+), posticus [adj.] 'at the back' (Pl.+),posticulum 'small back shed* (PI,),posterus 'later, next' (Sis.+\posteri[m.pl] 'descendants, successors' (Pl.+)> posterior 'later, younger, inferior' (Pl.+), postremus

<!-- pdf-page: 498; printed-page: 484; running-header: 484                                       postis -->


'last, final' (R+),postumus 'last-born, final' (Cic.+), Postumus PN (Pl.+)-

Pit. *posti 'behind, after', *post-ero- 'later\

It. cognates: O. pust, post, pust, U. pus, post, pos [prep. + abl.] 'after, behind' < *posti; O. piistin, U. pustin, pusti, posti, SPic. postin [prep. + ace] 'along, according to' < *posti en; IL postne [datsg,n.?] 'which is behind' < *postino-\ U. pustnaiaf [acc.ptfj, pusnaes [abtpl.f.] < *postinaio- 'which is behind'; O. pustrei, pustr. [loc.sg.rn.], U.postra [acc.ptf.] 'later', maybe 'second' < *postero-\ U. pustra, pustru, postro [adv.] 'back, backwards' < nom.acc.pl.n. *postera; O. pustiris [adv.] 'later' < norn.acc.sg.n. *posterios\ O.posmom [acc.sg.n,], pustm[as [nom.pl.f.] 'last' < *pos(ti?)tmHo-,

PIE *pos(ti) 'after'.

IE cognates: Skt. posca [adv,] 'behind, after, back', pascat 'from behind, afterwards, backwards', YAv.pasca 'after, behind, later';paskat 'from behind, afterwards', OP pasa 'after', pasava 'thereupon, then' < Ilr. *pa(s)(t)-sk/ca(t) < *po(s)-sK*-eht 'following'; Gr. (dial.) πός 'at, to'; Lith. pas 'at, on'; OCS pozde 'late', Ru.pozdyj (dial.) < *pos(t)~cthro-. Whereas posterns seems to be the old comparative, matched by Sabellic forms, posterior must be a secondary formation built on it Nevertheless, it too can be found in O. pustiris. Lat postremus reflects *poster-isamo-, while pone was formed from *post-ne. Lat. posticus reflects

PIE *posti-h₃kʷ-o- 'facing the back', cf. anficus. The primary PIE form seems to have been *pos, extended by means of *-tf in PIE or in

Pit. The model probably was the antonym *h₂enti 'in front of.

### potis, pote
potis, pote 'able, having the power' [adj. indecl. /] (Naev>: potis esse)

Derivatives: (I) potis esse 'to be master, be capable' > posse » potisse; ptpotui; utpote [adv.] 'as one might expect, as is natural' (Pl.+) (2) potior, potius 'better, more powerful' (Naev.+),potissimus 'most powerful' (Pl.+), potvre 'to put under the power of (Pl.+), pottfri) 'to become master, obtain control' (Naev.+), potestas 'command, power' (Lex ΧΠ+); compos 'endowed with, having command' (Naev.+), compotire Ho put in possession, attain' (Pl.+), impos 'not having control or possession, demented' (Pl.+); (3) potens 'powerful, capable, provided' (Pl.+), impotens 'powerless, lacking control' (Ter.+), impotentia 'weakness, violence' (Ter.+), praepotens 'outstandigly powerful' (Pl.+).

Pit. *poti-, 'master, in control of, *pot-e- 'to be master'.

It. cognates: O. putiad,

<!-- pdf-page: 499; printed-page: 485; running-header: prae                                      485 -->

putiiad [3s.pr.sb.], putians, putiians [3p.pnsb.] 'to be able' < *pof-e-.

PIE *pot-i-.

IE cognates: Skt pati- [m.] 'lord, master', Av. paiti- [m.J 'lord, husband', OP *daua-pati- 'commander of ten soldiers'; Gr. πόσις [m.] 'husband'; OLith. patis, Lith. pats [m.] 'id/, Go. brup-faps [m.j 'bridegroom', ToA pats 'husband'» The PIE noun *pot-i- 'master, lord' has become adjectival in combination with the verb esse (1), and in prepositional compounds (2). The adj. then acquired a comparative and superlative, and the verb poti-ie- > potire was derived from it. Potens probably derives from a Pit. verb *pot-e- with the stative meaning 'to be master'; this seems a better explanation than a causative or iterative *pot-eie- which Meiser 2003 posits. Its pf. potui was later reanalysed as the pf to posse. The exact process leading from potis + esse to the paradigm of posse is disputed. There may have been a regular development from *potis est/pote est > *poti/est, leading to the analogical introduction of *potsum > possum, or maybe *potis sum regularly developed to possum.

Bibl.: WH II: 347,350, EM 526, 528, IEW 842, Leumann 1977: 524f., Meiser 1998: 73,222, Sihler 1995: 538f., Untermann 2000: 625f., Meiser 2003: 65. → hospes, -pte



### potus
potus 'drunk' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: potus, -us (m.; Varro+) 'drinking, drink, draught', potio (Lex XII+) '\±\pdtdre (Pl.+) 'to drink', pdtatiq 'drinking party' {fl+j, potator 'drinker' (Pl.+), potitare cto drink habitually' (Pl.+); epdtare 'to swallow, drink down' (Pl.+), perpotare 'to drink heavily' (Pl.+); pdsca (Pl.+) 'acid drink of vinegar and water', poculum (Pl.+)'drinking vessel', pdcillum 'little cup' (Cato+), depoculare 'to min by expenditure on cups' (Lucil.), compotrix 'female drinking companion' (Ten)-

Pit. *poto- 'drunk', *po-tlo- 'cupJ,

It. cognates: maybe IL punes [gen.sg.], pune [acc.sg.], puni,pone,poni [abl.sg.] 'sacrificial drink' < *po-ni-.

PIE *peh₃-/*ph₃- [aor.] 'to drink', *ph₃-to- 'drunk',

IE cognates: Hit. pas-11 pas- 'to swallow, gulp down', CLuw. pass- 'to swallow' < *poh₃-s-; Skt. aor. apat [3s.act], panti [3p.sb.j 'to drink', pita- ' drunk\ patar-, patar^ [m.] 'drinker', Gr. (Aeol.) πώΰι 'drink!', έπότϊην [IsTaor.] 'to drink'; OPr.pout cto drink', Lith.puota 'drinking-bout'. The full grade of potus 'drunk' must have been introduced from the root aorist Potus and ρδ-culum have caused the spread of ρδ- in the other derivatives. The noun pdsca was probably formed on the model of esca.

Bibl.: WH I: 103f, EM 529, IEW 839f., Schrijver 1991: 147, Meiser 1998: 123, Untermann 2000: 606f, LIV *peh₃(i)-· -* bibo



### prae cin front of, before'
prae cin front of, before' [adv., prep. + abL] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: praeter [prep. + ace] 'past, beyond, besides' (Naev.+); praesto [adv.] 'available, ready' (Pl.+).

Pit. *praL

It. cognates: O. prai, U. pre, pre [prep. + abL] 'before, in front of; O. prae-, U. pre-,pre-, SPic. prai-, maybe PaeLpral· [prev.] cin front, fore, at hand'; U. pretra [acc.pl.f.?] 'thefirst*< *prai-tero~.

PIE *preh₂i 'before' (loc.sg. *preh₂-i?) .

IE cognates: Gr. πάρα 'besides, by, from,

<!-- pdf-page: 500; printed-page: 486; running-header: 486                                    praestolo/or -->


next to, against', also παρά, epic also παραί, outside Ion.-Att mostly παρ, Myc.pa-ro <*prhr 'by'. The adv. praesto is probably an old abl!sg. *praistod of an adj. *praisto- 'ready, available*, the analysis of which is uncertain. It might be from < *prai-sthro- 4 standing in front5, or *prai-si-to- 'placed in front'.

Bibl.: WH Π: 351, 356fM EM 529f, 532, IEW 810-816, Schrijver 1991: 302, Sihler 1995: 428, Beekes 1995: 221, Garcia-Ramon 1997, Untermann 2000: 568£, 575f. → prior



### praestelo/or, -are/-arl
praestelo/or, -are/-arl 'to wait for, expect' [v. I] (Andr.+)

Pit. *sto!o-i

PIE *stol-o- 'being ready, waiting'?

IE cognates: Gr. στέλλω 'to put in order, make ready', στόλος 'equipment (of a campaign), troop', στολή 'armor, dress', στολμός [nx] 'equipment, clothing5, Arm. stelcanem 'to create', Alb. shtiell 'to wind up, reel up, collect' (< *stel-n-); OPr. stallit 'to stand5, OCS postblati, Is. posteljg, Ru. stlat' 'to spread'; PGm. *stalla- 'place, stable5 (whence *stati-ija- 'to place') < *stol-no- or tsthrctlo-l IEW explains the verb as *praestodran9 built directly on the abl.sg. *praistod > praesto [adv.] 'ready, available5; thus also Steinbauer 1989: 255. This is ingenuous, but a derivation from an ablsg- form would be rather unique. Livingston 2004: 65f. proposes to analyse praestolare as a compound of (an originally directive adv.) praesto 'to the ready5 and *a/- *to wander5 (as in amb-ulare). This is based on one Plautine instance where the verb means 'to go to meet' rather than 'to wait5. Yet 'to wander' is a rather unspecified movement to convey the sense of 'going to meet5, and the compounding process involved (with a ready-made adverb) is not impossible, but rare. An alternative analysis would be to derive -stdla- from the PIE root *stel- 'to arrange, place, spread5, the present being of the type conan. The basis could have been an adj. *stol-o- 'being ready, waiting' > *prai~stdl-aje- 'to wait for5.

Bibl.: WH II: 356f., EM 532, IEW 1004-1010, LIV *stel-. → praesto

prandium 'lunch5 [n. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: prandere 'to eat one's morning or midday meal' (Pl.+; prandi, pransum), pransor 'who takes lunch' (Pl.+); impransus 'not having had breakfast' (Pl.+), deprans, -ndis 'fasting5 (Naev.).

Pit. *pramo- 'first*.

### prehendo, -ere
prehendo, -ere 'to grasp, seize' [v. Ill; prehendi, prehensum] (Pl.+; contracted prendo Ter.+)

Derivatives: praeda 'booty' (Andr.+), praedan 'to acquire loot, plunder' (Pl.+), praedator 'plunderer' (Pl.+), praedatus, -lis 'robbery' (Pl.+), praeda 'brigand, pirate' (Pl.+), praedonulus 'little brigand* (Cato); apprehendere / apprendere "to grasp, seize, attach' (Pl.+), compre(he)ndere 'to seize, find, embrace' (Pl.+), depre(he)ndere 'to intercept, come upon' (Pl.+), repre(he)ndere 'to grasp, hold back, censure' (Pl.+).

Pit. *{prai-)xendr 'to seize' [pn], *{prai-)xod-a~ 'booty'.

PIE *gʰ~n(e>d- [prj 'to grab' » *gʰ(e)nd-, *gʰod-h₂- [f.].

IE cognates: Olr. rogeinn, MW genni 'to be situated* < PCI. *gan-n-dt-e/o-\ Gr. χανδάνω 'to grab', Alb. gjen *finds',med. gjindet 'is situated"; Go. du-girman 'to begin*. Praeda is usually explained as *prai~xed-a- 'what one acquires', but Livingston 2004: 52 convincingly argues that the compound may rather contain an o-grade noun, thus *ρται-χοά-ά~. Sfie explains prehendo as follows: *prai~xend- regularly contracted to *praind-y and further developed to *-prend- after preverbs: *kom-prend-. For the vowel development, cf. obscaenus, maybe alienus. From *-prendt> a new simplex *prendere resulted, which could alternatively be speWzna prehendere, as in vehemens next to vemens. Finally, shortening yielded prendere. The pf. and ppp. can be recent formations.

Bibl.: WH II: 352f., 359, EM 530f, IEW 437f, Leumann 1977: 199, Meiser 1998: 62, 83, 2003: 210f., Livingston 2004: 47-56, Schumacher 2004: 330, LIV *gʰed-. → hedera



### premu, -ere
premu, -ere 'to press' [v. Ill; pf pressi, ppp. pressum] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: pressare 'to press, weigh down' (Pl.+), prelum 'wine or oil press, other kind of press' (Cato+); comprimere 'to press together, copulate, hold back' (Pl.+), compressio 'sexual embrace, squeezing' (Pl.+)> compressus, -Us 'sexual intercourse' (Pl.+), deprimere 'to press down, sink' (Pl.+), exprimere 'to squeeze, stamp, express'

<!-- pdf-page: 502; printed-page: 488; running-header: 488                                      pretium -->

(Naev.+), imprimere 'to press, imprint' (Enn.+), opprimere 'to squeeze, crush, overpower' (Pl.+), oppressio 'taking by surprise, overpowering' (Ter.+), oppressiuncula 'a light squeeze' (Pl.), oppre^us, -us Crushing' (Lucr.), reprimere 'to check, repress' (Pl.+), supprimere 'to hold back, withhold' (Pl.+).

Pit. *pres-. PEE *pr-es- 'to press',

IE cognates: SkLpft-, YAv./?araf- [f.] battle, strife, fight' < *pr~t-, Lith. perti, Latv. /?#r/ 'to beat, lash', RIL peret', 3s. pret 'to go, make one's way, drag', Cz.pfiti 'to quarrel'. The pr. requires a stem *prem-, whereas the pf. and ppp. seem to have *pres- or maybe *preT-. Since the verb 'to shiver' shows the allomorphs *trem- and *tres- in PIE, most handbooks assume that premd : press- was formed on the model ottremo : *tress- ( » tremui). Prelum cannot reflect *prem(V)lo-, so it must reflect *preslo-* Leumann's proposal *premslo- is not very likely, since one would expect *premV-slo-. Thus, the orignal stem would have been *pres-9 the pr. prem- being an innovation on the basis of tremd. Italic *pres- might be an s-present to

PIE */?er- 'to hit', but this remains guesswork.

Bibl.: WH II: 359£, EM 533f, IEW 8I8f, Leumann 1977: 208, 212, 591, Sihler 1995: 213,583, Meiser 1998: 209, Meiser 2003: 116, LIV 3.*per-.



### pretium
pretium 'reward, prize; penalty" [n. o] (Andr.+)

Derivatives:pretiosus 'expensive, precious' (Pl.+).

Pit. *preti-o- 'exchange value'.

PIE *preti 'against'.

IE cognates: Skt pratU Av. paiti, Gr. πρότι, προτί, προς, OCS protivb, protivg 'against, towards', Ru. proti (dial.), protb (dial.) 'against' <

PIE *p(r)oti; Gr. περτ' (Pamph.), πρές (Aeol.) < *preti? WH and IEW derive pretium from a preposition *preti, with e-ablaut for *proti. There is no viable alternative; see s.vJnterpres, -lis 'intermediary' for the difficulties involved in the connection via a common preform.

Bibl.: WH II: 360f, EM 534, IEW 810-816, Beekes 1995: 221.

Pit. *preh-.

PIE *prek- [f] 'question'.

IE cognates: W. rheg 'gift; curse' < PCI. *<prek- (Hamp 1977); Skt. pros- [f] 'dispute, questioning, statement or assertion in a debate or lawsuit', OAv. f(a)rasa- [f ] 'question'. Root noun *prek- to the root of.posed and procus.

### prior 'in front, ahead, earlier'
prior 'in front, ahead, earlier' [adj.comp. r] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: (1)priusquam [cj.] 'earlier then, before', pridem [adv.] 'formerly, long

<!-- pdf-page: 503; printed-page: 489; running-header: pro                                       489 -->

ago' (Pl.+), prtdie [adv.] 'the day before, in the time preceding' (Pl.+); (2) primus 'furthest in front, foremost' (Naev.+), adprimus 'the very first' (Andr.+), pnmanus 'belonging to the first legion' (Cato+)5pnmarius 'leading, excelling' (Pl.+),pnmatus, -us 'supremacy' (Varro+), pnmitus [adv.] 'at first' (Lucil.+), prlmulus 'first' (Naev>)5 primdris [adj.] 'first member or part, nearest1 (Cato+), prmceps* -cipis "fist, earliest, leading' (Pl.+), principium 'start, origin' (Pl.+), apprTmus 'very first, most excellent' (Andr.+); (3) priscus 'ancient, of old' (Enn.+), prlstinus 'antique, ancient, previous' (Pl.+). Pit comp. *prf-jos- 'earlier, former', n. *prf-is, sup. *pri-isamo- 'first'.

It. cognates: Pael. pri- 'in front of, maybe U. pri-, pri- in prinuvatu- < *pri-\ Pael. prismu [nom.sg,f.] fern, praenomen; Pael. pritrome [acc.sg. + -en] 'further' < *pn-tero-.

PIE *pr(e)i 'before'.

IE cognates: Gr. πριν 'before, previously'; OVx.prei 'at, with, to', L\th.prie,prie- 'at, with, to', OCSpri 'at, with, by' <

PIE *prei All seem to be built on an adv. *pri 'before', which is attested by PauL ex F. as pri 'prae\ It might also be phonologically *pri. The PIE ancestry of pri is uncertain: it can hardly continue *prh₂i (zero grade of *preh₂i > prae) since this would yield Lat. *pari. It might be connected with BSl *prei ^ ζ with' The sup. contains the suffix *-wamo-, cf. Pael. prismu; it is used in pnnceps < *prTmo~kap-* The sup, prior reflects *pn-ios, and its zero grade *prf-is seems to have been used aspns- mprtdem < *pns-dem 'long ago' and in the adj. *pris-ko- > priscus. and pris-timts. The suffix -tinus also appears e.g. in diutinus 'daily' and crastinus 'of tomorrow', where it seems more originalrsee s.v. dm. ; }

Bibl.: WH II: 361-363, EM 534f., IEW 810-816, Cowgill 1970: 125, Leumann 1977: 317, 321, Sihler 1995: 360, 428, Meiser 1998: 174, Untermann 2000: 577, 579, -» prae,prTvus



### privus
privus 'peculiar, individual' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: prTvare 'to deprive of, rob* (Pl.+), privates 'private, of a citizen' (Lex ΧΠ+), privatim 'privately, separately' (Pl.+); prlvigna 'step-daughter' (Titin.+).

Pit. *prei-wo- 'separate, individual'.

It. cognates: U. prever [abl.pl.m.], preve [advj 'one at a time, separately'; O. preiuatud [abl.sgj 'the defendant', probably a loan from Latin.

PIE *prei-uo-. Probably a derivative in *-uo- from the stem *prei- 'in front of, before' seen in prior etc. Thus, a semantic shift from 'being in front' to 'being separate'.

Bibl.: WH II: 363f, EM 536, IEW 810-816, Untermann 2000: 572f., 576. ^ prior



### pro
pro 'in front of, before; for' [prep. + abL, prev.] (Tiburbasis+, Andr.+; var. pwd-C9 pro-V)

Derivatives: pro- (prefix) 'before, previous' (in proavus 'great-grandfather' etc.), promts 'leaning forward, bending down, inclined' (Nazv.+)yprotinus [adv,] 'forward, straightaway' (Vl.+\protinam [adv.] 'directly' (Naev.+).

Pit. *pw, *pro(-) 'before'.

It. cognates: O. pru, pr.,pr. [prep. + abL], (λ pru-, pru-, U, pru-,/?w- [prev.] 'for, before' < *proypro\ U, promom, prumum, pruimi [adv,]

<!-- pdf-page: 504; printed-page: 490; running-header: 490                                    probrum -->

4 at first' < *pro-mom 'primum5; O.pruter [adv.]'before' < *pro-ter.

PIE *pro 'in front of, before', *proH (?); *pro-mo- 'further, up front'.

IE cognates: Olr. ro°[pref.] (perfectivizing); Skt. pqa [adv.] 'before, forward', Av. fra- 'for, before', Khot. ha-; Gn πρό 'before, forwards, forth', Lith. pra- 'by, through', OCS pro- 'through', Go. fra- [pref ] <

PIE *pro-; Skt. pratar [adv.] 'early, in the morning, the next day', Gr. πρωί 'early, in the morning', Latv. pruojam 'away, ofF, Lith.pro- 'pre-, fore-, between', pro 'through, along, past', OCS pra- 'through', OHG fruo [adj.] 'early'; Gr. πρόμος 'protagonist, commander', OIc. framr, OEfram 'excellent'. The form prod- is recent and was probably built after re-C- : red-V- 'back'. Short pro- occurs in some forms as a variant of,pro-, but also in some older compounds, such as procul, probus, proprius, etc. Most of the Sabellic forms also go back to */?ro-. Leumann regards *pro as the most original form, which was shortened to *pro already in PIE. Schrijver 1991, however, follows Beekes in assuming an ins.sg. *pro-hj (or *pr-ohfi) beside */?ro; Untermann 2000: 582 also assumes an ins.sg. Alternatively, one might suggest that *proH arose when *pro- stood before roots in *HC-.

Bibl.: WH II: 364f, EM 536, IEW 810-816, Leumann 1977: 560f, Schrijver 1991: 81, Beekes 1995: 221, Sihter 1995: 427,439, Schafiher 1996: 155, Untermann 2000: 58 If, 586, 590. → per, por,prope, re-

### probus
probus 'excellent, good' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: (1) probitas 'honesty, integrity' (Pac.+); approbus 'excellent5 (Caecil.+), improbus 'of poor quality, improper, greedy' (Lex XII+); probare cto approve of, commend, prove' (Naev.+), probaiio 'approval, proof (Varro+), probabilis 'commendable, plausible' (Afran.H-); approbare 'to commend, endorse' (Pl.+), comprobare 'to confirm, justify' (Pl.+), improbare 'to reject, repudiate5; (2) superbvs 'proud, haughty' (Naev.+), superbia 'pride, disdain' (Pl.+).

Pit. *pro-f(w)-o- 'favourable5.

It. cognates: Pael. pros [nom.sg.m.] 'righteous', U. prufe [adv.] 'correct', O. amprufid [adv.] 'incorrect' < *pro-bʰu-o-; O. prufatted, prufated, πρωίατεδ [3s.pf], prufattens [3p.pf ] 'to approve of, denom. verb to *profo-, in its meaning influenced by Lat. probare.

PIE *pro-*bʰ(h₂)u-o-,

IE cognates: Skt. pra-bhu- [adj.] 'excelling, excellent'; Gr. ύπέρφευ [adv.] 'excessively5 (Att.), ύπερφυής 'excessive', υπερφίαλος 'arrogant' (if

<!-- pdf-page: 505; printed-page: 491; running-header: procul                                    491 -->


< *-phuwaIos with de Lamberterie), ύφέαρ 'mistletoe' (< *h₂iu-bʰeh₂u-r or *h₂iu-bʰeu-r (?) 'ever-growing'). The Sab. forms show that there was a Pit. form *profo~; hence, we must start from a thematized form in *-bʰu-o- < *-bʰHu-o-, with loss of the PIE laryngeal in the compound. In or after Pit., *w was lost after a labial obstruent (cf. aperio, operio\ Skt. has a w-stem which might be an internal formation of Skt. Superbus is not found in Sab., which may be coincidental; but since superbus functions more or less as an antonym to probus, superbus might be an inner-Latin formation. De Lamberterie 1994: 334 has suggested that Gr. ύπέρφευ 'excessively' represent the same formation as superbus.

Bibl.: WH II: 366, EM 537, IEW 146-150, Meiser 1998: 121, Untermann 2000: 583-585, LIV *bʰueh₂-. ^Jul



### proceres, -urn
proceres, -urn 'leaders, society' [m.pl. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: procf, -urn [pi.] 'class of leading citizens in the Servian constitution' (Cic, Fest.).

Pit. *proko- 'who is at the front, leader'?

PIE *pro-k(o)- .

IE cognates: Gr. πρόκα 'immediately', OCS prokb 'remaining', procb 'remaining' [adj.]; proce 'further, then' [adv.], Ru. prok 'use, benefit' [m.], procij 'other' [adj.];/?wc' 'away' [adv.] <

PIE *pro-k-(i)o-. Unclear formation. According to*WH, the gen.ph procum shows that the stem was *prok(o)-y to which -er-es was added by analogy wifh pauperes 'the poor'. This explanation is of course ad hoc, but *prok(o)- does seem to be the older stem. Maybe proceres did not replace *prok-T, but *prok-eri, the pi. of the ero-derivative of the stem *prok(o)-.

Bibl.: WH II: 367, EM 537, IEW 810-816. →pro



### procerus
procerus 'tall, lofty' [adj. o/o] (Enn.+)

Pit. *k(r)ero-. :

PIE *kreh r ro- 'growing'.

IE cognates: see s.v. crescd. Schrijver regards'the usual connection with crescd as very uncertain, since it is not compelling that 'tall' derives from 'growing upward'. I think this is too pessimistic. Rieken 2003: 45 reconstructs a PIE adj. *kerhjO- 'growing' which she recognizes in HLuw, zi+ra/i-la-mi-i 'fruitful, thriving' < *zira- 'growth' < *kero-. She analyzes procerus as orig. *'having a high growth' and sincerus *'of one growth, unmixed, real'. This is possible, but the meaning 'fruitful, thriving' of HLuw. is less clearly connected with 'to grow, increase' than procerus. Hence, I still regard a dissimilation οϊ *prd-kreros >procerus quite possible.

Bibl.: WH II: 367, EM 537, IEW 577, Leumann 1977: 315, Schrijver 1991: 124, Rieken 2003. → crescd, sincerus



### procul
procul 'some distance away, (far) away, apart' [adv.] (Pl.+)

Pit. *pro-k(w)o- 'at the front'?

PIE *pro 'in front of.

<!-- pdf-page: 506; printed-page: 492; running-header: 492                                    procus -->

Lat. procul might be a derivative of

PIE *pro-ko- 'at the front', hence *prokelo-. Schaffher 1996 compares PGm. *niyuula- 'down, directed downwards' < *ni-kue-lo- and Skt visva- 'all, whole' < *w/-Aj/o-, and posits *proculo- < *prokuolo- <

PIE *pro-kue-lo- * somewhat to the Front'. The only problem is the zero ending of procul: n. *prokulom should yield *proculum* Schaffner 1996: 163 suggests that *-«m may have dropped as in nihil and donee (this does not seem likely, since it only happens in frequent forms) or by analogy with simul and facul < n. *-//. In that case, a preform *prokelom « *proko- would also be possible. Thus, this aspect of the etymology remains open.

Bibl.: WH II: 368, EM 537, IEW 810-816, Schaffher 1996: 162-171. →/ro, re-

### proelium
proelium 'battle' [η. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: proeliare cto engage in battle' (Luc\L+), proeliaris Of battles' (Pl.+). KHngenschmitt 1980 posits a sound law *oweli > *owili > *oili > -oeli-, for which only the PN Cloelius, Coelius and Boelius are adduced as evidence. Proelium would continue a form *pro-gʷe/ol-io~, with cognate forms such as MW ryvel, W. rhyfel 'war* < PCI, *ro-bel-, bel 'hits, strikes', erdifel 'smites' (< ^ori-cn-bel-X Lith. gelti, 3s. gelia 'ache, sting, bite', and OHG quelan 'to be in pain', OE cwelan 'to die'. Yet in the relative chronology, the Pit. lenition of *gʷ to *w precedes the Latin development of *-owe/oCV- > *-ouCV- > -tiC-^ so that one would expect *pro-gʷe!io- to yield *prulium or, like motus, *prolium. Klingenschmitts proposal may be salvaged if the root sequence *we/- was restored at a later stage, but no cognate forms are extant in Latin that would seem to conserve such a root. If we are to explain proe- from an earlier disyllabic sequence, the syllable boundary must have been present until quite recently. An alternative candidate would be a preform *pro-xeUio-9 but there is no PIE root *gʰel(H)- that comes to mind as a likely root.

Bibl.: WH II: 369, EM 538, KHngenschmitt 1980: 221, Meiser 1998: 87, Brachet 1999, Schumacher 2004: 218.



### prope
prope 'near, near by, close' [adv.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: propior 'nearer, closer' (Pl.+); proximus 'nearest, next' (Lex XII+); propter [adv; prep. + ace, + abl.] 'near, close by; because of, for the sake of (Naev.+); propinquus 'near, neighbouring' (Pl.+), propinquare 'to approach, be near' (Lucr.+), propinquitas 'nearness, kinship' (Pl.+); propitius 'well-disposed' (Naev.+), propitiare 'to win over, soothe' {Y\.+\propitiabilis 'able to be propitiated' (Enn.)·

<!-- pdf-page: 507; printed-page: 493; running-header: prosperus                                      493 -->

PIE *propro 'on and on, ever further'.

IE cognates: Skt. pr&pra [adv.] 'on and on', Gr. πρό-προ 'before; on and on'. The etymology of these forms has been solved by Dunkel 1980, who convincingly argues that prope reflects an iterative preverb *propro 'very much in front, near', with dissimilatory loss of the second *r. Propior is its regular comparative, and proximus < *propsamo- may be explained as a replacement of the unusual sequence *-psimos by *-ksimos (British Celtic shows a sound change *ps > *fa in W. uchel 'high' < *oupselo-9 but an Italo-Celtic date for this change would place the loss of the second *r in *propro very early, which is unlikely). The adv. propter < *prope-ter cannot be separated from prope semantically, Dunkel's explanation of propitius < *prope-tio- also seems superior to previous explanations of this adj. The suffix of propinquus is not completely clear: was it built as an antonym to relinquere 'to leave, abandon'?

Bibl.: WH II: 371-373, EM 538f, IEW 810-816, Dunkel 1979, 1980. ^pro, re-



### proprius
proprius 'one's own, peculiar, specific' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: propntim 'particularly' (Lucr.),propriassit 'proprium fecerit' (Fest. 229M).

Pit. *prijo- 'own, dear', *prijaje/o- 'to make one's own'.

PIE *priH-o- Own, dear'.

IE cognates: MW rhydd 'free', Skt. priya-, Av. friia- * dear, own', Skt. prestha-, Av.fraesta- 'dearest', Go.freis> ORG fii 'free' < PGm. *frija- 'free, dear, own'; Skt. pr. pnnanti [3p.act], apriyamana- [neg.ptc.med.], aor. presat [3s.sb.act.] 'to please', prita- 'pleased, satisfied', OAv. friiqnmahi (for *frinmahi) [lp.pr.act.] 'to satisfy\/rina/ [ls.pr.subjned.] 'to please', ΥΑν: frmamahi [lp.pr.act] 'to satisfy', huua-frita- 'very much loved', frWa- 'dear, loved', OE frid-hengest 'horse that is well-cared for', OHG friten 'to look after', OCS prijati 'to take care of, prijatelb 'friend, beloved', Go. frijon 'to love', Go. frijonds, OIc, frcendi, OHG friunt 'friend'. Forssman 2004 has elaborated the connection with *priH- 'to be dear', Skt. priya-, which seems-the most likely etymology. Since pro- can hardly be explained as a nominal prefix, he argues that it was prefixed to *prius from the verb *pro-priare, of which the hapax propriassit in Festus (belonging to the s-pr. in -ass- which was unproductive in CLat) would be a remnant. The adv\ propritim might conceal an old ti-stem *priff-tl·, which is also continued in Ilr.

Bibl.: WH II: 373f, EM 539f, IEW 829, Forssman 2004, LIV *preiH-.

Derivatives: prosperare 'to succeed, further' (Pl.+).

### prOtervus
prOtervus 'bold, violent; impudent' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+; var. proptervus (PL, Pac.) is older)

Derivatives:protervitas 'boldness' £Ter>).

Pit. *propetes-wo-*

PIE *pet-os, -es- [n.] 'impetus, haste'.

IE cognates: Gr. -πέτης 'flying'; see s.v. peto. The older etymologies with *pet-r/n- 'wing' are semantically unsatisfactory. Rix 1981: 118 (= 2001: 286) proposes a preform *propetes-j4o- with regular change of *su to rv. The preform would be a contamination of * pro-petes 'inclined, hasty' (Gr. προπετής 'falling down, precipitate') and *petes-uo- 'provided with impetus'. This seems at least better than other solutions.

Bibl.: WH II: 377, EM 541, IEW 825E, LIV 2.*peth₂-. -^peto



### prulna
prulna 'hoar-frost, rime' [f. a] (Pac.+)

Pit. *prus-wo- 'freezing'.

PIE *prus-uo- 'sprinkling, drop',

IE cognates: Skt. prusva /prusva 'drop of dew, cool drop' (cf Gerow 1973). Hamp 1973 discusses the etymology at length, and concludes that the most likely development is the following: *prusuma > *prurwina > *pruwma (dissimilation of r) > *pruma > prulna. Skt. prusva may preserve the nominal basis, derived from the verb which is still attested in Germanic (seeprurio); from this nominal basis, an adj. in *-ϊηο- was derived in Latin.

Bibl.: WH II: 378f, EM 541, IEW 846, Hamp 1973, Leumann 1977: 328, LIV *preus-. -^prQrid



### prurlo, -Ire
prurlo, -Ire 'to itch, tingle' [v. IV] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: prima 'glowing charcoal' (Cato+).

Pit. *prous-je/o- 'to itch', *prus-no- 'itching'.

PIE *preus-i-, *prus-no- '(cold and) wet, itching'.

IE cognates: W. rhew, Bret, rev, reo 'frost' < *preuso-, Skt. pr. prusnuvanti [3p.act.], pru$nute [3s.med.], aor. prusa [ls.sb.act.], ppp. prusita- 'to (besprinkle, wet'; maybe Lith. prausti 'to wash', SCr. pfskati, S\n.pfskati 'to splash' < *prus-sk-\ Go. frius [m./n.] 'frost', Olc.frjosa 'to freeze' < PGm. *freus-, OIc. OHG OE frost < PGm. *frusta- 'frost'. The meaning 'to be wet, itch' was metaphorically also applied to high temperatures, hence burning' inpruna.

Bibl.: WH II: 379, EM 541, IEW 846, Hamp 1973, LIV *preus-. -^pruma



### -pte
-pte 'emphasis' [ptcle.] (Naev.+: meopte, meapte, tuopte, tuapte, etc.)

Pit. *-pe/o-te.

PIE *-pe/o + *-te.

IE cognates: Lith. -pat Occurs mainly after poss. adj. (meus, tuus, suus) and sporadically after personal pronouns and is. Traditionally, -pte is explained as a syncopated form of *-pote < *-poti 'master', in the sense of 'self. Dunkel 2005 rejects this derivation because uno

<!-- pdf-page: 509; printed-page: 495; running-header: pubes                                        495 -->


specific parallel for the bleaching of * master' to limiting 'exactly, precisely, just, -self or even a plausible ambiguous environment in which this change might have occurred has ever been suggested" (p. 182). Instead, he proposes to connect -pte with Latin -pote in u/pote (but to me the indefinite analysis of -pote here seems preferable), with Gr. "emphatic or limiting" ποτέ (which Dunkel separates from interrogative/indefinite ποτέ), Hit. -pat < *pat(V) (not *-/), Lith. -pat and BrCl. *-pVtVas per Klingenschmitt 1980: 218-220. Kloekhorst 2008: 652f. argues that the spelling of Hit. -pat after vowels indicates that it contained a lenis first consonant, and hence cannot reflect

PIE */?-. Kloekhorst proposes *-bʰod. The British forms on which Klingenschmitts reconstruction is based (nom.sg.m. MW hunnoid, Bret. hennezh 'that') have received a different explanation by Schrijver 1997b; 66-70. That Gr. ποτέ represents two different words is uncertain and uneconomical. Still, for Latin and Lithuanian, Dunkel's analysis could be right. Ultimately, he analyzes *-pote as "an extension of adversative 2. *po- by means of the adverbial ending *-te." This analysis is worth considering, especially since *poti 'master' is indeed awkward for Latin; but in view of Latin -pe, an origin *-pe-te is also conceivable.

Bibl.: WH II: 379f, EM 542, IEW 842, Leumann 1977:466, Meiser 1998: 169. -* -pe



### pubes, -is
pubes, -is 'adult population, company; puberty; private parts' [f. e\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: pubes, -eris ^grown-up, adult' (Cic.+)? impubes, -eris/-is [adj.] 'who has not reached puberty' (Lucr>), pubescere 'to come to'maturity, ripen; grow to manhood5 (Enn.+); pubertas 'puberty, virility' (Cic.+); publicus 'of the people, public, state-' (Naev.+), publicanus [adj.] 'contractor, publican' (Pl.+), publicitus 'publicly, at public expense' (Pl.+),publicare 'to make public property, exhibit' (Naev.+).

IE cognates: Skt. pitmams- 'male, man' (nom.sg. pitman, ace. pumamsam (< *pu-mos-m), loc.pl. pumsu) , Shughmpwm 'down' (< PIr. *paumah < IE *peumos?), "The adj. publicus has adopted u from pubes, with which it was secondarily associated. Originally it was *popliko-, as shown by the OLat. spellings poplicod, poblicai (CIL), and was derived from populus* Since pubes, -eris probably continues an s-stem, and since poss.^-stem adjectives with the ablaut nom.sg, *-es, gen.sg. *-es-os were compounds ;in PIE, it is assumed that impubes was the older form, from which pUbes was back-formed. Adams 1985a has proposed to connect pubis with Skt. pumams- *man, male'/deriving both from a root *pum- 'pubic hair'. The meaning 'man' could then be explained from 'having pubic hair'. Latin -b- would have arisen from the PIE adj. *pumro-, replaced by full grade *peumro- whence phonetically Lat. *peubro- > puber^ arose. The -b- was then introduced into *(im)pumes. The main inner-Latin drawback of this explanation is the chronology of attestations: pubescere and publicus are attested in OLat., whereas (im)pubes only appear from Lucr. onwards. Stuber 2002 adduces further arguments against Adams' expanation from Skt. and PIE morphology. Schrijver 1991 favours the older explanation by Pedersen from a compound of *pu- 'man' (as in Skt ρύπιάηΎ) and *d h eh r 'to put' (for the formation, ctplebes), e.g. *pe/ou-ctehri but the analysis of the first element remains unclear.

Bibl.: WH II: 338f., EM 522, Leumann 1977: 117, Schrijver 1991: 375f., EWAia II: 144, Sihler 1995: 310, Stuber 2002: 43. -^populusypumilus

<!-- pdf-page: 510; printed-page: 496; running-header: 496                                     pudeo -->



### pudeO
pudeO 'to be ashamed' [v. II; pf pudut\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: pudor, -oris 'shame, decency' (Pl.+), pudicus 'sexually pure, chaste' (Pl.+), pudicitia 'chastity, virtue' (Pl.+), impudens 'shameless' (Andr.+), impudentia 'shamelessness, immodesty' (Pl.+), impudicus 'unchaste, immoral' (Pl.+), irnpudicitia 'unchastity' (Pl.+); depudicare 'to violate the chastity of (Lab.), dispudet 'to be utterly ashamed' (Pl.+), propudium 'shameful creature or action' (Pl.+), propudiosus 'shameful' (Pl.+), repudium 'rejection, divorce' (Pl.+), repudiare 'to reject formally, refuse' (Pl.+), repudiosus 'liable to be rejected' (Pl.).

Pit. *pud-e- cto be ashamed or shy or honourable'. WH connect pavid 'to hit', whereas EM hesitatingly refer to

PIE *speudr 'to press, hurry'. The latter is semantically more attractive, but this root is not otherwise attested without initial *$-. Another conceivable semantic connection would be with paved 'to be frighthened'. But the root *pw- itself is not certainly reconstructed for PIE, and we would have to add a root enlargement *-c/-.

Bibl.: WH II: 381, EM 542f, 571, IEW 827.



### puer
puer 'boy' [m. o] (Andr.H-; alsopuerus PL, Caecil.)

Derivatives: puera 'girl' (Andr.+), puerculus 'little son' (Pl.+), puenlis 'of a boy/child' (Pl.+), puenlitas 'childhood' (Varro+), pueriiia 'boyhood' (Ter.+), puella 'girl, young woman' (Pl.+), puellula 'girl' (Ter.+), puellascere cto become girlish' (Varro), puellitani cto act like a girl' (Lab.), puellus 'young boy' (Pl.+); repuerascere 'to become a boy again' (PL lx). Pit ^puwero-.

PIE *ph₂u~ero- 'smaller', Lat. puella < *puere-la 'little girl'. Since

PIE *ph₂u-ero- would possibly yield pre-Lat. *pawero-, puer may have been built more recently to the Pit. root *pau-y *pu- 'small, young'. Alternatively, puer was built to a stem 'masculine / pubic' possibly found in pubes.

Bibl.: WH II: 382, EM 543, IEW 842f. → paucus,pubis



### pulcher
pulcher 'beautiful' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: pulchralia [n.pL] 'delicacies' (Cato), pulchritudd 'beauty' (Pl.+), pulchritas 'beauty' (Caecil.+);perpulcher 'very beautiful' (Ter.+). Maybe earlierpolcher, according to Prise, and CIL. The spelling -ch- is Hellenizing, hence we can posit a stem *polkro- or *pelkro-. WH and IEW assume that this belongs to

PIE *perkr 'variegated' with a dissimilation of *perk-ro- to *pelk-ro→ but one would rather expect a dissimilation of the second *r to *perko- in such a case. No etymology.

### pūlex, -icis
pfllex, -icis 'flea' [m. k\ (Andr.+)

Pit. *pusl-l

PIE *plus- 'flea'?

IE cognates: Sktplusi-, Sogd. 'fi&'h < PIr. *fr$a-9 Yidghz friyo < PIr. *fhtsi-ka-, Gr. ψύλλα (< *p.sw/-)3 Arm. low (<

PIE *plus ); Alb. plesht [m.]; Lith.

blusa, RuCS blhcha, Cz. blecha; OHGfloh< *ρΙακχ-, all
blusa, RuCS blhcha, Cz. blecha; OHGfloh< *ρΙακχ-, all 'flea'. The many different forms of 'flea' seem to belong together, but it is impossible to reconstruct one PIE preform. Taboo deformation and folk etymology often interfere with the names of insects. Latin pul- might reflect *pusl·, with metathesis from *plus-,

### pullus
pullus 'drab-coloured, sombre' [adj. o/a] ( Andr.+)

Pit. *polwo-.

PIE *p(o)l-u-o- *gray\

IE cognates: Skt.parusa- 'grey, grey-brown', YAv.pourusa- 4 grey (of hair)' < *pe/ol(H)-wso-, Gr. πολιός 'grey, grizzled' (< *πολι-(ρ)ό-). Has been derived from the same root as palled (itself not completely clear), but the semantics of 'pale' and 'sombre' are not necessarily connected, and the sequence pull- is difficult to combine with pall·. IEW states that (expected) *po/- < *p/- was changed into pul· under the influence of/?-, but this is an ad hoc assumption which is furthermore contradicted by polliiced and pollud (to mention only the examples of *poll- plus a back vowel). Nussbaum 1997 proposes to connect -pullus with PIE ^pe/ol^i/u-) 'gray, dark colour' found in other languages; this seems the best solution proposed so far. Similarly, Driessen 2005: 46f.

Bibl.: WH II: 386, EM 544, IEW 804f„ Nussbaum 1997: 191. -^palled ■■ ^

2 t

pulmo Mungs' [m. n] (Pl.+) *

Derivatives: pulmoneus 'consisting of the lung' (Pl.+).

Pit. *plu-moril

PIE *plu-mon-? [adj.] 'floating > lung\

IE cognates: Skt. kloman- (< *ploman- < *pleu-mon-) 'the right lung; [pi.] the lungs', Gr. πλεύμων 'lung', Lith. plauciai [pi.], OCSpljusta [pi.] 'lungs' < BSl. *plou-tio-, *pleu-tio- [n.]. Two etymologies are mostly found in the handbooks. It has been suggested that pulmo goes back with metathesis to *plumo> and was borrowed from Gr. πλεύμων. This has the disadvantage that we expect a long vowel *ρΙύιηδ, and furthermore, that the word for Mung' is not often borrowed (which does not exclude the possibility, of course). The other option is that pulmo continues an inherited adj. *pleu-mon 'the floating one" (since lungs float on water); we would then still expect an outcome *pliimo, maybe with metathesis *pulmo. Alternatively, one could posit *plu-mon with the zero grade of *pleu-\ still, a metathesis to *pulmon would have to be assumed,

Bibl.: WH II: 386, EM 545, IEW 837f., Leumann 1977: 101, 371, Biville 1990 Ϊ: 353, LIV*pleu-.-^/?/wi/



### pulpa
pulpa 'fleshy parts of a body, best part of the meat' [f. a] (Cato*)

Derivatives: pulpamentum 'small piece of meat' (Andr.+), pulmentum 'small portion of meat or fish to start a meaF (Pl.+), pulmentarium 'condiment of a pulmentum' (Cato+).

Pit. *pelp-a- 'meat', *pelp-m(e)n- 'meat'.

It. cognates: U. pelmner [gen.sg.] n. 'meat' < *pelp-men-.

U. shows that the vowel in the first syllable was *e3 hence pulpa < *pelpa- and
U. shows that the vowel in the first syllable was *e3 hence pulpa < *pelpa- and pulmentum < *pelp-mento-,

Bibl.: WH Π: 386f., EM 545, Unterm^nn 2000: 528.



### puis, -ltis
puis, -ltis 'kind of porridge' [f. t] (Cato+)

Derivatives:pultiphagus 'eating porridge' (Pl.).

Pit. */?<?//-/-.

IE cognates: Gr. πόλτος 'porridge'. Since pult- can reflect *po//-, puis is suspect of being a loan from Gr. πόλτος 'porridge'. Yet Gr. o-stems are usually borrowed into Latin as o-stems, so there must be more to puis. EM consider an Etruscan mediary, which cannot be ascertained. Since πόλτος itself does not have an IE etymology, both words may go back to a basis *polt-, Latin to an i-stem *polti-. The root *pol(H)~ 'flour' can then be compared with pollen < *pol(H)-n-. Putative *pol(H)- 'flour' might be a loanword from a Mediterranean language, or belong to an as yet unknown PIE root.

Bibl.: WH II: 387f., EM 545, IEW 802. -* pollen



### pulvis, -eris
pulvis, -eris 'dust' [m. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: pulvisculus 'dust' (Pl.+), pulverare 'to be dusty; pulverize' (Pl.+), pulverulentus 'dusty* (Varro+), pulvmus 'cushion, pillow' (Pl.+), pulvinar [n.] 'support for a beached ship; cushioned couch' (Pl.+), pulvlnaris 'of a cushion5 (Varro-l·); dispulverare 'to pulverize' (Naev.).

Pit. *pe/oknt{-isy 'dust'.

PIE *pe/olH-u- 'chaff.

IE cognates: see s.\.palea. Pulvis may reflect *pe/ol(V)w-is-, with a rare /s-stem which may have been adopted from cinis, -eris 'ashes'. WH and IEW derive pulvis from

PIE *pelH-u-(h₂) 'chaff seen in Skt. palava- 'chaff, husk', OPr. pelwo, Lith. pelus 'chaff, Ru.polova (see s.v. paled), but Schrijver 1991 objects that the semantic connection of 'dust' with 'chaff is uncompelling. In my view, it is also difficult to connect 'flour' and 'chaff, since they are each other's opposite when processing grain. Of course, via a primary meaning 'to grind' or 'fine dust', they may be connected. Thus, it remains possible to derive pulvis from *pe/olH-u- (>

Pit. *pe/olaw-\ but not from *polHu- >

PIE *polu- (Saussure's effect), since *polw- would yield Lat *poll-* The noun pulvmus can stem from *poluis-no-: a pillow used to be filled with straw or chaff

Bibl.: WH Π: 388, EM 545, IEW 802, Leumann 1977: 321, Schrijver 1991: 256f. → palea



### pumex, -ids
pumex, -ids 'pumice' [m. k] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: pumiceus Of pumice' (Pl.+)> pumicare 'to rub smooth with pumice' (Lucil.+).

Pit. *poim-Vk-.

PIE *(s)poHi-m- 'foam'. EM argue that the close similarity of pumice with a sponge explains why *(s)poim- 'foam' was used for 'pumice'. But since spuma does not mean 'sponge', one might more

<!-- pdf-page: 513; printed-page: 499; running-header: pungo                                        499 -->

safely argue that pumice looks like petrified foam, and that this explains the metaphor.

Bibl.: WH II: 388f., EM 545, IEW 1001. → spuma



### pumilus
pumilus 'dwarf, pygmy' [adj. ο/ά] (Mart.+)

Derivatives: pumilio 'dwarf, midget' (Lucr.+; CJLpoum-).

IE cognates: Gr. 7υυγμαΐος 'as laige as a fist, dwarf-like; pi. pygmies' (from πυγμή 4 fist'); Πυγμαλίων PN, maybe originally a non-IE loanword. According to EM, pumilio was the primary form, from which pumilus was back-formed. For pumilio, the morphology suggests a borrowing from the Greek PN Πυγμαλίων, but this was rejected by WH, who doubt the change of *-ugm- > -urn-. If direct borrowing from the Greek PN is rejected (note that pumilio is not a PN), one might save the borrowing hypothesis by supposing that the Romans borrowed πυγμή 'fist' as uptime and derived pumilio 'who is only of the size of a fist' from it, or that they borrowed an (unattested) Greek noun *πυγμιλος. For Latin gm > mm? ctflomma. Quite differently, Adams 1985b: 244 interprets pumilio as * little hairy one' and connects it with pubis 'pubic hair, body hair' which might reflect *peum-. Morphologically, this interpretation would be easier.

Bibl.: WH Π: 389, EM 545. -^ pubis Λ



### pungo
pungo 'to pierce, sting' [v. Ill; pf. pupugl, ppp. puncium\ (Naev.+)

Derivatives: punctariola Skirmish' (Cato apud Fest), expungere 'to prick thoroughly; cancel a debt' (Pl.+); pugio 'dagger, poniard' (Cic.+); pugil, -is [m.] 'boxer' (Ter.+), pugilici 'like a boxer' (Pl.), pdgilatus, -us 'boxing' (Pl.+), pugilatorius 'used in boxing' (Pl.); pugnus 'fist' (Pl.+), pugillus 'handful' (Cato+), pugillaris 'writing-tablet (small enough to hold in the hand' (Lab.+); pugna * fight' (Naev.+), pugnare 'to fight, combat' (Pl.+), pugnaculum 'bulwark' (Pl.), pugneus 'made of fists' (Pl.), dipiigndre 'to fight, do battle' (Pl.+), dipugnatio 'method of fighting a battle' (Cato), expugnare 'to capture, storm' (Pl.+), oppugnare 'to attack, oppose' (Pl.+), prdpugnare 'to fight in defence of (Varro+), prdpugnaculum 'bulwark, defence' (Pl.+), repugnare 'to fight back, defend' (Pac.+).

Pit. *pung- 'to.pierce, sting', *puk/g-n(o)- 'fist'.

PIE *pu-n(e>g/k-,

IE cognates: Gr. πύξ 'with the fist', πυγμή [f ] 'fist, fist-fight', πυγών, -όνος [m;] 'certain measure, distance from the elbow to the first finger-joint'; έχε-πευκής 'with a point', πεύκη [f.] 'pine-tree'. The nasal present can go back to a root *pugr or to *puk-y with voicing of the velar stop between nasals. In the latter case, pugil and pugio would have secondarily adopted this g; yet in view of the difference in meaning, *pug- seems to have been present separately from pungo, and with the meaning 'fist'. The noun pugillus presupposes *pug-en-lo- and hence an original w-stem; Lat pugnus and pugna (length of ν is automatic in front of -gn-) may then be derived from the «-stem. But they may also reflect *puk/g-no-. The meaning of pugnaculum suggests that it is an abbreviated form of prdpugnaculum^ rather than an immediate derivative of pugnare (Serbat 1975: 194).

Bibl.: WH II: 383f, EM 543-546, IEW 828, Meiser 2003: 189, LIV *peu(g}-.

<!-- pdf-page: 514; printed-page: 500; running-header: 500                                        pupa -->



### pupa
pupa 'girl; dolF [f. a] (Varro+)

Derivatives: pupus 'boy' (Varro+), pupula 'little girl, pupil of the eye' (Lucr.+), pupulus 'little boy* (Cat+), pupilla 'little girl; pupil of the eye' (Lucr.+), pupillus 'minor, ward* (Cato+). According to EM, the f. forms are original, while the m. forms are sporadic derivatives from them. This remains uncertain. The forms pupa/-us seem to be a reduplicated form of *p£- as found in puer. IEW derives pupa from a root for 'to inflate', which is theoretically possible; but in view of the productivity of reduplication in Latin when it comes to affective words ('father', 'mother', etc.), a connection with other words in */»7- seems preferable.

Bibl.: WH II: 389f., EM 546, IEW 847f. →puer
