### pOr(i)go, -are
pOr(i)go, -are 'to clean, clear' [v. I] (Pl.+; purigare in PL, Varro)

Derivatives: purgamentum 'means of cleansing; that which is cleaned away, rubbish' (Varro+), purgatio 'ridding, clearing' (Cato+); expurgare 'to free from dirt or blame, purify' (Pl.+), expurgatio 'cleansing, excuse' (Pl.+), perpurgare 'to clean thoroughly, purge' (Pl.+; PL perpurigare).

Pit. *p£r, *pun- [n.] 'fire'.

It. cognates: U. pir, pir [nom.acc.sg.], pure [abl.sg.], pureto [abl.sg. + -to] n. 'fire' < */wr, abl. *pured.

PIE *peh₂-ur [nom.acc], ph₂-uen-s [gen.] 'fire'.

IE cognates: Hit. pahhur Ipahhuen- [n.] 'fire, embers, fever', CLuw. pahur- [n.] cfire'(?) < *peh₂-ur, *ph₂-uen-; Gr. πυρ, gen. πυρός, Ann. howr, Go. fon* gen.funim, OIc. funi [m.] Olc. furr, fyrr, fyri, OHG OS OFr. jiur, OHG alsofuir 'fire'; ToApor, ToBpuwarypwar 'fire'. Since Lat purigare does not show a medial long vowel which one would expect from *puro-ago-, it will contain the word for 'fire' */?wr- (rather than *pttro- 'clean') and have meant 'to lead the fire about' (so as to purify); thus Dunkel 2000a: 94. The noun 'fire' is also attested in U. pir, pur-. According to Janda 2000: 44-46,

PIE *peh₂ur 'fire' might refer to the 'striking' of sparks used to light a fire.

Bibl.: WH II: 390f., EM 546f., IEW 828, Untermann 2000: 557f., LIV *pieh₂-. → pavioypurus



### pūrus
pQrus 'clean, pure' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: impums 'dirty, fouF (Pl>), impuritia 'impurity' (Pl.+), impuratus 'filthy' (Pl.+).

Pit. *puro- 'pure'.

PIE *ph₂u-ro- 'clear, cleansed'.

IE cognates: Olr. ι/r, W. ir 'green, fresh' < *piiro- 4 clean', Skt. pavate [3s.med.] 'to become clean', punati [3s.act.] 'to clean', YAv. puitika- 'serving for purification*, OHG fouwerj.fewen 'to sieve, to sift'; Lith. puras, puraT 'winter corn', Latv. piirs 'grain measure', RuCS pyro 'spelt', Ru. pyrej 'couch-grass', Gr. πυρός, Dor. σπϋρός 'wheat' [m.] < *puHro- 'wheat' (< **clean'?).

<!-- pdf-page: 515; printed-page: 501; running-header: pustula                                     501 -->

Latin and Celtic show a stem *pwro 'clean' which is cognate with Ilr. *puH- 'to clean*. Janda 2000: 39^43 connects this with the Gr. and BSL words for 'wheat5. He argues that wheat was referred to as 'pure' or 'cleansed' because the grains could be separated from the hulls more easily than in the case of other, older kinds of grain. He connects Lat ρανιδ and posits an original root *peh₂- 'to strike', with w-extension *peh₂u-. The meaning 'to clean' would have developed through cleaning by means of striking. Thus, we would have *peh₂u- 'to strike' > 'to clean', *puH-ro- 'clean' > Gr., BSl. 'wheat'. According to Weiss 1996b, the hapax salaputium in Catullus may also belong here. He explains it as a loanword from O. *salaputim 'purification of salt' < *sal-put-io-7 derived in Oscan from a cp. *sal-put- 'purifier of salt' < *-puH-t- 'who purifies'· In the Catullus passage, 'purification of salt' would be a metaphor for 'refinement of wit'.

Bibl.: WH II: 390f., EM 546£, IEW 827, Schrijver 1991: 247, Untermann 2000: 557f., LIV l.*peuH-, -^ρανιδ,plusypur(i)g5



### pūs, pūris
pus, pfiris 'pus5 [n. r] (Lucil.+)

Derivatives: purulentus 'with much pus' (Cato+); suppurare 'to fester under the surface' (Cato+); piitere 'to rot, stink5 (?l.+\ pHtidus 'rotting, rotten5 (Pl.+), putescere 'to begin to rot' (Cato+), expiitescere 'to rot away' (Pl.+),pw/or 'rottenness5 (Cato+); puter, -tris [adj.] 'rotten, foul* prumbly' (Andr.+), putr^re 'to be in a state of decay' (Pac.+), putrescere 'to decay, disintegrate' (Varro+), putrefacere 'to cause to rot' (Varro+), putridus 'rotten' (Caecil.+). *

Pit. *pits- 'pus', *puto- 'rotten', *putri- 'rotten'.

PIE *puH-o/es- [n.] 'pus\ *puH-to-, *puH-tro- 'rotten'.

IE cognates: Olr. othar 'sickness, sick man' < *putro-\ Skt. puvas- [n.] 'pus' < *puH-os, puyati 'to rot, stink', puti- 'foul, stinking', YAv. puiia- 'to decay', puiti- [f] 'becoming foul, decay'; Gr. πόον [n.], ττύος [n.] 'pus', πϋός [m.] 'animal milk', πύύομαι 'to rot, decay'; Lith. puvhis 'piece of mouldered wood, (pi.) rotten stuff, Latv. puvesis 'pus5 < *puH-es-io-, Lith.jpw// 'to rot, decay'; OIc. fuinn [adj.] 'rotten'. Nom.acc.sg. pus may be due to generalization of *puH-s- from the gen.sg., or to a phonetic development *puos > *puus > pus in the nom.sg. The forms piitere etc. are probably based on a ppp. *piito- to the root *puH-. For puter and its possible Olr. cognate othar, Schrijver 1991 is hesitant to choose between *pHu-tro/i- and *puH-tro/i-. In either case, the short vowel resulting in Italo-Celtic is surprising.

Bibl.: WH II: 391 f., EM 547, IEW 848f, Leumann 1977: 380. Schrijver 1991: 234f, 339,341,534, Stuber 2002: 136f., LIV 2.*peuH-.



### pustula
pustula 'blister, pustule, bubble' [f. a\ (Tibullus+; \Bn&nXspusula,pussula)

Derivatives: praeputium 'foreskin of the penis' (Varro+).

IE cognates:

PIE *pus< Sktpusyati 'thrive, flourish', Lith. piisla 'blister, bladder', Ru. pwdyj 'chubby5, pwcnut' 'to swell5, Norw.y^y-sa 'swell';

PIE *pw/-: Lith. piisti, 3s.pr. jpiwta, pretpwfo 'to swell', Lith. pus//, 3s. pucia 'to blow' (with analogical long vowel).

<!-- pdf-page: 516; printed-page: 502; running-header: 502                                        puteus -->


Might be related to PIE words continuing *put- 'to blow' or *pus- 'to blossom, swell', but nothing specific can be said about the relationship, which in any case is uncertain because the semantics are npt compelling. The appurtenance of praeputium *'what is at front of the *ρΰί(ο)-* (Weiss 1996b: 355) here is uncertain, but a denomination of'penis' as 'which swells' is conceivable.

Bibl.: WH II: 392, EM 547, IEW 847f.



### puteus
puteus 'well (for water), pit' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: putealis 'of a weir (Lucr.+X puticuli [m.pl.] 'a pauper's graveyard1 (Varro+), Puteofi [m.pl.] 'town near Naples' (Varro+), Puteolanus 'of PuteolF (Cato+). Short u makes it impossible to directly derive puteus from pavio 'to strike\ It might be related to putare 'to prune', but this is semantically less attractive, and the suffix -eus can then hardly be interpreted as indicating a material. Therefore, puteus may well be a loanword.

Bibl.: WH II: 393, EM 547, IEW 827.



### putō, -āre
puto, -fire 'to prune (trees), scour (wool); make up (accounts), think, reckon' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: putus 'pure, genuine1 (Pl.+); putator 'who trims trees or bushes' (Varro+), putatio 'pruning, opinion* (Varro+), putamen 'outer cover, shell' (Pl.+); amputare 'to cut off, prune away' (Lucil.+), computare 'to calculate, reckon' (PI-+), deputare 'to complete the pruning; regard as, assign' (Pl.+), disputare 'to argue, debate' (Pl.+), exputare 'to cut off; examine thoroughly' (Pl.+), interputare 'to prune here and there' (Cato+), perputare 'to give a clear outline of (Pl.), supputare 'to prune below, reckon' (Cato+).

Pit. *puto- 'cut off, clean1.

PIE *pHu-t<5-. The adj. putus must be the primary word of this family, putare the verbal derivative. Its original meaning clearly was 'to cut off unwanted branches, prune a tree', whence 'to ponder over, reckon'. On the hapaxperputare, see Kiimmel 2004b: 353. The short vowel in putus is ascribed to original oxytonesis by Schrijver 1991. For the reconstruction of the root, see s. v. pavio. If Lith. pfauti goes back to *peut-, the root must be *phiU~; but LIV posits *pih₂u- on account of Gr. πταίω 'to thrust', ToB pyakar 'they struck down' Latin putus does not allow to decide between *ht and *h₂.

Bibl.: WH II: 393f, EM 548, IEW 827, Schrijver 1991: 241, LIV *pieh₂-. -^ pavio



### putus
putus 'boy' [m. o] (Verg. - a conjecture)

Derivatives: putillus 'very young, tiny' (VL);pullus 'foal, chick, young of an animal' (Pl.+), pullitra 'young chicken' (Varro), pullarius Of chickens' (Pl.+); pusus 'boy' (Vmo+),pusa 'girl' (Varro+), pusillus 'very small, tiny' (Naev.+).

Pit. *put-o- 'boy\ *put-lo- 'little boy, young of an animal1.

It. cognates: Fat putellio [nom.sg.] 'little son'; CX puklum, puklu [acc.sg.], puklui [datsg.], puk. [nom.sg.], pukelef [gen.sg.?], Mars, puclefs, PaeL puclois [dat.pl.], SPic. puqloh [datsg.?] 'son' < *pu-tlo-.

<!-- pdf-page: 517; printed-page: 503; running-header: quaero                                     503 -->

PIE *put-lo- 'son'.

IE cognates: Skt putra- 'son, child, young of an animal', Av. pudra- 'son'; Lztv.putns 'bird'; OCS p-btica 'bird', Ru.potka 'bird', Cz. ptak 'bird', all <BS\.*put- 'bird'. Lat. pullus < *put-slo-, as shown by the dim, pusillus < *pussitto- < *putslo-lo-. Thus, whereas Sabellic continues

PIE *putlo-, Latin has inserted an -s-. The dim. putillus was productively built to putus. The latter seems the most basic form *puto-, but it is attested only in Empirical Latin in a conjectural form. The form piisus might be a back-formation to pusillus. Romance continues a VLat. preform *puttus with the characteristic geminate consonant of expressive words. A connection of the stem *put- v/ith puer is difficult to establish.

Bibl.: WH II: 385f.f 392-394, EM 544-549, IEW 842f., Giacomelli 1963: 253L, Hamp 1983, Meiser 1998: 119, 125, Untermann 2000: 599. ~>puer

## Q



### quaerō, quaesō
quaero, quaesfi 'to seek, request' [v. Ill; pf. quaesivT, ppp. quaesitum] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: quaeritare 'to kgep looking for, seek'' (Pl.+); quaesitio 'searching' (Pl.+X quaestio 'examination, inquiry' (Pl.+), quaestor 'magistrate, quaestor' (Pl.+), quaestus, -T/-0s 'the gaining or pursuit of income' ".(Pl.+), quaestuosus "lucrative, successful' (Pl.+), quaesticulus "petty profit' (Lucil.+); quaesere [pr. tantum] 'to seek, request* (Naev.+); conquirere 'to collect, hunt down' (Varro+), conqutsitor "inspector* (Pl.+), exquirere 'to ask about, inquire' (Pl.+), exquisitim 'with diligence' (Varro), inquirere 'to investigate' (Pac.+), inquisitus 'unexamined' (Naev.+), perquirere 'to search everywhere' (Pl.+), perqumtor "one who hunts out' (Pl.), requfrere 'to seek, inquire' (Pl.+), reqmritare 'to seek repeatedly' (Pl.).

Pit. *kʷai-s-e/o- [pr.].

It. cognates: O. kvaisstur, κραιστορ [nom.sg.], kvaisturei, kvaizstur, Mars, qestur [nom.pl.], U. kvestur [nom.sgj, cvestur [nom.pl.] 'quaestor', a loanword from Latin, U. kvestretie [loc.sg.£] 'the office of quaestor', derived with *-e//o- or *-etia- from kvestur.

PIE *kueh₂-i- [pr.] 'to acquire*.

IE cognates: Gr. πεπάμαι (Dor. Argos), fut πάσομαι 'to get, acquire'; OPr. quoi Ί , you want', quaits "desire', Lith. kviesti 'to invite'; Possibly also OAlb. 3s. kaa "he has' <

PIE *kuoh₂-e [pf.] 'has got' (thus Matzinger 2003). The etymology as *kuois- to *kueis- 'to see' is deemed uncertain by Schrijver 1991. LIV adopts the etymology proposed by Szemerenyi 1960b: 232, viz. as *ko-ais-(s)-e/o- to

PIE *h₂eis- 'to ask, seek'. Yet the preverb *ko(m)- is usually still recognizable as such, Nussbaum (2007b) proposes to analyze quaero as a se/o-pr. to an /-pr, *kuehri- to a root *kuehr 'to acquire'. For this root, he compares Gr. πεπάμαι (Dor. Argos), fut. πασομαι "to possess, to acquire' < *kueh₂-* The pr. quaeso can be interpreted as a recharacterized, new desiderative *kuais-s-e/o-. This seems to

<!-- pdf-page: 518; printed-page: 504; running-header: 504                                      qualus -->

me the best proposal to date. Leumann 1977: 591 suggests that the suffix -Γ- in quaesm, -Ttus would have been analogically adopted from semantic neighbours such as cupire and petere, -w. But the ί-pfi ^s well-established in Plautus for quaesfvT, whereas it is found only a few times with peto and not at all yet with cupio. Hence, things may be reversed: quaerd seems to be the origin of the /-pf. in peto and cupid. The -5·- in quaesivi can then continue an s-aorist *kuais-s~» The original ppp. must have been *kʷaistos.

Bibl.: WH II: 3961, EM 551, Schrijver 1991: 461 f, Untermann 2000: 423-425, Meiser2003: 126, LIV ?*(i^eh₂-.



### qualus/m
qualus/m 'wicker basket' [m./n. ο] (Pl.+; pi. quala Cato)

Derivatives: quasillum/s 'small wicker basket' (Cato+).

Pit. *kʷat-slo- 'sieve, filter'?

PIE *kuot-i-? Lat quasillum < *quats-lrelo- (for the phonetics, cf pullus vs. pusillus; Leumann 1977: 208). Lat. qualus has often been compared with OCS kosb 'big basket of wickerwork' < *k?os-io-. In that case, Lat. qualus /colum would go back to *kʷos-lo-, with *uo > *ua in the case oiqualus. But it is unlikely that *sl>had become */before this unrounding (which was Proto-Italic, cf. Schrijver 1991: 475), in which case the condition for the unrounding (viz. an open syllable) was not given. A connection with quatio 'to shake, toss', which is rejected by WH, is advocated by Leumann 1977: 208, and seems quite conceivable to me: cf. Dutch door^slag 'sieve' to slaan 'to beat'. This would imply an instrument noun *quat-slo- 'sieve', which would have been formed after

PIE *kuot-i- 'to shake' became

Pit. *kʷat-i- (Schrijver 1991: 462f). Whereas Schrijver reconstructs PIE o-grade, LIV assumes a secondary α-grade present to an e-aorist (unattested in Latin). The only way which I see to connect colum with these forms, is by assuming that a s/o-derivative was formed from *kuot- before the sound change to *kʷat-, and another one after this sound change. Thus: *kuot-slo- > *kuosslo- > *kuolo- > colum; and *kuat-slo- > *kuasslo- > qualum. Obviously, this whole story hinges on the likelihood of the connection with 'to shake', so that a completely different origin remains possible.

Bibl.: ·: WH II: 397, EM 551, IEW 635, Schrijver 1991: 462, LIV *(s)kuehlt-- → cdlum, quatio



### quatio, -ere
quatio, -ere 'to shake, toss, hurry along' [v. Ill; ppp. quassum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: quassus, -us 'shaking' (Pac.+), quassare 'to shake repeatedly, damage' (Naev.+X conquassare 'to shake violently' (Cato+); concutere 'to agitate, strike' (Pl.+), discutere 'to shatter, disperse' (Lucil.+), excutere 'to shake out, throw off (Pl.+), percutere Ho strike heavily, kill' (Pl.+).

Pit. *kʷat-i-.

PIE *(s)kuot-i-?

IE cognates: Lith. kiisti, 3s. kunta 'to recover*, kutki, 3s. kuta 'to thrive, prosper' < *£w/-; OS scuddian, OHG scutten 'to shake' < *skut-, OIc. hossa 'to throw' < *kut-s-. Together with the Gm. words for 'to shake', and maybe Lith *fcw/~ 'to recover', quatio

<!-- pdf-page: 519; printed-page: 505; running-header: quattuor                                   505 -->

may point to a root *ku(o)t-, with awkward schwebe-ablaut. It might be a non-IE word in origin. The appurtenance Gr. πάσσω 'to sprinkle* is possible from the semantic side, but since complicated and unwarranted analogies are necessary to explain Greek -a-, Latin and Greek would almost certainly point to non-IE *kuat- 'to shake, strew'.

Bibl.: WH II: 399£, EM 552f., IEW 632, Schrijver 1991: 260, 462f„ Meiser 2003: 115, LIV *(s)kuehtt-. → qualus



### quattuor
quattuor 'four* [num. indecl.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: quattuordecim ' 14*; quater [adv.] 'four times, on four occasions' (Pl.+), quaterm 'four at a time' (Cato+); quadru/i- 'four' (in compounds) (Naev.+), quadrans, -ntis [m,] 'one quarter (of an ass, of a pound, etc,)' (Lucil.+)> quadrantal [n.] 'measure of the volume of a cubic foot* (Pl.+), quadratus 'divided into four parts, square' (Afran.+), quadraginta '40' (Pl.+), quadragesimus 'fortieth' (Cato+), quadrageni 'forty at a time* (Cato+), quadragenarius 'holding 40 units' (Cato+), quadragiens '40 times' (Varro+), quadringentT '400' (Pl.+), quadnm (Cato) 'four at a time' (Cato+); quartus 'fourth' (Pl.+; Quorta Praeneste, CIL L328), quartarius 'a quarter of a sextarius' (Cato+), quartato 'for the fourth time* (Cato).

Pit. nom.n. *kʷetwor ( » Lat. *Kvatw6r)3 nom.m.f. *kʷe/atwores> n. *kʷetwora; *kʷatworto-.

It. cognates: maybe the Ven. theonym trumusiiati- contains *fcw/rw- 'four'; O. pettiur 'four' (< *lFetur), petiropert, petirupert [acc.pl.n. + -pert] 'four times' (< *kʷeWr-), Festus 226 petoritum Gallicum vehiculum: alii Osce quod hi quoquepitora quattuor vocent U.peturpursus 'quadruped' [datpl.] < *\Fetur-pod-*

PIE *kʷetuor [nom.n.], *kʷetuores [nom.m/f], *kʷtuerns [acc.m/f,], *kʷturom [gen.] 'four', *kʷturo- ( » *kʷtuf-to-) 'fourth', *kʷturs 'four times'; *kʷtur- in compounds; *kʷt(u)r-dJcmt- 'forty'.

IE cognates: Olr ceth(a)ir [m.], cetheoir [f], MW pedair [f.] 'four'; Skt. catvaras [nom.m.], caturas [acc.mj, catvari [nom.acc.n.], catasras [nom.acc.f.] 'four', catur [adv.] 'four times', YAv. caufiard [nom.m.], catura [acc.m.], caturqm [gen.], cataqw [acc.f.] 'four', catffiarvsat- 'forty', cauru-dasa- 'fourteenth', caprus [adv.] 'four times*; Gr. τέτορες 'four* (Dor.), πίσυρες (Η.) [nom.pl.], τέτρασι [datpL], τέτρατος 'fourth', τρυ- 'four*; Arm. c'ork\ Lith. keturi, OCS cetyre, Go!fidwor, ToA stwar, ToB stwer 'four'. Lat. quattuor as opposed to Sab. pet- has introduced *kʷat- with secondary -a- from the oblique case forms with

PIE *kʷt-, and from the other usages of 'four' with a zero-grade root, such as in compounds *kʷtru-. The ending -or can directly reflect

PIE *-<?r, but maybe also *-or-es* The form quadr- found in '40' and in compounds is explained by Schrijver from voicing of */ between *C and *CCQ e.g. in *kʷtr-dkmt- '40' and in the ordinal 'fourth'. The same element quadr- was then used in other compounds. For 'fourth', Schrijver posits *A:wft/fto- > *kʷduiio- > *kʷaduorto-, and then loss of *i/ in front of *w, as in suavis, hence *kʷauorto- > quartus. The long a in quadraginta must be phonetic, and can be explained from *kʷtr-dkmt- > *kʷadr-Hkmt- (Kortlandt 1983a), and subsequent *-drHk- > *drak. In quadringentT, medial -n- must be analogical to quingentL Most of the secure quadra- spellings occur in front of a labial, cf. Coleman 1992: 424. For quater, Coleman suggests PIE

<!-- pdf-page: 520; printed-page: 504 -->

506 -que

*kʷturs (Skt. catuh) > *quaturs > *quatru$ > *quatrs > quater.

Bibl.: WH H:394f, 399-401, EM 553f, IEW 643f, Lejeune 1974: 330, Leumann 1977: 486, 488, 492, Schrijver 199^: 182, 491f., Coleman 1992: 393ff., 417, Sihler 1995: 41 If., 430, Beekes 1995: 212-216, Meiser 1998: 171, Untermann 2000: 550f. → triquetrus



### -que
-que 'and' [cj. postpos.] (VOLat. (Madonnetta-inscr.)+)

Derivatives: quisque, quaeque, quidque [pron.] 1,Q\^Ty\quodque [adj.] 'every'.

Pit. *-kʷe.

It. cognates: Ven. -kve, FaL -cue 'and', 0,lL -p, -p, CX -p]e 'and'.

PIE *-kʷe 'and, -ever'.

IE cognates: Hit -kku 'now, even, and', PaL -Ai/ 'and?', CLuw. -ku 'and, furthermore?'; Skt. -ca, Av. -ca, OP -ca 'and, also, if, Gr. -τε 'and', Myc. -qe 'and'; Go. -uh "and, also5, nih 'if not'. After pronouns and adverbs, -que can also have a generalizing meaning.

Bibl.: WH II: 401f., EM 555, IEW 635f., Giacomelli 1963: 241, Lejeune 1974: 338, Dunkel 1979,1980, Untermann 2000: 520. → qui



### queo, quire
queo, quire 'to be able' [v. II/IV; pf. quivi\ forms pr. queo, quis, quit, qmmus, qmtis, queunt, ipf. quibam, fut. qulbo, ps. qmfur] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: nequeo^ nequls, nequit etc. 'to be unable' (Pl.+; Andr. nequinoni), ptc. nequiens^ -euntis.

IE cognates: see s.v. eo. The alternation between Is. -eo, 3p. -eunt and the ί-conjugation in most other forms points to the verb eo> ire 'to go' as a basic element of these forms. It is generally accepted that the verb was derived from a contracted form of 3s. *neque it or *neque itur 'it does no go', 'it is impossible' > 'he is unable' > nequitinequitur. By removing the negative element we-, reanalysis led to a new verb queoy quire 'to be able'.

Bibl.: WH II: 402, EM 555, Leumann 1977: 521, Meiser 1998: 223, LIV *h,ei-. → eo



### quercus
quercus 'oak-tree' [f. u] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: querneus Of oak' (Cato+), querquetum 'a wood of oaks' (Varro+).

Pit. *kʷerl?u/o-.

PIE *pericw-o/u- c(kind of) oak\

IE cognates: OHG fereheih, Langob. fereho 'kind of oak', OIc. jfarr [m.] 'tree, man' < PGm. *ferxu-; OHG forha, MHG vorhe, OE jurh, OIc. fura [f.] 'fir-tree' < */κ/χο-; OIc. jyri [n.] 'fir forest', NHG Fohre, MoDu, vuur-hout < *βνχ-ΐη-. The word for 'fir-tree' is unrelated according to Kluge-Seebold 1999, but in my view the similarity in form is too close to keep them apart. Still, the root ablaut is strange. The Latin word shows the regular Italo-Celtic assimilation of *p - kw - > *kʷ - kw-. The oldest attestations are of a w-stem, but since the cognate Gm. forms can also be explained from a stem in *-χ™α-9 since m, and f. u- and o-stems tend to influence each other in nom. and acc.sg., and since o-stem tree names tend to be feminine, it cannot be excluded that quercus continues a Pit. o-stem. IEW and other handbooks also connect Go. fairhus 'world', ORG fer(a)h, OE feorh 'life, soul' < *perkʷ-o-, but Schaffiier 2001 convincingly rejects this connection. His alternative etymology of the

<!-- pdf-page: 521; printed-page: 507; running-header: qui                                      507 -->


latter words as belonging to the root *perk- 'to plough' (see Lat. pored) is attractive, Gothic fairguni [n.] 'mountain1 must also be separated; Schafiher 2001: 193 connects Av.pauruuata- fountains', Gr. πεϊραρ 'end, border1, Hit. pi-e-ru-ni [dat.sg.] 'stone' <

PIE *per-ur, -un-, which is semantically much more straightforward than a connection with 'oak'. Finally, also unrelated to quercus are probably the BSl. words for the 'god of thunder': OLith. perkunas, Lith. perkunas 'thunder', perkunija f. 'thunderstorm', Latv. p^rkuons 'thunder, god of thunder\ ORu. Perunt 'god of thunder', Ru.perun 'thunder, lightning'. This means that only Latin and Gm. contain certain reflexes of a stem *perkʷ-u/o- 'oak\

Bibl.: WH II: 402f, EM 555, IEW 822f, Schaffner 2001: 190-194



### queror, -ī
queror, -ϊ 'to complain, protest1 [v. Ill; ppp. questum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: querela 'complaint, lament1 (Pl.+), querimdnia 'complaint, protest1 (Pl.+); conqueri 'to utter a complaint, bewail' (Pl.+).

Pit. *kʷes-e/o-1 *kʷes-e/o-.

IE cognates: Skt. svasiti 'to hiss, snort1, susma- [m.] 'hissing, roaring', YAv. susi [du.f] 'the lungs'; OIc, hvcesa 'to hiss, to snort', OE hwdsan 'to cough'. WH, IEW and LIV derive Lat queror from a PIE root *kues- 'to hiss' via 'to sigh'. This is possible, but semantically by no means compelling. EM are very hesitant about this etymology. Note that the Gm. forms in *hwos- and *hwes- may also belong to the root *kʷeh₂s- 'to coiigh' which is reflected m Gm. (OHG huosto, OE hwosta 'cough'), BSl. (koseti *to; cough', Ru. kasljat' 'tojeough') and Celtic; queror cannot be derived from this root. There is no alternative etymology, however.

Bibl.: WH II: 402f, EM 555, IEW 63If., LIV *lcues-.



### qui, quae, quod
qui, quae, quod 'what, which? who, that1 [pron. relat., indef] (VOLat.+: Forum cippus quoi 'qui' (sg. and pi.), quos 'quos1. Paradigm: nom.sg.m. qui, nom.sg.f quae, indef. qua, nom.acc.sg.n. quod; gen.sg. quoius (Elog.Scip.) > cuius (/cuiiusf), datsg. quoiei > cui, acc.sg.m. quern, acc.sg.f. quamy abl.sg.m.n. quod^ > quo, abl.sg.f qua; abl.sg. qui? nom.pl.m. qui, VOLat. ques (indef), f. quae, nom.acc.pl.n. quae, qua, gen.pl.m.nXguorum, f. quorum, dat.abl. quibus, VOLat. queis > quis, acc.pLm. quos, i.quas)

Derivatives: (1) qua 'by which road or means, how?; inasmuch as' (Lex XII, Pl.+), quaqua 'in every place' (Pl.+), quasi (CIL quansei, quasei) [cj., adv.] 'as if, like; practically1 (Andr.+), quo [adv.] 'where to, what for?' (Andr.+), quo [cj., adv.] 'whence, whereby, that1 (Lex XII+), quoquo 'to whatever place' (Pl.+); quoque 'also1 (Andn+); (2) qualis 'what kind of?; such as' (Pl.+); (3) quam [adv.] 'how?; as' (Lex XII, Andr.+), quamde, quande 'than' (Andr.+); quamquam 'however much, although' (Pl.+), nequam 'bad, useless1 (Naev.+; comp. nequior, sup. nequissimus), nequiter 'badly, wickedly' (Pl.+), nequitia 'moral worthlessness, villainy' (Pl.+); quantus [adj, interr., rel.] 'how great, how many' (Naev.+), quantusquantus 'however great' (Pl.+), quantillus [adj.] 'how little?', quantisper 'for how long?' (Caecil.+), quantulus 'how small' (Lucr.+); quando 'when' (Andr.+); quisquam, quicquam 'any, anyone' (Naev.+); (4) A number of compound indeiinite pronouns is discussed s.v. their

<!-- pdf-page: 522; printed-page: 508; running-header: 508                                       quies -->

second member: -cumque, -libet, -nam, -vis.

Pit. m.sg. nom. *kʷoi, gen. *kʷojjos (see s.v. cuius), dat. *kʷosmoi // *kʷojjei, ace. *kʷom, abl. *k*5d, f.sg. nom. *kʷai9 ace. **kʷam, abl. *kʷad, n.sg. nom.acc. *fr"W; nom.pl m. *Γσ/ // **"&, £ **"& ( » ***"&' in Lat), n. *K"a // *kʷai, gen. m.n. *kʷosom, f. *kʷasom, dat.abl. *kʷois (< *kʷois), ace. m. 'Woro*, f. *bTans.

It. cognates: (1) U. poi,poei,poe [nom.sg.m.], O. pai, paUpaei.pae [nom.sg.f.], pud, pod [nom.acc.sg.n.], O. pui, U. pusme [dat.sg.m.], O. paam, pam, Pael pam [acc.sg.f.], O. poizad, U. pora [abl.sgX], O. pus, Pael puus, U. pure, puri [nom.pI.m.], O. pas, pas [nom.plf.j, O. pai, pai [nom.acc.pln.], U. pafe [acc.plf.]; U. svepu, suepo < *suai 'if + [nom.acc.sg.n.] 'that which, who\ In U., *-Fwas added in the nom.acc. Other forms: O. pod [cj.] 'that' < *kʷod; O. pod, U. pure, porsi, porsei, porse [indeel relat. pron.] < *kʷod, U. *kʷod-T; U. pue, pue 'whereto* probably *#1o+i; O. pukkapid, pjocapid, pocapit 'whenever' < *kʷod-ka-kʷid. The origin of *-ka- is unknown* O. adpud [cj.] 'as long as' < *ad-Jfod; (3) O. pam 'than' (after comp.); maybe SPic. panivu if from *kʷam-diitou; O. pontes [gen.sg.n.j, U. panta [nom.sg£] 'how big*; U. pane, pane 'when' < *kʷam-de, panupei [adv.] 'whenever' < *kʷam-do-kʷeiAid.

PIE sg. nom.m. *kʷo+i, f. *kʷeh₂, α *kʷod, acc.m. *kʷod, gen.m.n. *kʷoso, datloc. *kʷosm-, ins. *kʷoi; pi nom.m. *kʷoi, n. *kʷ(e)h₂,

IE cognates: Skt ka-, Av. ka- 'who, which, someone*, OP kas-ciy 'someone', Gr. τέο [gen.sg.] 'whose', πό-ύι 'where'; Lith. kas 'who', OCS kb-to 'who', ceso [gen.sg.] 'whose'; Go. has 'who'. The paradigm of the relat. and indef. pronoun in Latin is a mixture of reflexes of *kʷo- and *kʷi-; for the forms of the latter (in the paradigm of Latin qui, these are quern, ques, quibus, ablsg. qui), see s.v. quis. The acc.sgm. *kʷom has been preserved as quom > cum in the conjunction. The gen.sg. cuiius must be from *kʷosio + *-s. The dat.sg. *kʷoiiei is probably analogical to the gen.sg. Sabellic retains the older PIE form. The adv. quasi and quoque (< *quo-que) show shortening of the first long vowel in front of the originally enclitic -si and -que, and then shortening of the final long vowel through iambic shortening. Lat. qualis < *kʷeh₂-li-, quantus < *kʷanto- < *kʷehrnt^

Bibl.: WH II: 394, 397,404f, EM 551,556, IEW 644-648, Leumann 1977: 472-475, van der Staaij 1995: 124-135, Beekes 1995: 206, Meiser 1998: 165-167, Untermann 2000: 53, 510-513, 591-599. → cuius, cum, quis, qot, ubi9 uU uter



### quiēs, -ētis
quies, -6tis 'sleep, rest, repose' [£ /] (Pl.+; abl. quie Laev.)

Derivatives: quies, -tis [adj.] 'quiet, peaceful' (Naev.+), quietus 'at rest, peaceful, quiet' (Pl.+); quiescere 'to repose, rest, sleep' (pf. quievf, ppp. quietum) (Pl.+), acquiescere 'to rest, find peace' (Pl.+), conquiescere 'to take repose, go to sleep' (Pl.+), requiescere 'to rest, be idle* (Pl.+), requies, -etis 'rest, relaxation' (ace. requiem, dat. requiei, abl. requie) (Lucr.+).

Pit. ***/*■#■ [f.], *JHe- [aor.].

PIE *kʷiehrti- 'rest, peace', aor. *kʷi(e)hr 'to rest'.

IE cognates: Av. $(ii)a- [pr.] 'to be calm, glad, rest', OP s(i)yata- 'peaceful, happy (on earth)', s(i)yati- 'welfare, peace

<!-- pdf-page: 523; printed-page: 509; running-header: quins                                      509 -->


(on earth), happiness (after death)', Khot tsata- 'rich, happy'; Ann. han-geaw 'rested', OCSpociti, Is. pocijg 'to rest', Ru. pokoj 'rest, (obs.) chamber'. The e-inflexion of requies must be recent, built on the nom.sg., as is shown by its late appearance (Meiser 1998: 150). The Latin pf quie-νϊ probably continues the PIE root aorist; on the model of gnoscere : gnovi, crescere : crevi, a new pr. quiescere was built.

Bibl.: WH II: 406, EM 557, IEW 638, Schrijver 1991: 140, Meiser 1998: 150, 205, LIV *k y ieh r .



### quīnque
quTnque 'five' [num. indecl.] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: quindecim 'fifteen' (Lex XIK), quinquaginta 'fifty' (Pl.+), quinquagenarius 'containing fifty of anything' (Cato+), quinquagesies 'fifty times' (Pl.), qumgend 'five hundred' (Pl.+); quinquatrus, -uum [f-pL] 'festival of Minerva, lasting five days in March' (Pl.+), quinquertid 'one who competes in the pentathlon' (Andr.); quim 'five at a time' (Pl.+), quvnarius 'containing five each; coin worth five asses9 (Varro+), quJnavicenarius 'relating to twenty-five' (Pl.), quincunx, -ncis 'a pattern of five, five-twelfths' (Varro+); qum(c)tus 'fifth' (Pl.+), quintanus 'of the fifth' (Varro+), QuintTlis 'name of the fifth month' (Varro+).

Pit. *kʷenkʷe 'five', *kʷ(e)ntfvto- 'fifth'.

It. cognates: O. pumperias, pumperias [nom.pL] , pumperiais [dat.abl.pl.], U. pumperias [nonr.pl.] in O. probably 'fifth day', in U. unclear meaning, < ^pompe ' 5 ' + *-(e)ro *+ jo- (er probably from *peteria- « *peturia- '4'); O. pomtis 'five times' < *pompe-tis, suffix *-is (Lat. -ies) » *-tis by analogy with sept-, oct- cent-. U. puntes [nom.pL], puntis [databl.pL]4?' < *pomp-ti- 'group of five'?

PIE *penkʷe 'five', *pnkʷ-(t)o- 'fifth'.

IE cognates: Olr. coic, W.pymp, Skt. panca 'five', pancama- 'fifth', paktha- PN, pankti- [fj 'set of five', YAv. panca 'five', puxba- 'fifth', Gr. πέντε, Thess., Lesb. πέμπε 'five', πέμπτος 'fifth', Aim. hing 'five'; PAb.pese, OCS^ffc, Ru.£/af 'five', OCS j?fte, Ru.pjatyj 'fifth\ LitL· penki 'five', penktas 'fifth', Go^m/'five', OUG fimfio 'fifth', ΎοΑράη, ΤοΒpis ' five\

Pit. *kʷenkʷe yielded *Ifinkʷe by regular sound change. Subsequently, the *k in the ordinal *kʷinkto- was spirantized and the preceding vowel phonetically lengthened: tkTinxto-. Finally, the spirant was lost, yielding quintus. From the ordinal, ϊ was analogically introduced into the cardinal *quinque » quinque. Lat. quim < *kʷenkʷ-(s)no-, quincunx < *qmnqu- + uncia. The -a- in quinquaginta for *quinqueginta was drawn from 'forty'. In Sabellic, the surrounding stops *kʷ apparently had a rounding effect on the vowel, yielding *pompe, which is attested indirectly through its derivatives.

Bibl.: WH II: 407f, EM 558, IEW 808, Coleman 1992: 395, 411, Beekes 1995: 214-216, Sihler 1995:413, Meiser 1998: 78, 171, 175, Untermann 2000: 601-604, 608.



### quirts, -Itis
quirts, -Itis 'name for Rome's citizens in their peacetime functions' [m. i] (Enn.+; usually pi.)

Derivatives: quintare 'to make a public outcry' (Lucil.+); Quirinus a god worshipped on the Quirinal hill (Lex Reg., Lucil.+), Quirmalis 'of Quirinus'

<!-- pdf-page: 524; printed-page: 510; running-header: 510                                        quis -->

(Varro+); proquiritata 'publicly announced' (Lex XII). The etymology as *ko-wir- to vir 'man' is not credible phonetically (cf. curia) and not very compelling semanticaUy. Most likeley, qitiriti- is derived from a basis *quiri-. In view of the sporadic assilation of *e to an i in the following syllable, an etymology as *queri- cannot be excluded. One may thus think of *kʷes-, *k*is-, *Kver-, *kʷir-. Also *kuHi-s-, zero-grade to quaero, is theoretically possible. But since Roman legend has it that Quirts and Quirinus are connected with Sabellic immigrants into Rome, it may also be a loanword.

Bibl.: WH II: 409, EM 559, Leumann 1977: 133.



### quis, quae, quid
quis, quae, quid 'who? what?' [pron. inten\] (Lex XII, Andr.+; forms: sg. m.nom. quis, ace. quern, abl. qui, n^nom.acc. quid, pi. m.nom. ques (rarely), m.f.n dat.abl. quibus)

Derivatives: qui [adv.] 'in what way, how? in that way' (Naev4+), quia 'because' (Pl.+), quisquis, quidquid 'whoever, whatever' (Andr.+), nequiquam 'in vain, without cause' (Pl.+); A number of compound indefinite pronouns is discussed s.v. their second member: -cumque, -dam, -dem, -nam, -pe, -piam, -quam, -que, -v&; for aliquis 'someone', see s.v. alius. Pit interrog.: m.f.sg. nom. *kʷis, ace. *kʷim, ins. *kʷi, n.sg. nom.acc. *kʷid; nom.pl. m.f. *kʷes, ace. *kʷins, n. nom.acc. * Γ ί ( » *kʷia), databl. *kʷifos; indef.: *kʷiskʷis 'whoever', *kʷidkʷid, 'whatever'.

It. cognates: Presam. νεπις [nom.sg.], νετηες [gen.sg.?] 'nobody', O, pis,/?*?, Vol.pis, U. pisi, U.pisi [nom.sg.mX], O. pid, Pael. pid, SPic. pid, U. piri, pife, pefe, pirse [nom.acc.sg.n.], SPic. pim [acc.sg.m.f.], O. piei [datsg.], piis, Marr. peis [nom.pl.m.f.], U.pifi [acc.plm.f.]; enclitic after svai 'if O. suaepis, suae.pis, SPic. suaipis, U. svepis [nom.sg.m.£], O. svaipid, svai.pid [nom.sg.n.] 'who? what?', 'every'; U. pifi < +-/; UL pefe, persi, persei, perse, pirsi, pirse 'if < *pidrV, O. pidum, pidum [nom.acc.sg.n.],pieisum [gen.sg.m.] 'someone, something' < pis + -urn; U. pisher 'whoever wants'; O. pis.pis [nom.sg.m.f ], SPic. pimpih [acasg.m.f], O. poizeipid [loc.sg.n.] 'every5 or 'whichever'; Marr. nipis [nom.sg.], O. nephim (mistake for *ne.pim) [acc.sg,] 'nobody'.

PIE m.f.sg. nom. *kʷe // *kʷis, ace. *kʷim, gen. *kʷeso, datloc. *kʷesm-, ins. *kʷihi, n, nomuacc. *kʷid; nom.pl. m.f. *kʷeies, ace, *kʷins, n. nom.acc. *kʷih₂ 'who, what; someone, something'.

IE cognates: Olr. ciay W, pwy, Co. pyw, Bretpiou 'who' < T e z ; Hit, kui- / kue- / kuua- 'who, what' < *FV-, **V, *kʷo-y Pal. kui-, CLuw. kui-, HLuw. kwi-, Lyd. qi-, Lye. ti- 'who, what', Skt. kirn 'what, which; why, what for', ηά-kih 'nullus', ma-kih 'ne quis\ kidis- 'what sort of, cid [enc.ptcle.] 'even, indeed', Av. ma cis 'no one', YAv. dm 'because', Av. -eft, OP -ciy [encl.] 'indeed; -ever'; Gr. τίς [m, £], τί [n.] 'who? which?', τις, τι 'someone, something*, OCS Cb-to 'what'. The paradigm of the indef. pronoun is a mixture of reflexes of *kʷo- and *kʷi-; for the forms reflecting *kʷo- (among others, all fem.sg. forms, and the m.n. gen.dat.sg.) see s.v. qui The abl.sg. form and adv. qui 'how' probably reflects an old ins.sg. *kʷi < *kʷihi. It is also found in some compound adverbs, such as nequiquam. The cj. quia continues the PIE nom.acc.pl. n. *kʷih₂.

<!-- pdf-page: 525; printed-page: 511; running-header: racemus                                   511 -->

Bibl.: WH II: 405, 410, EM 559, IEW 644-648, Leumann 1977: 472-475, Schrijver 1991: 8If, van der Staaij 1995: 124-135, Beekes 1995: 206, Meiser 1998: 164-167, Untermann 2000: 498, 52If., 558-561, Lazzarini-Poccetti 2001. → qui



### quot
quot 'how many?, as many as' [adj. indecl.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: cottidie 'every day, daily' (Pl.+), cottidianus 'normal, regular, daily' (Pl.+X quotanms [adv.] 'annually' (Pl.+), quotiens 'how many times?, as often as' (Pl.+), quotquot 'however many' (Varro+), quotumus [adj.] 'the howmanieth?', quotus [adj.] 'howmanieth, in what proportion?' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *ΓοΛ\

PIE *kʷe/o-ti 'how many'.

IE cognates: Skt. kati 'how many', YAv. caiti 'how much, how many times?' < Ilr. *cati <

PIE *kʷetft; Gr. πόσος 'how great, how many' < *kʷotjo-, Bret pet 'how many'. Cottidie from *kʷoti dies 'how many days?', with influence fiompostridie. The reflex co- < *kʷo- is regular; in quot, qu- has been restored on the model ofiquantus or other related words in qu-.

Bibl.: WH II: 412, EM 561, IEW 644-648, Leumann 1977: 270, 277. → qui

## R



### rabio, -ere
rabio, -ere 'to be frenzied, rave' [v. Ill] (Caecil.+)

Derivatives: rabies 'savageness, passion' (Ter.+), rabidus 'raging, mad' (Lucr.+), rabiosus 'rabid, mad' (Pl.+).

Pit. *rab-1 *ra/-. Schrijver 1991 rejects the connection with Skt. rabh- 'to grab' and proposes to connect ToA rapunie 'desire' and maybe Gr. εραμαι 'to desire, love' instead. Yet this etymology is not very convincing from the semantic side. LIV connects Parth. rf- 'to attack', MoPrav~'to go'. Cheung 2007 derives the latter from a root *rabʰ- 'to be in violent commotion', which may be seen also in Khot. aruh- 'to move, shake' (+ *5-), barah- {brah-) 'to soar up' and in other Iranian languages. In theory, Iranian could reflect *(H)reb^- while Latin would have a secondary foil grade *rab- « *e/a/orb- (< •Hrb(h>-). Since we are dealing with two isolated verbal stems, however, this etymology remains uncertain. MHG reben 'to move' is isolated within Germanic, and may belong to *reiban 'to rub'.

Bibl.: WH II: 413, EM 562, IEW 852, Schrijver 1991: 305f., LIV 1 ,*rebʰ-.



### racemus
racemus 'bunch, cluster (of grapes or other fruit)' [m. o] (Bibaculus-l·)

IE cognates: Gr. ραξ, ραγός 'grape', ρώξ, ρωγός 'id.', Alb. rrush 'resin'. Probably a loanword from a Mediterranean language.

Bibl.: WH II: 414, EM 562, Schrijver 1991: 306.

<!-- pdf-page: 526; printed-page: 512; running-header: 512                                      radius -->



### radius
radius 'ray of light; spoke' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: radiosus 'radiant' (Pl.+)> radiatus 'radiant; with spokes' (Varro+), radiare 'to shine' (Lucr.+). « It is unlikely that radius belongs to radix 'root', as WH and IEW suggest: the meaning is completely different. Initial rod- may reflect a root *(H)rHcth)-, for which there is no clear candidate. The element *-<Pt)- might be a root enlargement.

Bibl.: WH II: 414f., EM 562, IEW 1167, Schrijver 1991: 182.



### radix, -Tcis
radix, -Tcis 'root' [f. k] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: radicitus [adv.] 'by the roots, completely' (Pl.+), radicula 'a little root' (Laev.+X eradicare Ho tear by the roots, exterminate' (Pl.+).

Pit. *wrad-i-.

PIE *ur(e)h₂-d-iH- 'root'.

IE cognates: W. gwraidd 'roots', OCo. grueiten, Bret. gwrizienn 'root7 < *uradio- < %urh^d-io-^ Gr. ραδιξ, -ικος 'branch, twig', Go. waurts, OE wyrt, MHG wurz < *urh₂d-i-, OIc. rot < *ureh₂d-; Alb. rreze, Geg rraze 'root'. In view of. the ablaut, it seems likely that

PIE *-d- was a suffix, but this cannot be ascertained.

Bibl.: WH II: 415, EM 562f, IEW 1167, Schrijver 1991: 182f., 1995: 173-175, Sihler 1995: 179, Vine 1999b. → ramus



### rado, -ere
rado, -ere 'to scrape, scratch, shave' [v. Ill; pf. rasi, ppp. rasum] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: rostrum 'drag-hoe7 (Pl.+), rastellus 'wooden rake7 (Varro+), ramentum 'shaving' (Pl.+), ramenta 'shaving' (Pl.+), rasus, -us 'shaving, scraping' (Varro), rattum 'a scraper' (Plin.); abradere 'to rub away, knock off (Ter.+), corradere 'to rake together, amass' (Pl.+), deradere 'to scrape, shave off (Cato+), eradere 'to scrape away, erase' (Varro+), irradere 'to scrape (onto)' (Cato); rallus 'sheer, fine'.

Pit. *rasd-e/o-,

IE cognates: W, rhathu, Bret, rahein 'to scrape, shave7 < PCI. *rasd-e/o- 'to scrape, scratch'. Lat. ramentum < *rad-m-, rallum < %radAo-, The adj. rallus is used with tunica, and it may belong here if it meant 'smoothely woven, polished' < *mdrlo-; but note that this is morphologically a strange formation. EM derive rallus from rarus, but then one would rather expect *rarulus. Lat. rostrum has often been explained from *radrtro-, but Serbat 1975: 316 points out that the length of. a is unproven, and that rostrum could go back to *ra<2-/ro- < *rHdrtro-. Since this word family is only found in Italo-Celtic, a PIE origin is uncertain. If *rasd- is a secondary full grade for *rsd-, one might consider an original root present *r(e)sd- (thus Meiser 2003); but not *Hrsd→ which would give Lat. * Vrsd-.

Bibl.: WH II: 415, EM 563, IEW 854, Leumann 1977: 307, Schrijver 1991: 309£, Meiser 2003: 120, Schumacher 2004: 528, LIV ?*rasd-.



### raia
raia 'a sea-fish, ray' [f. a] (Plin.)

Pit. *ragja- or *rajja-.

IE cognates: MDu. rogghe, rochghe, MoDu. rag, MLG rugge 'ray' < PGm. *rugg→

<!-- pdf-page: 527; printed-page: 513; running-header: rapio                                      513 -->


OE reohhe (once in a gloss), ME rezge, reyhhe 'ray' < *τεγχε (or *Γαχχ/α-Ί). Rata must be interpreted as IrajjaL Whether the English words for 'ray' can go back to *Γαχχ- is disputed; if they do, we have an interchange a/u within Germanic. In the absence of any further cognates and in the semantic field of animals, this could point to a loanword from a substrate language. The similarity between the Gm. words and rata is striking, and since Latin Ijjl can reflect *gj, we might posit *raK-y *ruK- 'ray'.

Bibl.: WH II: 415, EM 563.



### ramus
ramus 'branch, twig' [m. o] (Cato+)

Derivatives: ramosus 'with branches, branching' (Lucr.+), ramulus 'little branch' (Cato+); rames, -itis [f.] » ramex, -icis 'the lungs; varicocele' (Pl.+).

Pit. *wra(d)mo-l

PIE *ur(e)h₂-(d-)mo-?

IE cognates: see s.v. radix. Possibly from *radrmo- < *wrad-mo-, if cognate with radix 'root'. The semantics can be justified by referring to Gr. ραδιξ, -ΐκος 'branch, twig', showing that *uite)h₂d- could mean either 'branch' or 'root\ Gr. ράδαμνος [m.] 'branch, twig' is probably not cognate, cf. Schrijver. Of course, if the PIE root was merely *urh₂-, ramus can also go back to *ur(e)h₂-mo-. The noun rames seems to be older in its f-stem appearance (Pl.) than as a Λ-stem ramex. According to EM, the lungs were called ramites 'branches' J»

due to the similarity of their form with the branches of a tree. .;

Bibl.: WH II: 416, EM 564, IEW 1167/Schrijver 1991:182, Sihler 1995: 209. → radix



### rana
rana 'frog' [f. a] (Varro+)

Pit. *ra-na-7 Onomatopoeic form, probably from a sound "r<f \ In theory, the root could be ?*reh₂(i)- 'to roar' as reconstructed by LIV for BSl. Since Gm. contains forms for 'to roar' with a final velar (MHG ruohen, ruohelen, riiehelen\ a preform *rak-sna- is also conceivable, although the Gm. suffix can easily be secondary; and Latin ragit ' brays' (of a foal), found in one gloss, is hardly a trustworthy comparandum.

Bibl.: WH II: 416, ΈΜ 564, IEW 859f.



### ranceo
ranceo 'to be rotten' [v. II] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: rancidus 'rotten' (Lucr.+). No etymology.

Bibl.: WH II: 417, EM 564.



### rapiō, -ere
rapiO, -ere 'to seize, take away' [v. Ill; pf. rapmy ppp. raptuni] (Naev.; surrepsit [5-filt.] Pl.)

Derivatives: rapidus 'strong-flowing, quick' (Pl.+), rapax 'greedy, rapacious, predator' (Pl.+), raptio 'carrying off (Ter.+), raptor 'robber, abductor' (Pl.+), raptare 'to carry away by force, drag ofF (Pl.+), rapister, -trl 'robber' (Lucil.), raptim 'in a rush' (Lucr.+), rapmator 'pillager' (Varro+); abripere 'to snatch away, kidnap' (Pl.+), arripere 'to grasp, get hold of (Pl.+)> corripere 'to grab, hurry off

<!-- pdf-page: 528; printed-page: 514; running-header: 514                                      rapum -->

(Pl.+), deripere 'to tear off, pull down' (Pl>), dnipere 'to tear to pieces, run after' (Pl.+), eru/ipere 'to seize away* (Pl.+), iproripere 'to snatch forth, rush forth₁ (Pl.+), surru/ipere, surpere 'to steal* (Pl.+), subreptTcius 'stolen* (Pl.+); rapsare 'to hurry along1 (Caes>).

Pit. *rap-i- 'to seize'.

PIE *hirp-i- [pr.] 'to seize1.

IE cognates: Gr. έρέπτομαι 'to devour, snatch away' < *rep-ie/o-, aor. 3. pi. άν-ηρέψαντο, Alb. rfep 'tear of, away, rob', Lith. ap-ripti 'to take, catch₁ (< *(H)reh!p-). If to a PIE root *h,rep-, rapid could reflect the zero grade *h₁rp- > *erp-, then with a secondary zero grade *rap- (to full grade *rep-). Differently Schrijver 1991, who posits a separate root *hirhip- for rapid and Lith. rdpti; yet both roots would have an identical meaning.

Bibl.: WH II: 417, EM 564, IEW 865, Schrijver 1991: 17, 306, Rasmussen 1993: 193, Meiser 2003: 250, LIV *(h0rep-. → repens, utor



### rapum
rapum 'turnip1 [n. o] (Cato+)

Derivatives: rapicius 'of turnips' (Cato+), rapa 'turnip' (Varro+), raptna 'turnip-crop, turnip-field' (Cato+), rapula 'small turnip' (Titin.+).

Pit. *rapo.

IE cognates: Gr. ράπυς, ράφυς [f.] 'turnip1, ράφανος, ραφάνη 'radish', Lith. rope 'turnip', RuCS repa, OHG ruoba, ruoppa 'turnip*. This cannot be a regular PIE word: Latin, Baltic and OHG ruoba require *aH> Slavic has /e/, and Greek single -a-. There is no prothetic vowel in Greek, and the vacillation between π and φ also suggests a loanword.

Bibl.: WH II: 418, EM 564, IEW 852, Schrijver 1991: 310.

ranis *of loose structure, sparse, rare1 [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: raro 'seldom' (Pl.+), rare 'seldom; sparsely, loosely' (PL; Col.)> rarenter 'seldom1 (Andr.+), raritudo 'looseness, openness1 (Lucr.+), rarescere 'to thin out, open out' (Lucr.+), rarefacere 'to make less solid' (Lucr.+). Antonym of densus. EM state that Lat. rarenter was formed on the example of frequenter. Rams has been connected with

PIE *fj2erH~ 'to disappear, perish₁ by EM, but this is not obvious semantically. Others connect Gr. έρημος 'lonely'. Schrijver is critical of all proposals: "The connection of rete and rams must be given up on formal grounds, and that of rete and έρημος on semantic grounds."

Bibl.: WH II: 418, EM 564, IEW 332£, Beekes 1969:36, Schrijver 1991: 17,310f.



### ratis
ratis 'collection of wooden beams, raft; boat, ship1 [f i] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: ratitus 'bearing the figure of a raft1 (Lucil.+).

Pit. *rati-.

PIE *h,rh r ti- [f.] 'rowing'? Uncertain. Ratis has been connected with Lat. retae 'trees in the bed of a stream1 and/or with OHG ruota, OIc. roda 'rod, pole1, OE rod, but neither is semantically

<!-- pdf-page: 529; printed-page: 515; running-header: ravus                                      515 -->

compelling. If cognate, this etymology would imply *Hrhjti- > Lat ratis. Since the

PIE root for 'to row' is reconstructed as *hirhj- (see s.v. remits), ratis might be interpreted as *Hrhrti- 'the rowing' > 'raft, boat'; note that rafts are normally propelled and steered by means of a pole.

Bibl.: WH II: 420, EM 565, IEW 866, Schrijver 1991: 306f„ LIV 2.*h,rehr. → remus



### raudus, -eris
raudus, -eris 'rough piece, lump; bronze coin' [n. r] (Lucil.+; var. rodus, rudus, roudus Paul, ex F.)

Derivatives: raudusculurn 'bronze coin' (Varro+).

IE cognates: Skt. loha- [m.] 'reddish metal', MP rdy 'copper, brass' <

PIE *h,roudh-o-, OCS ruda, Ru. ruda, Scr. ruda 'ore' <

PIE *h,roudh-h₂-, OIc. rauoi 'red iron ore'. Because of -au- and intervocalic -dr, raudus cannot be an inherited word from

PIE *hjr(e/o)udh-os 'red'; we would expect Lat. *rQbus. Raudus is probably a loanword from another IE language, in which *-</- became ~d~. The other IE languages show that the colour 'red' was used to refer to reddish ore, whether copper or iron or another metal.

Bibl.: WH II: 420f., EM 565, IEW 872f., Schrijver 1991: 265. → robur, ruber, rufus

ravus 'hoarse' [adj. o/a] (Paul. exF,) ?

Derivatives: raucus 'hoarse, harsh-sounding' (Pl.+), ravis\i.] 'hoarseness' (Pl.+), raviare 'to be hoarse' (Pl.+), rausurus 'about to become hoayse' (Lucil.).

Pit. *rawo-.

PIE *h₃rouH-o- 'roaring'.

IE cognates: Skt. ruvati [pr.], rauti [pr.], rava- [pr.] 'to roar', aor. aravii\ rava- [m.] 'roaring, howling, noise', YAv. uruuato [geasg.] 'roaring', Khwar. rw- 'to grunt', Gr. ώρύομαι 'to howl, roar, complain', OCS rjuti 'to howl\ If raucus reflects *rawVko-, all forms can go back to

Pit. *rawo- or *rowo-. Schrijver dismisses raviare (or ravlrel) from the evidence since it occurs only once in PL in a corrupt passage. For *rawo- or *rowo-, he considers two possible etymologies: (1) to a root *Hrehj- 'to Ishout, sound' (Skt. rayati 'barks', OIc. ramr 'hoarse') as *Hrhruo-, or to a root *Hreu- 'to roar' (Skt. rauti, ruvati "to roar5, OE reon 'to wail') as *Hrou-o-. The latter form yields a better connection with forms outside Italic, and a better explanation for -a-. Vine 2006a: 237 therefore reconstructs *h₃rouH-o- 'roaring' > *rawo→ whence the abstract *rawi- would be derived. Raucus could then reflect *rawi-ko-.

Bibl.: WH II: 420f., EM 565, IEW 867f, Leumann 1977: 340, Schrijver 1991: 257f, 446, LIV *h₃reuH-.



### ravus
ravus 'tawny, grey' [adj. o/a] (Varro+)

Derivatives: (g)ravastellus 'old man' (Pl.).

IE cognates: OIc. gror* OE gr&g, MoE gray, OFr. gre, OS gra, gre, OHG grao 'grey' < *gre-uo-. The form (g)ravastellus (mss, ravisiellus and gravastellus) presupposes *(g)ravaster.

<!-- pdf-page: 530; printed-page: 516; running-header: 516                                       re- -->

With van der Staaij 1995: 57, I assume that

PIE *gfrr became Latin gr-. Hence, the variant gravastellus retains the original Latin reflex of the colour adj., whereas ravus could be explained as a loanword from Sabeljic - although the reflex in Sab. is also uncertain. We could posit *g h rh r uo- for Italic beside *gʰrehruo- for Germanic, but the ablaut would be disturbing- If a full grade, Schrijver suggests that original *gʰrehj-uo- may have been delabialized to *gʰreh₂-uo- in Latin and to *gʰrehruo- in Germanic. This is hazardous, and quite ad hoc. There may have been influence on ablaut and suffix from other colour terms, ctflavus.

Bibl.: WH I: 620, II: 421 f, EM 282, 565, BEW 853, Leumann 1977: 166, 302, Schrijver 1991: 298f,301,311, Sihler 1995: 158, Meiser 1998: 103.



### re-, red-
re-, red- 'back, reverse' [pref.] {red- in front of vowels and h-)

Derivatives: retro [adv, prep.] 'backwards, to the rear* (Ter.+); reciprocus 'moving backwards and forwards, moving in the opposite direction' (Acc.+), reciprocare 'to move a thing backwards and forwards' (Enn.+), reciprocicornis 'with the horns turned around' (Lab.).

Pit. *wre.

It. cognates: U. re- 'again' in restatu, revestu.

PIE *ure 'back1.

IE cognates: Alb. rre- [prev.] 'back'; Ru. rak, gen.sg. raka, SCr. rak, Sin. rak < PS1. *rakb 'crayfish, lobster' < *ure-hjkʷo- 'looking backwards' (Klingenschmitt 2004). The only acceptable etymology has recently been proposed by Klingenschmitt 2004, who reconstructs *ure 'back', The form rear is analogical to prddr beside pro-, and from metanalysis in verbs such as reddo < *re-di-d5. Dunkel 1979 explains reciprocus as a nominalization of *re-ttYe *pro-k"e 'both backwards and forwards', and connects it with other Skt. and Gr. instances of two semantically opposed preverbs coordinated by *-k™e 'and'. The first labiovelar was apparently delabialised in front of the following ρ and *kʷ; if Klingenschmitt's etymology of re as *ure is correct, there would even have been a fourth labial consonant in the word *ure-k"e *pro-kʷe. The resulting *(u)reke-prokwo- did not yield *recupricus because its two elements were for a long time recognised as two separate words, so that they developed as *reke-proko- until after vowel weakening (Cowgill apud Dunkel 1979: 189).

Bibl.: WH II: 4223 424, EM 565f, Leumann 1977: 340, 559f, Dunkel 1979, Klingenschmitt 2004: 247-252. -+procul



### recens, -ntis
recens, -ntis 'of recent origin, new, fresh' [adj. ni\ (Pl.+)

Pit. *wre-kent{i)-.

PIE *ken-t- 'young, new'.

IE cognates: Mir. cana, cano 'wolf cub'; Gaul. Cintu-gnatus, Olr. cat- 'first', MW cynt, MCo. kens, Bret, kent 'earlier' < PCX *kento/u-; Skt. kanya-, Av. kaine, kainw- 'girl' < kaniHn- <

PIE *ken-i-Hn- (or *keniH-n-l\ Skt. kanma- 'young', Gr. καινός 'new, unheard of < *kn-io~; ORu. kom> [m.] 'start', OCS is-koni 'from the start' < *kon-; maybe Go. hindumists 'last', OHG hintana, hintar 'behind', OE hindema 'last' < *ken-t-. Probably a compound of re- 'again, back' and *kent(i)- 'young, first'.

Bibl.: WH II: 423, EM 566, IEW 563f.

<!-- pdf-page: 531; printed-page: 517; running-header: rego                                        517 -->



### redimiu, -ire
redimiu, -ire 'to encircle with a garland, surround' [v. IV] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: redimlculum 'a band to the back of a woman's head-dress' (Pl.+).

Pit. *do/em-o/i- 'binding, band'.

PIE *do/emh₂-o/i-.

IE cognates: see s«v. domare. The isolated position of this word makes it difficult to establish the etymology. It has been connected with Skt yam- <

PIE */em-, in which case we must analyse the word as *red-im-y and suppose a zero-grade form *im-i- 'binding, garland', or reduction of *-/em- in non-initial syllable to -i/w-. But the original meaning of yam- seems to have been 'to reach, obtain', which makes a connection more difficult. EM note that redimiculum is attested earlier than redimio, and suggest that it was based on amiculum 'cloak', prefixed with rear and with analogical lengthening of the vowel in front of -culum. Yet the use of red- in nominal cp. is secondary to verbal compounds (see s.v. re-\ and lengthening to -T- in this position is not trivial. Also, the difference in dates of attestation is not decisive (thus also Serbat 1975: 185). Alternatively, Latin -/- in redimio may reflect any short vowel in open syllable, which makes a connection with domdre 'to tame' possible. If re-dimlre is a denominative to a noun *dom-o/i- or maybe *dem-o/i- 'binding, band', it can be explained through regular sound change.

Bibl.: WH II: 424, EM 567, IEW 505. → domo



### regō, -ere
regfl, -ere 'to direct, guide, govern- [v. Ill; pf. rexi, ppp. rectum] (Pl.+; rected [adv.] in a Faliscan inscr.) V

Derivatives: regillus 'upright, vertical' (Varro+), regimen [n.] 'control, steering' (Enn.+), regio 'district, region; direction, line' (Pl.+), regula 'rod, rule' (Varro+), recta 'directly' (Pl.+), rector 'ruler, guide' (Cic.+); ergo [adv., prep*] 'therefore, then; on account of (Lex XII+), erga [prep.] 'against, next to; towards' (Pl.+), corgo [adv.] 'forwards' (Paul, ex K); arrigere 'to make to stand upright, raise' (Pl.+), corrigere 'to make straight, put right' (Pl.+), corrector 'who sets right' (Ter.+), derigere (> dirigere 'th c. AD) 'to align, steer, direct' (Andr.+), erigere 'to raise, erect' (Lucil.+), pergere 'to move on, proceed' (Naev.+), por(ri)gere 'to extend, hold out' (Andr.+), subrectitare 'to get up frequently from table' (Cato), surgere cto get up, rise, emerge' (Andr.+), consurgere 'to stand up, rise' (Lucr.+); expergere 'to awaken' (Lucil.+; -Γ, -itus\ experglscor, -scl 'to wake up, become awake' (Pl.+; III; ppp. -perrectus and -pergitus sum), expergefacere 'to stir up, arouse' (Pl.+).

Pit. *reg-efo~.

It. cognates: Marr. regenfai [datsg.] 'queen' (suffix vowel unclear), Ο. ρεγο '?' maybe gen.pl. *regom; U. rehte [adv.] 'right'.

PIE *h₃reg-e/o- 'to stretch, direct'.

IE cognates: Olr. a-traig 'to stand up', MW reaf, 3s. re 'to stand up' < PCI. *rege/o-, MCo. gorre 'to put', MBret. go(u)rren 'to raise, rise' < *uor-rege/o-; Skt. raj- 'to stretch, direct (oneself)' (pr. pyanti [3p.act], fiijate [3p.med.]), raji- [f.] 'direction', YAv. xrazaiia- 'to draw a line; lead', hqm.razaiia- 'to rise, become erect', OP rasta- 'straight, right, true; [n.] 'justice'; Gr. όρέγω 'to stretch'; Go. -rakfan, OHG re(c)chan 'to stretch'. The ppp. has -e- due to Lachmann's law. The pf. rexi continues a PIE ^-aorist *h₃r8g-s-. Corgo and ergo reflect *co-rogo and *e-rogo 'from the direction of, from a noun or adj. *rogo- 'direction' or 'stretching', which seems to be continued by the

<!-- pdf-page: 532; printed-page: 518; running-header: 518                                      remus -->


verb rogare 'to request' too. The etymology of experglscor is still disputed. Its ppp. experrectus contains the ppp. -rectus of rego, and there is a cp. pergere 'to move on', which is why some regard it as a compound *ex^per-reg- 'to move on up, wake up\ On the other hand, YAv. frayrisamna- 'waking up' seems to continue the root

PIE *higer- 'to wake up' with a suffix *-iske/o-, which is why some have regarded experglscor as a reflex of

PIE *higr-iske/o-. The most recent discussions are by Keller 1980 and Klingenschmitt 1982. Keller shows that expergo is probably a recent formation grafted on expergiscor. Klingenschmitt duely mentions the arguments for both solutions, and then opts for the latter; his discussion is adopted by LIV. His main argument for the separation of expergiscor from rego is the suffix -tsco, which, as Klingenschmitt mentions (p. 74), is mostly used for inchoative verbs derived from basic verbs in -/-. He observes that rego does not have such a suffix, and that hence expergiscor must contain a PIE sequence *-i(H)sk-, i.e. *eks~per/pro-hjgr-iske/o- (*eks-pro- would match the Av. form better). Yet since the verb 'to wake up' is not otherwise attested with a suffix *-£-, we are free to look for other solutions. Klingenschmitt himself notes that verbs in -Jsco sometimes alternate with verbs in -escd (conticisc- FL,perdolisc- Ace); the origin of this alternation is uncertain, but in view of tacere, dolere, it seems that -e- was original. The verbs in -escd have spawned new compound verbs in -efacio in OLat, cf. Leumann 1959: 277ff.: calesco > calefacio, etc. All these verbs conform to the same pattern, except expergefacio, attested from PL onward, which is built on a verb in -iscor. Leumann 1977: 258 has noted the irregularity, and notes "expergef- (statt ^expergif-y. But there is no indication that *expergifacio ever existed. The question is now: do we assume that *expergifacio was changed to expergefacio because of the pressure from the group in -efacio? Or do we assume that expergiscor has replaced ^expergiscor on the model of the group in -Tscol In view of the ppp. experrectus, I favour the latter explanation.

Bibl.: WH I: 273, 415, 429f, II: 426f, EM 201, 206, 567-569, IEW 854-857, Giacomelli 1963: 254, Klingenschmitt 1982: 73-77, Schrijver 1991: 127, Sihler 1995: 77, Untermann 2000: 631-634, Meiser 2003: 111, Schumacher 2004: 530-533, LIV *h₃reg-. —»rex, rogo, rogus



### remus
remus 'oar' [m. o] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: remex, -igis Oarsman, rower' (Pl.+), remigium 'outfit of oars; rowing' (Pl.+); biremis 'having oars arranged in pairs' (Sis.+), triresmos [acc.pl.f.] (CIL 1.25, Columna Rostrata), triremis (Caes.+) 'having three oars', [quinqueresmjos 'ship with oars arranged in five' (CIL 1.25), septerfesmom 'ship with oars arranged in seven' (CIL 1.25).

Pit. *re(s)mo- 'oar*.

PIE *hirehr(s)mo- 'oar'.

IE cognates: Olr. raid, -ra 'to row' < *ra-ie~ < *hjrohrie/o-; Skt. aritar- 'rower, ferryman', antra- [n.] 'oar' < *hlerh,-ter-, -tro-, Gr, έρέτης [m.] 'rower', έρέσσω 'to row', έρετμόν 'oar', Lith. irti 'to row', irklas 'oar'; OIc. roa, OE rowan, MHG riiejen 'to row', OHG ruodar, OE rodor 'rudder' < *h₁roh!-. The forms in -resmo- from the Columna Rostrata, an inscription from 260 BC which

<!-- pdf-page: 533; printed-page: 519; running-header: reor                                     519 -->

was probably renewed in the first century AD (Diehl 1964: 36) seems to show that remus reflects *resmo-. On the other hand, the same inscription has primes 'first' and not *prismos. This is one of the reasons for assuming that the whole inscription is archaizing (cf. Leumann 1977: 7), in which case -resmo- is uncertain evidence. If the s is old, we may reconstruct *hjrehrsmo- 'oar' (thus Schrijver 1991: 19). This has the disadvantage of schwebe-ablaut when compared to Skt. aritor-, but it would fit the place of the o-ablaut in Germanic and Celtic, which may well be an iterative ograde. If the s of CIL 1.25 -resmo- is hypercorrect, remus may reflect *hirehrmo-. Since the other IE languages show instrument nouns in *-f/o- or *-iro- (maybe remade to *-tmo- in Greek), I have a slight preference for *-smo- in Latin, since this is more typically an instrumental suffix. Lat. remex, remigium contain (the root of) ago 'to do'.

Bibl.: WH II: 428, EM 569, IEW 338, Leumann 1977: 7, 209, Schumacher 2004: 529, LIV 2.*h,reh r . → ratis



### renes, -him
renes, -him 'kidneys' [m.pl. ί] (Pl.+)

Pit. *ren-.

PIE *h₂r-en, -en- 'kidney'? *sren- * loins'?

IE cognates: Olr. aru [f. n] 'kidney, gland', W. arenn [f.] 'kidney, testicle' < PCI. *aron-; Hit. iVZV)hah(ha)rl· [c/nj 'lung(s), midriff [nom.sg.c. hahris, nom.sg.n. hahri, hahhari, dat.-loc.sg. hahrisni] ; ToB arance 'heart' (both the physical heart and the seat:of the emotions), ToA arinc 'id.' < PTo. *arance < PIE ^h^h^ri- (EIEC 329). : Mastrelli 1979 connects renes with OPr. straunay, Lith. strinos [f.pL] 'loins', dial. also srinos, Latv. striena 'loins' < Proto-Baltic *srin-l, Av. rana- 'thigh' < *srana-l < *sren- 'hip, loins' (IEW 1002). The semantic shift from 'loins' to 'kidneys' is quite conceivable. Less certain seems the connection with Gr. ράχις 'spine', since there is evidence for a PGr. anlaut cluster *ur-. Alternatively, one could connect the Celtic words for 'kidney'. The etymology as unreduplicated *h₂r-en- next to PCI. *aron- from reduplicated *h₂e-h₂r-en- is possible in theory, but the difference in reduplication remains unexplained.

Bibl.: WH II: 4?8f.t EM 569f, Driessen 2003b: 358.



### renldeo
renldeo 'to shine, reflect' [v, II] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: remdescere 'to grow bright' (Lucr.). Could be cognate with niteo if the root was *(H)ni(H)-, with different suffixes -t- and -d^-. IEW connects OP naiba- 'beautiful' and Olr. noib 'holy', but this remains a mere root comparison.

Bibl.: WH II: 429, EM 570, IEW 760. → niteo



### reor, reri
reor, reri 'to think, believe' [v. II; ppp. ratus 'constitutional, determined'] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ratio 'calculation, account, reason' (Pl.+), ratiuncula 'small account' (Pl.+), irritus 'not ratified, unrealized' (Pl.+).

Pit. *re-(jefo-) 'to think', *rafo- 'thought, counted'.

PIE *Hreh r [aor.?], *Hrh r to- [ppp.]-

IE cognates: Go. rapjo 'number, account',

<!-- pdf-page: 534; printed-page: 520; running-header: 520                                     repens -->

ga-rapana 'counted', OS rethia, OHG radja, redea 'accountability\ The original meaning 'to count' was preserved in ratus 'counted' > 'legal', esp. in expressions with pars: pro rata parte 'ιΛ proportion' (Cic.+), rata pars 'a fixed proportion'. The noun ratio was derived from ratus, either directly (cf. the model natus : natid) or via an earlier /i-abstract *ratis 'reckoning'. The PIE root *Hreh r might also be the basis of

PIE *(H)rehtdh- 'to bring in order, arrange'. Schrijver ascribes the root to

PIE *h₂r- 'to fix'; another candidate would be *hireh r 'to ask, investigate' (Hit. arfiie/a)-21 'to consult an oracle' < *hiriirie/o-), for which LIV reconstructs a root aorist

Bibl.: WH II: 419, 429, EM 570, IEW 59, Leumann 1977: 366, Schrijver 1991: 140, 307,LIV2.*reh r .



### repens, -ntis
repens, -ntis 'sudden, unexpected' [adj. nt] (Turp.+)

Derivatives: repente 'suddenly, at once' (Pl.+), derepente 'suddenly' (Pl.+), repentTnus 'sudden, without warning' (Pl.+)*

Pit. *rep-nt-.

PIE *htrep-nt- [aor.ptc] 'seizing'. WH, IEW and Leumann assume *wrep- to the root *uerp- 'to twist', positing 'with a sudden move' as the original semantics. This has the disadvantage of schwebe-ablaut in the root, so that an alternative solution would be welcome. EM consider the preverb re- plus a root *pew-/- to φεηάό, but the semantics are unclear, and the root enlargement *-*- is ad hoc. I am more sympathetic towards a connection with rapid, at least as far as the semantics are concerned; for a reconstruction, see s.v. rapid.

Bibl.: WH II: 429, EM 570, IEW 1156, Leumann 1977: 190. → rapid



### repo, -ere
repo, -ere 'to crawl, creep' [v. Ill; pf. repsl> ppp. repturn] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: reptare 'to crawl, move furtively'; adrepere 'to creep up to' (Varro+), conrepere 'to move stealthily' (Pl.+), derepere 'to crawl down' (Varro+), erepere 'to creep out' (Pl.+), obrepere 'to creep up, sneak in' (Pl.+), obreptare 'to creep up' (Pl.+)-

Pit. *rep-e/o-,

PIE *hLrehip- or *hjrep- 'to creep'.

IE cognates: Lith. replioti 'to creep', roploti, Latv. rapaU rdpt 'to creep'; OHG rebo, reba, repa, MHG rebe 'offshoot, bud\ MLG wmrave 'vine'.

### rēs, reī
res, reT 'property, goods, thing, affair' [f. e] (Lex XII+; acc.sg. rem)

Derivatives: recula 'small amount' (Pl.+).

Pit. *re-.

It. cognates: U. ri [dat.sg.], ri [abl.sg.], reper [abl.sg. + -per] 'thing, interest'.

PIE *Hreh₁-i- 'wealth, goods' (nom.sg. *rehri-s, acc.sg. *rehri-m, datsg. *rehi-i-ei) .

IE cognates: Skt. rqyi- [m. (rarely f.)] 'property, goods' (rqyim [acc.sg.], rayah [gen.abl.sgO), Av, raii-i- 'wealth' (OAv. raiid [gen.abl.sg.], YAv. raem [acc.sg.]).

<!-- pdf-page: 535; printed-page: 521; running-header: reus                                       521 -->

The nom.sg. ^eh^s would have become Lat. *reis > *w, the acc.sg. *rehri-m > *rim. The dat.sg. *rehjiei > *rejei > rei is regarded as the regular reflex by Meiser 1998; one might also adduce the gen.sg. *rehri-os (if with full grade root) > *rejos » *rej-l The new stem *rej- would yield an ace. *rejem > *rem, and this in turn may have prompted a new nom.sg. res. The root may be the same as the root *(H)rehr of the Ilr. aor. *ra-s- 'to bestow'.

Bibl.: WH II: 430f, EM 571, IEW 860, Schrijver 1991: 140, 379, Sihler 1995: 341, Meiser 1998: 148, Untermann 2000: 635, LIV l.*reh r . -^ reus



### restis
restis 'rope, cord' [f. ί] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: restio 'dealer in rope' (Pl.+), resticula 'small cord' (Cato+).

Pit. *reskti- 'rope'.

PIE *Hresg-ti- 'rope, cord5.

IE cognates: SkL rajju- [f] 'rope, string5, Sogd. ryzy (/rayzi/) '(woollen?) cloth5 < Ilr. *Hrazgu- / *Hrazju-, Lith. rezgu, rezgiu 'to braid, bind', OLittu rekstis 'basket*.

### rete / retis
rete / retis 'net' |Wf, m. i] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: reticulum * small net' (Varro), reticulatus Covered with a net, like a net' (Varro+). ;;

Pit. *reti-, '· <, * Maybe original m. * retis, pLn. *retia; thus Niedermann apud EM 572. The sg. rete would then be a back-formation. Schrijver argues that the proposed connection with rams must be given up on formal grounds. If BSl. forms such as Lith. retas 'rare, thin, slow', ritis 'sieve', resti, Is. rentu 'to become rare', Latv. r$ns 'standing apart', OCS redbkh 'rare' (< BSl. *reto-* *ret-, *re?d-) are cognate, we would posit a root *ret-, not *reh}t- (Slav, has *-d-). But in view of the isolated position and the exclusively nominal meanings, retef-is might wel be a loanword. Rosen 1995 suggests borrowing from a Semitic source, cf. Biblical Hebrew reset 'net (for fishing and bird-catching)', Ugaritic rot '(probably) id.'.

Bibl.: WH Π: 431, EM 572, IEW 332f, Schrijver 1991: 17f. reus 'party in a lawsuit; defendant' [m. o] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: rea [f ] 'female defendant'·

Pit. *ra-w- Of wealth' » 'of a case'?

PIE *Hreh₁-i-unt- 'possessing wealth'? WH supports the ancient connection with res 'case' and posits *re-i-o- 'connected with a court case', whereas EM term the semantic connection with res weak. I do not share their pessimism. For a suffix *-o- to have this derived meaning, the derivation must be very old, which makes the etymology *re-i-o- somewhat less attractive. But one may also posit a suffix *-wo-, whence *rehji-uo- > *reiwo- > *rewo- > reus, with the same phonotactics as in deus 'god' < *deiwos. Since *-wo- does not appear to function as a productive denominal suffix in Latin, *rehji-uo- might be a remake of *rehji-uent- as attested in Skt rayi-vant- and Av. raeuucmt- 'rich'; its meaning would

<!-- pdf-page: 536; printed-page: 522; running-header: 522                                        rex -->

have been renewed within Latin.

Bibl.: WH II: 432, EM 572, IEW 860, Schrijver 1995:186, LIV l.*reh r . → res • τδχ, regis 'king' [m. g] (VOLat.+: Forum cippus recei 'regi', refxj 'rex')

Derivatives: regulus 'petty king' (Varro+), regalis 'of a king' (Naev.+), regius 'of a king, by a king' (Andr.+), regia 'royal residence, capital' (Acc.+), regma 'queen' (Andr.+), regillus 'of the queen' (PL Epid. 223 acc.sg.f. regillarn); regificus 'royal, sumptuous' (Enn.+); regmun 'kingship, reign, kingdom' (Naev.+), regnare 'to rule as a king' (Pl.+), regnator 'who rules as a king' (Naev.+).

Pit. *reg- 'king'.

It. cognates: maybe FaL rex [nom.sg.] (uncertain reading).

PIE *h₃reg-s, *h₃reg-m [m.] 'ruler, king'.

IE cognates: Olr. ri (gen.sg. rig) [m.] 'king', rige 'kingdom' (~ Skt. rajya- [n.] 'rule, reign'); Skt raj- 'king' (nom.sg. rat\ OAv. razor- / razan- [n.] 'rule, direction' (YAv. only razor-); YAv. barvzi-raz- 'reigning in the height'. Nowicki 2002: 337ff. argues that regma is based on an adi. *reg-mo- 'regal, meant for the king'. The adj. regillus would be a derivative of regina.

Bibl.: WH II: 432, EM 572, IEW 854-857, Schrijver 1991: 121, Meiser 1998: 57, LIV *h₃reg-,

### rideo
rideo 'to laugh' [v. II; pf. risJ, ppp. rlsitm] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: ndibundus 'in a state of laughter' (Pl.+), risio 'laughing' (Pl.), risusy -us 'laughter' (Pl.+), ndiculus 'funny, absurd' (Pl.+), ridicularius 'concerned with jokes' (Pl.+), fisitare 'to laugh repeatedly at' (Laev.); ddridere 'to smile at' (Pl.+), dendere 'to laugh at' (Pl.+), deridiculum 'a ridiculous thing' (Pl.+), irrxdere 'to make fun of (Pl.+), irndiculum 'laughing-stock' (Pl.+), conridere 'to laugh together' (Pl.+), subridere 'to smile' (Varro+). ### rigeo
rigeo 'to be stiff [v. II] (Cic,+)

Derivatives: rigor 'stiffness, cold' (Lucr,+), rigidus 'stiff, inflexible' (Enn.+),

Pit. *rig-e-,

PIE *Hrig-ehr 'to be stretched, be stiff,

IE cognates: Olr, rigid, *r/g* 'to stretch, rule', OBret, diren 'to lead' < *rige/o- 'to stretch', Olr, do rig* 'to lay bare', MW gwarwy 'to play', MBret, aeren 'to tie' < PCI, *rig-e/o- 'to tie', Olr. ringid* 'to torture' < *ringe/o-\ OHG reihhon, reihhen, OE rcec(e)an 'to reach, achieve' < *roig-eie-. IEW derives rigeo from rego 'to stretch, rule', in which case 'stretch' would have been the earlier meaning, and the vowel rig- would have developed in non-initial syllable. This seems unlikely, especially since rigidus is attested earlier than rigeo and rigor. WH derived rigeo from *(s)rig-, the root that also yields frigus 'cold': the original meaning would have been 'stiff from the cold'. But since its root is reconstructed as *sriHg-, this would leave short rig- 'stiff unexplained, LP/ connects two verbs *reig- 'to stretch, bind', which are probably just one PIE verb, as Schumacher argues- If the original meaning was 'to stretch', we can posit a stative verb *rig-e- 'to be stretched, be stiff,

Bibl.: WH II: 434, EM 573, IEW 854-857, Schumacher 2004: 543-548, LIV *reig-, *rei(g)-· η



### rigo
rigo 'to irrigate, make wet' [v, I] (Lucr,+)

Derivatives: irrigare 'to make wet, irrigate' (PK+), jirriguus 'flooded, drenched' (Pl.+), irrigrvus 'well-watered' (Cato), irrigatid 'irrigation' (Varro+),

Pit. *rig-a- 'to lead toward'.

It. cognates: maybe O, regaturei [dat,sg.] m, epithet of Iuppiter, *rega-tor-cl

PIE *Hrig- 'to stretch',

IE cognates: see s,v. rigeo. Quite clearly, the form *in-rigare is older than rigare. Hence, it is conceivable that the verbal root was that of rego 'to lead': *in-rigare 'to lead water onto (the fields)' (thus Panagl 1991), The ^-conjugation can be denominal to a noun *irt-refogo- 4 drainage channel' (vel sim,), but maybe it was built by a productive process to regere like occupare tOv capere. Connecting Alb, rrjedh, one might posit a different root *reg- 'to flow', as LIV does; but this does not change the problem of Latin -/-. Finally, it is conceivable that ir-rigare belongs to the root *rig- 'to stretch' reflected in rigeo. Semantically this is quite satisfactory; it would then be a fairly old (frequentative) derivative */«-ng-a- 'to lead into'. This etymology yields the most straightforward explanation of-/-·

Bibl.: WH II: 435, EM 573f, IEW 857, Sihler 1995: 211, Untermann 2000: 631, LIV ?*reg-, → rego, rigeo



### rlma
rlma 'narrow cleft, crack' [f, a] (Cato+)

Derivatives: rimare/ϊ'Χο examine, scrutinize' (Pac.+). Several etymologies are theoretically possible. To the root *hireik- 'to tear, break' (see s.v. rixa), a preform *h₁reik-sm-hz- 'breach, fissure' would yield rima; but also *reiPma→ *reidma-, and roots in *wr~ would lead to the same Latin form. For

<!-- pdf-page: 538; printed-page: 524; running-header: 524                                     ringor -->


*wreid-, cf. Gm. *writancm 'to write'. Thus, the etymology remains unclear.

Bibl.: WH II: 435, EM 574, IEW 857-859. → rixa

### ringor, -ī
ringor, -Γ 'to show the teeth' [v. Ill; ppp. rictum] (Ter.+)

### ritus, -us
ritus, -us 'religious observances, rites' [m. u] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: rite 'with the proper rites, duly' (Pl.+).

Pit. *reitu-, *reiti- 'rite'.

PIE *h₂r-(e)i- 'to count, observe carefully'.

IE cognates: Olr. renaid*, -ren 'to sell, exchange5 < PCI. *rina- < *h₂ri-n-H-; Olr. rim, W. r/u/'number5 (maybe borrowed from Gm. according to Schrijver 1991); Gr. αριθμός 'number, payment', νήριτος 'countless5; Old Norwegian rim [n.] 'account5, OHG rim [m.] 'row, number'. According to EM, final -e of rite is always short, which would point to a stem *ritl· or *rit→ Since root nouns are rare in Latin (except in compounds), the former seems more likely. It has been suggested that the root is laryngeal-final, but it is impossible to explain the Greek forms in in that case. Germanic could have *h₂rei-m-. A root *h₂ri- is not recognized by LIV, but in view of the meaning, we could analyse it as the root *h₂er- 'to join, arrange' with a suffix *-/→ which came to mean 'to count'. Thus, ritu- may be cognate with artus 'limb, member'.

Bibl.: WH II: 437, EM 574, IEW 60, Schrijver 1991: 22, Rasmussen 1989: 102, Schumacher 2004: 55If., LIV I.*h₂er-. → artus

### robur, -oris
robur, -oris 'oak-tree; strength' [n. r] (Cato+; nom.acc.sg* robus Cato, robor Varro)

Derivatives: rdbustus 'made of oak; strong' (Cato+), roborare 'to give strength to' (Varro+); robosem [acc.sg.] (Paul. exF\

Pit. *roupos [n.] 'red substance'.

PIE *hireudʰ-os, -es- [n.].

IE cognates: Gr. έρευύος 'redness'. The inner part of the oak was apparently referred to as 'red'. The vowel δ must be interpreted as a non-Roman dialectal trait, showing that the word was borrowed from the surrounding dialect area. The form robosem shows an animate s-stem in -ds-; or is it a pseudo-archaism?

### rōdō, -ere

The root etymology is uncertain. Schrijver connects Skt. radati, which may reflect *ra//d- with laryngeal loss in front of *-dC- (but in which form? it is a thematic present), or it can be connected with the Gm. root *wr<5/- 'to dig, rummage' <

### rota
rota 'wheel' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: rotula 'small wheel' (Pl.+), rotundus 'round' (Cato+).

### ruber, -bra, -brum
ruber, -bra, -brum 'red' [adj. o/a] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: rubere 'to be red' (Lucr.+), rubicundus 'ruddy, reddish' (Pl.+), rubidus 'reddish'? (uncertain meaning) (Pl.+), rubor 'redness' (Acc.+), rubrica 'red ochre, red clay' (Pl.+), rubncosus 'rich in red clay' (Cato+); russus 'red, red-haired' (Enn.+), russescere 'to turn red' (Enn.); rutilus 'of a warm or glowing red colour, ruddy' (Pl.+), rutilare 'to glow with a red colour' (Acc.+).

PIE *h,rudh-ro- [adj.] 'red'.

IE cognates: Skt. rudhira- 'red, bloody', Gr. έρυΰρός, RuCS rodn,, ToB ratre 'red'; Olr. rondaid*, roind* 'to paint red' < PCI. *runde/o-. Hill 2003: 224f. explains russus from *rw</-fo~ to rubeo, because thematization of an «s-stem is expected to yield *riisus < *reudʰ-s-o-. The verbal use of the root *π/</- in Italo-Celtic is also apparent from PCI. *ru-n-d-e/o- 'to paint red'. There is no good explanation for rutilus,

### rudis
rudis 'unwrought, not cultivated, crude' [adj. i] (Varro+)

Derivatives: nidus, -eris [n.] 'broken stones, rubble' (LucilH-), rudectus 'full of small stones' (Cato+).

<!-- pdf-page: 542; printed-page: 528; running-header: 528                                       rfldo -->

There are two competing etymologies. The connection with OHG grioz 'sand, graveF and Lith. grudas 'com' would presuppose the disappearance of initial *g*- in Latin, which is questionable. Most handbooks pjefer a derivation from the word for 'red'. This has been argued most extensively by Risch 1979, who suggests that rudis might continue the PIE /-stem adj. *hjrudʰ-i- taken from compounds. Yet there is not a shimmer of a meaning 'red' in rudis or in rudus 'rubble', so that the supposed shift from 'crude (meat)5 > 'crude' rests in the air.

Bibl.: WH II: 447f., EM 579, IEW 868-871, Leumann 1977: 73, Risch 1979: 713, Sihier 1995: 158. → raudus, ruber, rufus

The reconstruction of the (suffix?) *-H- is based on Skt. rodi$L In view of Skt. ruvati 'roar', Gr. ώρύομαι 'to howl, roar', Lat. rumor < "hsru-, final *-rf- may have been a suffix, and the initial laryngeal may be *h₃-.

apart1 (Pl.+), erumpere

### runcō, -āre
runcfl, -are 'to grub up (plants), weed1 [v. I] (Cato+)

Pit. *runk-a-,

PIE *h₃ru-n-k- 'to dig out'.

IE cognates: Skt. luncati 'to pluck' (ep.), Gr. όρύσσω, Att. όρύττω 'to dig, grub'. The verb seems to be denominal, maybe to a noun or adj. *runk-o- which in its turn was derived from a PIE nasal present. Lat. runcina ca carpenter's plane' (Plin.) and runcinare 'to plane (as a carpenter)' (Varro), deruncinare 'to plane off (Pl.) probably rest on the borrowing of Gr. ρυκάνη 'plane' influenced by runcare 'to grub up'»

Bibl.: WH II: 452, EM 582, IEW 868-871, Steinbauer 1989: 254, LIV *h₃reuk-.



### ruo, -ere 14to rush, hurry on; tumble down, collapse'
ruo, -ere 14to rush, hurry on; tumble down, collapse' [v. Ill; pf. ruiy ppp. rutuni] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: nana 'headlong rush, collapse, remains' (Lucr.+), runa 'kind of weapon' (Naev.); corruere 'to collapse' (Andr.+), obruere 'to cover, overwhelm' (Enn.+), proruere 'to hurl forward, overthrow' (Varro+).

Pit. *rowe/o-.

PIE *h₃reu-e/o- 'to run, hurry'.

IE cognates: Olr. ruathar 'assault*; Gr. όρούω 'to huny\ Alb. ra [3s.aor.] 'to fall (down); strike, hit' (< PAlb. *(a)rau-l)\ BeL, Ukr. rax 'movement, bustle', gen.sg. ruxu, Cz. ruch 'movement' < *(H)rous-o-. Meiser 2003 considers the possibility that the root *h₃reu- 'to run, hurry' was ultimately derived from *h₃r- 'to set in motion'.

Bibl.: WH II: 453, EM 582f., Schrijver 1991: 24, Meiser 2003: 236. → -grud



### ruō, -ere 2
rufl, -ere 2 'to churn or plough up, dig out* [v. Ill; ppp. rutum] (Varro+)

Derivatives: rutus, -us 'digging, rushing' (Cato+), mtabulum 'long stick with a flattened end' (Naev,+), rutellwn 'small shovel' (Lucil.+), rutrum 'shovel' (Cato+); arruere cto heap up (earth)' (Varro-l-), diruere 'to pull down' (Ter.+), eruere 'to dig up, tear out' (Cato+); ruta caesa 'minerals and timber already quarried and felled at the time an estate is put up for sale1 (Paul, ex F.9 Cic.+),

Pit. *rowe/o-.

PIE *(H)reuH-e/o-.

IE cognates: Skt. aor. ravisam [ls.sb.act.], ravat [3s.sb.act] 'to wound, hurt1, ppp. ruta-, Lith. rauti 'to tear out, pull', Lith. raviti 'to weed', Ru, ryt \ Is. roju 'to dig', SCr. riti 'dig', OCS rylo 'spade', Ru. rylo 'snout, mug1, SeCS rbvati,

<!-- pdf-page: 545; printed-page: 531; running-header: sabulum                                      531 -->

ORu. rbvati, Ru. rvat7 'to tear\ 01c. ryja 'to tear out wool'; ToB ru- 'to pull out (from under a surface [with violence])', pr. V Iruwa-I < PTo. *rwvra- <

PIE *ru(H)-ehr. It seems that the derivatives of ruo cto rush' and ruo 'to dig' have become mixed up: we find short-vowel forms meaning 'dig\ in spite of the PIE root etymology *(H)ruH-.

Bibl.: WH II: 453f.> EM 582f., IEW 868-871, Schrijver 1991: 234, Meiser 2003: 124, LIV *reuH-.
