### spuma
spuma 'foam' [f. 3] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: spumare 'to foam, froth' (Enn.+),

Pit. *spoima- 'foam'.

PIE *spoHi-m- / *speh₃i-m- 'foam\

IE cognates: Skt. phena- 'foam' [m./n.], OPr. spoayno, Lith. spaine> OCS pena, Ru. pena, SCr. pjena 'foam' < *(s)poHi-nh₂- or *(s)peh₃i-nh₂-; OE Jam [n.], OHG feim 'foam' [m.] < PGm. *faima- <

PIE *poHi-mo-. The different IE reflexes can be interpreted as derivatives in *-m- and *-/i- from a root *spoHi- or *spehsi-\ the *-/- may be a present suffix. Alternatively, with Matasovic 2004: 126, one may posit a PIE neuter mn-stem: nom.acc.sg. *spoHimn9 gen.sg. *spHimnos. The only disadvantage of the latter solution is that no language retains a trace of the suffix *-mn- in tact.

Bibl.: WH II: 580, EM 644, IEW 1001, Schrijver 1991:270. ^pumex



### spuō, -ere
spu65 -ere 'to spit' [v. Ill; pf. spui, ppp> sputum] (Varro+)

Derivatives: sputare 'to spit out, at' (Pl.+)> spUtatpr 'who habitually spits' (Pl.+), sputum 'spittle' (Lucr.-l·), sputatilicius 'detestable' (Sis.+); despuere 'to spit on the ground, spurn' (Naev.+), exspuere 'to emit, spit out' (Pl.+)> Insputare 'to spit upon' (Pl.+X respuere 'to reject, repeF (Lucr.+).

Pit. *spu-je/o→ *spHto~.

PIE *sp(i)uH-ie/o- 'to spit', *sp(i)Huto- 'spat'.

IE cognates: Skt. sthivatU aor. a$thavi$am 'to spit', Gr, πτύω 'to spit', Arm. t'owk''spittle\ t'k'anem 'to spit', Alb. pshtyj, Lith. spictuti, Latv. spfaut, CS plbvati, Is. pljujg, Ru. plevat\ Go. speiwan, OIc. spyja, OE OS OHG splwan 'to spit'. Lat. spud belongs to a PIE onomatopoeic formation meaning 'to spit'. Especially the anlaut of the word has probably been subject to onomatopoeic reformation; but also the stem is not identical in all languages. For Latin, both the pr. and ppp. may be old, but the pr. alone would have sufficed to form the attested paradigm which is that of a regular verb in -were.

Bibl.: WH II: 5801, EM 644f., IEW 999f., Schrijver 1991: 247, Meiser 1998: 194, 2003: 235, LIV *sptieuH-.



### squalus
squalus 'unkempt, dirty' [adj. o/a] (Enn.)

Derivatives: squalere 'to be caked with dirt, covered with a rough layer' (Pl.+), squalor 'filth, roughness of surface' (Pl.+)> squales, -is [f.] 'dirt, filth' (Pac.+), squalitas * dirtiness' (Acc.+), squalitudo * dirtiness' (Acc.+), squalidus 'rough, filthy' (Pl.+); squama 'scale (of a fish or reptile), scale-like object' (Pl.+), squamdsits 'scaly, scurfy' (Pl.+), desquamare 'to remove scales or skin' (Pl.+).

<!-- pdf-page: 598; printed-page: 584; running-header: 584                                       squalus -->

No etymology. In view of the sequence squ-, probably not an inherited word.

Bibl.: WH 11:582, EM 645. t



### squalus
squalus 'unidentified sea-fish' [m. o] (Var., Plin.)

Derivatives: squatus 'a fish' (Plin., Fest).

IE cognates: OPr. kalis 'catfish'; OIc. hvalr [m.], OE hwa>l, OS OHG hwal, OHG hwelira 'whale'; Finn, kala, Northern Lappish guolle 'fish'. Since the word only occurs in prose, the quantity of the first vowel is unknown. Therefore, it might as well be Isqualusl with the stem *squal- 'scale' which is found in the previous etymon. The fish would be denominated after its scales. In that case, the explanation as a loanword from an unknown language, cognate with the Gm. words for 'whale', OPr. 'catfish' and Uralic 'fish' (for which see Schrijver 2001: 423) must be dropped; and squalus is not an example of the sound change *-fcwo- > -qua-.

Bibl.: WH II: 581f., EM 645, IEW 958, Schrijver 1991: 463,2001: 423.



### Ven. sselboisselboi [datsg.]
Ven. sselboisselboi [datsg.] 'for oneself.

Pit. *selfo- 'self.

IE cognates: OPr. sups, subs 'own* < *su-bʰo-; Go. silba7 OIc. sjalfr, OE self, OHG selb < PGm. *selba- 'self < *s(u)elbho~. The pronoun seems to originate from suffixation of *-bʰo- to the pronoun *s(u)e 'self, but the origin of *-/- is unclear. IEW suggests a contamination of *sue with the pronoun *o/(b,)-, but this seems implausible.

Bibl.: IEW 882-884, Lejeune 1974: 338, Beekes 1995: 210.



### (s)ta-
(s)ta- 'to steal' [v. (Duenos inscr. <nemedmalostatod>)

Pit. *(s)ta(jeytod 'must steal'.

PIE *(s)th₂-ie/o- 'to steal'.

IE cognates: Olr. taid 'thief [m.], Hit. taie/a-zi 'to steal' < *teh₂-ie/o-, Skt. stdyat [adv.] 'secretly', stayu [m.] 'thief, pickpocket', stena- [m.] c thief, robber', stiya- [n.] 'theft', OAv. taiia [adv.] 'secretly', YAv. taiia-, taiia- [n.] 'theft'; OCS tajiti, Is. tajQ cto conceal', tatb 'thief. It is disputed whether the final words of the Duenos inscription, which probably mean 'a bad one must not steal me', must be read as Ine med malos tatodl or Me med malo statodL The second option is favoured by Rix 1985b: 200-211, the first one by Eichner 1988-90. Eichner argues that metrical analysis would support his view (which is rather questionable) and that an original root form *sta- meant that the root was homonymous with sta- cto stand', which is indeed a valid argument. Of course, we cannot exclude an analysis as /malos statodL In the end, the analysis remains uncertain. The verb could be a root present or a verb *(s)th₂~ieh- of the type dare, stare: since Ππ, Slavic and Hit. show a suffix *-fe-, we may assume this for Latin, too. In view of the Πη evidence, the PIE root must be reconstructed with s-mobile.

Bibl.: IEW 1010, Meiser 1998: 4, LIV l.*teh₂-.

<!-- pdf-page: 599; printed-page: 585; running-header: stercus                                     585 -->



### stagnura
stagnura 'standing water, pooF [n. o] (Enn.+)

Pit. *stagno-7

PIE *steh₂g-,

IE cognates: OBret. staer, Bret, ster 'river, brook' (< *stagra); Gr. σταγών, -όνος [f.] 'drop7, στάζω cto let drip, shed5. LIV (hesitatingly) posits a PIE root *steh₂g- to account for stagnum, Celtic *stagra- and Gr. στάζω, but we find no matching formations, and the Greek word is semantically somewhat removed (though the connection is possible). If the etymology is correct, Latin would have a full grade and Greek and Celtic a zero grade.

Bibl.: WH II: 584f., EM 645, IEW 1010, LIV ?*steh₂(g)-,



### stella
Stella 'star5 [f. α] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: stellons 'starry' (Lucr.+), stellumicans 'shining with stars' (Varro), stel(l)io 'kind of lizard, gecko5 (Verg.+).

Pit. *sterla-.

PIE *h₂ster-s, *h₂ster- [m.] 'star'.

IE cognates: Olr ser, W. seren (pi. ser) , Co. sterenn (pi. steyr) , Bret, sterenn 'star' < PCI. *ster-; Olr. sell [m.] 'iris' < *$tiIlo-, Mir. sellaid 'to see', MW syllu 'to gaze\ Bret, sellout 'to watch5 < PCI. *stirlo- 'star5 < *h₂ster-lo-; Hit. haster(a)- [c] 'star' (nom.sg. hasterza IHsterff) < *h₂ster-; Skt. tarah [nom.pl.], strbhih [instr.pl.], Av. star- [m.] (YAv. acc.sg. starvm, nom.pl. staro, acc.pl. strsus for *strds, gen.pL strqrri) , KhoL staraa- 'star'; Gr. αστήρ, -έρος, Arm. astl, Go. stairnOy ToB scirye^ 'star'. Schrijver 1995 has established that Olr. sell 'iris' can go back to PCI. *$tIrlo-; together with Lat Stella, this points to an Italo-Celtic formation *ster-lo/a~ 'star'.

Bibl.: WH II: 587f., EM 646, IEW 1027£, Schrijver 1995: 421-423, Meiser 1998: 123.



### stercus, -oris
stercus, -oris 'excrement of animals, dung' [n. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: stercorens 'like dung' (Pl.+), sterco/erdre 'to supply with dung, manure' (Cato+), stercorosus 'rich in manure' (Cato+), sterco/erarius Of dung, manure' (Cato+), sterco/eratio 'manuring' (Varro), sterquiUrmm (or stercuttnum) 'manure-heap, midden' (Pl.+).

Pit. *sterkos- [n.].

PIE *sterk-o- 'befoubnent, exrements'.

IE cognates: Bret, stronk [m.] 'excrements', W. trwnc 'urine' < *(s)tronk-9 W. troeth, Bret, troazh 'urine5 < *trok-ta-; Hit. istar(k)-zi 'to ail, afflict' < *sterk-tiy istornifn)^ c id.\ Lith. tersti cto soil'. According to WH and EM (the latter based on Cato mss. readings), the best reading of 'manure-heap5 is sterculinum, which invalidates the theory that stereos reflects *sterkʷ-. British Celtic contains words of the structure *(s)tro(n)k-, which has the vowel on the wrong side; also, Lith. tersti 'to soil' has been compared, but there is no guarantee that s in this verb reflects

PIE *£ rather than *s. If it does, Lith. has

PIE *terk- without s-mobile; and Celtic may have a new full grade on the basis of the zero grade PCI. *(s)trik-. Kloekhorst 2008: 416-418 proposes to connect the Hit. forms mentioned,

Bibl.: WH II: 589, EM 647, IEW 103If, Leumann 1977: 327, Schrijver 1995: 453f, Stuber 2002: 153, LIV ?*(s)terk-.

<!-- pdf-page: 600; printed-page: 586; running-header: 586                                       sterilis -->



### sterilis
sterilis 'barren, sterile, without offspring' [adj. ί] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: sterilus 'sterile' (Lucr., Gell., Paul, ex F.).

Pit. *ster-eli-l *ster-i-? t

PIE *ster- 'lacking, sterile'.

IE cognates: Skt start- [f ] 'a cow that does not give milk and is not pregnant', MP starwan 'infertile, childless', Gr. στείρα [f] 'infertile (cow, goat, woman)', Arm. sterj 'infertile', Alb. shtjerre 'lamb' < *ster-en-\ Go. stairo 'infertile1, OE stierc 'calf, NHG Sterke 'heifer'. The IE cognates show a semantic development from 'sterile' via 'which has not yeaned yet' to 'young animal, Iamb, calf. In Latin, -///- may be a dim. suffix *-e/i-, but it is also conceivable that original f. *ster-T- was suffixed with *-//-, and analogically shortened the penultimate syllable. According to Eichner 1974, the root may have been

PIE *ster- (Skt. stpjoti 'lays down, destroys'). This would require a semantic shift from 'to lay down' > 'to make stiff > 'make infertile'. Yet the adjectives in *ster- mean 'infertile1, not 'making infertile'. Semantically, the best candidate for a root etymology seems to be Gr. στέρομαι 'to be robbed, lack' < *sterhr, which is said to have no cognates outside Greek.

Bibl.: WH II: 589, EM 647, IEW 1031, Eichner 1974: 37, LIV *ster-, ?*sterhr.



### sterno, -ere
sterno, -ere 'to lay out, spread' [v. Ill; pf. stravi, ppp. stratum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: sirages, -is [f.] 'devastation, destruction' (Lucr.+), stragulwn 'rug, blanket' (Varro+), stramentum 'straw1 (Pl.+), stramentarius 'of straw' (Cato+), stramenticius 'made of straw' (Varro+), stratus, -Us 'spreading' (Varro+); cdnsternere 'to cover, bring down' (Enn.+), Tnstratum 'saddle-cloth' (Cato), substernere 'to underlay, spread under' (Cato+), substramen 'litter' (Varro+); consternare "to confound, compel' (Pac.+).

Pit. *sterna- [pr.], *strato- [ppp.].

PIE *st(e)r-n-h₃- [pr.] 'to spread, strew', *strh₃-to- [ppp.].

IE cognates: Olr. sernaid* 'to spread, build1, MW gwassarnu 'to spread under1 < PCI. pr. *starna-; Skt. strnati 'to spread out, extend, strew', pra-stara- [m.] 'straw, sacrificial straw', YAv. fra-stdrsnaiti [act], fra-stsrvnata [med.] 'to spread out, extend', starvta- *strewn, spread', stairis- [n.] 'straw' < Ilr. *starH-; Gr. στόρνυμι, έστόρεσα [aor.] 'to extend, strew out', στρωτός 'extended1. The pf, was probably formed after the ppp. *strato-. The pr* sternere can be explained from vocalization of interconsonantal laryngeal in e.g. *-n-h₃-ti. The origin of a in con-sterndre is disputed: Schrijver 1991 posits a thematic verb in *-n-h₃-ie/o-, whereas Meiser 1998 derives -na- from the foil grade of the suffix *-nhr (with a replacement of *-no- by *-na- in con-sternare), Meiser's solution does not explain why -nare is restricted to compounds. The stem strag- seems recent, since strag-es and strag-ulus are not attested before Varro. They probably have -g- in analogy to other nouns such as corn-pages and amb-ages.

Bibl.: WH II: 590f., 600, EM 647, IEW 1029-1031, Schrijver 1991: 183, 376, 407-410, Meiser 1998: 187,2003: 228, Schumacher 2004: 601f., LIV *sterh₃--

<!-- pdf-page: 601; printed-page: 587; running-header: stilus                                       587 -->



### sternuō, -ere
sternuC, -ere 'to sneeze' [v. Ill; pf. stermti, ppp. sternutum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: stertere (pf stertui) 'to snore' (Pl.+).

Pit. *(p)ster-nu-.

PIE *(p)st~(e)r-nu- *to sneeze'.

IE cognates: Mir. sreod 'sneezing', W. trew, ystrew 'sneezing', LCo, striwhi, Bret strevian 'to sneeze' < *stru-7, Gr. πταρμός [m,] 'the sneezing', aor. πταρεΐν, ρι\ πτάρνυμαι 'to sneeze', Asm.p,fngam,p,fnem 'to sneeze'. Latin and Greek (and maybe Armenian) both show a nw-present, but in Greek, it is secondary to the thematic aorist The initial clusters might go back to

PIE *pst-, but this is also uncertain. Onomatopoeic deformation may have affected all cognates in the IE languages. However, it seems unlikely that all languages would have invented a form with internal *-r- to render the sound of sneezing, so Latin may well have inherited a PIE word of the form *(p)st-r-.

Bibl.: WH II: 591, EM 648, IEW 846f, Schrijver 1995:454, Meiser 1998: 113, LIV *pster-,



### stēla
stSla 'drop of liquid' [f. a] (Cic.+)

Derivatives: sfillicidium 'the falling of drops, trickle, drip1 (Varro+), stillare 'to fall in drops, drip' (Varro+), sfillatim 'drop by drop' (Varro); desfillare 'to drip down, off (Varro+), exsfillare 'to drip away, water1 (Pl.+), mstillare 'to pour in drop by drop' (Cato+); sfiria 'icicle' (Verg.+). ? :

Pit. *sti/eir-(la-). >

IE cognates: Gr. στίλη 'drop1; Lith. styrti 'to become stiff, Norw. sfiray Olc. stira 'to be(come) rigid, stiff. Stllla can be regarded as a diminutive *sfir-la- to sfiriay even though the latter is only attested in Imperial Latin. WH and IEW mainly compare Gm. and Baltic words in *sfir- meaning 'stiff, but it is uncertain whether 'stiff was the original meaning on which sfiria was based. In view oisfilla 'drop', sfiria may have referred to the form of drops and icicles, which would leave the connection with 'stiff unfounded. Gr. στίλη 'drop' cannot be directly compared with sfilla; at most, there would be a (IE or European?) base *sti-,

Bibl.: WH 11:^.595, EM 648, IEW 101 Of.



### stilus
stilus 'pointed piece of metal, stem, stylus1 [m. o\ (Naev.+)

Derivatives: stimulus 'goad, spike' (Pl.+), stimuleus 'inflicted with the goad' (Pl.), stimulare 'to urge forward, incite' (Pl.+), stimulatrlx [f.J 'who incites' (Pl.); sfiva 'shaft of a plough-handle' (Cato+). It is uncertain whether Latin sfilusy stimulus and sfiva all belong together, but one might see a root sti- 'sharp object' in them. It has been argued that they contain the core of the root *stig- 'to sting' (see s.v. stinguo), the -g- of which would be a root enlargement; although this is not completely impossible, there are no positive indications in this direction. Another possible cognate might be Av. staera-y taera- [m.] 'mountain-top' < *(s)te/oir/lo-y if to stilus. But since the root cannot be determined, this etymology remains gratuitous.

Bibl.: WH II: 592, 595, EM 648f, 651, IEW 1015.

<!-- pdf-page: 602; printed-page: 588; running-header: 588                                       stinguo -->



### stinguu, -ere
stinguu, -ere 'to extinguish' [v. Ill; -stinxi, stinctum] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: distinguere 'to keep separate, mark off, distinguish' (Pac.+), distinctus 'different' (Acc.+), exstinguere 'to extinguish, kill, wipe out' (Pl.+), interstinguere 'to extinguish temporarily; mark off (Lucr.+), restinguere 'to extinguish, suppress' (Pl.+).

Pit. *ste-n-g"-e/o~9 *in-stl/eigr.

It. cognates: U. anstintu, astintu [3s.ipv.Il] 'to connect, attach' < *an + stinge-tod>

PIE *stengʷ-e/o- 'to push, thrust'.

IE cognates: Go. stigqan 'to thrust', caus. ga-stagqjan. There is no reason to posit a separate verb *stingere, as EM do. The attestations of *stingere will have originated e.g. in the 3p. stinguunt > stingunt. The meanings of ex- and restinguere 'to extinguish' and distinguere seem quite distinct, but can be understood if the root meant 'to press' or 'push': exstinguere 'to put a fire out', restinguere 'to push back, suppress', and dis-stinguere 'to push apart > distinguish, mark off; cf Klingenschmitt 1989. Umbrian * ana-sting- 'to connect' is also unproblematic from "to push'. The verbal stem in *-uje/o- cannot be explained as denominal, which is why the etymological connection with Go. stinqan is attractive: root final *-gʷ- could have brought about the w-stem conjugation. It does not appear possible to link in-stlgare with -stinguere, since it presupposes root-internal *-/-. U. anstintu may belong here (< *-stimbtu < *stimbeto\ or to the root *stig- 'sharp' of m-stigare.

Bibl.: WH I: 706f, 11: 592, EM 649, IEW 1016f, Klingenschmitt 1989: 99f, Untermann 2000: 106, LIV *stengy-. → instigare



### stlpo, -are
stlpo, -are 'to compress, surround' [v. 1] (Varro+)

Derivatives: sttpator 'bodyguard, attendant' (Varro+); obstTpus 'slanted, inclined' (Enn.+), Tnstipare 'to pack closely' (Cato); stipes, -itis [m.] 'tree trunk, stick' (Cato+).

Pit. *stei/ipo-,

PIE *steip-o- 'stiff, erect'.

IE cognates: Lith. stipti, Is. stimpu 'to stiffen, grow rigid', stiprus 'strong', stiepti 'to stretch"; OE stif, MHG stif 'stiff, erect' < *steifa-, MLG stivele 'support', OIc. stifla 'to dam'. Seems to be based on an adj. *stipo- 'erect, rigid' < *stiipo~, on the evidence of the Gm. accent. The noun sfipet- can be interpreted as a deadjectival /-stem *steip-e/ot- 4 the stiff one'. There are possible cognate forms in Baltic and Germanic from a root *stip-.

Bibl.: WH II: 593, EM 650, IEW 1015f, LIV ?*steip-. → stips



### stips, -is
stips, -is 'small offering of money, alms' [f p\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: stipula 'stalk, straw, reed' (Ter.+), stipulan 'to exact a solemn promise' (Pl.H-), stipulatio 'demanding a guarantee' (Varro+), mstipulari 'to stipulate for' (Pl.+); stipendium 'regular cash payment, campaign' (Pl.+; sttpendium metri causa in Enn.).

Pit. *stip- 'stalk' > 'payment', *stip-elo- 'straw'.

It. cognates: U. steplatu, stiplatu, anstiplatu [3s.ipv.H], stiplo [2s.ipv.] 'to agree legally'.

PIE *stip- 'stalk'?

IE cognates: see s.v. siipo.

<!-- pdf-page: 603; printed-page: 589; running-header: sto                                        589 -->

The noun stipendium derives by haplology from *stipijpendium. Stipulate can be derived from the noun stipula, the original meaning of the verb was 'to draw/cut straws'. In view of U. ste/ipla-, this must have been an early development The noun stip- must have developed from a concrete object that was used for payments, but the nature of the object is unknown: a certain stalk of a plant? a measure of com? Since the root meant 'to be stiff, erect', the meaning 'stalk' is attractive.

Bibl.: WH Π: 594, EM 650, IEW 1015f, Untermann 2000: 703f, LIV ?*steip-, ^ stipo



### stirps, -pis
stirps, -pis 'stem, stump, stock, ancestral race' [f. (m.) ρ] (Pl.+; m. archaic) Has been compared with Lith. stirpti 'to grow up, grow high', sterptis 'to stiffen', which is a possible but not compelling semantic connection. This etymology would require that Lat stirps reflect *sterp-, which is possible, but less likely than *stirp~.

Bibl.: WH II: 595, EM 651, IEW 1022-1027.



### sto, stare
sto, stare 'to stand; to halt1 [v. I; pf. steti, ppp. statum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: (1) statim [adv.] 'unyieldingly, immediately' (Pl.+), statio 'halting-place, standing, guard' (Lucr.+), statarius 'quiet, stationary' (Ter.+), staticulus 'a pose' (Pl.+), statlvus 'standing still, permanent' (Varro+), stator 'who establishes1 (Varro+); abstare 'to keep at a distance' (Pl.+), a(d)stare 'to stand by, stand waiting' (Naev.+), antesfare 'to surpass' (Enn.+), cdnstare 'to stand together, remain; cost' (Pl.+), distare 'to be distant, different' (Lucr.+), exstare 'to exist, protrude' (Pl.+), instare 'to set foot on, press, be urgent' (Pl.+), obstare 'to meet, stand in the way, obstruct' (Pl.+), obstetnx 'midwife' (Pl.+), praestare 'to be superior, furnish' (Andr.+), praestabilis 'outstanding1 (Ter.+), praesto [adv.] 'ready' (Pl.+), prostare 'to offer for sale' (Pl.+), restore 'to remain, stand firm' (Pl.+), restitare 'to lag behind, keep offering resistance' (Pl.+), substare 'to hold one's ground, remain' (Ter.+), superstitare 'to cause to remain in existence' (Pl.+); (2) status, -us 'standing, position1 (Pl.+), statuere (statui, statutumy\o set, erect, establish' (Pl.+), statura 'body height, stature' (Pl.+), statua 'statue' (Pl.+); a(d)stituere *to place near' (Pl.+), cdnstituere 'to place, arrange' (Pl.+), destituere 'to set up, abandon' (Naev.+), Jnstituere 'to organize, establish' (Pl.+), praestituere 'to fix' (Pl.+), prostituere 'to expose, prostitute' (Pl.+), restituere 'to set up again, restore' (Pl.+); (3) superstes, -stitis 'standing by, surviving' (Pl.+), superstitidsus 'ecstatic, full of credulity1 (Pl.+), solstitium 'solstice' (Cato+), solstitialis 'of the summer solstice' (Pl.+); (4) stabulum 'stable, shed' (Pl.+), stabulare 'to house (animals)' (Varro+); stabilis 'steady, firm' (Pl.+), mstabilis 'unsteady, inconstant1 (Pac.+), stabilire 'to fix, make firm' (Pl.+)> stabiUmen 'support' (Acc.+), stabitimentum 'which gives stability' (Pl.+); constabilTre 'to establish, strengthen' (Pl.+), constibilis 'strong' (Cato), prdstibilis 'available as a prostitute' (Pl.+), prostibulum 'prostitute' (Pl.+), restibilis 'sown or planted every year1 (Cato+), restibitire 'to re-establish' (Pac); (5) destinare 'to arrange the purchase of (Pi.), determine, settle (Cic.+)\ obstinare 'to set one's mind on' (Naev.+), praestinare 'to bargain for, buy' (Pl.+); (6) stamen, -inis [n.] 'thread, loom, warp' (Varro+);.

<!-- pdf-page: 604; printed-page: 590; running-header: 590                                       stolidus -->

Pit. *$ta-e-je/o- 'to stand, be standing', *sta-n-e/o- 'to place*, *stato- 'placed1, * statu- 'setting up, erecting', *staplo- [n.] 'place, stable'.

It. cognates: Ven. atisteit [3s.pr.] < *ati-staiet, stati [ins.sg.] ,'weight'; FaL sta [3s.pr.] 'stands 5 ; Fal statuo Ί erect'; U. stahu [ls.pr.], stahitu [3s.ipv.II], stahituto [3p.ipv.II], staheren [3p.fut.], O. staft [3s.pr.], stahint, stahint, staiet [3p.pr.], staieffud [3s.pf.?] 'to stand', SPic. adstaeoms [Ip.pr.], adstaiuh [3p.p£?] 'to erect' or 'to place beside', O. eestint [3p.pf] 'to exist', SPic. praistait [3s.pr.]a praistafnt [3p.pr.], prajistaiuh [3p.pf.?] 'to stand up' < *sta-ie-\ U. stakaz est [3s.pf.ps.] 'to establish', denom. to a noun *sta-k-\ Mars, state [datsg.] name of a deity; O. statif ' ? ' [nom.sg.?]; U. statita [ppp acc.pl.n.] 'erected' < *statuto-y denom. to *statu- 'act of erecting'; U. statitatu [3s.ipv.II] 'must take position', denom. from *statuto-; U. stahmei [loc. or datsg.] 'agreement' vel sim. < *sta-mo-; U. stahmito [ppp nom.sg.n.], stahmitei, stahmeitei [loc. or dat.sg.m.] 'agreed/determined', denom. to stahmo-; O. staflatasset [3p.pf.ps.], σταβαλανο [gdve. nom.pl.n.] 'to erect', denom. v. *stafla-o- to *staflo- 'place' < *sthrcflo-\ U. staflarem [acc.sg.f-], stajlare [acc.sg.n.] ' ? ' < *stafl-ari-; U. staili'?'.

PIE *sth 2 -eh r ie/o- 'to stand', *st-n(e)-h₂- 'to place', *sth₂-to- 'placed', *sth₂tu- *placement\ *sth₂-dʰlo- 'a place',

IE cognates: Olr. ad-ta 'to be (situated)*, OW -tau 'is', MCo. otte, atta 'there is', OBret to 'it is' < PCI. *ta-ie/o-\ Arm, stanam [med.] 'to acquire', Alb. shton 'to add', OPr. postat 'to become', Lith. stoti, Latv. stat 'to stand', OCS stati9 Is. stanQ 'to stand, become', stojatU Is. stojg, OHG sten/stan^ OFr. stan. Go. standan, OIc. standa, OS OE standan 'to stand5. The inherited formations are: (1) a stative present *sta- < *stqje- < *sta-eie-, which is also found in Sabellic (U. stahi/e-^ O. sta(h)i-\ a to-ptc. *stato- 'placed1, (2) a fa-stem noun *statu- 'the erecting' (also in Sab.), (3) maybe a astern noun *stat- 'who stands' in compounds, (4) a Pit. instr. noun in *-plo- (also in Sab.), and (5) a nasal present *stane/o- 'to place'. Of the latter, only the derived 5-stem verb in prepositional compounds has been preserved. Lat. prdstibilis and prostitution are derived from the verbal base *pro-sta- rather than from stabulum (Serbat 1975: 65).

Bibl.: WH II: 584, 587, 596-599, 632, EM 651-655, IEW 1004-1010, Giacomelli 1963: 256f, Cowgill 1973, Klingenschmitt 1982: 112, Schxijver 1991: 98, 404f, Meiser 1998: 187, 2003: 71, 189Γ, Untermann 2000: 693-703, Schumacher 2004: 623ff., LIV *steh₂-. → instauro, sisto



### stolidus
stolidus 'insensible, dull, stupid' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: stultus 'stupid' (Naev.-l·), stultitia 'stupidity' (Pl.+). Whereas stolidus might belong to a verb *stolere 'to be insensible', stultus < *stolto- might reflect *stl-to-. The further etymology is uncertain. IEW derives them from the root *$tel- 'to place', so that stolidus and stultus could be interpreted as originally meaning 'inert' (< 'standing').

Bibl.: WH II: 599, EM 655, 658, IEW 1019f., Leumann 1977: 329.



### stolo
stolo 'shoot, sucker (in plants)' [m. n] (Varro+)

Pit. *steI-on~ or *stol-dn-.

PIE *stel-n- 'shoot, stem'.

IE cognates: Gr. στόλος 'equipment, armament; ship's

<!-- pdf-page: 605; printed-page: 591; running-header: stringo                                     591 -->


prow' [m.], στελεά [f.]
prow' [m.], στελεά [f.] 'shaft of an axe, hammer', στέλεχος [η. (m.)] 'the end of the stem at the root of a tree, stump, log', Arm. stein 'trunk, branch₁; OE stela [m.] 'stalk of a plant', Norw, stjel 'stalk'. We find a matching noun in Armenian, and Greek has a few nouns with similar form and semantics. The meaning of Lat. stoloy Gr. στέλεχος and OE stela can be well understood on the basis of the PIE root *stel- 'to place'.

Bibl.: WH II: 599fM EM 655, IEW 1019£, LIV *stel-.



### strēna
strSna 'auspicious sign' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: strenuus 'active, vigorous' (Naev.+), strenuitas 'activity' (Varro+), strenuosus 'active' (Pl.), instrenuus 'inactive' (Pl.+), Strenia 'goddess of health and luck' (Varro+).

Pit. *stregsno-l

PIE *streg-sno-? Often compared with Gr. στρηνής 'rough, harsh', στρήνος [n.] 'passion, pride', but the semantics are unconvincing. If the original adj. was *strenos (as one Pl. attestation seems to suggest), strenuus is due to the influence of the adj. in -uus, in particular *endostruos > industrius (see s.v. strud). The Romans regarded strena as a Sabine word. Szemerenyi 1989: 23f. connects Olr. tren 'strong' < *treg-sno-, and (if cognate) OIc. prek(r), OE pracu 'force' < *treg-, OIc. sterkr < *sterg-. There are no good verbal cognates for these nominal forms *(s)terg-, hence the PIE character remains uncertain. ,

Bibl.: WH II: 601, EM 655, IEW 1022-1027, Schrijver 1991: 140.



### strepo, -ere
strepo, -ere 'to make a loud noise' [v. Ill; pf. strepuT, ppp. strepitum] (Sall.+)

Derivatives: strepitus, -us 'noise' (Pl.+); perstrepere 'to make a loud noise' (Ter.+).

Pit. *strepe/o-.

PIE *strep-e/o-.

IE cognates: OIc. prefa 'to quarrel', OIc. prapt [n.] 'gossip', OE prceft, MLQ drevelinge 'quarrel9. It is uncertain that *strep- goes back to PIE, since it is only found in Latin and Geimanic. {

Bibl.: WH II: 602, EM 656, IEW 1037, LIV *(s)trep-.



### strido, -ere
strido, -ere 'to make a shrill sound' [v. Ill; pf. strTdi] (Enn.+; stfidere Luc.+)

Derivatives: stridor 'squeak, shriek' (Pac.+).

IE cognates: Gr. τρίζω 'to squeak*, pf. τέτρϊγα, ΤοΑ trisk- 'to resound'. Onomatopoeic formation of str- and -/- rendering a high, thrilling tone. In Gr. and Toch., we find initial tr-.

Bibl.: WH II: 603, EM 656, IEW 1036.



### strings, -ere
strings, -ere 'to bind fast, tighten; to strip off [v. Ill; pf. stnnxi, ppp. strictum] (Cato+)

Derivatives: (1) strictiira 'hardened mass of iron' (Lucil.+), strictim 'closely,

<!-- pdf-page: 606; printed-page: 592; running-header: 592                                       struma -->

slightly' (Pl.+), stringor 'contracting quality' (Lucn); adstringere 'to tie up tightly, commit' (Pl.+), distringere 'to stretch apart' (Pl.+), interstringere 'to throttle' (Pl.), obstringere 'to tie around, confine' (Pl.+), praestringere 'to blunt, tie up at the ends' (Pl.+), restringere 'to draw back, fasten* (Pl.+); strigor 'brute, man of solid strength' (Pl.+), obstrigillare 'to cause obstruction' (Enn.+), obstrigillator 'who hampers' (Varro+); (2) stridor 'a picker' (Cato), strictivus 'picked (of fruit)' (Cato), strigilis [f] 'oil-scraper' (Pl.+), praest(r)Tgiae [f-pl.] 'trick, deceit' (Pl.+), praestigiator 'trickster, juggler' (Pl.-*-),praestlgiatrJx 'female trickster' (Pl.); stria 'channel, furrow' (Varro+), striatus 'furrowed, grooved' (Pl.+); striga 'strip, row' (PauL ex F. et ah), strigare 'to stop, halt' (Verg.+). Pit- *string- 'to strip off, *strengefo- 'to tie' [prj, *strig-to- [ppp.], *strig-a- [f.] 'strip, row', *-streig-io-.

PIE *stri-n-g- 'to brush, strip', *strengʰ-e/o- 'to draw, tie\

IE cognates: OCS stristi, Is. strig$ 'to cut, shave', OHG strihhan 'to brush', OE strlcan 'to brush (past), rub, wander' < *strig-\ Mir. srengim 'to draw, drag', Khot strts- (strTs-? str(r)T-) 'to become stiff ^pastramj- 'to repress' < Ph\ *stranj\ Gr. στραγγός 'twisted, crooked', Olc. strengja 'to bind together', OE strer.gan 'to attach' < *strengʰ~. The simplex stringd has two meanings: (1) 'to tie, tighten', as in all the compounds of -stringd; the same meaning may account for strigor ('who holds'), obstrigillare and praest(r)igiae (with dissimilation of the second r), (2) 'to strip off, bare; to scratch, shave, etc.', which is difficult to derive from 'to tie' or 'stretch'. To this second meaning belong the instrument strigilis and possibly also stria 'furrow' (< *strig-ia; but it might also below to 'stretch') and striga 'strip, row'. It appears that a merger occurred of two different PIE verbs, *strig- 'to brush, strip' and *strengʰ- 'to tie' (> Lat. string- by phonetic development). The verb strigare 'to stop' is best interpreted as a denominative to stria and striga: 'to draw furrows' > 'stop and turn at the end of the field' > 'to halt'.

Bibl.: WH 11: 356, 602-605, EM 656f, IEW 1028f., Meiser 2003: 113, LIV 1 .*streig-, *strengʰ-.



### struma
struma 'swelling of the lymphatic glands' [f. a\ (Cic.+) In theory, a connection with Gm. words in

PIE *strudr (Olc. strutr 'top', OE strutiar\ "to stand still', NHG strotz 'stiff, MUG struz 'resistance') is possible, but the semantic link is not compelling, and the late attestation in Latin makes an IE etymology less certain.

Bibl.: WH II: 606f., EM 657, IEW 1022-1027.



### struō, -ere
struO, -ere 'to arrange, construct, compose, build' [v. Ill; pf. stribcT, ppp. structuni] (Lex XI1+)

Derivatives: strues^ -is [f.] 'heap, pile; row of sacrificial cakes' (Cato+), struix, -Tcis [f.] 'heap, pile' (Andr.+); construere 'to pile up, arrange' (Sis.+), exstruere 'to heap up, construct' (Pl.+)> instruere 'to build, arrange, equip' (Pl.+), instnimentum 'equipment' (Cato+), industria 'diligent activity' (Pl.+), industrius 'active' (Pl.+), industriose 'diligently' (Cato+), indostruum 'industrium' (PauL exF.).

<!-- pdf-page: 607; printed-page: 593; running-header: stumus                                       593 -->

Pit. *strow-e/o-.

It. cognates: U. struh^la, struma, struslay strusla [acc.sg.], struh(Ias [gen.sg.] £ 'kind of sacrificial cake' < *struwi-k-ela?

PIE *streu-e/o- 'to strew\

IE cognates: Olr. sru'id* 3s. 'to throw', as-sroither 'is dispersed5, OBret. strouis 'strew out5 <

PIE *strou-eie-, OCS o-strujg, -strujiti 'to destroy', Go. straujan 'to strew5 < *strou-eie-, Lat. struo can reflect a PIE thematic pr. *streu-e/o-. The velar in the pf. and ppp. must be analogical tofluo, where -glir- disappeared regularly between vowels. Mindustrius goes back to *endo-struwo-y this was probably directly built to the verb.

Bibl.: WH I: 696, II: 607, EM 316, 658, IEW 1029-1031, Leumann 1977: 591, Meiser 1998: 208, Untermann 2000: 704£, Schumacher 2004: 602, LIV *streu-.



### studeo
studeo 'to devote onself, strive after' [v. II; pf. studui\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: studium 'zeal, enthusiasm, study' (Pl.+), studiosus 'eager, zealous5 (Pl.+).

Pit. *stud-e-.

PIE *stud-ehr 'to thrust, hit'.

IE cognates: see s.v. tundd. Lat. studere can be understood as a stative to *(s)fud- 'to thrust', with the meaning 'to be thrusting' > 'strive after'.

Bibl.: WH II: 608, EM 658, IEW 1032-1034, LIV L*(s)teud-. → tundd

stupeo 'to be(come) numb, amazed' [v. II] (Ter.+) \

Derivatives: stupidus 'dazed, stunned, foolish' (Pl.+), stupiditas 'stupidity' (Acc.+); obstu/ipescere 'to be stunned' (Pl.+), obstupidus 'astounded' (PK+), obstupefacere 'to strike dumb, stun' (Ter.+); stuprum 'dishonour, illicit sexual intercourse' (Naev.+), stuprare 'to have illicit sexual intercourse with5 (Pl.+).

Pit. *stup-e- 'to be stunned5, *stup-ro- 'struck, hit\

PIE *stup-ehr 'to hit, push', *stup-ro-,

IE cognates: CLuw. dup(a)i→ Lye. tub(e)i 'to strike', Gr. τύπτω 'to hit, strike, sting', aor. έτύπη 'was hit', τύπος 'stroke, impression'; Alb.pertyp 'to chew', shtyp 'to crush'; Gr. στύπος [n.] 'stick, shaft'. The PIE verbal meaning 'to hit, push' explains the Latin stative 'to be stunned5 as well as the nominal form *stup-ro- 'struck, stunned5 > 'dishonoured'.

Bibl.: WH II: 6Q9£f EM 658f, IEW 1032-1034, LIV *(s)teup-.



### sturnus
sturnus 'starling' [m. o] (PIin.+)

Pit. *storno- 'starling'?

PIE *(h₂)st(o)r-no-. 'starling'?

IE cognates: Gr. άστραλός 'starling5 (Hsch.), OE steam, MoDu stem 'tern\ OPr. stamite 'seagull' (ms. stamite) ; OHG stasia) , NHG Star, MDu, sterre, OIc. stari, OE steer, dim. sterling * starling', Sturnus can be connected with Gm. words in * stern- and *stor-n- meaning 'tern5 and 'starling', and maybe with OPr. stamite. The Greek gloss word άστραλός would prove *h₂str-, but it stands isolated.

Bibl.: WH II: 610, EM 659, IEW 1036, Schrijver 1991: 23, Meiser 1998: 64. → turdus

<!-- pdf-page: 608; printed-page: 594; running-header: 594                                         suasum -->



### suasum
suasum 'dirty grey colour' [n. o] (Pl., Fest.)

Pit. *swarsso-rt

PIE *surd-to-?

IE cognates: see s.v. hordes. The meaning has suggested a connection to sordes < *suord-o/i-, but the exact form is unclear. A preform *suord-to- would probably yield *sorso-9 whereas *suerdto- would yield *suerso- > *sorso- too. Only if *su?drto- regularly developed into *suarsso- could suasum result; this would match the rule *#DT> *RaDT posited by Schrijver 1991: 485. Without external support for this morphological analysis, this etymology remains uncompelling.

Bibl.: WH II: 611, EM 659, IEW 1052, Leumann 1977: 211, Schrijver 1991: 463, 494. → sordes



### suavis
suavis 'sweet, pleasant5 [adj. /] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: suavitudo 'sweetness' (Pl.+), suavitas 'pleasantness, charm' (Pl.+), suavisaviatio 'amorous kissing* (Pl.), suavisonus 'sweet-sounding' (Naev.+); savium 'kiss5 (Pl>), savillum 'kind of cheesecake5 (Cato); suadere [suasi, suasutri] 'to recommend, advise' (Pl.+), consuadere 'to recommend strongly' (Pl.+), dissuadere 'to advise against' (Pl.+), persuadere cto induce, convince5 (Pl.+), persuastrix 'persuasive woman' (Pl.), suadela 'persuasion' (Pl.+), suosor 'who advises, advocates' (Pl>), suasus, -us 'advising' (Ter.+), suada 'persuasiveness' (Enn.+).

Pit. *swadu- 'sweet', *swadeje- 'to render agreeable5.

PIE *sueh₂d-u- 'sweet', *su(o)h₂d-eie- 'to render tasty'.

IE cognates: Skt. svadu- [m.], svadvi- [f.] 'sweet', pr. svadanti [3p.act.], caus. svadaya- 'to make savoury5, svatta- 'made savoury', OAv. hudama- 'sweetness', YAv. xvasta- 'cooked, made done', Bal. wad 'salt5 (< PIr. *huada-)\ Gr. ηδύς 'sweet, tasting good, pleasant', OE swdt 'sweet', OHG suozi, OE swete; To A swar 'sweet' < *suad-ro-. The PIE w-stem adj. yielded

Pit. *suadu~9 which was then remade into an /-stem as all other i/-adj. were: *suadwi-. Subsequently, -d- was lost in front of *-w~. The verb suadere shows by its meaning that it is a causative, but ^suoh^dreie- would have yielded *sdde-. Hence, the root form of the adj. was probably introduced into the verb (Schrijver 1991: 148). The semantics 'to recommend' can be understood from earlier 'to make sweet, render agreeable'. In savium and savillum < *swawio-, the first *-w- was dissimulated.

Bibl.: WH II: 483, 61 If., EM 597, 659, IEW 1039f, Schrijver 1991: 145, 148, Meiser 1998: 120, LIV *sueh₂d-.



### sub, su(b)s
sub, su(b)s 'under, below; reaching from below, up to' [prep., pref. + ace. (movement), + abl. (position)] (Elog,Scip., Naev.-l·); distribution: sus- < *subs- in front of p,t,c,qu, su- in front of sp-; sub- in front of vowels, h-9 /-, w-, i>-, dry /-, n-y r-, s~, t-; sue- in front of c-, suf- in front of/-, sug- in front of g-, sup- in front of p-, often also sum- in front of m- and sur- in front of r-. The meaning 'movement upwards5 can be seen e.g, in suspicion sublevd, surgo, sublatus.

Derivatives: subter 'underneath' [adv.]; 'towards/in a position at the base of, under' [prep,] (Varro+), subtus 'underneath, below' (Cato+); supinus 'lying face upwards'

<!-- pdf-page: 609; printed-page: 595; running-header: U. sukatu                                    595 -->

(Pl.+), resupfnare 'to tilt back, lay face upwards' (Ter.+), resupmus 'lying face upwards, sloping upwards' (Acc+).

Pit. *supV (probably *supo) 'down, below; upwards', *supmo- 'upwards'.

It. cognates: Ο, συπ (+ loc), U. su (+ abL) 'under*, as a preverb U. sub- (before vowel), su- (before vowel or consonant: sumtu < *sup-emtu, sutentu < *sup-tentu\ Probably also U. supu (+ loc.) 'at the foot of; maybe U. subahtu, subotu [3s.ipv.II], subator sent [3p.pf.ps,] 'to lay aside'.

PIE *(hi)upo 'up, from below'.

IE cognates: Gaul, vo-, Olr./o 'under', OW guo-, gu-, gua-, W. go-, gwa- < PCI. *wpo-\ Skt. upa 'towards, together with, under, near to, onto, on', OAv, upa, YAv. upa, OP upa 'towards, with, on, in'; Gr. ΰπο, ύπό 'under, below'; Go. w/'on, under', OHG oba, MHG obe, ob 'over', OIc. of 'over, on, in', OE ufe-, OHG uf(ufan) 'on*. Final -b may have arisen in front of voiced stops. Lat subtus < *sub-tos (cf intus). The theory that suplmis contains the root *e/- 'to go' (Leumann 1977: 328) is unattractive: it will be a simple adjectival derivative in -Tnus, which shows the old meaning 'upwards' of *sup(o). Initial *s- in Italic (in sub and super) and in Greek cannot be explained by comparing sine with *en- as EM do, since sine is now etymologized differently. Whatever the origin of the s-, the Celtic forms show that it was a specific Italic development.

Bibl.: WH 612-617, 633, EM 659-661, 668, IEW 1106f, Leumann 1977: 157, Beekes 1995: 222, Schrijver 1005: 116-130, Untennann 2000: 705f, 718f., 723. -* summits, super, suppus t



### suber, -ris
suber, -ris 'cork-oak' [n. r] (Verg.+)

Derivatives: subernus l of the cork-tree' (Varro), suberies 'cork-oak' (Lucil.+). No etymology. A borrowing from Gr. ουφαρ [n.j 'wrinkled skin', as proposed by WH, is doubtful on account of the deviant meaning. Latin and Greek might have borrowed a word in *subʰ- with a common semantic core from a third language.

Bibl.: WH Il:617,EM661.



### U. sukatu [3s.ipv.II], prusikurent [3p.futpf]
U. sukatu [3s.ipv.II], prusikurent [3p.futpf] 'to declare', SPic. persukant [3p.pr.] 'id/.

Pit. *soiko- 'swearing', *(se-)sik- 'to swear',.

PIE *se-s(o)ik- [pf] 'to reach', *soik-o- [m./n,].

IE cognates: Gr. ΐκω, ικάνω, ίκνέομαι [pn], ίκέσύαι [aor.] 'to come, reach', ένέϊκαι 'to carry off < *en~seik-; Lith. siektiy 3s. siefda 'to try to reach, strive for', saikas, siekas, Latv. sieks 'unit of dry measure'; ToB siknam, sb. saikam 'to set a foot'. The older etymology with *sekʷ- 'to say' was dismissed by Meiser, who instead derives pf. *pr6-sik-us- from

PIE *seik- 'to reach'. Meiser posits a semantic change from 'to reach' > 'to reach after, seek' > 'swear'. He explains the stem suka- in U. and SPic. as denominal *soikqje/o- to a noun *soiko- 'declaration'.

Bibl.: IEW 897f., Meiser 1986: 87f, Untermann 2000: 589f, 712f., LIV *sejk-.

<!-- pdf-page: 610; printed-page: 596; running-header: 596                                      sucus -->



### sucus
sucus 'juice* [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: sucidus 'fresh, juicy1 (Varro+), comucidus afresh, juicy' (Pl.).

Pit. *souko-. ι

PIE *soulc-o- 'juice' (or *seuk-o~?).

IE cognates: W. sugno 'to suck', MBret. sunaff 'juice \ W. sugnedydd 'pump1 (< *seuk-n-7); Latv, sukt, OCS stsati, Is. stsg, Ru. sosat' *to suck' < *suk-\ OIc. suga, OHG OE sugan 'to suck' < *sugan, OE sucan, MDu. *wcert < *sukan < *suk-n-7 We find the root for 'to suck' as *swfc- in Celtic, BSL and in some Germanic forms, and as *swg- in other Germanic forms and in Latin siigd. It is unlikely that this goes back to a root *su- with different root enlargements, so we must surmise that the root originally was *suk- or *s«g-, and changed to *sug- or *suk~ in certain phonetic environments. Both Latin and Germanic conserve both variants. Schrijver 2001: 423 regards the vacillation in root-final stop as a sign that these words for 'to suck' and 'juice' were borrowed from (a) substratum language(s). In its turn, this might have got the word from Uralic, since Proto-Uralic *soxi or *suwe 'mouth' looks quite similar to IE *suk/g-.

Bibl.: WH II: 622L, EM 662, IEW 912f, LIV *seuk-. -* sitgo



### sudor
sudor 'sweat' [m. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: sudare 'to sweat' (Naev.+), sudatdrius 'inducing sweat1 (Pl.+); cdnsudare 'to sweat weir (Pl.+), desudascere 'to sweat away' (Pl.).

Pit. *swe/oid-os-.

PIE *sueid-s- 'sweat' (colL)> *suoid-o- [m.] 'sweat'.

IE cognates: W. chwys, Co. whys, Bret chouez 'sweat' < *suidso-l, Skt sveda- [m.], YAv. xvaeSa- 'sweat', xvisa- 'to start sweating'; Gr. Ιδρώς, -ώτος [m.], ep. ace. ίδρώ 'sweat' < *suid-r-os-; ΐδίω 'to sweat', είδος (Hsch.), ΐδος [n.] 'sweat1 < *sueidos-; Arm. k'irtn, Alb. dirse < *suidrr-; Latv, sviedri [pL] 'sweat1 < *suoidro-; OHG sweiz, OE swat, ToB syelme cid.\ Sudare was probably formed on the basis of a m. *swoido- 'sweat1, as attested in Ilr. and Germ. The noun sudor can be inherited from *sweidos if Rix is correct in assuming a phonetic change of *swei- > *swoi-. Otherwise, the noun can be explained from the introduction ofsud- (or *$woid-) into the .s-stem.

Bibl.: WH II: 623, EM 662f, IEW 1043, Rix 1985: 341-343, Stuber 2002: 156-158, LIV l.*sueid-.



### sudus
sudus 'clear, bright (weather)* dry' [adj. o/a] (Pl.+)

Pit. *swoido~.

PIE *suoid~o- 'bright'. According to Lubotsky 1985> followed by Schrijver 1991: 54, sudus belongs to the IE adj. for 'dry* such as Lith, saiisas, and reflects *h₂Sus-do-. Nussbaum 1999a: 381 stresses that sudus refers to bright skies rather than to the absence of rain, and proposes an etymology *suoidro- 'bright', which he connects with Lith. svisti, Is. svindii 'to dawn', sviddti, svydhi (Dauksa) 'to shine', Latv. svist, Is. svidu 'to dawn' <

PIE *suid-. The root would thus be homophonous with 'to sweat' (Lat. sudare).

Bibl.: WH II: 624, EM 663, IEW 880f, Sihler 1995: 57, LIV ?2.*sueid-.

<!-- pdf-page: 611; printed-page: 597; running-header: suffragium                                    597 -->



### su€sco, -ere
su€sco, -ere 'to become accustomed to' [v. Ill; pf suevT, ppp. suetum] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: assuescere *to become or make accustomed' (Pl.+), assuetudo 'custom, intimacy' (Varro+), consuescere 'to become or make accustomed' (Pl.+), consuetio 'sexual intercourse' (Pl.), consuetiidd 'custom, disposition, intimacy5 (Pl.+), cdnsuefacere 'to make accustomed' (Ter.+), desuescere 'to disaccustom, become disaccustomed' (Titin,+), dissuefierl *to be disaccustomed' (Varro+), insuescere 'to become accustomed' (Pl.+); mansues, -etis 'mild, tame' (Pl.+), mansuetus 'tame, mild' (Ter.+), mansuescere 'to become tame' (Lucr.+), mansuefacere 'to tame' (Varro+).

Pit. *swe(p)-sk-e/o-. PEE *sue(hl-)dhh₁- 'to make one's own, get accustomed'. EE cognates: Gr. εϊωύα 'to be used, use', Lesb. ευωύα < PGr. *σέ-σρωτ3-α < *se-suohrct- (thus Schrijver; or from *se-suehrtf- with analogical -o?), Gr. ήύος [n.] 'custom, usage' < *sueh r d h h r os-; Go. swes 'own; property' [adj./n.], OHG OS swas, OE swces, OIc. svass 'dear' < *suect-s-o~. Suescere is an inchoative formation in *-ske/o- based on a PIE syntagm *sue-dʰh₁- or *sueh r d h h r 'to make into one's own', which may have as its first member the acc.sg. *sue 'self, own' (> *sue in monosyllable) or the ins.sg. *suehr. In Latin, the compound verbs are clearly older than the simplex suescere, which must be regarded as a back-formation. The adj. mansuetus < *manu-sueto- is probably older than mansues, which was formed on the model of inquies : inquietus (thus WH).

Bibl.: WH II: 32, 624£, EM 663, IEW 882-884, Schrijver 1991: 140f, Sihler 1995: 131, Hackstein 2002a: 12, Stuber 2002: 155f„ Meier-Efrugger 2004. — sodalis



### suffiō, -īre
suffiS, -Ire 'to fumigate' [v. IV] (Cato+)

Derivatives: exfir (Paul, ex F.) 'purgamentum unde adhuc manet suffitio'.

Pit. *-puje/o-l

PIE *dʰuh₂-ie-,

IE cognates: Hit. tuhhae-21 'to produce smoke' <

PIE *cfuh₂-o-je/o- (denominal to a noun *ctuhro-\ Hit. antuyiahhas- / antuhs- 'man' < nom. *hin-dtuehros, gen.sg. *hin-ctuhrs-os\ Gr. ϋύω 'to sacrifice', CS duti 'to blow5, Sin. diti 'to smell, blow', ToB twasastar 'bums, shines', ToA twasis 'burns'. Exfir might be a corrupted form or derivation of *exfio. The phonetic development leading from PEE *dʰuH-ie/o- to -fid is disputed. Most scholars seem to assume *dʰuH-ie- > *dW- > *jufe- > fije- (unrounding by means of the 'pius-Law'); whereas Kortlandt (2007: 136) posits a development *ctuHie/o- > *-cfwio > -fid.

Bibl.: WH I: 499, EM 663, IEW 261-267, Schrijver 1991: 322-325, Kloekhorst 2008: 188, 886, LP/ *dʰuehr. ^fimumjoeted^fuligo^mus



### suffragium
suffragium 'vote cast in an assembly, voting' [n. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: refragare/ϊ 'to oppose' (Pompon.+), suffragarefi 'to express public support' (Sis.-l-), suffrago, -inis 'joint in the hind leg of a quadruped; sucker shoot' (Col.+), sujfragator One who gives support to a candidate* (Pl.+).

Pit. *-ymg-. Clearly, these forms are based on a verb *sub-fragere or a noun *sub-frag-o-

<!-- pdf-page: 612; printed-page: 598; running-header: 598                                       sugo -->

* support'. Since the oldest meanings all refer to the process of voting for or against a candidate, one might support the view that *sub-frag- belongs to frangere Ho break': *sub-frag- 'who shout in support of 0 candidate' (but fragor 'noise' is not attested before Lucr.). This view is advocated in detail by Vaahtera 1993, who explains long -a- analogically on the model of e.g. tango - contagium. Szemerenyi 1989: 3 If. derives -frdgium from a (substratum?) root *bʰrag- 'hind part, tail-bone\ which he connects with Celtic braka and Germanic *bwk- 'tail-bone'. He hypothesizes for suffragium a semantic change from "support under the hind part' to 'political support'. This is too fantastic to be credible (cf Vaahtera 1993: 70).

Bibl.: WH II: 625f, EM 663, IEW 165, -*frango

sug6, -ere 'to suck' [v. Ill; pf suxi, ppp. suctum\ (Novius+)

### sulcus
sulcus 'furrow' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: sulcare 'to plough, draw furrows' (Varro+); bisulcis I bisulcus 'divided into two parts, forked' (Ρλ+).

Pit. *solL·-.

PIE *solk-o- ' furrow \

IE cognates: Gr. έλκω 'to draw, drag', ολκός [adj., m.] 'attractive, trailing; hauling-engine for ships; furrow', Alb. helq, heq 'to draw' (< *solkeie/o-\ OE sulh [f ] 'plough' < *s/Jfc-, ToB salk- 'to draw out\ In theory, sulcus can reflect

### sulpur, -uris
sulpur, -uris 'sulphur' [n. r] (Cato+)

Derivatives: sulpureus 'containing sulphur' (Enn.+).

Pit. *solpor I *solpur,

PIE *solp-r? 'fat'.

IE cognates: Skt. sarpis- [n.] 'melted butter, lard', srpra- [adj.] 'greasy, smooth, sleek\ Gr, δλττη [f.] 'oil-flask', Alb. gjalpe 'butter', OHG salba 'ointment, salve', ToA falyp, ToB salype 'butter, oil'. The best spelling is sulpur. The Seneca mss. mostly have sulphur\ those of Pliny have sulpur and sulphur. Szemerenyi (1995: 410) suggests that sulpur derivesfrom*sulpos < *solpos, as in sulcus. The preform *solpos would ultimately derive from *selpos Oily substance', with cognates in Greek, Tocharian and Germanic. The development of *selpos to *solpos would be irregular, however, and final -ur instead of -or is also unexpected. Maybe the PIE preform was *solp-r?

Bibl.: WH II: 628, EM 665, IEW 1046,

### sum, esse
sum, esse 'to be' [v. Irregular, pr,ind,: sum, es, est, sumus, estis, sunt pr.sb. fuam etc. » siem/sim, etc; pf JuT> fut. futurus sum, inf. fore] (VOLat.+: Forum cippus esed 'erit', Duenos inscr. sieds, Garigliano Bowl esom 'sum', Elog.Scip. fuit, fiiise, fuet, Lex Sacra (CIL 401) estod)

Derivatives: escit (Lex XII, Lucr., Paul, ex F,), escunt 'will be' (Cic), obescet (Paul, ex F.) Oberit vel aderit', superescit (Enn., Ace) 'supererit'; absens, -tis 'absent' (Pl.+), consens, -tis [adj./noun] (Van, CIL; only in di consentes 'the twelve major deities'), praesens 'present, ready' (Lex XII+), praesentarius 'in ready money' (Pl.+), praesentia 'presence' (Ter,+); obesse 'to be absent, different', adesse 'to be present' (Naev.+), deesse 'to be lacking' (Pl.+), mes.se Ho be in or on' (Naev.+), interesse 'to lie between, make a difference; attend' (Pl.+), obesse 'to be a hindrance' (Pl.+), praeesse 'to be in charge' (Pl.+), prodesse? prosum 'to be of use' (Naev.+), subesse 'to be underneath' (Pl.+), superesse 'to be higher, remain'.

Pit. pr,ind. *es-/s-, pr,sb. *es-e/o-, inf. *e1s-; pf, *fefii-; fut, fits-.

It. cognates: O. ezum, U. eru, erom [inf.], SPic esum, Presam. sim, esum, sum, O, sum, sum, Hern. esu, U, esu Ί am', O. est, ist, U, est, est 'is', O, sent, set, sunt, U. sent 'they are', U. sir, sei, si [2s.pr,sb,], U. si [3s.pr.sb.], U. sis, sins [3p.pr.sb.], \J.ifutu [2s.ipv.II], U. futu, O. estud, estud^ Vol. estu, [3s.ipv.II], JJ.fututo [2p.ipv.II], O. fufans [3p.ipf.], fusid [3s.ipf.sb.], O. fufens [3p,pf.],y«/<i[3s,pf,sb.],yks/, U. fust, fust, fits [3s.fut.pf,], furent, fefure [3p,fut,pf,]; in univerbation with pppT: U, antervakaze, anderuacose [3s,pr,sb,], O, teremnatust [3s,pf,ps.], with ptc.pr: U. atefafust, andersafitst, andirsafust [3s.fut.pf,]; with* preverbs: O, adfust [3s.futpf.], U, amprefu<u>s [3s,fut,pf.], ambrefurent [3p.fut.pf], SPic, isiins '[3p.sb.?] (*en-\ O, praesentid [ptc.pr., abl.sg.f], 0, upslm [ls.pr.sb,?], osins [3p.pr.sb.] (*op-si-).

PIE pr. *hles-/*his- 'to be'.

IE cognates: Gaul, ιμμι^ imi Ί am', Olr. is; W. ys [3s.pr.], oedd [pret], Co. us, vsy OBret. is 'is' < PCI. *es-; Hit. es-™ / as- 'to be, be present', PaL CLuw. as-/as-, HLuw. as-/s~, Lye. es-/ah- 'to be', Skt, as 'to be' [pr. asti, santi, opt. syat, pf, dsa] , Av. ah-/h-, OP amiy Ί am', hcTtiy 'they are', Gr. Att, είμί (= emi, Aeol, έμμι, Dor. ήμί) , 2s, ει, 3s. εστί, Ιρ, είμέν, 2p. έστέ, 3ρ, εισί (Dor. έντί)] 'to be.'; Alb. jam Ί am', 2s, je, 3s, eshte I a(n)shte\ 2s,sb, (te) jesh, ls,ipf. jeshe; OPr. αψιαι [Is,], ast, est [3s.], OLith, esmi, Lith. esti OCSjesmbJestt; Ru, est' , Cz.jsem,jest, OPo.jesm; Po.jest; Bulg. e; SCr.jesam, sam,jest\ Sin. ssm,je\ Go. im, is, ist [123s,], sind [3p.] 'to be'. In consentes, the pr.ptcact, may have been preserved. The development of the present paradigm in Italic is disputed, Joseph-Wallace (1987) argue for phonetic development of *esmi > esom > sum because of the enclitic status of the verb 'to be' in some contexts, but Dunkel 1998 contradicts this, joined by Schrijver 2006: 58, They explain

Pit. *esom as a reflex of the original thematic subjunctive *es-e/o- which is also found in Celtic.

Bibl.: WH I: 263, 420, II: 628f., EM 138, 665, IEW 340ff„ Leumann 1977: 523, Meiser 1998: 221, Untermann 2000: 245-252, Schumacher 2004: 295-317, LIV l,*hjes-. →fiti,possev sons

<!-- pdf-page: 614; printed-page: 600; running-header: 600                                    summus -->



### summits
summits 'highest' [adj. o/a] (Andn+)

Derivatives: summa 'total number, amount, whole' (Pl.+), Summanus 'the god of high places' (Pl.+), summatim 'summarily' (LucilH-), summatus, -us 'being chief (Lua\+), summas, -atis [adj.] 4 of the first rank' (Pl.+).

Pit. *sup(a)mo- 'highest'.

It. cognates: U. sume [loc.sg.], somo [acc.sg.m.], somo [abl.sg.m.] 'upper' < *sup-mo-,

PIE *(hi)up-m(H>> 'highest'.

IE cognates: Skt. upama-, YAv, upsma- 'upmost, highest, closest' < Ilr. *(H)upmHa-, Gr. ύπατος 'upmost, highest'; OE ufemest 'highest'. The Ilr. cognates suggest a superlative formation *supamo > *summo-. Yet a preform *sup-mo- cannot be excluded, cf. Gr. πρόμος.

Bibl.: WH II: 629£, EM 666, IEW 1106f., Meiser 1998: 152, Untermann 2000: 716f. → sub, super



### suo, -ere
suo, -ere 'to sew' [v. Ill; pf. suT, ppp. sutum] (Ter.+)

Derivatives: subula 'shoemaker's awl' (Sen.+), sutor 'shoemaker' (Pl.+), sutorius 'of a shoemaker' (Varro+), sutnnus 'of shoemaking' (VaiTO+), siitela 'cunning device' (Pl.+); pellesulna 'shop where skins are sold5 (Varro); consuere 'to sew together, plan' (Pl.+).

Pit. *s(j)iije/o-, *s(j)uto-*

PIE *siuH-ie/o- 'to sew', *siuH-to- 'sewn'.

IE cognates: Skt. pr. sTvya- 'to sew', syuta- 'sewn up', syuman- [n.] 'band, thong, bridle', sutra- [n.] 'wire, line, cord', Khot. hvya 'sewn stuffs' < *siuHC <

PIE *siHuC-\ Lith. siuti, Latv. sut, SCr. Siti; Go. siujan 'to sew'. Skt. contains traces of the original sequence *siHu- which was metathesized to *siuH- under certain conditions, probably in front of consonants. The resulting present *shtH-ie/o- lost its first *-i- (by means of dissimilation?) and subsequently developed to *suje/o-; the form su- was then also introduced into the ppp. The absence of the development of *w to ϊ in front of *i (cf. plus, inciens) is explained by Meiser from 'dissimilatory retention' of u between two V s (2003: 236; until after the fronting of *w), but maybe u was simply restored on the model of the derivatives in *su-.

Bibl.: WH II: 620, 631, EM 662, 667, IEW 915£, Schrijver 1991: 247f., Meiser 1998: 227, 2003:235f., LIV *sieuIK ^ moved



### super
super 'over, above, beyond' [adv., prep.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: superus [nom.sg.m. super Cato] 'upper, situated above' (Andn+), supra [adv, prep.] 'at a higher level; on top of, above' (Andr.+), insuper [adv., prep.] 'on top; above, over' (Pl.+), superare 'to surpass, defeat, climb over' (NaevH-), superior 'higher, stronger' (Pl.+), supremus 'highest, last' (Lex XII+), superne [adv.] 'at a higher level, on top' (Lucr.+), supernus 'situated at a higher level' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *super(i) 'above', *supero- 'upper', *super-J5s- 'higher', *superisamo- 'highest'.

It. cognates: U. subra, subra [adv.], subra [prep. + ace] 'above' < *superad\ U. super [prep. + loc] On top'; U superne [prep. + ace] 'over' < *$uper-noi [loc.sg.] or adv. *super~ned to *super-no-; O. supruis [dat.pLm.], U.

<!-- pdf-page: 615; printed-page: 601; running-header: suppus                                      601 -->

supru [abtsg.] 'upper' < *sup-ero-.

PIE *(hj)uper(i) [adv.] Over, above', *uper-o- [adj.] 'above'.

IE cognates: Celtib. ueramos 'highest', Gaul, uer- 'high', Olr. for(-) 'up, over, beyond', W. gor-, gwar- 'over', OW guar, MW ar, MCo. ar, OBret guar, Bret, war 'on' < *wp<?rs Skt. upari, YAv. upairi 'above, over', OP uporiy Over, on', Skt. itparo- [adj.] 'below, under, later', YAv. upara- 'upper, higher', Gr. υπέρ, ΰπερ 'over, above', Gr. ύπερος [m.] 'pestle', Ann. ι ver 'up', OHG ubir 'over', Go. ufar; OE ufera 'higher'. For the addition of s- in Latin super as opposed to

PIE *«/?-, see s.v. sub. Supra < abl.sg.f. *superad. The sup. developed from *supresmos < *suprismos < *superisamos. The adv. superne probably contains the suffix *-ne (cf. pone < *postne)\ from it, supernus was backformed.

Bibl.: WH II: 613, 630, EM 660, 667f., IEW 1105f., Leumann 1977: 320, Sihler 1995: 368, 441, Meiser 1998: 152, Untermann 2000: 706f., 720-722. → probus (for superbus), sub, summus, supinus



### supo, -are; -sipO, -are
supo, -are; -sipO, -are 'to pour, strew' [v. I] (Paul, ex F. supare 'to throw')

Derivatives: dissu/ipare 'to disperse, shatter' (Pl.+), Tnsipere, -sipio (Cato+), insu/ipare (Paul. exF.) 'to throw ίη\ obsipare 'to scatter' (PL·, Paul. exF.).

Pit. *sup-i-, -sup-aje- 'to throw*.

PIE *sup-i- 'to throw\

IE cognates: Lith. supti, 3s. siipa 'to rock, cradle'; OCS suti, Is. svp$, Cz. souti (obs.), SGv sasuti 'to pour, strew' < BSl. *soup-, ORu. stp-b 'embankment, hill, mountain'; A The analysis of these verb forms is somewhat uncertain. The simplex supare is only attested in Paul, ex i7,, and it cannot be excluded that it was backformed from insupare and dissupare; hence, its -w-colour does not prove a Pit. root *sup-. Lat. msipere might be showing a simplex *sVpere, to which -sulipare may have been made by a productive process. In that case, the oldest reconstructible stem is *sYp-i(e/o)-* The root vowel may have been any short vowel, but if the original meaning was 'to throw', Slavic *soup- 'to pour, strew' is clearly the closest semantic match. «;

Bibl.: WH I: 356f., EM 668, IEW 1049, LIV *seup-.



### suppus
suppus 'lying on one's back, upside down' [adj. o/a] (Lucil.+)

Derivatives: suppare 'to turn on their back' (Acc.+).

Pit. *soupo- 'on the top, upside down'.

It. cognates: U. sopam, sopa [acc.sg.], supaf, supa [acc.pl.f.], supes [abl.pl.f ], supa, sopo, supo [acc.pl.n.], supa [acc.sg.f. or pl.f. or n.] 'part of the sacrificed animal, guts'.

PIE *hjeup-(o-) 'up, above'.

IE cognates: GauL· Uxello-dunum, Uxoma [topon.], Olr. vasal, W. uchel, Co. huhel, Bret, uhel 'high* < PCI. ^ou(x)selo- 'high' < PIE ♦hjoup-s- 'high', Gaul, (a) uz, Olr. os [+dat], W. uch, Co. (a) ugh 'above' < PCI. *ouxso- <

PIE *hiOups-o-; Hit. upp-Zi 'to come up (of the sun)' < *hiiup-ti\ Gr. ΰψι [adv.] 'on high, above'; Ru. yyse, SCr. vise, Sin. vise 'higher', Ru. vys' [f.] 'height' < *up-s- (Derksen 2008), OCS vysokb, Ru. vysok 'high' (anal. vy-)y Go. iupa 'above', iup 'upward' < *eup-.

<!-- pdf-page: 616; printed-page: 602; running-header: 602                                    surdus -->

Weiss (fthc.b) discusses earlier etymologies, and argues that Sabellic IsSpal can reflect

Pit. *soup-3 *soip- or *sop-. If Latin suppus stems from *supus via the littera-rulc, then both may reflect

Pit. *soupo- <

PIE *seup-o-, an internal derivative to *swp 'below, upwards'- The meaning of

Pit. *soupo- would have been 'on the top' = 'upside down'. This would explain U. 'guts' from the supine position in which animals are mostly slaughtered- Weiss compares Gr. ύπτιος 'supine, upside down' from υπό, with the pi. τα ΰπτια often referring to the belly of quadrupeds. This solution would imply that Italic also evidences the full grade of

PIE *1ι^ρ- which is furthermore found in Hittite, Germanic, Slavic and Celtic.

Bibl.: WH II: 634, EM 660,669, IEW 1106f. → sub



### surdus
surdus 'deaf; muted' [adj. ο/ά] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: absurdus *ridiculous,awkward7 (Pl.+). Since 'deaf often has two semantic sides, viz. 'who cannot hear' and 'who is not heard', absurdus can be explained as 'which is unheard of, and regularly belongs to surdus. According to WH and IEW, surdus might belong to the PIE root *suer^ 'to sound', but this leaves the semantics unexplained, since surdus means 'unheard' or 'not hearing'. If absurdus was original, it may have meant 'off hearing' (> 'unheard of), in line with other compounds in ab-. Then surdus would have originally meant 'hearing', but changed its meaning to 'not hearing' under the influence of absurdus. This is very speculative, of course.

Bibl.: WH I: 5, II: 634, EM 4, 669, IEW 1049f, Nussbaum 1999a: 381, LIV 1. *suer-.



### surus
surus 'post, stake' [m. o] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: surculus 'twig, stick' (Cato+), surcularius 'living among twigs' (Varro+); sura 'calf of the leg' (Pl.+)? No certain etymology. The length of the first vowel in surus is uncertain; if it was long, it may be connected with sura 'calf of the leg': the 'post, stake' would have been denominated after the body-part, as is often the case. If it was short surus, it might be cognate with Skt, svaru- 'sacrificial post, stake'.

Bibl.: WH Π: 634f., EM 669f, IEW 1050.



### susurrus
susurrus 'whisper, soft rustling' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: susurrare 'to whisper, make a soft rustling sound' (Ter.+).

Pit. *susur(r)-o-, *swer-.

It. cognates: O. sverrunei [dat.sg.j name of an official of the town Abella.

PIE *su-sur-? *suer- 'to resound'.

IE cognates: Bret huernin 'to quarrel', MW chwyrn 'rapid, whirling', OCo. hwirnores, Bret, c'hwiliores 'hornet' < *suer-r}-, MW chwerthin, MCo. hwerthin, MBret. huerzin cto laugh' < PCL *suar-ie/o-; Skt. svara- 'to make a sound, snort', OAv. xvaraiuiia- [adj.] 'sounding'; OIc. svarra 'to sparkle, rustle', OE swerian 'to swear'. An onomatopoeic formation, using reduplication, a hissing sibilant and -rr- to reflect the sound of whispering and rustling. Still, the word may be formed on the basis of a

<!-- pdf-page: 617; printed-page: 603; running-header: tabeo                                      603 -->

PIE root *suer- 'to resound'- O. sverrun- could meaning 'speaker' < 'resounder'; possible reconstructions are then *suer-es-dn- or ^suer^wn-.

Bibl.: WH II: 637, EM 670, IEW 1049f„ Schrijver 1995: 57, Untermann 2000: 726f., Schumacher 2004: 608f., LIV L*suer-.



### sus, suis
sus, suis 'pig, sow' [m., f. u] (Pl.+; ace. suem, acc.pl. sues, dat.abl.pl. subus, subus, suibus)

Derivatives: stale [n.] "pigsty* (VarrcH-), suillus 'of pigs, pork' (Pl>), sucula 'a windlass' (Pl.+); subulcus 'swineherd' (Cato+), sucerda 'pig's dung' (Lucil.+), suctdia/succidio 'joint of pork' (Cato+), suovetaurilia, -ium 'purificatory sacrifice of a boar, a ram and a bull' (Cato+).

Pit. *sus [nomj, *siim [ace], *suwos [gen.].

It. cognates: U. sim, si [accsg.], sif, sif [acc.pl.fj, sif, si [acc.plm.] 'pig' < *sw-; U. sufum, sufu, sorsom, sorso [acc.sgj, sorser [gen.sgj, sorsu [abLsgJ, sufuf [acc.plJ m/n. '?', maybe 'of a pig' in one context, elsewhere unclear; *sodo- < *soudo-7

PIE *suhi/3S [nomj, *suhi/3-os [genJ 'pig, swine' (orig. 'sow').

IE cognates: Skt. sukara- [m.] 'wild boar', YAv. hu-, MP xwg, Gr. ύς, gen.sg. ύός, Alb. thi 'pig'; OHG, OE su 'sow', OIc. syr 'sow'. According to Schrijver, the alternation between nom.acc. sii-s, -m and oblique case forms su- led to the introduction of su- in compounds such as su-bulcus (formed after bubulcus) and su-cerda. The PIE word *suH- probably*meant 'sow', since it can be connected with Ilr. *suH- 'to give birth' (Skt sute 'gives birth', su- [f.] 'mother', a-su- 'barren', YAv. pr. hunami Ί give birth', haota- [n.] 'race, family'). In its turn, 'to give birth' can be derived via 'to be pregnant' from the PIE root *suhi/3- 'to be fair which Kioekhorst 2008: 785-798 reconstructs for Hit. sunna-* I sunn- [prj 'to fill' (< *su-no-hl/3_ / *su-n-h₁/3-), suu- / suuau- 'fair (< *souh₁/3-(o)u-) and Hit. suue/a-\ HLuw. suwa- 'to fill' (< *sHu-ie/o-).

Bibl.: WH II: 62If., 635f., EM 670, IEW 1038f.s Schrijver 1991: 533, Untermann 2000: 676f., 709f.

τ tabeO 'to rot away, decay' [v. II] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: tabum 'gore, putrified fluid matter' (Enn.+), tabes, -is [fj 'wasting, decay' (Lucr.+), tabescere (pf. tabui) 'to melt away, waste away' ( Andr.-H); contabescere 'to waste away, pine' (Pl.+), contabefacere 'to make to waste away' (Pl.); tabificus 'causing decay' (Lucr.+), tabificabilis 'causing decay' (Ace).

Pit. *tafe- 'to be melting'.

PIE *teh₂-bʰ-ehr 'to be melting'.

IE cognates: Olr. taid*, -taa* 'to melt, dissolve' < *ta-je/o-1 MW todi, OBret teuziff'to melt' < ^ta-de/o-, Oss. taj- 'to thaw, melt', Gr.

<!-- pdf-page: 618; printed-page: 604; running-header: 604                                      tabema -->


τήκω, Dor τάκω
τήκω, Dor τάκω 'to melt', τακερός 'melting, soft', Arm. t'anam 'to make wet', SeCS tajati 'to thaw', Ru. tajat' 'to melt, thaw, dwindle'. The original meaning 'to be melting' ifc only attested in Andr.; from 'melt away', the meaning developed to 'waste away', which is the basis of all subsequent attestations in Latin. In theory, the root enlargement reflected in Latin -b- could be

PIE *fc or *6Λ; since *6 is a very rare phoneme in PIE, *bʰ is much more likely.

Bibl.: WH Π: 639f, EM 672, IEW 1053f, Schrijver 1991: 145, Schumacher 2004: 627f,LIV2.*teh₂-,



### tabema
tabema 'inn, shop' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tabernaculum 'tent' (Pl.+), tabernula 'small shop' (Varro+); contubernium * comradeship' (Cic.+). A derivative *trab-erna to trabs 'tree-trunk, beam', with dissimilation of the first r. The original meaning was probably 'wooden shed'. The reluctance expressed by EM to accept this derivation is unfounded, since dissimilations in general are irregular.

Bibl.: WH 11:639, EM 672, IEW 1090, Leumann 1977: 322, Meiser 1998: 127. → trabs



### tabula
tabula 'board, plank' [f. α] (Pl.+; SCBac. tabold)

Derivatives: tabella 'wooden tablet, board' (Pl.+), tabulation 'floor made of boards, stage5 (Enn.+), tab(u)linum 'room between the atium and the peristyle5 (Varro+).

Pit. *taf/pla- 'board5.

It. cognates: U. tafle e [loc.sg. + -en] [f] 'instrument for transporting the sacrificial fire\

PIE *th 2 -dV? The etymology is uncertain. If the original form was

Pit. *ta/?/a-, one may analyze it as a root *ta- plus the instrument suffix

PIE *-<//o. As proposed by Southern (2000: 97, 128), the root could be Λ-less *teh₂- 'to stand'; we find

PIE *stehr in Latin stare, but compare PTo. and PCI. *ta- 'to stand'. The original meaning would be 'which stands' > 'board, tablet5. For the suffix, compare stabilis derived from stare.

Bibl.: WH Π: 640f., EM 672, IEW 1061, Leumann 1977: 103, Untermann 2000: 730f → sto



### taceo
taceo 'to be silent' [v. II; pf tacui, ppp. taciturn] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: taciturnus 'maintaining silence' (Pl.+), taciturnitas 'the maintaining of silence' (Pl.+), tacitulus 'silent' (Varro); conticescere 'to stop talking' (Pl.+), conticinium 'the quiet of the night' (Pl.+), obticere 'to remain silent' (Ter.+), obticescere 'to answer with silence5 (Pl.+), reticere 'to keep silent, leave unsaid' (Pl.+), reticentia 'silence' (Pl.+).

Pit. *tak-e- 'to be silent'.

It. cognates: U. ta?ez, tases, tasis [ppp. nom.sg.rn.], tasetur [nom.pl.m.] 'tacit' < *taketo-.

PIE *tHk-ehr 'to be silent'.

IE cognates: Go. pahan 'to keep secret', Olc.pegja, OS thagian, OHG thagen < PGm. *paxe-je-. LTV follows Klingenschmitt 1982: 78 in assuming that the Latin and Gm. words for

<!-- pdf-page: 619; printed-page: 605; running-header: talus                                      605 -->


'to keep silent' belong to Gr, πτήσσω, πτώσσω 'to duck (for fear)', aor, έπτηξα, Dor. έπτάξα, and Arm. t'dk'eaw 'he hid himself. This seems implausible. Formally, it is unknown whether

PIE *pi- would yield Germ. *p- and Latin /- (the case of perna, Germ. *fersn- < *tspersn-h₂- 'heel' rather suggests that *pi- would yield Germ. */-, Lat. p-). The Greek and Armenian words have a different meaning, and are better connected with *pet- 'to fair.

Bibl.: WH II: 641f., EM 673, IEW 1055, Schrijver 1991: 104, Untermann 2000: 731f., LIV*pteh₂k-.

### talea
talea 'cutting, thin piece of wood' [£ a] (Cato+) Has been connected with Gr. ταλις -ιδος 'young girl, bride' on the assumption of a basic meaning 'green, to sprout'. This seems a wild guess. There is no viable etymology for talea, unless it is a derivative of talus 'ankle, knuckle'.

talis 'of such kind' [adj. i] (Pl.+)

### talus
talus 'ankle, knuckle' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: taxillus 'a die, cube' (Pompon.+); subtel 'the hollow of the foot' (Prise.)-

Pit. *takslo-. If taxillus is old, and not a recent analogical form on the model of dla - axilla, mala -

<!-- pdf-page: 620; printed-page: 606; running-header: 606                                        tarn -->

maxilla (but these are <>stems), as EM object, taxillus would prove that talus goes back to *takslo-. The suggested connection with W. sawdl, Olr. sal 'heeP < PCI. *statla- is difficult, cf. Schrijver 1995* 421. It would only work in the case of an s-less variant of the root

### tam
tam 'so, so much' [adv.] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: tamen 'yet, nevertheless * (Andr.+), tandem 'after all, at last' (Pl.+), tamquam 'just as, as though' (Naev.+), tantus 'so great, as much' (Naev.+), tantillus 'so small' (Pl.+), tantTsper 'for so long as, all the time' (Pl.+).

Pit. *tam 'so', *tanto- 'so great*.

It. cognates: O. etanto, U. etantu [nom.sg.f.] 'so great' < *e-tanto~; maybe U. -ta, -tu, -to [postp. + abl.] 'from, away' < *Λ5, poss. to

PIE *to/ta-.

PIE *teh₂m, *teh₂-nt-(o-).

IE cognates: see s.v. so-. Tamen < *tam-em, tandem < *tam-dem with the secondary particle -dim. The form *tam might be an Italic creation, s.v. quam, -dam; but tantus probably reflects a PIE formation type of adjectives in *-nt(-o)- derived from pronominal stems.
