### tango, -ere
tango, -ere 'to touch' [v. Ill; tetigf, factum; sb. OLat. tag-α-, Pac. also taxis] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: dumtaxat 'at most, up to; as long as' (Pl.+), taxim 'gropingly' (Pompon.+), taxare ςΐο assess (the worth of)' (Sen.+); tagax 'light-fingered, thievish' (Lucil.+); tactio 'touching' (Pl.+), tactilis 'able to be touched* (Lucr,), tactus, -us 'touch' (Lucr.+); integer [o/aj 'untouched, unused' (Naev.+), integrascere 'to begin anew' (Ten), integratio 'renewal' (Ter.), integrare 'to renew' (Pac.+); attingere 'to touch, reach' (Pl.+; sb. attiga- in OLat.), contingere ςΐο touch, reach' (Pl.+), obtingere 'to fall as one's lot' (Pl.+); contages, -is 'contact' (Lucr,), contagid 'contact' (Pl.+), contagium 'contact' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *tang-e- [pr.], *tag-s- [pr.], *te-tag~ [pf.], *tagto- [ppp.], *ntagro- 'untouched'.

It. cognates: U. antakres, antakre [abl.pl.] probably 'untouched' < *n-tag-ro-; maybe Marr. taa [3s.sb.J 'to touch' or 'to take away' < *tag~a-f?; maybe Vol. atahus [3s.fut.pf.]'?' < *ad-tah-us-t or a-. The reflex h < *g is irregular, cf. Untermann.

PIE pr. *th₂-n-g- ςΐο touch', ppp. *th₂g-to-, *-teh₂g- in cp.

IE cognates: Gr. τεταγών 'having seized', Go. tekan 'to touch', taitok 'touched', OIc. tok 'took', ToB cesarri, 3p ceken- 'to touch'. The OLat. sb. forms in *tag-a- can be regarded as remains of an earlier aorist paradigm, either root aor. or redupL It is likely that tagax (although attested relatively recent) and integer were also derived from this stem. The finite forms in tax- and taxare point to an earlier ^-present {cf.fax5yfaxim Xofacio\ as is the clearest in the petrified expression dumtaxat. The length in tact- stems from Lachmann's Law. The

<!-- pdf-page: 621; printed-page: 607; running-header: taxus                                       607 -->

long a in contages and contagio and -ium is most disputed: it could phonetically continue a full grade *teh₂g- origin, or be due to analogy with other compounds having -ag- in the second membre (indages, compages, etc.).

Bibl.: WH II: 647, EM 676, IEW 183, Schrijver 1991: 98, 132ff., 136f, 145, Kortlandt 2000, Untermann 2000: 107f, 13If., 729f, LIV 616f → contaminare



### tardus
tardus 'slow' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: tarditudo 'slowness' (Pl.+), tardare 'to cause to slow down, delay' (Pl.+), tardor 'slowness' (Varro+), tardescere 'to become slow' (Lucn+X tardiusculus 'rather slow' (Pl.+). On the formation of tardiusculus, the date of which is difficult to ascertain, see Kiimmel 2004b: 354. Tardus has no etymology; the connection with Gr. τέρυ 'weak' and Skt taruna- 'young* fresh' proposed by WH is semantically unconvincing, and formally impossibly (if the latter forms continue

PIE *ferw-).

Bibl.: WH II: 648f, EM 677, IEW 1070f



### taurus
taurus 'bull' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: taura 'barren cow' (Varro+), taureus derived from a bulP (Lucr.+), taurinus 'of a bull, of ox-hide' (Pl.+).

Pit. *tauro- 'bull'.

It. cognates: U. turuf, torn [acc.pL]/tures [abl.pl J [m.] 'a certain sacrificial animaF, 'bull?'. -;

IE cognates: Olr. tarb, W. tarw, Gr ταύρος 'bull' [m.]^ Lith. tauras, Latv. tours [m.] 'aurochs', OCS turb 'bull', Ru. tur (gen. turd) , SCr. tur 'aurochs'. It is striking that Latin does not show the change of *-awr- > *-arw- as inparvum, and with -e- in nervus. This might indicate that taurus is a loanword post-dating the metathesis *-ur- > *-ru-, but Greek does not appear an obvious candidate for the lending language, in view of the semantic field The earlier history of the word is uncertain: there is no cognate in Ilr. or Tocharian, whereas there are Semitic words for 'bull' which are conspicuously similar. Hence, it may have been an early loanword of the form *tauro- into the western IE languages.

Bibl.: WH II: 650f, EM 677, IEW 1080-1085, Leumann 1977: 101, Schrijver 1991: 266, Untermann:2000: 777f.



### taxus
taxus 'yew-tree' [fi o] (Enn.+)

IE cognates: Gr. τόξον 'bow\ Myc. to-ko-so-wo-L· /tokso-worgos/'bow-maker'. Since yews are the usual wood for making bows from Mesolithic times onward, taxus must be connected with Gr. τόξον, which was regarded as a loanword from Scythian *tax$a- 'bow\ but is already present in Mycenaean. Yet taxus cannot be a direct borrowing from Greek, nor (as far as the meaning is concerned) from Scythian. Watkins (1985: 69) derives Gr. τόξον as

PIE *tok*-so-from the root *tekʷ- 'to flow, run, flee', which is some contexts is used as "to shoot5. Latin taxus could then be from a zero-grade ^tk^-s-o- with secondary full grade -a-. Alternatively, taxus could be a loanword from a different IE dialect.

Bibl.: WH II: 653, EM 678, IEW 1059f, EIEC 654f.

<!-- pdf-page: 622; printed-page: 608; running-header: 608                                       tego -->



### tegō, -ere
tegO, -ere 'to cover' [v. Ill; p i texi, ppp. tectum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: teges, -etis [f] 'piece of matting, covering' (Naev.+), tegeiicula 'small piece of matting' (Varro+), tegimen 'cQver' (Lucr.+), tectum 'roof, ceiling, dwelling' (Pl.+), tector 'plasterer' (Varro+), tectorius [adj,] 'of plaster, stucco' (Varro+), tectarium 'cover, lid' (Cato), tegula 'roof-tile' (Pl.+), tegillum 'piece of rush matting' (Pl.+); toga 'formal outer garment, toga' (Pl.+), togalis 'of togas5 (Cato), togatus 'wearing a toga' (Varro+), togula 'a (bad) toga' (Titin.+); contegere 'to cover over, conceal' (Ter.+), circumtectus 'covered' (Naev.), detegere 'to remove the roof, uncover' (Pl.+), integere 'to cover, roof (Pl.+), integumentum 'shield, wrapping' (Pl.+), obte/igere 'to cover, protect, conceal' (Pl.+), pertegere 'to cover completely' (Pl.+).

Pit. *teg-e/o- 'to cover', *tekto- 'covered', *tege-t- 'covering', *toga- 'cover, cloak'.

It. cognates: SPic. tokam [acc.sg.] 'grave stone' or 'figure' < *toga-\ possibly U. tettome [acc.sg. + -en] '?', a certain locality < *tek-to-l U- tehtefim [acc.sg.] '?' < *teg-t-ed/lio-?

PIE *(s)teg-e/o- [prj 'to cover', *(s)tog-h₂- [fj, *(s)teg-lo- [nj.

IE cognates: Olr. teg, gen.sg. tige 'house' < *tegos- [a], in-tuigethar 'to cover', imthach 'a cover', W. to [m.] 'covering, roof, arndo 'shroud', Bret, to 'roof; Gr. στέγω 'to keep off, keep in5, στεγνός 'covered, waterproof, τέγος [n.] 'roof, house', OPr. stogis, Lith. stagas [m.], Latv. stags 'roof (<

PIE *stogo-); OHG dah, OIc. pak [n.] 'roof (< *togo-\ pekja 'to cover'. Latin shows a thematic present, a f-stem noun, an ά-stem *toga-, and the noun tegula. Long e in tectum is due to Lachmann5s lengthening.

Bibl.: WH II: 654, EM 678f, IEW 1013f, Schrijver 1991: 127, Sihier 1995: 77, 625, Meiser 1998:227, Untermann 2000: 750,773, Stuber 2002: 152, LIV *(s)teg-. → ffgnum



### tellūs, -ūris
telliis, -Oris 'ground, earth' [f. r] (Varro+)

Derivatives: meditullium 'the interior of a country, centre' (Cic.+).

Pit. *teln-(us-?l

PIE *telh₂-o- 'ground'.

IE cognates: Olr. talam 'earth' < *telamon-, Gr τελαμών 'strap' < *te!h₂-mon-; Skt tala- [n.] 'surface, bottom' (SrSu,+), Lith. tiles 'bottom of a barge, flooring', OCS tbla [pi.], ORu. thlo 'foundation, bottom' <

PIE *tlH-o-; OPr. talus 'floor', Lith. patalas 'bed, (pi.) feather-bed', Ru. potolok 'ceiling' <

PIE *h₂po-tolH-o-; maybe OIc. pil(i) [n.] 'board5,pilja [f.] 'deal, plank', pel [n.] 'floor', OE dille 'deal', OHG dil, dilo 'wall, deal'. We find an ablaut alternation *e : *o between *tellu- and the compound *medi-toll-io- which recalls that of terra : extorris. Telliis is unique in being the only polysyllabic (original) s-stem with long u in the suffix. If it goes back to PIE, -Us- may reflect *-uHs- (cf. crus\ or maybe an old w-stem with case forms in *~e/ou- (> Lat. -w-) which was extended by means of an s-suffix. The root could be

PIE *telh₂- 'to bear, carry', from which Olr. talam 'earth5 can also be derived (cf. Stuber 1998: I50f.)- In fact, Sabellic shows a pr. *telne/o- where Latin has tollo 'to bear'; this stem *teln- may have been the derivational basis for telliis (and *toln- for medi-tullium, unless -//- was taken from telliis), since tell- cannot directly reflect

Pit. *tela- < *tetH-, Thus, the

<!-- pdf-page: 623; printed-page: 609; running-header: temno                                     609 -->


earth would have been referred to as 'bearer' or 'support' (of the sky, or of the creatures and objects on the earth); the ostem *t(e)lH-o- 'ground, bottom' found in Skt., BSl. and Gm. could have the same origin,

Bibl.: WH II: 655, EM 679, IEW 1061, Stuber 1998: 150f. → tollo



### telum
telum 'spear, missile1 [n. o] (Lex XII+) Form and meaning allow for several etymologies, of which *ten(d)slo- 'with which one aims' (to tendo) or *teks-lo- 'which has been built' (to texd 'to weave') are mostly given. None of these is completely convincing.

Bibl.: WH II: 656, EM 679, IEW I058f.



### temere
temere 'blindly, recklessly' [adv.] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: temeriter 'thoughtlessly' (Ace), temeritudo 'recklessness' (Pac), temeritas 'recklessness' (Pl.+), temerarius 'accidental, reckless' (Pl.+), temerare 'to violate, desecrate' (Verg.+),

Pit. *temesi [loc.sg.]

PIE *temH-os- [n.] 'darkness'.

IE cognates: Skt. tamos- [n.] 'darkness, blinding', Av. tzmah- [n.j 'darkness'; Lith. temti 'to become dark, to become evening5, tamsa 'darkness'. A petrified loc.sg.froma noun *temes- < *temH-o/es- [n.] 'darkness' with clear cognates in the other IE languages and in Lat. tenebrae. The reconstruction *temH-ro- in Schrijver 1991:105 is probably a lapsus when compared with p. 41*5 in the same book.

Bibl.: WH II: 656f., EM 679, IEW 1063f„ Schrijver 1991: 104£, 415, Stuber 2002: 158f, LIV *temH-. → temetum, tenebrae



### temetura
temetura 'intoxicating liquor' [η. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: temulentus 'drunken' (Ter.+); abstemius 'refraining from wine, self-restrained5 (Lucil.+).

Pit. *temo- 'intoxication' or 'intoxicating substance1.

PIE *temH- 'intoxication'.

IE cognates: Skt. pn tamyati [3s.act.], aor. tamat [3s.sb,act.j, tamra- 'dark red, copper-red', Arm. t'mrim 'to become stunned' < */em-; NHG damisch) damlich < PGm. */>ig/7w-. Lat. abstemius can be regarded as a hypostasis from *abs tem5\ together with temulentus 'containg temo-\ this points to an original o-stem *temo- 'intoxication1, which may continue a PIE root noun. Long e is confirmed by Armenian and Germanic, but Skt. tamyati probably has secondary -a-.

Bibl.: WH II: 657, EM 679£, IEW 1063, Schrijver 1991: 127, LIV *temH-, → temere



### temno, -ere cto scorn, despise'
temno, -ere cto scorn, despise' [v. Ill] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: contemnere 'to despise, scorn' [pf. contempsl, ppp. contemptum) (Pl.+), contemptim 'contemptuously' (Naev.+), contemptrlx 'she who despises' (Pl.+), contemnificus 'scornful' (Lucil.).

Pit. *(fcom-)tmne/o-.

PIE *tm-n(e)-hr 'to cut\

IE cognates: Mir. tamnaid 'cuts' (prob denominal), Gr.

<!-- pdf-page: 624; printed-page: 610; running-header: 610                                       temo -->


τάμνω, Att τέμνω
τάμνω, Att τέμνω 'to cut', aor. έταμον, Att. έτεμον, Lith, tinti, 3s. una 'to whet, (colloq.) beat', Ru. tfat' (arch.), Is, trm 'to beat', Sin. t£ti, Is. tnem 'to chop' < BSl. *tin?tei; Gr. στέμβω 'to shake about, agitate'. The compound contemnere is the older verb, from which temnere has been backformed more recently. The etymology is disputed: the meaning 'scom' has probably developed from a more concrete meaning, for which two candidates have been advanced:

PIE *stemb- 'shake violently, pound' (WH, EM, Szemerenyi 1995: 414; in fact, it is likely that this is a non-IE root, because of *-£>-, because of the unexplained variants in Greek and because it has few reliable cognates outside Greek) and

PIE *temhr 'to cut5 (LIV, Sihler, Meiser). Semantically, both can be defended: 'to shake' or 'crush' > 'despise' for the former (eft the shift in spernere from *'to trample' > 'reject'), and 'to cut' > 'cut off > 'despise' for the latter. Formally, the present -temnere would best match *temhi-, of which Greek and Celtic show a nasal present *tm-n-hr, and because Latin lacks the initial s- of *stemb-. For the pf., Meiser assumes (influence of) a preform aor. *stemb-s-y but the -p- would automatically arise in a preform *kom-temsi, and the latter can simply be a Latin innovation on the basis of the present stem tem-. Note also that the oldest attestations are all of the compound verb: contempsi Latin has made an s-pf. to all nasal presents with word-internal nasal (cf Meiser 2003: 112-114), whence pr. temno > pf. *tem-su The other presents in -nere (cernere, linerey spernere, sternere) synchronically show a perfect in -VvL

Bibl.: WH II: 657f, EM 680, Schrijver 1991: 407, Sihler 1995: 534, Meiser 2003: 113ft, LIV *temh r . —» contumax



### temō
tem6 'pole or yoke-beam of a cart or a plough' [m. n] (Enn.+)

Pit. *ten(k)smdn- 'pole'.

PIE *ten(K)s(-)m-n- 'to pull'.

IE cognates: OHG dihsala, OEpvel, OIc, pisl 'pole, thill' (<PGm. *penxslo-). The etymology is disputed. In view of the Germanic word for 'pole', which reflects *tenksla→ Sommer 1914: 259 reconstructs *tenksmo for Latin. Meiser 1998, comparing the Slavic verb forms in *tercg\ reconstructs *tengfsmo-7 whereas Eichner 1992: 72 posits *tensmo. The question cannot be definitely solved, since PIE shows various verbal roots meaning 'to pull' which would fit the phonetics: *ten- 'to stretch, tend' (LIV), *tens- 'to pulF (LIV) and *tengʰ- 'to pull' (IEW 1067, de Vaan 1999: 15-17).

Bibl.: WH II: 658, EM 680, IEW 1067, Eichner 1992: 72, Isebaert-Seldeslachts 1994: 174, Meiser 1998: 118. → tendo, teneo



### templum
templum 'area for auspices, shrine' [n. o] (Andr.+; templa^ -drum 'plank, purlin' Lucr.+)

Derivatives: contemplare 'to gaze at, observe' (Naev>), extemp(u)ld [adv.J 'immediately' (Naev.+); maybe antemna 'yard of a sailing-ship' (Pl.+).

Pit. *t(e)mp-lo- [n.].

PIE *t(e)mp-Io-.

IE cognates: Lith. tempti 'to pull, stretch', 3s. tempia, OIc. pgmb 'bow', ToB campam, A campas 'is able'.

<!-- pdf-page: 625; printed-page: 611; running-header: tempus                                     611 -->

Probably from the root *temp~ 'to stretch, string', hence 'stretching' > 'measuring*. The adv. extemp(u)W might be based on the verbal meaning 'to stretch', as 'tightly' (cf Flemish straks 'immediately' to Dutch strak 'tightly'). The appurtenance of antemna to a root *temp- is disputed; it has also been analysed as *anti-mno- (cf. Leumann 1977: 322, Forssman 1965).

Bibl.: WH I: 54, II: 659, EM 681, IEW 1064f, Meiser 1998: 81, LIV *temp-.



### tempto, -are
tempto, -are 'to feel, test* [v. I] (Pl.+; also tentare)

Derivatives: pedetemptim 'cautiously' (Pl.+); attemptare {-nt-) 'to lay hands on, attack' (Pac.+), pertemptare (-nt-) 'to try out, feel all over' (Ter.+).

Pit. *t(e)mp-to- 'touched'.

PIE *t(e)mp-to-.

IE cognates: see s.v. templum. Probably an iterative based on a ppp. *tempto-. This presupposes a verb meaning 'to touch, feel', for which the root *temp- 'to stretch* seems a good candidate. Szemerenyi 1995: 414 reconstructs an iterative *temb-ita- to the root *(s)temb- 'to shake violently', which is less likely formally, see the discussion s.v. temno.

Bibl.: WH II: 662, EM 681, IEW 1064f, Leumann 1977: 501, LIV *temp-. → templum

tempus, -oris 'time, moment' [n. r] (Pl.+) τ

Derivatives: tempe/ori [adv.] ';at the right time' (Pl.+), temperius [adv.] 'earlier' (Cic.+), temporalis 'denoting time, temporary' (Varro+), tempestas Occasion, season, weather, storm' (Lex ΧΠ+), tempestivus 'ready at the proper time, ripe' (Cato+), tempestiis, -litis 'tempestas' (Varro), intempestus 'at the wrong time, nightly' (Enn.+), intempestivus 'at the wrong time, ill-timed' (Pl.+); temperare 'to restrain oneself, modify, blend' (Pl.+), temperantia 'self-controP (Varro+), temperatwra 'mixture, constitution' (Varro+), intemperans 'unrestrained' (Pl.+), intemperiae [f.pL] / intemperies 'immoderateness, rage' (Pl.+), obtemperare 'to obey' (Pl.+); tempora9 -drum [n.pL] 'side of the forehead, temple* (Pl.+; rarely sg. tempus).

Pit. ^tempos- [n.] 'stretch, measure*.

PIE *temp-os, ;-es- [n.] 'stretch'.

IE cognates: see s.v. templum. All forms are based on a PIE s-stem 'stretching, what is stretched' > 'stretch (of time)' > 'time, occasion'.

PIE *tempos-formedthe basis for the abstract tempes-tdt-9 the adj. tempes-tivus and the verb *tempes-aie- > temperare. The meaning 'to restrain, modify' of the latter shows the semantic shift from 'stretching' to 'measuring' which we also find in templum. This means that the noun *tempos- still meant 'measure' in Pit. The noun tempora 'side of the forehead' is also generally accepted to be the same word, having developed from 'stretchings' to 'stretched skin' > 'temple'. IEW and Stuber compare OIc. punn-vangi, OHG dunwangi 'temple', lit. 'thin cheek'.

Bibl.: WH II: 658-661, EM 680-682, IEW 1064f., Schrijver 1991: 384, Stuber 2002: 159f., LIV*temp-.

<!-- pdf-page: 626; printed-page: 612; running-header: 612                                      tendo -->



### tendō, -ere 'to extend, stretch'
tendO, -ere Ho extend, stretch' [v. Ill; pf. tetendi, ppp. tentum/tensum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tentipellium 'device for stretching skin or leather' (Afran.+); attendere 'to pay attention, listen' (Ter.+), contendere 'to contrast, contend, assert' (Naev.+), distendere 'to stretch out, fill' (Pl.+), extendere 'to extend, arrange' (Pl.+), intendere 'to stretch, strain, direct' (Pl.+), ostendere 'to show, reveaF (Pl.+), pertendere "to continue resolutely' (Ter.+), portendere 'to indicate, reveal' (Pl.+), subtendere 'to stretch out underneath* (Cato+); extentare 'to stretch, exert to the full' (Pl.+), ostentare 'to exhibit, demonstrate' (Pl.+), ostentator 'who displays ostentatiously' (Pl.+); protelum 'tandem of draught animals' (Cato+), protelare Ho drive forth, beat back' (Ter.+).

Pit. *tendr [pr.], *t#to- [ppp.].

It. cognates: LL ustentu, ustetu, ostendu [3s.ipv.II], ustentuta [3p.ipvJI], ostensendi [3p.fut.ps.] maybe Ho prepare, lay ready'. Untermann is hesitant about the connection with ostendere because of the semantics. Meiser (2003: 192) thinks that these forms belong to tollo.

PIE *ten-d(h)- [pr.] 'to stretch', *tn-to- [ppp.].

IE cognates: See s.v. teneo. See Kiimmel 2005 on Skt. tandate as a secondary formation to -tandra-. The root *ten- Ho stretch' has yielded two Latin presents, teneo and tendo; both share the ppp. tentus. The pf tetini of teneo can go back to

PIE *te-ton-7 whereas tendo has acquired a novel perfect te-tend-. The root-final dental of the PIE present cannot be identified. The noun protelum probably represents *pro-tend-slo- 'instrument for pulling forth'; but *-tens-lo- to the root variant

PIE *tens- is also possible, cf. idles.

Bibl.: WH II: 227, 344, 376f„ 662f., EM 470f., 524, 540, 682f„ IEW 1065fl, Sihler 1995: 503, Meiser 1998: 193, 2003: 19If., Untermann 2000: 812-814, LIV *ten-. → teneo



### tenebrae
tenebrae 'darkness' [f.pL a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tenebrdsus 'dark' (Varro+), tenebrio 'who operates in the dark, dishonest person' (Afran.+), tenebricus * shrouded in darkness' (Pac.+), tenebricosus Mark' (Varro+); contenebrascere 'to become completely dark' (Varro+).

Pit. *temasra~ 'darkness'.

PIE *temH-s-n> [adj.] Mark'.

IE cognates: Skt. tamisra- [f.] 'dark night', YAv. tqura- [pi.] 'darkness', MP tar 'darkness', tang [adj.] Mark'; OHG demar 'twilight' (<

PIE *temH^so-Y Dissimilated from *temebrai < *temasro- < *temH$-rh₂-, the f. of a roadjective which is a derivative of the PIE j-stem *temH-os-, reflected in Latin temere.

### teneō 'to hold, grasp'
teneo Ho hold, grasp' [v. II; pf tetini (» tenuT), ppp. tentum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: tenax * holding tight, stubborn' (Pl.+), tenacia * stubbornness' (Enn.), tenor, -oris [m.] 'sustained movement, course' (Varro+), tensa 'wagon used for transporting images of gods' (Titin.+), tenus, -oris [n.] 'kind of snare' (Pl.), tenus [adv., prep.] 'as far as, up to' (Lucr.+); attinere 'to hold, delay, concern' (Pl.+), continere 'to hold together, keep, comprise' (PI+), continuus 'uninterrupted' (Pl.+X

<!-- pdf-page: 627; printed-page: 613; running-header: tenuis                                       613 -->

detinere 'to keep, retain* (PK+), obtinere 'to maintain, occupy, achieve' (Pl.+), pertinere 'to be concerned with, belong to, reach' (Pl.+), pertinax 'stubborn, tenacious' (Pl.+), pertinacia 'stubbornness' (PK+X retinere 'to detain, retain' (Pl.+), retinaculum 'rein, rope' (Cato+), retentare 'to keep hold of (Pl.+), sustinere 'to support, preserve* (Pl.+), sustentare 'to keep from falling, maintain* (Pl.+), transtinere 'to provide a link* (Pl.),

Pit. *t(e)ne- [pr.], *tenos- [n.] 'snare, stretch*.

It. cognates: U. femft/ [3s.ipv.II] 'to hold* < *fe*-e-.

PIE *tn-eh r 'to hold', *ten-os- [n.] 'stretch*.

IE cognates: MW tannu 'to spread out\ MCo. tan 'take!' < PCI. *tan-nu-; Skt pr. tanoti [act.],tanwte'[med.], root aor. atan [3s.act.], pf. tatana 'to stretch, extend', tata- 'extended, skilled, performed*, OAv. us-tana- "stretched out*, YAv. pairiJanuiia [ls.opt.med.], pairi .. . tanauua [ls.sb.act.] 'to keep away (from)*; Gr. τείνω 'to stretch, pull tight', τάνιπαι 'to stretch (intr.)', τόνος [m.] 'tension, cord, string, tone, sound*, άλι-τενής 'extending to the sea'; Go. ufpanjan 'to extend', OHG den(n)en9 NHG dehnen 'to stretch*. Stative present to the root *ten- "to stretch'. In Latin, ten- may derive from a full-grade form or from the expected PIE zero grade, but U. has a full grade. Latin furthermore continues an s-stem in tenus [n.] and its petrified form tenus. Meiser (1998: 183) explains tenus from a ppa. *tn-uos 'stretching to', but this seems less likely: the meaning does not>point to an original perfect, and reduplication is absent whereas tetint retains it. The -5- in retinaculum is unexpected: was it influenced by tenax and tenacial *

Bibl.: WH I: 267, II: 664-667, EM 6831, DEW 10651, Stiiber 2002: 1601, Meiser 2003: 19If, Schumacher 2004: 618f, LIV *ten-. → tendoy toles



### tener, -a, -um
tener, -a, -um 'soft, delicate* [adj. o/a\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tenellus 'delicate' (PK+), tenellulus 'tender' (Laev.+), teneritudo 'tenderness* (Varrof), tenerescere 'to become soft* (Lucr.+); terenus 'mollis* (Favorinus). .

Pit. *terVno- {*teru-no- or *teren-o-1) 'soft'.

PIE *teru-no-:/ *ter-en- 'tender*.

IE cognates: Skt. taruna- 'young, tender, fresh', Av. tauruna- 'young*; [m.] 'boy*, Oss. tceryn, tyryn/tcerna 'boy*; Gr. τέρυ 'weak, soft' (Hsch.), τερύνης 'wom-out ass, infirm old man* (Hsch.), Gr. τέρην 'soft, delicate*. Since Dr. and Gr. show adjectives *terw-, *ter-n- and *teru-n-y it is tempting to derive *tenVro- via consonant metathesis from *terVno-. The 'Sabine' word terenus 'soft' which Macrobius (late 'th c. AD) ascribes to Favorinus would match such a reconstruction quite well

Bibl.: WH II: 665, EM 684, IEW 10701



### tenuis
tenuis 'slender, thin, fine' [adj. ί\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tenuitas 'scantiness, thinness* (Cato+), tenuare 'to make thin, reduce, narrow* (Lucil.+); subtenuis 'rather fine in structure' (Varro); attenuare 'to make slender, weaken' (Lucr.+), extenuare 'to make thin, diminish' (Varro+).

<!-- pdf-page: 628; printed-page: 614; running-header: 614                                       tepeo -->

Pit. *tp(a)u-.

PIE *tnh₂-(e)u- 'thin'.

IE cognates: Olr. tanae, W. teneu, C. tanow, Bret, tanao 'delicate, thin' < PCI. *tanauo < *tnk2-eu-o-; Skt tanu- 'thin, slender', MP tanuk, 'thin, flat', Gr. ταναός 'thin, elongated, expanding far', τσνύ- [pref.] 'thin, slender' < *tnHu-; Lith. t$vas 'thin, slender, high (of voices)', Latv. tievs 'thin, slender' < *tenh₂-u-o-; OCS ttnhkb, Ru. tonkij, Sin. tanak 'delicate, fine' < *tnHu-k-; OHG dunnU OIc. pimnr 'thin' < *tnHu-. The Pit. w-stem adj. was remade into an /-stem, yielding *tn(a)ui- > *ten(a)ui- > tenuis. The PIE root form *tnh₂- might be an extended variant of *ten- *to stretch', but this cannot be certified.

Bibl.: WH II: 666, EM684, IEW 1069, Schrijver 1991: 219,Meiser 1998: 110.



### tepeo
tepeo 'to be warm' [v. II] (Cato+)

Derivatives: tepidus 'warm' (Enn.+), tepor 'warmth' (Varro+), tepefacere 'to make fairly hot' (Varro+).

Pit. *tep-e- 'to be warm'.

PIE *t(e)p-ehr 'to be warm', *tep-os- [n.] 'warmth'.

IE cognates: Olr. te 'hot', tess 'heat', W. tes, Bret, tez 'id.'; Skt. tapati Ho heat, be/become hot', tapas- [n.] 'heat, glow', YAv. tqfsa- 'to become hot', tapaiia- 'to make hot, create warmth', hqm.tapta- 'hot', MP tab 'fever' (< PIr. *tapah-)\ OCS teplostb [I] 'warmth', Ru. teplyj, Cz. tepty 'hot' < *tep-lo-, OCS topiti, Ru. topit' 'to heat, melt'. Latin only has productive derivatives. Tepor might be a continuation of the PIE n. *tepos-, but since it is not attested before Varro, it may just as well be a recent formation. The verb tepeo might be older, but it has no direct cognates in other IE languages except maybe in Albanian fioh 'to cool down' if from *tpe-sk-.

Bibl.: WH II: 667, EM 685, IEW 1069f, Stuber 2002: 161f, LIV l.*tep-.



### tergeo
tergeo 'to rub clean, polish' [v. II; pf. tersT, ppp. tersum] (Pl.+; pr. also tergo, -ere)

Derivatives: abstergere 'to wipe clean, wipe off (Pl.+), circumtergere 'to wipe round about' (Cato), detergere 'to wipe away, trim' (Andr.+), extergere 'to wipe clean' (Pl.+); mantelum 'hand-towel, napkin' (Lucil.+), mantelium (Varro+) 'hand-towel, napkin'.

Pit. *ferg-.

It. cognates: U. mantrahklu, mantraklu, mandraclo [acc.sg.] 'object which the priest is holding', maybe < *man-trag-tlo- 'towel' vel sim. Mantelum < *manu-terg-slo~. LIV opts for tergere as the original verbal formation, but especially the compounds show that -tergere was more common. Since this is not a stative verb, one might expect an iterative */org-e-, but this is not attested. There are no certain cognates of the form *terg~. One might connect Skt. tarh- 'to crush' <

PIE *(s)tergh- (if *-rgʰ- > Lat. -rg-); but Kloekhorst 2008: 411 reconstructs the Skt. words as *stelgʰ-, in order to connect Hit. istalk-zl 'to flatten'.

Bibl.: WH Π: 670, EM 685f, IEW 1071-74, Meiser 1998: 119, Untermann 2000: 451, LIV ?2.*ter(g)- or *(s)tergh-·

<!-- pdf-page: 629; printed-page: 615; running-header: termes                                     615 -->



### tergum
tergum 'back' [n. o] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tergus, -oris [n.] (Enn.+), tergmum 'raw-hide whip' (Pl.+), tergiversan 'to show reluctance' (Sis.+). WH and IEW interpret tergum as *terg-o-, from a root *(s)ter- 'to be stiff with a suffix *-g-. The underlying assumption is that tergum originally indicated the stiff hair on the back of animals (IEW). This, however, is not supported by any evidence. Thus, the etymology remains unclear.

Bibl.: WH II: 670, EM 686, IEW 1022-1027, Leumann 1977: 166.



### U. terkantur [3p.pr.sb.]
U. terkantur [3p.pr.sb.] 'to check' or 'to confirm'.

Pit. *derk-e/o- [pr.].

PIE *derk- 'to look'.

IE cognates: Olr. adccon-dairc [pf.] 'saw' « *de-dork-, derc 'eye'; Skt dors- 'to see', su-darsa- 'easily seen, conspicuous', A v. dans- 'to look', doraso- [m./n.] 'looking', Gr. δέρκομαι, pf. δέδορκα 'to look', ύπό-δρα [adv.] 'looking up from below', Go. ga-tarhjan 'to expose, make public', OE torht 'clear'.

Bibl.: IEW 213, Untermann 2000: 747f., Schumacher 2004: 271, LIV *deik-.



### termen, -inis
termen, -inis 'boundary-stone' [n. n\ (CIL, Acc.+)

Derivatives: terminus 'boundary-post' (Naev.+), termd 'finishing-post in a race' (Enn.+), terminals 'marking a boundary' (Varro+), terminare Ho mark the boundaries, limit' (Lucr.+); determinare 'to delimit' (Pl.+).

Pit. *ter-mn- [n.] 'boundary stone'.

It. cognates: Ven. termonios [acc.pl.m.] 'of the boundary' < *ter-mon-io-; Presam. τερμανι [loc.sg.?], O. teremenniu [nom.acc.pl.], teremniss [dat.abl.pl.] n. 'boundary stone'< *ter-mn-\ (X teremnattens [3p.pf.], teremnatust [3s.pf.ps.], U, termnas (est) [3s.pf.ps.] 'to delimit' < denom. *termn-a-; U. termnome [acc.sg. + -en] , termnuco [abl.sg. + -kom] , termnesku [abl.pl. + -kom] [m.] 'boundary stone' < *ter-mn-o-.

PIE *ter-mn- [n.] 'peg, post'.

IE cognates: Hit. tarma- [c], CLuw. tarma/i- 'peg, nail' < *t(o)r(hj)-mo-y Gr. τέρμα [n.] 'goal, end-point', τέρμων [m.] 'boundary'. Latin termen, together with the base *ter-mon- of. Venetic and *ter-mn- of Sabellic, shows that Pit.-still had an ablauting suffix in the κ-stem. From this, Latin derived *termn~o- > terminus, with zero grade of *-mw- in view of Sabell. *ter-mn-o-. The word termd might have been borrowed from Greek. The Hittite noun and the usage in Latin suggest that the PIE word denoted a concrete object which came to refer to a boundary-stone. For this reason, the etymology deriving termen from the PIE root *terh₂- 'to pass, cross over' (with a mn-stem in Skt. su-tarman- 'which provides a good passage') is unattractive. The PIE root is unclear: maybe *ter-, an anit variant of *terh r 'to rub'?

Bibl.: WH II: 671, EM 686, IEW 1074f, Lejeune 1974: 339, Leumann 1977: 371, Untermann 2000: 745-748, Lazzarini-Poccetti 2001, Rix 2002a: 70.



### termes -itis
termes -itis 'bough cut from a tree' [m. /] (Hon+) No etymology.

Bibl.: WH II: 670, EM 686, IEW 1070f.

<!-- pdf-page: 630; printed-page: 616; running-header: 616                                        tero -->



### terō, -ere
terO, -ere 'to rub' [v. Ill; pf. trm, ppp. tntum\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tritor 'who rubs or grinds' (Pl.+), trjtura 'rubbing, threshing' (Varro+), terebra [f] 'drill' (Pl.+), terebrare 'to drill a hole' (Pl.+), exterebrare 'to extract with an auger' (Pl.+), termentum 'sore caused by friction' (Pl.+), tfibulum 'threshing-sledge' (Varro+; Varro trivolum), trJbulare 'to press, squeeze' (Cato+X tnticum 'wheat' (Pl.+), triticeus 'of wheat' (Cato+); atterere 'to rub, grind, wear' (Pl.+), conterere 'to pound to pieces, wear out, use up' (Naev.+), deterere 'to wear down, rub ofP (Naev.+), detrimentum 'harm, diminishraent' (Cato+), exterere 'to thresh out, elide' (Varro+), extermentarium 'linteum quod teritur coipore' (Varro), interere 'to crumble on, up' (Cato+), intertrigo 'sore place caused by rubbing' (Cato+), intertnmentum 'wear and tear' (Ter.+), obterere 'to crush, destroy' (Naev.+), praeterere 'to rub away the end' (Pl.+), retrimentum 'scrapings' (Varro), subterere 'to wear away below' (Pl.+); tarmes, -itis [HL] 'woodworm' (Pl.+).

Pit. *tera- [pr.], */re/- [pf ], *terasro- 'drill'.

PIE *terhr/*trh r [aor.?], *treh r i- [pr.] 'to rub'.

IE cognates: Gr. τείρω 'to oppress, distress, weaken', τέρετρον 'drill', ppp. τρητός 'bored through', Gr. τρίβω 'to rub, wear out'; Lith. tlrti 'to investigate', SeCS treti, Is. tbrp, Ru. teret* 'to rub'; OHG draen 'totum\ The Latin present is isolated among the other IE languages, who show a fe/o-present or, in the case of Greek, the extension -Tb-. Since Latin also shows Tin the preterite, it seems that PIE had an athematic present *terhri- I *trhrei-, which was either thematized or otherwise enlarged The Latin pr. can therefore hardly go back to a PIE thematic present, but at most to a root present, or, maybe more likely, a root aorist Latin -Γ- in the pf and ppp. could go back to *treh r i-, since *trh r ei- would yield *tare/-; or tri- arose in the ppp. from *trhri-to-.

Bibl.: WH Π: 649, 672f, EM 686f, IEW 1071-72, Schrijver 1991: 244f, 395f, Rix 1999: 517, LIV *terh r , ?*treigV ^ tncae



### terra
terra 'dry land, earth' [f. o] (Andn+)

Derivatives: terreus 'earthen' (Varro+), terrestris *on/of land, earthly' (Pl.+), territorium 'territory' (Varro+), terrenus 'of dry land, of earth' (Lucn+); extorris 'exiled, banished' (Acc.+).

Pit. *tersa- 'earth', terso- 'area'.

It. cognates: O. teras [gen.sg. oracc.pl.] 'earth'; O. teerfum, terum [nom,sg.], tereis [gen.sg.], terei [loc.sg.] [n.] 'area' < *ters-o-.

PIE *ters-h₂- 'dry land'.

IE cognates: Olr. tir 'territory; dry' [n.], OW tir < *ter$-o/es- (or */ers-r-, Thurneysen 1946: 49). Lat. extorris goes back to *eks-t(o)rs-i-. Since -torium is a productive suffix only after verbal stems, the rise of terri-torium is unexplained.

Pit. *ters-o/a- is generally derived from the root *ters- 'to dry out' (s.v. torred): 'dry land'. If Olr. tir, W. tir are cognate, they represent either an s-stem *ters-o$-, or a derivative in *-r-. Nussbaum apud Livingston 2004: 56 (fn. 31) suggests that terrenus could be derived from a loc.sg. *terrai.

Bibl.: WH II: 673f, EM 687f, IEW 1078, Untermann 2000: 736, 745, LIV *ters-. → torreo

<!-- pdf-page: 631; printed-page: 617; running-header: testa                                      617 -->



### terreō
terreO 'to terrorize, deter' [v. II; terrm, territurn] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: territare 'to (try to) scare' (Pl.+), terribilis 'frightening' (Enn.+), terricula/-um 'object of terror, bogy' (Lucil.+), terror 'terror, extreme fear' (Naev.+); terrificus 'terrifying' (Lucr.+X terrificare 'to alarm' (Lucr.+); absterrere 'to frighten away, deter' (Pl.+), deterrere 'to discourage' (Pl.+), exterrere 'to scare' (Enn.+), perterrere 'to terrify' (Pl.+), perterrefocere 'to make extremely frightened' (Ter.+).

Pit. *tros-eje- 'to scare'.

It. cognates: U. tuse<t>u, tursitu [3s.ipv.IT), tusetutu, tursituto [3p.ipvJI], tursiandu [3p.pr.sb.ps.] 'to scare' < *tors-eje-; U, turse, tuse, turse [dat.sg.], tursar [gen.sg.], tursa [voc.sg.] f. name of a goddess, invoked in the curse of foes < *torso- 'fright'.

PIE *tros-eie- Ho make scared',

IE cognates: Skt. pr. trasonti [3p.act], caus. trasaya- 'to tremble, be afraid\ YAv. urayhaiia- 'to scare', tdrssa- 'to be afraid', tarsta- 'scared, afraid', OP trsa- 'to be scared'; Gr. τρέω 'to be scared, tremble, flee', 6τρεστος 'fearless'; Lith. trishi Ho tremble, to shiver'. Since the other IE languages show an ablaut *trs- : *tres-, terred can reflect neither

PIE *ters- nor *tors- (with replacement of *torr- by *terr- on the model of terror, as assumed by WH and EM). LIV compares tertins 'third' < *tritio- and reconstructs *tros-eie- > (post-)Pit. *trseje- > Sab. *tors-eje-, Lat. *terseje- by regular phonetic development. This seems to be the best option.

Bibl.: WH II: 674, EM 688,. IEW 1095, Nussbaum 1999a: 412f., Untermann 2000: 775-777, LIV *tres-.



### tescum
tescum 'certain augural term; wild land' [n, o] (Acc.+; n.pl. tesca Var. / tesqua Hor. Luc, dat.abl.pl. tesquTs) The vacillation between tesc- and tesqu- points to *teskʷ- or *teskʷ-, which yielded tesc- in the nom.accsg. The meaning is established by Chanut 1980 as 'terrain of difficult acces, crags, maquis'. He argues that the use of tescum as an an augural term originates in the designation of the unusable side slopes of the Capitol, where the main templum was situated. The connection with Skt. tucchya- 'empty' is impossible, since the root has ablaut grade I *teus-. Dunkel 2000b: 21 posits *tues-kʷo-, which would have yielded *teskʷo- by dissimilation of the first labial glide; but the ablaut and the meaning make a connection with *teus- difficult. Alternatively, one might think of *ters-kʷo- to torred, that is, 'arid soil' vel sim.

Bibl.: WH II: 675, EM 688, IEW 1085, Chanut 1980.



### testa
testa 'earthenware vessel, tile, sherd, shelF [f. a] (Cato+)

Derivatives: testatim 'in fragments' (Pompon.+), testu, -us [n.], testum 'earthenware pot* (Cato+), testuacium 'cake baked in an earthenware pot' (Varro), testudd 'tortoise, shell, roof (Lucil.+), testudineus 'of a tortoise; of/with a tortoise-shell' (Pl.+). Derivation from the root *telc- 'to build' is unlikely for semantic reasons; also the outcome of

PIE *-tk- (if we would reconstruct a reduplicated form *te-tk-o-) in Latin is uncertain (s seems most likely, cf. situs); see also s.v. texo. The word testa is probably a loanword, as words for vessels often are.

Bibl.: WH II: 675f,, EM 688f., IEW 1058f.

<!-- pdf-page: 632; printed-page: 618; running-header: 618                                        testis -->



### testis
testis 'witness' [m. /] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: testes, -ium [m.pl] 'testicles' (Pl.+)> testicali [m.pl.] 'testicle' (Varro+), testimonium 'evidence in court, testimony' (Lex ΧΠ+), testankto invoke as a witness, testify' (Lex XII+), testamentum 'will, testament' (Pl.+), intestatus 'without having made a will, without having called a witness' (Lex XII+), intestabilis 'disqualified from calling witnesses, shameful' (Lex XII+); antestari 'to call as a witness' (Lex XII+), detestatum 'testatione denuntiatum' (Lex ΧΠ), obtestarl 'to beseech, invoke' (Pl.+)- Pit ^tri^sto/i-.

It. cognates: O. tristaamentud [abl.sg.] n, 'proof, loanword from Latin *tristamentom; O. trstus [nom.pL] 'witness' < *tristo- 'third'.

PIE *trito-sth₂-o- 'third standing (by)'.

IE cognates: Olr. triss, tres 'third' < *tristo-. The direct preform must be *tri-sth₂-i-, but the generally accepted meaning was 'third standing (by)' > 'witness', which would rather require the ordinal */ri/o-. Hence, Italo-Celtic *tristo- may be the result of haplological loss of the syllable *-to- from the original cp. The verb testari can have been derived directly from testis For the use of 'witness' in the meaning 'testicle', cf Gr. παραστάται δύο. Leumann 1977: 234 explains antestarifrom*anti-testan.

Bibl.: WH II: 676f, EM 689, IEW 1090-92, Coleman 1992: 424, Sihler 1995: 411, Meiser 1998: 80, Untermann 2000: 768-770, LIV *steh₂-. → tres



### te trie us
te trie us 'frowning, stern' [adj. o/a] (Varro+) The connection with terd 'to rub', proposed by WH and IEW, is semantically unconvincing. Possible preforms are *tetr- and *tedr-, but there is no good etymology.

Bibl.: WH II: 677, EM 689, IEW 1071-74.



### Ven. teuta [nom.sg.3, teuta[m?] [acc.sg.?], [teuta]i? [datsg.]
Ven. teuta [nom.sg.3, teuta[m?] [acc.sg.?], [teuta]i? [datsg.] 'civitas'; O. iouto, τωΡτο [nom.sg.], tautam [acc.sg.], toutad^ [abl.sg.], Mam toutai, totai [datsg,], U. tuta, totam, iota [acc.sg.], tutas, totar [gen.sg.], tute, tote [dat.sg.], tote, toteme [loc.sg.], tutaper, totaper [abl.sg- + iper], SPic. touta [nom.?], tutas [gen.?], toutaih [datsg./pl.?] 'town, society'; Presam. τοΡτιδ [abl.sg. of *toutio-]; O. tuvtiks [nom.sg.m.], τουτικες [gen.sg.m.], tuvtikfud] [abl.sg.m.], [toutijeas [gen.sg.f], tuv[tikai] [loc.sg.f], tuv[tikad] [abl.sg.f.], touiico [nom.sg.f], touticom, tuvftikom] [nom.acc.sg.n.], Vol toticu [abUg.n.], U. totco [nom.acasg.n.?], todcome [acc.sg.n. + -en] , totcor [nom.pl.n_], todceir [dat.abl.pl.n.], toce [adv_] 'of the touta-y .

Pit. *touta- 'town, society', *toutiko/a- [adj.].

IE cognates: Olr. tuath 'people, tribe', W. tud 'land', Co. tus, MBret. tut 'people'; OPr. tauto, Lith. tauta, Latv. tauia\ Go. piuda, OHG diot(a) , OS thiod(a) , OE deods OIc. pjod 'people' < PGm. *peupo-, OE gepiedan 'to translate', OIc. pyda 'to interpret, mean', Go. piudans *king\ OIc, pjodann, OE deoden, OS thiodan < *teutono-. A western IE word (Italo-Celtic, Germanic, Baltic). Gm. and Italic point to *-ew- and Baltic to *-oa-. whereas Celtic cannot be decided. Beekes 1998 explains * teuta- from

<!-- pdf-page: 633; printed-page: 619; running-header: tignum                                     619 -->

a non-IE substratum language, since it cannot be derived from a known PIE root. The word denotes a social group larger than any for which a PIE etymon is reconstructed; furthermore, terms meaning 'people* are often loanwords.

Bibl.: IEW 1080-85, Lejeune 1974: 339, Beekes 1998, Untermann 2000: 779-783, Lazzarini-Poccetti 2001.



### texo, -ere
texo, -ere 'to weave, construct' [v. Ill; pf. texui, ppp. textum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: textitis 'woven, plaited' (Lucr.+), textor * weaver' (Pl.+), textrinurn 'place of weaving, of constructing' (Enn.+), textus, -us 'structure' (Lucr.+), textura % structure, weaving' (Lucr.+); tela 'cloth on a loom, spider's web, plan' (Pl.+), subtilis [adj.] 'fine in texture, precise' (Lucr,+)7 subternen [also subtegrneri] 'weft, threads in a loom' (Pl.+); extexere 'to unweave' (Pl.+), praetexta 'toga with a purple border' (Lucil.+).

Pit. *tekse/o-.

PIE *te£-s- [pn] cto fashion'.

IE cognates: Hit. taks-ZI cto devise, undertake' < *teks-/tks-, MHG dehsen 'to break flax'; Skt. pr. tak?ati [3p.act.], tadhi [3s.ipv.act.], tasti [3s.act], pf. tataksa [3s.act.j, ppp. tasta- 'to hammer, form, fashion', tastar^ [m,] 'carpenter, master', Av, tasat [3s.aor.inj.], OAv. tast [3s.pr,inj.] 'to fashion', tasta- 4 created'; YAv. auui.. . tasti [3s.pr.act.]. Lat. tela < *teks-la-, subtilis <t*subtell·* Meiser 1998 argues that texo does not belong to the pr. *te-/£- cto build' but to a root *tek-s^ seen in Gr. τέχνη 'skill', OHG dehsala 'axe', Olr; tal caxe'; but the latter root may simply be *tek-s-, that is, the root 'to fashion' with an s-suffix. Meiser 2003 points to the possible PIE formulaic expression 'weaving words' (sermdnes texere Pl.), which involves in Skt. and Gr. the verb *te-tk- c to fashion'. For Latin tex-, this implies that it can either go back to

PIE *tek-s-, or to *te-tk- 'to fashion*. Since the outcome of word-internal *-/£- is uncertain, but might rather be single -s- (in ursus, where the cluster is preceded by r), I have a preference for *tek-s-.

Bibl.: WH II:,619f, 678, EM 662, 690, IEW 1058f, Sihler 1995: 225, Meiser 1998: 96f, 2003: 127, LIV 2.*tek-, *tetk. -* tignum



### fibia
fibia 'reed-pipe; shin-bone' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tlblcen, -inis [m.] 'piper* (Pk+), tJbicina 'female piper* (Pl.+).

IE cognates: Gr. σίφων, -ωνος [m.] 'tube, siphon'. The original meaning may have been either 'stalk' or 'shin-bone' (even though the latter meaning is only attested from Pliny onwards: flutes were often made from shin-bones). The compounds represent *tibio-kan- 'playing on a reed-pipe'. There is no good IE etymology; maybe the word is a loanword from the same source as Gr. σίφων.

Bibl.: WH II: 680, EM 691, IEW 1102.



### tignum
tignum 'piece of timber, building-wood' [n. o] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: tigillum 'small plank' (Pl.+)*

Pit. *teg/k-no- 'plank, timber'.

<!-- pdf-page: 634; printed-page: 620; running-header: 620                                        tilia -->

PIE *(s)teg-no- 'covering' or *te£-no- 'product5. Lat tignum probably represents *tegno-. The derived dim. tigillum could phonetically reflect *tegno-lo- if the raising of *e" in front of a velar nasal preceded vowel reduction in non-initial syllable (with the inverse chronology, one would expect *tegillum). As for the PIE etymology, WH and IEW derived tTgnum from a word *(s)teg- for 'stake', which is unattractive since this root is not otherwise attested in Italic. It seems more likely that ffgnum derives from *(s)teg- 'to cover\ which yields other constructional terminology such as tectum 'roof, ceiling, dwelling' and teguh 'roof-tile\ However, since tTgnum has no evident connotation of 'cover5, one could also connect it with *tek- 'to fashion", but this has the preform *teks- in all certain Latin derivatives (see s/v. texo).

Bibl.: · WH II: 681, EM 691, IEW 1014, Meiser 1998: 81, 122. → tego, texd



### tilia
tilia 'lime-tree5 [f. o\ (Verg.+)

IE cognates: Gr. πτελέα 'elm-tree', Myc. /pterewo/. The only close cognate seems to be the Greek word for 'elm-tree\ Both may be borrowings from a third language, or Latin has borrowed the Greek word as *telia and changed its meaning to 'lime-tree'.

Bibl.: WH II: 681f, EM 691, IEW 847.



### timeo
timeo 'to fear5 [v. II; pf. timuf\ (Naev.-l·)

Derivatives: timor [m.] 'fear' (Naev.+; timos Naev. apud Non.), timidus 'afraid, timid' (Naev.+), timiditas 'proneness to fear' (Pac.+), timefactus 'intimidated5 (Lucr.+), extimescere 'to be alarmed, dread* (Pl.+), pertimescere 'to become very scared' (PK+),praetimere 'to worry' (Pl.+). The connection with words for 'stiff (thus hesitatantly WH and IEW) is hardly possible in view of the modem reconstruction of this root as *stieH- (LIV); if indeed initial *s- were mobile, we would expect

PIE *tiH-m- > Lat. *tim-.

Bibl.: WH II:682,EM69If



### tingō, -ere
tingfi, -ere 'to wet, imbue, dye' [v. Ill; pf. tmxf, ppp. tinctum] (Lucr.+; var. tinguo Varro)

Derivatives: attingere 'to smear on' (RhetHer, +), inting(u)ere 'to plunge in, colour' (Pl.+).

Pit. *teng-e/o-.

PIE *teng- 'to make wet' [pr.], maybe *teng-s- [aor.].

IE cognates: Gr. τέγγω 'to make wet', aor. τέγξαι, OHG thunkon, dunkon *to immerse', Swiss German tink 'wet'. The variant tinguo is due to analogy with unguo 'to anoint5.

Bibl.: WH II: 684, EM 692, IEW 1067, Meiser 2003: 110, LIV L*ten(g}-.



### titio
titio 'piece of burning wood' [m. n] (Varro*-)

Pit. */F-/z- 'heating5.

PIE *tih r ti-.

IE cognates: Olr. tinaid, 'tin* *to melt, disappear' < PCI. */i-m-, Olr.

<!-- pdf-page: 635; printed-page: 621; running-header: tollo                                      621 -->

uathad [n.] 'small quantity', MW odit [m.] 'rarity' < *au-tJ-to- 'melting away'; Hit. ze-Q<ri> /z- 'to cook (intr.), be cooked' < *tiehro-, zanu-zi 'to cook (tr.)' < *tihrneu-, zinni-zi / zinn- 'to end, finish' < *ti-n(e)-hr; OE δϊηαη 'to wet; become wet\ OIc. pidr 'melted, thawed' < *tihrto-. Maybe derived from the PIE root *teih r 'to become hot, melt', according to LIV. In that case, it would be a /i-stem 'heating', Kloekhorst 2008: 1036f. argues that the original meaning of the root was 'to end, finish', on account of Hit. zinni-.

Bibl.: WH Π: 685f, EM 693, Schrijver 1995: 195, Schumacher 2004: 640f., LIV *teih r .



### tōfus
tOfus 'volcanic rock, tufa' [m. o\ (Verg.)

Pit. *tauf-o-?

It. cognates: maybe O. tavffud [abl.sg.] if 'tufa\ Probably a loanword from Sabellic, because of-f-

Bibl.: WH II: 687, EM 693, Untermann 2000: 736.



### toles, -ium * goitre'
toles, -ium * goitre' [f pL i] (Fest.)

Derivatives: tdnsillae [fpl.] 'tonsils' (Cic.+).

Pit. *ton-sli- 'goitre, tonsil'.

PIE *ton-sl-i-,

IE cognates: see s.v. teneo. Generally, idles is explained as the pi. of*tonsli- 'what is/can be stretched' (vel sim.), and tonsillae as its diminutive *tohsli-la-. Lat. * tonsli- can be regarded as /-derivative to *tens- 'to stretch', or as an instrument noun in *-slo/i- to */en- 'stretch'. It must be pointed out that the semantic connection to 'stretch' is possible, but hardly compelling; one might sooner expect a basic meaning 'to sweir to account for 'goitre' and 'tonsils'. Since we often find the meaning 'to expand' for derivatives of *ten-, not for those of *tens-, the analysis * tonsli- seems preferable. The o-grade of the noun remains unexplained, however.

Bibl.: WH II: 688, EM 693, IEW 1068f, Leumann 1977: 208, Meiser 1998: 119, LIV *ten-, → teneo



### tollo, -ere
tollo, -ere 'to pick up, raise, remove' [v. Ill; pf sustuli, ppp. sublatuni] (Lex XII+; pr.sb. tulat Ace.) -

Derivatives: tolerare 'to support, endure' (Andr.+), tolerabilis 'tolerant, bearable' (Ter.+), intolerandus 'unbearable' (Pl.+), pertolerare 'to endure to the end' (Acc.+); tolutim [adv.] 'at a trot' (Pl.+), toliitilis 'moving at a trot' (Varro); abstulas 'you take away' (Pl.), attollere 'to lift, erect' (Pl.+), contollere 'to step up' (Pl.+), extollere 'to raise, praise' (Pl.+), protollere 'to stretch forth, prolong' (Pl.+); opitulus 'who brings help' (Paul. exF.), opitularVe 'to give help' (Andr.+).

Pit. *tolna/o- [pr.], *tela-s- [pr.], *tel(-a)- [aor.], *tetol- [pf.], *tlato- [ppp.].

It. cognates: Ven. tolar, toler, tuler [3s.pr.med.] 'offers' < *tol-efar(V?)\ Fal. tulom [ls.pf./aor.] Ί offer' (thus Giacomelli); U. andendu, antentu, atentu [3s.ipv.II] 'to lay onto', endendu, ententu [3s.ipv.II], entelust, entelus [3s.fut.pf ] 'to lay into', pertentu [3s.ipv.II] 'maybe 'to spread out', sutentu [3s.ipv.II]'?' < pr. *te!ne/o-, pf *te/Ar-

<!-- pdf-page: 636; printed-page: 622; running-header: 622                                    tomentum -->

PIE *tl-n(e)-h₂- [pr.] 'to cany', *tel-h₂- [aor.], *te-tolh₂- [pf], *tlh₂-to- [ppp.].

IE cognates: Olr. tlenaid*, *tlen 'to lift, take away' < PCI. *tlina-m, Gr. aor. έτλην, Dor. έτλάν 'to endure, endeavour', aor. έτάλασσα 'endeavoured', τάλαντα [n.pl.] 'scales', Go, pulcrn 'to endure, bear', ToB tallam 'raises'. The PIE nasal present yielded Latin *toln- > toll-, whereas Umbrian introduced the e-grade of the root (from the aorist), Thematization may go back to Pit. The aorist root *tel(a)- seems to be preserved in U. pf. entel-, and is seen in Lat sb. -tulas, -tulat < *tel-a-. The pf. tetulT {to fero) may reflect the PIE ρβτίβοζ whereas latus < *tlato- continues the PIE /o-adj. The verb opitulari is regarded as denominal to opitulus, which is only attested in Paul. exR; it may reflect *opi-tolH-o-, To explain tolerare, Nussbaum 2007b proposes a Latin s<5-present built to the root *telh₂-, hence *telh₂-s- > *telasa- > tolera-. Finally, tolutim can be interpreted as 'lifting its feet' > 'at a trot', and would seem to require a stem *to/w-.

Bibl.: WH II: 688f., EM 694, IEW 1060f., Giacomelli 1963: 257, Lejeune 1974: 339, Schrijver 1991: 216, 407, 510, Untermann 2000: 742-744, Meiser 2003: 192, Schumacher 2004: 641 f., LIV *telh₂-. -+feroy telliis



### tomentum
tomentum 'flock, stuffing material' [n. o] (Varro+)

Pit. *towamnl

PIE *teuh₂-mn [n.] 'swelling'? *touh₂-eie- [prxaus.]?

IE cognates: see s.v. tueor. WH and IEW assume tomentum < *towementom to the root *teuhr 'to swell, increase' (a caus.pr. *toilH-eie- 'to stufF?). A preform *teuhrmn would yield

Pit. *towamn > *towamen > *tomen. A preform *towamentom would yield *Wmentumy but *towa/i- could have been restored on the model of still present verb forms (cf. Schrijver 1991: 279-82). Thus, tomentum can be explained on the basis of earlier *tomen, or on the basis of verb forms. Of course, no verb continuing *teuhr is actually attested in Latin, which weakens this explanation. EM hesitantly suggest a connection with tondeo 'to shear', which is possible, but would be less direct than from a verb meaning 'to stuff.

Bibl.: WH II: 689, EM 694, IEW 1080-85, Meiser 1998: 168. → tueory turned



### tondeo
tondeo 'to cut the hair, shear' [v. II; pf totondi, ppp. tonsum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tdnsilis 'made smooth by clipping' (Pl.+), tonsus, -lis 'style of haircut' (Pl.+), tonsor 'barber' (Pl.+), tdnstrix 'female barber' (Pl.+), tonstrlna 'barber's shop' (Pl.+), tonsHro 'shearing, clipping' (Varro+), tonsitare 'to shear' (Pl.+); ottondere 'to strip of, shear' (Pl.+), detondere 'to shear, prune' (Pl.+).

Pit. *tond-eje-.

PIE *tond-eie- 'to shear'.

IE cognates: Olr. teinnid* 'to break, cut' < *tend-e/o- or *tan-n-d-e/o-, pret. tethainn < *te~tond-, Gr. τένδω 'to gnaw at'. The pf. totondican be recent, or continue an old pf. *te-tond-. It cannot be proven that the root *tend- was derived from *tem- (as in *temhr 'to cut'), but the meaning suggests a connection.

Bibl.: WH II: 689f, EM 694f., IEW 1062f, Schumacher 2004: 614, LIV 2.*tend-.

<!-- pdf-page: 637; printed-page: 623; running-header: tonsa                                       623 -->



### tongSre
tongSre 'to know' [v. II] (Paul ex F. tongere, Enn. (apud Paul, ex R) tongent)

Derivatives: tongitio 'notio' (Fest: Praenestinian).

Pit. *tong-eje- 'to know', *tng-m- 'opinion'.

It. cognates: O. tanginom [acc.sg.], tangineis [gen.sg.], tanginud, tanginud, tanginud, τανγινοδ, τανγινοτ [abl.sg.] 'opinion, decision' < *tng-Tn/ion-,

PIE *tong-ei(e?)- 'to think, know'.

IE cognates: Go. pagkfan 'to think' < *tong-(e)ie-, Olc.pekkfa 'to understand, know', OHG denchen, OE dencan 'to think', Go. pugkjan 'to appear, think'. In view of the meaning which is stative rather than iterative, and in view of Gm. *punkri- 'to appear (to so.)', it seems possible that tongere continues a PIE /-present with o-grade of the root.

Bibl.: WH II: 690, EM 695, IEW 1088, Untermann 2000: 733f, LIV 2.*tenig)-,



### tonō, -āre
tonfi, -are 'to thunder' [v. I; pf. tomn, ppp. tonitum] (Pl.+; Ip.act tonimus Varro)

Derivatives: tonitrus, -Us [m.] 'thunder' (Pl.+), tonitr(u)alis Of thunder' (Lucr,+), tonescere 'to begin to thunder' (Varro); contonai -it thunders violently' (PI); Quomne tonas (Carmen Saliare).

Pit. *tena- [prVaor.], *tonaje- [pr.].

PIE *(s)tenh₂- [aor.] 'to thunder', *(s)tonh₂-eie/o- [pr.].

IE cognates: Skt pr. stanaya- (intr. in RV), tanya-, aor, stanihi [2s.ipv.act], stan [3s.inj.act.] 'to thunder', ta-ptc. stanita-, also [n.] 'thunder'; OEpunor 'thunder'. : Probably, tonere was the older present (or aorist, as Skt. suggests), which was replaced by tonare. The vowel ο was introduced from tonare into *tenere > tonere. The origin of tonare is disputed: an original causative *(s)tonhreie- (Eichner 1974: 58; but the meaning of tonare is not causative), a derivative from the noun tonus (Schrijver 1991: 396, as one of the possibilities; but tonus does not mean 'thunder', and is attested too recently), or an iterative *(s)tonhreie/o-, as Skt. stanaya- would suggest. The noun tonitrus has an unexpected suffix -tru-; Serbat (1975: 331) explains it from an original fa-stem *tona-tu- > *tonitu- which was affected by a ''popular" tendency to replace suffix-initial -/- by -/r-. One may think especially of influence by fiilgetrum 'lightning'. The PIE root *(s)tnh₂- Ho thunder' might be cognate with

PIE *sten- 'to sigh, groan'. Hit. tith-a 'to thunder' cannot be connected with this root in any meaningful way.

Bibl.: WH II: 690f„ EM 695, IEW 1021, Steinbauer 1989: 124, Schrijver 1991: 396, Rix 1999: 519, LIV *(s)tenh₂-.

tonsa Oar' [f a\ (Enn.+)

### topper
topper 'quickly' [adv.] (Andr.+)

Pit. */orf? */otf? Lat topper is generally regarded as a unfverbation of *tod (nom.acc.sg.n. of the stem */o- 'that') and the suffix -per. Whether the first element really was *tod remains uncertain: in view of the meanings initial tot 'that many' seems equally possible.

torpeO 'to be numb, paralysed' [v. Π] (Pl.+)

### torqueo
torqueo 'to twist, wind' [v. II; pf. torsi, ppp. tortum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: torques, -is [fj 'collar of twisted metal' (Pl.+), torculus 'of a wine- or olive-press' (Cato+), torculum 'press' (Varro-H), torcularium 'pressing-room, press' (Cato+); tortivus 'obtained from pressing' (CatoH-), tortare 'to torture, twist' (Pompon.+); tormina? -drum [n.pL] 'griping pains in the bowels' (Cato+), tormentum 'rope made of twisted hair, ballista, torture, pain' (Pl.+); contorquere 'to make twisted, turn about' (Enn.+), contortor 'a twister' (Ter.+), contortiplicatus 'compounded in an involved fashion' (Pl.), distorquere 'to distort torture' (Ter.+), extorquere 'to wrench away, sprain' (Pl.+), extortor 'one who extorts' (Ten), pertorquere 'to hurl, twist strongly' (Afran.-H), praetorquere 'to twist, wring' (Pl.+).

Pit. *torkʷ-eie- 'to twist', *for#v-fo- [ppp.]· It- cognates: maybe O. turumiiad [3s.pr.sb.] 'may he tremble/suffer' (vel sim.) if denom. to a noun *torkʷ-mo~ 'vexed' (but see also s,v. tremo) .

PIE *torkʷ-eie- 'to twist'.

IE cognates: Hit. tar(k)u-zi 'to dance' {*/tarkʷ-f) < *terkʷ-f*trk"-y Skt. tarkaya- 'to consider', ni$-tarkya- 'what can be unscrewed', tarku- 'spindle', Khot. hataljs- 'to flutter' (< PIr. *fra-tark-) < Ilr. *tark- 'to move to and fro, turn'; ToB tark- 'to turn'. A causative verb *torkʷ-eie-; the noun tormentum reflects *torkʷ-mn-to-.

Bibl.: WH II: 692f., EM 696, IEW 1077, Untermann 2000:778, LIV *terky-,



### torreo
torreo 'to scorch, roast' [v. II; pf torrvJ, ppp, torstum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: torrens 'burning, hot; flowing, torrential' (Lucr.+), torrescere 'to be scorched' (Lucr.), forms (later torris) [m.] 'piece of firewood' (Acc.+), torridus 'dried, parched' (Acc.+), retorridus 'dried up, shrivelled' (Varro+).

Pit. *tors-eje 'to scorch', *tors-e- 'to be dry', *tors-to- 'scorched'.

PIE *tors-eie- 'to make dry', *trs-eh,-, *trs-to-, IE cognates; Hit, tars- 'to become dry' or 'to make dry'? < *ters-, Skt. trsyant- [ptc.act.], aor. trsa- 'to be thirsty', root

<!-- pdf-page: 639; printed-page: 625; running-header: totus                                     625 -->

aor. tfsana- 'thirsty', caus. vi-tarsaya-,tysta-'sharp, stinging, raw', YAv. tarsu- 'dry, not fluid'; Gr. τέρσομαι 'to become dry'; OHG derren 'to make dry5, durst 'thirst', Go.paursjan* 'to be thirsty'. As M. Weiss points out to me, Lat torrens can be explained as a ptc. 'the dry one' to a stative pr. *trs-ehr 'to be dried out\ This obviates the need for positing a semantic shift from 'burning' to 'torrential' as assumed by LIV.

Bibl.: WH II: 694, EM 696, IEW 1078f., LIV *ters-. → terra



### torus
torus 'strand, thong, muscle' [m. o] (Cato+)

Derivatives: torulus 'thong, cord, muscle' (Pl.+). Has been derived from sterno (Leumann 1977: 276), but WH are hesitant. One would expect a verb like 'to turn' or 'knot'. In theory, torus might go back to *torh₂-o- to the root *terhr 'to cross, go through', which would be semantically acceptable; but this cannot be further ascertained.

Bibl.: WH II: 694f, EM 697.



### torvus
torvus 'grim, fierce' [adj. o/a\ (Pac.+)

Derivatives: torviter 'grimly' (Enn.+),

Pit. *torfo-.

PIE *torgʷ-o- 'grim, angry'·»IE cognates: Hit tarkiiuant- 'looking angrily' < *trgʷ-enl·, tarkuua [adv.] 'angrily/, Epic Skt tarjati 'to threaten' (EWAia III: 238), Gr. ταρβέω 'to be startled'. f ; WH connects torvus with trux, which seems formally difficult
