### totus
totus 'the whole of, all' [adj. o/a\ (Andr.+) The etymology *touH-eto~ 'stuffed' accepted by WH is a moot possibility; the assumed semantic shift from 'stuffed' to 'whole' is neither trivial nor convincing. Zimmer 1985 discusses all previous attempts to etymologize totus, and finds them unconvincing. His own solution *to-ie/ot^o~ 'sich fest dazu stellend, vollstandig' (to

PIE *iet-) is hardly more plausible: the preverb and the verbal root are otherwise unattested in Latin (see s.v. nitor for the alleged occurrence of this root), and the semantic shift to 'all' is not straightforward.

Bibl.: WH II: 695f., EM 697, IEW 1080ff., Zimmer 1985, Meiser 1998: 168, LIV *teuh₂- (?).

<!-- pdf-page: 640; printed-page: 626; running-header: 626                                      trabs -->



### trabs, -is
trabs, -is 'tree-trunk, beam' [f. b/i] (Pl.+; nom.sg. trabes Enn., Varro)

Derivatives: trabecula 'small wooden beam' (Cato+), trabica 'kind of boat' (Pac.H-), trabalis 'of/for wooden beams' (Enn.+).

Pit. *trab- 'wooden beam' (> Latin), *treb- 'to build, dwell' (> U.), *treb- 'house' (> Sab.).

It. cognates: O. triibum, τρεχβ[ωμ [acc.sg.], tribud [abl.sg.] [f] 'house' < *treb-\ O. tribarakavum, triibarakav[iim [inf.], tnbarakattms [3p.pf.sb.], tribarakat.tuset [3p.fut.pf.] 'to build' < *treb-ark-a- 'to be a house-builder'; 0. tribarakkiuf [nom.sg.] 'building' < *treb-ark-ion-s. U. trebeit [3s.pr.] 'lives, dwells' < *treb-t/e/je-. O. tribuf [nom.sg,] 'tribunus' < *treb-o/un-s. Maybe also U. tremnu [abl.sg.] 'place from where the augur observes the birds' (< ^treb-no-Ί).

PIE *trb- 'wooden beam', *treb- 'to build'?

IE cognates: Olr. treb, W. tref 'hamlet, village', Lith. troba 'cottage, farmhouse', Latv. traba 'Hut, hovel', Go. paurp 'field', OIc. porp, NHG Do^'village'. The nom.sg. trabes is an innovation with regard to trabs. Sabellic points to a stem *treb- 'house, building' but it is uncertain whether *treb- and Latin trab- originally belonged to one nominal paradigm, or whether they represent different derivatives of a root *trb-. Schrijver 1991 argues that a possible original ablaut nom.sg. *trebs, obi. *trb- > *torb- may have been replaced by *trebs vs. */rai>-, but there are no direct models for such a replacement; all of Schrijver's examples concern verb forms. Since Schrijver sets up a rule according to which *#DC developed into Lat. *RaDC, trabs might stem from a paradigm with *trb- in some case-forms. U. has a present in *treb- 'to dwell'; since the meanings of the Sab. forms are closer to this verb than to trabs, they may have been derived from the verb. We furthermore find a noun *tr(e)b- in Celtic, *trb- in Germanic and *trob- in Baltic. Maybe the full grade is also attested by Sabellic names such as Lat. Trebulanus, U. treblanir (the name of several minor settlements in Sabellic territory) 'village (vel sim.)\ In view of the difFiculties of reconstructing common preforms for more than one subbranch, of the presence of *-έ-, and of the European distribution of the root, *trb- may belong to the vocabulary borrowed from a non-IE language. O. tribuf seems to be built on Sab. *treb- 'house', either with the suffix of Lat. tribiinus, or with

PIE *-όη- (cf Adiego 2001).

Bibl.: WH II: 696f., EM 698, IEW 1090, Schrijver 1991: 376, 481f.f Meiser 1998: 99, Untermann 2000: 759-766. -^ taberna



### trahō, -ere
trahO, -ere 'to pull, drag, haul' [v. Ill; pf. traxl, ppp. traction] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: trahax 'grabbing' (Pl.); tragula 'spear, sledge, dragnet' (Pl.+); tractim [adv.] 'in a long-drawn-out manner' (Pl.+), tractum 'a thin sheet of pastry' (Cato+), tractus, -us * drawing, trail, track' (Lucr.+); detrahere 'to pull off, remove' (Pl.+), distrahere 'to pull apart' (Pl.+), intertrahere 'to draw away' (Pl.); tractare 'to drag along, handle' (Naev.+), attrectare 'to touch, handle' (Pl.+), attrectatus, -us 'the handling' (Pac.+), pertra/ectare 'to pass the hand over, handle' (Pl.+), pertractate 'in a hackneyed manner' (Pl.); trama 'warp' (Pl.+).

Pit. *traxe/o-.

IE cognates: Olr. tethraig? [pret.] 'ran away, receded' < PCL *te-trag-, Olr. traig 'ebb, beach', MW trei, OBret. tre (< *tragio-)9 MW treul 'trouble, weakness' (<

<!-- pdf-page: 641; printed-page: 627; running-header: trans                                     627 -->

*traglo-). The form trahax appears to be a nonce-formation of Plautus (thus EM). The g in tragula must have been retained in *tragʰla; it can be compared with MW treul if from *traglo-* Long -a- in tragula might be analogical after coagulum, repagula. Tractare was derived from the ppp. tractus. Trama can reflect *trax(s)ma-, Semantically, the closest cognates are found in Celtic: Olr. tethraig and the words for 'ebb' can all be explained from a meaning 'to pull away' (cf Schumacher 2004: 636). Formally, Go. pragjan 'to walk' (and cognates) and Olr. traig, W. troed, Bret. troad 'foot' could be cognate; semantically, they require some special pleading (but a connection is not impossible). This would point to a root *trHgʰ- or *tr5g\ with a non-IE root structure *T-Dh and maybe root-internal *-a-. Thus, this could be an Italo-Celtic (and Germanic?) loanword, A connection with Germanic *dragan 'to drag, draw' (Go. -dragan 'to carry4, OIc. draga 'to pull, ride') is formally impossible, unless this is another reflex of a European loanword. The connection with In trog 'offspring', SCr, trag 'family, race' is dismissed by Schrijver 1991: 349 for semantic reasons.

Bibl.: WH II: 697-699, EM 698f, IEW 257, Schrijver 1991: 188-191, Sihler 1995: 148, Meiser 2003: 116, Schumacher 2004: 635f., LIV *dʰreg°h-.



### tranquillus
tranquillus 'calm, still' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tranquillitas 'calmness, quiet condition' (Pl.+), tranquillare 'to make calm' (Pl.+). The etymology as * trans 'across' + *kʷilno- 'quiet' (root *k w ih r 'be quiet') is semantically vague (the meaning of tram does not fit); phonetically, the assumed development of *-rafcw- > -nqu- is disputed. Also, the suffix is unclear.

Bibl.: WH II: 700, EM 699, IEW 638, Nyman 1982, Sihler 1995: 547.



### trans
trans 'across, through' [prep. +acc., adv.] (Pl.+; tra- in front ofd-J-, /-, m-, n-, v-)

Pit. *tran(t)s.

It. cognates: U. trahaf, tra [prep. + loc], traha, trqf, tra [prep. + ace] 'on the other side of; U. trah- prefix.

PIE *trh₂-nt-s ^'crossing' [nom.sg.m7f.].

IE cognates: MW tardu, MCo. tardha, OBret. tardom 'to spring up, burst out' < PCL *tarie/o~ < *trh₂-ie-; Hit. tarhu-2* 'to prevail, be able* < *terh₂-u-ti9 ^trhr-u-enti, dTarhunna-9 CLuw. 6Tarhuuant- / Tarhunt-, HLuw. Tarhunt- / Tarhunza-, Lye. Trqqnt- 'the Storm-God' < *trh₂-uent-s, *trh₂-unt-os; Skt. pr tara-, -tira 'to pass, overcome', taranta- [m.] PN, turva- 'to overcome1, YAv. titara-, taurwaiia- 'id.', Gr. τρανής *'pervasive' → 'clear, distinct'. Since U. has Itraf /, the final cluster must have been

Pit. *-m, which cannot be directly derived from

PIE *-nt (pace Schrijver). Thus, we must assume a m./f. form of a pr.ptc.act. *trh₂nts 'crossing' >

Pit. *tran(t)s 'across'. Dunkel 2000b: 24 posits *trh₂m-skʷe, an instrumental in his view, but in view of the meaning I would rather expect an ace, - which agrees with the ending *-/w.

Bibl.: WH II: 700, EM 699f., IEW 1075f., Steinbauer 1989: 236f., Schrijver 1991: 224, Untermann 2000: 757f, LIV *terh₂-. → intro

<!-- pdf-page: 642; printed-page: 628; running-header: 628                                      tremo -->



### tremd, -ere
tremd, -ere 'to tremble' [v. HI; pf. tremuf] (Pl.+; tremonti [3p.pr.?] Carmen Saliare)

Derivatives: tremor 'trembling, terror' (Pl.+), tremulus 'trembling, shaking' (Pl.+), tremebundus 'id.' (Lucn+), tremescere 'tot tremble' (Lucr.+); contremere 'to tremble violently' (Pac.+), contremulus 'shimmering' (Varro), contremescere 'to shake violently' (Enn.+).

Pit. *trem-e/o- [pr.], *trom-eje- [prxaus.]-

It. cognates: U. tremitu [3s.ipv.ll] maybe 'to scare' < *trem-eie-tdd, with -e- from the present.; O. turumiiad [3s,pr.sb.] 'may he tremble/suffer' (vel sim.) (but see also s.v. torqueo) .

PIE *tr(e)m- [pr.] 'to tremble'.

IE cognates: Gn τρέμω 'to tremble', Lith. tremti 'to shake up', trimti 'to tremble', ToA tramas [3s.], tarminc [3p.] 'to tremble'.

Bibl.: WH II: 701, EM 700, IEW 1092f, Untermann 2000: 761, Meiser 2003: 124f., LIV *trem-, → trepidus



### trepidus
trepidus 'fearful, anxious' [adj. o/a] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: trepidare 'to act in a state of alarm, be anxious, hurry' (Pl.+), trepidulus 'trembling with apprehension' (Enn.+); attrepidare 'to bestir oneself (Pl.+); trepit 'turns' (Paul, ex F.\

Pit. *trep-.

PIE *trep- 'to tremble'.

IE cognates: OCS trepetb 'trembling, fear', trepetati 'to tremble'. The appurtenance of trepit here is uncertain: it might be a nonce-formation based on Gr. τρέπει 'turns', or it might go back to

PIE *trep- 'to turn', but have nothing to do with trepidus. In fact, neither the root *trep- 'to tread, crush' nor *trep- 'to turn', which are acknowledged for PIE by LIV, are obvious candidates for the etymology of trepidus. In view of its meaning, Latin-Slavic *trep- 'tremble' may rather be compared with

PIE *trem- (see s.v. tremd) and *tres- 'to tremble'.

Bibl.: WH II: 701f., EM 700f., IEW 1094, Nussbaum 1999a: 380, LIV L*trep-, → tremo



### tres
tres 'three' [num. adj. m.f. tres, n. tria] (VOLat. (Garigliano Bowl trifos [abl.pl.m.]), Lex XII+; acc.mf. tris, gen. trium, databl. tribus; tri~, trj- 'three' in compounds)

Derivatives: tertius 'third' (Lex XII, Andr.+), tertiarius [adj.] 'one-third' (Cato+), tertiare 'to plough/attempt for the third time' (Cato+); ter 'thrice' (Pl.+); term 'three at a time' (Pl.+), trim 'three at a time, three' (Cato+), triginta 'thirty' (Pl.+), trecenti [pL] 'three hundred' (Pl.+), treceni 'three hundred each' (Varro+), triens, -ntis [m.] 'a third part' (Varro+), trientabulum 'property in the value of a third of a debt' (Lex Agraria+); terruncius 'coin weighing three unciae' (Pl.+). Pit nom.nLf. *trejes7 acc.m.f. *trins7 nom.acc.n. *tria, dat.abl.pL *trifos 'three', *tritio- 'third', *tris 'thrice',

It. cognates: maybe the Ven. theonym tribusiiati- contains *tri- 'three'; O. tris [nom.pLf.], tris [accpl.f.], trium [gen.pL], U. trif, tref, tre, trif, treif [acc.pl.m.], tref, tre, trif [BccplS.], triia [accpl.n.], triiuper, trioper [ac.pl.n. + ψβτ(ί)] , tris [abl.pl.] 'three'; U. terti, tertim [acc.sg.m.n.?], tertiama [acc.sgX + -ad] , tertiame [acc.sg.f +- -en] , tertiu, tertiu, tertio [abl.sg.m.n.?], tertie [loc.sg.m.n.] 'third' < *tri-tio-; U. tripler [abl.pl.m.] 'three at a time' < *tri→plo-. U.

<!-- pdf-page: 643; printed-page: 629; running-header: tncae                                        629 -->

tribfifu [nom.sg J, tribrmne [abl.sg.] f. maybe 'three, trio' < *tri- + ?

PIE nom.m.f. *treies, acc.m.f. *trins, nom.acc.n. *trih₂, gen. *triom 'three', *trio- ( » *trito- » *tritio-) [adj.] 'third', *tris 'thrice', *tri- (in compounds), *trih₂d£:omth₂ 'thirty*.

IE cognates: Olr. tri, tri [m.], tre [n.], teoir [f.], W. Bret, tri [m.], te/r [f.] 'three*; Skt. fray<3s [nom.pl.m.], tisros [f. ], tri [nom.acc.pl.n.] 'three', trirnsat- [f.] 'thirty*, tguya- 'third', YAv. ϋτωιδ, uraiias-ca [nom.pl.m.], tisro [nom.pl.f.], ΰη [nom.acc.pl.n.] 'three', urisant- [f.] 'thirty', dritiia- 'third', urisuua- [n.] 'one third'; Skt. tris, YAv. uris 'thrice'; Gr. τρεις, Cret. τρέες [nom.pl.m.f.], Gr. *τρινς (OAtt. τρις) [acc.pl.], τρισί [dat.pl.], τρία [nom.acc.pLn.]; Lith. trys, OCS trbje [m.], tri [f.]; OIc. prir [nom.pl.m.], Go. prins [acc.pl.m.f.]; ToB trey, trai [m.], ToB tarya [f.], ToA tre [m.], tri [f.] 'three'. According to Coleman, it is unclear whether terra or the rare trim regularly reflect *trisno-. But maybe they go back to different preforms: term< *tri-no- (as expected from PIE) and trim< *tri$no- (a more recent formation, as bmi).

Bibl.: WH II: 702f„ EM 701, IEW 1090-92, Lejeune 1974: 329f„ Coleman 1992: 393, 420, Beekes 1995: 212-217, Meiser 1998: 171, Vine 1998b: 261, Untermann 2000: 749,763£, 767L → tribus



### tribus
tribus 'division of the people, tribe' [f. u] (Varro+)

Derivatives: tribuere 'to divide, bestow' (Acc.+), tribunus 'magistrate, commander' (Pl.+), tributus 'levy, tax' (Pl.+), tribiilis 'fellow tribesman' (Ter.+), tributim 'by tribes' (Varro+); attribuere 'to'assign, appoint' (Varro+), retribuere 'to hand back duly* (Lucr.+). /

Pit. *tripu- 'tripartite entity, trinity'.

It. cognates: U. trifu, trifo [acc.sg.], trifor [gen.sg.], trifo [dat.sg.], trefiper [abl.sg. + -per] 'trinity, district'.

PIE *tri-dʰhru- 'tripartite division'. Weiss (2007a: 369-376) bases his account of the etymology of tribus on a PIE or Pit. adjectival compound *tri-dthro- 'rendered in three, tripartite', which could be compared e.g. with Skt. multiplicatives in -dha < *-d h eh r such as tridha 'threefold', and with thematized derivatives of it such as Lith. indas 'plate' < *-d h h r o-. The adj. *tricfhiO- would have been substantivized by conversion to a w-stem *tri-(thrU- c tripartite thing', 'a third*, yielding

Pit. *tripu- (the w-stem points to a rather old formation; cf. Lith. vidus 'middle'). Since we have no other certain evidence for the development of Pit. intervocalic *p in front of vocalic *w, we may as well assume that it is identical to the development after *w, where it yields Latin -£>-. Hence,

Pit. *tripu- would regularly yield U. trifu- and Latin *tribu-* Unlike in tertius and ter, *tri- was not reduced to ter- because it was supported by other compounds in *tri-.

Bibl.: WH II: 703f., EM 702, IEW 1090-92, Meiser 1998: 194, Untermann 2000: 764f., Weiss 2007a. → -fariam9 tres



### tricae
tricae 'complications, tricks; trifles' [f.pl. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tried 'twister' (Lucil.+), tricosus 'given to trickery* (Lucil.+); extricare 'to set free, solve* (Pl.+), intrrcare 'to entangle, embarass' (Pl.+). Uncertain etymology. One might compare Toch. *treiK- in ToB aor. traiksa 'missed,

<!-- pdf-page: 644; printed-page: 630; running-header: 630                                    triquetrus -->


did not succeed', sb. trisaip 'must miss', but Tocharian allows for several different phonological reconstructions- If the meaning 'trifles' was the oldest, then one might think of an original meaning 'crumby seeds' (cf nugae 'nuts' > 'trifles'), and compare the derivatives of tero 'to rub' in /ri-, esp. triticwn 'wheat' and intertrigo 'sore place from rubbing'. A ^-extension to the pr. *trhri- 'to drill' (see s.v. tero) would yield *tri-k-.

Bibl.: WH II: 704f, EM 702, IEW 1071-74.



### triquetrus
triquetrus 'triangular' [adj. o/d] (Lucr.+) Maybe from *trj-quadro- with the noun *quadro- 'rectangle'. The date of attestation and the use of *quadro- in a novel meaning 'angle' would suggest a recent date of this compound, but the reduction of word-internal *σ to e in closed syllable, and the change of *-dr- to -/r-, go back to the VOLat. period. However, quadro- itself is from *tivtur-, so its -dr seems relatively recent. Alternatively, triquetrus might contain (a form of) quater 'four times'. An alternative etymology derives -quetrus from the same root as Germ. *xwat- 'to whet, sharp' (thus WH, IEW), a root without further IE connections. While this is phonologically possible, the semantics are not straightforward.

Bibl.: WH II: 706, EM 703, IEW 636, Leumann 1977: 198. ^ quattuor



### tristis
tristis 'depressed, unhappy5 [adj. i] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: trTstitos 'gloom' (Pac.-f), tristitia 'unhappiness, gloom' (Ter,+).

IE cognates: OHG drlstU OS thnsti, OE driste 'audacious'? Hill 2003: 128 considers the possibility that tristis goes back to *trenksti- and is cognate with the Gm. words such as OE thriste. But in view of temo < *tenksmon-, the raising of *e > i post-dates the cluster simplification, so that *trenkstl· could at most yield *tresti~. No good other etymology.

Bibl.: WH II: 706f, EM 703, IEW 1092.



### trudu, -ere
trudu, -ere 'to thrust, push' [v. Ill; trust; trusum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: (mola) trusatiUs [adj.] 'pushing-mill' (Cato+); contrudere 'to thrust, cram' (Lucr.+), detrudere 'to push away, compel' (Pl.+), obtrudere 'to thrust, push down the throat' (Pl.+), retrudere 'to thrust back' (Pl.+).

Pit. *treude/o-.

PIE *treud-(e/o-) 'to push, thrust'.

IE cognates: W. cythrudd 'to vex', Litli. triusas 'work, effort, pains', OCS trudh 'labour, work', SCr. triid 'effort, labour, work' < *troudro~\ Go. us-priutan 'to maltreat', Olc.prjota 'to lack', OHG -driozan 'to cause sorrow'; Alb. tredh 'castrates' (uncertain). The Latin verb can be compared to Gm. *preutan, suggesting a thematic verb with e-grade.

Bibl.: WH II: 710, EM 704, IEW 1095f, LIV *treud-.



### truncus
truncus 'mutilated, maimed, trimmed' [adj. o/a] (Varro+)

Derivatives: truncus 'trunk, torso' (Lucil.+); contruncare 'to hack to pieces' (Pl.+),

<!-- pdf-page: 645; printed-page: 631; running-header: tn                                        631 -->

distruncare 'to chop in half (Pl.+), obtruncare 'to cut to pieces, kill' (Pl.+). In spite of the fact that the adj. 'maimed, trimmed' is attested later than truncus 'trunk, torso' (which can be interpreted as 'maimed or beheaded tree/body') and the compound verbs, it seems that the most original form was the adj. *tru/onko- 'maimed, robbed of its branches or head'. This may or may not be another adj. of 'physical impediment' in *-fa>-. I see no compelling reason to connect *tru/onko- with Go. preikan 'to push' or Lith. trinkti 'to thrust' (see LIV s.v. *trenk* 'to push'). The meaning of truncus is clearly not 'compressed' but 'maimed, missing some of its original parts*. Similarly, the etymology as *druni/o-ko- 'pertaining to a tree' by Hamp 1978: 186 is unsupported by the actual meaning of the word.

Bibl.: WH II: 7101, EM 704f, IEW 1093.



### trux, -cis
trux, -cis 'harsh, savage' [adj. k] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: truculentus 'ferocious' (Pl.+), trucidare 'to slaughter, butcher' (Lucr,+), trucidatid 'slaughter' (Cato+).

IE cognates: MW drwg, MCo. drok, Mir. droch- 'bad' < PCI *dntko-. If we follow Hamp 1978 in positing

PIE *dr- > Latin tr- also in initial syllable, trux can be connected with PCI. *druL·- 'bad'. The verb trucidare has been variously explained as a haplologized form of *truki-ladare, a denominal verb to a compound *truki-kaido- *hacking savagely' (to caedd); or from *k"tru-kaidare 'hacking in four pieces', with the reduced form of 'four'. Yet a form *k"tru- 'four' is unattested elswhere in Latin (in itself not really a counterargument), and the meaning of underlying *kʷtru-kaid-o- would be a '(having) four hackings'; why 'to slaughter' was expressed by means of the numeral 'four' would remain unexplained. Hence, I derive trucidare from *truk(o)-kaidro~.

Bibl.: WH II: 709, 711, EM 704f., IEW 1102, Schrijver 1991: 496. → caedd



### to
to 'you' [pron. pers, 2s. nom. tu^ tute, acc.abL te(d), gen. tui, tis (Pl.) dat. tibei, tibi\ (VOLat.+)

Derivatives: tuus 'your' (Andr.+).

Pit. *tii [nom.], *te [ace], *toi [gen.], *tefei [dat.], *ted [abl.] 'you', *towo- [adj,] 'you, your'.

It. cognates: O. tiium, tiu [nom.], U. tiom, tiu, tio, teio, SPic. tiom [ace], U. tefe, tefe, O. tif[ei] , tfei, SPic. tefei, tefeh [dat.] 'you' < nom. *tu-om, ace. *te-om, dat. *tebʰei; O. tuvai [dat.sg.f.], (X touery tuer [gen.sgji.], tuua, tua [abl.sg.f] 'your'.

PIE *ti(H) [nom.], *tue [ace], *toi [gen.dat.], *teue [gen.], *tued [abl.] 'you', *tu-o- 'your\

IE cognates: Hit. zik [nom.], tu- [obi.], CLuw. ti, tu- < *tiH·, *ft/-; Skt. t^varn [nom.], t(u)vam [ace], tubkya(m) [dat.], t(u)vat [abl], tava [gen.], tva, tuva [accencl.], te [gen.abl.dat.encl.], OAv. tuuam [nom.], ΰβςιη [ace], taibiid, taibiiaβ [dat.] , tauua [gen.], ϋβα- [adj.poss.]; OP tuvam [nom.], uuvam [ace] < Ilr. *tiiH-am [nom.], *tuaH-am [ace], *taua [gen.]; Gr. τϋ (Dor), τυνη (Dor, Horn), σε, τρέ [ace], τεός, σός [adj.]; Lith. ώ, OCS ty [nom.]; Go.pu; ToB twe, ToA tu 'you'. As argued by Kloekhorst 2008: 111-115, the vowel i in the nom.sg. forms of Anatolian is difficult to explain secondarily. Hence, the PIE nom.sg. may have been

<!-- pdf-page: 646; printed-page: 632; running-header: 632                                       tuba -->

*ti(H), which was replaced by *tu(H) after the Anatolian branch split off PIE.

Bibl.: WH II: 712, EM 705, IEW 1097f., Leumann 1977: 46M63, Beekes 1995: 207-209, Meiser 1998: 156-159, Untermann 2000: 751-753, 78If.



### tuba
tuba 'trumpet' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tubus 'pipe, tube, trumpet' (Varro+), tubulus 'small pipe' (Varro+); tubicen 'trumpeter' (Cato+X Meaning and form recall tibia 'reed-pipe; shin-bone', which, if Gr. σίφων 'tube, siphon' is cognate, might reflect *fwi-. These forms point to a non-IE loanword; see s.v. tibia,

Bibl.: WH II: 712, EM 705.



### tuber, -eris
tuber, -eris 'swelling, tumour' [n. r] (Ter.+)

Derivatives: tuberosus 'covered with lumps or tumours' (Varro+); tuburcinan 'to eat greedily, gobble' (Pl.+). Might be related to other words of the structure TuP9 especially Gr. τύφη 'a plant used for filling pillows and beds' (probably with ΰ), OIc. pufa 'knoll, hillock'. Yet the Greek word has a very different meaning, whereas Gm. has a different suffix. These forms do not make an IE impression, and there is no good etymology. Tuber might reflect the root *teuH- 'to swell', but the extension -ber is difficult to explain. The verb tuburcinan is of uncertain appurtenance: in the very uncertain case that it meant 'to be(come) a fat, stuffed person (by eating greedily)', it could be built on an earlier verb *tuburka- to a noun *tubVrko- 'fat person' (thus hesitantly Steinbauer 1989: 254). This is of course very speculative.

Bibl.: WH II: 712f., EM 705, IEW 108O85.



### tueor
tueor 'to look at, watch over' [v. II; ppp. tuitum, tuturri] (Pl.+; var. tuor, tui Enn., Lucr.)

Derivatives: tutor 'guardian' (Pl.+), tutare/ί fito watch over, protect' (Naev.+X Tutanus 'deity giving protection' (Varro+), TutilTna 'goddess giving protection5 (Varro+), tutela 'guardianship, protection' (Pl.+); contuert [also contut PL to Lucr.] 'to look at, see' (Pl.+), contuitus, -us 'gaze' (Pl.+), indotuetur '?' (Enn. Ann. 70), intuerJ [also intui] 'to look at, consider' (Pl.+), obtu(er)x 'to look at' (Pl.+).

Pit. *-/ow-e- 'to protect'.

PIE *touh₂-eie- [pr.caus.]? *te-touh₂- [pf.]?

IE cognates: Skt. pr. ut taviti, pf- tutava 'to be strong', tavas- 'strong, powerful', OAv. tauua [ls.pr.sb.act.], YAv, fra-tuiia [2s.pnopt.act.] 'to be able', haiant- 'capable, competent'; tutatma [pf.] 'is possible'; Gr. ταϋς 'grzat, much' (Hsch.), σώς, σάος 'safe and sound' (< *tueh₂us, *tuhru-os)\ Lith. titkti 'to become fat (of cattle)', RuCS tyti 'to become fat'. Tutus is the old ppp. belonging to tuor (whence tutore, tutor). Tuor and tueor might reflect earlier *tu(w)V-y or *tewV-, *towV- in non-initial syllable. In view of the old derivatives, the original meaning was probably 'to watch over, protect', whence 'to look at1 in the compound verbs (see Gamier 2004: 282f). This renders a derivation from the root *tuh r 'to be strong' attractive: *teuH-ti Ms strong' > 'keeps strong,

<!-- pdf-page: 647; printed-page: 633; running-header: tundo                                      633 -->


protects' (thus Gamier 2004: 284, in which case tuor is older), pr.caus. *touH-eie- 'keeps strong, protects' (> *tawe-\ or a pf. *(te-)touh₂-e 'is strong' > 'protects', cf. the Ilr. pf of this root. The pr. tueor could reflect the introduction of the pr. or pf vocalism *tow- into the caus, *tawe-,

Bibl.: WH II: 713f, EM 706, IEW 1079f, Leumann 1977: 544, LIV ?*teuH-. → tomentum



### turn, tunc *at that moment, then'
turn, tunc *at that moment, then' [adv,]: turn (Andr.+), tunc (Naev.+) Fit *tom.

PIE *tom [acc.sg.m.]. Tunc < *tom-ke was originally more emphatic than turn < *tom,

Bibl.: WH II: 715, EM 675, IEW 1086f, Sihler 1995: 389. ^ so-, tarn, topper



### turned
turned 'to swell, be swollen' [v. II; pf tumui\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tumor 'tumour, swollen condition, passion* (Cato+), tumidus 'swollen, inflated5 (Lucr.+), tumulus 'knoll, burial-mound5 (Pac,+); extumere 'to swell up' (Pl.+), extumidus 'convex' (Varro+); tumultus, -us 'commotion, uproar' (Naev.+)> tumultudsus 'disturbed and noisy5 (Ph+), tumultuarVXo make uproar' (Pt+).

Pit. *tum-e-.

PIE *tum-ehr 'to be swelling'.

IE cognates: MW tyfu, MCo. /evi, Bret tenvin 'to grow' < PCI. *tum-i- < *tum;ehr, Skt. tumra- Strong, thick', tutuma- 'strong, effective', YAv. tumaspana- [PN]; Lith. tumeti 'to become thick', OIc. Jiumal-fingr 'thumb'. The noun tumultus is explained by Leumann 1977: 354 from *tumulitus to a verb *tumulare, which seems unwarranted. WH II: 716 suggest imitation of singultus 'sighing', but a clear-cut proportion cannot be set up, since there is no verb *singeo\ Nevertheless, the meaning of tumultus does suggest that it belongs here. Turned goes back to a root *tum-, which has a closely similar meaning to the root *tuH- 'to swell*. The short vowel in turn- is explained by some from an o-grade form *touH-m- with de Saussure's effect, but since we find turn- also in Ilr., Baltic and Celtic, this would require a PIE generalization of a novel laryngealless ablaut variant * t o - 3 which seems a lot to ask for. Alternatively, one might think that *tu-h₂- and */w-m- are two different suffixations of one and the same root */w- (cf. *gl"/w- and *gʷʰr 'to come').

Bibl.: WH II: 715f, EM 707, IEW 1080-85, Schrijver 1991: 340, 530, Schumacher 2004: 646, LIV *tuem-, → contumax, contumelia



### tundo, -ere
tundo, -ere 'to strike, beat1 [v. Ill; pf. tutudi, ppp. tunsum (tusum)] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: tudes, -itis [m.] 'hammer, mallet' (Paul, ex F.), tuditare 'to strike repeatedly with a hammer' (Enn.+), tudiculare 'to crush with a small hammer1 (Varro); contundere 'to crush, bruise' (Pl.+), detudes [acc.pl.] 'a piece struck off (Paul ex F.)y extundere 'to extort, wring' (Pl.+), obtundere 'to beat, assail, make blunt' (Pl.+), pertundere 'to bore a hole through' (Pl.+), retundere 'to beat flat, repress' (Pl.+).

Pit. *(s)tund-(eh-) 'to thrust'.

It. cognates: maybe U. tuder [acc.sg.], tuderor

<!-- pdf-page: 648; printed-page: 634; running-header: 634                                       turba -->


[nom.pl.], tudero [acc.pl.], tuderus [abl.pl.] [n.] border' < *tud-es-, PEE *(s)tu-n-d- [pr.] 'to thrust5.

IE cognates: Olr. do-tuit, arch, dvtuitt 'to crumble, fall' < PCI. *-tudre/o^ MW kystiid ifate'; Skt. pr. tudati; pr. tundate, tundana- [ptc.med.], pf. (-)tutoda 'to thrust, crush', tunna- 'crushed, ground', Sogd. twnt /tund/ Violent', MP fund 'sharp, violent'; Arm. Vndam Ί am shattered', Go. stautan 'to thrust5 (?). With Schumacher 2004: 645, we can interpret U. tuder as the place where two areas 'hit' each other, that is, border on each other.

Bibl.: WH II: 716f., EM 707, IEW 1032-34, Untermann 2000: 77If., Meiser 2003: 192f., Schumacher 2004: 642-645, LIV l.*(s)teud-, → studeo



### turba
turba 'commotion, upheaval5 [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: (1) turbulentus 'disturbed, stormy* (Pl.+), turbelae [fpl.] 'petty disturbance' (Pl.+), turbare 'to upset, agitate, revolt5 (Pl.+), turbo, -inis 'spinning-top, eddy, whirlwind5 (Pl.+), turbidus 'wild, disordered, murky' (Pl.+); conturbare cto confound, dismay' (Pl.+), disturbare 'to demolish, upset5 (Pl.+), obturbare 'to interrupt, dazzle5 (Pl.+), perturbare 'to upset, disrupt' (Pl.+); (2) turma 'small squadron, company5 (Cato+), turmatim 'in troops' (Lucr.+). (1) The connection with Skt. tvarate *to hurry' and

PIE *ft/er-, which IEW and Meiser adhere to, seems very uncertain. Not so much semantically, but formally: the suffix (*ό? *όΛ?) would have been added to the bare root, of which no other forms occur in Italic. There is also no known PIE root *(s)terbh- with the appropriate meaning. One might think of Gr. στρέφω cto turn': a zero-grade form *(s)tfbh-hr 'turmoil5 >

Pit. *trf-a- > pre-Latin *torba- > *torba, with a dialectal variant turba (normally, -orb- remains, cf orbis, morbus, with ρ torpeo). Yet there are no other known cognates of Gr. στρέφω, and - as indicated - Latin turba would be deviant for *torba. Turba seems most similar to Gr. σύρβη, Att τύρβη cnoise, commotion', σύρβά, Att. τύρβα [adv.] 'confused' (< PGr. */rfi/ro~? *tuorb-?)9 which are probably loanwords. In that case, Latin would have borrowed the word from a Greek dialect, or both Greek and Latin borrowed it from a third source. In view of the quite well-developed word family already in Plautus, which suggests that turba had been in the language for some time, the latter option seems preferable. (2) The noun turma may or may not be related to turba. If related, it might point to both words being loanwords, with b and m as different renderings of a labial consonant in the donor language, Turma is compared by IEW to OIc. pruma, prymr 'noise, racket', OE drymm 'troop, crowd', OS hem-thrum 'sword-violence', which does not explain the different order of r and w.

Bibl.: WH II: 718f., EM 707f., IEW 1100f., Biville 1990II: 271, Meiser 1998: 63.



### turdus
turdus 'thrush (or similar bird)' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: turdarium 'enclosure for thrushes' (Varro), turdetice 'magpie (?)' (Varro).

Pit. *torzdo~.

PIE *(s)tr(o)sdho- 'thrush'.

IE cognates: Olr. truit, -d 'thrush' [f.] < *trozdi-y W.

<!-- pdf-page: 649; printed-page: 635; running-header: tussis                                    635 -->

tresglen; Aim. tordik, OPr. tresde 'thrush', Lith, strazdas, Latv. strazds 'thrush, blackbird, (dial) starling', Ru. drozd*, gen.sg. drozda 4 thrush', cerryj drozd blackbird' < BSl. *(s)trozdo- <

PIE *(s)trozdho-; OIc. prgstr 'thrush' [m.] (< *prastu- < *trosd(h)-)9 OHG drosela, OS throsla, MHG drostel, OE prostle (< *prustlo-\ OE drysce, MoE thrush (< *pruskjdn\ Since *or and *r merge in pre-Latin, turdus could reflect *torzda- or *trzd-\ but since Celtic, BSl. and Gm. have *-ro-, chances are that turdus continues *tjzdo-. The hypothesis by Bracchi (1999: 87) that *t(o)rzdo- would reflect a compound *trs-sdr 'sitting (nestling) on the dried (mud)5 has little to recommend itselfi If thrushes make their nests from mud and twigs, it seems to me that their nests are less prone to be called 'dry' than the nests of birds who weave only twigs, without mud.

Bibl.: WH II: 718, EM 708, IEW 1096, Hamp 1978: 188, 1981b: 81, Meiser 1998: 63, Bracchi 1999. → sturnus



### turgeō 'to swell'
turgefl ςίο swell' [v. II; pf. tursT\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: turgescere 'to begin to swell1 (Pl.+), turgidus 'swollen' (Pl.+), obturgescere 'to begin to swell' (Lucil.+). The meaning renders a connection with -turare 'to block, impede' possible. A derived verb from an adj. *tur-ago- 'swelling' (as given by WH as one possible etymology) seems unlikely, since such adj; always yield verbs in -igare. No clear etymology.

Bibl.: WH II: 718fi, EM 708, IEW 1080-85. → obturo 4



### turpis
turpis 'disgusting, foul' [adj. i] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: turpiculus 'somewhat indecent' (Varro+), turpitudd 'ugliness, indecency' (Pl.+), turpare 'to pollute, disgrace' (Enn.+). WH and IEW compare Epic Skt. trapate 'to be ashamed1 from

PIE *trep- 'to turn\ and interpret turpis as 'repelling1. This is too constructed a meaning to be credible: one would expect a different suffix to express the deontic meaning here implied *T(o)rp-i- could mean 'turning', which is not 'turning away', which is not 'making so. turn away from sth.', which is not necessarily 'disgusting'* One would sooner connect torpeo 'to be numb1 or 'stiff, but this too does not yield a smooth explanation for the semantics of turpis.

Bibl.: WH II: 719, EM 708, IEW 1094.



### tussis
tussis 'cough' [f.;] (Ter.+)

Derivatives: tussirefitosuffer from a cough' (Pl.+). Semantically, the connection with tundd 'to beat' is conceivable, but since the ppp. of tundo is tusum < *tudrto-, with vowel lengthening according to Lachmann's Law, we would expect *tusis from an old formation *tud-ti-. Tussis might be explained from onomatopoeic consonant gemination in an earlier form *tusis. Alternatively, it was not derived from tundo at all but represents a pure onomatopoeia. Note that the PIE root *Icveh₂S- 'to cough' also ends in -s-.

Bibl.: WH II: 721, EM 709, IEW 1032-34, Meiser 1998: 80.

<!-- pdf-page: 650; printed-page: 636; running-header: 636                                     fiber -->

■υ Qber, -lis 'breast, udder' [n. r] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: iiber, -eris [adj.] 'abundant, rich' (Pl.+), ubertas 'abundance1 (Enn.+); exuberare 'to flow copiously, be abundant5 (Acc.+).

Pit. *oup-r-.

PIE *hiOuHdʰ-r 'udder', ^euHd^n-s?

IE cognates: Skt. udhar / tidhan- [n.] 'udder' < Ilr. *HuHctar-, Gr. ουΰαρ, -ατός [ηJ, Ru. vymja, gen.sg. vymeni 'udder' < PSL *vym?, Ru. udit' 'to ripen, fill (with)' <

PIE *HouHdʰ-?; OHG utrin [dat.sg.], MHG uter, inter, OS OE uder [n.] 'udder' < *uct-9 OIc. ju(g)r, OE ieder, OFr. iader < *euct-. According to Schrijver, *HuHdʰ- would have become *wap-, whereas *He/ouHdʰ- > *owap- would have yielded *udr. Hence, we must posit the loss of the second laryngeal before it could vocalize. For this loss to happen, the root must have had 0-grade. The adjective Uber is mostly explained from the use of the noun as an adjective (as in vetus 'old5 < *uetos 'year'), but Sihler posits an i-stem *ouc?-ri- 'abundant'.

Bibl.: WH II: 738f., EM 715, IEW 347, Schrijver 1991: 327f., Sihler 1995: 301,353.



### ubi
ubi 'where' [adv.] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: alicubi 'somewhere' (Pl.+), nuncubi 'whether...anywhere' (Varro+), necubi "that nowhere' (Varro+), sicubi 'if anywhere' (Cato+); ubfcitmque 'wherever' (Pl.+), ubinam 'where in the world?' (Pl.+), ubiqmque 'everywhere' (Pl.+), ubique 'anywhere' (Lucil.+), ubivls 'whenever, anywhere' (Ter.+).

Pit. *k"u-p/fei 'where'.

It. cognates: O. puf, U. pufe, pufe [adv.] 'where' < *kʷu-b/dʰei.

PIE *kʷu-dʰi/-bʰi 'where'.

IE cognates: Skt. huha, OAv. kuda 'where', YAv. kudat 'where from5, OCS kbde 'where, when' < *kVd h e. Theoretically, ubi can reflect *kʷubʰei or *kʷu<fei. The suffix *-bʰi would be the PIE ins. ending, whereas *-dʰei could be an Italic innovation for *kʷu-dʰe 'where' as encountered in Ilr. and Slavic. In view of the latter correspondence, a preform

Pit. *kʷupei seems slightly more likely. Hit. ku-wa-pi 'where, when5 reflects *kʷo-b i, cf. Kloekhorst 2008: 490.

Bibl.: WH II: 739, EM 715f, IEW 644-648, Leumann 1977: 149f„ Schrijver 1991: 262, Sihler 1995: 399, Meiser 1998: 99, Untermann 2000: 594. → -de (for unde\ ut



### ulciscor, -scl *to take revenge'
ulciscor, -scl *to take revenge' [v. Ill; ppp. ultum] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ultor 'avenger; person avenged' (Acc.+), inultus "unpunished, unavenged' (Pl.+); ullo Ί will have avenged' (Ace).

<!-- pdf-page: 651; printed-page: 637; running-header: ulmus                                      637 -->

Pit. *ol-k- 'to destroy, take revenge\

PIE *h₃elhr [aon] 'to perish'»

IE cognates: see s.v. deled. The form ullo is generally reconstructed as *uls6 < *w/&-$-o; this would imply that extant -&- in other forms, e.g. the pf.fidlsJto falcire, is secondary. The ppp. ultus can reflect *ulk-to~» LIV's etymology as *h₂elk- 'to ward off (to Gr. αλκή 'resistance, courage') is semantically uncompelling. The traditional etymology is a derivation of ulcus 'ulcer', assuming a semantic development 'to grow sores (against someone)' > 'avenge one's griefs'. This is not compelling either (cf Keller 1992: 257). Schrijver is reluctant to accept an equation with Gr. όλέκω 'to destroy', a ^-enlargement to the root *h₃lhr; but semantically, this seems the best option.

PIE *h₃lhrk- would yield Lat. *M- according to Schrijver's rules (1991: 304ff,), but *h₃olhrlo > *hsolk- (with de Saussure's effect) would yield ulc-. Since a PIE o-grade is not easy to justify in a preform *h₃olhrk-, one could assume *h₃elh r > *-o/- with a more recent addition of

Bibl.: WH II: 810f., EM 743, IEW 310, Schrijver 1991: 70, LIV *h₂elk-.



### ulcus, -eris
ulcus, -eris 'sore, ulcer' [n. r] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ulcerosus 'full of ulcers' (Pl.+); extdcerore 'to make sore, wound' (VarrcH-).

Pit. *elkos;

PIE *hie!lc-os 'ulcer'.

IE cognates: Skt. orsos- [n.] 'hemorroids', Khot. osT 'itch', Sogd. "rsx, 'rs *nx9 hemorroids*, Gr. έλκος [η,] * wound, ulcer \ Less certain: Olr. olc 'bad' < *h}olk-o-, Olc. Mr 'bad' < PGm. *elh-ila-.

Pit. *elkos regularly developed into *olkos and then ulcus.

Bibl.: WH II: 811, EM 743f., IEW 310, Schrijver 1991: 37, Stiiber 2002: 190f., LIV ?*h,elk-.



### ulmus * elm-tree, elm-wood'
ulmus * elm-tree, elm-wood' [f. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ulmeus cof elms, of elm-woord* (Pl.+); ulmitriba [m,] 'one who wears out elms* (Pl.). -

Pit. *e/o/wo-. ν

IE cognates: Olr. lem 'elm' < PCL *Iimo-9 GauL Lemo-uices [ethnonym] > Limoges, W. llwyfen 'elm' < PCL *!emo- <

PIE *h,l(e)im-; Ru. il'em 'common elm', ilm 'wych elm', gen.sg. Wma, Czjilmjilma 'elm' < PSL *jb!bim> <

PIE *hil-mo- (?); OHG elmboum, MoE elm, Olc. almr (NHG Ulrne, MoDu. olm borrowed from Latin and Old French). Ulmus can reflect

Pit. *elmos > *olmos > ulmus (cf. Leumann 1977: 47). The restricted distribution (Italo-Celtic, Gm., Slavic) and the difficulty to reconstruct a PIE form suggest non-IE origin. Schrijver 1997a posits two forms *V-lm- (> Gm., Lat) versus */Kw- (Celtic). The Slavic word may also reflect the former, if it is not a loan from Germanic. In theory, one may try to derive PCL *Iimo- and Slavic from a zero grade

PIE *(H)lmo-y with secondary *leimo- developing in British Celtic. But this would still imply a PIE ablaut *hjelmo-, *hjolmo-, *hjlmo-, which is hardly credible.

Bibl.: WH II: 81 If., EM 744, IEW 302-304, Schrijver 1991: 66, 1997: 311.

<!-- pdf-page: 652; printed-page: 638; running-header: 638                                      ulna -->



### ulna
ulna 'forearm' [£ a] (Catullus+)

Pit. *olena- 'forearm'.

PIE *Heh₃i-en- (or *HoHl-en-) or *Hh₃-el-en- (or *HHol-en-) 'elbow'.

IE cognates: Olr. uilen 'angle', W. elin, BreL ilin < PCI. *ο/έκ-; Skt. aratni- [m.] 'elbow, ell', YAv. fraradniMrqjah- [n.] 'length of an ell', aredna [du,] 'two elbows', OP arasnis [acc.pl.] 'ell', Gr. ώλενη [f], ώλήν, -ένος [f.] 'elbow, underann', ώλλόν 'elbow' (Hsch.) (< *ol-n-om\ OPr. woaltis 'ell', woliis 'forearm', Lith. uolektis; Latv. uolekts 'ell' < *HoHl-ek-ti-, OPr. alkunis, Lith. alkune, elkune (archM dial.) 'elbow', Latv. t&uoms 'elbow' < *HHol-k-, OCS lakbtb [m. i], Ru. lokof 'id.' < *HHoikuti-; OHG el(i)na [f.j 'ell', Go. aleina < *ol-en-\ ToB alyiye 'palm' < PTo. *alen-. Many languages show an «-stem, but Ilr. and BSl. have different suffixes. Also, there is ablaut *d/- (acute) vs. *a/r (circumflex) within BSl. Hence, the word may have been a PIE /-stem *Heh₃-l- (or *HoH-l-), *Hh₃-eI-, to which an «-stem was made already in PIE. It is unclear whether this «-stem followed the regular ablaut rules of

PIE «-stems (as Schrijver assumes), or whether different «-stems were made to different ablaut forms of the /-stem. The preform *olr$n- shared by Italo-Celtic and Gm. could have been built on a stem *Hh₃-el- (or *HH-ol-), or be explained from pretonic shortening of a long vowel in front of a resonant (Dybo's shortening: *dlen- > *olen-i).

Bibl.: WH II: 812, EM 744, IEW 307-309, Lubotsky 1990: 131f., Schrijver 1991: 78, 352.



### uls
uls 'on the far side of, beyond' [prep. + ace] (Varro, Gel, Paul, ex F.)

Derivatives: ulterior (Pl.+) 'farther away'; ultra 'to a point farther off, conversely' (Naev.+); ultra (Cato+) 'on the far side, beyond'; ultimus 'farthest away' (Pl.+).

Pit. *ol-tero-, *ο!-ίωηο-.

It. cognates: (λ ultiumam [acc.sg.f.] Mast'.

PIE *h₂ol-tero- ''further', *h₂ol-tmHo- 'furthest, last'.

IE cognates: see s.v. olle. The prep, uls is rare, and probably not ancient. According to Leumann 1977: 220, uls was formed to ultra on the example of cis versus citra. The forms ultra, ultro and ulterior presuppose a stem *ultero- < *olrtero- 'more to that side', and ultimus < *ol-tamo- < *ol-tmHo- 'most to that side'. These words contain the same root as olle/ollus > Me.

Bibl.: WH II: 813, EM 744, IEW 24-26, Schrijver 1991: 68, Untermann 2000: 794f → olle/ollus



### ululō, -āre
ululo, -Sre 'to howl' [v. I] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: ulula 'the tawny owl' (Varro+).

IE cognates: Skt. uluha- [m.] 'owl', Gr. ύλάω, ύλακτέω 'to bark', Lith. ulula bangos 'the waves are howling', OHG ula 'owl'. Onomatopoeic verb for a w-like sound. Possibly, *«/- or *w/w/- was already PIE, but this cannot be proven.

Bibl.: WH II: 813f, EM 744f, IEW 1105.

### umbilicus
umbilicus 'navel, centre' [m. ο] (Pl.+)

Pit. *omb-(e/o)U » *omb-elo- (or *ombi-» *ombi-lo-).

PIE *ti3nbʰ-(e/o)l- 'navel'.

IE cognates: Olr. imbliu 'navel' < PCL *embli-ion- <

PIE *h₃nbʰ-li-; Skt nabhya- [n.] 'hub (of wheel)', nabhi- [f] 'hub, centre, navel (of the body or the world), origin, relationship, family*, YAv. nabanazdista- 'next-of-kin', nqfa- [m.] 'navel, origin, blood relationship*, MP nafag 'navel', nqf 'family' < Pllr. *Hnabʰ-, *Hnabʰia-\ Gr. ομφαλός [m.] 'navel, navel string; knob, center'; OPr. nabis 'hub, navel', Latv. naba 'navel*, OHG nabay OE nafii9 OIc. *p/'hub\ OHG nabalo, nabulo, napulo, OE nabula, nafela, OIc. nqfli 'navel' < PGm. *nabulon-. Latin umbilicus has a complex suffix, which in theory can be explained in several ways. In view of the /-suffixes in Celtic, Greek and Gm., it seems likely that umbilicus too contains an original /-stem. This was then thematized to *-(ς)/ο-, after which the suffix *-iAo- was added.

Bibl.: WH II: 814, EM 745, IEW 314f, Schrijver 1991:61f, Meiser 1998: 107 → umbo



### umbo
umbo 'boss (of a shield); protuberance* [m. n] (Enn.)

Pit. *omb-n-.

PIE *h₃nbʰ-n- 'hub, navel\

IE cognates: OHG amban, ambon [m.], OS ambon [acc.pl] 'belly' < PGm. *amban- < *h₃e/onbʰ-n-. Either an Italic formation oh the basis of a PIE root noun *h₃nbʰ-, or a shared / innovation with Germanic.

Bibl.: WH II: 814, EM 745, IEW 314f, Schrijver 1991: 61f → umbilicus



### umbra
umbra 'shadow, shade' [f a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: umbraticus 'that lives in the shade' (Pl.+), umbraticulus Mazy-bones' (PI,), umbraculum 'shelter, shade* (Varro+), umbrare cto cast a shadow' (Varro+); umbnfer 'shady' (Varro+).

IE cognates: Lith. unksma, paimksme 'shade', unksne = paitnksni, imksna 'shade*, maybe Latv. iiksme 'hiding-place'. Generally connected with Lith. imksna 'shade*, and reconstructed as *unksra-. This is the only word for which a cluster *-nksr- is reconstructed, but in view of temo < *tensm- < *tenksmon, it is conceivable that *unksra- would have yielded > *unsra- > *unfra- > umbra. This etymology suggests that the root was *(H)unh- (full grade *(H)uenh-\ but the only root of that form in LIV is *uenk- 'to bend', which does not seem a likely semantic candidate. No other root etymology.

Bibl.: WH II: 814f, EM 745, IEW 1173f



### umeo
umeo 'to be wet' [v. II] (Verg.+)

Derivatives: umidus 'wet, moist* (Pac.+), umidiias 'moisture' (Pl.+), umor 'moisture, fluid* (Pl.+), inumigare 'to make wet' (Andn), umecius 'moist, wet' (Cato+), umectare 'to make wet* (Lucr.+); uligo 'waterlogged ground, marsh* (Cato+), uligindsus 'ill-drained' (Varro+).

Pit. *umo- 'wet'.

PIE *uh r mo- 'wet*.

IE cognates: see s.v. urina-.

<!-- pdf-page: 654; printed-page: 640; running-header: 640                                    umerus -->

WH and EM derive um- from an extension in *-(s)m- to the root *u(e/o)gw- seen in Lat. uvidus, Gr. υγρός 'wet', etc. Weiss 1994 rejects this for semantic reasons, but to me the semantic difference between utfdus and derivatives on the one hand, and umidus and derivatives on the other, does not seem large enough to warrant their separation. The formal objections are bigger: the root *wgM- is not well-established, being based mainly on Germanic forms (OIc. vgkr 'wet'). Weiss therefore derives Umidus and umor from an adj. *uhrmo-, which he also sees in Lith. umos ' quick", dial. 'fresh, not dried out'. If urma and Skt var- 'water' are also derived from this root, it provides a solid etymological basis for *uhrmo. The appurtenance of iitigd is uncertain: according to Weiss (p. 145), UlTgo was probably created analogically to umidus and *umo- on the model of fiiligo. Yet we cannot exclude that uttgo was grafted on *uwidus > udus, with a change of intervocalic *-</- > -/-.

Bibl.: WH II: 815, EM 745, IEW 1118, Weiss 1995: 143f. → uvidus

It. cognates: U. uze, onse [loc.sg.] Ionise/ 'shoulder' < *omes(e)L

PIE *h₂om-es- 'shoulder1.

IE cognates: Skt. amsa- [m.] 'shoulder', Oss. (Iron) on 'shoulder-blade' < PIr. *anha-, Gr. ώμος [m.] 'shoulder, armpit' < *omso-; Arm. ows, Go, amsans [acc.pl.], ToA es [m.], ToB anise [m.] 'shoulder'. Hit. anass(a)- 'lower part of the back' cannot be connected with this reconstructed word for 'shoulder' (Kloekhorst 2008: 178).

Bibl.: WH II: 815, EM 746, IEW 778, Schrijver 1991: 51, Meiser 1998: 83, Untermann 2000: 798.

(dial.) anka 'line used to fasten a sail to the mast' (connection uncertain on accentological and semantic grounds), SeCS gkotb [f.] 'hook', ORu. ukotb [f ] 'claw, anchor' < PS1. *Qkotb.

### unda
unda 'wave, sea (Pl.+), river, running water (Acc.+)' [f, a] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: undare 'to rise in waves, gush' (Pl.+); abundare 'to overflow, be plentiful' (Pl.+).

Pit. *udor, *udn-.

It. cognates: U. utur [acc.sg.], une [abLsg.] 'water' <*udor, *udn-.

PIE *ud-or, *ud-n- 'water5.

IE cognates: Olr. uisce [m.] 'water' < PCL *uden-sk-io-, Hit. uatar I uiten- [n.] * water' < *uod-r, *ud-en-, Skt. udan- [n.], Gr. ΰδωρ, -ατός [n.], Alb. ujS9 Lith. vanduo, -ens, OCS vodb, Go. wato [n.] (dat.pl. watnam) , OIc. vatn, OS watar, OHG wazzar * water', ToA war, ToB war < PTo. *war (probably <

PIE *udrom\ The r-stem nom.acc.n. has survived in U. utur The rc-stem from the oblique cases survives in une and has probably been anticipated in Latin *ud-n- > *undrn-. The α-stem inflection and the diverging meaning 'wave' may point to an original collective *ud-n-om 'body of water', pi. *udna, which was reinterpreted as an individualized singular 'wave'.

### unguis
unguis 'finger-nail, claw' [m. ί] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: unguiculus 'finger- or toenail, tip' (Pl.+), ungula 'hoof, claw' (Pl.+).

Pit. *ongu(-i)-9 *ongla-. > ; h h 5

PIE *h₃ng -u- *nail\ *h₃ng -t~Jir 'nail .

The PIE w-stem was remade into an i-stem in Latin.
