### veru, -us
veru, -us 'spit (for roasting)' [n. u] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: verutum * short throwing spear' (Enn.+), vervina 'spit (or similar pointed instrument)' (Pl.+). ■ w

Pit. *g eru- *spit\

It. cognates: U. berva [acc.pl.], bents [abl.pL] n. 'utensil used during the sacrifice' < *gʷeru-.

PIE *gʷer(H>u- 'spit'.

IE cognates: Olr. bi(u)r [n. > £], W. Co. Bret ber [f, m.j 'spear, spit* < *gʷeru~. In theory, Italo-Celtic *gʷeru- could reflect

PIE *gʷerh₂-u- 'the heavy one', from which it is not inconceivable that a meaning 'pole, spit' would derive. Nor is it compelling, of course. The Go. word qairu n. 'pole, sting1 must be read as pairu (see Ebbinghaus 1977: 188f.) and does not belong here.

Bibl.: WH II: 766, EM 727, IEW 479, Untermann 2000: 145.



### O. veru
O. veru [acc.pl.], veruis [loc.pl.], U. vera [accpl.], verufe, verofe [acc.pl. + -en] , veres, uerir [abl.pl.], uerisco [abl.pl. + -com] 'city gate*.

Pit. *wero- 'gate' [n.].

PIE *Huer-o- * cover, protection'.

IE cognates: Skt. vala- [m.] 'cave, enclosure", Av. vara- [m.] 'fenced place, artificial cave' < Ilr. *Huara-, ORu. obora 'area for cattle', Cz. obora 'game preserve', SCr. obor 'courtyard* < ob + *Huor-h₂-.

Bibl.: IEW 1160-62, Untermann 2000: 8451, LIV l.*uer-, *Huer-. → aperio, operid



### verus
verus 'real, true' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: verax 'truthful' (Pl.+), verare 'to speak the truth' (Enn.+), veratrum 'poisonous or medicinal plant, hellebore' (Cato+).

Pit. *wero-.

PIE *ueh r ro- 'true1.

IE cognates: OIr.^/% W. gwir < PCI. *vw*o~; OCS vera, R u vera, Po. wiara 'faith, belief < PS1. *νέτα [f,], OHG war 'true'. Possibly also Gr. ήρα in (em) ήρα φέρειν 'to please somebody' [ace. sg. (pi. n.?)] if from *z/er-. The appurtenance of veratrum here is uncertain. It would suppose that it was derived from verare, in the sense that hellebore was used to restore mental health in patients (this was Isidores etymology); cf- Serbat 1975: 318f,

Bibl.: WH II: 768, EM 727, IEW 1165£, Schrijver 1991: 141. → sevens



### vervex, -ecfo
vervex, -ecfo 'wether, castrated male sheep' [m, k] (Pl.+)

Pit. *werw-e-m

PIE *ueru-?

IE cognates: Gr. είρος [n.] 'wool' < *ueruos-, Myc. /werwes-ieia/ 'women who work the wool5, Att. ευ-ερος 'with beautiful wool9, έπ-ερος 'sheep'. Has been connected with

PIE *urh r en- 'lamb' (to Skt iiran- [m.] 'Iamb' < Ilr. *urH-an-, Sogd. wr'n 'lamb' < Pin *varan-am, Gr. άρήν [m.], Kret. j/αρην 'Iamb', Gr. πολύ-ρρην-ες 'possessing many lambs5, Arm. garn Mamb*), but it is hard to think of a derivational scenario by which this could have happened: *uerh r u- > *ueru- + *-e- (on the model of verres) » *-efc- is conceivable, but all other IE languages have the zero grade *wrAr of the root in this word. A zero grade *urhru- would probably yield Latin *raw- or possibly *rmv-. Instead of with PIE 'lamb', vervex might be

<!-- pdf-page: 683; printed-page: 669; running-header: vesica                                     669 -->


connected with Gr. είρος [n.] 'wool' < *yeruo$-, which is semantically less attractive, but possible. We could assume *ueru-e- 'the male woolly one' (on the model of verres), built on an older w-stem or on the truncated s-stem. Thefc-suffixmust, under any scenario, be a later Latin addition.

Bibl.: WH II: 767f, EM 727, IEW 1170.



### vescor, vesci
vescor, vesci 'to make use of, enjoy' [v. Ill] (Pac.+)

Pit. *wes-(s)ke/o- 'to feed, pasture'.

It. cognates: Ven. vesces [nom.sg.], vesketel [dat.sg.] 'pupil' (male and female reference) < *wesk-et~; possibly U. veskla, vesklu [acc.pL], veskles, uesclir, Vol uesclis [abl.pl.] < *wesk(V)lo- [n.] 'kind of vessel'.

PIE *ues- 'pasture, food'.

IE cognates: OIn fess 'food' < *tfes-th₂-, Hit. uesi- /uesai- [c] 'pasture' (< *ues-(e)i-\ Skt svasara- 'pasture, meadow' (< *su-uas-ra-)9 Av. vastra- [n.] 'pasture', vastar- 'herd', OIc. fist 'nourishment' < *}'esti-, ToA wasri 'pasture' < *uesri-. WH's derivaton from *ve-escor 'to eat off is unconvincing because of the semantic gap with 'to enjoy', and because of the passive conjugation of vescor. Muller 1926 and Keller 1982 assume a merger of two verbs, one being

PIE *ues- 'to wear', and another verb meaning 'to use, enjoy'. Yet the contexts where vescor means 'to wear' are very rare, and the oldest attestations adduced by Muller and Keller (vescor armis) can just as well mean 'to use'. The verb vescor can be compared with IE terms for 'pasture' which go back to a root *ues-. Latin could reflect *ues-ske/o-y with introduction of e as (possibly) in Av. vastra-, vastar-. In view of the absence of verbal derivatives of the root outside Latin, this -e- would probably have a nominal origin (e.g. from a root noun *ues, *uesos). The suffix -sk- would be a Latin addition, or an earlier Italic one, if U. vesklo- 'vessel' is cognate, or an even older one (maybe only a suffix *-&-) if Ven. vesket- is cognate.

Bibl.: WH II: 769, EM 727f, IEW 72f, Lejeune 1974: 340, Untermann 2000: 846f., LIV 3.*ues-, → edoy vestibulum



### vesica
vesica 'bladder' [f. a\ (Pl.+)

Derivatives: ve(n)slcula 'small bubble' (Lucr.+). Andre 1984 argues, to my mind convincingly, that the original form was vesica, which is sometimes spelled venslca because the sequences -es~ and -ens- started to merge in VLat Andre compares the fact that the 'bladder' is often referred to using words for 'to blow, inflate' in different languages, e.g. in Germanic (NHG Blase, En. bladder), which is due to the fact that bladders were often recycled as balloons, lanterns or musical instruments involving air bags. Andre compares Lat. vtsire / vissire 'to fart softly' (Lucil.+) and regards the root *vi/es(s)- as onomatopoeic; the sound of a deflating bladder would thus have been compared to the sound of a fart. Indeed, words for 'to fart' often show a sequence of a labial obstruent plus a sibilant, cf. Lat. pedo <

PIE *pesd-. The new Latin formation *ve/is(s)- is comprehensible in the light of the development *pesd- > ped-, which obscured the sound symbolism originally present in that word.

Bibl.: WH II: 750f., EM 728, IEW 1104f, Andre 1984.

<!-- pdf-page: 684; printed-page: 670; running-header: 670                                     vespa -->



### vespa
vespa 'wasp' [f. a] (Varro+)

Pit. *we/op-s-h₂- 'wasp'.

PIE *(hi)uobh-s-h₂- 'wasp'.

IE cognates: OCo. guhi-en 'a wasp', MW gw(y)chi, OBret guohi 'wasps'; Αν. vafizaka- 'scorpion', MP ναβζ, BaL gwabz 'wasp' < PIr. *wabza-\ OPr. wobse, Lith. vapsva, vapsa (dial.), Latv. vapsene 'wasp', RuCS osa, Ru. osa, SCn osa (< BSL *uapsa); OE wcefs, weeps, wcesp OHG wefsa, wqfsa, waspa, Bav. webes. It is questionable whether 'wasp' was derived from

PIE *(hi)uebh- 'to weave', since wasps are not typically known for 'weaving'. On the other hand, the way in which they build their nests from paper pulp could be described as 'weaving', or its appearance as a net.

Bibl.: WH II: 770, EM 728, IEW 1179,



### vesper, -eri
vesper, -eri 'evening' [m. ο] (Pl.+; also vespera)

Derivatives: vespema 'the evening meal' (Pl.+), vesperascere, advesperascere 'to become evening' (Ter.+), vespertinus 'of the evening' (Varro+), vesperugd 'the Evening Star' (Pl.+), vespertitio 'bat' (Varro+).

Pit. *we(k)spero-.

PIE *ue-k(w)sp-er-o- 'evening'.

IE cognates: W. ucher 'evening', Hit ispant- [c] 'night', Skt ksap- [f], Av. xsapan- I xsafh-, YAv. xsap- [f.], xsapar-, OP xsap- [fJ 'night' <

PIE *k(w)sep(-r-/-n-); Gr. έσπερος [m.] Evening' (< ^uekwsp-ero-)\ Arm. giser 'night' (< *uekʷsero-), Lith. vakaras, Latv. vakars, OCS vecerb, Ru. vecer 'evening' < *uekspero-. The PIE word for 'evening' is clearly a compound of an element *ue- and a word for 'night' *tfw)sep~* The zero grade of the root brought about a consonant cluster *-hf"v)sp- which was simplified in different ways in the IE languages. The first element *we- is difficult to expain. It has been equated with Lat ve-, but this has a very restricted distribution, and chances are that it is an inner-Latin development; see s.v. Also, it means 'hyper / hypo-', which does not match the alleged meaning of ve- in vesper. Ve- in vesper has been compared with Latin au-tem, Gr. αΰ 'again', but

PIE *h₂ue- would leave other traces in Greek than ε-. Of course, one might assume that the initial laryngeal was dropped in the compound. It is possible that *ue- in *ue-k(w)sp-ero- has a completely different origin, e.g. the reduction of an earlier noun or adjective.

Bibl.: WH II: 770f., EM 728, IEW 1173f → ve-



### vespillō [m.?]
ve/ispillO [m. ή] 'one employed to bury those too poor to afford a funerar (Mart+)

Pit. *wospo- 'clothing'?

PIE *uos-p-l

IE cognates: Hit {T(}G)uaspa- [c] 'clothing', CLuw. uaspant- 'wearing shrouds (?)' <

PIE *uos-bʰo-7 As argued by Watkins 1969, the original function of a vespillo was to provide the dead with the right clothing for their burial Since vesp- may reflect earlier *vosp-, Watkins hypothesizes that vespillo goes back to a PIE word for * clothing, shroud' found also in Anatolian. Janda 2000: 85 and 208f also connects Gr. δσπριον 'pulse,

<!-- pdf-page: 685; printed-page: 671; running-header: vestis                                     671 -->

legume' (*'in a pod'), suggesting an original r/w-stem *uosp-r 'cover, pod\ Semantically, this is a convincing etymology. The main problem is the source of the suffix *-/>-, which is an infrequent suffix in PIE,

Bibl.: WH II: 772, EM 728, Watkins 1969. → vestis



### Vesta
Vesta 'goddess of the domestic hearth" [f. a] (Enn.+; Pocula Deorum Vested datsg.)

Derivatives: ves talis 'of Vesta or her cult' (Varro+). The closest cognate seems to be Gr. εστία 'hearth, fireplace', Ion. ίστίη, which has *w- in ριστισυ (ΡΝ, Mantineia), γιστία 'hearth' (Hsch.). This cannot be derived from

PIE *h₂ues- 'to spend the night, stay', since *h₂- would have yielded a-. Possibly a loanword.

Bibl.: WH II: 772f, EM 729, IEW 1170, LIV 2.*h₂ues-.



### vestibulum
vestibulum 'fore-court' [n. ο] (Pl.+)

Pit. *westV-plo- 'place of feeding'.

PIE *ues-ti- 'feeding' or *ues-to- 'fed'.

IE cognates: See s.v. vescor. Many different etymologies have been proposed The most popular ones have been reviewed by Serbat 1975: 50-53, who points out that all of them are semantically unsatisfactory. Serbat himself argues that the vestibulum may have referred to the courtyard of the pre-classical farm, where the domesticated animals were kept and fed. This leads him to derive vestibulum from the root *ues- 'to pasture, graze', its original meaning being 'which permits feeding'. For the suffix, compare Lat sessibulum.

Bibl.: WH II: 774, EM 729, Leumann 1977: 314, LIV 3.*ues-.



### vestigium
vestigium 'footprint, track' [n. ο] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: vestigare 'to follow the trail, search out' (Pl.+), vesttgator 'who tracks down' (Varro+), investlgare 'to track down, search out1 (Pl.+).

Pit. *w(e/o)rsti-Hg-.

PIE *u(e)rs-ti- 'furrow'.

IE cognates: see s.v. verro. It is often assumed that vestigium was primary and vestigare secondary, but this is uncertain. Since a connection with vestis 'clothes' does not make much sense, the suggestion that vestigium continues *verstigium, which was defended by Muller 1926: 539 but rejected outright by WH, seems attractive. If connected with vera? 'to sweep' < *w/-s-, vestigium may originally have meant a 'track' or 'furrow\ The verb vestigare can reflect *w(e)rsti-Hg- 'to draw a furrow', from a noun *urs~ti- (or *uers-ti-) 'furrow'.

Bibl.: WH II: 774f, EM 729, Dunkel 2000a: 95, LIV l.*uers-. ^ vo/errd

vestis 'clothes' [f. i] (Andr.+)

Pit. *wes-ti- 'clothes'.

PIE *ues-ti-.

IE cognates: Hit. uess-na / uasse/a-*1 'to be dressed, wear, put on\ CLuw. uass- 'to wear' < *ues-to and *us-ii-ti-\ Skt. vaste 'to wear', vasana- [n.] 'garment, cloth', vasti-vama- 'colour of the garment', OAv. vaste, YAv. vaste 'is clothed', ναφαηα- [n.] 'clothing', vastra- [n.] 'garment, cloth', Gr. έννυμι 'to clothe, put on' < *ues-n(e)u-, aor. έσ(σ)αι, pfmed. είμαι, ειμένος < *ues-; εϊματα [pi.] 'clothes' (< *ues-mn-)y Arm. z-genowm 'clothe oneself < *ues-nu-, z-gest 'cloth(es)' < *ues-tu-, Alb. vesh 'to clothe', Go. and-wasjan 'to take off\ OE werian 'to clothe' <

PIE *uos-eie-; ToB was- 'to wear; put on'. Probably an independent formation of Latin, since Skt vasti- is only attested in the Taittinya Aranyaka.

### vetō, -āre
ve/ot6, -are 'to forbid' [v. I; pf. vetui, ppp. vetitum] (Pl.+; votd in Nonius)

Pit. *we/ot-a(je)-1 *wotaje~.

PIE *uet(h₂)- / *uoth₂-eie/o- / *ue/ot-eh₂- 'to say'.

IE cognates: OW guetid 'says', MW dy-wedaf'l say1 < PCI. *uete/o-\ MW gwadu 'to deny', gwad [m.], OBret. guad 'denial' < *uot-. If the hapax vo- in Nonius is the oldest form, we must reconstruct

Pit. *wot-; since this did not undergo unrounding to *wa-, it would imply a Pit. preform *wotH- with a closed initial syllable (Schrijver 1991). The verb could be equated with MW gwadu 'to deny' < *uot-. Rix 1999 argues that the original construction was vetare ne 'to say that not\ which came to mean 'to forbid that'; this development might have been Italo-Celtic, in view of the MW meaning of gwadu 'to deny. But other solutions are equally possible. Lat. votd could be a deliberate archaism; compare the change vo- > ve- in front of s,t,rC from the second century onwards. Or Lat. voto could be a phonetically regular remnant of the change *e > ο after *w and before a back vowel in the next syllable (as in vomo, soror, etc.). In that case, we can reconstruct

Pit. *wet-a- 'to say1 to an ami root *uet-.

Bibl.: WH I: 776, EM 730, Schrijver 1991: 472f., Rix 1999: 520, Schumacher 2004: 679f, LIV *ueth₂-.

### vetus, -eris Old'
vetus, -eris Old' [adj. r] (Pl.+; Enn. nom.sg. veter)

Derivatives: vetulus 'elderly, ageing' (Pl.+), veteranus 'mature, experienced' (Varro+), vetustus 'ancient' (Pl.+), vetustas 'being old, age, antiquity' (Pl.+), veternus 'torpor, lassitude' (Pl.+), veternosus 'lethargic' (Cato+), veterator 'an experienced practitioner' (Ter.+); irtveterascere 'to grow old' (Ter.+); veterinus [adj.] 'used for draught, draught-animals' (Cato+).

<!-- pdf-page: 687; printed-page: 673; running-header: via                                      673 -->

Pit. *wetos- 'year', *wetes-ino- 'old', *wetso- 'of the year', IL cognates: U. vesune, Mars, uesune [datsg.] 'the goddess *wetsona 'lady of the year'; maybe 0. vezkei [datsg.] 'a certain deity'.

PIE *uet-os- 'year' [n.], *uet-s-o- 'of the year'.

IE cognates: Hit. uitt- (< vet-) [a] 'year', CLuw. ussa/i-y HLuw. usa/i- 'year' (< *ut-s-o-)y Hit. saudist- / sauitist- 'weanling, young calf (< *so-ut-es-h t *so-uet~es-t- 'the one of this year'), Skt. vatsara- [m.] 'year', Gr. έτος [n.], dial, ψετος 'year', Myc. we-to, we-te-i (/uetos, ueteh-i/) [n.] 'year', Alb. vit9 pi. (also sg.) vjet 'year'; Lith. vetusas, OCS vetbcht 'old', Ru. vetxij 'old, ancient, decrepit' <

PIE *uetus-o-. Go. wiprus 'year-old lamb', Olc. veor, OHG widar 'wether'. Adams 1992 observes that vetermus usually refers to equines, and suggests that these came to be seen as 'the old stock' because by nature they lived longest of all cattle, and because the Romans did not as a rule eat horse meat Hence, especially equines could be referred to as 'old',

vex6, -are 'to agitate, damage' [v. I] (Cato*)

Derivatives: vexamen 'disturbance' (Lucr.+), divexare 'to ravage, drag about' (Pl.+). ### via'road'
via'road' [f 5](LexXII+)

Derivatives: vialis 'of roads' (Pl.+), viaticus 'of/for a journey' (Pl.+), viaticatus 'furnished with supplies for a journey' (Pl.), viator traveller, agent' (CIL I.583+); obviam 'in the path of, facing up' (Naev.+), obvius 'in the path of, confronting' (?\m+\pervivs 'passable' (Pl.+), proevius 'leading the way' (Pl.+); viocurus 'who has charge of roads' (Varro+); veia 'plaustrum' in Oscan (Paul, ex F.)y veiar(i)i 'stipites in plaustro', veiatura 'vectura' (ibidem).

Pit. *wija- 'road'.

It. cognates: Ο. νίύ, viu [nom.sg.], viam, via [acc.sg.], viai [loc.sg.], viass [acc.pl.], U. via, vea, uio [abl.sg.], SPic. viam [acc.sg.] f. 'road' < *uiia\ O. amviannud, amvianud '?' probably < *aw- or an-via-ndo-y denoni. verb to *v/<5- 'road'.

PIE *uih r eh 2 - 'pursuit'.

IE cognates: Skt viyanti [3p,act] 'they pursue', νί/ά- 'turned to', YAv. viia- *to pursue', vitar^ [m>] 'pursuer'; Gr, ϊεμαι 'to strive after, wish'; Lith. vyti 'to drive, pursue'. According to Nieto 1988, PauL ex F. veia 'waggon* is a VLat development from via, and the following form in the same gloss veiari represents *viarl 'of the road'; both would have VLat. lowering of/ in hiatus. This is confirmed by the form veham 'viam' given by Varro as a 'rustic' form. The meaning 'waggon' could be directly preserved in Italian veggtia 'waggon; barrel'. This, in turn, means that the etymology *uegʰia

<!-- pdf-page: 688; printed-page: 674; running-header: 674                                    vlbices -->

can be discarded, and we may rather posit

Pit. *wija-„ This may derive from

PIE *ueih r 'to strive after, try to get', as has been suggested by WH and others. The same root is reflected in Latin vis 'you want' antl invitus 'unwilling'.

Bibl.: WH II: 778f, EM 731, IEW 1123f, Untermann 2000: 93, 860f, LIV *ueihr* → invitus, ν/ο/ο, νϊίδ

### vibro, -are
vibro, -are 'to move to and fro, flash' [v. I] (Lucr.+)

Derivatives: vibrissare 'to sing with vibrato' (Titin. apud Paul. exF,).

Pit. *wib-ro- trembling'.

PIE *uip/b-ro-.

IE cognates: Skt. vipra- [m. / adj.] 'trembling, seer\ YAv. vifra- [adj.] 'ecstatic'; Skt. pr. vepate 'to tremble', vipaya- 'to become inspired', aor. νιράηά- [ptcmed.], pf vivipre [3p.med.j 'they have become excited', vip- [f.] 'inspired speech', OAv. vaepiio- 'catamite, pathic', YAv. apa vaepa- 'to yield, give off, vaepaiia- 'to be homosexual', Go. weipan 'to crown', faur-waipjan 'to bind7, OIc. veifa 'to swing'; ToB wByp- 'to shake, swing', 3s. wTpa(ssam) , ToB waipe* [m.sg.] 'banner*, obhsg. waipe, ToB waipalau 'vertigo'. Derived from an adj. *wibro- 'swinging, agitated', which can be connected to IE words of the same meaning from *ueip- (Gm* *weib- may also go back to

PIE *ueip-, cf. LIV). The outcome of

PIE *-pr- in Latin can be seen in caper, copray although these words may be loanwords. The PIE root-final consonant apparently varied in voicing, consider also the root *kfw)sueibh/p- 'to swing' in Skt ksipati 'to hurl', Av. xsuuaePaiiatastra- 'swinging the whip', Skt. ksipra- 'quick, fast', YAv. xsuuifira- 'quick\ xsuuifii-isu- 'with fast arrows'. The Ilr. root *ks(u)ip/b{h)- looks like a compound with *uib/p- as its second member.

### vieis [gen.sg.]
vieis [gen.sg.] 'turn, occasion' [f. k] (Pl.+; ace. vicem, abl. vice, nom.pl. vices, dat.abl.pl. vicibus\ not attested in nom.sg. and gen.pl.)

Derivatives: vicissaiim 'in turn(s)' (Naev.+), vicissim 'in turn' (Pl.+), vicissitas 'alternation' (Ace), vicissitudo 'reversal, alternation' (Ter.+), vicarius [adjVm.] 'substitute, deputy' (Pl.+).

Pit. *wik- 'turn\

PIE *uik- 'a turn',

IE cognates: see s.v. vincio. As to the PIE root, this may well have been *uik- 'to tie, turn'. All simplex forms point to a root noun vik-, but for vicissim, an /-stem is mostly assumed. Schrijver judges the etymology *viki-dtim < *-dʰjt-im 'given, giving in turn' not impossible. This would derive from aft'-stem*-dʰ₃ti-1 whereas vicissaiim points to a *vicissus <

*-dʰrto- 'given in turn', The alternative solution *vici-cessim 'im Wechsel schreitend' (Leumann 1977: 234, 501) also seems possible: via haplology, vici-ssim would have arisen. None of these etymologies is immediately convincing.

### victima
victima 'sacrificial animal' [f a] (Naev>+)

Pit. *wiktV-ma-,

It. cognates: U. eveietu, e.veietu [acc.sg.m. of ppp.] ' ? \ If vic-is 'turn, occasion' derives from a root meaning 'to turn', the application of 'turn' in a context of exchange can easily lead to a meaning 'exchange', as in the Gm. cognates. The victima could then be the 'exchange' with the gods, hence *uikti- ' exchange' + *-mo-. But there are other options. Victima may also be connected with

PIE *ueik- 'to select, sift', as in Skt vec- ςΐο sift, to separate, to winnow', pra-vikta- 'selected', vivici- 'separating', YAv. hqm.vuiia 'to separate completely5, YAv, x vaecaiia- [caus.], MP wextan/weztan 'to sift, select, winnow', Khwar. prwycy- 'to sift'. One could connect Go. weihs, OHG wih 'holy', in the same field of religious terminology as victima.

### vicus
vicus 'village, block of houses\[m. o] (Pl.+; OLat ueicus, necus, uecos CIL)

Derivatives: vicatim 'street by street* (Sis.+), vicinus [adj./m.] 'neighbour, neighbouring' (Naev>+), vicinia 'adjoining land, neighbourhood' (Pl.+), vicinitas 'proximity, neighbourhood' (Cato+), villa 'rural dwelling, farm' (Pl.+), villaticus 'of a farmstead5 (Varro+), vilicus 'farm-overseer' (Pl.+), vilica 'wife of a farm-overseer5 (Cato+), vilicare 'to perform the duties of a farm-overseer' (Cato+).

Pit. *weik-o- 'settlement5, *weik-sla- 'farm'. f r

PIE *ueik- / *uik- 'settlement'.

IE cognates: Skt. pr. vesa- 'to sit down, go in', Av. visa- 'to get ready5, Skt. vesa- 'inhabitant', YAv. vaesa- [m.] Servant (who lives in the master's house)', Skt. vis- [f ] 'settlement, dwelling-place, people', YAv. vis- [f.] * homestead, community', OP νιϋ- [f] 'royal house, palace, house, farm' < Ih\ *uic- <

PIE *uik-; Gr. ροΐκος, οίκος [m.] 'house, household', Myc. wo-i-ko-de /woikon-de/ 'home5, Lith. viespats 'lord', OPr. waispattin 'mistress' [acc.sg.] < *uoikrpoti-; OCS vbsb [f.], Ru. ves' (obs.) 'village' <

PIE *uik-, Go. weihs [n.] (gen. weihsis) 'village, settlement'. It is generally assumed that *woik- yielded vie- instead of **wc- due to initial *w-, which would have exerted a dissimilatory influence on the diphthong or the long vowel. This is uncertain, since the OLat. spellings neic- and uec- could also represent

Pit. *we/£-, and since there is no other example of a development *wo/- > vf-. In view of Go, weihs; an ablaut grade *ueik- was also present in PIE; in fact, the original noun may have been a root noun *ueik- / *uik-, with a thematic derivative *uoik-o- in Greek. The noun villa < *weik-$la- was probably derived from the verb *ueik- *to settle' or from the root noun; from villa was then derived vllicus, with simplification of the geminate -//-.

Bibl.: WH II: 782f., 790f., EM 732f., IEW 1131, LIV *ueik--

### video
video 'to see' [v. II; pf. viiff, ppp. visum] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: videlicet [adv.] "evidently, of course' (Pl.+); prudens 'well aware, clever' (Pl.+), imprudens 'ignorant, unaware5 (Pl.+), imprudentia 'ignorance' (Ten+); invidere 'to be jealous of, refuse5 (Pl.+), invidus "malevolent, envious' (Pl.+), invidia 'dislike, jealousy' (Pl.+), invididsus 'unpopular, enviable' (Cato+), pervidere 'to discern, see fully' (Varro+), providere 'to foresee, see to it' (Pl.+), improvisus 'unexpected' (Pl.+), revidere 'to pay another visit' (Pl.+); viso, -ere 'to go and look, visit' (Pl.+X visus, -us 'sight, vision5 (Lucr.+), visitare 'to see frequently, visit' (Pl.+), circumvisere 'to look round at5 (Pl.+), convisere 'to watch, visit' (Acc.+), intervlsere 'to go and see' (Pl.+), provisere 'to be on the look-out' (Pl.+), revlsere 'to pay another visit' (Pl.+).

Pit. *wid-e- 'to see', *weidrs- 'to see (in future)'.

It. cognates: U. uirseto auirseto [ppp. nom.sg.] 'seen unseen5, SPic. videtas [2p.pr.?] 'to see' < *uid~e~; U. revestu [3s.ipv.ll] 'to check5 < *re-ueid-s-e-tdd.

PIE *uid-eh r 'to see1.

IE cognates: Olr. ro-finnadar* 'to find out' < PCI. *windre/o-, W. gwybot, Is. gwrm, Co. godhvos, Bret. gouz(o)ut, Is. goun, gonn 'to know' < *wind-nu- « *wind-e/o-; Olr. rojitir 'to know', MW gwyr, MBret. goar, MCorn. gor 'knows' < PCI. *uid- «

PIE *uoid-/*uid~ [pf.]; MW gwydyat, MCo. gozye, OBret. goyzye [3s.ipf.] 'knew' < *ueidrT- < *ueid-ehr\ Olr. adcfit < *ad-weid-e/o- 'to tell, relate5 ( « *uinde/o-); Skt. ved- ' 1. to find, discover, desire, 2. to know, be acquainted with' [pr. vindali, aor. vidat\ pf. veda, ppa vidvams-, ppp. vitta-]\ OAv. vinasti 'finds', vidat 'finds', vista- 'found', vaeda [pf] Ί know, he knows', ppa. viduuah- 'knowing, capable'; a-uuaedaiiamahi 'we announce5; YAv. vinda- 'to find5 [3s. vinasti] ; OP vindafarna [PN], MP windadan/wind- Ho find, desire', Parth wynd- 'to find'; Gr. οΐδα Ί know' [pi. ϊδμεν (Att. ΐσμεν) , ipv. ΐσΰι, ptc. ειδώς] , ιδυΐοι [m.pl.] 'witnesses5, aor. ιδεΐν 'to behold, recognise', εΐδομαι cto seem, appear5, είδος 'species, appearance'; Arm. egit 'he found5, 3s.aor. gt-i9 pr. gtanem < ^hje-uid-et, gitem 'to know' < *uoid-\ Lith. veizdhi, 3s. veizdi 'to look (for)' < BSl. *yei?d-; OCS videtU Ru. videt\ SCr. vidjeti 'to see' < PS1. *videti; OPr. waist 'know', OCS vedeti, Is. vemb, 3s. vest* 'to know', Ru. vidat9 'to manage, (obs.) know', Cz. vedeti 'to know5 < PSI. *vedeti < BSL *uoi?d-; Go, wait, witum Ί know, we know5, weitwops 'witness', fraweitan 'to take revenge5, in-weitan 'to worship5, OE gewiian 'to die5, Go. fairweitjan 'to observe, heed'; Go. witany -aida 'to heed'. Prudens regularly reflects *prowidens9 *prowident- > *proudr > prudr. The pr. viso reflects an s-desiderative *weid-s-o. The pf. vidi could formally reflect a root aorist *ueid<- or a pf. *uoid-; in view of the perfective meaning (CI saw'), LIV opts for a root aorist. The stative pr. *uide- is confirmed by Umbrian and South Picene; we also find it in Gothic and in Lith. payyditi, Is. -vydziii 'to envy5, but these can easily be independent formations.

Bibl.: WH II: 378, 784£, EM 541, 733E, IEW 1125-27, Schrijver 1991: 276, Untermann 2000: 634f, 854f, Schumacher 2004: 664f, 690-695, LIV *ueid-.



### viduus
viduus 'deprived of husband or wife' [adj. ο/α] (Pl.+)

<!-- pdf-page: 691; printed-page: 677; running-header: vigeo                                        677 -->

Derivatives: vidua [f.] 'widow' (Naev.+), viduitds 'state of being deprived, widowhood' (Pl.+), vidertas 'dearth' (Cato+), viduare 'to deprive of (Lucr.+)·

Pit. *wipowo- [adj.].

PIE *hiui-dʰhreu-o- 'separated'.

IE cognates: Olr.fedb, Skt vidhava- [f.] 'widow', a-vidhava- [f.] 'not a widow', YAv. viOauua [nom.sg.f.j, MP wewag, MoP beway Oss. idcez 'widow' (< PIr. *uidanacT) < Plln ^HuidtauaH- 'widow, deprived of a partner'; Gr. ηίύεος [m.] 'unmarried young man'; OPr. widdewu, OCS vbdova, vbdova [f.], Ru. vdova (ace. vdovu) < PSL *vbdova\ Go. widuwo^ OHG wituwa 'widow', Latin and Greek show a them, adj. used for both sexes, whereas the other languages have a fern. noun. Since the word came to be used for women more often than for men (in most IE societies), it is unlikely that the f. noun was original, and adjectivized without any suffix in Latin and Greek. I therefore assume the primacy of the o-stem adj. in PIE. Initial * φ - was dissimilated to */?/w- in front of *-i/1-; cf. Lubotsky 1994.

Bibl.: WH II: 785£,~ΕΜ 734, IEW 1127£, Beekes 1992, Lubotsky 1994. → divido



### vieo
vieo 'to plait, weave' [v. II] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: vietor/vltor 'basket-maker' (Pl.+), vTmen [n.] 'flexible branch, used for wickerwork' (Cato+), vlmineus 'made of wickerwork' (Pl.+), viminalis 'offfor withies; hill of Rome' (Varro+), vlminetum 'osier-bed' (Varro), vldulus 'kind of carrying-bag' (Pl.); viescere [ppp. vieius Ter.+] 'to* shrivel' (Col.+); vitta 'linen headband, woollen band' (Pl.+j,

Pit. *wijeje/o- 'to plait', *weimn- 'branch for plaiting'.

PIE *uh₁i-eie-cto plait', *uehii-mn 'plaiting'.

IE cognates: Olr. ar-fen 'to close off, imnt-fen* 'to enclose' < PCI. *-wi-na-; Skt. vyayati 'to wind, wrap', ppp. vF/ά-, Gr. ΐτυς, -υος, Aeol. ρίτυς 'felloe, rim of a shield', Lith. vyti, Latv. vf/, OCS viti, SCr. vlti 'to twist, wind' < *uHi-, Lith. vytis 'twig', SCr. pavit 'vine' < *uHi-ti-; OCS vejay Skt. vaya- 'branch, twig' < *uoHi-eh₂-; Av. vaeiti- 'willow', Go. waddjus < *uoiH-u-. The appurtenance of viescd and vietus here is disputed. If one assumes that vietus is a (secondary) ppp. to vieo meaning 'plaited, woven' > 'wrinkled', viescd can have been formed as an 'inchoative to vied but with the meaning of vietus, thus 'to become wrinkled, shrivel'. The noun vitta, if from *vf/a, may continue an older /o-ptc. *uiH-to- of the" root. For vldulus, we must assume an extended root *uiH-d-.

Bibl.: WH II: 785-787, 806f, EM 734f., IEW 1120-22, Schrijver 1991: 245, 335, Schumacher 2004: 688, LIV *ujehr. → vmum> vltis



### vigeō
vigeo 'to be active, flourish' [v. II; pf. vigui, no ppp. attested] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: vigor 'vigour' (Caes.+), vigescere 'to acquire strength' (Lucr.+), vigil, -is [m.] 'guard, sentry' (Pl.+) [gen.pl. vigilum], vigil\ -is 'awake, wakeful' (Lucr.+), vigilia 'the act of keeping watch, patrol' (Pl.+), vigilium 'the action of keeping watch' (Varro), vigilare 'to stay awake' (Pl.+); advigitdre 'to be on the watch* (Pl.+), obvigilare 'to be watchful' (Pl.), pervigilare 'to stay awake all night' (Pl.+).

Pit. *weg-e-, *wegli-.

PIE *ueg-eh r 'to be strong', *ueg-l-i- 'strong'.

IE cognates: Skt. vqja- 'contest, reward', vajayati 'impels', Go. -wakjan 'to waken', OHG wahhen 'to be awake'.

<!-- pdf-page: 692; printed-page: 678; running-header: 678                                      viginfi -->

The PIE etymology with *neg- is adopted by all scholars. The i in vigeo may be explained as analogically adopted from the vigil (LIV adduces "Homonymenflucht von vegere" as a possible impetus), or ^rom raising of *e to ι after a preceding labial, as in firmus, vitulus, villus,fiber,,pinna, according to Watkins 1973b: 196- The latter explanation seems more likely, since vigil and vigeo are not closely associated in the synchronic semantics of Latin (thus also Watkins 1973b: 197), so that analogical introduction of vi- from vigil seems less likely. Together with vectTgal, vigil is the only stem in *-/£? which shows the regular development *-& > *& > -/. The nom.sg. vigil must have caused the (partial) switch from z-declination to a consonant stem (gen.pl. vigilum), which in its turn explains why we find vigilare already in the earliest literature. Initial vi- in vigil can be explained from assimilation to the i of the second syllable, as in nihil, similis, cinis. Final -il instead of *-«/ in the nom.sg. must be due to analogy with other case forms (*veglim, *vegleis, *veglj), where / was exiliSy yielding a stem *vegili-.

Bibl.: WH II: 788, EM 735, IEW 1117f.> Sihler 1995: 40, 497, Niedemiann 1945: 69, LIV *ueg-, → veged



### vigintī
viginfi 'twenty' [num. indecl] (Lex XII, Pl.+)

Derivatives: vice(n)simus 'twentieth' (Pl.+; <vFg- > SalL, Col.), vicie(n)s 'twenty times' (Cic.-H), vicem 'twenty at a time' (Varro, Cic,+); duodevlginti 'eighteen1 (Pl.+), undevlginti 'nineteen' (Cic.+).

Pit. *wikenti(» *wigenti), *mkenssamo-,

PIE *dui-dkmt-ihl 'twenty'.

IE cognates: Olr. fichey fichit [datVacc], OW uceint, MWfiggit; Skt vimsatU [f.], YAv. visaiti, Khot bista, MP wist, Oss. ysscez/inscej, Gr. είκοσι, Η. also έείκοσι, Dor. ρίκατι, Arm. k'san^ Alb, zet, ToB ikcan, ToA wiki 4 twenty\ The unexpected voiced g in Latin vJginti is explained by Kortlandt 1983a: 101 from voicing after a nasal in the words for '70' and *90\ and subsequent analogical introduction in the other decads. The / of -int- must be secondary for *-ent-, it may have arisen phonetically between the Ts of the surrounding syllables (thus Coleman). Lat vlcesimus from vikensumo- (CIL 6.7872) shows the same PIE suffix *-tmHo- as Indo-Iranian, Originally, 'twenty' was formed as a dual form meaning 'two teens'.

PIE *dui-dicmt-ihi is redundantly marked as a pair by *dul· 'two' and the dual ending.

Bibl.: WH II: 788f, EM 735, IEW 1177, Kortlandt 1983a, Schrijver 1991: 83, 182, Coleman 1992: 397f, 414, Sihler 1995: 418ff„ Meiser 1998: 172f. → centum, decern, -ginta

Pit. *wesli-*

PIE *ues-li- [adj.],

### vincio, -ire
vincio, -ire 'to tie up, bind' [v. IV; pf νίηχϊ, ppp. vinctum] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: vicia 'vetch* (Cato+), vinc(u)lum 'bond, chain' (Pl.+), vinctio 'binding together' (Varro+), vinctura 'which binds' (Varro+), vinctus, -lis 'tie' (Varro+); circumvincire 'to bind round' (Pl>), devincire 'to tie fast, bind' (Pl.+); provincia 'special task of a magistrate, command, district' (VL+\ provincialis 'of or belonging to a province' (Varro+).

Pit. *wink-(e/o~) 'to tie, bind'.

It. cognates: U. preuislatu, preuilatu [3s.ipv.II] 'to tie' < *prai-uinkela-tdd9 denom. to *ui(n)k(e)lo-.

PIE *ui-n-k-,

IE cognates: see s.v. vincd. Lat. vicia 'vetch' (< 'winding plant') may continue the nasalless root *uik-. The noun vinculum is derived from the verbal stem *uink- (either with *-(e)lo- or with *-i/o-). The nasal spread from the present to the pf. and ppp., whereas vincid itself can be a derivative of the earlier pr. *uink- (cf. vincd\ or from a nominal derivative *uink-i(o)-; Meiser 2003: 122 explains it as a back-formation to the pf. νϊηχΰ Lat. provincia seems based on an adj. *pro-wink-io- 'load, burden, charge' > 'task', cf Hamp 1997: 124f The reconstruction of the root with a final labiovelar is based on the very uncertain appurtenance of Gr. Ιψον 'ivy; prison' here; but Lat. vicis, U. preuislatu (thus Meiser 2003; 122) and the Gm. forms cited s.v. vincd point to a non-labialized velar. *

### vinco, -ere
vinco, -ere 'to conquer, overcome' [v. Ill; pf. vfci, ppp, victum] (Naev.+)

Derivatives: victor 'winner, conqueror' (Naev.+), victoria 'victory' (Naev.+), victoriatus 'with an image of Victory' (Cato+), victnx 'victorious' (of a woman)' (Pl.+), vincibilis 'that can be won' (Ter.+); convincere 'to prove wrong, find guilty5 (Pl.+), pervicax 'stubborn, determined' (Ter.+), pervicacia 'resolution' (Enn.+), pervicus 'stubborn' (Ace), pervincere 'to prevail over' (Pl.+), invictus 'invincible, not overcome,;,(Pl.+).

Pit. *wink-(e/c») 'to tie, bind' > 'tie in, overcome', aor. *weik~.

It. cognates: O, uincter [3s.pr.ps,] 'to prove someone's guilt', probably borrowed from Latin; PaeL uicturei [datsg,] 'winner' < Lat, victor-; O. vikturrai [datsg.] 'Victoria', loanword from Latin.

PIE *ui-n-k- [pr.] 'to bend, tie', *uik-to- [ppp.].

IE cognates: Oh.fichid* 'to battle' < PCI. *uike/o-, Olr. fecht [f a] , MW gweith [f.], OBret. gueth 'battle' < *uik-ta-\ Skt. pr. viviktas [3du,act.], pf. vivyaca [3s.act.] 'to contain, embrace', vyacas- [n.] 'extent, content', MP winj-, ps. winjlh- 'to be contained' < Ilr. *uik/c-y *uiak/c-; Gr. εΐκω 'to give way, yield' < *ueik-\ Lith. vezfoi, Is, veikiii 'to work; overcome', Latv, vikstu, vikt 'to give way, bend', viksts 'yielding, soft', Lith, vikriis 'agile, swift, skilful', veikus I veikus 'quick, strong, firm', Latv, veiks 'diligent, agile, skilful, limited', OIc. vega 'to battle, kill', Go. -waih, OIc. νά 'battled', Go, wigana [dat,sg.] 'battle'; Go. waihsta 'corner', MHG weigen 'to waver', OE wicga 'beetle', OFr. OE

<!-- pdf-page: 694; printed-page: 680; running-header: 680                                      vinum -->

wag, OE weg 'wall1 (< *uoiko-)\ OIc. gjafa-vixl 'gift-exchange1, OE wehsal 'commerce, money1, OHG wehsal, wehsil 'exchange, commerce"; OHG wehhay wohha, OS wika, OE wucu, wicu, OIc. vtka 'week' (< **change') < *uik-n-. Probably from the same root as vincio, pace LIV: 'to bend' can easily develop into 'overcome, conquer' (one bending the other) or 'give in, yield' (the one being bent by the other). Intransitive use can explain the meaning 'to fight, contend' (e.g. in Celtic). The pf. vwi could directly reflect a PIE root aorist *ueik-.

### vīnum
vTnum 'wine' [n. o] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: vinea 'vines in a vineyard; moveable penthouse' (Lex XII, Pl.+), vineaticus 'connected with vine-growing' (Cato+), vinetum 'vineyard' (Varro+), vinaceus 'the refuse from wine-pressing' (Cato+), vTnalia, -ium 'the name of two wine festivals' (Varro+), vinarius *for/of producing wine' (Pl.+), vTnolentus Of immoderate wine consumption' (Pl.+)> vinosus 'immoderately fond of wine' (Pl.+), villum 'small quantity of wine' (Ter.); vwibua 'female tippler of wine* (Lucil.).

Pit. *wmo-.

It. cognates: Fal. uinom, uino [acc.sg.], U. vinu [acc.sg.], vinu, uinuy Vol. uinu [abl.sg.] 'wine'.

PIE *ueihr(o)n, *uihre/on-m, *uih r n- [m./f.] 'vine; wine\

IE cognates: Hit. uiian- [cj 'wine' < *uihron-, {a)Uiniiant- 'wine (deified)1, CLuw. uiniia- 'of wine', HLuw. wiiana/i- 'vine'; Gr. οίνος, dial, ροΐνος [m.], Arm. gini (< *uoiniio-\ Alb. Tosk vere, Geg vene 'wine' < *uoihjno-m For vindemia^ see s.v. emo. The IE words for lvine' and 'wine' go back to an «-stem derived from the root 'to weave, wrap', hence 'vine' was referred to as 'the weaving one' (Beekes 1987b: 24). Whereas Hittite preserves thefl-stem,it has been thematized outside Anatolian, either with (Gr., Arm., Alb.) or without (Latin) introduction of an o-grade into the root. This explanation renders Oettinger's explanation for the Anatolian forms (2003: 143) superfluous. He assumes a development *uoihfno- > *uoino- > *ueno- > *uiyana-, with diphthongization of *e9 but the Italic forms would remain unexplained in his scenario.

Bibl.: WH II: 792f., EM 737f> IEW 1120-22, Giacomelli 1963: 258, Beekes 1987b, Untermann 2000: 857. → emo, vied, vitis



### violo, -are
violo, -are 'to violate, disturb' [v. I] (Lex Sacra CIL L366, Pl.+)

Derivatives: violentus 'violent1 (Pl.+), violenter 'violently' (Ter.+), violentia 'violence1 (Pl.+), inviolabilis 'indestructible' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *wijelo- 'chasing, violating'.

PIE *uiH-elo-.

IE cognates: see s.v. via. Probably derived from a deverbal adj. in *-elo- to the root *uiH- 'to strive after, chase'. Since the root *uiH- of vis 'power' is only nominal in the IE languages, it is less attractive to derive *vielo-fromthat root. Still, the Romans must have associated violo with vis, since derivatives in -(u)lentus are characteristically made from nouns (cf opulentus).

<!-- pdf-page: 695; printed-page: 681; running-header: vireo                                      681 -->

Bibl.: WH II: 801, EM 740, IEW 1123L, LIV *ueih r . → via, vis



### vīpera
vTpera 'poisonous snake' [f a] (Cic.+)

Derivatives: vjpermus Of a viper' (Acc.+).

Pit. *weip-es-a-?

PIE *ueip-es-? There are two competing etymologies. Pliny's explanation as *vivo-para 'who gives birth to living young* has been adopted by WH and Leumann, but it has several drawbacks. Semantically, *vivo-para would mean 'who gives birth to a living one' (cf. puerpera cwho gives birth (to a child)', but this goes for all mammals too, and is therefore hardly a specific description of snakes. It is clear from Pliny, Nat. 10, 62 (82) that the Romans knew that snakes lay eggs (ova pariunt serpentes), but observed the hatchlings as coming out of their eggs in the snake itself (dein singulis diebus singulos parity Formally, since the stress would be on the penultimate syllable, *wiwo-para would not contract (cf Meiser 1998: 92); the loss of *-vvo- could be due to haplology, but this is unattractive since vivas was always present in the language, and Apuleius even has an adj. viviparus 'giving birth to live young'. Thus, this explanation is better dropped. The alternative etymology is that vipera continues *weip-es-a to the root *wip/b- 'to swing, agitate' (cf. vibrdre), which makes semantic sense. Formally this solution is not completely transparent (*weipes- "swing, agitation' > 'snake', » *weipesa-7), but it is conceivable. The name for the viper may have referred to its behaviour (curling, hissing) or its form (patterns on the snake, form of the head) which leaves many possibilities for etymologies.

Bibl.: WH II: 796, EM 738, Leumann 1977: 394, LIV *ueip-. → vibrd



### vir
vir 'man' [m, o] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: virilis 'of a man, male' (Pl.+), virago, -inis [f ] 'strong or warlike woman' (Pl.+), viracius 'manly' (Varro), virJtim [adv.] 'man by man, individually' (Pl.+), virosus 'having an excessive sexual craving for men' (Lucil.H-), virtus, -tutis 'manly qualities, valour' (Andr.+); evirare 'to unman' (Varro+).

Pit. *wiro-.

It. cognates: UL uiro, ueiro [acc.pl.] 'company of men, troops' [n.] < *wiro-.

PIE *uiH-ro- 'man, young man, warrior'.

IE cognates: Olr. fer^ Skt vlra- 'man, hero', Av. vlra- 'man, human', Lith. vyras 'man, husband', Latv. virs, Go. wair 'man' < *uiro- < *mro-, ToA wir 'youthful, young, fresh*. The short vowel in Latin is due to Dybo's law, cf Schrijver 1991: 340. Since nouns in -ago are usually built to nouns or verbs in -«-, Leumann 1977: 369 suggests that virago might be built to the form vira 'femina' found in PauL exF. Alternatively, WH wonder whether virago 'warlike woman' was formed after imago 'image' - thus meaning 'man-like'. The adj. virilis may be formed on the basis of the gen.sg. via; thence was formed virJ-tim.

Bibl.: WH II: 796f, EM 738f, IEW 1177f, Schrijver 1991: 235, 340, Untermann 2000: 858f. → vis vireo 'to be green5 [v. II; pf virui] (Lucr.+)

<!-- pdf-page: 696; printed-page: 682; running-header: 682                                         virga -->

Derivatives: virescere 'to turn green' (Lucr.+), viridis [adj.] 'green' (Cato+), viridare 'to be green' (Acc.-l·), viridulus 'young and tender' (Naev.); praeviridans 'very green' (Lab.). %

Pit. *w(e)is-l

IE cognates: OPr. weisin 'fruit' [acc.sg.], Lith. veisti, 3s. veisia 'to breed, rear', visti, 3s. vysta 'to multiply, breed', vaisius 'fruit'; OIc. visir 'sprout' [?]; OE wise 'sprout, stem' [f.]; OHG wlsa 'meadow' [f.]. Uncertain etymology. None of the adduced set of cognates (Lat 'green', Baltic 'multiply, fruit', Gm. 'sprout, meadow') undoubtedly belong together.

Bibl.: WH II: 797, EM 739, IEW 1133, Schrijver 1991: 238, LIV L*ueis--



### virga
virga 'shoot, twig, rod' [f. a] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: virgeus 'made of twigs' (Cato+), virgator 'who wields a rod' (Pl.+), virgulta, -drum 'brushwood' (Cato+); virgidemia ca harvest of rods' (Pl.+).

Pit. *wizg-l

PIE *uis-g-?

IE cognates: OIc. visk 'wisp', Swed. viska 'small broom', OHG wise 'wisp', OE wiscian, weoskian (< *wiskon) cto plait a hurdle'. Virga has been derived from *wizg-a- and connected with other IE words of the form *uis-K- which refer to sticks, bundles or similar objects. Yet the connection with the root *uiH- 'to weave', upheld in prelaryngealistic studies, must now be abandoned. Also, the velar consonants do not always agree, and the meaning of verbs of the type 'to quiver, shake' (Lith. vizgu, vizgiti 'to tremble', vizgoti cto waver') is too far off Only the Germanic forms in *uisk- are close enough to inspire some confidence in their connection with virga. The adj. virgultus, attested as a noun from Cato onwards, presupposes the dim. virgula, which is only attested from Cicero onwards.

Bibl.: WH II: 797f., EM 739, IEW 1133f



### virgo, -inis
virgo, -inis 'girl of marriageable age; virgin' [f. (m.) ri\ (Andr.-l·)

Derivatives: virginalis 'of a girl of marriageable age' (Pl.+), virginarius 'concerned with girls of a marriageable age' (Pl.+), virgineus 'of a girl; virgin' (Lucr.+). WH interpret virgo as an individuation of virga 'twig, rod', implying a shift from 'young, virgin' > 'young plant'. Ledo-Lemos 2002 posits a compound *wir-gʷon- 'young woman', with an unacceptable formal and semantic analysis of the word for 'man' (recte

PIE *uiHro-) and formally of PIE 'woman'.

Bibl.: WH II: 799, EM 739f, IEW 1133f.



### virus
virus 'venom, poison' [n. o] (Lucil.+; almost restricted to nom.acc.sg.)

Derivatives: virosus 'with an unpleasant smell or taste' (Cato+).

Pit. *weis-o-(s-) [n.] 'poison'.

PIE *ueis [nom.], *uis-os [gen.] [n.] 'poison'.

IE cognates: Mlr.^z [gender and stem unknown], W. gwy 'poison' < PCL *uiso- 'poison', SkL visa- [n.], YAv. visa- [n,] 'venom, poison'; YAv. vis [n.] 'poison, poisonous juice'; Gr. ΐος [m.j'poison', ToA was, ToB wase 'poison, venom' (< *uiso-).

<!-- pdf-page: 697; printed-page: 683; running-header: vitis                                     683 -->

The restriction to nom.acc.sg. may mean that virus is an original n. s-stem *mros-; the origin of the s-stem inflection is unclear. Szemerenyi reconstructs *ueisos for Latin, and argues that the Greek word (Pindar+) has secondary lengthening in hiatus. Matasovic argues that the length of -/- is best accounted for by positing an original root-noun *ueis / *uis-os; Lat. preserved the stem of the nominative, whereas the other languages generalized the stem of the oblique cases.

Bibl.: WH II: 800, EM 740, IEW 1134, Szemerenyi 1989: 91, Schrijver 1991: 232, Matasovic 2004: 130.



### vis, vis
vis, vis 'strength, force' [f] (Aiidr.+; acc.sg. vim, nom.pl. vires, gen.pl. virium)

Derivatives: For vindex, see s.v. died.

Pit. *wis.

PIE *uiH-s * strength'.

IE cognates: Skt vayas- [n.] 'power, food\ Gr. Γς [f.] 'power, strength', ace. ΐν(α) , instr. ΐφι, Hsch. γίς (= γίς) D * strength' < *uiH-s, ~m. Traditionally analysed as an s-stem *uiH-$- with the same root as in *uiH-ro- 'man'. The Gr. and Lat. cognates, however, are more easily explained from an original root noun. If this is correct, the Skt. formation and the Lat. plural forms must be secondary. Any relation to *ueh r i- *to wind' or to *ueih,- cto hunt, pursue' is uncertain.

Bibl.: WH II: 793f., 800f., EM 737, 740, IEW 1123f., Untermann 2000: 146. → died, vir V

The original form may have been *w/fe- (in which case Latin has undergone a metathesis) or PwiKsk-, with various simplifications of the medial cluster in the different languages.

### vitis
vitis 'grape-vine' [f. i] (Enn.+)

Derivatives: vitilis 'made of wickerwork' (Cato+), viteus 'of a vine' (Varro+), vitigineus Of a vine or its wood' (Cato+), vitidrium 'nursery for vines' (Cato+),

PIE *uhii-ti-.

IE cognates: see s.v. vied.

<!-- pdf-page: 698; printed-page: 684; running-header: 684                                      vitium -->


Derivative in *-//- from the root *uh r i- 'to weave, wrap', hence 'weaving'. Compare the PIE etymology of vinum.

Bibl.: WH II: 804, EM 741, DEW 1123-22, Schrijver 1991: 245, UV *uieh r . → vied, vinum



### vitium
vitium 'defect, fault' [n. o] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: vitiare *to spoil, invalidate' (Ter.+), vitiosus 'faulty, defective' (Pl.+), vitiligo, -mis 'a form of skin eruption, psoriasis' (Lucil.+), vituperate 'to declare invalid, find fault with' (Pl.+).

Pit. *vwfto- 'deviation, fault'.

PIE *(d)ui-tio- 'apart, wrong'.

IE cognates: see s/v. duo. WH reconstruct a PIE adj4 *ui-ti-om * separation, deviation' to *(d)ui- 'apart', comparing Skt nitya- 'down'. This would amount to a similar dissimilation of two dentals as in

PIE *dui-tero- > Ilr., Grn., Slav. *(H)ui-tero- 'second, following'- Indo-Iranian, Tocharian and Umbrian have a form *du(i)~tio- 'second', but this is an /o-derivative of *du(i)-to-, and hence does not invalidate the solution for vitium. The verb vituperore presupposes an adj. *viti-paro- (with regular simplification of the lostem in the first member to -/-) or *vito-paro- 'bringing up a fault'. Vitiligo belongs to the names of diseases in -ig<5, but cannot be directly derived from vitium since -/- would remain unexplained.

Bibl.: WH II: 803f., 808, EM 741f., IEW 1175f., Leumann 1977: 296, 369, 390, Lindner 2002: 129. -^duo



### vito, -are *to move out of the way, avoid'
vito, -are *to move out of the way, avoid' [v. I] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: devTtare 'to avoid' (Pl.+), evitare *to avoid' (Varro+); Vitula 'goddess of joy' (Varro).

Pit. *wito- 'wanted'. PEE *uih r to- 'wished for'.

IE cognates: see s.v. via. WH assume *vi-ito 'to go out of the way', with a preverb *VJ- 'apart' < *dui- as in Ilr. Yet frequentative verbs in -itare are generally recent, which excludes an early dissimilation *dui-ita- > *ui-ita-. The alternative connection with Lat. invitus 'unwilling' (< *n-uiH-to~), vis 'you want' seems formally more probable to me. Semantically, we must then assume that *vitus was back-formed to *de-vitus 'avoiding'. Whether vltulari 'to utter a cry of joy' (Naev.+) also belongs here is uncertain.

Bibl.: WH II: 805, 807, EM 742, LIV *ueih r .



### vitricus
vitricus 'stepfather' [m. o] (CIL 1.583, Cic.+) WH and IEW derive vitricus from

PIE *(d)ui-tero- 'second, other' (as attested in Ilr., Germanic, Slavic), but this does not explain long 11 have no other solution.

Bibl.: WH II: 805, EM 742, IEW 1175f, Fruyt 1986: 232f.



### vitrum
vitrum 'glass; the plant *woad' (isatis tinctoria\ from whose leaves blue dye was produced' [n. o] (Lucr.-f-)

<!-- pdf-page: 699; printed-page: 685; running-header: vivo                                          685 -->

Derivatives: vitreus 'resembling glass; made of glass' (VarrcH-).

Pit. *wedro- 'glass'.

PIE *ued-ro- 4 water-like\

IE cognates: see s.v. undo. Szemerenyi 1989: 24f, derives uitrum from *ued-ro- on the basis of a semantic parallel in Middle Iranian, where 'glass' is derived from the word for 'water': ap-aka- 'water-like' > 'glass'. The Latin change of *dr > tr is regular. The change of *wet- > wit- is also found in vitulus 'calf and in other Latin words with a labial preceding *e. The plant and its dye will have been denominated after the colour of glass (in antiquity, a transparent green with a yellowish to biueish paleness).

Bibl.: WH II: 805f., EM 742. -» undo, uter



### vitulus
vitulus 'calf [m. o] (Cato+)

Derivatives: vitellus 'little calf (Pl.), 'yolk of an egg' (Varro+), vitulmus 'of a calf (Pl.+).

Pit. *wet-e!o- 'yearling, calf.

It. cognates: I I vitlu [acc.sg.m.], vitluf, uitlu [acc.pl. m.]? vitlaf, uitla [acc.pLf.] < *ueteIo-, -a- 'calf.

PIE *uet- 'year'.

IE cognates: see s.v. veins.

Bibl.: WH II: 807, EM 742, IEW 1175, Untermann 2000: 859f> → vetus

vlverra 'ferret (or similar animal)' [f. a] (Plin.+) ;

PIE *ue(r)-uer- (vel sim.).

IE cognates: Molr. iora rua 'squirrel', Scottish Gaelic feorag 'squirreP, OPr. weware 'squirreP, Lith. vaivens (vaivaras, vaivarys) 'male polecat or marten', vaiveri, voverey Latv. vavere, vaveris, ORu. veverica> Sin. veverica, Cz. veverka, Bulg. ververica, OE ac-weorna, OHG eihhurno, OIc. ikorni ' squirrel \ Irish, BSL and Gm. point to a word * (we(r))-wer- 'squirreP, and Lith. vaiveris shows that a shift to a meaning 'marten' is conceivable. In spite of their different status as herbivore vs. carnivore, they may have been compared due to their similar darting movements and their tree habitat, or on account of the fur for which they were hunted. Folk etymology may explain the ultimate outcome νϊ-ver-ra.

Bibl.: WH II: 808, EM 742f, IEW 1166, EIEC 540.



### vivo, -ere
vivo, -ere 'to live' [v. HI; pf. vaf, ppp. vTctum] (Lex XII+)

Derivatives: vivits 'alive' (Naev.+), vivescere 'to come to life' (Lucr.+), vlvax 'long-lived, energetic' (Afran.+), vividus 'full of vital force' (Lucr.+), vwtus, -us 'food, way of life' (Pl.+), vlctitare 'to keep oneself alive' (Pl.+); convlva [m.] 'guest' (Pl.+X convivium 'dinner-party' (Pl.+), convjvan 'to feast' (Enn.+), revivescere Ho come to life again' (Ter.+); vita 'life' (Pl.+), vitalis 'of life, life-giving' (Pl.+), evitare 'to kill' (Enn.+).

Pit. *gʷrwe/o- 'to live', *gʷfwo- 'alive', *gʷtia- 'life'.

It. cognates: Ven. vivoi [dat.sg.] 'alive', O. bivus [nom.pl.m.] 'alive' < *gʷT\vo-9 O. biitam [acc.sg.] 'life'.

PIE *gʷih₃-ue/o- 'to live', *gʷih₃-uo- 'alive'.

IE cognates: Skt. pr.pvati, aor. jlvit [3s.irij.act.], pf.jipva [3s.act] 'to live', jTvi/a- 'living5, also [n.] 'living being, life', βνά- 'alive, living person', Av. juua- 'to \\ve\juua- [adj.] 'alive', YAv.juuaiiarit-

<!-- pdf-page: 700; printed-page: 686; running-header: 686                                          νιχ -->


'making alive', OP βνα- 'to live', βνα- 'alive'; Gr. έβίων, βιώναι [aor.] 'to live' (< *gʷihrehrl βίος 'life' (< *gʷW-o-\ βιοτή 'life', βίοτος [m.] «id/ (< *gʷih5-eto-)\ OPr. gijwans [acc.pl.] 'alive5, giwasi [2s.pr.] 'you live\ Lith. gyvas, Latv. dzivs 'alive', Latv. dzivu [ls,pr,act] 'to live', OCS zivb, Ru. zivqj 'alive'; Lith- gyvata 'life', OCS zivotb [m.] 'life'; Lith. gyii, 3s. gyja 'to recover, heal (intr.)', Latv. dzit 'to heal (intr.)', OCS ίί/ι, Is. z/vp, Ru. Hf 'to live' < *gʷHi-. The adj. vfvus and the pr. vivere are both inherited. The pf. and ppp.-stem vie- is analogical after verbs in which *-ewgʷ- yielded *-uw- and then -w-, giving the impression that the velar stop had disappeared (Meiser 1998: 208), for instance fluo. The noun vita 'life' could reflect *mwita < *g"iH-uo-t-a-y cf. Lith. gyvata 'life'; thus Meiser 1998: 92. But vita could also directly reflect *gʷiH-t-hr, which would enable us to derive O. biitam from the same Pit noun (Leumann 1977: 335).

Bibl.: WH II: 808-810, EM 743, IEW 467-69, Lejeune 1974: 340, Schrijver 1991: 245, 526, Untermann 2000: 146-149, LIV *gyieh₃-.



### vix
vix 'hardly' [adv.] (Pl.+)

Pit. *wks [nom.sg.] / *wiksu [loc.pl.].

PIE *uik- 'a turn, bend'. A derivation from *uiki- 'turn, occasion' seems semantically possible ('only occasionally' > 'hardly'), but a nom.sg. *uikis is morphologically not the most straightforward candidate, and we would not expect *-kis > -ks after a short vowel in an open syllable. We may therefore adopt WH's solution of deriving vix from the root of vinco. A preform *uik-s could be the nom.sg. of a root noun *w/A> 'bond, tie' or 'victory'; clearly, the former meaning can lead to 'hardly' in a more straightforward may: 'bond' > adv. 'within bonds' > 'hardly'. As an alternative, we may consider a loc.pL *uik-su 'with ties, within bonds', compare mox 'soon' <

PIE *moksu for the loss of the final short vowel.

Bibl.: WH II: 810, EM 743, IEW 1128f — vicis, vincio, vinco



### vola * hollow in the palm of the hand or the sole of the foot'
vola * hollow in the palm of the hand or the sole of the foot' [f. a] (Varro+)

Pit. *wela-?

PIE *uel-h₂- 'round'?

IE cognates: OIc. valr 'round* < *uol-o-. Semantically, a connection with volvo 'to turn' <

PIE *uel-u- is attractive. Lat. vola can go back to *we/<5-, not to *wo/a- (which would yield *vala). The connection preferred by IEW, viz. with Gr. γύαλον 'a hollow', is improbable.

Bibl.: WH II: 825, EM 749, IEW 393ff. → volvo

### volō, velle
νοίδ, velle 'to want' [v. irr.; pf volul; pr. forms: void, vis, volt, volumus, voltis, volunt; sb. velim] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: voluntas 'will, choice' (Andr.+); benevolens, malevolens 'kind; ill-disposed' (Pl.+), benevolus, malevolus 'kind; ill-disposed' (Pl.+); nolle [nolo, nevls, nevolt, nolui\ 'not to want' (Naev.+); malle [mavold/malo, malm\ 'to prefer' (Naev.+); vel 'if you wish; or' (Naev.+), velut(i) 'for instance, just as, as if (Pl.+).

Pit. *wel-mi, -si, -ri [pr.], *wel-e/o- [sb.], *wel-i- [opt.].

It. cognates: Ven. leno [ins.sg.] 'volunary act' < *w/e-«o-; voltiio [ins.sg.n.] 'voluntary' < *wo!-ti-(i)o- 'of the will', volterkon [acc.sg.n.] 'voluntary' < *wol-tr-iko- to a noun *wol-tro~. Maybe O. velliam [acc.sg.] 'legal demand' < *wel-ja-; U. veltu, ehueltu [3s.ipvJI] 'to order', ehvelklu [acc.sg.] 'vote'.

PIE *uelh r [aor.], *ueih r 'to strive after, pursue'.

IE cognates: Skt. vj-nlte [3s.med.], aor. vfta [3s.inj.med.], pf. vav^se 'to choose, prefer', vrta- 'chosen, picked, picking', vara- [m.] 'wish, desire', OAv. var- [pr.] 'to choose', Av.-vairiia- 'preferable', vara- [n.] 'wish, aes\rz\frauuasi- 'tutelary spirit', OP avrnavata [3s.ipf med.] 'to choose' < Pllr. *uarH-\ Gr. λω, λης λη, λωμες 'to want, wish', λήμα [n.] 'will, courage, insolence', λησις (also ληΐς Dor.) 'will, choice' (if related, the Greek root reflects earlier *ρλη- which probably originated in the root-aorist); Lith. velti 'to wish, prefer' (Is. OLith. velmi) , OCS veleti, Is. veljQ, Ru. veleV 'to command, order', Sin. veleti 'to want, order, say', Is. velim < PSL *veleti, OCS voliti 'to want, wish', Cz. voliti 'to choose' < PS1. *voliti, OCS volja, Ru. volja 'will, wish, freedom' < PSL * ν φ [f.ja] , Go. wiljan 'to want', OHG wala [f], OIc. val [n.] 'choice'. The pr. shows the regular change of *«el· > *-o/- before non-front vowels. According to Dunkel 1998: 97, void and volumus may continue the pr.sb. *wel-o, *wel-o-mos. The conjunction vel retains the original 2s. *welsi > *well > vel. The 2s. vis is explained from *ueiH-s 'you wish' to the root *w///- 'to strive after' by Meiser 1998: 224; Cowgill 1978 derives vis by phonetic development from * wells, but this involves a lot of special pleading. The negated verb nolo is contracted from *ne + void, vis, volt, while maid Ί prefer' and its older counterpart mavolo reflect *mags-welo < *magis-welo Ί want more'.

Bibl.: WH Π: 743, 828-830, EM 717f, 750, IEW 1137f, Lejeune 1974: 336, 340£, Cowgill 1978, Untermann 2000:203,833f, Weiss 2002:358f, LIV *uelhr. → valeo, volup



### volo, -are
volo, -are 'to fly' [v. I] (Andr.+)

Derivatives: volaticus 'flying' (Pl.+), volatura 'flight' (Varro+), volitare 'to fly about, move swiftly' (Enn.+), volucer, -cris 'able to fly, swift' (Naev.+), volucris [f.] "winged creature' (Pac.+); velivolus 'speeding along under sail' (Enn.+), evolare 'to

<!-- pdf-page: 702; printed-page: 688; running-header: 688                                       volpes -->


fly up or out, escape1 (Pl.+), transvolare 'to fly across' (Pl>), transvolitare 'to fly across' (Lucr.+).

Pit. *gʷeh-je/o-.

PIE *gʷelh₁-ie/o- 'to raise the aim, throw\

IE cognates: Olr. a-vbaill 'to die' < *eks-bal-nU, MW ballaf 'to die' < PCL *balni- 'dies' < ""throws it'? (the appurtenance to

PIE *g w elh r is not certain); Skt. gar1 'to raise the arm (for striking), to raise a weapon* {ud-guramana- [ptc.pr.med.], apa-jargurana- [ptc.int.med.]), Gr, βάλλω 'to throw, to hit', βλήμα [n.] 'throw, hit'. Muller 1926: 213 derives volare from the root *gʷelhr 'to throw' whereas WH reject this. Yet if the original meaning was 'to raise the arm', a shift to 'fly' is quite conceivable in the connection with birds. Morphologically, one could reconstruct a je/o-present *gʷelhrje/o- > *welaje- > *wela- (cf arare\ or maybe an originally reduplicated intensive *g"e(l)-ffolhriefo-* WH regard volucer as an old /-stem *voluclis, but the vowel -w- remains unexplained.

MCo. gweles, OBret guelet 'to see' < PCh HieUeh-\ Olr.fili, filed 'seer' < *we/-ef- (Irslinger 2002: 56); Go. wulpus [m.] 'wealth' < *w/-rw- 'respect'. Probably from *wel-tu- 'gaze, view' > 'look' > 'face', thus Hamp 1984a: 149.

### volup
volup 'with pleasure' [adv.] (Naev.+; Naev. volop)

Derivatives: voluptos 'pleasure' (Pl.+), voluptobilis 'causing pleasure' (Pl.), voluptarius 'of or devoted to pleasure' (Pl.+), Vohtpia 'goddess of pleasure' (Varrcrf),

Pit. *we/olpi.

PIE *u(e/o)lp-i [n.] 'hope, pleasure'.

IE cognates: Gr. έλπομαι 'to expect, hope, think', pf. έολπα, έλπωρή 'hope' (for *έλπωλη?Π), έλπίς, -ίδος [f] 'hope'. The ms. variant volupe has probably arisen in volupest 'volup est', cf. Torzi 1991. Nevertheless, volup might reflect

Pit. *w(e/o)Ipi 'hopefully', which could continue the n. of a PIE /-stem 'hope' (which also Gr. έλπίδ- could reflect). An apocopated Proto-Latin form *we/olp could have acquired an anaptyctic vowel between / and p. Admittedly, there is no anaptyxis in volt 'he wants', but 3s. *wel-t could have been restored on the model of the other paradigmatic forms. In order to explain -u- from a real Pit vowel, Hamp 1980 reconstructs *ne/-A/p- 'desire reaching', i.e. 'attaining one's wish', a cp. from the root of void (*uelh r ) and the root of opio 'to reach'. In a syntagm *uel-hip *htesti 'there is desire-reaching' with enclitic copula,

Pit. *welap-est would have given *velup(est) > volup. Yet in the absence of any evidence for the combination of *uelhp plus *h₁p-, of a root nouii *uelh r , and of the forced semantics, this etymology does not seem likely.

Bibl.: WH II: 834, EM 752, IEW 1137f, LIV *uelp-,



### volva
volva 'womb' [f. a] (Varro+; CLat vulva)

Derivatives: volvula 'small womb' (Naev.).

Pit. *we/olwa-.

PIE *u(e/o)l-u-h₂-.

IE cognates: see s.v. volvo. The connection with

PIE *gʷelbb- 'womb' (Gr. δελφύς) is only possible for the spelling volba, bulba, but this appears late in Imperial times, and cannot be relied on. Hence, we need an etymon in VOLat *-w-. Since the womb 'envelopes' the foetus, a derivation from volvo is only natural, and it also explains the suffix *-w-. The ablaut can be assumed to have been the same as in the verb.

Bibl.: WH II: 831 f, EM 751, IEW 1140-44, LIV 2.*ueK → volvo, volvo

PIE *uel-u- 'to wind'.

### vōmer
vSmer 'ploughshare' [m. r] (Cato+; also nom.sg. vomis Verg. Col., vdmeris Cato)

Pit. *woywsmi-?

PIE *uogʷʰ-ni- 'coulter, ploughshare'?

IE cognates: Gr. όφνίς 'plough(share)' (Hsch.), OPr. wagnis 'coulter', OIc. vangsni, OHG waganso 'ploughshare'. Greek and Old Prussian point to a possible preform *uogʷʰni-; one could then posit *uogʷʰmi- for Latin. Yet the latter would probably yield *uoumi- > *uftmi- (unless there was a phonological constraint on *wi7-, and *uoumi- regularly yielded *vomi-). Alternatively, one might posit a suffix *-smi-, hence *uogʷʰ-smi- > *uogzml· > *uozmi- > vomi-. We more often find an s-stem for an earlier /-stem, cf. tints. The nom.sg. vomis seems to retain the old nom.

Bibl.: WH II: 835, EM 752, IEW 1179f.

Pit. *g*o/era-,

PIE *gʷo/erhj-o/h₂- 'devouring, devoured'.

IE cognates: Skt. pr. girati 'devours, swallows', aor. garan [3p.inj.act.], aor. garit [3s.inj.act.], pf. jagara [3s,act.], int. jalgulos [2s.sb.act.j, galgaliti [3s.acL], ppp. girna-, YAv. aspd.gar- 'devouring horses', ndw.gar- 'devouring men'; Gr. βιβρώσκω 'to eat, digest' < *gʷi-gʷrh₃-sk-, βρωτός 'edible', βορά [f.] 'fodder1; Arm. e-ker [3s.act.] 'ate'; Lith. gerti 'to drink', girtas ' drunk \ OCS po-zreti 'to eat (of animals), to devour' < BSl. *ger?→ Probably a denominative verb to a noun/adj. *gʷora- 'food' or 'devouring', cf. Gr. βορά. In theory, a preform *gʷera- is also possible.

<!-- pdf-page: 705; printed-page: 691; running-header: vox                                       691 -->

Bibl.: WH II: 836, EM 753, IEW 474-476, Steinbauer 1989: 216, Schrijver 1991: 217, Meiser 1998: 100, LIV *gyerh₃-,



### vos
vos 'you (pi.)' [pron. pers. 2p.] (Lex XII, Andr.+; nom.acc. vos, databl. vobTs; gen. vestrum Pl.+, ves/rf Ter.+, vostrorum [m.J, vostrarum [f ])

Derivatives: voster (before 150 BC) / vester (after 150 BC) 'your' [pron. poss. 2p.] (Naev.+).

Pit. *wosy *w5-f-, *wos-tero-.

It. cognates: FaL ues 'you'; PaeL uus [nom.], uus [dat.] 'you' (pi.) < *wos [nom.], *wo-fos [dat.]; U. uestra [abl.sg.f ] 'your' (pi.) < *wes-tero-.

PIE ace. *uos, gen. *uo/es, adj.poss. *us-tero-?

IE cognates: Skt. vas 'you' [acc.pL, gen.pL, daLpl.], OAv. V3 [gen.dat.pl.], ΥΑν. vo (< *uas); OAv. va [acc.pl.] (< *uas) 'you', OCS vy [nom.], van? [gen.acc.loc], vamb [dat.]. The original nom. *iuH(s) was replaced by the stressed ace. form *wo$, apparently already in Pit The gen. vestrum is originally the gen.pl. of vester, it was replaced by vestri. The e of U. uestra probably shows

Pit. *westero-, while Latin has analogical voster after noster. In Faliscan, the e spread to the accented personal pronoun.

Bibl.: WH II: 836f, EM 753, IEW 513f, Giacomelli 1963: 257f, Leumann 1977: 463-466, Sihler 1995: 379-383, Beekes 1995: 208, Meiser 1998: 156-159, Untermann 2000:851,865.



### voveo
voveo 'to promise, vow' [v. II; pf. νδνϊ, ρρρ. votum] (Pl5+)

Derivatives: votum [vootum in a Fal. inscr.] Wow, prayer' (Naev.+); convbvere 'to join in taking a vow' (SCBac.), devotare 'to put a spell on' (Pl.+).

Pit. *wow-eje/o- 'to promise'.

It. cognates: maybe U. vufru [acc.sg.m.] 'fulfilling an oath' < *uogʷʰ-ro-\ also U. vufetes [dat.abLpl.n.] 'votive?' < *uogʷʰeto^

PIE *h₁UOgʷʰ-eie- 'to promise'.

IE cognates: Skt. pr. ohate [3p.med.], ohana- [ptamed.] 'to praise, announce', oha- [m.] 'praising', ohas- [n.] 'praise', OAv. aogdda [3s.inj.med.], pairiiaoyza [2s.inj.pr.med.], YAv. aoxta [3s.inj.med.]3 aojana- [ptc.med.] 'to say, announce' < Ilr. *Ha(H)ugh- <

PIE *A/eA/w^/j- [pr.red.]; Skt. vaghat- [m.] 'singer, priest' <

PIE *h}uogwh-et-/-nt-; Gr. εύχομαι 'to pray, vow, boast' < *h₁eugʷ -e-, Myc. e-u-ke-to 'εύχεται* 'to declare', Gr, pret. εύκτο; Gr. εύχος 'glory'; Arm* gog 'say!'. The causative voveo < *uogʷʰ-eie~ shows that the lenition of medial *-gvh- > *~w- postdates the change of

PIE *-ow- > *-aw-. Since

PIE *uo- generally developed into va- in open syllable, voveo must have restored the o-vowel after this sound change took place, on the model of other causative verbs. The ppp. votum was contracted from *wowito-y in which -i- is the unsyncopated reduced medial vowel.

Bibl.: WH II: 837, EM 753, IEW 348, Giacomelli 1963: 258f, Schrijver 1991: 76, 279, 472, Untermann 2000: 861-63, LIV *h,uegyh-.



### vox, vocis
vox, vocis 'voice' [f. k] (Pl.+)

Derivatives: vocula 'soft voice' (Titin.+), vocalis 'able to speak, with a voice' (Varro+), vocare "to call, summon' (Lex XII+), vocabulum "name, noun' (Pl.+),

<!-- pdf-page: 706; printed-page: 692; running-header: 692                                      vox -->

vocamen 'designation' (Lucr.+), vocitare 'to call' (Pl.+); vociferare/ϊ 'to shout, cry' (Varro+), vociferatid 'clamour' (Afran.+), vocificare 'to utter loud sounds' (Varro+); advocare 'to call upon, summon' (Pl.+J, arvocitat 'summons often' (Paul, ex / \ ) , convocare 'to convoke' (Pl.+), evocare 'to evoke, summon' (Pl.+), invocare 'to call upon, pray for' (Naev.+), provocare 'to call forth, challenge' (Andr.+), provocator 'challenger' (Pl.+), revocare 'to summon back, recall' (Pl.+)*

Pit. *wokʷs, *wokʷ-.

It. cognates: U. suboco [acc.sg. or pi.] 'invocation' < *sub-wok-(o-)\ IL subocamii subocau Ί invoke' < *sub-wok-a-je-.

PIE *uokʷ-s, *uokʷ-m [f.] 'voice'.

IE cognates: Skt. να£ [nom.sg.], vacam [ace], v<5cos [gen.] [f] cvoice, speech', vagvin- 'eloquent', OAv. vaxs [nom.sg.], vacam [ace], vaco [gen.], YAv. νάχί [nom.sg.], vacim [ace], vara [ins.], vacqm [gen.pL] 'voice', Gr. όπ- [f.] 'voice, sound, word': δπα [acc.sg.], οπός [gen.], όπί [dat.]; ToB wek [m,sg.], ToA wok 'voice, noise'. The verb vocare must be a denominative from the oblique stem *uokʷ- of vox Possibly, it went through an intermediate derivative *yokʷ-a- 'voices, call'.

Bibl.: WH II: 823f., EM 753f.t IEW 1135f., Schrijver 1991: 472, Untermann 2000: 707f., LIV *ueky-.

# Bibliography

## Abbreviations of Literature

```text
CGL              Corpus Glossariorum Latinorum
CIL              Corpus Inscriptionum Latinarum
EIEC             Mallory-Adams 1997
EM               Ernout-Meillet 1979
EWAia            Mayrhofer 1986-2002
IEW              Pokorny 1959
LEIA             Lexique Etymologique du VieiUlrlandais
UV               Lexikon der indogermanischen Verben, 2, Auflage
OLD              Oxford Latin Dictionary
Paul, ex Ft      Paulus Diaconus: Excerpta ex Ubris Pompeii Festi de significatione
                 verborum      '■■ ·                   ■·
TLL              Thesaurus Linguae Latinae           ■
WH               Walde-Hoffinann 1930-1954           *
```

ABAG = Amsterdamer Beitrdge zur alteren GermanistiL· AJPh = American Journal of Philology. BSL = Bulletin de la Societe de Linguistique. ECIEC — East Coast Indo-European Conference. HS = Historische Sprachforschung. HSCP = Harvard Studies in Classical Philology. IBS = Innsbrucker Beitrdge zur Sprachwissenschafi. IF = Indogermanische Forschungen. IJDLLR = International Journal ofDiachronic Linguistics and, Linguistic Reconstruction. JIES = Journal of Indo-European Studies. MSS = Miinchener Studien zur Sprachwissenschqft. NOWELE = North-Western European Language Evolution. NTS = Norsk Tidsskrififor Sprogvidenskap. ITS = Transactions of the Philological Society. ZVS - Zeitschrifi fur Vergleichende Sprachforschung.
